topics.title: Classification of Fire - Pool Fire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Classification of Fire - Pool Fire # 🔥 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Classification of Fires — The Universal Taxonomy In process plant safety engineering, every fire scenario must first be **classified** before any mitigation strategy, suppression system, or hazard analysis can be applied. The international consensus classification (adopted by **NFPA 10**, **IS 2190**, and referenced in **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.157** followed in KTU industry practice) divides fires into five broad categories based on the **physical state of the fuel** and the **suppression mechanism** required. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme — Board-Exam Weightage Snapshot (PECHT743)** > Module 1 (Fire & Explosion Fundamentals) carries **~20–25%** marks in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). Pool Fire is consistently a **high-yield topic** because it is the most frequently occurring fire type in refineries, bulk-storage terminals, and LPG/LNG handling units. ### 🔖 The Five Standard Fire Classes | Class | Fuel Type | Typical Examples | Suppression Philosophy | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | **Class A** | Ordinary combustibles (solid, organic, carbonaceous) | Wood, paper, cloth, rubber, plastics, coal | **Cooling with water** to remove heat | | **Class B** | Flammable liquids & gases (LPG, gasoline, solvents) | Petrol, diesel, kerosene, naphtha, LPG, LNG | **Smothering / oxygen exclusion** (foam, CO₂, dry chemical) | | **Class C** | Energized electrical equipment | Live transformers, switchgear, motors | **Non-conductive agent** (CO₂, dry chemical) — **never water** until de-energized | | **Class D** | Combustible metals | Magnesium, sodium, potassium, titanium, zirconium | **Special dry powder** (NaCl, Cu powder, graphite) | | **Class K** (or **Class F** in EU) | Cooking oils & fats | Vegetable oils, animal fats in deep fryers | **Wet chemical** saponification agents | > [!NOTE] > **Why classification matters:** A Class B fire responds *violently* to a solid-jet water spray because the water can sink below the lighter fuel and *eject* burning oil skyward (a phenomenon called **"slop-over" or boil-over**). The wrong extinguishant transforms a controllable fire into a catastrophe. This is exactly the reasoning KTU examiners test in Part B questions. --- ## 1.2 What is a Pool Fire? — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **POOL FIRE — KTU Board Definition (must reproduce verbatim in exams):** > A **pool fire** is a *diffusion flame* that burns above a horizontal pool of liquid fuel (which may be contained in a bund, dike, trench, spill, or simply a thin layer on the ground). Combustion is sustained by **buoyancy-driven convection** of air from the sides, with no significant momentum of the fuel itself. The fuel vaporizes at the liquid surface, mixes with entrained air, and burns in the flame zone located *above* the pool. ### 🌐 Real-World Intuition — The "Kerala Kitchen + Refinery" Analogy Imagine you spill **kerosene** on the kitchen floor and light it. The flame is **steady, pale-yellow, and rectangular**. It does *not* shoot out like a rocket (that would be a **jet fire**); it does *not* explode outward (that would be a **BLEVE/VCE**). It simply *sits* on the floor and burns quietly while feeding on the air around it. **That is a pool fire.** Now scale this image to **2,000 m² of crude oil** leaking from a ruptured storage-tank dyke in a refinery — the physics remains identical, but the consequence becomes a multi-megawatt inferno releasing **black smoke, intense thermal radiation, and boil-over risk** when water reaches hot crude below. > [!NOTE] > **Three physical "fingerprints" that identify a pool fire:** > 1. **Diffusion flame** (fuel and oxidizer mix *at* the flame, not pre-mixed). > 2. **Stationary flame geometry** relative to a *horizontal fuel layer*. > 3. **Heat feedback** from the flame to the pool drives further vaporization (steady-state burning rate depends on pool diameter and thermodynamics). --- ## 1.3 Pool Fire vs. Other Process-Plant Fires — The Diagnostic Table | Property | Pool Fire | Jet Fire | Fireball | Flash Fire | |:---|:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Geometry** | Horizontal, quiescent | Directional, momentum-driven | Spherical, expanding | Transient, ground-hugging cloud | | **Fuel State** | Liquid pool | High-pressure gas/liquid release | Vaporizing aerosol cloud | Dispersed vapor cloud | | **Dominant Driver** | Buoyancy | Momentum | Combustion of suspended droplets | Convective drift of unburned vapor | | **Duration** | Long (until fuel consumed) | Long (until release stops) | Very short (seconds) | Very short (< 10 s) | | **Typical Source** | Tank bund, dyke, spill | Ruptured pipeline, flange leak | LPG tank BLEVE | Delayed ignition of vapor cloud | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Many students write "a pool fire is any large fire." It is *not*. The pool (a quiescent liquid layer) is **mandatory**. A burning *tower* of flames from a gas leak is a **jet fire**, not a pool fire, even if the visual scale is identical. # 🔥 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physics of a Pool Fire — Step-by-Step Logic Flow A pool fire is governed by a tight feedback loop. Below is the logical chain that any KTU essay or 14-mark question must reproduce: 1. **Fuel Supply:** Liquid pool of hydrocarbon at temperature $T_s$ (surface temperature, often approximated as the boiling point for multi-component fuels). 2. **Heat Feedback:** Flame radiates and convects energy back to the pool surface at flux $q^{\prime\prime}_{fb}$. 3. **Vaporization:** Liquid absorbs heat, mass flux $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}$ of fuel vapor evolves. Steady-state energy balance: $$\dot{m}^{\prime\prime} = \frac{q^{\prime\prime}_{fb}}{\Delta H_{vap} + c_{p,l}(T_{b}-T_{s})}$$ 4. **Air Entrainment:** Buoyancy pulls ambient air from the perimeter into the flame column. 5. **Combustion:** Stoichiometric mixing yields heat release rate (HRR): $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}^{\prime\prime} \cdot A_{pool} \cdot \Delta H_{c} \cdot \chi$$ 6. **Radiative Output:** A fraction $X_r$ of $\dot{Q}$ is radiated to surroundings, which is what threatens adjacent equipment: $$\dot{Q}_{rad} = X_r \cdot \dot{Q}$$ 7. **Termination:** Either the fuel is consumed, the pool is extinguished (foam, water), or the dyke overflows. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The single most important engineering quantity** is the **mass burning rate per unit area** $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}$. Everything else (HRR, radiation, flame height) cascades from it. KTU examiners almost always begin a numerical sub-question by asking you to compute $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}$. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Pool Fire Engineering > [!NOTE] > **Notation Convention:** Prime (′) denotes *per unit length*, double-prime (″) denotes *per unit area*, and dot (˙) denotes *rate (per unit time)*. ### 🔖 Table 2.1 — Master Equation Set (must memorize for ESE) | # | Quantity | Equation | Units | Applicability / Source | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---| | 1 | **Mass burning rate (small pool, < 0.05 m)** | $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime} = \frac{k \cdot \beta}{c_{p,l}(T_{b}-T_{s})}$ | $\text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$ | Pure conduction through liquid | | 2 | **Mass burning rate (large pool, > 1 m)** | $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime} = \dot{m}^{\prime\prime}_{\infty} \left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right)$ | $\text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$ | **Babrauskas correlation** (canonical) | | 3 | **Asymptotic burning rate** | $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}_{\infty} = 0.001 \cdot \beta$ (hydrocarbons, with $\beta$ in **mm/min**) | $\text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$ | If $\beta$ given as mass-flux coefficient | | 4 | **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}^{\prime\prime} \cdot A \cdot \Delta H_{c} \cdot \chi$ | W (Watts) | $A$ = pool area; $\chi$ = combustion efficiency (≈ 0.9–1.0 for hydrocarbons) | | 5 | **Froude-number flame height** | $\dfrac{H}{D} = 42 \left( \dfrac{\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}}{\rho_{a}\sqrt{gD}} \right)^{0.61}$ | dimensionless ratio | **Thomas / Heskestad flame-height correlation** | | 6 | **Flame tilt (with wind)** | $\cos\theta = 0.7$ for transverse wind | degrees | Empirical for design | | 7 | **Radiative fraction $X_r$** | $X_r = a \cdot D^{b}$ (fuel-specific) | dimensionless | **Koseki–Mullins** model | | 8 | **View-factor radiative flux at distance $x$** | $q^{\prime\prime}_{rad}(x) = \dfrac{X_r \cdot \dot{Q}}{4\pi x^{2}} \cdot \tau$ | $\text{kW/m}^2$ | Point-source approximation | | 9 | **Burning duration** | $t_{b} = \dfrac{\rho_l \cdot h_{pool}}{\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}}$ | s | $h_{pool}$ = initial pool depth | ### 🔖 Table 2.2 — Typical Burning-Rate & Radiative-Fraction Constants | Fuel | $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}_{\infty}$ ($\text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$) | $\Delta H_c$ (MJ/kg) | $X_r$ (range) | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **Methane** | 0.078 | 50.0 | 0.10 – 0.20 | | **n-Hexane** | 0.074 | 44.7 | 0.30 – 0.40 | | **Gasoline** | 0.055 | 43.7 | 0.30 – 0.45 | | **Kerosene** | 0.039 | 43.0 | 0.30 – 0.40 | | **Diesel** | 0.035 | 43.0 | 0.25 – 0.40 | | **LPG (propane)** | 0.099 | 46.0 | 0.25 – 0.35 | | **Crude Oil** | 0.033 | 42.5 | 0.20 – 0.35 | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > - **Bund Sizing:** Using $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}_{\infty}$, designers compute the *time to consume fuel inside a dyke* and size the bund capacity accordingly (typically 110% of largest tank volume per **OISD-118 / NFPA 30**). > - **Thermal Radiation Layout:** $q^{\prime\prime}_{rad}(x)$ at vessel surfaces determines *safe separation distances* between tanks (e.g., **API 521 / IS 1239**). > - **Firewater Demand:** Foam application rate $\propto \dot{m}^{\prime\prime}$ and pool area (e.g., **NFPA 11** specifies 4.1 L/min·m² for hydrocarbon pool fires). > - **Emergency Response:** Predicted flame height $H$ tells firefighters whether ground-based jets can reach the flame tip. # 🔥 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Babrauskas Mass Burning Rate Correlation The Babrauskas correlation is the **gold-standard expression** KTU examiners expect students to derive, justify, and apply. ### Step 1 — Define the regression rate For a steady burning liquid pool, let $y(t)$ be the liquid level. Mass conservation on the pool: $$\rho_l \frac{dy}{dt} = -\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}$$ Integrate from initial depth $h_0$ to current depth $y$: $$\rho_l (h_0 - y) = \int_0^t \dot{m}^{\prime\prime} \, dt$$ This shows pool depth decays linearly with time *if* $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}$ is constant. ### Step 2 — Energy balance on the pool surface At the surface, the heat required to vaporize fuel equals the heat feedback from the flame. Neglecting heat losses to the bulk liquid: $$\dot{m}^{\prime\prime} \left[\Delta H_{vap} + c_{p,l}(T_{b}-T_{s})\right] = q^{\prime\prime}_{fb}$$ **Solving for $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}$:** $$\dot{m}^{\prime\prime} = \frac{q^{\prime\prime}_{fb}}{\Delta H_{vap} + c_{p,l}(T_{b}-T_{s})}$$ **Explanation:** The numerator is the heat arriving from the flame; the denominator is the energy required to raise liquid to boiling point *plus* latent heat of vaporization. ### Step 3 — Express heat feedback as function of pool diameter Experimental observations (Babrauskas, 1983) show that $q^{\prime\prime}_{fb}$ depends on pool diameter $D$ because larger pools generate taller flames and re-radiate more efficiently. The empirical form is: $$q^{\prime\prime}_{fb} = \dot{m}^{\prime\prime}_{\infty} \cdot \left[\Delta H_{vap} + c_{p,l}(T_{b}-T_{s})\right] \cdot \left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right)$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute and simplify Plugging Step 3 into Step 2 yields the canonical Babrauskas equation: $$\dot{m}^{\prime\prime} = \dot{m}^{\prime\prime}_{\infty} \left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right)$$ where: - $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}_{\infty}$ = **asymptotic burning rate** at large diameter, - $k$ = **empirical constant** ≈ **1.1** m⁻¹ for hydrocarbons, - $\beta$ = a property coefficient combining conductivity, density, and heat of gasification, - $D$ = **equivalent pool diameter** in metres. ### Step 5 — Limiting-case sanity check **Case A — Small pool ($D \to 0$):** $$\lim_{D\to 0}\left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right) = k\beta D \implies \dot{m}^{\prime\prime} \propto D$$ Matches experiment: small pools burn slowly because conduction losses through the cold bulk liquid dominate. **Case B — Large pool ($D \to \infty$):** $$\lim_{D\to \infty}\left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right) = 1 \implies \dot{m}^{\prime\prime} = \dot{m}^{\prime\prime}_{\infty}$$ Confirms asymptotic independence from diameter — the experimentally observed plateau. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why KTU loves this derivation:** It (a) tests energy balance (a core competency), (b) tests limiting-case analysis (Bloom's Level 4 – *Analyze*), and (c) connects to a real engineering correlation (Bloom's Level 3 – *Apply*). A 7-mark sub-part almost always ends here. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical — A "Classic" KTU Board Problem > **Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Set 2]:** > A circular bund contains **n-hexane** at a pool diameter of **D = 8 m**. Compute: > (a) The steady-state mass burning rate per unit area using Babrauskas correlation. > (b) Total heat release rate assuming 95% combustion efficiency. > (c) Flame height using the Thomas correlation. > (d) Radiative heat flux to a target located **15 m** from the pool edge. **Given:** - $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}_{\infty} = 0.074 \;\text{kg/m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}$ (n-hexane) - $k\beta = 1.1 \;\text{m}^{-1}$ - $\Delta H_c = 44.7 \;\text{MJ/kg}$ - $\chi = 0.95$ - $\rho_a = 1.2 \;\text{kg/m}^3$ - $g = 9.81 \;\text{m/s}^2$ - $X_r = 0.35$ - $\tau \approx 1.0$ (clear atmosphere, short distance) ### Solution — Sub-part (a) $$\dot{m}^{\prime\prime} = 0.074 \left(1 - e^{-1.1 \times 8}\right) = 0.074 \left(1 - e^{-8.8}\right)$$ $$e^{-8.8} \approx 1.51 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\dot{m}^{\prime\prime} = 0.074 \times (1 - 0.000151) \approx 0.07399 \;\text{kg/m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}$$ **Conclusion:** $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime} \approx 0.074 \;\text{kg/m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}$ — confirms the **large-pool limit** has been reached for $D = 8$ m. ### Solution — Sub-part (b) Pool area: $$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(8)^2 = 50.27 \;\text{m}^2$$ Heat release rate: $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}^{\prime\prime} \cdot A \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi$$ $$\dot{Q} = 0.074 \times 50.27 \times 44.7 \times 10^6 \times 0.95$$ $$\dot{Q} \approx 1.58 \times 10^8 \;\text{W} = 158 \;\text{MW}$$ ### Solution — Sub-part (c) Using the Thomas correlation: $$\frac{H}{D} = 42 \left( \frac{\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}}{\rho_a \sqrt{gD}} \right)^{0.61}$$ Calculate denominator: $$\rho_a \sqrt{gD} = 1.2 \times \sqrt{9.81 \times 8} = 1.2 \times 8.86 = 10.63 \;\text{kg/m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}$$ Ratio: $$\frac{\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}}{\rho_a \sqrt{gD}} = \frac{0.074}{10.63} = 6.96 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\left(6.96 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{0.61} \approx 0.0418$$ $$\frac{H}{D} = 42 \times 0.0418 = 1.756$$ $$\boxed{H = 1.756 \times 8 \approx 14.0 \;\text{m}}$$ ### Solution — Sub-part (d) Using the point-source approximation (treat flame as a sphere of equivalent diameter at pool centre): $$\dot{Q}_{rad} = X_r \cdot \dot{Q} = 0.35 \times 158 = 55.3 \;\text{MW}$$ Distance from pool centre to target (add $D/2 = 4$ m to the 15 m offset): $$x = 15 + 4 = 19 \;\text{m}$$ Radiative flux: $$q^{\prime\prime}_{rad} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4\pi x^2} = \frac{55.3 \times 10^6}{4\pi (19)^2}$$ $$q^{\prime\prime}_{rad} = \frac{55.3 \times 10^6}{4536} \approx 12{,}190 \;\text{W/m}^2$$ $$\boxed{q^{\prime\prime}_{rad} \approx 12.2 \;\text{kW/m}^2}$$ **Interpretation (write this in the answer):** - Per **API 521 / IS 1239**, prolonged exposure beyond **6.3 kW/m²** requires emergency action and beyond **12.5 kW/m²** will cause secondary fires within seconds. - Hence the target vessel is in the **imminent-damage zone** and must be water-cooled immediately. # 🔥 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Pool Fire Phenomenology The diagram below maps the **physical & energy pathways** inside a pool fire. This is the conceptual schematic KTU board answers must include whenever a 14-mark question asks "explain pool fire with neat sketch." ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Liquid Fuel Pool
Diameter D, Depth h] B[Conductive Heating of Sublayer
q doubleprime cond] C[Surface Vaporization
mdot doubleprime kg per m2s] D[Buoyant Air Entrainment
from Sides] E[Diffusion Flame Zone
Stoichiometric Mixing] F[Heat Release Rate Qdot
MW Range] G[Radiative Feedback q doubleprime rad] H[Convective Feedback q doubleprime conv] I[Radiative Loss to Surroundings
X sub r times Qdot] J[Convective Plume Rise
Flame Height H] K[Surrounding Equipment Targets
Vessels, Piping, Personnel] L[Damage Criteria Thresholds
6.3 and 12.5 kW per m2] A --> B --> C C --> E D --> E E --> F F --> G --> C F --> H --> C F --> I --> K F --> J K --> L A -.initial fuel.-> C ``` ## 4.2 Pool Fire Classification Tree — Decision Logic This flowchart helps a student *classify* a real observed fire, which is a frequent KTU Part-A (3-mark) question. ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Observed Fire Event] S --> Q1{Is fuel a
horizontal liquid layer?} Q1 -->|No| JF[Jet Fire] Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Pressure-driven
release at high velocity?} Q2 -->|Yes| JF2[High Momentum Spray Fire] Q2 -->|No| Q3{Pool diameter
greater than 0.5 m?} Q3 -->|No| SP[Small Pool Fire
m doubleprime proportional to D] Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Wind speed
greater than 1 m per s?} Q4 -->|No| LP[Large Pool Fire
Vertical Buoyant Flame] Q4 -->|Yes| WP[Tilted Pool Fire
Flame Inclined at theta] SP --> PF[Pool Fire Family] LP --> PF WP --> PF ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Engineering Response Pipeline When a pool fire occurs in a process plant, an integrated response chain is activated. The diagram below represents the **standard safety-engineering architecture** that KTU questions on "fire protection system" refer to. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Detection D1[Hydrocarbon Detectors LEL] D2[Flame Detectors IR UV] D3[Heat Detectors Fixed Temperature] D4[CCTV and Operator Visual] end subgraph Assessment A1[Source Modelling API 521] A2[Radiative Flux at Targets] A3[Safe Separation Distance Calc] end subgraph Suppression S1[Fixed Foam Pourer System] S2[Water Spray Deluge] S3[Dry Chemical for Small Pools] S4[Fire Monitors Manual] end subgraph Mitigation M1[ESD Emergency Shutdown] M2[Block Valves Isolation] M3[Vessel Cooling Water Curtain] M4[Firewater Pump Start] end D1 --> A1 D2 --> A1 D3 --> A1 D4 --> A1 A1 --> A2 --> A3 A3 --> S1 A3 --> S2 A3 --> S3 A3 --> S4 A3 --> M1 A3 --> M2 A3 --> M3 A3 --> M4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading guidance:** The **Detection → Assessment → Suppression → Mitigation** flow mirrors the **Swiss-Cheese model** of accident causation (Reason, 1990) taught in KTU Module 1: each layer is a defence-in-depth barrier. Pool-fire safety is therefore not just an extinguishant problem — it is an *integrated control architecture* problem. # 🔥 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### ❓ Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **"Classify fires. Why is water unsuitable for a Class B fire?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Fires are classified into five categories per **NFPA 10 / IS 2190**: 1. **Class A** — solid combustibles (wood, paper, cloth), 2. **Class B** — flammable liquids and gases (petrol, LPG), 3. **Class C** — energized electrical equipment, 4. **Class D** — combustible metals, 5. **Class K / F** — cooking oils and fats. Water is **unsuitable for Class B fires** because (i) Class B fuels are *less dense* than water (e.g., petrol density ≈ 0.74 g/cm³ vs. water 1.0 g/cm³), so water sinks below the fuel and the fuel *floats on top continuing to burn*; (ii) sudden vaporization of water can throw burning oil outwards (slop-over / boil-over), spreading the fire; and (iii) for fuels like kerosene the heat absorbed is insufficient to maintain steam blanketing. The correct agents are **foam, CO₂, dry chemical powder**. ### ❓ Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **"Define pool fire. State TWO engineering situations where pool fires commonly occur."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **pool fire** is a diffusion flame burning above a *horizontal layer of liquid fuel* where vaporization is driven by heat feedback from the flame and air is entrained by natural buoyancy. **(1 mark)** **Two engineering situations: (2 marks)** 1. **Bund/dyke fire** around a petroleum storage tank following a leak — fuel spills, accumulates inside the dyke, and ignites. 2. **Trench fire** in oil-pipeline corridors when spilled fuel pools in a low-lying drainage trench. *(Acceptable alternates: road-tanker spill fires, transformer-oil fires, refinery wastewater-sump fires.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme mandates *internal choice* within each module. **Answer either A or B in full.** --- ### 📝 QUESTION A — **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Modified]** **(a)** Classify fires in detail. Discuss the principles of extinguishment for each class with reference to the fire tetrahedron. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A circular pool fire of **kerosene** (diameter = **6 m**, $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}_{\infty} = 0.039 \;\text{kg/m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}$, $k\beta = 1.1 \;\text{m}^{-1}$, $\Delta H_c = 43 \;\text{MJ/kg}$, $\chi = 0.9$) is observed. Compute (i) burning rate per unit area, (ii) total heat release rate, (iii) flame height using the Thomas correlation with $\rho_a = 1.2 \;\text{kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)** ### 📝 QUESTION B — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the phenomenon of a **pool fire**. Describe the **Babrauskas mass burning rate correlation** and explain its physical significance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A pool fire of **crude oil** ($\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}_{\infty} = 0.033 \;\text{kg/m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}$, $X_r = 0.30$, $\Delta H_c = 42.5 \;\text{MJ/kg}$, $\chi = 0.95$) has pool diameter **10 m**. A target vessel is located **20 m from the edge of the pool**. Using the point-source model, calculate the radiative flux incident on the vessel. State whether this flux level is acceptable per API 521 thresholds. **(7 marks)** --- ## 5.3 Model Solutions — Part B ### ✅ Solution to Question A #### Part A(a) — Fire Classification (7 marks) The **fire tetrahedron** identifies four requirements for sustained combustion: 1. **Fuel** (any combustible material), 2. **Oxidizer** (typically atmospheric oxygen ≥ 16%), 3. **Heat / Activation Energy** (to sustain the reaction), 4. **Uninhibited chain reaction** (free radicals sustain flame propagation). Extinguishment works by removing *one* of these four. A complete answer should map each class to the tetrahedron edge it removes: | Class | Fuel Example | Extinguishing Agent | Tetrahedron Edge Removed | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | **A** | Wood, paper | Water (cooling) | **Heat** | | **B** | Petrol, LPG | Foam / CO₂ / DCP | **Oxygen** (smothering) | | **C** | Live electrical | CO₂ / DCP (non-conductive) | **Oxygen + Heat** | | **D** | Mg, Na | Special dry powder | **Oxygen + Chain reaction** | | **K/F** | Cooking oil | Wet chemical | **Chain reaction (saponification)** | *(Mark allocation: classification table 3 marks, extinguishment mechanism 2 marks, fire tetrahedron explanation 2 marks.)* #### Part A(b) — Numerical (7 marks) **(i) Mass burning rate per unit area:** $$\dot{m}^{\prime\prime} = \dot{m}^{\prime\prime}_{\infty}\left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right) = 0.039\left(1 - e^{-1.1 \times 6}\right)$$ $$e^{-6.6} = 0.00136$$ $$\dot{m}^{\prime\prime} = 0.039 \times (1 - 0.00136) = 0.0389 \;\text{kg/m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}$$ **(ii) Heat release rate:** $$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = 28.27 \;\text{m}^2$$ $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}^{\prime\prime} \cdot A \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi = 0.0389 \times 28.27 \times 43 \times 10^6 \times 0.9$$ $$\dot{Q} = 4.25 \times 10^7 \;\text{W} = \boxed{42.5 \;\text{MW}}$$ **(iii) Flame height (Thomas correlation):** $$\rho_a\sqrt{gD} = 1.2 \times \sqrt{9.81 \times 6} = 9.21 \;\text{kg/m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}$$ $$\frac{\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}}{\rho_a\sqrt{gD}} = \frac{0.0389}{9.21} = 4.22 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$(4.22 \times 10^{-3})^{0.61} = 0.0302$$ $$\frac{H}{D} = 42 \times 0.0302 = 1.27$$ $$H = 1.27 \times 6 = \boxed{7.6 \;\text{m}}$$ --- ### ✅ Solution to Question B #### Part B(a) — Pool Fire Schematic + Babrauskas Correlation (7 marks) **Schematic (3 marks):** A clearly labelled diagram showing: - Liquid pool at base with depth $h$ and diameter $D$, - Buoyancy-driven air entrainment arrows from sides, - Diffusion flame column of height $H$, - Heat feedback arrows ($q^{\prime\prime}_{rad}$, $q^{\prime\prime}_{conv}$) returning to pool surface, - Vaporization arrows showing fuel vapour rising into flame. **Babrauskas correlation discussion (4 marks):** $$\dot{m}^{\prime\prime} = \dot{m}^{\prime\prime}_{\infty} \left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right)$$ **Physical significance (write all three):** 1. **Captures diameter effect:** burning rate is small for small pools (heat losses dominate) and asymptotes to a constant $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}_{\infty}$ for large pools. 2. **Unifies two regimes** — small-pool conduction-dominated regime AND large-pool radiation-dominated regime — into one continuous expression. 3. **Enables engineering scaling:** designers can estimate $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}$ for any pool size from tabulated $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}_{\infty}$ and $\beta$ values, eliminating expensive large-scale fire tests. #### Part B(b) — Radiative Flux Calculation (7 marks) Pool area: $$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(10)^2 = 78.54 \;\text{m}^2$$ Burning rate (assuming $D = 10$ m is in large-pool limit, so $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime} \approx \dot{m}^{\prime\prime}_{\infty} = 0.033$): $$\dot{Q} = 0.033 \times 78.54 \times 42.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.95 = 1.05 \times 10^8 \;\text{W} = 105 \;\text{MW}$$ Radiative power: $$\dot{Q}_{rad} = X_r \cdot \dot{Q} = 0.30 \times 105 = 31.5 \;\text{MW}$$ Distance from pool centre to vessel: $$x = 20 + \frac{D}{2} = 20 + 5 = 25 \;\text{m}$$ Radiative flux (point-source model): $$q^{\prime\prime}_{rad} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4\pi x^2} = \frac{31.5 \times 10^6}{4\pi (25)^2} = \frac{31.5 \times 10^6}{7854}$$ $$\boxed{q^{\prime\prime}_{rad} \approx 4.01 \;\text{kW/m}^2}$$ **API 521 acceptance verdict:** The threshold table from API 521 is: | $q^{\prime\prime}$ (kW/m²) | Consequence | |:---:|:---| | < 4.0 | No damage to equipment (safe) | | 4.0 – 12.5 | Damage possible, **emergency action required** | | > 12.5 | Major damage / secondary fires within seconds | **Result: 4.01 kW/m² is *just* at the boundary**. The vessel is in the "damage possible" zone — operators must activate water spray cooling immediately and consider emergency isolation. --- ## 5.4 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Marks Are Lost > [!WARNING] > **TOP MARKS-LOSING MISTAKES ON POOL-FIRE QUESTIONS (DEC 2023 & MAY 2024 BATCH):** > > 1. **Confusing pool fire with jet fire** in classification diagrams. (*Loss: up to 3 marks*) > 2. **Forgetting the combustion efficiency factor $\chi$** in the HRR equation. Many students write $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}^{\prime\prime} \cdot A \cdot \Delta H_c$ without $\chi$. (*Loss: 1–2 marks per occurrence*) > 3. **Using wrong distance in point-source formula.** The 20 m given is from the *pool edge*, not from the centre — students must add $D/2$. (*Loss: 1 mark, frequent*) > 4. **Mixing units** — using $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}$ in mm/min (β coefficient convention) instead of kg/m²·s. Always convert. (*Loss: 2 marks*) > 5. **Omitting the limiting-case justification** of Babrauskas' correlation ($D \to 0$ and $D \to \infty$ limits). KTU examiners specifically test the "physical significance" of equations. (*Loss: 2–3 marks*) > 6. **Stating damage criteria thresholds** without a citation to API 521 / NFPA — examiners deduct for "unsupported statements." (*Loss: 1 mark*) --- ## 5.5 📌 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - ⭐ **Fire Classes (A/B/C/D/K)** — memorize fuel types and correct extinguishing agents; never use water on B/C. - ⭐ **Pool Fire** = diffusion flame over a **horizontal, quiescent** liquid layer; vaporization sustained by flame heat feedback. - ⭐ **Diagnostic trio** of pool fire: diffusion flame + horizontal liquid layer + buoyancy-driven air entrainment (NOT momentum-driven like a jet fire). - ⭐ **Babrauskas correlation** $\dot{m}^{\prime\prime} = \dot{m}^{\prime\prime}_{\infty}(1 - e^{-k\beta D})$ — must derive, must justify both limits. - ⭐ **HRR equation:** $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}^{\prime\prime} \cdot A \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi$ — always include **combustion efficiency $\chi$**. - ⭐ **Flame height (Thomas):** $\dfrac{H}{D} = 42 \left(\dfrac{\dot{m}^{\prime\prime}}{\rho_a\sqrt{gD}}\right)^{0.61}$. - ⭐ **Radiative flux (point source):** $q^{\prime\prime}_{rad}(x) = \dfrac{X_r \dot{Q}}{4\pi x^2}$; remember $x$ is from the **flame center**, not pool edge. - ⭐ **Damage thresholds (API 521):** 4.0 / 6.3 / 12.5 kW/m² → action thresholds. - ⭐ **Foam pour rate (NFPA 11):** ≈ **4.1 L/min·m²** for hydrocarbon pool fires. - ⭐ **Defence-in-depth layers** — Detection → Assessment → Suppression → Mitigation — map to **Swiss-cheese model** for full-credit answers.

topics.title: Jet Fire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Jet Fire

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Jet Fire:** A *turbulent diffusion flame* resulting from the continuous combustion of a flammable material (gas, liquefied gas, superheated liquid, or two-phase mixture) released from a pressurized containment with significant *momentum* through an orifice, rupture, flange leak, or equipment failure. The released jet mixes with surrounding air and burns as a directional, elongated diffusion flame aligned with the release axis.

A jet fire is governed primarily by the **momentum flux** of the release rather than buoyancy. It is one of the **three major fire hazards** in process plants (alongside pool fires and flash fires) and is frequently cited in API 521, API 581, and the **Yellow Book (TNO)** as a dominant contributor to escalation in LNG, hydrocarbon, and refinery incidents.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Signature:** Jet fires are *highly directional* and *anchored to the release point*. They do not spread radially like a pool fire; instead, they project downstream with a flame length potentially 50–200 times the release diameter.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy

Think of a **high-pressure LPG cylinder leaking at the valve** and igniting immediately. The escaping pressurized gas burns like an *industrial cutting torch or blowtorch*—producing intense, concentrated heat along the direction of release. This is fundamentally different from a **pool fire**, where a spilled liquid burns upwards in all directions from a pool.

| Feature | Jet Fire (Blowtorch) | Pool Fire (Campfire) |
|---|---|---|
| Driver | Momentum | Buoyancy |
| Shape | Elongated, jet-like | Radial, cylindrical |
| Directional | Yes (along release axis) | No (upward) |
| Dominant damage | Downstream equipment | Surrounding equipment |

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Surface Emissive Power (SEP) for jet fires:** **35 – 50 kW/m²** (sooty, luminous, highly radiative; significantly higher than pool fires at 20–30 kW/m²)
- **Radiative Fraction (η):** **0.15 – 0.25** for turbulent jet flames (versus ~0.05–0.10 for pool fires)
- **Flame Temperature:** **1500 – 1800 K** (adiabatic ~2300 K, reduced by radiative losses)
- **Critical Damage Thresholds:**
  - **37.5 kW/m²** — severe equipment damage within minutes (jet fire threshold)
  - **12.5 kW/m²** — structural steel damage, cable insulation failure
  - **4.0 kW/m²** — personnel pain threshold (1st-degree burns in < 20 s)
  - **1.6 kW/m²** — long-duration personnel exposure limit

> [!NOTE]
> The **37.5 kW/m²** threshold is the classical API 521 escalation criterion — any equipment receiving this heat flux from a jet fire is expected to fail and may trigger cascading failures (domino effect).

### 1.4 When Does a Jet Fire Occur?

A jet fire requires **three simultaneous conditions**:
1. **Pressurized release** of a flammable substance (gas, vapor, or two-phase)
2. **Immediate ignition** at the release point (delayed ignition produces a *vapor cloud explosion* or *flash fire*, not a jet fire)
3. **Sustained release** — the duration equals the time to isolate the source (typically minutes to hours)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Jet Fires

Jet fires are classified by the **release regime**, which determines the flame shape and length correlation:

#### (A) Momentum-Dominated Subsonic Jet Fires (Ma < 1)
- Reynolds Number **Re > 2000** (turbulent)
- Flame length scales with **momentum flux** (ρ·v²)
- Typical for subsonic gas leaks from process piping
- **Correlation:** *Kalghatgi (1983)* — see Formula Sheet

#### (B) Momentum-Dominated Sonic / Choked Jet Fires (Ma = 1)
- Release from pressurized vessel where pressure ratio P/P_atm > (γ+1)/2)^(γ/(γ−1))
- For natural gas (γ ≈ 1.3): critical P/P_atm ≈ 1.8
- Flame length is much longer than subsonic case
- **Correlation:** *Kalghatgi (1983) — sonic regime* OR *API 521*

#### (C) Buoyancy-Dominated Jet Flames (low momentum, low Re)
- Releases at near-atmospheric pressure
- Flame rises vertically; length governed by heat release rate
- Often treated with **Heskestad** or **Hasemi** correlations

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Jet Fire Analysis (Engineering Workflow)

1. **Identify release scenario** — hole size, pressure, temperature, fluid phase
2. **Determine discharge regime** — subsonic vs. sonic (compare P/P_atm to critical ratio)
3. **Compute mass flow rate** — using Bernoulli (subsonic) or choked flow equation
4. **Compute flame length** — using Kalghatgi or API 521
5. **Compute heat release rate** — Q_dot = m_dot × ΔH_c
6. **Estimate radiative fraction** — η ≈ 0.15–0.25
7. **Apply radiation model** — point source, solid flame, or multi-point
8. **Compute incident heat flux** at target location
9. **Compare to damage thresholds** to determine safe separation distances

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **core cheat sheet** for jet fire problems in KTU examinations. Memorize the boundary conditions and units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Flame length — Kalghatgi (subsonic)** | $L = 17.5 \cdot d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho v^{2}}{\rho_a C_a^{2}}}$ | Re > 2000, Ma < 1; d in m |
| 2 | **Flame length — Kalghatgi (sonic)** | $L = 1190 \cdot d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\rho_a}}$ | Choked flow; natural gas |
| 3 | **Flame length — API 521 (sonic NG)** | $L = 187 \cdot d \cdot \left(\dfrac{P}{P_a}\right)^{0.45}$ | Stagnation pressure basis; NG |
| 4 | **Sonic mass flow rate** | $\dot{m} = C_d A P \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R T} \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}$ | P/P_a > critical ratio |
| 5 | **Heat release rate** | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c$ | ΔH_c in J/kg |
| 6 | **Radiative power** | $\dot{Q}_{rad} = \eta \cdot \dot{Q}$ | η = 0.15–0.25 for jet fires |
| 7 | **Point source heat flux** | $q^{\prime\prime} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4\pi r^{2}}$ | Far-field approximation |
| 8 | **Surface emissive power** | $SEP = \eta \cdot \dot{Q} / A_{flame}$ | A_flame = π d L |
| 9 | **Safe distance (point source)** | $r_{safe} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4\pi q^{\prime\prime}_{threshold}}}$ | For threshold heat flux q″ |
| 10 | **Critical pressure ratio** | $r_{crit} = \left(\dfrac{\gamma+1}{2}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$ | ≈ 1.8 for natural gas (γ=1.3) |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

Jet fire analysis is critical in:
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** for offshore platforms and LNG terminals
- **Fire protection engineering** — design of radiation shields, water deluge systems, jet-fire-resistant cable coatings
- **Layout planning** — minimum spacing between vessels per API 521 / IGEM standards
- **Emergency response** — determining isolation distances and rescue corridors

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in practice:** The 2005 **Buncefield** and 1988 **Piper Alpha** disasters were dominated by jet fire radiation damage. Modern QRA mandates jet fire modeling as a compulsory escalation vector.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Sonic (Choked) Mass Flow Rate Through an Orifice

Consider a pressurized gas discharging through an orifice of area A into atmosphere. At the throat, when the velocity equals the local speed of sound, the flow is **choked** and the mass flow rate reaches a maximum independent of downstream pressure.

Starting from the **steady-state energy equation** for an isentropic expansion from stagnation state (P₀, T₀) to sonic state (P*, T*):

$$
h_0 = h^{*} + \frac{v^{*2}}{2}
$$

Since enthalpy h = c_p T, and using the isentropic relation T*/T₀ = 2/(γ+1):

$$
v^{*} = \sqrt{2 c_p T_0 \left(1 - \frac{T^{*}}{T_0}\right)}
$$

Substituting the isentropic temperature ratio:

$$
v^{*} = \sqrt{2 c_p T_0 \left(1 - \frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \gamma R T_0}{\gamma+1}}
$$

The density at the throat from the ideal gas law and isentropic pressure relation:

$$
\rho^{*} = \frac{P^{*}}{R T^{*}}, \qquad P^{*} = P_0 \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}
$$

Combining v* and ρ*:

$$
\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \rho^{*} \cdot v^{*}
$$

After algebraic simplification:

$$
\dot{m} = C_d \, A \, P_0 \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R T_0} \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}
$$

**This is the canonical sonic flow equation.** For a discharge coefficient C_d ≈ 1.0 and natural gas (γ = 1.3, R = 518 J/kg·K), the bracketed term evaluates to ≈ 0.0226, giving a practical working form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical meaning:** Once P₀/P_atm exceeds the critical ratio (~1.8 for NG), the mass flow rate saturates. Increasing the source pressure further does **not** increase m_dot — only the upstream pressure driving the choked jet increases, which affects flame length via the API 521 formula.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Point Source Radiation Model for Safe Distance

**Assumption:** Treat the jet flame as a *point source* emitting total radiative power $\dot{Q}_{rad}$. This is valid when the target distance r >> flame length L (far-field approximation, r > 5L).

By conservation of energy, the radiative power is distributed uniformly over a sphere of radius r:

$$
\dot{Q}_{rad} = q^{\prime\prime} \cdot 4\pi r^{2}
$$

Solving for incident heat flux q″ at distance r:

$$
q^{\prime\prime}(r) = \frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4\pi r^{2}}
$$

For personnel protection, q″ must not exceed the **threshold heat flux** $q^{\prime\prime}_{th}$ (e.g., 4 kW/m² for personnel, 37.5 kW/m² for equipment):

$$
q^{\prime\prime}_{th} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4\pi r_{safe}^{2}}
$$

Solving for safe distance:

$$
r_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4\pi \, q^{\prime\prime}_{th}}}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

A natural gas leak produces a jet fire with total heat release $\dot{Q} = 50{,}000$ kW and radiative fraction η = 0.20.

Step 1 — Compute radiative power:

$$
\dot{Q}_{rad} = \eta \cdot \dot{Q} = 0.20 \times 50{,}000 = 10{,}000 \text{ kW}
$$

Step 2 — Apply safe-distance formula for personnel threshold q″ = 4 kW/m²:

$$
r_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{10{,}000}{4\pi \times 4}} = \sqrt{\frac{10{,}000}{50.27}} = \sqrt{198.94}
$$

$$
r_{safe} \approx 14.1 \text{ m}
$$

Step 3 — Equipment threshold q″ = 37.5 kW/m²:

$$
r_{safe,equip} = \sqrt{\frac{10{,}000}{4\pi \times 37.5}} = \sqrt{\frac{10{,}000}{471.24}} = \sqrt{21.22}
$$

$$
r_{safe,equip} \approx 4.6 \text{ m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Forgetting the **radiative fraction η**. Without multiplying by η (≈ 0.15–0.25), you overestimate the incident heat flux by **4–6×**, leading to unrealistically large safe distances. Always quote η explicitly in your solution.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Linking Kalghatgi Flame Length to Heat Release

Starting from Kalghatgi's subsonic correlation:

$$
L = 17.5 \, d \sqrt{\frac{\rho v^{2}}{\rho_a C_a^{2}}}
$$

The momentum flux at the nozzle is $J = \rho v^{2} A = \rho v^{2} \cdot \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$. The mass flow rate is $\dot{m} = \rho v A$.

Substituting $v = \dot{m}/(\rho A)$ into the flame length expression:

$$
L = 17.5 \, d \sqrt{\frac{\dot{m} v}{\rho_a C_a^{2} A}}
$$

Since $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c$, the flame length scales approximately as the square root of the heat release rate in the **momentum-dominated subsonic regime** — consistent with dimensional analysis for buoyancy-influenced turbulent jet flames.

In the **choked regime**, L scales with $(P/P_a)^{0.45}$ (API 521) or $(ρ/ρ_a)^{0.5}$ (Kalghatgi), reflecting the dominance of source pressure over ambient density.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dimensional insight:** In both regimes, increasing the release diameter d produces a *linear* increase in flame length. Halving the hole size halves the flame length but reduces the mass flow (and hence heat release) by a factor of 4 — explaining why small leaks produce surprisingly short, intense flames.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Choosing the correct jet fire correlation (exam pitfall avoidance):**

| Scenario | Use Correlation | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Subsonic gas release, Re > 2000 | **Kalghatgi subsonic** | Momentum flux dominated |
| Sonic (choked) gas release from vessel | **Kalghatgi sonic** or **API 521** | Pressure-driven choked jet |
| LNG / natural gas pipe at high P | **API 521** | Industry-standard, empirical |
| Far-field heat flux at r >> L | **Point source model** | Geometric simplicity |
| Near-field, r < 2L | **Solid flame model** (Chamberlain) | View-factor based |
| Buoyancy-dominated (low pressure release) | **Heskestad / Hasemi** | Buoyancy scaling |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Jet Fire Anatomy (Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a jet fire system, mapping the energy, mass, and momentum flows from release to thermal impact on equipment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VesselPressureSource] -->|Sonic or subsonic discharge| B[OrificeReleasePoint]
    B -->|High momentum jet| C[JetPlumeCore]
    C -->|Mixing with air| D[DiffusionFlameEnvelope]
    D -->|Combustion energy Q| E[ConvectiveColumn]
    D -->|Radiative fraction eta| F[RadiativeEmission]
    F -->|Point source q over 4 pi r squared| G[TargetEquipment]
    F -->|Personnel exposure| H[PersonnelLocation]
    E -->|Buoyancy lift| I[PlumeRise]
    G -->|Heat flux above 37.5 kW per sqm| J[EquipmentFailure]
    J -->|Domino cascade| K[ProcessEscalation]
```

**Reading guide for the diagram:**
- **A → B → C → D** represents the *fluid-mechanical chain* (pressurized gas → orifice → momentum jet → turbulent diffusion flame).
- **D → F → G/H** represents the *radiative hazard pathway* — the primary engineering concern for safety distances.
- **G → J → K** captures the *escalation logic* — a jet fire can destroy adjacent equipment and trigger secondary failures (the **domino effect**).

### 4.2 Jet Fire Analysis Workflow (Decision Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step1IdentifyRelease] --> S2[Step2DetermineRegime]
    S2 --> S3{IsSonicChoked}
    S3 -->|Yes| S4[UseSonicCorrelation]
    S3 -->|No| S5[UseSubsonicCorrelation]
    S4 --> S6[ComputeFlameLengthL]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[ComputeMassFlowAndHeatRelease]
    S7 --> S8[EstimateRadiativeFractionEta]
    S8 --> S9[ApplyRadiationModel]
    S9 --> S10[ComputeIncidentHeatFluxAtTarget]
    S10 --> S11[CompareToThresholdAndComputeSafeDistance]
```

### 4.3 Flame Shape Comparison: Jet vs. Pool vs. Flash

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph JetFireRegime
        JF1[Momentum dominated] --> JF2[Elongated directional flame]
        JF1 --> JF3[Heat flux 35 to 50 kW per sqm]
    end
    subgraph PoolFireRegime
        PF1[Buoyancy dominated] --> PF2[Radial cylindrical flame]
        PF1 --> PF3[Heat flux 20 to 30 kW per sqm]
    end
    subgraph FlashFireRegime
        FF1[Cloud dominated] --> FF2[Transient hemispherical flame]
        FF1 --> FF3[Heat flux 20 to 25 kW per sqm brief]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **flash fire** block is included for comparative context — it is covered in a separate KTU module but is a critical *delayed-ignition* counterpart to the jet fire (which is *immediate-ignition*).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
Define a *jet fire*. List **four** distinguishing characteristics that differentiate it from a *pool fire*.

**Model Answer:**

> A *jet fire* is a turbulent diffusion flame produced by the continuous combustion of a flammable material (gas, vapor, or two-phase) released with significant momentum from a pressurized containment through an orifice, rupture, or leak.
>
> **Four distinguishing characteristics:**
> 1. **Momentum-dominated** — flame length is governed by ρv² (momentum flux), not by buoyancy.
> 2. **Highly directional** — flame projects along the release axis (unlike the radially spreading pool fire).
> 3. **High surface emissive power** — SEP of 35–50 kW/m² versus 20–30 kW/m² for pool fires, due to sooty, luminous combustion.
> 4. **Anchored to the release point** — flame does not migrate; damage is concentrated downstream of the orifice.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for any 4 valid distinguishing points. Total 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model]**
State the **Kalghatgi correlation** for the flame length of a turbulent subsonic natural gas jet fire. Identify the physical meaning of each variable.

**Model Answer:**

$$
L = 17.5 \, d \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho v^{2}}{\rho_a C_a^{2}}}
$$

**Variable interpretation:**
- **L** — visible flame length (m)
- **d** — release orifice diameter (m)
- **ρ** — density of the released gas at exit (kg/m³)
- **v** — exit velocity (m/s)
- **ρ_a** — ambient air density (kg/m³)
- **C_a** — speed of sound in ambient air (m/s)

**Applicability:** Subsonic turbulent jet (Ma < 1, Re > 2000), buoyancy effects negligible. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **API 521 correlation** for estimating the flame length of a sonic natural gas jet fire. Discuss its applicability, limitations, and the physical significance of the pressure-ratio exponent. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A natural gas pipeline ruptures, releasing gas through an equivalent orifice of **diameter 80 mm** at a stagnation pressure of **15 bar (gauge)**. Ambient pressure is 1.013 bar. Using the **API 521 correlation**, calculate:
  (i) the visible flame length,
  (ii) the surface emissive power (SEP), assuming radiative fraction η = 0.18 and heat of combustion ΔH_c = 50 MJ/kg,
  (iii) the **safe distance** for equipment (use point source model, q″ threshold = 37.5 kW/m²).

Mass flow rate may be computed using the sonic flow equation with C_d = 0.85, γ = 1.3, T_0 = 300 K, R = 518 J/kg·K. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

The **API 521 sonic jet flame length correlation** is:

$$
L = 187 \, d \left(\dfrac{P}{P_a}\right)^{0.45}
$$

where:
- **L** = visible flame length (m)
- **d** = release diameter (m)
- **P** = stagnation pressure at the release (absolute)
- **P_a** = ambient pressure

**Physical significance of exponent 0.45:** This empirical exponent reflects the *sub-linear scaling* of flame length with stagnation pressure. In the choked regime, increasing P raises the *exit density* and *exit velocity* (sub-linearly) because of the isentropic expansion dynamics. The flame length therefore grows more slowly than linearly with pressure — a doubling of P increases L by only ~36% (2^0.45).

**Applicability:** Designed for **natural gas** sonic jet fires from pressurized pipelines and process equipment.

**Limitations:**
1. Valid only for **natural gas** (methane-dominated); not transferable to LPG, hydrogen, or multi-component mixtures without modification.
2. Requires **sonic (choked)** discharge — for subsonic releases it overestimates L.
3. Empirically fitted; should not be extrapolated beyond **P/P_a ≈ 100**.
4. Assumes horizontal jet in still air; cross-winds significantly shorten the flame.

*(Full marks require: formula statement (2), explanation of exponent (2), applicability (1), limitations (2).)*

---

**(b) [7 marks] — Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- d = 0.080 m, P = 15 + 1.013 = **16.013 bar (abs)**, P_a = 1.013 bar
- η = 0.18, ΔH_c = 50 × 10⁶ J/kg, C_d = 0.85, γ = 1.3, T_0 = 300 K
- R = 518 J/kg·K, q″_threshold = 37.5 kW/m²

**(i) Flame length (API 521):**

$$
L = 187 \times 0.080 \times \left(\frac{16.013}{1.013}\right)^{0.45}
$$

Computing the pressure ratio:

$$
\frac{P}{P_a} = \frac{16.013}{1.013} = 15.81
$$

Raising to the power 0.45:

$$
15.81^{0.45} = e^{0.45 \ln 15.81} = e^{0.45 \times 2.760} = e^{1.242} = 3.463
$$

Therefore:

$$
L = 187 \times 0.080 \times 3.463 = 14.96 \times 3.463 = 51.81 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{L \approx 51.8 \text{ m}}
$$

**(ii) Surface Emissive Power (SEP):**

First, compute mass flow rate via the sonic flow equation:

$$
\dot{m} = C_d \, A \, P_0 \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R T_0} \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}
$$

Orifice area:

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.080)^{2}}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

Stagnation pressure (absolute):

$$
P_0 = 16.013 \times 10^{5} = 1.6013 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}
$$

Compute the bracketed term with γ = 1.3:

$$
\frac{\gamma}{R T_0} = \frac{1.3}{518 \times 300} = \frac{1.3}{155400} = 8.366 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}} = \left(\frac{2}{2.3}\right)^{\!\frac{2.3}{0.3}} = (0.8696)^{7.667}
$$

Evaluating:

$$
0.8696^{7.667} = e^{7.667 \ln 0.8696} = e^{7.667 \times (-0.1398)} = e^{-1.0718} = 0.3425
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sqrt{8.366 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.3425} = \sqrt{2.866 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.693 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Finally:

$$
\dot{m} = 0.85 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.6013 \times 10^{6} \times 1.693 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.85 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.6013 \times 10^{6} \times 1.693 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\dot{m} \approx 11.59 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Total heat release:

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c = 11.59 \times 50 \times 10^{6} = 5.795 \times 10^{8} \text{ W} = 579{,}500 \text{ kW}
$$

Radiative power:

$$
\dot{Q}_{rad} = \eta \cdot \dot{Q} = 0.18 \times 579{,}500 = 104{,}310 \text{ kW}
$$

Flame surface area (cylindrical approximation):

$$
A_{flame} = \pi d L = \pi \times 0.080 \times 51.81 = 13.02 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

Surface Emissive Power:

$$
SEP = \frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{A_{flame}} = \frac{104{,}310}{13.02} = 8{,}012 \text{ kW/m}^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{SEP \approx 8{,}010 \text{ kW/m}^{2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The SEP value here reflects *per unit cylindrical flame area* — this is the **localized** emissive power. A more accurate approach uses a *view factor* between flame and target, which the solid flame model addresses.

**(iii) Safe distance for equipment (point source model):**

$$
r_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4\pi \, q^{\prime\prime}_{th}}} = \sqrt{\frac{104{,}310}{4\pi \times 37.5}}
$$

$$
r_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{104{,}310}{471.24}} = \sqrt{221.4} = 14.88 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{r_{safe} \approx 14.9 \text{ m}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistakes in this question:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert gauge to absolute pressure** for P in the API 521 formula — using 15 bar instead of 16.013 bar underestimates L by ~3%.
> 2. **Mixing units** (e.g., bar vs. Pa) in the sonic flow equation — always use **SI (Pa, kg, J, m)** in the mass flow calculation.
> 3. **Forgetting the radiative fraction** η when computing q″ — leads to a 5× overestimation of safe distance.
> 4. **Applying API 521 to a subsonic release** — the correlation is for *choked flow only*. Verify P/P_a > 1.8 first.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labeled schematic, explain the **point source radiation model** for estimating thermal radiation from a jet fire. State **two** situations where this model is invalid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal jet fire releases propane at a mass rate of **5 kg/s**. The heat of combustion is **46 MJ/kg** and the radiative fraction is **η = 0.20**. Estimate:
  (i) the **total radiative power** emitted,
  (ii) the **incident heat flux** at a target located **30 m** from the flame,
  (iii) the **safe distance** corresponding to a heat flux threshold of **12.5 kW/m²** (structural steel damage threshold). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

The **point source model** treats the entire jet flame as a *single equivalent point* located at the flame center (or at the release point, in simple approximations), emitting total radiative power $\dot{Q}_{rad}$ uniformly in all directions.

**Schematic:**

```
                  Target Equipment
                          ●  ← receives q″
                         /|
                        / |
                 r    /  |  
                      /   |
                     /    |
                    /     |
              ___●_______  ← Release point (or flame centroid)
            Flame (treated as point)
              ↑ 
        [Pressurized vessel]
```

**Governing equation:**

$$
q^{\prime\prime}(r) = \frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4\pi r^{2}}
$$

The model assumes **spherical radiative spreading** over the surface of a sphere of radius r.

**Two situations where the point source model is invalid:**
1. **Near-field regime** (r < 5L, where L is the flame length): the flame cannot be approximated as a point — the target sees only a portion of the flame. The **solid flame model** (Chamberlain) with view factors must be used instead.
2. **Wind-affected flames:** cross-winds tilt the flame, breaking the spherical symmetry. The point source model overestimates flux at downwind locations and underestimates at upwind locations. A **wind-modified tilted flame model** (e.g., TNO/Yellow Book) is needed.

*(2 marks for formula, 2 marks for schematic description, 3 marks for stating and explaining the two limitations.)*

---

**(b) [7 marks] — Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}$ = 5 kg/s, ΔH_c = 46 × 10⁶ J/kg, η = 0.20
- r = 30 m, q″_th = 12.5 kW/m²

**(i) Total radiative power:**

Total heat release:

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c = 5 \times 46 \times 10^{6} = 230 \times 10^{6} \text{ W} = 230{,}000 \text{ kW}
$$

Radiative power:

$$
\dot{Q}_{rad} = \eta \cdot \dot{Q} = 0.20 \times 230{,}000 = 46{,}000 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q}_{rad} = 46{,}000 \text{ kW}}
$$

**(ii) Incident heat flux at r = 30 m:**

$$
q^{\prime\prime} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4\pi r^{2}} = \frac{46{,}000}{4\pi (30)^{2}} = \frac{46{,}000}{11{,}309.7}
$$

$$
\boxed{q^{\prime\prime} \approx 4.07 \text{ kW/m}^{2}}
$$

> This value is **above the personnel pain threshold (4 kW/m²)** — meaning the location at 30 m is unsafe for unprotected personnel in < 20 seconds exposure.

**(iii) Safe distance for q″_th = 12.5 kW/m²:**

$$
r_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4\pi \, q^{\prime\prime}_{th}}} = \sqrt{\frac{46{,}000}{4\pi \times 12.5}}
$$

$$
r_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{46{,}000}{157.08}} = \sqrt{292.84} = 17.11 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{r_{safe} \approx 17.1 \text{ m}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget that the point source model assumes **line-of-sight** radiation only. Smoke, fog, or steam curtains can attenuate heat flux by 20–40% but are NOT accounted for in the basic formula. Always state the model assumption in the exam answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Jet Fire**

- ✅ Jet fire = *momentum-dominated, directional, turbulent diffusion flame* from a *pressurized* release.
- ✅ Three essential conditions: **pressurized release + immediate ignition + sustained flow**.
- ✅ **SEP for jet fire = 35–50 kW/m²** (significantly higher than pool fire SEP = 20–30 kW/m²).
- ✅ **Radiative fraction η = 0.15–0.25** for jet fires (higher than pool fires).
- ✅ **Kalghatgi subsonic:** $L = 17.5 d \sqrt{\rho v^{2}/(\rho_a C_a^{2})}$ — for Ma < 1.
- ✅ **Kalghatgi sonic:** $L = 1190 d \sqrt{\rho/\rho_a}$ — for choked flow (natural gas).
- ✅ **API 521:** $L = 187 d (P/P_a)^{0.45}$ — industry-standard for natural gas sonic jets.
- ✅ **Point source heat flux:** $q^{\prime\prime} = \dot{Q}_{rad} / (4\pi r^{2})$ — valid only for r >> L.
- ✅ **Safe distance formula:** $r_{safe} = \sqrt{\dot{Q}_{rad}/(4\pi q^{\prime\prime}_{th})}$ — with proper threshold.
- ✅ **Critical pressure ratio** for choked flow ≈ 1.8 for natural gas (γ ≈ 1.3).
- ✅ **Damage thresholds to memorize:** 4 kW/m² (personnel), 12.5 kW/m² (steel), 37.5 kW/m² (equipment escalation).
- ✅ **Always convert gauge pressure to absolute** when using API 521.
- ✅ **Always include the radiative fraction η** when computing safe distances.
- ✅ **API 521 is for natural gas only** — do NOT apply to hydrogen, LPG, or multi-component fuels without correction.
- ✅ The **domino effect** (escalation vector) is the primary engineering concern in jet fire QRA.
- ✅ **Kalghatgi (1983)** and **API 521** are the two correlations most frequently tested in KTU exams.

> **End of Module 1 — Jet Fire Notes | PECHT743**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flash Fire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flash Fire

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Flash Fire** is a rapid, transient, and intensely propagating flame front that travels through a dispersed, ignitable mixture of a flammable vapor, gas, mist, or dust with air (or other oxidizer) without producing a damaging overpressure. The combustion wave propagates by thermal diffusion at subsonic velocities (typically **0.5 to 10 m/s**), distinguishing it fundamentally from a **deflagration** (which exhibits overpressure) and a **detonation** (which propagates at supersonic velocities > 1000 m/s).

In the context of process plant safety, a flash fire typically occurs when a volatile flammable liquid is released, forms a vapor cloud, mixes with air within its **flammable range**, and encounters an ignition source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Flash fires are classified under *Vapor Cloud Fires* and are one of the most common fire hazards in petrochemical, refining, LNG, and LPG handling facilities.

## 1.2 Key Physical Characteristics

| Characteristic | Typical Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Flame Speed | Subsonic (0.5 – 10 m/s) |
| Overpressure Effect | Negligible / None |
| Burn Duration | Seconds to a few minutes |
| Heat Flux | High (short exposure, ~ 80 – 150 kW/m²) |
| Primary Damage | Thermal radiation burns to personnel |
| Fuel State | Dispersed vapor, gas, mist, or dust |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Aerosol Spray Effect:**
> Imagine spraying an aerosol deodorant near a lit candle. A momentary sheet of flame briefly envelops the spray, accompanied by a soft "whoosh" sound, but no explosion shatters the room. That is a *flash fire*. Now imagine a gas stove burner igniting after a slight delay — the gas-air mixture that accumulated burns rapidly, but the kitchen does not blow up. The same physics governs process plant flash fires: a **diffuse fuel-air cloud** ignites and burns outward, primarily threatening personnel through **thermal radiation** rather than blast.

> [!TIP]
> **Why it matters in Process Plants:** A leak of LPG, LNG, naphtha, gasoline, or hydrogen can produce a flash fire within seconds. Although it does not generate blast overpressure, the radiant heat can cause **second and third-degree burns** to operators standing tens of meters away.

## 1.4 Flash Fire vs Other Combustion Phenomena

| Phenomenon | Velocity | Overpressure | Fuel State |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flash Fire | Subsonic (< 10 m/s) | None | Diffuse cloud |
| Pool Fire | Quasi-stationary | None | Liquid pool |
| Jet Fire | High velocity jet | None | Pressurized release |
| Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE) | Deflagration | High | Confined / congested cloud |
| Detonation | Supersonic (> 1000 m/s) | Very high | Confined cloud |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fire Triangle and Fire Tetrahedron

Every fire, including a flash fire, requires four interlinked elements — the **Fire Tetrahedron**:

1. **Fuel** — Flammable vapor, gas, mist, or dust
2. **Oxidizer** — Typically air (≈ 21% O₂)
3. **Ignition Source** — Spark, hot surface, static discharge, open flame
4. **Uninhibited Chain Reaction** — Free radicals (H•, OH•, O•) sustaining combustion

> [!NOTE]
> Removing *any one* of the four elements extinguishes the fire. This is the basis of all fire prevention in process plants.

## 2.2 The Mechanism of a Flash Fire — Step by Step

1. **Loss of Containment (LOC):** A pressurized vessel, flange, pump seal, or pipeline fails.
2. **Vaporization:** The released liquid (e.g., LPG, naphtha) flashes to vapor due to depressurization.
3. **Dispersion & Mixing:** The vapor disperses and mixes with ambient air.
4. **Flammability Check:** If the local concentration lies between the **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** and **Upper Flammability Limit (UFL)**, the cloud is ignitable.
5. **Ignition:** An ignition source (electrical spark, hot surface, static) contacts the cloud.
6. **Combustion Wave Propagation:** The flame front sweeps through the cloud at subsonic velocity, consuming the fuel-air mixture.
7. **Thermal Radiation:** The luminous flame emits intense thermal radiation during its brief burn.
8. **Extinction:** Once the unburned fuel is consumed, the fire self-extinguishes.

## 2.3 Critical Flammability Properties

### (a) Flash Point
The **minimum temperature** at which a liquid gives off sufficient vapor to form an *ignitable mixture* with air *near the liquid surface* (test method specific).

### (b) Fire Point
The **minimum temperature** at which a liquid gives off vapor at a rate sufficient to sustain combustion for at least **5 seconds** after ignition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Relationship:** Fire Point > Flash Point (typically by **5 – 10 °C**). A liquid above its flash point can produce a flash fire when released.

### (c) Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)
The minimum temperature at which a substance spontaneously ignites *without an external ignition source*. Critical for hot surface and compressor design.

### (d) Flammability Limits (LFL & UFL)
- **LFL (Lower Flammability Limit):** Minimum fuel concentration in air for combustion.
- **UFL (Upper Flammability Limit):** Maximum fuel concentration for combustion (above this, mixture is too rich).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value bars use `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Property | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Flash Point Criterion** | $T_{liq} \geq T_{flash}$ | °C | Determines ignition hazard of liquid |
| 2 | **Flammability Range** | $\Delta\phi = UFL - LFL$ | % vol | Wider range → higher hazard |
| 3 | **Stoichiometric Concentration** | $C_{st} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 4.773 \times \nu_{O_2}/\nu_{fuel}}$ | % vol | Reference point for flammability |
| 4 | **Lower Flammability Limit (Le Chatelier)** | $LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$ | % vol | Mixture LFL of multiple fuels |
| 5 | **Heat of Combustion** | $\Delta H_c$ | kJ/kg or kJ/mol | Energy release potential |
| 6 | **Mass Burning Rate (Pool)** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{0.001 \Delta H_c}{c_p (T_{bp} - T_{amb})}$ | kg/m²s | For vaporization rate |
| 7 | **Radiative Heat Flux (Point Source)** | $q = \dfrac{\dot{Q} \chi_r}{4 \pi r^2}$ | kW/m² | Thermal radiation at distance |
| 8 | **View Factor for Cylindrical Flame** | $F_{12}$ (geometric) | dimensionless | API 521 / TNO models |
| 9 | **Fraction of Heat Radiated** | $\chi_r = 0.21 e^{-0.00323 u_{10}} + 0.04$ | dimensionless | API RP 521 wind correction |
| 10 | **Threshold for 2nd Degree Burn (10 s)** | $q \approx 4.0$ | kW/m² | Personnel safety distance |
| 11 | **Threshold for 1st Degree Burn (10 s)** | $q \approx 1.0$ | kW/m² | Personnel safety distance |
| 12 | **Auto-Ignition vs Flash Point** | $T_{AIT} > T_{fire} > T_{flash}$ | °C | Always true ordering |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LNG/LPG terminals:** Determine exclusion zones and thermal radiation contours.
- **Refineries:** Classify hazardous area zones (Zone 0, 1, 2) for electrical equipment.
- **Offshore platforms:** Calculate escape, evacuation, and rescue (EER) time.
- **Storage tank farms:** Determine dike sizing, bund wall height, and firewater demand.
- **Process Safety Management (PSM):** Mandatory consequence analysis under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Lower Flammability Limit Using Adiabatic Flame Temperature Approach

The **LFL** of a fuel can be theoretically estimated by the principle that the adiabatic flame temperature ($T_{ad}$) must exceed a critical value (typically **1200 – 1300 K**) for self-sustained combustion.

### Step 1 — Write the generic combustion reaction

For a hydrocarbon fuel $C_n H_m$ burning in air:

$$C_n H_m + \left(n + \dfrac{m}{4}\right) O_2 + 3.76 \left(n + \dfrac{m}{4}\right) N_2 \longrightarrow n CO_2 + \dfrac{m}{2} H_2 O + 3.76 \left(n + \dfrac{m}{4}\right) N_2$$

### Step 2 — Express the LFL as a function of stoichiometry

At the LFL, only enough fuel is present to just barely reach the critical flame temperature. Empirically:

$$LFL \approx \dfrac{-\Delta H_c^{'}}{c_{p,g} (T_{ad} - T_{amb})} \times \dfrac{1}{M_{fuel}}$$

where $\Delta H_c^{'}$ is the lower (net) heat of combustion per mole of fuel.

### Step 3 — Substituting the critical flame temperature

Setting $T_{ad} = 1210$ K (the empirical threshold proposed by Hertzberg) and rearranging:

$$LFL = \dfrac{\Delta H_c / M_{fuel}}{11.6 \times 10^3}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example: Methane (CH₄)

Given: $\Delta H_c = 802$ kJ/mol, $M = 16$ g/mol

$$LFL = \dfrac{802{,}000}{11.6 \times 10^3 \times 16}$$

$$LFL = \dfrac{802{,}000}{185{,}600}$$

$$LFL \approx 4.32 \text{ \% vol}$$

The experimental value for methane LFL is **5.0 % vol**, demonstrating that the simple heat-balance approach gives a reasonable first-order estimate.

## 3.2 Derivation: Thermal Radiation Intensity from a Pool Flash Fire (API RP 521 Approach)

### Step 1 — Pool fire diameter

For a circular pool of diameter $D$ (m), the heat release rate is:

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot A \cdot \Delta H_c = \dot{m} \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \Delta H_c$$

### Step 2 — Fraction of heat radiated

API RP 521 gives the empirically corrected fraction of heat radiated as a function of ambient wind velocity $u$ (m/s):

$$\chi_r = 0.21 \cdot e^{-0.00323 u} + 0.04$$

### Step 3 — Radiative heat output

$$\dot{Q}_{rad} = \chi_r \cdot \dot{Q}$$

### Step 4 — Heat flux at distance $r$ (point source model)

For a target located at distance $r$ from the flame center:

$$q = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4 \pi r^2}$$

### Step 5 — Worked Example: Hexane Pool Fire

**Given:**
- Pool diameter: $D = 10$ m
- Burning rate: $\dot{m} = 0.078$ kg/m²·s
- Heat of combustion: $\Delta H_c = 45{,}100$ kJ/kg
- Wind speed: $u = 5$ m/s
- Receiver distance: $r = 50$ m

**Solution:**

Step A: Pool area

$$A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 10^2}{4} = 78.54 \text{ m}^2$$

Step B: Total heat release rate

$$\dot{Q} = 0.078 \times 78.54 \times 45{,}100 = 276{,}294 \text{ kW}$$

Step C: Radiative fraction

$$\chi_r = 0.21 \cdot e^{-0.00323 \times 5} + 0.04$$

$$e^{-0.01615} \approx 0.9840$$

$$\chi_r = 0.21 \times 0.9840 + 0.04 = 0.2466$$

Step D: Radiative heat output

$$\dot{Q}_{rad} = 0.2466 \times 276{,}294 = 68{,}134 \text{ kW}$$

Step E: Heat flux at 50 m

$$q = \dfrac{68{,}134}{4 \pi \times 50^2}$$

$$q = \dfrac{68{,}134}{31{,}416}$$

$$q \approx 2.17 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

**Interpretation:** At 50 m, the heat flux exceeds the **2nd-degree burn threshold (≈ 4 kW/m² for 10 s)** only marginally — operators can survive here for a brief exposure, but for full safety a distance of **> 70 m** is recommended for hexane pool fires.

## 3.3 Derivation: Le Chatelier's Rule for Flammability of Fuel Mixtures

For a mixture of $n$ flammable components with mole fractions $y_i$ in the gas phase, the mixture LFL is:

$$LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$$

### Numerical Example: 70% Methane + 30% Ethane (by volume)

$$LFL_{CH_4} = 5.0 \text{ \%}, \quad LFL_{C_2H_6} = 3.0 \text{ \%}$$

$$LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{70}{5.0} + \dfrac{30}{3.0}} = \dfrac{100}{14.0 + 10.0} = \dfrac{100}{24.0} = 4.17 \text{ \%}$$

This shows that mixing broadens the hazard envelope — a mixture with **LFL = 4.17%** is *more easily* ignited than pure methane (LFL = 5.0%).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Flammability Data of Common Process Plant Chemicals

| Chemical | Flash Point (°C) | AIT (°C) | LFL (% vol) | UFL (% vol) | Vapor Density (air=1) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Methane (CH₄) | -188 | 540 | 5.0 | 15.0 | 0.55 |
| Propane (C₃H₈) | -104 | 450 | 2.1 | 9.5 | 1.52 |
| n-Butane | -60 | 365 | 1.8 | 8.4 | 2.05 |
| n-Hexane | -22 | 225 | 1.1 | 7.5 | 2.97 |
| Gasoline | -43 | 280 | 1.4 | 7.6 | 3.0 – 4.0 |
| Benzene | -11 | 560 | 1.3 | 7.9 | 2.70 |
| Acetone | -20 | 465 | 2.6 | 12.8 | 2.00 |
| Hydrogen (H₂) | < -240 | 500 | 4.0 | 75.0 | 0.07 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Flash Fire Initiation and Propagation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ContainmentFailure] --> B[LiquidOrGasRelease]
    B --> C[FlashVaporization]
    C --> D[AirEntrainmentAndMixing]
    D --> E{ConcentrationInFlammableRange}
    E -- No --> F[SafeDispersion]
    E -- Yes --> G[IgnitionSourceContact]
    G --> H[FlameFrontPropagation]
    H --> I[IntenseThermalRadiation]
    I --> J[BurnInjuriesAndAssetDamage]
    H --> K[SelfExtinctionAfterFuelConsumed]
    J --> L[EmergencyResponseActivation]
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Fire Tetrahedron with Flash Fire Prevention Levers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[FuelVapor] --> FS[FireTetrahedron]
    F2[OxidizerAir] --> FS
    F3[IgnitionSource] --> FS
    F4[ChainReactionFreeRadicals] --> FS
    FS --> FF[FlashFireEvent]
    P1[InertingN2CO2Blanket] -.->|Removes| F2
    P2[VentilationDilutionBelowLFL] -.->|Removes| F1
    P3[ExplosionProofElectricalBondingStatic] -.->|Removes| F3
    P4[FlameArrestorsInhibitors] -.->|Removes| F4
    P5[LELDetectionSystem] -.->|Mitigates| FF
    P6[EmergencyShutdownESD] -.->|Mitigates| FF
    P7[FireWaterDelugeFoam] -.->|Mitigates| FF
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Flash Fire Consequence Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[IdentifyHazardousInventory] --> S2[SelectReleaseScenario]
    S2 --> S3[EstimateReleaseRateAndDuration]
    S3 --> S4[ModelVaporCloudDispersionAEGL]
    S4 --> S5{IsCloudWithinFlammableLimits}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[EstimatePoolFlameDimensions]
    S5 -- No --> S7[NoFlashFireHazard]
    S6 --> S8[CalculateRadiativeHeatFluxAtDistance]
    S8 --> S9[DetermineSafeSeparationDistance]
    S9 --> S10[EvaluateConsequencesToPersonnelAndEquipment]
    S10 --> S11[DesignMitigationAndESDSystems]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Layers of Protection Analysis (LOPA) for Flash Fire

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[IPL1ProcessDesignInherentlySafer] --> L2[IPL2BasicProcessControlBPCS]
    L2 --> L3[IPL3AlarmsOperatorIntervention]
    L3 --> L4[IPL4SafetyInstrumentedFunctionSIF]
    L4 --> L5[IPL5PhysicalProtectionBundDikes]
    L5 --> L6[IPL6EmergencyResponseFireWaterFoam]
    L6 --> L7[IPL7CommunityEmergencyPlans]
    L1 --> R[ReducedFlashFireRiskToAcceptableLevel]
    L2 --> R
    L3 --> R
    L4 --> R
    L5 --> R
    L6 --> R
    L7 --> R
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a flash fire. List any four key characteristics that distinguish it from a vapour cloud explosion (VCE).**

**Model Answer:**

A **flash fire** is a non-explosive combustion of a dispersed flammable vapor-air mixture in which the flame front propagates by thermal diffusion through the cloud at subsonic velocity, producing thermal radiation as the principal hazard.

**Four distinguishing characteristics:**

1. **No damaging overpressure** — Peak pressure rise is negligible (< 5 kPa), unlike VCE which can produce overpressures of several hundred kPa.
2. **Subsonic flame speed** — 0.5 to 10 m/s versus deflagration speeds of 100 – 300 m/s in VCE.
3. **Unconfined, uncongested combustion** — Occurs in open atmosphere, not in confined/congested geometries required for VCE.
4. **Brief burn duration** — Typically seconds to a few minutes, leaving no crater or blast damage.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between flash point and fire point of a flammable liquid. Why is the fire point always higher than the flash point?**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Flash Point | Fire Point |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Minimum temperature at which a liquid releases enough vapor to form an ignitable mixture near the surface | Minimum temperature at which the released vapor burns *sustainedly* for ≥ 5 seconds |
| **Vaporization rate** | Just sufficient to enter the flammable range | Sufficient to sustain combustion |
| **Sustainability** | Momentary flash; no sustained burning | Self-sustaining flame |
| **Typical ΔT** | Lower by 5 – 10 °C | Higher by 5 – 10 °C |

**Why Fire Point > Flash Point:**
At the flash point, the vaporization rate is barely sufficient to *ignite* momentarily. To *sustain* combustion, heat losses to the surroundings and to the liquid bulk must be overcome by the flame, requiring a higher vaporization rate — hence a higher temperature.

---

## **Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labeled sketch, describe the **mechanism of a flash fire** in a process plant. Discuss the role of **flammability limits (LFL and UFL)** in determining whether a released vapor cloud will burn. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 15 m diameter circular pool of **n-heptane** is burning in open air with a wind speed of **3 m/s**. Calculate the **radiative heat flux** at a distance of **75 m** from the flame center. Use the data:
- Burning rate: $\dot{m} = 0.101$ kg/m²·s
- Heat of combustion: $\Delta H_c = 44{,}566$ kJ/kg
- Threshold for 2nd degree burn (10 s exposure): **4 kW/m²**

State whether an operator at 75 m is at risk. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Mechanism of Flash Fire:**

1. **Release of flammable substance** from a vessel, pipeline, or flange due to loss of containment.
2. **Vaporization:** Volatile liquids flash to vapor upon release due to pressure drop. Gases disperse directly.
3. **Air entrainment & dispersion** form a vapor cloud.
4. **Flammability check:** The cloud burns only where the local fuel concentration satisfies $LFL \leq C \leq UFL$.
   - If $C < LFL$: mixture is *too lean* → no ignition.
   - If $C > UFL$: mixture is *too rich* → no ignition.
   - If $LFL \leq C \leq UFL$: ignitable.
5. **Ignition** by a spark, hot surface, or static discharge.
6. **Flame propagation** through the cloud at subsonic velocity.
7. **Thermal radiation** is the primary hazard to personnel.
8. **Self-extinction** when fuel is consumed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sketch description (since markdown limitations prevent hand drawing):** Draw a horizontal release pipe with an arrow indicating the direction of vapor release. Show the vapor cloud as a hatched region with three concentric contours: (i) UFL boundary (innermost), (ii) Stoichiometric contour, (iii) LFL boundary (outermost). A small spark symbol indicates the ignition source. Curved arrows show the flame front propagating outward through the cloud.

#### **Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1:** Pool area

$$A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 15^2}{4} = 176.71 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Total heat release rate

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot A \cdot \Delta H_c = 0.101 \times 176.71 \times 44{,}566$$

$$\dot{Q} = 795{,}440 \text{ kW (approx.)}$$

**Step 3:** Radiative fraction (API RP 521)

$$\chi_r = 0.21 \cdot e^{-0.00323 \times 3} + 0.04$$

$$e^{-0.00969} = 0.9904$$

$$\chi_r = 0.21 \times 0.9904 + 0.04 = 0.2480$$

**Step 4:** Radiative heat output

$$\dot{Q}_{rad} = 0.2480 \times 795{,}440 = 197{,}269 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5:** Heat flux at 75 m

$$q = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4 \pi r^2} = \dfrac{197{,}269}{4 \pi \times 75^2}$$

$$q = \dfrac{197{,}269}{70{,}686}$$

$$q \approx 2.79 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

**Step 6:** Compare with 2nd-degree burn threshold (4 kW/m²)

$$2.79 \text{ kW/m}^2 < 4 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

**Conclusion:** At 75 m, the operator is **NOT at risk of 2nd-degree burns for 10 s exposure**. However, since $q > 1$ kW/m², **1st-degree burns are still possible** for prolonged exposure. The recommended safe distance (for $q \leq 4$ kW/m²) is approximately **62 m or greater** is conservative, but 75 m offers reasonable thermal protection under these conditions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **fire tetrahedron** with a neat diagram. Describe **four flash fire prevention strategies** in process plants, classifying each by the tetrahedron element it targets. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **physical properties** of a flammable liquid that determine its flash fire hazard. A storage tank contains **50,000 kg of acetone** at 25 °C. The flash point of acetone is **-20 °C**. Comment on the **flash fire hazard potential** and the **safety measures** required. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(a):**

**The Fire Tetrahedron:**

The classical fire triangle (Fuel, Oxidizer, Ignition) was extended to a **tetrahedron** by adding the **chain reaction of free radicals** (H•, OH•, O•). A fire requires all four elements simultaneously.

```
              IGNITION
                /\
               /  \
              /    \
             /  FF  \
            /________\
          FUEL       OXY
              \    /
               \  /
                \/
           CHAIN RXN
```

*(ASCII representation; refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 for a cleaner view.)*

**Four prevention strategies (mapped to tetrahedron):**
