Practical application: dry chemical extinguishers (e.g., MAP) suppress fires by interrupting the chain reaction, which is effective only on the 4th element, not directly on the original three.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Hazard Triangle** in detail. Discuss the role of each of the three components (Fuel, Oxygen, Heat) with one process plant example for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A process plant stores **n-hexane** in a fixed-roof tank. The following data are given:
- LFL of n-hexane in air = **1.1% by volume**
- UFL = **7.5% by volume**
- Flash point = **−22°C**
- AIT = **225°C**

*(i)* Calculate the **flammability range**.
*(ii)* Comment on the fire hazard at ambient temperature (assume ambient = 30°C).
*(iii)* Suggest **two engineering controls** to break the Hazard Triangle for this tank. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hazard Triangle — Detailed Explanation (7 Marks):**

1. **Fuel (3 Marks sub-content):** The combustible material that undergoes oxidation. In process plants, fuels include hydrocarbons (methane, propane, gasoline, benzene, naphtha), solvents, polymer dust, and even metals like magnesium. The hazard is determined by **Flash Point**, **LFL/UFL**, and **vapor pressure**.
   *Example:* A leaking flange on a naphtha pipeline releases flammable vapor that mixes with air.

2. **Oxygen (2 Marks sub-content):** Atmospheric air (20.9% O₂) or pure O₂. Oxygen enrichment dramatically lowers ignition energy and widens flammability limits.
   *Example:* A leaking liquid oxygen line in a gasification unit enriches surrounding air to >25% O₂, causing normally non-flammable materials to ignite.

3. **Heat (2 Marks sub-content):** Energy input required to start combustion. Sources include sparks, hot surfaces, static electricity, and auto-ignition at AIT.
   *Example:* Welding sparks during maintenance work near a hydrocarbon storage tank act as ignition source.

> Removing *any one* side prevents fire — this is the practical use of the model.

---

**(b) Numerical & Engineering Solution (7 Marks):**

*(i) Flammability Range:*

$$
\text{FR} = \text{UFL} - \text{LFL} = 7.5\% - 1.1\% = 6.4\%
$$

*(ii) Fire Hazard at 30°C:*

Since the **Flash Point of n-hexane is −22°C**, the liquid is already emitting sufficient vapor at 30°C to form an ignitable mixture with air (30°C ≫ −22°C). The vapor concentration in the tank headspace easily exceeds the LFL of 1.1%, placing the tank in the **flammable regime** at all times. **Fire hazard is HIGH.**

*(iii) Engineering Controls to Break the Hazard Triangle:*

1. **Inerting (Remove O₂ side):** Install a **nitrogen blanketing system** on the tank to keep O₂ below the **LOC of n-hexane (≈ 11.8%)**.
2. **Vapor Control (Remove Fuel side):** Install a **floating roof** or **vapor recovery system (VRU)** to prevent accumulation of flammable vapor above the liquid surface.
3. **Ignition Control (Remove Heat side):** Implement a **hot work permit system** and ensure **bonding/grounding** during all transfer operations to eliminate static sparks.

*(Any two of the above are acceptable.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **Fire Tetrahedron**. Explain the role of the **fourth element** and how it differs from the original Hazard Triangle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A storage vessel contains **methanol** at atmospheric pressure. Using the data below:
- LFL = **6.0%**, UFL = **36%**
- AIT = **464°C**
- Heat of combustion $\Delta H_c = 726$ kJ/mol

*(i)* Determine the **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (by mass)** for complete combustion of methanol (CH₃OH).
*(ii)* If the vessel headspace has **15% O₂** (inerted with N₂), assess whether combustion is possible.
*(iii)* Justify the use of **N₂ inerting** using the LOC principle. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Fire Tetrahedron (7 Marks):**

The **Fire Tetrahedron** adds a fourth face to the Hazard Triangle — the **Self-Sustaining Chemical Chain Reaction**. Once ignition occurs, combustion propagates through free radicals (H•, O•, OH•) in a chain-branching mechanism. The 4th element is critical for **sustained combustion** rather than just initial ignition.

*Key difference:* The Hazard Triangle (3 elements) explains **ignition**, while the Fire Tetrahedron (4 elements) explains **ignition + sustained burning**.

*Practical implication:* Halogenated agents (e.g., halons), **dry chemical powders (MAP, ABC type)**, and **water mist** suppress fires by interrupting the chain reaction, providing rapid knockdown that cooling alone cannot achieve.

*(Sketch: Tetrahedron with apex labeled "Chain Reaction", base triangle with Fuel/Oxygen/Heat.)*

---

**(b) Numerical & Conceptual Solution (7 Marks):**

*(i) Stoichiometric Combustion Reaction for Methanol:*

$$
2\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

Stoichiometric molar ratio CH₃OH : O₂ = 2 : 3 = 1 : 1.5

Convert to mass basis:
- Mass of 1 mol CH₃OH = **32 g**
- Mass of 1.5 mol O₂ = 1.5 × 32 = **48 g**
- Air contains 23.2% O₂ by mass, so air required = 48 / 0.232 ≈ **207 g**

**Air-Fuel Ratio (stoichiometric):**

$$
\text{AFR}_{st} = \dfrac{207}{32} = 6.47 \; \text{kg air / kg fuel}
$$

*(ii) Combustion Assessment at 15% O₂:*

The **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC) of methanol** is experimentally around **10%** (NFPA 69). Since the headspace has **15% O₂** > 10%, **combustion IS possible** if the methanol vapor concentration lies within its flammability range (6–36%).

**Conclusion: Inerting is INSUFFICIENT — O₂ must be reduced to below 10% LOC.**

*(iii) Justification of N₂ Inerting:*

N₂ inerting works by displacing O₂ to a level **below the LOC** of the specific fuel. For methanol, the tank headspace must be maintained at **O₂ < 10%** (typically a design margin reduces this to 8% or 5%). This removes the **"Oxygen side"** of the Hazard Triangle, ensuring that even if fuel vapor and an ignition source are present, combustion cannot be initiated or sustained.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing LFL/UFL with LOC:** LOC refers to the *oxygen* concentration limit, not fuel. Students often write "below LFL" when they mean "below LOC".
> 2. **Ignoring units in flammability equations:** Always quote LFL/UFL/LOC in % by volume.
> 3. **Omitting the 4th element:** In Tetrahedron questions, marks are lost if the "chain reaction" face is not discussed.
> 4. **Forgetting the design safety margin on LOC:** Industrial inerting uses 1–2% margin below the measured LOC for safety.
> 5. **Using stoichiometric A/F ratio without converting O₂ fraction to air fraction** — air is only ~23% O₂ by mass, not 100%.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Hazard Triangle** = **Fuel + Oxygen + Heat (Ignition Source)** — all three must be present *simultaneously* for fire.
- ✅ Removing **any one** side extinguishes the fire — foundational principle of all fire protection engineering.
- ✅ **Fire Tetrahedron** = Triangle + **Self-Sustaining Chain Reaction** (free radicals).
- ✅ **LOC (Limiting Oxygen Concentration)** is fuel-specific; design inerting to keep O₂ **below LOC** with safety margin.
- ✅ **LFL/UFL** define the fuel-side concentration window for combustion.
- ✅ **Flash Point** of a liquid is *always below* its Fire Point, which is *below* its AIT.
- ✅ **MIE (Minimum Ignition Energy)** classifies fuel hazards: hydrogen (~0.02 mJ) is far more sensitive than methane (~0.28 mJ).
- ✅ Real plant examples: **inert gas blanketing** (N₂), **floating roof tanks** (vapor control), **bonding/grounding** (static control), **halon/dry chemical systems** (chain reaction interrupters).
- ✅ The Hazard Triangle is universally applied in **HAZOP, LOPA, FMEA, and What-If analyses** in chemical process industries.
- ✅ Industrial standards to remember: **NFPA 69** (inerting), **ASTM E2079** (LOC measurement), **NFPA 30** (flammable liquids code).
- ✅ Always consider **stochastic factors**: a real process may have transient excursions where all three sides momentarily coincide — design must account for these via **deflagration vents, suppression systems, and emergency isolation**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Classification in Process Plant Safety

In the discipline of **Process Plant Safety Engineering**, the term *Classification* is a foundational umbrella concept that governs how engineers systematically categorize hazards, materials, equipment, spaces, and protective systems. Without a rigorous classification framework, risk communication fails, regulatory compliance breaks down, and engineering protective measures cannot be correctly designed or audited.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> *Classification* in process plant safety engineering refers to the systematic, standardized grouping of hazardous substances, process equipment, geographic operating zones, and safety systems into discrete categories based on their physical, chemical, toxicological, or operational characteristics, to enable the correct selection of engineering controls, mitigation strategies, and regulatory compliance frameworks.

## 1.2 The Five Pillars of Classification

Within PECHT743 – Safety Engineering of Process Plants, the syllabus defines **five principal classification domains**:

1. **Classification of Hazardous Areas** (Electrical Area Classification)
2. **Classification of Chemicals & Hazardous Materials** (NFPA 704 / GHS)
3. **Classification of Fires** (Class A, B, C, D, K)
4. **Classification of Process Equipment / Plant Units**
5. **Classification of Safety Instrumented Functions** (SIL – Safety Integrity Levels)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Classification Matters (Engineering Rationale):**
> A refinery may store thousands of tonnes of hydrocarbons. Without classification, every square metre of the facility would need identical (and prohibitively expensive) explosion-proof equipment. Classification allows *graded protection* — the most stringent safeguards are applied only where truly needed.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Triage" Model

Imagine a hospital emergency room during a mass-casualty event:

- **Triage nurses classify** incoming patients into **Red (critical)**, **Yellow (urgent)**, and **Green (minor)** categories.
- Resources (doctors, ICU beds, ventilators) are then allocated **proportionally** to each category.
- **No doctor wastes time on a Green patient while a Red patient is dying.**

Process plant classification follows the **exact same logic**:

- **Classify first, then design the right protection level for each zone / material / system.**
- A flammable-gas-handling pump room needs explosion-proof motors; an admin office does not.
- A solvent (low flash point) needs different fire-suppression than a metal fire.

## 1.4 Key Standards & Codes Governing Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **International Standards Body Mapping (Mandatory for KTU):**
>
> | Domain | Primary Standard(s) |
> |---|---|
> | Hazardous Area Classification | **IEC 60079-10-1 / 60079-10-2**, IS 5572, API RP 500/505 |
> | Chemical Hazard Classification | **NFPA 704**, GHS (UN GHS Rev. 9), OSHA HCS |
> | Fire Classification | **IS 2190 : 1992**, NFPA 10, EN 2 |
> | Process Equipment Classification | **ASME B31.3**, API 650/620 |
> | Safety Integrity Levels | **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511**, ANSI/ISA S84 |

## 1.5 Fundamental Units & Metrics Used in Classification

The following metrics are the *numerical spine* of all classification activities:

- **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)** – unit: **% v/v** or **ppm**
- **Upper Explosive Limit (UEL)** – unit: **% v/v** or **ppm**
- **Flash Point** – unit: **°C**
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** – unit: **°C**
- **Vapour Pressure** – unit: **kPa** or **mmHg**
- **Threshold Limit Value (TLV)** – unit: **ppm** or **mg/m³**
- **LD₅₀ / LC₅₀** – unit: **mg/kg** (oral) or **ppm** (inhalation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU theory questions frequently test the *numerical cut-off boundaries* between classes. Memorize these carefully (provided in Section 2).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Hazardous Areas

A *hazardous area* is any location in which an explosive gas-air mixture, vapour-air mixture, or dust cloud is, or may be expected to be, present in quantities requiring special precautions for the construction and use of electrical equipment.

### 2.1.1 Sources of Release (Pre-requisite Logic)

Before any area can be classified, one must identify the **source of release** — a point where a flammable gas, vapour, mist, or dust *can escape* into the atmosphere.

**Logic Step Chain:**
1. **Identify process equipment** containing flammables (pumps, compressors, flanges, valves, relief valves, sample points).
2. **Determine the grade of release** based on frequency & duration:
   - **Continuous Grade** → release is continuous or expected to occur for long periods (>1000 hr/yr).
   - **Primary Grade** → release is likely to occur periodically or occasionally during normal operation (10–1000 hr/yr).
   - **Secondary Grade** → release is not expected during normal operation, and if it does, it is infrequent and of short duration (<10 hr/yr).
3. **Map the release grade to a zone** (only then).

### 2.1.2 The IEC Zone System (Gas/Vapour)

| Zone | Definition | Typical Equipment |
|---|---|---|
| **Zone 0** | Flammable atmosphere **present continuously or for long periods** (>1000 hr/yr) | Inside storage tanks, vent pipes of closed reactors |
| **Zone 1** | Flammable atmosphere **likely during normal operation** (10–1000 hr/yr) | Around pump seals, sample points, fill/empty points |
| **Zone 2** | Flammable atmosphere **not likely in normal operation, and if present, only briefly** (<10 hr/yr) | Areas around flanges, valve glands in well-ventilated process areas |

### 2.1.3 The IEC Zone System (Dust)

| Zone | Definition | Typical Equipment |
|---|---|---|
| **Zone 20** | Combustible dust cloud **continuously or long periods** | Inside dust collectors, blenders, mixers |
| **Zone 21** | Combustible dust cloud **likely during normal operation** | Areas near bagging stations, mills |
| **Zone 22** | Combustible dust cloud **not likely and short duration** | Areas around dust-handling equipment with good housekeeping |

### 2.1.4 The NEC Class/Division System (US Equivalent)

| Class | Hazard Type | Division 1 | Division 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class I** | Flammable gases/vapours | Normally hazardous | Not normally hazardous |
| **Class II** | Combustible dusts | Normally hazardous | Not normally hazardous |
| **Class III** | Ignitable fibres/flyings | Normally hazardous | Not normally hazardous |

### 2.1.5 Ventilation Influence on Zones

Ventilation can either **upgrade or downgrade** a zone's hazard level:

$$\text{Effective Zone} = f(\text{Release Grade}, \text{Ventilation Degree}, \text{Availability})$$

Where:
- **Ventilation Degree:** *High* (≥6 air changes/hr with unrestricted circulation), *Medium*, *Low*
- **Availability:** *Good*, *Fair*, *Poor*

> [!NOTE]
> A **secondary-grade release with HIGH ventilation** often downgrades from Zone 1 to Zone 2. This is a frequently-tested KTU concept.

### 2.1.6 Hazardous Area Extent — Distance Calculations

The extent of a hazardous area around a release source is calculated using:

$$D = k \cdot \sqrt[3]{\dot{V}_g / V_{d,min}}$$

Where:
- $D$ = extent of hazardous zone (in metres)
- $\dot{V}_g$ = volumetric release rate (m³/s)
- $V_{d,min}$ = minimum ventilation volumetric flow rate to dilute below LEL (m³/s)
- $k$ = empirical safety factor (typically 0.5 to 2.0)

For **gas/vapour density > air density**, the hazardous cloud travels **horizontally and accumulates in low points**; for gases **lighter than air** (e.g., methane), the cloud rises and disperses.

## 2.2 Classification of Chemicals (NFPA 704 Diamond)

The **NFPA 704 Standard System** classifies chemical hazards using a four-colour diamond placard:

$$\text{Total NFPA Score} = H + F + R + S$$

Where each parameter is rated on a **0–4 scale** (0 = no hazard, 4 = severe hazard):

| Position | Colour | Code Letter | Hazard Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Left** | Blue | **H** | Health |
| **Top** | Red | **F** | Flammability |
| **Right** | Yellow | **R** | Reactivity |
| **Bottom** | White | **S** | Special Hazards (OX, W, SA) |

### 2.2.1 NFPA Rating Detailed Cut-offs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NFPA 704 Rating Criteria (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):**
>
> | Rating | Health (H) | Flammability (F) | Reactivity (R) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **0** | No hazard | Will not burn | Stable |
> | **1** | Slight irritation | Must be preheated to burn | Mildly reactive |
> | **2** | Temporary injury | Must be heated before ignition can occur | Violent reaction with water |
> | **3** | Serious or permanent injury | Can be ignited under most ambient conditions | Detonation possible with strong initiator |
> | **4** | Fatal or irreversible | Rapidly or completely vaporizable at ambient | Capable of detonation under normal conditions |

### 2.2.2 GHS Hazard Pictograms (UN Globally Harmonized System)

GHS uses **9 hazard pictograms** to classify chemicals:

1. **GHS01** – Explosive (unstable explosive)
2. **GHS02** – Flammable (liquids, solids, gases, aerosols)
3. **GHS03** – Oxidizing
4. **GHS04** – Compressed Gas
5. **GHS05** – Corrosive
6. **GHS06** – Acute Toxicity (skull & crossbones)
7. **GHS07** – Harmful / Irritant (exclamation mark)
8. **GHS08** – Health Hazard (carcinogen, mutagen, etc.)
9. **GHS09** – Environmental Hazard (aquatic toxicity)

### 2.2.3 GHS Acute Toxicity Classification (Oral LD₅₀)

| Category | Oral LD₅₀ (mg/kg) | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Category 1** | ≤ 5 | Skull & Crossbones |
| **Category 2** | > 5 and ≤ 50 | Skull & Crossbones |
| **Category 3** | > 50 and ≤ 300 | Skull & Crossbones |
| **Category 4** | > 300 and ≤ 2000 | Exclamation Mark |
| **Category 5** | > 2000 and ≤ 5000 | No Symbol |

## 2.3 Classification of Fires

| Class | Fuel Type | Extinguishing Media |
|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | Ordinary combustibles (wood, paper, cloth, rubber) | Water, foam, ABC dry powder |
| **Class B** | Flammable liquids (petrol, oil, solvents) | CO₂, BCF/Halon, dry chemical, foam |
| **Class C** (or K/EU) | Flammable gases (LPG, methane, H₂) | Dry chemical, CO₂ — **never water** |
| **Class D** | Combustible metals (Mg, Al, Na, K, Ti) | Special dry powder (NaCl, Cu powder) |
| **Class E** | Electrical equipment (live) | CO₂, dry chemical (non-conductive) |
| **Class F** (or K) | Cooking oils & fats | Wet chemical (saponification) |

## 2.4 Classification of Safety Integrity Levels (SIL)

The **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** classifies the reliability of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF), per **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511**.

The Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) is the core metric:

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}$$

Where:
- $\lambda_{DU}$ = dangerous undetected failure rate (per hour)
- $T_{proof}$ = proof test interval (hours)

| SIL | PFDavg | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) |
|---|---|---|
| **SIL 1** | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | 10 – 100 |
| **SIL 2** | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 100 – 1,000 |
| **SIL 3** | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | 1,000 – 10,000 |
| **SIL 4** | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | 10,000 – 100,000 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SIL 4 is rarely used in process industries** — it is reserved for high-consequence scenarios such as nuclear reactor shutdown systems.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Definition | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $PFD_{avg} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}$ | dimensionless | SIL classification |
| 2 | $RRF = 1 \div PFD_{avg}$ | dimensionless | Risk reduction quantification |
| 3 | $\dot{V}_{min} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{LEL \cdot \rho_{air}} \times 100$ | m³/s | Ventilation sizing for dilution |
| 4 | $LEL_{mixture} = \dfrac{1}{\sum (y_i / LEL_i)}$ (Le Chatelier) | % v/v | Multi-component gas mixtures |
| 5 | $D = k \cdot \sqrt[3]{\dot{V}_g / V_{d,min}}$ | metres | Hazardous zone extent |
| 6 | $NFPA_{total} = H + F + R + S$ | dimensionless | Chemical hazard severity |
| 7 | $E = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | Unreliability function |
| 8 | $MTBF = 1 / \lambda$ | hours | Component reliability |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Examinations:**
> Students often confuse **Division 1 = Zone 0/1** and **Division 2 = Zone 2** in the NEC system. This is **incomplete** — Division 1 includes BOTH Zone 0 and Zone 1; Division 2 is roughly Zone 2. Always specify which system you are using.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries & Petrochemical Plants** — Use **IEC Zone classification** to specify Ex-proof equipment ratings.
- **Pharmaceutical Plants** — Use **NFPA 704** to label solvent storage rooms.
- **Offshore Platforms** — Use **Class I Div 1/2** alongside IEC zones (US operators) to size cable glands.
- **Nuclear & Aerospace** — Use **SIL 3/4** for shutdown systems.
- **Warehousing** — Use **fire Class A/B/D** to select the correct extinguisher type and placement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this classification framework is economically vital:**
> Misclassification in a hazardous area can lead to (a) use of non-explosion-proof motors (catastrophic ignition), or (b) over-engineering with expensive certified equipment (wasted capital). The classification framework optimises the **risk-versus-cost** trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: SIL Classification from Failure Rate Data

**Problem Statement (Illustrative):**
A pressure transmitter in a refinery has a dangerous undetected failure rate $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. The proof test interval is $T_{proof} = 8760$ hours (1 year). Determine the SIL classification.

**Step 1 — Recall the fundamental equation.**

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}$$

*Logic:* This assumes a constant failure rate with on-line detection only at proof testing.

**Step 2 — Substitute the given numerical values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg} &= \frac{1}{2} \times (5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{hr}^{-1}) \times (8760\ \text{hr}) \\
PFD_{avg} &= \frac{1}{2} \times (5 \times 8760) \times 10^{-6} \\
PFD_{avg} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 43800 \times 10^{-6} \\
PFD_{avg} &= 21900 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute final numerical value.**

$$PFD_{avg} = 2.19 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the SIL table.**

The computed $PFD_{avg} = 2.19 \times 10^{-2}$ falls within the range $[10^{-2}, 10^{-1}]$.

Therefore, the loop is classified as **SIL 1**.

**Step 5 — Calculate the Risk Reduction Factor (RRF).**

$$\begin{aligned}
RRF &= \frac{1}{PFD_{avg}} \\
RRF &= \frac{1}{2.19 \times 10^{-2}} \\
RRF &= 45.66
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Interpretation: A single SIF can independently reduce the demand frequency by a factor of ~45. To achieve SIL 2 ($PFD_{avg} = 10^{-3}$), the proof test interval must be reduced by ~10× (to ~1 month) **or** a redundant (1oo2 or 2oo3) architecture must be adopted.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hazardous Zone Extent Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A flange on a 2-inch pipe carrying propane leaks at a rate of $\dot{m} = 0.05\ \text{kg/s}$. The minimum ventilation required to dilute propane below its LEL is $V_{d,min} = 0.25\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. Using $k = 1.0$, calculate the hazardous zone extent.

**Step 1 — Recall the empirical extent equation.**

$$D = k \cdot \sqrt[3]{\dot{V}_g / V_{d,min}}$$

**Step 2 — Convert mass release rate to volumetric release rate.**

Using propane molar mass $M = 44.097\ \text{g/mol}$, gas density at STP $\rho_{gas} = 1.83\ \text{kg/m}^3$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_g &= \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{gas}} \\
\dot{V}_g &= \frac{0.05\ \text{kg/s}}{1.83\ \text{kg/m}^3} \\
\dot{V}_g &= 0.0273\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\dot{V}_g}{V_{d,min}} &= \frac{0.0273}{0.25} \\
&= 0.1093
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the cube root.**

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= 1.0 \times (0.1093)^{1/3} \\
D &= 1.0 \times 0.4777 \\
D &= 0.478\ \text{metres}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Apply safety factor (typically 1.5× for practical engineering).**

$$D_{design} = 1.5 \times 0.478 = 0.72\ \text{metres}$$

Therefore, all electrical equipment within **0.72 m radius** of the flange must be rated for **Zone 1 / Zone 2** as appropriate.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Le Chatelier's Rule for Multi-Component LEL

**Problem Statement:**
A gas mixture contains:
- Methane (CH₄): $y_1 = 70\%$, LEL₁ = 5% v/v
- Ethane (C₂H₆): $y_2 = 20\%$, LEL₂ = 3% v/v
- Propane (C₃H₈): $y_3 = 10\%$, LEL₃ = 2.1% v/v

Calculate the LEL of the mixture using Le Chatelier's rule.

**Step 1 — Recall Le Chatelier's formula.**

$$LEL_{mix} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{LEL_i}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term of the sum.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 1} &= \frac{70}{5} = 14.0 \\
\text{Term 2} &= \frac{20}{3} = 6.667 \\
\text{Term 3} &= \frac{10}{2.1} = 4.762
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the terms.**

$$\sum = 14.0 + 6.667 + 4.762 = 25.429$$

**Step 4 — Take the reciprocal.**

$$\begin{aligned}
LEL_{mix} &= \frac{1}{25.429} \\
LEL_{mix} &= 0.0393 \\
LEL_{mix} &= 3.93\%\ \text{v/v}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The mixture's LEL is **lower than the lowest individual LEL** (2.1% for propane) when propane concentration is high relative to its LEL. This is **counter-intuitive** but proves that *lean mixtures can be more hazardous than the worst individual component suggests*. Always use the mixture LEL, never the single-component LEL, in design calculations.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Equipment Protection Level (EPL)

The relationship between Zones and **Equipment Protection Levels (EPL)** per IEC 60079-0 is given by:

| Zone | EPL Required | Equipment Marking |
|---|---|---|
| **Zone 0** | **Ga** | Ex ia IIC T4 Ga |
| **Zone 1** | **Gb** | Ex db IIC T4 Gb |
| **Zone 2** | **Gc** | Ex nR IIC T4 Gc |
| **Zone 20** | **Da** | Ex ta IIIC T120 °C Da |
| **Zone 21** | **Db** | Ex tb IIIC T120 °C Db |
| **Zone 22** | **Dc** | Ex tc IIIC T120 °C Dc |

> [!NOTE]
> **Equipment Marking Decoding (KTU Favourite Question):**
> *Ex db IIC T4 Gb*
> - **Ex** → Explosion-proof certified
> - **db** → Flameproof enclosure (d) with increased safety (b)
> - **IIC** → Gas group (acetylene + hydrogen — most stringent)
> - **T4** → Temperature class (max surface temp 135 °C)
> - **Gb** → Equipment Protection Level for Zone 1

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — IEC vs NEC Systems

| Feature | IEC System | NEC (US) System |
|---|---|---|
| **Adopted in** | Europe, India, Asia | USA, Canada |
| **Gas zones** | 0, 1, 2 | Class I Div 1 (≈Zones 0+1) / Div 2 (≈Zone 2) |
| **Dust zones** | 20, 21, 22 | Class II Div 1 / Div 2 |
| **Gas groups** | IIA, IIB, IIC | A, B, C, D |
| **Temperature class** | T1 – T6 | T1 – T6 (identical) |
| **EPL concept** | Yes (Ga, Gb, Gc) | No equivalent |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification Framework for Process Plant Safety

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PROCESS PLANT SAFETY CLASSIFICATION"]
    A --> B["AREA CLASSIFICATION"]
    A --> C["CHEMICAL CLASSIFICATION"]
    A --> D["FIRE CLASSIFICATION"]
    A --> E["EQUIPMENT CLASSIFICATION"]
    A --> F["SAFETY INSTRUMENTED FUNCTION SIL"]

    B --> B1["IEC Zone System"]
    B --> B2["NEC Class Division System"]
    B1 --> B1a["Zone 0 Continuous"]
    B1 --> B1b["Zone 1 Normal Operation"]
    B1 --> B1c["Zone 2 Abnormal"]
    B2 --> B2a["Class I Div 1"]
    B2 --> B2b["Class I Div 2"]

    C --> C1["NFPA 704 Diamond"]
    C --> C2["GHS Pictograms"]
    C1 --> C1a["Health H 0 to 4"]
    C1 --> C1b["Flammability F 0 to 4"]
    C1 --> C1c["Reactivity R 0 to 4"]
    C1 --> C1d["Special S"]
    C2 --> C2a["GHS01 to GHS09"]

    D --> D1["Class A Solids"]
    D --> D2["Class B Liquids"]
    D --> D3["Class C Gases"]
    D --> D4["Class D Metals"]
    D --> D5["Class F Cooking Oils"]

    E --> E1["Pressure Vessels"]
    E --> E2["Piping Systems"]
    E --> E3["Storage Tanks"]

    F --> F1["SIL 1 PFD 10-2 to 10-1"]
    F --> F2["SIL 2 PFD 10-3 to 10-2"]
    F --> F3["SIL 3 PFD 10-4 to 10-3"]
    F --> F4["SIL 4 PFD 10-5 to 10-4"]
```

## 4.2 NFPA 704 Diamond — Visual Layout

```mermaid
graph TD
    N["NFPA 704 DIAMOND PLACARD"]
    N --> TOP["TOP RED FLAMMABILITY F 0 to 4"]
    N --> LEFT["LEFT BLUE HEALTH H 0 to 4"]
    N --> RIGHT["RIGHT YELLOW REACTIVITY R 0 to 4"]
    N --> BOTTOM["BOTTOM WHITE SPECIAL HAZARDS S"]
    BOTTOM --> B1["OX Oxidizer"]
    BOTTOM --> B2["W Water Reactive"]
    BOTTOM --> B3["SA Simple Asphyxiant"]
```

## 4.3 Logical Flowchart for Hazardous Area Classification (IEC)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST1["START Identify flammable material"]
    ST1 --> ST2["Identify process equipment containing flammables"]
    ST2 --> ST3["Determine release source location"]
    ST3 --> ST4{"Grade of Release"}
    ST4 --> G1["Continuous Grade"]
    ST4 --> G2["Primary Grade"]
    ST4 --> G3["Secondary Grade"]
    G1 --> Z0["Assign ZONE 0 20"]
    G2 --> Z1["Assign ZONE 1 21"]
    G3 --> Z4["Apply ventilation correction"]
    Z4 --> Z4a["High Ventilation"]
    Z4 --> Z4b["Medium Ventilation"]
    Z4 --> Z4c["Low Ventilation"]
    Z4a --> Z2["Downgrade to ZONE 2 22"]
    Z4b --> Z2b["Retain ZONE 1 21"]
    Z4c --> Z2c["Retain or Upgrade Zone"]
    Z2 --> END1["Select EPL Ga Gb Gc"]
    Z2b --> END1
    Z2c --> END1
    Z0 --> END1
    Z1 --> END1
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Classification System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        I1["Material Safety Data Sheet MSDS"]
        I2["Process Flow Diagram PFD"]
        I3["Piping and Instrumentation Diagram P and ID"]
        I4["Equipment Datasheets"]
        I5["Site Layout Drawings"]
    end

    subgraph ANALYSIS["ANALYSIS LAYER"]
        A1["Hazard Identification HAZID"]
        A2["Source of Release Identification"]
        A3["Grade of Release Assignment"]
        A4["Ventilation Assessment"]
        A5["LEL Flash Point Auto Ignition Evaluation"]
        A6["Failure Rate Data Collection"]
    end

    subgraph CLASS["CLASSIFICATION LAYER"]
        C1["Area Zone 0 1 2 20 21 22"]
        C2["Chemical NFPA 704 GHS"]
        C3["Fire Class A B C D F"]
        C4["Equipment PED ASME Classification"]
        C5["Safety Integrity SIL 1 to 4"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1["Area Classification Drawings"]
        O2["Equipment Protection Level Selection"]
        O3["Emergency Response Plan"]
        O4["Inspection and Maintenance Schedule"]
        O5["Regulatory Compliance Report"]
    end

    I1 --> A5
    I2 --> A2
    I3 --> A2
    I4 --> A6
    I5 --> A4
    A1 --> A3
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> C1
    A4 --> C1
    A5 --> C2
    A5 --> C3
    A6 --> C5
    C1 --> O1
    C1 --> O2
    C2 --> O2
    C2 --> O3
    C3 --> O3
    C4 --> O2
    C5 --> O4
    C5 --> O5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note for KTU Students:**
> The Block-Level Functional Architecture diagram maps how raw plant data (MSDS, P&IDs) flows through analysis stages and emerges as classified outputs that drive engineering design, inspection, and regulatory reporting. This is exactly the structure the KTU Board expects in a 14-mark descriptive answer.
