# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define physical hazards. List any four major categories of physical hazards encountered in process plants.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

**Definition (1 mark)**: Physical hazards are environmental agents or factors in the workplace that can cause bodily harm or property damage through the transfer of physical energy (mechanical, acoustic, thermal, radiant, or electrical) without requiring a chemical or biological reaction with the body.

**Four Categories (2 marks — 0.5 each)**:
1. **Noise and Vibration** — from compressors, turbines, pumps
2. **Thermal Extremes** — heat stress (furnaces) and cold stress (cryogenic storage)
3. **Radiation** — non-ionizing (welding UV) and ionizing (NDT X-ray)
4. **Mechanical Hazards** — rotating machinery, conveyor belts, falling objects

### Question 2: State the OSHA permissible exposure limit (PEL) for occupational noise and the 5 dB doubling rule.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

**OSHA PEL (1.5 marks)**: The OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) for occupational noise is **90 dB(A)** for an **8-hour time-weighted average (TWA)** exposure. At this level, the worker may be exposed for the full 8-hour shift without exceeding the legal limit.

**5 dB Doubling Rule (1.5 marks)**: According to OSHA's standard, for every **5 dB increase** in sound pressure level above 90 dB(A), the **maximum permissible exposure time is halved**. Hence:
- 90 dB(A) → 8 hours
- 95 dB(A) → 4 hours
- 100 dB(A) → 2 hours
- 105 dB(A) → 1 hour
- 110 dB(A) → 0.5 hours (30 minutes)

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — Choose Either (a) OR (b)

#### Part A(a): Noise Hazards — Calculation and Control

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(7 marks)** In a process plant, two compressors operate simultaneously. Compressor 1 produces a sound pressure level of **88 dB(A)** at the operator station, and Compressor 2 produces **91 dB(A)** at the same point.

**(i)** Calculate the total sound pressure level at the operator station. **(3 marks)**

**(ii)** If the noise level is to be reduced to **85 dB(A)** by installing an acoustic enclosure that reduces the source by a uniform amount, determine the required insertion loss of the enclosure. **(2 marks)**

**(iii)** List any **two engineering controls** to reduce noise in process plants. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Part (i) — Total SPL (3 marks)**:

We use the logarithmic addition formula:

$$ L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}\right) $$

Substitute $L_1 = 88$ dB and $L_2 = 91$ dB:

$$ L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{8.8} + 10^{9.1}\right) $$

Compute each term:
- $10^{8.8} = 6.31 \times 10^{8}$
- $10^{9.1} = 1.26 \times 10^{9}$

Sum:

$$ 6.31 \times 10^{8} + 12.6 \times 10^{8} = 18.91 \times 10^{8} $$

$$ L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1.891 \times 10^{9}\right) $$

$$ L_{total} = 10 \times 9.277 = 92.77 \text{ dB(A)} $$

**Part (ii) — Required Insertion Loss (2 marks)**:

$$ \text{IL} = L_{total} - L_{target} = 92.77 - 85 = 7.77 \text{ dB(A)} $$

**Part (iii) — Engineering Controls (2 marks — 1 each)**:
1. **Acoustic enclosures** (barriers) around the compressors
2. **Vibration isolation mounts** and **silencers** on intake/discharge piping

---

#### Part A(b): Vibration Hazards — Calculation and Health Effects

**(7 marks)** A pneumatic grinding tool used in a process plant produces a vibration with frequency **125 Hz** and amplitude **1.5 mm** at the handle.

**(i)** Calculate the vibration acceleration. **(3 marks)**

**(ii)** Calculate the **maximum permissible exposure time** if the tool produces a weighted RMS acceleration of **12 m/s²** (OSHA/ISO HAV standard is 5 m/s² for 8 hours; safe time follows a TLV/√t relationship for vibrational total value). Use the simple rule that permissible time is inversely proportional to the square of the ratio (a_tool / a_TLV). **(2 marks)**

**(iii)** State **two health effects** of hand-arm vibration exposure. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Part (i) — Vibration Acceleration (3 marks)**:

Using $a = (2\pi f)^2 \cdot A$:

$$ a = (2\pi \times 125)^2 \times 0.0015 $$

$$ 2\pi \times 125 = 785.4 \text{ rad/s} $$

$$ a = (785.4)^2 \times 0.0015 = 616849 \times 0.0015 $$

$$ a = 925.3 \text{ m/s}^2 $$

**Part (ii) — Permissible Exposure Time (2 marks)**:

Using $T = T_0 \times (a_{TLV}/a_{tool})^2$:

$$ T = 8 \times (5/12)^2 = 8 \times 0.1736 = 1.39 \text{ hours} \approx 1.4 \text{ hours} $$

**Part (iii) — Health Effects (2 marks — 1 each)**:
1. **Vibration White Finger (VWF)** / Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome (HAVS) — episodic blanching of fingers
2. **Peripheral neuropathy** — numbness, tingling, loss of tactile discrimination

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

#### Part B(a): Radiation Hazards in Process Plants

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(7 marks)**:

**(i)** Differentiate between **ionizing and non-ionizing radiation** with two examples of each as encountered in process plants. **(3 marks)**

**(ii)** A worker stands **2 m** from a welding arc producing **40 W/m²** of UV irradiance. What is the irradiance when the worker moves to **5 m**? **(2 marks)**

**(iii)** List the **three primary protective measures** against radiation in process plants. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Part (i) — Ionizing vs Non-Ionizing (3 marks)**:

| Property | Ionizing | Non-Ionizing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Energy | > 10 eV (can ionize atoms) | < 10 eV (cannot ionize) |
| Source in plant | X-ray NDT, radioactive level gauges | Welding UV, IR furnace, lasers |
| Health effect | Cancer, radiation sickness, genetic damage | Burns, cataracts, skin erythema |

(2 marks for table; 1 mark for examples.)

**Part (ii) — Inverse Square Calculation (2 marks)**:

Using $I_2 = I_1 (r_1/r_2)^2$:

$$ I_2 = 40 \times (2/5)^2 = 40 \times 0.16 = 6.4 \text{ W/m}^2 $$

**Part (iii) — Protective Measures (2 marks — 0.67 each)**:
1. **Time** — minimize exposure duration
2. **Distance** — maximize distance from source
3. **Shielding** — use lead aprons, welding curtains, goggles

---

#### Part B(b): Mechanical and Fall Hazards

**(7 marks)**:

**(i)** A maintenance worker of mass **75 kg** falls from a height of **3 m** into a safety net that stretches **0.8 m** during deceleration. Calculate the **impact force** on the worker. **(4 marks)**

**(ii)** State the **KTU-recommended hierarchy of controls** in descending order of effectiveness. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Part (i) — Impact Force (4 marks)**:

Step 1: Compute free-fall velocity:

$$ v = \sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 3} = \sqrt{58.86} = 7.67 \text{ m/s} $$

Step 2: Apply work-energy principle for stopping distance d = 0.8 m:

$$ F_{impact} = mg + \frac{mv^2}{2d} $$

Step 3: Calculate each term:
- $mg = 75 \times 9.81 = 735.75$ N
- $\frac{mv^2}{2d} = \frac{75 \times 58.86}{1.6} = \frac{4414.5}{1.6} = 2759.06$ N

Step 4: Sum:

$$ F_{impact} = 735.75 + 2759.06 = 3494.8 \text{ N} \approx 3.5 \text{ kN} $$

**Part (ii) — Hierarchy of Controls (3 marks — 1 each for first three, 0.5 each for last two, OR 0.6 each for five)**:

1. **Elimination** (most effective)
2. **Substitution**
3. **Engineering Controls**
4. **Administrative Controls**
5. **Personal Protective Equipment / PPE** (least effective)

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PECHT743 Module 2:**
>
> 1. **Using arithmetic addition for noise**: Students often add decibels arithmetically (88 + 91 = 179 dB). This is **physically wrong** and costs **2-3 marks**. Always use $L_{tot} = 10\log_{10}(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10})$.
>
> 2. **Confusing p₀ with atmospheric pressure**: Forgetting that $p_0 = 20$ µPa (NOT 20 Pa) for SPL calculations. Wrong reference value gives 60 dB error.
>
> 3. **Mixing up WBV and HAV TLVs**: Whole-body vibration TLV is 0.5 m/s²; hand-arm is 5 m/s². Confusing them costs 1-2 marks.
>
> 4. **Forgetting units in vibration amplitude**: Amplitude must be in **meters** in the formula $a = (2\pi f)^2 A$. Using mm directly is a common error.
>
> 5. **Skipping the "+ mg" term** in fall impact force: Work-energy gives the *additional* force; the static body weight must be added.
>
> 6. **Not writing final units**: KTU examiners specifically check units in formulas. Always write **dB(A)**, **m/s²**, **J**, etc.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Physical Hazards (Module 2):**

- **Physical hazards** = physical energy transfer to humans/equipment; no chemical reaction needed.
- **OSHA PEL for noise** = **90 dB(A) for 8 hours**; **5 dB doubling rule**.
- **SPL formula**: $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p/p_0)$, with **p₀ = 20 µPa**.
- **SPL addition is logarithmic**, not arithmetic.
- **Distance attenuation**: **6 dB drop per doubling of distance** in free field.
- **Vibration acceleration**: $a = (2\pi f)^2 A$; remember A in **meters**.
- **WBV TLV** = 0.5 m/s²; **HAV TLV** = 5 m/s².
- **WBGT** for heat stress: $0.7T_{nw} + 0.2T_g + 0.1T_a$.
- **Inverse square law** applies to both **radiation intensity** and **noise intensity**.
- **Ionizing radiation** = X-ray, gamma, beta, alpha, neutron; **non-ionizing** = UV, visible, IR, microwave, radio frequency.
- **Free-fall velocity**: $v = \sqrt{2gh}$.
- **Fall impact force**: $F = mg + mv^2/(2d)$.
- **Hierarchy of Controls** (KTU 2024): **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE**.
- **Kinetic energy** of moving equipment is the dominant mechanical hazard parameter: $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$.
- A **5 kg impeller at 3000 RPM** carries **> 5000 J** — equivalent to a rifle muzzle.
- **Permissible time formula**: $T(L) = 8 / 2^{(L-90)/5}$ — must be memorized for 14-mark problems.
- **Reference pressure** $p_0 = 20$ µPa; **reference power** $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W.
- **KEC Module Internal Choice**: Be prepared to choose between (a) and (b) — both must be studied.
- **Always show units in final answer** — KTU valuation key penalizes missing units.
- **Memorize the formula sheet** (Table 2.7) — it covers 95% of 14-mark numerical problems.
- **Fire and Explosion is Module 3**; do not write about it in Module 2 answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrical Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrical Hazards** in process plants refer to any unsafe condition of electrical origin that has the potential to cause harm to personnel, damage to equipment, fire, or explosion. In the context of chemical process industries (CPI), the primary concerns are **electric shock**, **arc flash**, **secondary ignition of flammable atmospheres**, and **static electricity discharge**.

According to KTU 2024 PECHT743 (Module 2), electrical hazards in process plants are broadly classified into:

1. **Hazards from energized electrical systems** (motors, switchgear, control panels).
2. **Hazards from static electricity** (fluid flow, powder handling, non-conductive surfaces).
3. **Hazards from lightning** (direct strike on storage tanks or process columns).
4. **Hazards due to inadequacy of earthing / bonding / protection devices**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> Think of electricity flowing through a plant like **water flowing through pipes**. Voltage is *water pressure*, current is *flow rate*, and resistance is *pipe friction*. If a worker accidentally becomes part of the "pipe" (completes a circuit to ground), current flows through their body the same way water would rush through a hole in a damaged pipe. The damage depends not just on the pressure (voltage), but on the actual flow through the body — which is why *low-voltage domestic shocks are rarely fatal*, while even a few tens of milliamperes through the chest can stop a human heart.

In a refinery or a solvent plant, this "leakage" is far more dangerous because:
- **Flammable vapors** may be ignited by a tiny spark (≤ 1 mJ of energy is enough for many hydrocarbons).
- **Wet / sweaty skin** lowers the body's resistance drastically, increasing current.
- **Equipment housings** may become live due to insulation failure.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Limits

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold limits for 50/60 Hz AC currents (IEC 60479):**
> - Perception threshold: **~1 mA**
> - Let-go threshold: **~10 mA** (muscles lock, cannot release)
> - Respiratory paralysis: **~30 mA**
> - Ventricular fibrillation (potentially fatal): **~100 mA for > 0.2 s**
> - Severe burns: **> 5 A**
> - Minimum ignition energy (MIE) of typical hydrocarbon vapors: **0.2 – 1.0 mJ**
> - Human body resistance (dry skin): **≈ 100,000 Ω** ; (wet/broken skin): **≈ 1,000 Ω**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Electrical Hazards (Structured Logic)

### A. Hazards of Energized Systems
1. **Electric Shock** — current passes through the body.
2. **Burns** — resistive (Joule) heating of tissue; also contact burns from overheated conductors.
3. **Arc Flash** — an electrical short circuit through air, producing a plasma jet with temperatures **> 19,000 °C** (4× the Sun's surface), intense UV/IR radiation, a pressure blast wave, and molten metal projectiles.
4. **Arc Blast** — the mechanical pressure wave from an arc vaporizing metal (copper expands ~67,000×).
5. **Fire / Explosion** — secondary ignition of flammable vapors or dust.
6. **Secondary Injuries** — falls from height after a shock-induced startle reflex.

### B. Hazards of Static Electricity
- Generation by **triboelectric charging** (friction between dissimilar materials).
- Charge accumulation when **generation rate > dissipation rate**.
- Sudden discharge → incendive spark.

### C. Hazards of Lightning
- Direct strike on a tank → vapor ignition.
- Induced surges on instrumentation lines.

## 2.2 Why Static Electricity is a Major Concern in Process Plants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three conditions must coincide for an ignition: **(1)** an effective **discharge** of adequate energy, **(2)** a **flammable atmosphere** of suitable concentration, and **(3)** an **oxidizer** (typically air). Static electricity satisfies (1) trivially.

Common static-generating operations:
- Flow of petroleum, solvents, or LPG through pipes, filters, or fine-mesh strainers.
- Powder handling (Pneumatic conveying, sieving, pouring from height).
- Steam / water mist jets.
- Conveyor belts (rubber on metal).
- Helicopter / tank-truck bonding failures during transfer.

## 2.3 Why Earthing & Bonding Work

If two conductors are **bonded** (electrically connected) and then **earthed** (connected to earth via a low-impedance path, **typically < 10 Ω**), any generated static charge flows harmlessly into the infinite earth reservoir instead of accumulating and discharging as a spark.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ohm's Law | $V = I R$ | V, A, Ω | Foundation of all shock calculations |
| 2 | Body current under touch voltage | $I_b = V_t / R_b$ | A | $V_t$ = touch voltage, $R_b$ = body resistance |
| 3 | Power dissipated in body | $P_b = I_b^{2} R_b = V_t^{2}/R_b$ | W | Determines heating & burn severity |
| 4 | Energy stored on a charged body (capacitor model) | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | J | C in F, V in V |
| 5 | Energy stored on a charged liquid | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} Q V$ | J | Q in C, V in V |
| 6 | Charge transfer during discharge | $Q = C V$ | C | Compare with incendivity thresholds |
| 7 | Energy of static spark | $E_s = \tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_s^{2}$ | J | $C_s$ = stray capacitance, $V_s$ = surface potential |
| 8 | Arc-flash incident energy | $E_i = 4.184 \cdot 10^{\,N} \cdot t_a \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{D}\right)^x \cdot F$ | cal/cm² | Empirical; constants from IEEE 1584 |
| 9 | Let-go current (50 Hz) | $I_{LG} \approx 10$ mA | A | Threshold above which muscle lock occurs |
| 10 | Ventricular fibrillation threshold | $I_{VF} \approx K / \sqrt{t}$ | A | $K \approx 116$ mA·s^0.5 for hand-to-foot path |
| 11 | Body resistance model | $R_b = R_s + R_i + R_{ext}$ | Ω | Skin ($R_s$) + internal ($R_i$≈300 Ω) + external contact |
| 12 | Earthing resistance limit (plant equipment) | $R_{earth} < 10$ Ω | Ω | IS 3043 / IEEE 80 for hazardous areas |
| 13 | Lightning stroke energy (typical) | $E \approx 10^5$ – $10^6$ J | J | Can vaporize metallic roofing |
| 14 | Charge relaxation time | $\tau = \varepsilon \rho$ | s | $\varepsilon$ = permittivity, $\rho$ = resistivity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Always compare the calculated spark energy $E_s$ with the **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE)** of the specific chemical. If $E_s \ge \text{MIE} \times 5$ (safety factor), the hazard is unacceptable.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Design of safe earthing grids** in substations and tank farms (IEEE 80).
- **Selection of intrinsically safe (IS) instruments** for Zone 0/1 areas (IEC 60079).
- **Arc-flash hazard calculations** for PPE category selection (NFPA 70E / IEEE 1584).
- **Static dissipation design** for tanker loading, powder transfer, and LPG bottling plants.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between Touch Voltage and Body Current

We model the human body as a series combination of three resistances:
1. $R_s$ — skin resistance at the contact point,
2. $R_i$ — internal tissue resistance (≈ **300 Ω**, hand-to-foot),
3. $R_{ext}$ — external contact resistance (shoes, floor).

**Step 1.** Express the equivalent body resistance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_b &= R_s + R_i + R_{ext}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** Apply Ohm's law across the touch voltage $V_t$ (line-to-earth voltage that appears on the equipment enclosure during a fault).

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_b &= \frac{V_t}{R_b}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** Compute the power dissipated inside the body (which causes heating and burn injury).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_b &= I_b^{2} R_b = \frac{V_t^{2}}{R_b}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.** Compute total thermal energy deposited over exposure duration $t$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{body} &= P_b \, t = \frac{V_t^{2}}{R_b} \, t
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the energy that causes Joule-type burns inside the body, **independent of current**.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Static Discharge Energy from a Solvent Transfer

> **Problem:** A road tanker transfers toluene (MIE ≈ 0.24 mJ) through a 6 m flexible hose of estimated capacitance $C_s = 200$ pF. The hose becomes charged to a potential of $V_s = 10$ kV due to inadequate bonding. Determine whether the resulting spark is incendive. Assume a safety factor of 5.

### Step 1 — Compute spark energy.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_s &= \tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_s^{2} \\
    &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 200 \times 10^{-12} \times \left(10 \times 10^{3}\right)^{2} \\
    &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 200 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{8} \\
    &= 10 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{J} \\
    &= 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{J} \\
    &= 1.0 \; \text{mJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compare with MIE.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MIE}_{\text{toluene}} &= 0.24 \; \text{mJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply safety factor.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{limit} &= 5 \times \text{MIE} = 5 \times 0.24 = 1.2 \; \text{mJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Decision.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_s \; (1.0 \; \text{mJ}) &< E_{limit} \; (1.2 \; \text{mJ}) \quad \text{Borderline safe} \\
&\text{BUT} \; E_s / \text{MIE} = 1.0 / 0.24 \approx 4.17 < 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The spark is **potentially incendive**. *Action required:* install proper bonding (≤ 10 Ω), keep transfer velocity below the **safety velocity** ($v < 0.5\sqrt{1/\rho}$ m/s for hydrocarbon liquids), and use a **reticulated grounding cable with continuous monitoring**.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Body Current for a Worker Touching a Live Cabinet

> **Problem:** A 415 V, three-phase induction motor control cabinet develops a phase-to-enclosure fault. Earth-fault loop impedance is $Z_e = 0.3$ Ω. A worker touches the cabinet with one dry hand (assume $R_b = 5\,000$ Ω) while standing on a wet concrete floor (assume $R_{ext} = 0$).

### Step 1 — Touch voltage (line-to-earth under fault).

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_t &= V_{phase} = \frac{415}{\sqrt{3}} = 239.6 \; \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Assuming negligible drop across the fault loop — worst case for the human.)

### Step 2 — Body current.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_b &= \frac{V_t}{R_b} = \frac{239.6}{5000} = 0.0479 \; \text{A} = 47.9 \; \text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare with thresholds.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_b \; (47.9 \; \text{mA}) &\gg I_{LG} \; (10 \; \text{mA}) \quad \Rightarrow \text{Muscle lock — cannot let go} \\
&\gg I_{VF} \; (\approx 100 \; \text{mA}) \quad \text{Risk if exposure > 0.5 s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** **Lethal if sustained.** *Mitigation:* residual current device (RCD / ELCB) rated ≤ **30 mA, ≤ 40 ms** must trip the circuit.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Ignition Sources vs. Hazard Class

| Ignition Source | Minimum Energy | Typical Plant Source | Affected Hazard Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| Static spark (human) | 10–30 mJ | Walking on non-conductive floor | Class I, II, III |
| Static spark (liquid transfer) | < 1 mJ | Hose not bonded | Class I (Group D) |
| Switch / relay arc | 100–500 mJ | Control panel | Class I |
| Lightning | $10^{5}$ – $10^{6}$ J | Direct strike | All |
| Arc-flash plasma | $10^{4}$ – $10^{5}$ J | LV switchgear | Personnel & equipment |
| Hot surface | — | Overheated motor | Class I, II |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Overall Electrical Hazard Control Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazard Source] --> B1[Energized System]
    A --> B2[Static Electricity]
    A --> B3[Lightning]
    A --> B4[Inadequate Earthing]

    B1 --> C1[Engineering Controls]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1

    C1 --> D1[Earthing and Bonding < 10 ohm]
    C1 --> D2[RCD or ELCB < 30 mA]
    C1 --> D3[Intrinsic Safety Barriers]
    C1 --> D4[Explosion Proof Enclosures]
    C1 --> D5[Lightning Arrestors]
    C1 --> D6[Static Dissipative Flooring]

    C1 --> E[Administrative Controls]
    E --> F1[Permit to Work]
    E --> F2[Lockout Tagout]
    E --> F3[PPE Insulating Gloves Arc Flash Suit]

    C1 --> G[Emergency Response]
    G --> H1[First Aid and CPR]
    G --> H2[Fire Suppression]
    G --> H3[Incident Investigation]

    D1 --> I[Safe Plant Operation]
    D2 --> I
    D3 --> I
    D4 --> I
    D5 --> I
    D6 --> I
    F1 --> I
    F2 --> I
    F3 --> I
    H1 --> I
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
```

## 4.2 Architecture — Hazardous Area Classification and Equipment Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Plant Hazard Analysis] --> B[Flammable Atmosphere Identified]

    B --> C{Gas Vapor Mist}
    B --> D{Combustible Dust}
    B --> E{Fibers Flyings}

    C --> C0[Zone 0 Continuous]
    C --> C1[Zone 1 Intermittent]
    C --> C2[Zone 2 Abnormal Only]

    D --> D20[Zone 20 Continuous]
    D --> D21[Zone 21 Intermittent]
    D --> D22[Zone 22 Abnormal Only]

    E --> E3[Zone for Fibers]

    C0 --> P1[Ex ia Intrinsic]
    C1 --> P2[Ex d Flameproof or Ex e Increased Safety]
    C2 --> P3[Ex n Non Incendive]

    D20 --> Q1[Ex tD or DIP A20]
    D21 --> Q2[Ex tD or DIP A21]
    D22 --> Q3[Ex tD or DIP A22]

    P1 --> R[Certified Equipment with ATEX or IECEx Marking]
    P2 --> R
    P3 --> R
    Q1 --> R
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R

    R --> S[Safe Operation in Hazardous Area]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Static Electricity Control Loop During Tanker Loading

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Tanker Arrives at Gantry] --> S2[Driver Connects Bonding Cable]
    S2 --> S3{Continuity Check < 10 ohm}
    S3 -- NO --> S4[Alarm and Block Loading]
    S3 -- YES --> S5[Start Transfer at Safe Velocity < 7 m per s]
    S5 --> S6[Dead Man Control via Overfill Sensor]
    S6 --> S7[End Transfer and Disconnect Bonding Cable Last]
    S4 --> S8[Investigate and Retest]
    S8 --> S2
    S7 --> S9[Tanker Departs]

    subgraph Controls In Place
    T1[Earthing Clamp with Retraction]
    T2[Overfill Probe]
    T3[Vapor Recovery Hose]
    T4[Velocity Limiter]
    end
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Electric Shock Protection Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electric Shock Risk] --> B[Primary Protection]
    A --> C[Fault Protection]
    A --> D[Additional Protection]

    B --> B1[Basic Insulation]
    B --> B2[Enclosures IP2X or Higher]
    B --> B3[Safety Extra Low Voltage SELV < 50 V AC]

    C --> C1[Protective Earthing]
    C --> C2[Automatic Disconnection via MCB or MCCB]
    C --> C3[Double or Reinforced Insulation Class II]

    D --> D1[RCD or ELCB < 30 mA]
    D --> D2[Equipotential Bonding]
    D --> D3[Safe Isolation and Lockout Tagout]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between "let-go" current and "perception" current. Why are these values important in electrical safety design?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> - **Perception current** (≈ **1 mA** at 50 Hz AC): the minimum current at which a person can *feel* a tingling sensation through the skin. It does not cause pain or involuntary muscle reaction.
> - **Let-go current** (≈ **10 mA** at 50 Hz AC): the maximum current at which a person can *still voluntarily release* the conductor. Beyond this, the muscles of the hand and forearm tetanically contract and the victim cannot let go, leading to prolonged exposure and possible ventricular fibrillation.

**Importance in safety design:**
1. They form the basis of **RCD/ELCB trip thresholds** (typically 30 mA, well above let-go but well below ventricular fibrillation threshold).
2. They are used in **IEC 60479 body-current vs. time** curves to determine permissible touch voltages for different exposure durations (e.g., 50 V AC for unlimited exposure in dry conditions, 25 V in wet).
3. Equipment and PPE specifications (insulating gloves, mats, tools) are rated against these thresholds.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is "static electricity"? List three situations in a process plant where it is generated, and state two control measures.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Static electricity** is the accumulation of electric charge on the surface of a non-conductive or isolated conductive material, produced when two materials come into contact and then separate (triboelectric effect). Because the dissipation rate is lower than the generation rate, charge builds up to a high potential (often several kilovolts) and may suddenly discharge as a spark.

**Three process-plant situations that generate static charge:**
1. **Flow of low-conductivity hydrocarbons** (e.g., toluene, hexane, kerosene, LPG) through pipes, filters, fine-mesh strainers, or flexible hoses — especially during road-tanker or ship-to-tank transfer.
2. **Powder handling** — pneumatic conveying, sieving, micronizing, pouring from height, or filling of FIBC (big-bag) sacks.
3. **Steam, water, or air jets** impinging on a non-conductive surface, or motion of conveyor belts (rubber on metal pulleys).

**Two control measures:**
1. **Bonding and earthing** all conductive parts of the transfer system (tanker–hose–tank) to maintain potential equality and provide a low-impedance (< 10 Ω) path to earth.
2. **Maintaining flow velocity below the safe limit** — typically **v ≤ 7 m/s** for hydrocarbon transfer, and **v ≤ 0.5 / √ρ** m/s in fine-mesh strainers — to limit charge generation rate.
   *(Acceptable alternatives: increasing liquid conductivity with anti-static additives like Stadis 450; using conductive / static-dissipative flooring; humidification; ionizers.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the phenomenon of **static electricity generation in process plants** with a neat diagram. Discuss the conditions under which a static discharge becomes incendive.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A road tanker is unloading toluene into a storage tank. The capacitance of the system is measured as 600 pF and a surface potential of 8 kV develops due to faulty bonding. Calculate the spark energy. Comment on the safety of the operation. The minimum ignition energy (MIE) of toluene is 0.24 mJ. Apply a safety factor of 5.

---

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Step 1 — Define generation mechanism.**
Static electricity is generated by the **triboelectric effect** — when two materials of differing work function come into contact and then separate, electrons transfer from one to the other. In a flowing liquid, this occurs at the pipe wall; in powders, at every particle–particle and particle–wall collision.

**Step 2 — Charge accumulation condition.**
Charge accumulates whenever the **rate of generation exceeds the rate of dissipation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dQ}{dt}\Big|_{gen} &> \frac{Q}{\tau}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\tau = \varepsilon \rho$ is the **charge relaxation time**. For petroleum products, $\tau$ can be several seconds to minutes.

**Step 3 — Three conditions for an incendive discharge.**
An ignition by static requires the **fire triangle** plus the **discharge**:
1. **Effective discharge** of sufficient energy $E_s \ge \text{MIE}$.
2. **Flammable mixture** of optimum concentration (between LFL and UFL).
3. **Sufficient oxidant** — typically atmospheric oxygen.
4. Discharge must occur in the **vapor space**, not inside the bulk liquid.

**Step 4 — Diagram (refer Section 4.3 Block Diagram).**

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Given:** $C_s = 600$ pF $= 600 \times 10^{-12}$ F; $V_s = 8$ kV $= 8000$ V; MIE = 0.24 mJ; safety factor SF = 5.

**Step 1 — Spark energy.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_s &= \tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_s^{2} \\
    &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 600 \times 10^{-12} \times \left(8000\right)^{2} \\
    &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 600 \times 10^{-12} \times 6.4 \times 10^{7} \\
    &= 300 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5} \\
    &= 1920 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{J} \\
    &= 1.92 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{J} \\
    &= 19.2 \; \text{mJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Safe limit using safety factor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{limit} &= \text{SF} \times \text{MIE} = 5 \times 0.24 = 1.2 \; \text{mJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_s \; (19.2 \; \text{mJ}) &\gg E_{limit} \; (1.2 \; \text{mJ}) \\
\text{Ratio} &= 19.2 / 0.24 = 80 \; \text{times MIE}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**
**The operation is extremely unsafe.** The spark energy is **80× the MIE**, far exceeding even the conservative safety-factor limit. **Immediate corrective actions:**
- Stop transfer immediately.
- Re-establish bonding with verified continuity < 10 Ω using a continuity-checked cable.
- Reduce flow velocity to the safe limit (< 7 m/s; < 1 m/s for fine filters).
- Consider anti-static additive (e.g., Stadis 450) to raise conductivity.
- Use an overfill probe and vapor-recovery hose.
- Re-inspect the loading gantry for floating roof / floating roof seal integrity.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Describe the various **types of electrical hazards** encountered in a process plant. Explain in detail the **arc-flash phenomenon** and its consequences.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of an **Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker (ELCB)**. A worker comes in contact with a 230 V live conductor while standing on a wet floor. The body resistance is 1000 Ω. Compute the body current. State whether the situation is lethal.

---

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Types of electrical hazards:**
1. **Electric shock** — current through the body.
2. **Burns** — electro-thermal and contact.
3. **Arc flash** — short-circuit plasma through air.
4. **Arc blast** — pressure wave from vaporized metal.
5. **Fire / Explosion** — secondary ignition of flammable atmospheres.
6. **Static discharge** — spark from accumulated charge.
7. **Lightning** — direct strike damage.
8. **Secondary injuries** — falls, blunt trauma.

**Arc-flash phenomenon:**
An arc flash occurs when a short circuit develops across an air gap between energized conductors, or between an energized conductor and ground. The arc ionizes the air, creating a low-resistance plasma path. Temperatures can reach **19,000 °C** (≈ 4× the Sun's surface), vaporizing copper conductors (Cu expands ~67,000× in volume). Consequences include:
- Intense **UV/IR radiation** causing blindness and skin burns even at distance.
- **Pressure wave** (arc blast) of several tons/m² capable of throwing a worker across a room.
- **Projectile molten metal** causing puncture wounds.
- **Sound blast** up to **160 dB** rupturing eardrums.
- Ignition of **clothing** (which continues burning at 800 °C).

**Mitigation:** arc-flash study, label panels with incident energy, PPE selection (NFPA 70E), arc-resistant switchgear, IR windows for thermography, remote racking, work distance.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Working of ELCB / RCD:**

The ELCB continuously monitors the **line current** and **neutral current**. Under normal conditions the vector sum is zero. When a person touches the live conductor, a small **residual current** flows through the body to earth, which is detected by a **toroidal current transformer (core-balance CT)**. The imbalance induces a secondary current that trips the relay and disconnects the circuit within **≤ 40 ms**.

**Given:** $V = 230$ V; $R_b = 1000$ Ω.

**Body current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_b &= \frac{V}{R_b} = \frac{230}{1000} = 0.230 \; \text{A} = 230 \; \text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Threshold comparison:**

| Threshold | Value |
|---|---|
| Let-go | 10 mA |
| Respiratory paralysis | 30 mA |
| Ventricular fibrillation (>0.2 s) | 100 mA |
| **Calculated** $I_b$ | **230 mA** |

Since $I_b = 230$ mA $\gg$ 100 mA and exposure > 0.2 s, **the situation is lethal** — ventricular fibrillation will occur. **Mandatory requirement:** install a **30 mA RCD** in the circuit so that the supply trips before fibrillation onset.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where KTU students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — writing $E_s = 1.92 \times 10^{-2}$ without converting to mJ → full marks lost on comparison step.
> 2. **Skipping the safety-factor step** — direct comparison $E_s$ vs. MIE is incomplete; examiners explicitly test for the 5× safety margin.
> 3. **Confusing ELCB with MCB** — ELCB trips on *residual current to earth*; MCB trips on *overload / short-circuit current*. Many students interchange these.
> 4. **Writing arc-flash temperatures incorrectly** — recall that the **Sun's surface is ~5500 °C** and an arc reaches **~19,000 °C**.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention Lockout-Tagout (LOTO)** in administrative controls → a 2-mark loss guaranteed.
> 6. **Using $V_{line}$ instead of $V_{phase}$** in body-current calculations for a 3-phase system — read the question carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Electric shock severity** depends on **current (mA) × duration (s)**, not voltage alone.
- **Threshold currents (50 Hz AC):** perception 1 mA | let-go 10 mA | fibrillation 100 mA | burns > 5 A.
- **Body resistance:** dry skin ~100 kΩ | wet skin ~1 kΩ | internal ~300 Ω.
- **Minimum ignition energy** of most hydrocarbons ≈ **0.2 – 1.0 mJ**.
- **Static electricity** needs three conditions for ignition: spark, flammable mixture, oxidizer.
- **Spark energy formula:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ — memorize and apply.
- **Bonding + Earthing** is the **primary control** for static; resistance **< 10 Ω**.
- **Safe transfer velocity:** $v \le 7$ m/s for bulk hydrocarbon; $v \le 0.5/\sqrt{\rho}$ m/s with filters.
- **Charge relaxation time:** $\tau = \varepsilon \rho$ — petroleum products have long τ.
- **ELCB/RCD rating:** **≤ 30 mA, ≤ 40 ms** for personnel protection.
- **Arc flash:** temperature ≈ **19,000 °C**, copper expands ~67,000×.
- **Hazardous area zones:** **0 / 1 / 2** (gas) and **20 / 21 / 22** (dust).
- **Equipment protection types:** Ex d (flameproof), Ex e (increased safety), Ex i (intrinsic), Ex n (non-incendive), Ex t (dust).
- **Lightning arrestors** mandatory on tall columns, storage tanks, and signal lines.
- **PPE for electrical work:** insulating gloves, face shield, arc-flash suit, dielectric boots.
- **Administrative controls:** Permit-to-Work, Lockout-Tagout (LOTO), competent person authorization.
- Always compare calculated spark energy with **MIE × Safety Factor (typically 5)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Hazards and Environmental Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Mechanical Hazards and Environmental Hazards ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** > *"Mechanical Hazards — types and control measures; Environmental Hazards — noise, vibration, thermal stress, illumination, ventilation and their control strategies in process plants."* ### 1.1 Mechanical Hazards — Formal Definition A **Mechanical Hazard** in a process plant is any physical condition, energy state, or machine configuration that has the potential to cause human injury (or equipment damage) through the transfer of mechanical energy — kinetic, potential, rotational, vibrational, or impact — to a worker. According to the KTU 2024 scheme classification (aligned with **ILO-OSH 2001** and **OSHA 29 CFR 1910 Subpart O**), mechanical hazards arise whenever a worker's body (or any part of it) is exposed to: - Energized machinery components - Stored or residual mechanical energy - Unexpected machine motion (start-up, shut-down, jamming) - Failure of structural integrity (rupture, collapse, ejection) > [!NOTE] > **Single-Line Board-Examiner Definition (memorize verbatim):** > *"A mechanical hazard is the unsafe condition created by the presence of kinetic, potential, or rotational energy in machinery or structures that can cause harm to personnel through impact, entanglement, crushing, shearing, cutting, abrasion, or ejection."* #### Intuitive Analogy — The "Spinning Fan" Model Imagine a ceiling fan spinning at high speed in a closed room. Even though the blades are smooth and look "safe," they carry enormous **kinetic energy**. A finger poked in will be cut. Now imagine the same fan but **stationary** — it carries only **potential energy** (stored in the elasticity of the mounting). Drop a heavy object on it, and it will spring up. The fan is the *machine*; the **energy it contains or transfers** is the *hazard*. This is the essence of mechanical hazards: they are not the machine, they are the **energy** the machine can deliver to you. ### 1.2 Environmental Hazards — Formal Definition An **Environmental Hazard** in a process plant refers to workplace conditions or physical agents in the surrounding atmosphere that cause harm to human health, comfort, or performance. Unlike mechanical hazards (which require direct contact), environmental hazards typically act through **chronic exposure** — the worker is exposed continuously to the agent. Per KTU Module 2, the principal environmental hazards are: | Agent | Physical Nature | Typical Source in Process Plants | |---|---|---| | **Noise** | Pressure waves (acoustic energy) | Compressors, turbines, valves, vents | | **Vibration** | Mechanical oscillation | Pumps, crushers, conveyors | | **Thermal Stress** | Heat / Cold | Furnaces, reactors, cryogenic lines | | **Illumination** | Light (luminance) | Control rooms, outdoor yards | | **Ventilation** | Air quality, vapor concentration | Confined spaces, storage tanks | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Tip:** Environmental hazards are *invisible* hazards — they cannot be seen, often cannot be felt immediately, yet cause long-term occupational diseases. This is why KTU examiners emphasize them as "silent killers" in process plants. #### Intuitive Analogy — The "Invisible Smoke" Model Picture a worker standing next to a large compressor. They cannot see, smell, or touch the hazard, but **over 8 hours**, the 95 dB noise damages their inner-ear hair cells permanently, and the 10 Hz vibration affects their spine. The danger is *real but imperceptible*. Environmental hazard control is therefore about **measurement and exposure limits**, not visual inspection. > [!NOTE] > **Threshold Concept:** All mechanical hazard control begins with the **hierarchy of controls**: Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Control → Administrative Control → PPE. This hierarchy is examinable in every KTU module. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Mechanical Hazards (KTU Board-Standard Taxonomy) Mechanical hazards in process plants are classified into **seven primary categories** (per OSHA / KTU 2024): #### A. Entanglement Hazard - Created by **rotating shafts, spindles, gears, couplings, chain drives, belts, and conveyors**. - The hazard acts like a "hook" that catches loose clothing, hair, jewelry, or limbs and pulls the worker into the machine. - **Control**: Fixed guards, rotating-component covers, "No Loose Clothing" rule, hair nets, removal of rings/watches. #### B. Crushing Hazard - Caused by **closing motion of two parts**, e.g., rolls of rolling mills, press brakes, gear meshes. - The energy involved is **directly proportional to the closing force** × closing distance. - **Control**: Two-hand controls, light curtains, pressure-sensitive mats, guard rails. #### C. Cutting / Shearing Hazard - Created by **sharp edges moving past one another** or stationary sharp edges exposed to the worker's path. - Examples: shear blades in cutting machines, lathe chips, sheet-metal edges. - **Control**: Fixed blade guards, retractable shields, tool design. #### D. Stabbing / Puncture Hazard - Caused by **projecting sharp objects**: rebar ends, nails, burrs, ejector pins. - **Control**: End-caps, protective boots, mechanical handling aids. #### E. Impact Hazard - Caused by **objects falling, swinging, or being ejected** with kinetic energy. - A falling mass of $m$ kg from height $h$ carries kinetic energy $E = mgh$. - **Control**: Hard hats, toe guards, ejection shields, machine guarding. #### F. Drawing-In / Trapping Hazard - Caused by **nips between rolls**, between belt and pulley, between gear teeth. - The "nip point" pulls material (or fingers) into the machine. - **Control**: Distance guarding (inching-rule: minimum 3 ft from nip). #### G. Stored-Energy Hazard - The most underestimated mechanical hazard: **hydraulic accumulators, compressed-air reservoirs, suspended loads, capacitor banks, flywheels**. - Even after the machine is "switched off," energy remains and can release catastrophically. - **Control**: Lockout–Tagout (LOTO), bleed valves, blocking devices, dissipation timers for flywheels. ### 2.2 Sources of Mechanical Hazards in Process Plants | Plant Equipment | Dominant Mechanical Hazard(s) | |---|---| | Centrifugal pumps | Rotating-shaft entanglement, fluid-jet injection | | Reciprocating compressors | Impact, vibration, stored pneumatic energy | | Conveyors (belt/screw) | Drawing-in at head/tail pulley, nip points | | Mixers / Agitators | Rotating-shaft entanglement | | Press brakes | Crushing (tonnage force), shear | | Milling machines | Rotating cutter, ejected chips | | Crane / Hoist | Suspended-load impact, swing | | Valves (large) | Sudden release of stored line pressure | | Gear boxes | Entanglement, crushing, vibration | ### 2.3 Environmental Hazards — Detailed Analysis #### 2.3.1 Noise **Definition:** Undesirable sound that can cause hearing loss, physiological stress, and reduced communication. **Physical Measure:** Sound Pressure Level (SPL) in decibels. $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right) \quad \text{[dB]}$$ where $p$ is the measured RMS sound pressure and $p_0 = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ is the reference threshold of human hearing. **Key KTU Numbers (memorize):** | Source | Typical SPL (dBA) | |---|---| | Whisper | 30 | | Office conversation | 60 | | Process plant (general) | 80–90 | | Gas turbine (1 m) | 110–120 | | Rocket engine (close) | 180 | **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) per OSHA / Indian Factories Act:** **90 dBA** for 8-hour TWA (Time-Weighted Average). Every +5 dB doubles sound intensity and halves permissible exposure time. #### 2.3.2 Vibration **Definition:** Mechanical oscillation of a system about an equilibrium position, characterized by **frequency (Hz)**, **amplitude (mm or m/s²)**, and **direction (x, y, z)**. **Whole-Body Vibration (WBV):** Transmitted through the seat/feet of a vehicle or operator (e.g., forklift, crane cabin). Affects the spine, causes "vibration white finger," motion sickness. **Hand-Arm Vibration (HAV):** Transmitted through power tools (grinders, jackhammers). Causes Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome (HAVS) — vascular, neurological, and musculoskeletal damage. **RMS Acceleration:** $$a_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\!\int_0^T a^2(t)\,dt} \quad \text{[m/s}^2\text{]}$$ **KTU Standard:** ISO 2631 / ISO 5349 define exposure limits. Indian Factories Act adopts a vibration TLV of **0.5 m/s²** (8-hr average) for WBV. #### 2.3.3 Thermal Stress **Definition:** The net heat load on a worker's body from environment + metabolic heat production, minus heat loss. **Heat Stress Index (HSI) — Belding's Equation:** $$\text{HSI} = \frac{E_{\text{req}}}{E_{\text{max}}} \times 100 \quad \text{[\%]}$$ where $E_{\text{req}}$ is the heat load required to maintain body temperature and $E_{\text{max}}$ is the maximum evaporative capacity. HSI > 100 indicates heat-stroke risk. **WBGT (Wet Bulb Globe Temperature) Index — KTU standard:** $$\text{WBGT}_{\text{outdoor}} = 0.7\,T_{\text{nwb}} + 0.2\,T_{\text{gt}} + 0.1\,T_{\text{db}}$$ $$\text{WBGT}_{\text{indoor}} = 0.7\,T_{\text{nwb}} + 0.3\,T_{\text{gt}}$$ where $T_{\text{nwb}}$ = natural wet bulb, $T_{\text{gt}}$ = globe thermometer, $T_{\text{db}}$ = dry bulb. **Permissible WBGT limit (ACGIH):** 30°C for unacclimatized, 32.5°C for acclimatized workers (moderate workload). #### 2.3.4 Illumination Inadequate illumination causes eye-strain, accidents (poor visibility of hazards), and reduced productivity. **KTU / Indian Standard (IS 3646) minimums:** | Work Area | Lux (lm/m²) | |---|---| | General outdoor plant | 50 | | Control rooms | 300 | | Precision instrument work | 500–1000 | | Confined space inspection | 200 (with portable lamp) | #### 2.3.5 Ventilation Dilution of airborne contaminants to maintain concentration below TLV (Threshold Limit Value). **Dilution Ventilation Equation:** $$Q = \frac{G \times 10^6}{C_{\text{TLV}} - C_0} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/h]}$$ where $Q$ = required ventilation flow, $G$ = contaminant generation rate (g/h), $C_{\text{TLV}}$ = TLV in ppm, $C_0$ = inlet concentration in ppm. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **only formula cheat-sheet** you need for Module 2 numerical problems. Every row is board-exam-grade. | # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | KTU Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p/p_0)$ | $p$ in Pa, $p_0 = 20\,\mu$Pa | Noise measurement | | 2 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10\log_{10}(I/I_0)$ | $I$ in W/m², $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ | Intensity-based problems | | 3 | Combined Noise (n sources) | $L_{\text{total}} = L_1 + 10\log_{10}(n)$ | Equal sources | Multiple-equipment plants | | 4 | Permissible Exposure Time | $T = \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}$ | $L$ in dBA, $T$ in hours | OSHA-style calculations | | 5 | Kinetic Energy (Impact) | $E = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ | $m$ in kg, $v$ in m/s | Falling-object hazard | | 6 | Falling-Weight KE | $E = mgh$ | $h$ in m | Drop hazards, suspended loads | | 7 | Velocity from drop height | $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ | $h$ in m | Impact velocity | | 8 | RMS Acceleration | $a_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{(1/T)\int a^2\,dt}$ | $a$ in m/s² | Vibration measurement | | 9 | Vibration Dose Value | $\text{VDV} = \left[\int a^4\,dt\right]^{1/4}$ | m/s$^{1.75}$ | ISO 2631 WBV | | 10 | WBGT (outdoor) | $0.7\,T_{\text{nwb}} + 0.2\,T_{\text{gt}} + 0.1\,T_{\text{db}}$ | °C | Heat-stress assessment | | 11 | WBGT (indoor) | $0.7\,T_{\text{nwb}} + 0.3\,T_{\text{gt}}$ | °C | Indoor thermal hazard | | 12 | HSI (Belding) | $(E_{\text{req}}/E_{\text{max}})\times 100$ | % | Heat-stress index | | 13 | Dilution Ventilation | $Q = (G \times 10^6)/(C_{\text{TLV}} - C_0)$ | g/h, ppm, m³/h | Vapor/dust control | | 14 | Stopping Distance (LOTO) | $d = v^2/(2\mu g)$ | m | Flywheel / vehicle rundown | | 15 | Centrifugal Force | $F = m\omega^2 r$ | N | Rotating-equipment hazard | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Pitfall:** When using $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) or conditional expressions in answers, always write $\vert x \vert$ as `$\lvert x \rvert$` or `$\mid x \mid$` in LaTeX — never the keyboard pipe character, which breaks Markdown tables. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The formulas and classifications above are not academic exercises. They directly drive: - **PPE selection** (ear-plug SNR rating from $L_p$) - **Plant layout** (separation distance for noise attenuation: 6 dB drop per doubling of distance) - **Maintenance scheduling** (residual flywheel rundown time before LOTO is safe) - **Shift-rotation design** (work–rest cycles based on WBGT) - **Regulatory compliance** (Indian Factories Act Sec. 87, 88, 89; OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95; ACGIH TLV booklet) ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Combined Sound Pressure Level from Multiple Sources **Statement:** Show that $n$ identical sound sources, each producing SPL $= L_1$, produce a combined SPL of $L_{\text{total}} = L_1 + 10\log_{10}(n)$. **Given:** $L_1 = 10\log_{10}(I_1/I_0)$ for each source, where $I_1$ is intensity of one source. **Derivation:** For sound waves, intensity is proportional to the *square* of pressure: $$I \propto p^2$$ The Sound Intensity Level in dB is defined as: $$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$$ When $n$ identical incoherent sources are placed together, intensities **add linearly** (incoherent sources don't interfere destructively on average): $$I_{\text{total}} = I_1 + I_1 + \cdots + I_1 = n \cdot I_1$$ Substituting into the dB formula: $$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{n I_1}{I_0}\right)$$ Using the logarithm product rule $\log(ab) = \log a + \log b$: $$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(n) + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_1}{I_0}\right)$$ Recognising the second term as $L_1$: $$\boxed{\,L_{\text{total}} = L_1 + 10 \log_{10}(n)\,}$$ **Engineering meaning:** Two identical machines do NOT produce $2 \times L_1$. They produce $L_1 + 3$ dB. Ten identical machines produce $L_1 + 10$ dB. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world rule of thumb:** Doubling the number of identical machines adds **+3 dB**. This is why a single super-large pump is often quieter than two smaller ones. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Permissible Exposure Time (PET) at OSHA PEL of 90 dBA **Statement:** Show that for an SPL of $L$ dBA, the OSHA permissible exposure time is: $$T = \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}} \quad \text{[hours]}$$ **Derivation:** OSHA's exchange rate (29 CFR 1910.95) is **5 dB** — for every 5 dB rise above 90 dBA, the permissible exposure time is **halved**. Mathematically, this is a discrete exponential decay: $$T \propto 2^{-(L-90)/5}$$ At $L = 90$ dBA, $T = 8$ hours (the reference). So the constant of proportionality is 8: $$T = 8 \cdot 2^{-(L-90)/5}$$ Rewriting the negative exponent as positive using $2^{-x} = 1/2^x$: $$\boxed{\,T = \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}} \quad \text{[hours]}\,}$$ **Sanity check:** - At $L = 95$ dBA: $T = 8/2^1 = 4$ hours ✓ - At $L = 100$ dBA: $T = 8/2^2 = 2$ hours ✓ - At $L = 115$ dBA: $T = 8/2^5 = 0.25$ h = 15 minutes ✓ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — WBGT Index (Outdoor) **Statement:** Show the derivation of the outdoor WBGT formula: $$\text{WBGT} = 0.7\,T_{\text{nwb}} + 0.2\,T_{\text{gt}} + 0.1\,T_{\text{db}}$$ **Derivation:** WBGT was developed by the U.S. Marine Corps (Yaglou, 1957) as a weighted average of three temperature measurements: 1. **Natural Wet Bulb ($T_{\text{nwb}}$)** — represents humidity and evaporative cooling. Most heavily weighted (**70%**) because sweat evaporation is the body's primary cooling mechanism. 2. **Globe Temperature ($T_{\text{gt}}$)** — a black-copper sphere absorbs radiant heat. Weighted **20%**. 3. **Dry Bulb ($T_{\text{db}}$)** — ambient air temperature. Weighted **10%** only (because dry-bulb alone doesn't capture radiation or humidity). By definition (weighted mean): $$\text{WBGT} = w_1 T_{\text{nwb}} + w_2 T_{\text{gt}} + w_3 T_{\text{db}}$$ Substituting empirically derived weights $w_1 = 0.7$, $w_2 = 0.2$, $w_3 = 0.1$ (note: $0.7 + 0.2 + 0.1 = 1.0$): $$\boxed{\,\text{WBGT} = 0.7\,T_{\text{nwb}} + 0.2\,T_{\text{gt}} + 0.1\,T_{\text{db}}\,\quad \text{[°C]}\,}$$ For indoor environments, the dry bulb is replaced by the natural wet bulb (no direct sun), giving $w_1 = 0.7$ and $w_2 = 0.3$: $$\text{WBGT}_{\text{indoor}} = 0.7\,T_{\text{nwb}} + 0.3\,T_{\text{gt}}$$ --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Velocity of a Falling Mass (Conservation of Energy) **Statement:** A 2-kg tool falls from a height of 5 m onto a worker's hard hat. Compute impact velocity and kinetic energy. **Given:** $m = 2$ kg, $h = 5$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s². **Step 1 — Initial state (just before fall):** At rest at height $h$. Energy is all **potential**: $$E_i = mgh = 2 \times 9.81 \times 5 = 98.1 \text{ J}$$ **Step 2 — Just before impact (kinetic energy from PE):** By conservation of energy (neglecting air drag): $$mgh = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$ Solving for $v$: $$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 5} = \sqrt{98.1} \approx 9.905 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 4 — Impact kinetic energy:** $$E = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times (9.905)^2 = 98.1 \text{ J}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Cross-Check:** 98 J is roughly the kinetic energy of a 1 kg mass dropped from 10 m — equivalent to being struck by a cricket ball at 100 km/h. The hard-hat suspension system must absorb this energy without transmitting >5 kN to the skull (fracture threshold). --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Energy Source vs Hazard Type | Energy Source | Hazard Category | Typical Energy (J) | Threshold of Harm | Primary Control | |---|---|---|---|---| | Rotating shaft (1500 rpm, 5 kg on 0.1 m) | Entanglement | $E = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2 \approx 200$ J | 5–10 J (skin laceration) | Fixed guard + LOTO | | Falling 2 kg from 5 m | Impact | 98 J | 5 J | Hard hat (50 J absorption) | | Flywheel $I = 50\,\text{kg·m}^2$, $\omega = 100\,\text{rad/s}$ | Stored KE | 250 kJ | 50 J | Brake + dissipation timer | | Hydraulic accumulator at 200 bar, 10 L | Stored PE | 100 kJ | 100 J | Bleed valve + LOTO | | Compressor exhaust 120 dB | Noise (acoustic) | $I \approx 1$ W/m² | PEL 90 dBA (0.001 W/m²) | Acoustic enclosure + ear PPE | | Furnace wall at 200°C | Thermal | Heat flux ~1 kW/m² | Skin burn in 5 s | Insulation + heat PPE | | Forklift WBV 0.8 m/s² | Vibration | Energy ~10 J/kg | TLV 0.5 m/s² | Suspension seat + shift limit | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hierarchy of Mechanical-Hazard Controls ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[HAZARD IDENTIFICATION
Risk Assessment
HIRA / JSA] --> nodeB[ELIMINATION
Remove the machine
or task] nodeA --> nodeC[SUBSTITUTION
Safer machine
Less energy] nodeC --> nodeD[ENGINEERING CONTROL
Guards, interlocks,
light curtains, two-hand control] nodeD --> nodeE[ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROL
LOTO procedure
Training, signage] nodeE --> nodeF[PPE
Hard hat, ear plug,
gloves, harness] nodeG[HIGHEST RELIABILITY] -.-> nodeB nodeH[LOWEST RELIABILITY] -.-> nodeF style nodeA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style nodeB fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style nodeC fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style nodeD fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style nodeE fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style nodeF fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style nodeG fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style nodeH fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagram above is the **mandatory control-hierarchy diagram** that KTU examiners expect in any 14-mark question on hazard control. Always draw it **top-down with PPE at the bottom** (lowest reliability). ### 4.2 Classification of Mechanical Hazards ```mermaid flowchart TD node1[MECHANICAL HAZARDS
IN PROCESS PLANTS] --> node2[KINETIC ENERGY
BASED] node1 --> node3[POTENTIAL ENERGY

Crane hoists] node3 --> node3B[Compressed gases
Hydraulic accumulators] node3 --> node3C[Elevated liquids] node4 --> node4A[Shaft entanglement] node4 --> node4B[Gear nip points] node4 --> node4C[Belt and pulley] node4 --> node4D[Centrifugal ejection] node5 --> node5A[Press crush] node5 --> node5B[Shear blades] node5 --> node5C[Pinch points] node6 --> node6A[Hydraulic jet] node6 --> node6B[Steam injection] node6 --> nodeC6[Vacuum collapse] style node1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 style node2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 style node3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 style node4 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 style node5 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 style node6 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.3 Environmental Hazards Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeENV[ENVIRONMENTAL
HAZARD AGENT] --> nodeNO[NOISE] nodeENV --> nodeVIB[VIBRATION] nodeENV --> nodeTH[THERMAL STRESS] nodeENV --> nodeIL[ILLUMINATION] nodeENV --> nodeVE[VENTILATION] nodeNO --> nodeNO_M[MECHANISM

List any four types with one example each.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** > *"A mechanical hazard is an unsafe condition arising from the presence of kinetic, potential, or rotational energy in machinery or structures, capable of causing human injury through impact, entanglement, crushing, shearing, or ejection."* Four types: - **Entanglement** — rotating shaft catching loose clothing (e.g., agitator shaft). - **Crushing** — closing motion of two parts (e.g., press brake ram). - **Cutting / Shearing** — sharp moving blades (e.g., lathe cutter). - **Impact** — falling tool from elevation (e.g., dropped wrench from scaffold). *(Award 0.5 mark per type, 0.5 for example, 0.5 for definition = 3 marks.)* --- #### Question 2. **State OSHA's permissible exposure limit (PEL) for noise in industry. What is the rule for exposure-time halving?** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** - **PEL:** $L_p = 90$ dBA for an 8-hour time-weighted average. - **Exchange Rate:** 5 dB (i.e., for every +5 dB rise, the permissible exposure time is halved). - **Worked example:** At 95 dBA, $T_{\max} = 4$ h; at 100 dBA, $T_{\max} = 2$ h. $$\text{PEL: } T = \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}} \text{ hours}$$ *(Award 1 mark for PEL, 1 mark for exchange rate, 1 mark for the formula/example = 3 marks.)* --- ### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 Style, Module 2]` --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **a) Classify the various mechanical hazards encountered in process plants. Discuss in detail the control measures for hazards arising from rotating machinery. (7 Marks)** **b) A process plant has three identical compressors, each producing an SPL of 92 dBA. Compute:** - **(i) The combined SPL when all three operate together.** *(3 Marks)* - **(ii) The OSHA permissible exposure time (PET) at this combined SPL.** *(2 Marks)* - **(iii) Two engineering control measures to reduce the hazard.** *(2 Marks)* #### Model Solution **a) Classification of Mechanical Hazards (5 Marks for classification + 2 Marks for rotating-machinery control):** Mechanical hazards in process plants are classified into the following seven categories: 1. **Entanglement** — created by rotating shafts, gears, belts, conveyors; loose clothing or hair is drawn in. 2. **Crushing** — closing motion of press brakes, rolling mills, gear meshes. 3. **Cutting / Shearing** — sharp moving blades, shears, lathe chips. 4. **Stabbing / Puncture** — projecting sharp objects, rebar ends, burrs. 5. **Impact** — falling or ejected objects (mass $\times$ velocity transfer). 6. **Drawing-In / Trapping** — nip points between rolls, belt–pulley, gear teeth. 7. **Stored Energy** — pneumatic / hydraulic accumulators, suspended loads, flywheel inertia. **Control Measures for Rotating-Machinery Hazards (2 Marks):** - **Fixed perimeter guards** at 1.8 m minimum height with bolted access panels. - **Interlocks** that trip the machine if the guard is opened. - **Lockout–Tagout (LOTO)** procedure for maintenance. - **Distance guarding** (at least 0.9 m from nip point) and warning signs. - **Removal of jewellery, ties, and loose clothing**; hair nets for operators. --- **b) Numerical Solution:** **Part (i) — Combined SPL:** $$L_{\text{total}} = L_1 + 10\log_{10}(n) = 92 + 10\log_{10}(3)$$ $$\log_{10}(3) = 0.4771$$ $$L_{\text{total}} = 92 + 10 \times 0.4771 = 92 + 4.771 = \mathbf{96.77 \text{ dBA}}$$ **Part (ii) — Permissible Exposure Time:** $$T = \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{(96.77-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{1.354}}$$ $$2^{1.354} \approx 2.555$$ $$T = \frac{8}{2.555} \approx \mathbf{3.13 \text{ hours}}$$ **Part (iii) — Engineering Controls (any 2 of the following):** - **Acoustic enclosure** around each compressor (typical attenuation: 25–30 dB). - **Vibration isolation mounts** plus flexible couplings to prevent structural transmission. - **Distance separation** — relocate control room 50 m from source (≈ 6 dB drop per doubling). - **Silencers / mufflers** on intake and exhaust ducts. - **Routine LOTO program** to ensure access only during maintenance. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **a) Explain the concept of Whole-Body Vibration (WBV) and Hand-Arm Vibration (HAV). Discuss the long-term health effects and the control measures prescribed by ISO 2631. (7 Marks)** **b) In a chemical reactor control room, the WBGT readings are: dry bulb 38°C, natural wet bulb 28°C, globe temperature 42°C. Compute the outdoor WBGT. If the workers are acclimatized, comment on whether the work should continue. (7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **a) WBV and HAV (7 Marks):** **Whole-Body Vibration (WBV):** WBV is mechanical oscillation transmitted through the supporting surface — typically the seat of a vehicle (forklift, crane, haul truck) or the floor of a workstation. The dominant frequency range is **0.5 to 80 Hz**, with the spine's natural resonance around **4–8 Hz** (most damaging). Long-term WBV exposure causes spinal degeneration, herniated discs, motion sickness, blurred vision, and reduced vigilance. **Hand-Arm Vibration (HAV):** HAV is mechanical oscillation transmitted through hand-held power tools — grinders, jackhammers, chain saws, riveting hammers. The frequency range is typically **20 to 1500 Hz**. Long-term exposure causes **Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome (HAVS)**, which has three components: - **Vascular:** Vibration White Finger (VWF) — episodic finger blanching. - **Neurological:** Loss of tactile sensation, tingling. - **Musculoskeletal:** Reduced grip strength, joint pain. **Control Measures per ISO 2631 / 5349:** - **Limit daily exposure time** A(8) ≤ 0.5 m/s² (WBV) / 2.5 m/s² (HAV). - **Anti-vibration gloves** and anti-vibration mountings on tools. - **Suspension seats** for vehicle operators. - **Job rotation** to limit individual exposure. - **Health surveillance**: pre-employment and annual tests for HAVS. - **Tool maintenance** — worn tools vibrate more. --- **b) Numerical Solution:** **Step 1 — Given data:** - Dry bulb $T_{\text{db}} = 38°C$ - Natural wet bulb $T_{\text{nwb}} = 28°C$ - Globe temperature $T_{\text{gt}} = 42°C$ **Step 2 — Apply outdoor WBGT formula:** $$\text{WBGT}_{\text{outdoor}} = 0.7\,T_{\text{nwb}} + 0.2\,T_{\text{gt}} + 0.1\,T_{\text{db}}$$ $$= 0.7 \times 28 + 0.2 \times 42 + 0.1 \times 38$$ $$= 19.6 + 8.4 + 3.8 = \mathbf{31.8°C}$$ **Step 3 — Compare with ACGIH TLV:** | Worker Acclimatization | TLV Limit (moderate workload) | |---|---| | Unacclimatized | 30.0°C | | Acclimatized | 32.5°C | **Step 4 — Conclusion:** - **Acclimatized worker:** WBGT = 31.8°C < 32.5°C → **Work can continue**, but with strict hydration and rest-break protocol. - **Unacclimatized worker:** WBGT = 31.8°C > 30.0°C → **Work must be suspended or limited**, with re-evaluation after 2 hours of rest. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > - **Do NOT confuse** WBGT (°C) with HSI (%). They are different indices. > - **Always state the workload** when interpreting WBGT (light / moderate / heavy). > - **Always state acclimatization status** — it changes the TLV. > - **For indoor WBGT**, use $0.7\,T_{\text{nwb}} + 0.3\,T_{\text{gt}}$ — the dry bulb is excluded. > - **Loses 1 mark** if units are missing in the final answer. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Last-Minute Revision (Read This Before the Exam)** - **Mechanical hazards** = any condition where mechanical energy can transfer to the worker. Seven types: Entanglement, Crushing, Cutting/Shearing, Stabbing, Impact, Drawing-In, Stored Energy. - **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE (top to bottom reliability). - **LOTO** (Lockout–Tagout) is the gold-standard for stored-energy hazards. - **Noise PEL** = 90 dBA (8-hour TWA). **5 dB exchange rate** (halving time). - **Combined noise** for $n$ equal sources: $L_{\text{total}} = L_1 + 10\log_{10}(n)$. - **Every +3 dB doubles intensity** — but the ear perceives +10 dB as "twice as loud." - **Vibration**: WBV = whole-body (forklift, crane); HAV = hand-arm (tools). TLV for WBV ≈ 0.5 m/s² (8-h TWA). - **WBGT (outdoor)** = $0.7\,T_{\text{nwb}} + 0.2\,T_{\text{gt}} + 0.1\,T_{\text{db}}$; **(indoor)** = $0.7\,T_{\text{nwb}} + 0.3\,T_{\text{gt}}$. - **HSI (Belding)** > 100% → heat-stroke risk. - **Illumination (lux)** minimums: General 50 lux, Control Room 300 lux, Precision 500–1000 lux (per IS 3646). - **Dilution ventilation** for airborne contaminants: $Q = (G \times 10^6) / (C_{\text{TLV}} - C_0)$. - **Falling-object KE**: $E = mgh$; **impact velocity** $v = \sqrt{2gh}$. - **Indian Factories Act** sections 87 (lighting), 88 (ventilation), 89 (noise) cover environmental hazards. - **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95** governs industrial noise exposure; **ACGIH TLV booklet** is the global reference. - **Always** express final answers with **units** — examiners are ruthless about missing units.

topics.title: Prevention Techniques for Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Prevention Techniques for Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prevention Techniques for Hazards** are systematic engineering, operational, and managerial strategies designed to *eliminate* or *reduce the likelihood* of process deviations, equipment failures, and human errors that could lead to hazardous events in chemical and process plants. Prevention operates **upstream** of mitigation — it stops incidents before they initiate, unlike control/mitigation which limits consequences once an event has begun.

In the context of KTU PECHT743, prevention techniques are categorized under the **Hierarchy of Controls**:

$$
\text{Elimination} > \text{Substitution} > \text{Engineering Controls} > \text{Administrative Controls} > \text{PPE}
$$

The hierarchy is *inverted* in effectiveness — elimination is the most effective, PPE the least.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 2):**
> Students must be able to differentiate between *prevention* and *protection/mitigation*, list the four pillars of inherent safety (Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify), describe plant layout principles, and apply the principles of *Inherently Safer Design (ISD)* as articulated by **Trevor Kletz** in his foundational work.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine driving a car on a hilly, winding mountain road:

- **Elimination of the hazard**: Don't drive on that road. (E.g., removing a flammable solvent from a process entirely.)
- **Substitution**: Drive on a better, safer highway. (E.g., using water-based paint instead of solvent-based.)
- **Engineering Control**: Install guardrails, anti-lock brakes, and airbags. (E.g., rupture discs, relief valves, dikes.)
- **Administrative Control**: Drive slowly, follow speed limit signs. (E.g., Standard Operating Procedures, Permit-to-Work systems.)
- **PPE**: Wear a seatbelt and helmet. (E.g., respirators, hard hats.)

**The "Why"**: Prevention is exponentially cheaper than mitigation. The **TCE (Total Cost of an Event)** study by the Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS) and **Marsh & McLennan** shows that for every **$1** spent on prevention, **$10–$100** is saved in incident response, downtime, legal liability, and reputational damage.

## 1.3 Core Metrics in Process Safety Prevention

- **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis):** Determines the number of independent protection layers (IPLs) needed.
- **SIL (Safety Integrity Level):** Ranges from **SIL 1 to SIL 4** per **IEC 61511**.
- **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand):** Target values: SIL 1 ≥ **10⁻² to 10⁻¹**, SIL 4 ≥ **10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁴**.
- **Inherent Safety Score (ISI):** Kletz's qualitative/quantitative ranking metric.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Inherently Safer Design (ISD)

> Trevor Kletz (1922–2013), the "father of inherent safety," proposed **four** principles, later extended to include **Simplify** (CCPS):

### 2.1.1 Minimize (Intensification)

Reduce the inventory of hazardous materials to the smallest amount necessary.

- Use **microreactors**, **continuous flow** instead of batch.
- Reduce pipe diameters; minimize storage tank volumes.
- Example: Replacing a **10,000 L** solvent storage tank with on-demand **100 L** day tanks.

### 2.1.2 Substitute

Replace a hazardous substance with a less hazardous one that performs the same function.

- Replace **benzene** (carcinogen) with **cyclohexane** or **toluene**.
- Replace **chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs)** with **hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs)**.
- Replace **HF (hydrofluoric acid)** alkylation with **solid acid / ionic liquid** catalysis.

### 2.1.3 Moderate (Attenuation)

Use less hazardous process conditions — lower temperatures, lower pressures, less reactive forms.

- Use **aqueous suspensions** instead of dry powders.
- Operate at **atmospheric pressure** instead of vacuum/compression.
- Refrigerate below the flash point of flammables.
- Dilute strong oxidizers.

### 2.1.4 Simplify

Design processes that are forgiving, with fewer opportunities for error.

- Eliminate complex sequential valving.
- Use **gravity feed** instead of pumps.
- Design **fail-safe** valves (air-to-open, spring-to-close).
- Reduce number of instruments and connections.

### 2.1.5 Eliminate (Avoidance)

Remove the hazard entirely.

- Use mechanical cleaning instead of solvent cleaning.
- Eliminate intermediate storage of unstable chemicals.
- Example: The **Bhopal Disaster (1984)** could have been prevented if MIC (Methyl Isocyanate) storage had been eliminated by immediate consumption in the process.

## 2.2 Engineering Controls for Hazard Prevention

| Technique | Purpose | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Bunds / Dikes** | Containment of spills | Dyke around bulk fuel storage (110% of largest tank volume) |
| **Double-walled tanks** | Prevent leak to environment | Underground petrol station tanks |
| **Ventilation (LEL < 25%)** | Prevent flammable atmospheres | Paint spray booths |
| **Inerting (Purging)** | Reduce O₂ below LOC | Nitrogen blanketing of reactors |
| **Grounding & Bonding** | Prevent static discharge | Tank truck loading/unloading |
| **Explosion-proof equipment** | Contain ignition sources | ATEX/IECEx rated motors |
| **SIS (Safety Instrumented Systems)** | Automatic emergency shutdown | Emergency Shutdown (ESD) valves on offshore platforms |

## 2.3 Administrative & Procedural Controls

- **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)** — written, validated instructions.
- **Permit-to-Work (PTW)** systems — for hot work, confined space, working at heights.
- **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)** — energy isolation during maintenance.
- **Management of Change (MOC)** — formal review before any process/equipment change.
- **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** — before commissioning modified plants.
- **Operator Training & Competency Assurance.**

## 2.4 Plant Layout & Siting (Per API RP 752 / NFPA 30)

> [!NOTE]
> The **API RP 752** standard defines three concentric zones:
> - **Zone 1** (closest, highest occupancy): Control rooms, offices.
> - **Zone 2**: Auxiliary equipment, warehouses.
> - **Zone 3** (farthest, highest hazard): Process units, storage tanks.

Minimum spacing requirements (typical): **15 m** between process units; **60 m** between LPG storage and occupied buildings.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear frequently in KTU university exams.**

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Probability of Failure on Demand | $PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}$ | $\lambda_D$ = dangerous failure rate (hr⁻¹); $T_{proof}$ = proof test interval (hr) |
| Risk Reduction Factor | $RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Safety Integrity Level (SIL) equation | $\frac{1}{RRF_{total}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$ | Cumulative probability for $n$ independent layers |
| Average Frequency of Event | $f_{event} = f_{initiating} \times PFD_{IPLs}$ | Events/year |
| LEL Safety Margin | $\%LEL_{alarm} \leq 25\%$ | OSHA / NFPA recommendation |
| Oxygen Limiting Concentration | $LOC = \frac{Z \cdot p_O_2}{p_{total}}$ | $Z$ = stoichiometric ratio |
| Bund Capacity (API 650) | $V_{bund} \geq 1.10 \cdot V_{largest\,tank}$ | 110% rule for single tank |
| Minimum Safe Distance | $D_{min} = k \cdot \sqrt[3]{m_{explosive}}$ | Cube-root scaling law |
| TNO Multi-Energy blast radius | $R = C \cdot E^{1/3}$ | $E$ = explosion energy (J); $C$ = site factor |
| Ventilation Air Required | $Q = \frac{100 \cdot G_{max}}{24 \cdot LEL \cdot \rho_v}$ | $m^3/s$; for flammable vapors |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry**: Micro-reactor technology reduces hazardous inventory by **99%+**.
- **Offshore oil & gas**: Subsea production systems eliminate topside hazardous inventories.
- **Refineries**: FCCU (Fluid Catalytic Cracking Unit) shutdown systems use **SIL 3** SIS.
- **Semiconductor manufacturing**: Replacement of **perchloroethylene** with aqueous cleaning agents reduces chronic exposure risks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis) Equation

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

Determine the total **PFD** (Probability of Failure on Demand) of a safety instrumented function composed of *n* independent protection layers, given the individual failure rates.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Let us define:

- $f_i$ = initiating event frequency (events/year)
- $PFD_i$ = probability of failure on demand of the $i$-th independent protection layer (dimensionless, between 0 and 1)

Each IPL is independent. The conditional probability that **all** IPLs fail simultaneously is the product of individual failure probabilities (because they are statistically independent events):

$$
P_{total\,failure} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i
$$

The **Risk Reduction Factor** is the reciprocal:

$$
RRF_{total} = \frac{1}{\prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i}
$$

The mitigated event frequency is:

$$
f_{mitigated} = f_{i} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i
$$

Taking the logarithm (useful for safety integrity target calculations):

$$
\log_{10}\left(f_{mitigated}\right) = \log_{10}(f_i) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log_{10}(PFD_i)
$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A runaway reactor has an initiating frequency of $f_i = 10^{-1}$ /year. There are **3 IPLs**: a Basic Process Control System (BPCS) with $PFD_1 = 10^{-1}$, an alarm + operator intervention with $PFD_2 = 10^{-1}$, and a SIL 2 relief valve with $PFD_3 = 10^{-2}$. Calculate the mitigated event frequency and the required SIL to meet a tolerable frequency of $10^{-5}$/year.

**Step 1:** Compute cumulative PFD.

$$
PFD_{total} = PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \times PFD_3 = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} = 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute mitigated frequency.

$$
f_{mitigated} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-4} = 10^{-5}\,/\text{year}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with tolerable target.

$$
f_{mitigated} = 10^{-5}/\text{year} = f_{tolerable} = 10^{-5}/\text{year}
$$

**Conclusion:** The existing protection layers *just meet* the tolerable target — no additional IPL needed.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Bund Capacity Equation (API 650)

For a **single** storage tank within a bund, the bund must contain **110%** of the largest tank's volume to allow for foam expansion and sloshing:

$$
V_{bund} \geq 1.10 \cdot V_{tank,\,max}
$$

For **multiple** tanks, the rule is the larger of:

- **110%** of the largest tank volume, OR
- **25%** of the aggregate volume of all tanks within the bund

$$
V_{bund} \geq \max\left(1.10 \cdot V_{max},\ 0.25 \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{N} V_j\right)
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the LOC (Limiting Oxygen Concentration)

The **LOC** is the maximum oxygen concentration below which combustion cannot occur, even with a stoichiometric mixture of fuel:

$$
LOC = \frac{Z \cdot p_{O_2,\,stoich}}{p_{total}}
$$

Where:

- $Z$ = stoichiometric oxygen-to-fuel molar ratio
- $p_{O_2,\,stoich}$ = partial pressure of oxygen in the stoichiometric reaction
- $p_{total}$ = absolute total pressure

For **methane combustion in air** at 1 atm:

$$
CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O
$$

Stoichiometric $O_2$ in air = **20.95%**; stoichiometric ratio $Z \approx 2$.

$$
LOC = \frac{2 \times 0.2095}{1} = 0.419 \approx 12\%\, O_2 \text{ (approx. measured value)}
$$

Inerting systems aim to keep $O_2 < LOC$ — typically **< 8%** for organic solvents.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Prevention vs. Protection

| Parameter | Prevention (Upstream) | Protection/Mitigation (Downstream) |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Stop the initiating event | Limit the consequences |
| **Cost** | Lower (designed-in) | Higher (added equipment) |
| **Reliability** | Inherent (independent of operators) | Requires testing & maintenance |
