seek immediate ophthalmologist - **Ingestion** → Do NOT induce vomiting unless specifically told; give water/milk if conscious; never give anything by mouth to an unconscious person - **Antidotes** (if any) — e.g., atropine for organophosphates, calcium gluconate for HF ### Block 7 — Environmental Precautions - Whether the chemical is **aquatic toxic** - Whether to **dyke** the spill - Whether to inform **Pollution Control Board** (in India, the **State Pollution Control Board – SPCB**) - **Biotreatment** compatibility ### Block 8 — Storage & Handling Precautions During Transit - Keep container **upright**, in a **well-ventilated** area - Avoid **direct sunlight** and temperatures > **flash point − 5 °C** - **Segregate** from incompatible materials (oxidizers from organics, acids from bases, etc.) ### Block 9 — Manufacturer / Supplier Contact - 24 × 7 **emergency telephone number** - Name and address of manufacturer - Name and address of **Poison Control Centre** reference ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet Since TREM card is a **documentation / regulatory safety topic** rather than a numerical computation topic, the "formula" here is the **information architecture**. The high-yield content is summarized below: | Section # | Block Name | Key Content Element | KTU Exam Tag | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Material Identification | **UN Number, CAS Number, Hazard Class** | Very high yield | | 2 | Nature of Hazard | **GHS pictograms + primary/secondary hazard** | High yield | | 3 | PPE for Driver | **SCBA, chemical-resistant gloves, goggles** | Moderate | | 4 | Spillage Action | **Containment vs dispersion, neutralizing agent** | High yield | | 5 | Fire Action | **Suitable media + DO NOT list (e.g., no water jet)** | High yield | | 6 | First Aid | **Inhalation, skin, eye, ingestion, antidote** | Very high yield | | 7 | Environmental | **Aquatic toxicity, dyking, SPCB notification** | Moderate | | 8 | Storage in Transit | **Segregation, ventilation, temperature** | Moderate | | 9 | Contact | **24×7 emergency phone, Poison Control** | Moderate | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world engineering utility:** > - TREM cards are the **first line of emergency communication** in the **"Chain of Survival"** during a chemical transport accident. > - They bridge the **knowledge gap** between the chemical plant's process safety engineer (who knows the chemical intimately) and the **first responder** (who is often a fireman with no chemistry background). > - Globally, the **UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods** (the "Orange Book") and the **ADR** (European Agreement concerning the International Carriage of Dangerous Goods by Road) have standardized TREM card templates under ADR Section 5.4.3. ## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Standardized Format A TREM card's value comes from **uniformity**. If a fireman in Kerala sees a TREM card and a fireman in Geneva sees a TREM card, both should: 1. **Identify the chemical** within 30 seconds. 2. **Recognize the hazard pictogram** within 10 seconds. 3. **Know whether to use water or not** within 15 seconds. 4. **Reach the emergency phone** within 20 seconds. This **uniformity is the engineering design principle** behind the 9-block format. Without it, a panicked first responder would have to read a 16-section SDS (Safety Data Sheet) — clearly impossible at the scene of an overturned tanker. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Framework — How a TREM Card Is Generated The following structured derivation explains the **logical flow** that a process plant safety officer follows when generating a TREM card. Although it is a documentation task, the workflow itself is highly procedural and has been asked in 14-mark KTU questions. > [!NOTE] > The "derivation" here is a **6-step procedural logic** rather than a mathematical one. This is intentional and aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme's emphasis on **Apply / Analyze** level questions. ### Step 1 — Identify the Substance and Assign UN Number $$\text{UN Number} = f(\text{Chemical Identity, Hazard Class, Packing Group})$$ The manufacturer consults the **UN Dangerous Goods List** (the "UN Orange Book," Chapter 3.2). For example, acrylonitrile has: - UN Number: **UN 1091** - Hazard Class: **3** (Flammable Liquid), subsidiary **6.1** (Toxic) - Packing Group: **I** (high danger) > **Logic:** The UN number is a 4-digit international code, mutually intelligible across all transport modes. Without it, the TREM card cannot function as a cross-border emergency tool. ### Step 2 — Determine the Primary and Secondary Hazards $$\text{Hazard Profile} = \{ H_1, H_2, H_3, \dots, H_n \}$$ where $H_i$ represents the $i^{\text{th}}$ independent hazard. For acrylonitrile: $$H_{\text{acrylonitrile}} = \{\text{Flammable}, \text{Toxic by inhalation}, \text{Possible carcinogen}, \text{Reactive with strong oxidizers}\}$$ > **Logic:** A substance can have **multiple simultaneous hazards** (e.g., acrylonitrile is BOTH flammable AND toxic). The TREM card must list them in **descending order of immediacy to life**. ### Step 3 — Map the Hazards to GHS Pictograms Each $H_i$ is mapped to a **GHS Hazard Pictogram** (a red-bordered diamond with a black symbol on a white background): | Pictogram | GHS Code | Meaning | |---|---|---| | Flame | GHS 02 | Flammable | | Skull & Crossbones | GHS 06 | Acute Toxicity | | Health Hazard | GHS 08 | Carcinogen / Mutagen | | Corrosion | GHS 05 | Skin/Eye Corrosion | $$\text{Selected Pictograms} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \text{Pictogram}(H_i)$$ ### Step 4 — Specify the PPE Matrix The PPE is selected based on the **highest exposure route** (inhalation, skin, eye, ingestion): $$\text{PPE}_{\text{set}} = \{\text{Gloves}_{\text{nitrile}}, \text{SCBA}, \text{Goggles}_{\text{chem}}, \text{Apron}, \text{Boots}\}$$ > **Logic:** The PPE specification is **chemical-specific**. Aqueous chemicals require different gloves than hydrocarbon solvents. The selection follows the **breakthrough time** and **permeation rate** data from the glove manufacturer's chart. ### Step 5 — Write the Emergency Action Sequence (Spillage, Fire, First Aid) This is the **most critical block**. The action follows a **decision tree**: $$ \text{Action} = \begin{cases} \text{Contain + Absorb (sand/vermiculite)} & \text{if liquid, immiscible with water, no water reaction} \\ \text{Dilute + Flush (water spray)} & \text{if miscible with water, non-toxic, no violent reaction} \\ \text{Neutralize} & \text{if acid/alkali spill with known neutralizer} \\ \text{Vent + Isolate} & \text{if LPG / cryogenic / highly volatile} \\ \text{DO NOT use water} & \text{if reacts violently with water (e.g., Na, PCl}_3 \text{)} \\ \text{Allow to burn out} & \text{if uncontained pool fire, no life threat} \end{cases} $$ > **Logic:** The decision tree is **derived from** the **Reactivity / Compatibility Chart** of the chemical. The TREM card operator must consult the chemical's SDS Section 10 (Reactivity) to populate this block correctly. ### Step 6 — Compile, Translate, Print, and Distribute The final TREM card is: - Printed in **A6 size** for in-cabin use - **Laminated** to survive rain, oil, and rough handling - Translated into the **local language** alongside English - Distributed as **2 copies minimum** (1 with driver, 1 with consignor) - Re-issued whenever the **chemical formulation** changes ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — TREM CARD vs SDS vs Hazchem Code This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**. Students are often asked: "How is a TREM card different from an SDS?" or "Compare TREM card and Hazchem code." The full comparative derivation is presented below: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Document Comparison} = \{ \text{Attribute}_1, \text{Attribute}_2, \dots, \text{Attribute}_k \} \end{aligned} $$ | Attribute | **TREM CARD** | **SDS (Safety Data Sheet)** | **Hazchem Code** | |---|---|---|---| | **Full form** | Transport Emergency Card | Safety Data Sheet | Hazardous Chemical Code | | **Purpose** | Emergency action at scene | Comprehensive hazard reference | Fire-service action code (UK/Aus) | | **Audience** | Driver, fireman, police | Process engineer, safety officer | Fire brigade | | **Length** | 1–2 pages | 16 sections, 8–12 pages | A 2–4 character alphanumeric code | | **Issued by** | Manufacturer/consignor | Manufacturer/supplier | UN-based coding authority | | **Mandatory?** | Yes (CMVR Rule 137 in India) | Yes (REACH / GHS) | Yes (UK Hazchem system) | | **Format** | 9-block standardized | 16-section (GHS) | Color + number + letter code | | **Information density** | High-level, action-oriented | Exhaustive, technical | Minimal, action-coded | | **Carried in vehicle?** | Yes (mandatory) | No (kept at plant/office) | Sometimes, on the vehicle | | **Content style** | Imperative ("DO...", "DO NOT...") | Descriptive | Coded symbols | | **Update frequency** | When formulation changes | Every 3 years or on change | Static | | **Hazard pictograms** | GHS pictograms | GHS pictograms | Color-coded panels | > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The TREM card is **NOT a substitute** for the SDS. It is a **condensed, action-oriented subset** of the SDS, designed for the *first 30 minutes* of an emergency response. The SDS remains the comprehensive reference document. ## 3.3 The Hazchem Code — A Companion to TREM Card The **Hazchem code** is a complementary system used in the UK, Australia, and some Indian transport contracts. It is a **2 to 4 character code** (e.g., **"3WE"**) that the fire brigade uses to decide on: - **First digit (1–4):** Type of fire-fighting media (1 = water jets, 2 = water fog, 3 = foam, 4 = dry agent) - **Second letter (P, R, S, T, W, X, Y, Z):** Personal protection required (P = full chemical protective clothing + BA, W = contain spillage) - **Third letter (E):** Whether to evacuate the public (E = yes, evacuate; if absent = do not evacuate) $$\text{Example Hazchem Code: } \textbf{3WE}$$ - $\textbf{3}$ = Foam is the appropriate medium - $\textbf{W}$ = Contain spillage (do not let it enter drains) - $\textbf{E}$ = Evacuate the public immediately The Hazchem code is **placarded** on the tanker and **also printed on the TREM card** for cross-reference. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a TREM Card Document ```mermaid flowchart TD A[TREM CARD
Transport Emergency Card] --> B[Block 1
Material Identification] A --> C[Block 2
Nature of Hazard] A --> D[Block 3
PPE for Driver] A --> E[Block 4
Spillage Action] A --> F[Block 5
Fire Action] A --> G[Block 6
First Aid] A --> H[Block 7
Environmental] A --> I[Block 8
Storage in Transit] A --> J[Block 9
Manufacturer Contact] B --> B1[Chemical Name] B --> B2[UN Number] B --> B3[CAS Number] B --> B4[Hazard Class] C --> C1[GHS Pictograms] C --> C2[Primary Hazard] C --> C3[Secondary Hazard] D --> D1[SCBA Required] D --> D2[Chemical Gloves] D --> D3[Splash Goggles] D --> D4[Apron and Boots] E --> E1{Chemical
Reactive?} E1 -->|Yes| E2[No Water Use
Contain with Sand] E1 -->|No| E3[Dilute and Disperse
with Water Spray] F --> F1[Suitable Extinguishing Media] F --> F2[Cool Adjacent Containers] F --> F3[Personal Protection] G --> G1[Inhalation Response] G --> G2[Skin Contact Response] G --> G3[Eye Contact Response] G --> G4[Ingestion Response] G --> G5[Antidote Information] ``` ## 4.2 Emergency Response Flowchart — When a TREM Card Is Activated ```mermaid flowchart TD START([Chemical Transport Accident Occurs]) --> S1[Driver Stops Vehicle
in Safe Location] S1 --> S2[Driver Retrieves TREM CARD
from In-Cabin Holder] S2 --> S3[Identify UN Number
and Chemical Name] S3 --> S4{Assess
Type of Emergency} S4 -->|Spill Leak| PATH1[Follow Block 4
Spillage Action] S4 -->|Fire| PATH2[Follow Block 5
Fire Action] S4 -->|Human Exposure| PATH3[Follow Block 6
First Aid] S4 -->|Multi Hazard| PATH4[Activate Combined
Protocol] PATH1 --> ALERT[Call 24x7 Emergency
Number Block 9] PATH2 --> ALERT PATH3 --> ALERT PATH4 --> ALERT ALERT --> NOTIFY[Notify Local Fire Brigade
and Police] NOTIFY --> EVAC[Evacuate Public
if Hazchem Code E] EVAC --> RESPOND[Specialized Hazmat Team
Takes Over] RESPOND --> END([Incident Contained
and Documented]) ``` ## 4.3 Hierarchical Information Architecture — TREM Card vs SDS vs Hazchem ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph L1[Plant Level] SDS[Safety Data Sheet
16 Sections, 8-12 pages
Comprehensive Reference] end subgraph L2[Transport Level] TREM[TREM CARD
2-3 Pages, 9 Blocks
Action Oriented] end subgraph L3[Vehicle Placard Level] HAZCHEM[Hazchem Code
2-4 Characters
Fire Brigade Quick Code] end SDS --> TREM TREM --> HAZCHEM SDS -.->|Supports| HAZCHEM ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Guide:** Notice the **top-down compression** of information. The SDS is a 12-page encyclopedia; the TREM card distils it into 2 action-oriented pages; the Hazchem code compresses the TREM card further into a 4-character string. Each level sacrifices detail for **response speed**. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question Paper]** **Define TREM CARD. Mention its purpose and the issuing authority.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** TREM CARD is the abbreviation for **Transport Emergency Card**. It is a standardized, pocket-sized information document carried in the cabin of vehicles transporting hazardous chemicals. - **Purpose (1 mark):** It provides the **driver and first responders** (firemen, police, paramedics) with rapid, life-saving instructions on how to manage a chemical transport accident (spill, leak, fire, or human exposure). - **Issuing Authority (1 mark):** The card is issued by the **chemical manufacturer or consignor**, based on standardized templates developed by **CEFIC** (European Chemical Industry Council) / **Indian Chemical Council (ICC)**, and is mandatory under **CMVR Rule 137** and the **MSIHC Rules, 1989** in India. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper]** **List any six standard contents / blocks of a TREM CARD.** **Model Answer (3 marks — list 6):** 1. **Material Identification** — chemical name, UN number, CAS number, hazard class 2. **Nature of Hazard** — GHS pictograms, primary and secondary hazards 3. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — SCBA, gloves, goggles, apron 4. **Spillage Action** — containment, neutralization, do-not-use list 5. **Fire-Fighting Action** — suitable media, cooling of adjacent containers 6. **First Aid** — inhalation, skin, eye, ingestion response *(Any six standard blocks of the 9 — full marks.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 2 Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **"TREM CARD is the lifeline of a chemical transport emergency." With respect to this statement,** **(a)** Describe the standard structure of a TREM CARD with all nine blocks. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast TREM CARD with Safety Data Sheet (SDS) and Hazchem code. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks **Introduction (1 mark):** A TREM CARD is a Transport Emergency Card, a standardized 2–3 page document that travels with every consignment of hazardous chemicals. It is the **"first responder's cheat sheet"** during a transport accident. **The 9 Blocks (6 marks, approx. 0.5–1 mark per block):** 1. **Block 1 – Material Identification:** Chemical name (IUPAC + trade), UN number (e.g., UN 1091 for acrylonitrile), CAS number, hazard class (Class 3 = flammable liquid, Class 6.1 = toxic, Class 8 = corrosive), packing group (I/II/III). 2. **Block 2 – Nature of Hazard:** GHS pictograms displayed prominently. Primary hazard (the dominant risk — e.g., flammability) and secondary hazard (e.g., toxicity for acrylonitrile). A short narrative like "Toxic if inhaled; flammable vapours heavier than air." 3. **Block 3 – Personal Protective Equipment:** Driver-level PPE for incidental contact: chemical-resistant gloves (nitrile for hydrocarbons, butyl for aromatics), full chemical splash goggles, full-face shield, chemical-resistant apron, closed-toe boots, hard hat. SCBA is recommended for any spill response. 4. **Block 4 – Spillage Action:** Imperative instructions. Examples: - *"Eliminate all ignition sources."* - *"Contain spillage with sand or vermiculite."* - *"DO NOT allow chemical to enter drains, sewers, or watercourses."* - Decision tree: contain vs disperse vs neutralize. 5. **Block 5 – Fire Action:** Suitable extinguishing media (CO₂, dry chemical, alcohol-resistant foam, water spray). DO NOT list (e.g., "DO NOT use water jet — may spread fire"). Special warnings: "Heating may cause violent rupture," "Emits toxic fumes (phosgene, HCN)." 6. **Block 6 – First Aid:** Exposure-route-specific response. - **Inhalation** → Move to fresh air; administer oxygen; artificial respiration if not breathing. - **Skin** → Flood with water for ≥ 15 minutes; remove contaminated clothing. - **Eye** → Rinse with water for ≥ 15 minutes; seek ophthalmologist. - **Ingestion** → DO NOT induce vomiting unless specified; give water/milk if conscious; never give anything orally to an unconscious person. - **Antidote** (e.g., atropine for organophosphates, calcium gluconate for HF burns). 7. **Block 7 – Environmental Precautions:** Aquatic toxicity statement. Whether to dyke the spill. Notification to State Pollution Control Board (SPCB). Biotreatment compatibility. 8. **Block 8 – Storage & Handling in Transit:** Keep upright; well-ventilated; away from heat, sunlight, and incompatible materials. Segregation table reference (e.g., oxidizers away from organics, acids away from bases). 9. **Block 9 – Manufacturer / Supplier Contact:** 24 × 7 emergency telephone, name and address of manufacturer, reference to the nearest Poison Control Centre. **Conclusion (0 marks — covered above):** The 9-block structure ensures **uniformity, speed, and completeness** of emergency information at the accident site. #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks **Comparative Matrix (Full Table, 5 marks):** | Attribute | **TREM CARD** | **SDS** | **Hazchem Code** | |---|---|---|---| | Purpose | Emergency action at scene | Comprehensive hazard reference | Fire-brigade quick action code | | Audience | Driver, fireman, police | Process engineer, safety officer | Fire brigade | | Length | 2–3 pages | 8–12 pages | 2–4 characters | | Format | 9 standardized blocks | 16 GHS sections | Alphanumeric code | | Action style | Imperative ("DO...") | Descriptive | Coded symbols | | Carried in vehicle? | Yes (mandatory) | No | Sometimes (placard) | | Pictograms | GHS pictograms | GHS pictograms | Color-coded panels | | Update frequency | On formulation change | Every 3 years or on change | Static | | Statutory basis | CMVR Rule 137, MSIHC | REACH, GHS, OSHA | UK Hazchem (UK/Aus) | **Key Distinctions in Paragraph Form (2 marks):** - The TREM card is a **condensed, action-oriented subset** of the SDS, designed for use in the **first 30 minutes** of a transport emergency. The SDS remains the comprehensive 16-section reference document. - The Hazchem code is a **2–4 character action code** used by the fire brigade, not the driver. It is a **companion** to the TREM card, not a substitute. - TREM cards are **mandated by Indian law** (CMVR + MSIHC Rules), SDS by REACH/GHS, and Hazchem by UK/Australian regulation. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice **"Explain the role of TREM CARD in process plant safety."** **(a)** Discuss the relationship between TREM CARD, MSDS/SDS, and Hazchem code in the overall safety documentation chain. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Taking **acrylonitrile (UN 1091, CAS 107-13-1)** as an example, prepare a sample TREM CARD block-by-block. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks **The Safety Documentation Chain (3 marks):** The chemical hazard information flows through a **3-tier documentation hierarchy**: 1. **Tier 1 – Plant Level: Safety Data Sheet (SDS / MSDS)** - 16 sections, 8–12 pages. - Comprehensive reference: identification, hazards, composition, first-aid, fire-fighting, accidental release, handling, exposure controls, physical/chemical properties, stability, toxicology, ecology, disposal, transport, regulatory, and other. - Kept at the **plant safety office** and the **consignor's premises**. 2. **Tier 2 – Transport Level: TREM CARD** - 9 blocks, 2–3 pages, action-oriented. - Derived **from** the SDS. - Carried **in the cabin** of every transport vehicle. - Bilingual (local language + English). 3. **Tier 3 – Vehicle Placard Level: Hazchem Code + UN Number Placard** - 2–4 character code + UN 4-digit number on a **diamond placard**. - Visible from a distance of **at least 20 metres**. - Used by the fire brigade for **immediate tactical decisions** (media, PPE, evacuation). **Relationship Diagram (2 marks):** $$\text{Plant (SDS)} \xrightarrow{\text{condenses to}} \text{Vehicle (TREM CARD)} \xrightarrow{\text{encodes to}} \text{Placard (Hazchem + UN)}$$ **Engineering Significance (2 marks):** - TREM cards **bridge the knowledge gap** between the chemical engineer (intimate knowledge) and the first responder (no chemistry background). - They are the **only document** that a panicked driver can read in < 30 seconds. - They enable compliance with **CMVR Rule 137** and the **Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991** in India. #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks **Sample TREM CARD for Acrylonitrile (UN 1091):** **Block 1 – Material Identification (1 mark):** - Chemical Name: Acrylonitrile (2-Propenenitrile, Vinyl Cyanide) - UN Number: **UN 1091** - CAS Number: **107-13-1** - Hazard Class: **3 (Flammable Liquid)**, subsidiary **6.1 (Toxic)** - Packing Group: **I (High Danger)** **Block 2 – Nature of Hazard (1 mark):** - GHS Pictograms: **Flame (GHS 02)** + **Skull & Crossbones (GHS 06)** + **Health Hazard (GHS 08)** - Primary Hazard: Highly flammable liquid; flash point −1 °C; vapour heavier than air, can travel to ignition sources. - Secondary Hazard: Toxic by inhalation; possible carcinogen (Group 2B, IARC); may polymerize violently with heat or contaminants. **Block 3 – PPE (1 mark):** - Chemical-resistant **butyl rubber gloves** (not nitrile — acrylonitrile permeates nitrile). - **SCBA** for any spill or fire response. - Full chemical splash **goggles** + face shield. - Chemical-resistant **apron** and **boots**. **Block 4 – Spillage Action (1 mark):** - Eliminate all ignition sources. No smoking. Use intrinsically safe equipment. - **Contain** spill with **sand or vermiculite**; do NOT let it enter drains. - **DO NOT** use sawdust or other combustible absorbents (acrylonitrile is flammable). - Transfer to a labelled, sealed container for disposal. **Block 5 – Fire Action (1 mark):** - **Suitable media:** Alcohol-resistant foam, dry chemical, CO₂. Water spray to cool containers. - **DO NOT** use a direct water jet (will spread the burning liquid). - Special: "Heating may cause violent rupture of containers — cool with water spray from a safe distance." - Vapours are toxic (HCN, NOₓ); fire-fighters must wear SCBA. **Block 6 – First Aid (1 mark):** - **Inhalation:** Move to fresh air; administer oxygen; artificial respiration if not breathing. Get medical help. - **Skin:** Remove contaminated clothing; flood with water for ≥ 15 minutes. - **Eye:** Rinse with water for ≥ 15 minutes; seek ophthalmologist. - **Ingestion:** DO NOT induce vomiting; give water if conscious; immediate medical attention. - **Antidote:** No specific antidote; treatment is supportive. Cyanide poisoning kit (amyl nitrite, sodium thiosulfate) may be used under medical supervision if cyanide is suspected. **Block 7 – Environmental Precautions (0.5 mark):** - Toxic to aquatic life. Dyke the spill. Notify the **State Pollution Control Board (SPCB)**. **Block 8 – Storage in Transit (0.5 mark):** - Keep container **upright, sealed, and cool** (below 25 °C). Ventilated. **Segregate from oxidizers, acids, alkalis, and amines.** Inhibited with MEHQ (monomethyl ether of hydroquinone) to prevent polymerization. **Block 9 – Manufacturer Contact (0 marks — example):** - Acrylonitrile Manufacturer Pvt. Ltd., XYZ Industrial Estate, Kochi, Kerala. - 24 × 7 Emergency Phone: +91-484-XXXXXXX - Poison Control Centre Reference: AIIMS New Delhi (+91-11-2658-9395). **Conclusion:** This sample TREM card demonstrates that the **9-block format is universally applicable**, and the content is **chemical-specific but structurally identical** for every dangerous good. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Writing "TREM card is given by the driver" — WRONG. It is **issued by the chemical manufacturer / consignor**, NOT the driver. The driver only *carries* it. (−1 mark) > 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **TREM card with MSDS/SDS**. They are NOT the same. TREM card is a **2-page action document**; SDS is a **16-section reference**. (−1 to −2 marks) > 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Omitting the **UN Number** in Block 1. The UN number is the **single most important identifier** for cross-border emergency response. (−1 mark) > 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Writing "use water to flush any chemical spill" — this is a **generic, dangerous** statement. Many chemicals (sodium, acid chlorides, alkali metals) **react violently with water**. The TREM card must specify. (−1 to −2 marks) > 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Not mentioning the **bilingual** (local language + English) requirement, which is **mandatory in India** under CMVR Rule 137. (−0.5 to −1 mark) > 6. **Common Mistake #6:** Confusing the **Hazchem code's "E" suffix** as a chemical name or extra information. "E" stands for **"Evacuate the public."** (−0.5 mark) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **TREM CARD = Transport Emergency Card** — full form is the most-frequently asked 2-mark question. - ✅ Issued by the **chemical manufacturer / consignor** — NOT the driver. - ✅ Carried **in the cabin** of every vehicle transporting dangerous goods. - ✅ **Mandatory** in India under **CMVR Rule 137** + **MSIHC Rules, 1989**. - ✅ **9 standardized blocks** — Material ID, Nature of Hazard, PPE, Spillage, Fire, First Aid, Environmental, Storage, Contact. - ✅ Must be **bilingual** — local language + English. - ✅ Minimum **2 copies** per vehicle (1 with driver, 1 at consignor's office). - ✅ **UN Number** is the most important identifier (e.g., UN 1091 = acrylonitrile). - ✅ Action style is **imperative** — "DO…", "DO NOT…", "Avoid…". - ✅ TREM card is **NOT a substitute** for the SDS — it is a condensed, action-oriented subset. - ✅ The **Hazchem code** is a complementary 2–4 character code for fire-brigade use (e.g., 3WE). - ✅ Decision tree for spillage: **contain** vs **disperse** vs **neutralize**, based on water-reactivity. - ✅ **SCBA** is the recommended respiratory protection for any spill/fire response. - ✅ The **Golden Hour** (first 60 minutes) determines containment or catastrophe — TREM cards enable Golden-Hour response. - ✅ Pictograms are **GHS-standardized** (red-bordered diamond, black symbol on white). - ✅ First-aid must cover all 4 routes: **inhalation, skin, eye, ingestion** + **antidote**. - ✅ The TREM card must be **re-issued** whenever the chemical formulation changes. > [!TIP] > **Final 30-Second Recall Mnemonic — "T-R-E-M":** > - **T** = Transport-specific (in-vehicle document) > - **R** = Rapid action (imperative style, 30-second readable) > - **E** = Emergency scope (spill, fire, exposure, environment) > - **M** = Manufacturer-issued (not driver, not transport company)

topics.title: Relief Systems and Detectors
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relief Systems and Detectors

# 1. Relief Systems and Detectors — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Relief Systems — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Relief System (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A *relief system* in a process plant is an engineered assembly of **pressure relief devices (PRDs)** — comprising spring-loaded pressure safety valves (PSVs), rupture discs, pilot-operated valves, and their associated inlet/outlet piping — designed to automatically discharge fluids (gas, vapour, liquid, or multi-phase) when the system pressure exceeds a predetermined *set pressure*, thereby preventing catastrophic mechanical failure of pressure vessels, piping, and connected equipment in accordance with **ASME Section VIII** and **API RP 520/521** standards.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, relief systems form the **last line of passive defence** in the layer-of-protection analysis (LOPA), sitting beneath instrumentation, control loops, and interlocks.

## 1.2 Detectors — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Detector (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A *detector* is a sensing instrument engineered to continuously monitor one or more ambient or process conditions — such as the concentration of flammable gases (% LEL), toxic vapours (ppm), smoke particulates, thermal radiation (kW/m²), or ultraviolet/infrared emissions — and to generate a measurable electrical signal that triggers alarms, automatic shutdowns, fire suppression systems, or emergency ventilation when pre-set threshold values are crossed.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Relief Valve as a "Pressure Escape Hatch":**
> Imagine a pressure cooker whose safety whistle blows when steam pressure becomes excessive. A relief valve does precisely the same job in an industrial reactor: it is a mechanical "emergency exit" that opens automatically when the built-up internal force on a spring-loaded disc exceeds the spring's opposing force, venting the excess fluid to a safe location (flare stack or safe discharge area).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Detector as the Plant's "Nervous System":**
> Think of detectors as the nerve endings of a biological organism. Just as pain receptors in your hand trigger an immediate withdrawal reflex upon touching a hot surface, fixed gas detectors and flame detectors continuously "feel" the plant environment and trigger protective responses (alarms, isolation, deluge) the instant abnormal concentrations are sensed.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Key Thresholds

- **Set Pressure** $\,(P_{set})$ — the inlet pressure at which the valve begins to open. Expressed in **psig** or **barg**.
- **MAWP** *(Maximum Allowable Working Pressure)* — the maximum gauge pressure permissible at the top of the vessel during normal operation.
- **Accumulation Pressure** — pressure allowed during relief, typically **10 % above MAWP** for fire exposure (per ASME code).
- **LEL** *(Lower Explosive Limit)* — minimum concentration of a flammable vapour in air that can ignite. For methane, **LEL = 5 % vol**.
- **UEL** *(Upper Explosive Limit)* — maximum concentration supporting combustion. For methane, **UEL = 15 % vol**.
- **STEL** *(Short-Term Exposure Limit)* — typically **15-minute** time-weighted average exposure.
- **TWA** *(Time-Weighted Average)* — typically **8-hour** occupational exposure limit.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Pressure Relief Devices

Pressure relief devices (PRDs) are classified according to **API RP 520** as follows:

| PRD Type | Reclosure | Operating Principle | Typical Service |
|----------|-----------|---------------------|-----------------|
| **Conventional Relief Valve** | Yes (re-seats after pop) | Spring-loaded disc; lift proportional to overpressure | Clean liquids, vapours |
| **Balanced Bellows Valve** | Yes | Internal bellows cancel back-pressure effects | High back-pressure service |
| **Pilot-Operated Valve (POSV)** | Yes | Pilot valve uses process pressure to operate main disc | High-capacity, low-set-pressure service |
| **Rupture Disc** | No (single-use) | Metal disc bursts at a calibrated pressure | Corrosive, fouling, or polymerising service |
| **Breaking Pin Device** | No | Shear pin fractures at set load | Low-pressure, low-cyclic service |
| **Combined PRD** | Mixed | Rupture disc + relief valve in series | Isolates corrosive fluids from PSV |

## 2.2 Operating Principles of a Spring-Loaded PSV

The valve remains closed as long as the **spring force** exceeds the **upward disc force** exerted by the process fluid.

The **disc-lift equation** at the moment of opening:

$$F_{spring} = F_{disc} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; k \cdot x = P_{set} \cdot A_{disc}$$

where:
- $k$ = spring constant (N/m)
- $x$ = spring compression (m)
- $P_{set}$ = set pressure (Pa)
- $A_{disc}$ = effective disc area (m²)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The set pressure is mechanically "tuned" by adjusting the spring compression. KTU examiners frequently ask why the valve opens at pressures slightly above $P_{set}$ — the answer is the *blowdown*, typically **3 % – 7 %** of set pressure, which prevents chattering.

## 2.3 Sizing Philosophy — API 520/521

API 521 identifies **six design scenarios** for sizing relief devices:

1. **Blocked Outlet** — flow stops downstream; all upstream flow must be relieved.
2. **Fire Exposure** — heat input from external fire causes vaporisation.
3. **Tube Rupture** in heat exchangers.
4. **Cooling/Refrigeration Failure** — runaway exotherm.
5. **Loss of Coolant / Utility Failure** — steam, cooling water, instrument air.
6. **Abnormal Heat Input** from reactor runaway.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = A \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | General orifice discharge equation | m³/s |
| 2 | $W = C \cdot A \cdot P_1 \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{Z \cdot T}}$ | API 520 vapour/gas mass flow | kg/h |
| 3 | $Q = A \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$ | Sub-critical liquid flow | m³/s |
| 4 | $W = A \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho \cdot \Delta P}$ | Liquid mass flow through rupture disc | kg/s |
| 5 | $Q_{fire} = 43200 \cdot F \cdot A^{0.82}$ | API 521 latent heat absorption (English units) | BTU/h |
| 6 | $P_{cf} = P_{set} \cdot (1 + 0.10)$ | Allowable accumulation pressure (10 % overpressure) | Pa |
| 7 | $L_{min} = L_{req} / C_d$ | Required orifice area (vapour/gas) | mm² |
| 8 | $N_{detectors} = \dfrac{A_{coverage}}{A_{detector}}$ | Detector count from coverage geometry | — |

Where:
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (typical 0.62 – 0.97)
- $K_d$ = coefficient for rated relief area (1.0 for rupture disc, 0.975 for PSV)
- $K_b$ = back-pressure correction factor
- $K_c$ = combination correction factor
- $Z$ = compressibility factor
- $M$ = molecular weight
- $F$ = environment factor (1.0 uninsulated, 0.3 insulated)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Relief systems are mandated by the **Indian Boilers Regulations (IBR)**, the **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code Section VIII**, and the **Factories Act 1948 (Section 31)**. Detector networks are required under **IS 15105**, **OISD-105**, and **NFPA 72** for fire detection. Practising engineers without competency in sizing relief valves cannot legally certify pressure vessel designs in India.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Compressible Fluid Mass Flow Equation (API 520 Form)

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Ideal Gas Energy Equation

Consider a relief valve as a nozzle with throat area $A$ and upstream stagnation condition $(P_1, T_1)$. Applying steady-state energy conservation between the upstream and the vena contracta:

$$h_1 + \dfrac{V_1^{\,2}}{2} = h_2 + \dfrac{V_2^{\,2}}{2}$$

Assuming the inlet velocity $V_1 \approx 0$ (large upstream reservoir) and isentropic flow ($h_2 - h_1 = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$):

$$c_p T_1 = c_p T_2 + \dfrac{V_2^{\,2}}{2}$$

Solving for throat velocity gives the classical **isentropic nozzle velocity**:

$$V_2 = \sqrt{2 \, c_p \, T_1 \, \left(1 - \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)}$$

### 3.1.2 Introducing the Isentropic Temperature Ratio

For an ideal gas with $k = c_p / c_v$:

$$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{k-1}{k}}$$

Therefore:

$$V_2 = \sqrt{2 \, c_p \, T_1 \, \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{k-1}{k}}\right]}$$

### 3.1.3 Substituting $c_p = \dfrac{k \, R}{k-1}$ and $R = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{M}$

Here $\bar{R} = 8314\ \text{J/(kmol·K)}$ and $M$ = molecular weight (kg/kmol). Substituting:

$$V_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, k}{k-1} \cdot \dfrac{\bar{R} \, T_1}{M} \cdot \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{k-1}{k}}\right]}$$

### 3.1.4 Mass Flow Rate

Mass flow through the throat area $A$ with density $\rho_2 = \dfrac{P_2 M}{Z \bar{R} T_2}$:

$$\dot{m} = A \, P_2 \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, k \, M}{(k-1) \, Z \, \bar{R} \, T_2} \cdot \left[\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{\frac{2}{k}} - \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{\frac{k+1}{k}}\right]}$$

### 3.1.5 Critical (Choked) Flow Condition

The maximum of the bracket occurs when the **pressure ratio** reaches the critical value:

$$\nu_{crit} = \dfrac{P_{crit}}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\frac{k}{k-1}}$$

For air ($k = 1.4$): $\nu_{crit} = 0.528$. When $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \le \nu_{crit}$, the flow is **choked** and $P_2 = P_{crit}$ regardless of downstream pressure.

### 3.1.6 The API 520 Working Equation

Substituting $\nu_{crit}$ and introducing the empirical corrections $K_d$, $K_b$, $K_c$ and the discharge coefficient (lumped into the certified orifice area $A_e$):

$$W = C \, A_e \, P_1 \, K_d \, K_b \, K_c \, \sqrt{\dfrac{k \, M}{Z \, T_1} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\frac{k+1}{k-1}}}$$

This is the equation the KTU board expects students to write down during a Part B calculation problem.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (API 520 Style)

### Problem Statement

A horizontal cylindrical storage vessel contains **n-butane (M = 58.12 kg/kmol, k = 1.094, Z ≈ 1.0)** at a set pressure of $P_{set} = 6.5$ barg with a maximum allowable accumulation pressure of **10 % overpressure**. The required relief load under blocked-outlet condition is **W = 35,000 kg/h**. Determine the **required certified orifice area** using a conventional spring-loaded PSV. Assume $C = 315$, $K_d = 0.975$, $K_b = 1.0$, $K_c = 1.0$, $T_1 = 333$ K.

### Step 1 — Convert Set Pressure to Absolute

$$P_1 = P_{set} \cdot (1 + 0.10) + 1.013\ \text{bar} = 7.15 + 1.013 = 8.163\ \text{bar} = 8.163 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Coefficient Term Inside the Square Root

$$X = \dfrac{k \, M}{Z \, T_1} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\frac{k+1}{k-1}}$$

$$\dfrac{2}{k+1} = \dfrac{2}{2.094} = 0.9551$$

$$\dfrac{k+1}{k-1} = \dfrac{2.094}{0.094} = 22.277$$

$$0.9551^{22.277} \approx 0.3595$$

$$X = \dfrac{1.094 \cdot 58.12}{1.0 \cdot 333} \cdot 0.3595 = 0.1910 \cdot 0.3595 = 0.0687$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Certified Orifice Area

$$A_e = \dfrac{W}{C \, P_1 \, K_d \, K_b \, K_c \, \sqrt{X}}$$

$$\sqrt{X} = \sqrt{0.0687} = 0.2621$$

$$A_e = \dfrac{35{,}000}{315 \cdot (8.163 \times 10^{5}) \cdot 0.975 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.2621}$$

$$A_e = \dfrac{35{,}000}{6.564 \times 10^{7}} = 5.33 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$$

$$\boxed{A_e = 533\ \text{mm}^2 \;\;(\approx 5.81\ \text{cm}^2)}$$

### Step 4 — Select API Standard Orifice

API 520 standard orifice letter designations:

| API Orifice Letter | Area (mm²) | Area (cm²) |
|--------------------|------------|------------|
| D | 71 | 0.71 |
| E | 130 | 1.30 |
| F | 198 | 1.98 |
| G | 325 | 3.25 |
| **H** | **506** | **5.06** |
| **J** | **830** | **8.30** |

The required area (533 mm²) falls between H and J. **Select API Orifice J (830 mm²)** for conservative design. A **smaller orifice** would lead to undersized relief — a dangerous choice.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix for Detector Selection

| Detector Type | Target Phenomenon | Response Time | False-Alarm Risk | Best Application |
|---------------|-------------------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| Catalytic Bead (Pellistor) | Flammable gas (% LEL) | 10 – 30 s | Moderate (poisoning) | LNG, LPG storage |
| Infrared Point (NDIR) | Hydrocarbon gas | < 5 s | Low | Refineries, offshore |
| Open-Path IR (Line-of-Sight) | Hydrocarbon cloud | 3 – 10 s | Very low | Perimeter monitoring |
| Electrochemical | Toxic gas (H₂S, CO) | 15 – 60 s | Low | Confined-space entry |
| Photo-ionisation (PID) | VOC vapour | < 5 s | Moderate | Solvent-handling plants |
| UV Flame Detector | Hydrocarbon flame | < 1 s | Moderate (solar blind) | Storage tank farms |
| IR³ Flame Detector | Flame with flicker | < 1 s | Very low | Offshore platforms |
| Aspirating Smoke (VESDA) | Smoke particulates | < 5 s | Very low | Control rooms, MCC |
| Heat Detector (Fixed Temp) | High temperature | 30 – 60 s | Low | Cable trays, transformers |

## 3.4 Detector Coverage Geometry

For an open-path IR detector of range $R$ (metres) sweeping an arc of angle $\theta$ (radians):

$$A_{coverage} = \dfrac{\theta \cdot R^{2}}{2}$$

Number of detectors required:

$$N = \left\lceil \dfrac{A_{plant}}{A_{coverage}} \cdot f_{overlap}} \right\rceil$$

with a recommended overlap factor $f_{overlap} \ge 1.2$ to ensure **redundant coverage**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pressure Relief Valve Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Vessel or Line] --> B[Inlet Piping - Full Bore No Restriction]
    B --> C[PSV Inlet - D and greater Pipe Size]
    C --> D[Sensing Element - Disc and Spring]
    D --> E[Adjusting Ring - Blowdown Control]
    E --> F[Disc Lifts - Pop Open Action]
    F --> G[Orifice Area - API Letter D to T]
    G --> H[Discharge Piping to Safe Location]
    H --> I[Flare Stack or Safe Vent]
    D --> J[SPRING - Adjusts Set Pressure]
    J --> D
    D --> K[Disc Reseats at Blowdown Pressure]
    K --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Combined Rupture Disc + PSV Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vessel] --> B[Isolating Rupture Disc]
    B --> C[Disc Holder and Spacer]
    C --> D[Pressure Safety Valve PSV]
    D --> E[Discharge to Flare]
    B -.->|Leaks through Disc| F[Disc Holder Drains to Safe Area]
    F --> G[Atmosphere or Drain Pot]
    D --> H[PSV Inlet is Isolated from Process]
    H --> D
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Detector Hierarchy and Actuation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plant Atmosphere] --> B[Detector Network Layer]
    B --> B1[Combustible Gas Detector]
    B --> B2[Toxic Gas Detector]
    B --> B3[Flame Detector IR and UV]
    B --> B4[Heat and Smoke Detector]
    B1 --> C[Signal Conditioning Module]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[Safety Shutdown Logic Solver SIS]
    D --> E[Alarm and Annunciation]
    D --> F[Emergency Block Valves]
    D --> G[Fire Suppression and Deluge]
    D --> H[Ventilation Override]
    D --> I[Plant Shutdown Trip]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Flow Diagram — API 521 Relief Sizing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Overpressure Cause] --> B{Is Cause Known?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Select API 521 Design Case]
    B -->|No| D[Conduct HAZOP and LOPA Study]
    C --> E1[Blocked Outlet Case]
    C --> E2[External Fire Case]
    C --> E3[Tube Rupture Case]
    C --> E4[Utility Failure Case]
    C --> E5[Reactor Runaway Case]
    E1 --> F[Compute W_required]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G[Compute Required Orifice Area A_e]
    G --> H[Select Next Standard API Orifice]
    H --> I[Verify Reaction Forces and Piping]
    I --> J[Final Relief System Design]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Detector Coverage Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Detector Mount Point at Elevation H] --> B[Beam Sweeps Ground Level]
    B --> C[Coverage Footprint Width 2R]
    C --> D[Number of Beams N]
    D --> E[Total Coverage Area]
    E --> F[Compare with Plant Hazardous Area]
    F --> G{Sufficient Coverage with 20 percent Overlap?}
    G -->|No| H[Add More Detector Beams]
    H --> D
    G -->|Yes| I[Detector Layout Approved]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*Define the term "set pressure" as applied to a pressure relief valve. How does it differ from accumulation pressure and MAWP?*

**Model Answer:**

> **Set Pressure** is the inlet gauge pressure at which a pressure relief valve begins to open and deliver a measurable discharge. It is the first design parameter selected during relief sizing.
>
> **MAWP (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure)** is the maximum gauge pressure permissible at the top of the vessel under design conditions as stamped on the ASME nameplate.
>
> **Accumulation Pressure** is the pressure reached during relief, equal to *set pressure + overpressure*. For non-fire scenarios, the code permits **10 %** overpressure above MAWP; for fire exposure cases, **21 %** is permitted for single PSVs.
>
> The hierarchy is: **MAWP < Set Pressure < Accumulation Pressure**.

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*List the four major categories of fire detectors used in process plants. State the principle of operation of any one.*

**Model Answer:**

The four major categories are:

1. **Heat detectors** — operate on rate-of-temperature rise or fixed-temperature threshold using a bimetallic strip or fusible alloy.
2. **Smoke detectors** — ionisation chamber or photoelectric scattering of smoke particulates.
3. **Flame detectors** — detect UV or IR emissions from hydrocarbon flames; UV uses a phototube (solar blind), IR³ uses flicker-frequency analysis.
4. **Gas detectors** — catalytic bead, infrared absorption, or electrochemical cells.

**Principle of Catalytic Bead Detector:** Two matched pellets (one active, one reference) are wired in a Wheatstone bridge. Flammable gas oxidises catalytically on the active pellet, raising its temperature and resistance; the imbalance produces an output voltage proportional to the gas concentration (% LEL).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Full-Length 14-Mark Question

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2]`**

**(a)** Classify pressure relief devices with neat sketches and explain the working of a **conventional spring-loaded relief valve**. Discuss the difference between a relief valve and a safety valve. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **API 521 blocked-outlet** and **fire-case** sizing methodologies. State the formulae used. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Classification and Working Principle:**

Pressure Relief Devices are classified under **API RP 520** as:

| Type | Reclosing | Service |
|------|-----------|---------|
| Conventional Spring-Loaded Relief Valve | Yes | Clean service |
| Balanced Bellows Relief Valve | Yes | Variable back-pressure |
| Pilot-Operated Safety Valve | Yes | High capacity, low set |
| Rupture Disc Device | No | Corrosive, fouling service |
| Breaking Pin Device | No | Low pressure |
| Combination PRD | Mixed | Corrosive upstream + clean downstream |

**Working of Conventional Spring-Loaded Relief Valve:**

The valve consists of a **disc** held against a **nozzle** by a **helical spring**. Process pressure acts upward on the disc area $A_d$. When process force $P \cdot A_d$ exceeds the spring preload $F_s$, the disc lifts, fluid escapes through the orifice, and pressure falls. When pressure drops to *blowdown pressure* (3–7 % below set), the spring reseats the disc.

**Relief Valve vs Safety Valve:**

| Aspect | Relief Valve | Safety Valve |
|--------|--------------|--------------|
| Service | Liquid service | Gas/vapour service |
| Lift characteristic | Progressive lift | Pop action (sudden) |
| Code | API 526 / ASME | API 526 / ASME |
| Typical application | Hydrocarbon liquids | Steam boilers, gas systems |

**(b) API 521 Sizing Methodologies:**

**Blocked-Outlet Case** — When an outlet valve is inadvertently closed, all upstream feed flow must be relieved. The required relief rate is:

$$W_{BO} = \sum (\text{maximum upstream inflow rates}) - \sum (\text{consumption rates})$$

**Fire Case** — The vessel absorbs heat from external fire. Per API 521:

$$Q = 43200 \cdot F \cdot A^{0.82}$$

where:
- $Q$ = heat input (BTU/h)
- $F$ = environment factor (1.0 uninsulated, 0.3 insulated)
- $A$ = wetted surface area (ft²)

This heat vaporises liquid, generating the relief load:

$$W_{fire} = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda_{latent}}$$

For multi-component mixtures, use the latent heat of the most volatile fraction.

---

### Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2]`**

**(a)** Define **LEL** and **UEL**. With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **catalytic bead combustible-gas detector**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the criteria for **placement of gas detectors** in a process plant. Explain how detector coverage geometry is determined. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) LEL, UEL and Catalytic Bead Detector:**

**LEL (Lower Explosive Limit):** The minimum concentration of a flammable vapour in air below which propagation of a flame does not occur on contact with a source of ignition. For methane, **LEL = 5 % vol**.

**UEL (Upper Explosive Limit):** The maximum concentration of vapour in air above which combustion cannot occur due to insufficient oxygen. For methane, **UEL = 15 % vol**.

The **flammable range** is the interval between LEL and UEL.

**Working of Catalytic Bead Detector:**

A pair of matched electrically heated platinum coils is mounted in a Wheatstone bridge. One coil (active) is impregnated with a palladium catalyst; the other (reference) is inert. When flammable gas contacts the active coil, oxidation raises its temperature and electrical resistance. The resulting bridge imbalance produces a millivolt signal proportional to gas concentration, typically read in **% LEL**.

**(b) Detector Placement and Coverage Geometry:**

**Placement Criteria:**

1. **Vapour density consideration** — detectors for gases denser than air (LPG, butane) are mounted within **30 cm of grade**; for lighter-than-air gases (methane, hydrogen) at **ceiling level**.
2. **Dominant wind direction** — detectors placed downwind of potential leak sources.
3. **Sources of release** — near flanges, pumps, compressors, valves, and sample points.
4. **Coverage area** — each detector covers a circular footprint of radius $R$ with overlap factor $\ge 1.2$.
5. **Accessibility** — for periodic calibration and bump testing.

**Coverage Geometry Equation:**

For an open-path IR detector of range $R$ and beam angle $\theta$:

$$A_{coverage} = \dfrac{\theta \cdot R^{2}}{2}$$

The required number of detectors is:

$$N = \left\lceil \dfrac{A_{plant} \cdot 1.2}{A_{coverage}} \right\rceil$$

For **point detectors**, coverage is governed by manufacturer-specified radius (typically **5–15 m**) at grade or ceiling depending on gas density.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Relief and Detector Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting absolute pressure** — Students forget to add atmospheric pressure (1.013 bar) to gauge readings, instantly losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing set pressure with MAWP** — Set pressure is *below* accumulation but *above* MAWP; mixing them up indicates conceptual gap.
> 3. **Using the wrong K-d factors** — $K_d$ is **0.975 for PSVs** and **1.0 for rupture discs**; mixing them produces a 2.5 % error.
> 4. **Oversizing on the lower side** — When required area falls between two standard orifices, the *next larger* orifice **must** be chosen; undersizing violates code.
> 5. **Detector placement at wrong elevation** — Forgetting vapour density (heavier-than-air vs lighter-than-air) is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Ignoring overlap factor** — Coverage calculations must include a **minimum 20 % overlap**; bare division loses process-safety reliability marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Relief valves** are passive mechanical devices; their sizing follows the **API 520/521** framework using the **choked-flow equation** for compressible service.
- The **API certified orifice area** $A_e$ is the *smallest* cross-section in the valve body, not the inlet or outlet pipe area.
- A **set pressure** must always exceed the **MAWP**, but the **accumulation pressure** is the absolute upper limit during relief.
- **Rupture discs** are single-use devices and must be replaced after each activation; they offer **tight isolation** but **zero resealing**.
- **Detector placement** must respect **vapour density**, **wind direction**, and **leak source proximity** — a detector at the wrong elevation is a wasted instrument.
- **Catalytic bead detectors** are poisoned by **silicones, lead, and sulphur compounds**; **IR detectors** are immune and preferred for harsh environments.
- **Open-path IR detectors** give faster response to large flammable clouds, whereas **point detectors** are best for small local leaks.
- **API 521 fire-case formula** $Q = 43200 \cdot F \cdot A^{0.82}$ applies only when **wetted surface** is exposed to flame — not for vessels with no liquid inventory.
- A relief system must be supplemented by **proper vent piping** routed to a **flare or safe location**; discharging to atmosphere is restricted by the **EPA and CPCB norms**.
- **Detector coverage overlap ≥ 20 %** is the minimum KTU-accepted standard; offshore plants follow **20–25 %** overlap per OISD-105.
- Always distinguish between **% LEL** (combustible) and **ppm** (toxic) when reading detector signals — units are not interchangeable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TLV
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** TLV

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Limit Value (TLV)** is defined by the **American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists (ACGIH)** as the airborne concentration of a chemical substance under which it is believed that nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed, day after day, for a working lifetime (typically 8 hours/day, 40 hours/week), without adverse health effects.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme for *Safety Engineering of Process Plants*, TLVs are cornerstone **occupational exposure limits (OELs)** used by industrial hygienists and process safety engineers to evaluate and control chemical exposure in workplaces such as refineries, petrochemical plants, fertilizer units, and pharmaceutical facilities.

### Key Terminology at a Glance

- **TLV-TWA (Time-Weighted Average)**: The average exposure over an 8-hour workday.
- **TLV-STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit)**: A 15-minute time-weighted average that should not be exceeded.
- **TLV-C (Ceiling Limit)**: The concentration that must never be exceeded, even instantaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL DISTINCTION**: TLVs are **NOT** sharp dividing lines between "safe" and "unsafe" concentrations. They are advisory guidelines developed by ACGIH, not legally enforceable standards (unlike OSHA's PELs). They represent conditions believed to be safe for **nearly all** workers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Swimming Pool Chlorine" Intuition

Imagine a swimming pool. The pool operator must add **chlorine** to kill bacteria. Too little, and swimmers get sick. Too much, and swimmers' eyes burn. There is a *sweet spot* — a concentration range where the water is both safe and effective.

**TLV works exactly the same way** for airborne chemicals in a process plant:

| Element | Pool Analogy | TLV in Process Plant |
|---|---|---|
| **Chemical** | Chlorine gas (Cl₂) | Benzene, H₂S, Ammonia, etc. |
| **Safe Limit** | 1–3 ppm free chlorine | TLV-TWA for Cl₂ = **0.5 ppm** |
| **Worker** | Swimmer | Plant operator, maintenance crew |
| **Exposure Time** | Time spent in pool | 8-hour shift |

A worker is like a swimmer repeatedly entering the "pool" of contaminated air. TLV is the chlorination level the safety engineer sets so that the worker can keep swimming (working) safely.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Threshold" and not "Limit"?** The word *threshold* emphasizes the boundary nature — below it, effects are believed absent; above it, effects may begin to manifest. This subtle linguistic choice reflects the scientific uncertainty baked into the values.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Metrics

- **Standard Workday Duration**: **8 hours** (480 minutes)
- **Standard Workweek**: **40 hours**
- **STEL Window**: **15 minutes** (with minimum 60 minutes between successive STEL excursions)
- **Reference Body Assumption**: Healthy adult worker
- **Volume Reference**: **ppm (parts per million)** for gases/vapors, **mg/m³** for particulates
- **Standard Temperature & Pressure for conversion**: **25 °C and 1 atm (101.325 kPa)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of TLV Classification

The ACGIH framework organizes TLVs into three distinct exposure-time categories. Each addresses a **different toxicological mechanism**:

### Pillar 1 — TLV-TWA (Time-Weighted Average)

- **Duration Basis**: 8-hour workday, 40-hour workweek
- **Toxicological Basis**: Protects against **chronic health effects** (e.g., cancer, organ damage, cumulative toxicity)
- **Use Case**: Long-term exposure assessment, routine monitoring
- **Example**: TLV-TWA for **Benzene = 0.5 ppm** (carcinogen — low TWA is critical)

### Pillar 2 — TLV-STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit)

- **Duration Basis**: 15-minute time-weighted average
- **Toxicological Basis**: Protects against **acute effects** like irritation, narcosis, CNS depression
- **Constraint**: Must not occur more than 4 times per day, with at least 60 minutes between exposures
- **Example**: TLV-STEL for **Hydrogen Chloride (HCl) = 2 ppm**

### Pillar 3 — TLV-C (Ceiling)

- **Duration Basis**: Instantaneous — must NEVER be exceeded
- **Toxicological Basis**: Protects against **rapidly acting toxicants** (e.g., sensitizers, asphyxiants, severe irritants)
- **Example**: TLV-C for **Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S) = 10 ppm** (olfactory nerve paralysis above this)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind Three Limits**: Chemicals don't all act the same. Benzene causes leukemia after *years* of exposure (chronic → TWA). Hydrogen chloride burns the respiratory tract within *minutes* (acute → STEL). Hydrogen sulfide knocks you out *immediately* (rapid → Ceiling). One size does not fit all.

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation — The Haber's Law Connection

The mathematical basis for TLV rests on **Haber's Law** (often called the concentration-time product rule), which states that the toxic effect is proportional to the product of concentration and exposure duration:

$$C \times t = k \quad \text{(constant for a given biological response)}$$

Where:
- $C$ = airborne concentration of contaminant
- $t$ = exposure duration
- $k$ = toxicological constant (specific to chemical and effect)

### Derivation Logic of the TWA Formula

When a worker is exposed to **multiple varying concentrations** during a workday, the TWA is calculated as:

$$\text{TWA} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot t_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}$$

This is simply a **weighted average** — the most fundamental formula in industrial hygiene.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU EXAM FAVORITE**: If a worker spends 2 hours at 50 ppm, 4 hours at 30 ppm, and 2 hours at 10 ppm, calculate TWA. You **MUST** show the substitution and final units in ppm. Always carry units through!

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units | Boundary / Application Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time-Weighted Average | $\text{TWA} = \dfrac{\sum C_i \cdot t_i}{\sum t_i}$ | ppm or mg/m³ | 8-hr workday; chronic effects |
| 2 | Haber's Law | $C \times t = k$ | ppm·hr or mg·hr/m³ | Constant biological response |
| 3 | ppm to mg/m³ Conversion | $\text{mg/m}^3 = \dfrac{\text{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}$ | mg/m³ | At **25 °C, 1 atm** |
| 4 | mg/m³ to ppm Conversion | $\text{ppm} = \dfrac{\text{mg/m}^3 \times 24.45}{MW}$ | ppm | At **25 °C, 1 atm** |
| 5 | Molar Volume Constant | $V_m = 24.45$ L/mol | L/mol | At 25 °C and 1 atm |
| 6 | Excursion Limit Rule | $\text{Excursion} \leq 3 \times \text{TLV-TWA}$ | ppm | For STEL not listed |
| 7 | STEL Constraint | $\text{Max} = 4 \text{ excursions/day, min } 60\text{ min apart}$ | — | For STEL-listed chemicals |
| 8 | Occupational Exposure Limit (OEL) | $\text{OEL} = \text{TLV} \times \text{Safety Factor}$ | varies | Where TLV is base reference |

> **Note on the constant 24.45**: This is the **molar volume** of an ideal gas at 25 °C (298.15 K) and 1 atm, expressed in L/mol. It is derived from the ideal gas law $V_m = RT/P$ and is essential for all unit conversions in industrial hygiene.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a typical **oil refinery or petrochemical plant**, the safety engineer uses TLV values in the following workflow:

1. **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)**: Identify chemicals released during upsets
2. **Dispersion Modeling**: Estimate airborne concentrations using software (e.g., ALOHA, PHAST)
3. **Compare with TLV**: Determine if predicted concentrations exceed safe exposure
4. **Ventilation Design**: Specify local exhaust ventilation (LEV) to maintain concentrations below TLV
5. **PPE Specification**: Select respiratory protection if engineering controls are insufficient
6. **Air Monitoring Program**: Establish routine sampling protocols

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Application Insight**: In the Bhopal disaster (1984), Methyl Isocyanate (MIC) was released. The TLV-TWA for MIC is **0.02 ppm**. Concentrations in the affected areas reached several ppm — thousands of times the TLV. This is exactly the kind of scenario where TLV-based emergency response planning saves lives.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Molar Volume Constant (24.45 L/mol)

Starting from the **Ideal Gas Law**:

$$PV = nRT \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = \frac{nRT}{P}$$

For one mole of gas ($n = 1$ mol):

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{P}$$

Substituting standard reference conditions for TLV:
- $R = 0.082057$ L·atm/(mol·K)
- $T = 25\,°C = 298.15$ K
- $P = 1$ atm

$$V_m = \frac{0.082057 \times 298.15}{1}$$

Evaluating the numerator:

$$0.082057 \times 298.15 = 24.466$$

Rounded to four significant figures:

$$V_m \approx 24.45 \text{ L/mol}$$

> This is the **molar volume** at 25 °C and 1 atm — the standard condition for all TLV unit conversions.

## 3.2 Derivation: ppm ↔ mg/m³ Conversion

We begin with the definition of parts per million:

$$\text{ppm} = \frac{\text{Volume of contaminant}}{\text{Volume of air}} \times 10^6$$

For one mole of air (24.45 L at 25 °C, 1 atm), one ppm corresponds to one-millionth of a mole of contaminant per mole of air:

$$\text{ppm} = \frac{n_{\text{contaminant}}}{n_{\text{air}}} \times 10^6 = \frac{n_{\text{contaminant}}}{1} \times 10^{-6}$$

Converting moles to mass using molecular weight (MW):

$$n_{\text{contaminant}} = \frac{m_{\text{contaminant}}}{MW}$$

Mass in grams per 24.45 L of air gives concentration in g/L, which we convert to mg/m³ by multiplying by $10^3$ (L → m³) and $10^3$ (g → mg):

$$\text{mg/m}^3 = \frac{\text{ppm} \times MW \times 10^6}{10^6 \times 24.45} = \frac{\text{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}$$

Final form:

$$\boxed{\text{mg/m}^3 = \frac{\text{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}}$$

The reverse conversion follows by simple algebra:

$$\text{ppm} = \frac{\text{mg/m}^3 \times 24.45}{MW}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — TWA Calculation

**Problem**: A maintenance worker in a process plant has the following exposure profile during an 8-hour shift:

| Interval | Duration (hr) | Measured SO₂ Concentration (ppm) |
|---|---|---|
| Morning task | 2.0 | 5.0 |
| Lunch break | 1.0 | 0.0 (unexposed) |
| Afternoon task | 3.0 | 3.0 |
| End-of-shift cleanup | 2.0 | 1.5 |

**Find**: TLV-TWA and compare with TLV-TWA for SO₂ (which is **2 ppm**).

**Step 1: Verify total time**

$$t_{\text{total}} = 2.0 + 1.0 + 3.0 + 2.0 = 8.0 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2: Apply the TWA formula**

$$\text{TWA} = \frac{\sum C_i \cdot t_i}{\sum t_i}$$

**Step 3: Compute numerator (concentration-time products)**

$$C_1 \cdot t_1 = 5.0 \times 2.0 = 10.0 \text{ ppm·hr}$$

$$C_2 \cdot t_2 = 0.0 \times 1.0 = 0.0 \text{ ppm·hr}$$

$$C_3 \cdot t_3 = 3.0 \times 3.0 = 9.0 \text{ ppm·hr}$$

$$C_4 \cdot t_4 = 1.5 \times 2.0 = 3.0 \text{ ppm·hr}$$

**Step 4: Sum the numerator**

$$\sum C_i \cdot t_i = 10.0 + 0.0 + 9.0 + 3.0 = 22.0 \text{ ppm·hr}$$

**Step 5: Divide by total time**

$$\text{TWA} = \frac{22.0}{8.0} = 2.75 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 6: Compare with TLV**

$$\text{Calculated TWA} = 2.75 \text{ ppm} \quad > \quad \text{TLV-TWA} = 2.0 \text{ ppm}$$

**Conclusion**: Exposure **exceeds** the TLV-TWA by 37.5%. Engineering controls (e.g., improved ventilation) or respiratory protection are required.

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Unit Conversion

**Problem**: TLV-TWA for acetone is **250 ppm**. Convert to mg/m³. (MW of acetone, C₃H₆O = **58.08 g/mol**)

**Step 1: Apply the conversion formula**

$$\text{mg/m}^3 = \frac{\text{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$\text{mg/m}^3 = \frac{250 \times 58.08}{24.45}$$

**Step 3: Numerator**

$$250 \times 58.08 = 14520$$

**Step 4: Final calculation**

$$\text{mg/m}^3 = \frac{14520}{24.45} = 593.87 \approx 594 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

**Verification (reverse)**: $\text{ppm} = (594 \times 24.45) / 58.08 = 250.03$ ppm ✓

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common TLV Values for Process Plant Chemicals

| Chemical | CAS Number | TLV-TWA (ppm) | TLV-STEL (ppm) | TLV-C (ppm) | Primary Hazard |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ammonia (NH₃) | 7664-41-7 | 25 | 35 | — | Respiratory irritant |
| Benzene (C₆H₆) | 71-43-2 | 0.5 | 2.5 | — | Carcinogen (leukemia) |
| Carbon Monoxide (CO) | 630-08-0 | 25 | — | — | Chemical asphyxiant |
| Chlorine (Cl₂) | 7782-50-5 | 0.5 | 1 | — | Severe irritant |
| Hydrogen Chloride (HCl) | 7647-01-0 | — | 2 | — | Respiratory irritant |
| Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S) | 7783-06-4 | 1 | 5 | 10 | Acute toxicant |
| Methanol (CH₃OH) | 67-56-1 | 200 | 250 | — | CNS depressant |
| Sulfur Dioxide (SO₂) | 7446-09-5 | 2 | 5 | — | Respiratory irritant |
| Toluene (C₇H₈) | 108-88-3 | 20 | — | — | CNS, neurotoxicity |
| Vinyl Chloride (C₂H₃Cl) | 75-01-4 | 1 | — | — | Carcinogen |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define Threshold Limit Value (TLV). Explain the three categories of TLV in brief.**

**Model Answer** (3 marks — approx. 80–100 words):

> **TLV (Threshold Limit Value)** is the airborne concentration of a chemical substance below which nearly all workers can be repeatedly exposed, day after day, throughout their working lifetime, without adverse health effects. It is published by the **American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists (ACGIH)**. The three categories are:
>
> 1. **TLV-TWA (Time-Weighted Average)**: 8-hour average exposure; protects against chronic effects.
> 2. **TLV-STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit)**: 15-minute time-weighted average; protects against acute effects like irritation.
> 3. **TLV-C (Ceiling)**: Absolute instantaneous limit that must never be exceeded; for rapidly acting toxicants.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Convert 50 ppm of toluene (C₇H₈, MW = 92.14 g/mol) to mg/m³ at 25 °C and 1 atm.**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

Using the formula:

$$\text{mg/m}^3 = \frac{\text{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}$$

Substituting:

$$\text{mg/m}^3 = \frac{50 \times 92.14}{24.45} = \frac{4607}{24.45} \approx 188.4 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

**Answer**: 50 ppm of toluene ≈ **188.4 mg/m³** at 25 °C, 1 atm.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q (a)** Define TLV-TWA, TLV-STEL, and TLV-C. Discuss their toxicological basis with suitable examples of process plant chemicals. (7 marks)

**Q (b)** A worker in a fertilizer plant is exposed to ammonia as follows: 1.5 hr at 30 ppm, 2 hr at 20 ppm, 1 hr lunch break (0 ppm), 2 hr at 25 ppm, and 1.5 hr at 10 ppm. Calculate the TWA and compare with TLV-TWA for ammonia (25 ppm). (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Definition of TLV-TWA (2 marks)**:
TLV-TWA is the time-weighted average concentration for a normal 8-hour workday and 40-hour workweek, to which nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed without adverse effects. *Example*: Benzene TLV-TWA = 0.5 ppm (protects against leukemia, a chronic effect).

**Definition of TLV-STEL (2 marks)**:
TLV-STEL is the 15-minute time-weighted average exposure that should not be exceeded at any time during the workday. Maximum 4 such excursions per day, with at least 60 min between successive STEL periods. *Example*: HCl TLV-STEL = 2 ppm (protects against acute respiratory irritation).

**Definition of TLV-C (2 marks)**:
TLV-C is the concentration that must not be exceeded during any part of the working exposure. *Example*: H₂S TLV-C = 10 ppm (protects against immediate olfactory nerve paralysis and asphyxiation).

**Toxicological Basis Synthesis (1 mark)**:
The three limits reflect the **kinetics of toxic action** — chronic (TWA), acute (STEL), and rapid-onset (C). A single 8-hour average is insufficient for fast-acting irritants or asphyxiants, which is why ACGIH developed this multi-tiered system.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Identify intervals (1 mark)**

| Interval | Duration $t_i$ (hr) | Concentration $C_i$ (ppm) |
|---|---|---|
| Task 1 | 1.5 | 30 |
| Task 2 | 2.0 | 20 |
| Lunch | 1.0 | 0 |
| Task 3 | 2.0 | 25 |
| Task 4 | 1.5 | 10 |

**Step 2: Verify total time (1 mark)**

$$\sum t_i = 1.5 + 2.0 + 1.0 + 2.0 + 1.5 = 8.0 \text{ hr} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3: Compute concentration-time products (2 marks)**

$$C_1 t_1 = 30 \times 1.5 = 45.0 \text{ ppm·hr}$$

$$C_2 t_2 = 20 \times 2.0 = 40.0 \text{ ppm·hr}$$

$$C_3 t_3 = 0 \times 1.0 = 0.0 \text{ ppm·hr}$$

$$C_4 t_4 = 25 \times 2.0 = 50.0 \text{ ppm·hr}$$

$$C_5 t_5 = 10 \times 1.5 = 15.0 \text{ ppm·hr}$$

**Step 4: Sum and divide (2 marks)**

$$\sum C_i t_i = 45.0 + 40.0 + 0.0 + 50.0 + 15.0 = 150.0 \text{ ppm·hr}$$

$$\text{TWA} = \frac{150.0}{8.0} = 18.75 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 5: Compare with TLV (1 mark)**

$$\text{TWA} = 18.75 \text{ ppm} \; < \; \text{TLV-TWA} = 25 \text{ ppm}$$

**Conclusion**: Exposure is **within** acceptable limits. The 8-hour TWA is below the TLV-TWA, so the workplace is in compliance — assuming peak concentrations do not exceed the excursion limit (3 × 25 = 75 ppm) and no STEL (35 ppm) violations occur.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q (a)** Explain the significance of TLV in process plant safety. Differentiate TLV from OSHA's Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) and IDLH. (7 marks)

**Q (b)** The TLV-TWA for hydrogen sulfide (H₂S) is 1 ppm. If the molecular weight of H₂S is 34.08 g/mol, calculate (i) the TLV in mg/m³, and (ii) the concentration in mg/m³ corresponding to 3 times the TWA (the excursion limit). (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Significance of TLV in Process Plant Safety (3 marks)**:
TLVs provide **quantitative benchmarks** for evaluating airborne chemical exposure in industrial settings. They are used in:
- **Ventilation design** (local exhaust systems sized to keep concentrations below TLV)
- **Air monitoring programs** (compliance verification)
- **PPE selection** (respirators assigned when engineering controls cannot reduce exposure below TLV)
- **Hazard communication** (Safety Data Sheets, training)
- **Emergency planning** (defining safe evacuation zones during releases)

**TLV vs PEL (2 marks)**:

| Aspect | TLV | PEL (OSHA) |
|---|---|---|
| Issuing body | ACGIH | Occupational Safety and Health Administration |
| Legal status | Advisory | Legally enforceable |
| Update frequency | Annual | Infrequent (1970s values, partly updated) |
| Basis | Latest toxicology | Older standards, regulatory compromise |

**TLV vs IDLH (2 marks)**:

| Aspect | TLV | IDLH |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Long-term safe exposure | Immediate life-threatening concentration |
| Duration | 8-hr (TWA) or 15-min (STEL) | 30-minute escape window |
| Use | Routine industrial hygiene | Respiratory protection selection, emergency planning |
| Example (H₂S) | 1 ppm TWA | 100 ppm IDLH |

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given**:
- TLV-TWA for H₂S = 1 ppm
- MW of H₂S = 34.08 g/mol
- Molar volume at 25 °C, 1 atm = 24.45 L/mol

**Part (i) — TLV in mg/m³ (3 marks)**:

$$\text{mg/m}^3 = \frac{\text{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}$$

$$\text{mg/m}^3 = \frac{1 \times 34.08}{24.45} = \frac{34.08}{24.45} = 1.394 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

**Answer (i)**: TLV-TWA = **1.39 mg/m³**

**Part (ii) — Excursion limit concentration (3 marks)**:

$$3 \times \text{TLV-TWA} = 3 \times 1.0 = 3.0 \text{ ppm}$$

Converting to mg/m³:

$$\text{mg/m}^3 = \frac{3.0 \times 34.08}{24.45} = \frac{102.24}{24.45} = 4.18 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

**Answer (ii)**: Excursion limit = **4.18 mg/m³**

**Final Summary Statement (1 mark)**: The excursion limit applies only when the chemical has **no STEL** listed. For brief deviations above TWA, the 15-min average must remain ≤ 4.18 mg/m³ (3 ppm), and the 8-hr TWA must still be ≤ 1.39 mg/m³.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in TLV Problems (as flagged by KTU Board Examiners)**:
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion**: Students frequently write "mg/L" instead of "mg/m³" or forget to specify temperature for ppm ↔ mg/m³ conversions. **Always state 25 °C, 1 atm** when using 24.45.
> 2. **Molar Volume Error**: Using **22.4** (STP value) instead of **24.45** (25 °C value) — costs 1 mark in Part A and 2 marks in Part B conversions.
> 3. **TWA Time Mismatch**: Forgetting to include lunch breaks (with 0 ppm concentration) or treating exposure intervals incorrectly. The total time must always sum to **8 hours**.
> 4. **TLV vs PEL Confusion**: In exam answers, do not mix TLV and PEL values. TLV is from **ACGIH**; PEL is from **OSHA**. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 5. **No Comparison Statement**: A TWA calculation without a final comparison to the TLV value is **incomplete**. Always end with "TWA = X ppm ≤ TLV-TWA = Y ppm" or the equivalent exceedance statement.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TLV** = Threshold Limit Value, defined by **ACGIH**, an **advisory** occupational exposure limit.
- **Three categories**: **TWA** (8-hr chronic), **STEL** (15-min acute), **C** (instantaneous ceiling).
- **Core TWA formula**: $\text{TWA} = \dfrac{\sum C_i t_i}{\sum t_i}$ — weighted average.
- **Haber's Law**: $C \times t = k$ — basis of time-concentration tradeoffs.
- **Conversion constant**: $V_m = 24.45$ L/mol at **25 °C, 1 atm** — use this, not 22.4.
- **Conversion formula**: $\text{mg/m}^3 = \dfrac{\text{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}$ and its inverse.
- **Excursion limit**: When no STEL exists, brief peaks should not exceed **3 × TLV-TWA**.
- **STEL rules**: Maximum **4 excursions per day**, separated by at least **60 minutes**.
- **TLV is NOT**: a sharp line between safe and unsafe; legally enforceable; protective for hypersensitive individuals.
- **Practical use**: TLVs drive ventilation design, air monitoring, PPE selection, and emergency planning in refineries, fertilizer plants, and chemical process industries.
- **High-priority chemicals** to memorize for KTU exams: **Benzene (0.5 ppm TWA, carcinogen), H₂S (1 ppm TWA, 10 ppm C), NH₃ (25 ppm TWA), Cl₂ (0.5 ppm TWA), HCl (2 ppm STEL), CO (25 ppm TWA)**.
- **Key distinction in exam answers**: Always cite ACGIH for TLV, OSHA for PEL, and NIOSH for IDLH — examiners look for this precision.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
