**Flash Point**: in **°C** (closed cup, ASTM D93) > - **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)**: in **°C** > - **Lower/Upper Flammable Limits (LFL/UFL)**: in **% vol** in air ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of an Inventory Analysis Procedure The complete inventory analysis workflow, as practiced in industry, follows five sequential logic layers: 1. **Layer 1 — Material Identification** - Record chemical name, CAS Registry Number, synonyms, and trade names. - Document physical state (solid, liquid, gas, liquefied gas) under plant operating conditions. - Identify mixture compositions and impurities that may alter hazard. 2. **Layer 2 — Quantity & State Determination** - Tally the inventory of every vessel, tank, drum, cylinder, and pipeline segment. - Distinguish between **in-process inventory** (inside reactors/columns) and **storage inventory** (bulk tanks, warehouses). - Note temperature and pressure conditions — these dramatically alter vapour pressure and therefore the *effective* inventory available for release. 3. **Layer 3 — Hazardous Property Evaluation** - **Flammability** (NFPA rating, flash point, LFL/UFL). - **Reactivity / Instability** (NFPA Reactivity rating, decomposition temperature, water reactivity). - **Toxicity** (TLV, LC₅₀, IDLH, ERPG values). - **Corrosivity, Peroxide-forming tendency, Pyrophoricity**. 4. **Layer 4 — Location & Exposure Mapping** - Plot each inventory item on a Plant Layout (P&ID and Plot Plan). - Compute distances to: occupied buildings, ignition sources, public roads, and emergency response infrastructure. - Identify **domino clusters** — units whose inventories could escalate an incident. 5. **Layer 5 — Threshold Screening & Index Triggering** - Compare each inventory item against regulatory and corporate screening thresholds. - If exceeded, escalate to **Dow F&EI**, **CEI**, or full **QRA**. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Layer - *Why Layer 1?* Misidentification is the #1 cause of wrong MSDS application. The **Bhopal disaster** partly stemmed from miscategorisation of MIC's reactivity. - *Why Layer 2?* Quantity is the multiplier — 1 kg of chlorine is a nuisance; 40 tonnes of chlorine (as at Bhopal's Tank 610) is a catastrophe. - *Why Layer 3?* Same chemical at different states (e.g., propane liquid vs. propane gas) has radically different consequence envelopes. - *Why Layer 4?* The **1974 Flixborough disaster** in the UK was a domino scenario — a temporary bypass pipe carried 50 tonnes of hot cyclohexane inventory past an unsupported scaffold. - *Why Layer 5?* Screening avoids the analytical cost of QRA on low-risk inventories. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Quantity | Symbol | Equation | Typical Units | Boundary / Notes | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Dow Material Factor | $MF$ | $MF = N_f \times N_r$ (with possible 0.1 adjustment for oxidisers) | dimensionless | $N_f$ = flammability hazard rating, $N_r$ = reactivity hazard rating (both from NFPA 704) | | 2 | Process Unit Hazards Factor | $PUHF$ | $PUHF = 1.0 + GPH + SPH$ | dimensionless | $GPH$ = General Process Hazards; $SPH$ = Special Process Hazards | | 3 | Dow Fire & Explosion Index | $F\&EI$ | $F\&EI = MF \times PUHF$ | dimensionless | Often categorised: Light (1–60), Moderate (61–96), Heavy (97–127), Severe (>127) | | 4 | Damage Factor | $DF$ | Read from Dow F&EI Radius vs. Damage Radius chart | dimensionless | Empirical, derived from F&EI | | 5 | Base Maximum Probable Property Damage | $MPP_D^{base}$ | $MPP_D^{base} = Replacement\ Cost \times DF \times 0.82$ | currency | 0.82 is the process control loss credit in legacy Dow tables | | 6 | Actual MPPD | $MPP_D^{actual}$ | $MPP_D^{actual} = MPP_D^{base} \times LCCF$ | currency | $LCCF$ = Loss Control Credit Factor (typically 0.10–1.00) | | 7 | Chemical Exposure Index (CEI) | $CEI$ | $CEI = 65.5 \times \dfrac{V}{\text{ERPG}-1}$ (simplified atmospheric form) | dimensionless | $V$ = airborne release rate in **kg/min**; **ERPG-1** = Emergency Response Planning Guideline level 1 in **ppm** | | 8 | Airborne Release Rate (liquid pool) | $V$ | $V = \dfrac{Q \cdot H_v \cdot \rho}{\Delta T \cdot C_p \cdot 60}$ | kg/min | $Q$ = pool heat loss rate (W), $H_v$ = latent heat of vaporisation, $\rho$ = liquid density | | 9 | Threshold Inventory (regulatory) | $I_{thr}$ | $I_{thr}$ values prescribed by EPA RMP / OSHA PSM | kg or tonnes | E.g., chlorine threshold = **1500 lb (680 kg)**; propane = **10,000 lb (4536 kg)** | | 10 | Hazard Rating (NFPA Composite) | $HR$ | $HR = \max(N_f, N_h, N_r, N_s)$ | 0–4 | Health, flammability, reactivity, special | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** > These formulas are the *backbone* of every process plant's safety report submitted to the **Kerala State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB)** and **Directorate of Factories and Boilers**. Plant managers use them to (i) justify insurance premiums, (ii) determine plant layout, and (iii) size emergency shutdown (ESD) systems. ### 2.4 The Dow Material Factor — A Closer Look The **Material Factor (MF)** is the cornerstone of inventory analysis because it *characterises the intrinsic energy content* of every chemical in the plant. The original Dow Chemical method uses NFPA 704 ratings: $$N_f \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\} \quad \text{(will not burn to can be vaporised rapidly at normal pressure and temperature)}$$ $$N_r \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\} \quad \text{(stable to detonates)}$$ For oxidising substances (e.g., oxygen, chlorine dioxide, hydrogen peroxide), Dow multiplies the basic MF by an additional factor of **0.1** added before squaring — historically written as $MF = (N_f \times N_r) + \text{oxidiser correction}$. **Example substance entries (commonly tested in KTU):** | Chemical | CAS | $N_f$ | $N_r$ | $MF$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | Acetone | 67-64-1 | 3 | 0 | **16** | | Methane | 74-82-8 | 4 | 0 | **21** | | Toluene | 108-88-3 | 3 | 0 | **16** | | Hydrogen | 1333-74-0 | 4 | 0 | **21** | | Hydrazine | 302-01-2 | 3 | 3 | **29** | ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation of the Dow Fire & Explosion Index The F&EI is *not* derived from first principles — it is an **empirical index**. However, its mathematical structure can be reconstructed as a weighted linear aggregation of process hazards. The published derivation follows this logic: **Step 1 — Establish baseline material energy potential.** A substance with $MF = 1$ is practically inert (e.g., water, sand). A substance with $MF = 40$ releases energy at rates comparable to high explosives (e.g., nitroglycerine). The MF therefore acts as a *linear scaling constant* on total incident energy. **Step 2 — Introduce process-context modifiers.** The actual energy release in a unit is amplified by operational realities: exothermic reactions, dust-handling, high inventories, low-temperature storage of liquefied gases, etc. These are partitioned into two categories: - **General Process Hazards (GPH)** — apply to most process units (e.g., accessibility for firefighting, drainage). - **Special Process Hazards (SPH)** — apply only when a specific dangerous condition exists (e.g., presence of combustible dust, operation in or near the flammable range). **Step 3 — Combine modifiers into a single multiplier.** The unit-level multiplier is defined as: $$PUHF = 1.0 + GPH + SPH$$ where the leading "1.0" represents the *unmodified* baseline operation. By convention, each hazard factor is a non-negative integer or 0.5 step. **Step 4 — Compute the F&EI.** $$\boxed{F\&EI = MF \times PUHF}$$ **Step 5 — Determine the Radius of Exposure.** The empirical relation used in the Dow method (8th edition) is: $$R = 0.256 \times F\&EI \quad \text{(metres)}$$ This is the radius within which property damage can be expected from a fireball or vapour cloud explosion centred on the analysed process unit. **Step 6 — Determine the Damage Factor.** The damage factor $DF$ is read from the Dow F&EI Radius vs. Damage Radius chart, but is analytically approximated (for textbook use) by: $$DF = \left( \dfrac{0.8 \times F\&EI}{100} \right)^{2} \quad \text{(for } F\&EI \le 100\text{)}$$ For $F\&EI > 100$, the curve flattens and saturates near $DF \approx 1.0$. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Typical KTU-style Problem **Problem Statement (KTU Style):** A process unit handles **acrylonitrile (CH₂=CH–CN, CAS 107-13-1)** under the following conditions: - Operating temperature: **85 °C** (above its flash point of **0 °C**) - Inventory in the reactor: **12,000 kg** - Presence of dust-handling operation: **Yes** - Pressure relief devices installed: **Yes** - Number of operator actions per shift: **8** - Toxic substance release potential: **Yes** Given: - NFPA ratings for acrylonitrile: $N_f = 3$, $N_r = 2$ - Selected GPH items: exothermic reaction (+1), operation in flammable range (+1), enclosed process unit (+0.5), access for firefighting (+0) → **$GPH = 2.5$** - Selected SPH items: toxic substance (+1), dust handled (+1), high inventory (+0.8), low-temperature storage (No), pressure relief installed (−0.5 credit... not allowed in Dow; used in CEI) → **$SPH = 2.8$** **Find:** F&EI, radius of exposure, and damage factor. --- **Step 1: Compute Material Factor.** $$MF = N_f \times N_r = 3 \times 2 = 6 \quad (\text{Dow rounded value for acrylonitrile is } 24; \text{ using simplified values here for pedagogy})$$ Using the **published Dow value for acrylonitrile** (which students are expected to memorise or look up): $$MF_{\text{acrylonitrile}} = 24$$ **Step 2: Compute Process Unit Hazards Factor.** $$PUHF = 1.0 + GPH + SPH$$ $$PUHF = 1.0 + 2.5 + 2.8$$ $$\boxed{PUHF = 6.3}$$ **Step 3: Compute F&EI.** $$F\&EI = MF \times PUHF$$ $$F\&EI = 24 \times 6.3$$ $$\boxed{F\&EI = 151.2}$$ **Step 4: Categorise severity.** Per Dow's classification table: | F&EI Range | Severity | |---|---| | 1 – 60 | Light | | 61 – 96 | Moderate | | 97 – 127 | Heavy | | **> 127** | **Severe** | Since $F\&EI = 151.2 > 127$, this unit is categorised as **SEVERE** — a typical KTU exam answer requires the student to (i) state the category, (ii) suggest risk-reduction measures. **Step 5: Radius of Exposure.** $$R = 0.256 \times F\&EI$$ $$R = 0.256 \times 151.2$$ $$\boxed{R \approx 38.7 \text{ metres}}$$ **Step 6: Damage Factor (using the analytical approximation since $F\&EI > 100$, we use the saturated branch).** $$DF \approx 1.0 \quad \text{(severe unit, damage to nearly all equipment within radius)}$$ **Step 7: Estimated Property Damage.** If the replacement value of the unit and contents is, say, **₹ 50 crores** and the Loss Control Credit Factor is **LCCF = 0.40**: $$MPP_D^{base} = 50{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 1.0 \times 0.82 = ₹ 41{,}00{,}00{,}000$$ $$MPP_D^{actual} = 41{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 = ₹ 16{,}40{,}00{,}000$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When Does Inventory Analysis Trigger a Full QRA? | Inventory Signal | Plant Decision | |---|---| | Any single chemical inventory > EPA RMP threshold | Trigger full QRA, file Risk Management Plan | | Two adjacent units each with F&EI > 97 (Heavy/Severe) | Domino analysis mandatory | | Toxic chemical with IDLH < 100 ppm AND inventory > 1 tonne | Off-site consequence analysis required | | Flammable inventory with LFL < 1% vol AND quantity > 100 tonnes | Vapour cloud explosion modelling (TNT equivalent or TNO Multi-Energy) | | All inventories below threshold | Routine Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) suffices | ### 3.4 The CEI — Complementary to F&EI The **Chemical Exposure Index (CEI)**, also developed by Dow, focuses on **acute toxic hazards**. While F&EI deals with fire/explosion, CEI deals with airborne toxic releases. The simplified form for an instantaneous or short-duration release is: $$CEI = 65.5 \times \dfrac{V}{\text{ERPG}-1}$$ where: - $V$ = airborne release rate (kg/min) - **ERPG-1** = maximum airborne concentration below which nearly all individuals could be exposed for up to 1 hour without experiencing more than mild, transient adverse health effects (in **ppm**) - The constant **65.5** is a unit-conversion and dilution-stack factor specific to the Dow CEI methodology A **CEI > 1000** is generally considered severe enough to require significant engineering and administrative controls. ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Inventory Analysis Workflow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Plant Inventory Audit] --> B[Layer 1: Material Identification
CAS, Name, Synonyms] B --> C[Layer 2: Quantity and State
Mass Volume Temp Pressure] C --> D[Layer 3: Hazard Property Evaluation
Flammability Reactivity Toxicity] D --> E[Layer 4: Location Mapping
Plot Plan Distances Domino Check] E --> F[Layer 5: Threshold Screening
OSHA PSM EPA RMP Corporate] F --> G{Inventory exceeds threshold?} G -- No --> H[PHA Only
Routine Safety Review] G -- Yes --> I[Compute Material Factor MF] I --> J[Compute PUHF from GPH and SPH] J --> K[Calculate F and EI] K --> L{F and EI Severity Category?} L -- Light 1 to 60 --> M[Standard Controls] L -- Moderate 61 to 96 --> N[Enhanced Controls and Inspection] L -- Heavy 97 to 127 --> O[Severe Controls and QRA] L -- Severe greater than 127 --> P[Severe Controls and Full QRA with Domino] I --> Q[Compute CEI in Parallel] Q --> R{CEI greater than 1000?} R -- Yes --> P R -- No --> O P --> S[Document in Safety Report] O --> S N --> S M --> S S --> T[End] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Inventory Database ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InputSources[Input Data Sources] P1[Piping and Instrumentation Diagram P and ID] P2[Material Safety Data Sheet MSDS] P3[Plant Operating Procedures SOP] P4[Equipment Data Sheets] end subgraph DatabaseCore[Inventory Database Core] D1[Chemical Master Table
CAS Name Formula] D2[Quantity Table
Mass Volume State] D3[Properties Table
NFPA TLV LFL UFL] D4[Location Table
Unit ID Coordinates Plot Plan] end subgraph ProcessingEngine[Analysis Engine] E1[Threshold Screener] E2[Dow F and EI Calculator] E3[Chemical Exposure Index CEI] E4[Domino Effect Mapper] end subgraph OutputModules[Decision Support Output] O1[Risk Register] O2[Inspection Schedule] O3[Emergency Shutdown Logic] O4[KSPCB Compliance Report] end P1 --> D4 P2 --> D1 P2 --> D3 P3 --> D2 P4 --> D2 D1 --> E1 D2 --> E1 D3 --> E1 D4 --> E1 D1 --> E2 D2 --> E2 D3 --> E2 D4 --> E2 D1 --> E3 D2 --> E3 D3 --> E3 D4 --> E4 E1 --> O1 E2 --> O1 E3 --> O1 E4 --> O1 E1 --> O2 E2 --> O3 E3 --> O4 ``` ### 4.3 Domino Effect Logical Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Unit A has high inventory
F and EI greater than 127] --> S2[Primary Event
Vapour Cloud Explosion] S2 --> S3{Adjacent Unit B within
38.7 metre radius?} S3 -- Yes --> S4[Overpressure Impact on Unit B] S4 --> S5[Unit B Inventory Breach] S5 --> S6[Secondary Event
Pool Fire or BLEVE] S6 --> S7[Escalation to Unit C if within thermal radius] S7 --> S8[Tertiary Event] S8 --> S9[Cascading Major Accident] S3 -- No --> S10[Primary Event Contained
No Domino] ``` ### 4.4 Material Factor Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Identify Substance and NFPA Ratings] --> B{Nf and Nr Available?} B -- No --> C[Refer to Dow Fire and Explosion Index Guide] B -- Yes --> D[Compute MF equals Nf times Nr] D --> E{Is substance an oxidiser?} E -- Yes --> F[Apply Oxidiser Correction
MF equals MF plus 0.1 factor] E -- No --> G[Use Base MF] F --> H[Look up GPH and SPH Items] G --> H H --> I[Sum PUHF equals 1 plus GPH plus SPH] I --> J[Compute F and EI equals MF times PUHF] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **Inventory Analysis** in the context of process plant safety. List any four hazardous properties that must be documented for each substance during this analysis. **Model Answer:** Inventory Analysis is the systematic identification, quantification, and qualitative evaluation of all hazardous materials present in a process plant, including their locations and conditions of storage. It is the first step in hazard identification and forms the data foundation for indices such as the Dow F&EI and CEI. Four hazardous properties to be documented: 1. **Flammability** (NFPA rating, flash point, LFL/UFL) 2. **Reactivity / Instability** (NFPA reactivity rating, decomposition temperature, water/air reactivity) 3. **Toxicity** (TLV-TWA, LC₅₀, IDLH, ERPG values) 4. **Physical state** at operating conditions (solid, liquid, gas, liquefied gas), plus **corrosivity** and **peroxide-forming tendency** as auxiliary properties. --- **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the **Material Factor (MF)** in Dow's F&EI method? How is it computed for a non-oxidising substance? **Model Answer:** The Material Factor (MF) is a dimensionless number that quantifies the intrinsic energy release potential of a chemical substance. It is the multiplier that scales process-context hazards to compute the Fire & Explosion Index. For a non-oxidising substance: $$MF = N_f \times N_r$$ where $N_f$ is the NFPA 704 flammability rating and $N_r$ is the NFPA 704 reactivity rating, each on a 0–4 scale. For example, acetone ($N_f = 3, N_r = 0$) gives $MF = 16$. --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure of **Inventory Analysis** as applied to a process plant. Discuss its role as a prerequisite to the Dow Fire & Explosion Index calculation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A process unit handles **cyclohexane (CAS 110-82-7)** at 150 °C and 5 bar. The following data apply: | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Inventory | 25,000 kg | | Operating in or above flash point | Yes | | Enclosed process unit | Yes | | Toxic substance released | No | | Dust handled | No | | Reactive with water | Yes | | Pressure relief devices | Yes | | Operators per shift | 6 | | Replacement value | ₹ 80 crores | | Loss Control Credit Factor | 0.35 | | NFPA ratings for cyclohexane | $N_f = 3, N_r = 0$ | Calculate the **F&EI**, the **radius of exposure**, the **damage factor**, and the **actual maximum probable property damage**. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Part (a):** Inventory Analysis is performed in five sequential layers: **Layer 1 — Material Identification:** Every chemical in the plant is listed with its chemical name, CAS number, and synonyms. Mixture compositions are noted. *Example:* cyclohexane vs. cyclohexanone vs. cyclohexylamine. **Layer 2 — Quantity & State Determination:** Inventory is quantified for each unit (reactor, storage tank, pipeline). State (solid, liquid, gas) is noted under actual T and P. *Example:* LPG at 25 °C is liquid but flashes to vapour instantly on release. **Layer 3 — Hazardous Property Evaluation:** NFPA ratings, flash point, AIT, LFL/UFL, TLV, LC₅₀, IDLH, ERPG values are catalogued. **Layer 4 — Location & Exposure Mapping:** The P&ID and plot plan are referenced. Distances to occupied buildings, public areas, ignition sources, and adjacent units are measured. Domino clusters are identified. **Layer 5 — Threshold Screening:** Each inventory item is compared to regulatory (OSHA PSM, EPA RMP) and corporate thresholds. Only those exceeding thresholds trigger further indices. **Role as Prerequisite to Dow F&EI:** The MF used in the F&EI is computed from inventory item's NFPA ratings (from Layer 3). The GPH and SPH values depend on inventory quantities (Layer 2) and locations (Layer 4). The Loss Control Credit Factor depends on engineered controls documented throughout the inventory study. Without a complete inventory analysis, every subsequent index is **arbitrary and unsupportable**. In particular, the $GPH$ factor "Quantity of Material" and the $SPH$ factor "Substance Released to Atmosphere" both directly require Layer 2 data. --- **Model Solution for Part (b):** **Step 1: Compute Material Factor.** For cyclohexane (NFPA $N_f = 3$, $N_r = 0$): $$MF = N_f \times N_r = 3 \times 0 = \text{invalid}$$ Dow's published MF for cyclohexane (via the standard tables; the actual Dow value is **16** because the NFPA flammability rating is reinterpreted as a non-zero penalty for combustible liquids): Using the **published Dow value for cyclohexane: MF = 16**. **Step 2: Identify GPH Items.** | GPH Item | Applicable? | Value | |---|---|---| | Exothermic reaction | No | 0 | | Endothermic reaction | No | 0 | | Material handling and transfer | Yes (high inventory) | +0.85 | | Enclosed or indoor process units | Yes | +0.50 | | Access for firefighting | Limited | +0.20 | | Drainage and spill control | Inadequate | +0.50 | $$GPH = 0 + 0 + 0.85 + 0.50 + 0.20 + 0.50 = 2.05$$ **Step 3: Identify SPH Items.** | SPH Item | Applicable? | Value | |---|---|---| | Toxic substances | No | 0 | | Sub-atmospheric pressure | No | 0 | | Operation in or near flammable range | Yes | +1.00 | | Dust explosion risk | No | 0 | | Relief pressure | 5 bar (<10 bar) | +0.20 | | Low temperature | No | 0 | | Quantity of flammable material (25,000 kg) | Yes | +0.80 | | Corrosive and erosion | No | 0 | | Leakage – joints and packing | Yes | +0.30 | | Use of fired heaters | No | 0 | | Hot oil heat exchange system | No | 0 | | Rotating equipment | No | 0 | $$SPH = 0 + 0 + 1.00 + 0 + 0.20 + 0 + 0.80 + 0 + 0.30 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 2.30$$ **Step 4: Compute PUHF.** $$PUHF = 1.0 + GPH + SPH = 1.0 + 2.05 + 2.30 = 5.35$$ **Step 5: Compute F&EI.** $$F\&EI = MF \times PUHF = 16 \times 5.35$$ $$\boxed{F\&EI = 85.6}$$ **Step 6: Radius of Exposure.** $$R = 0.256 \times F\&EI = 0.256 \times 85.6$$ $$\boxed{R \approx 21.9 \text{ metres}}$$ **Step 7: Damage Factor.** For $F\&EI = 85.6$, reading from the Dow Radius vs. Damage Radius chart, the damage radius is approximately 0.5 × R, giving: $$DF \approx 0.42 \quad \text{(approximate analytical value for } F\&EI < 96 \text{)}$$ **Step 8: Maximum Probable Property Damage.** $$MPP_D^{base} = \text{Replacement Cost} \times DF \times 0.82$$ $$MPP_D^{base} = 80 \times 10^7 \times 0.42 \times 0.82 = ₹ 27{,}55{,}20{,}000$$ $$MPP_D^{actual} = MPP_D^{base} \times LCCF$$ $$MPP_D^{actual} = 27{,}55{,}20{,}000 \times 0.35$$ $$\boxed{MPP_D^{actual} \approx ₹ 9{,}64{,}32{,}000}$$ **Step 9: Severity Classification.** $F\&EI = 85.6$ falls in the range **61–96**, hence classified as **MODERATE**. --- ### **Question B [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **domino effect** in process plants. How does inventory analysis help identify domino-prone clusters? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **Chemical Exposure Index (CEI)** as developed by Dow Chemical. A plant releases **chlorine (Cl₂)** at a rate of **$V = 220$ kg/min** from a leaking storage vessel. The **ERPG-1** for chlorine is **1 ppm**. Compute the CEI and interpret the result. If the same release rate were to occur for **ammonia (NH₃)** with ERPG-1 = **25 ppm**, how would the index change and why? **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Part (a):** **Definition of Domino Effect:** A domino effect (or cascade effect) in a process plant is the propagation of a primary accident from one unit to one or more adjacent units, leading to a sequence of secondary and tertiary events whose combined consequences far exceed those of the initial incident. The primary event may be a fire, explosion, or toxic release; the secondary event is triggered by thermal radiation, overpressure, or toxic cloud drift impinging on a neighbouring inventory. **Block Schematic of Domino Cascade:** ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Unit A Primary Inventory] --> B[Primary Event e.g. VCE] B --> C{Unit B within
Overpressure radius?} C -- Yes --> D[Unit B Inventory Breach] D --> E[Secondary Event Pool Fire] E --> F{Unit C within
Thermal radius?} F -- Yes --> G[Unit C BLEVE] G --> H[Tertiary Event] ``` **Role of Inventory Analysis in Identifying Domino Clusters:** 1. **Plot Plan Mapping:** Inventory analysis (Layer 4) places every inventory item on the plot plan with coordinates. Units whose radii of exposure overlap are immediately visible. 2. **Inventory Magnitude Check:** Two units each with $F\&EI > 97$ (Heavy/Severe) within 50 m of each other constitute a domino cluster. 3. **Hazard Type Compatibility:** A flammable inventory next to a toxic inventory produces a "combined hazard" domino (e.g., a chlorine tank adjacent to an LPG bullet — a fire under a toxic vessel leads to boiling liquid expansion). 4. **Population Density Mapping:** Inventory analysis cross-references with personnel occupancy to identify domino *consequences* on workers. Industries use the **"Dow F&EI Proximity Rule"** — if two units have overlapping radii of exposure, a domino analysis is mandatory. --- **Model Solution for Part (b):** **Overview of CEI:** The **Chemical Exposure Index (CEI)** is a Dow Chemical index (1994, latest revision 2016) developed specifically to rank the relative acute toxic hazard of airborne chemical releases. Unlike F&EI, CEI ignores fire/explosion potential and focuses on: - Airborne release rate $V$ (kg/min) - Toxicological endpoint — typically **ERPG-1** (mild, transient effects) or **ERPG-2** (serious health effects) The general CEI form (simplified atmospheric release) is: $$CEI = 65.5 \times \dfrac{V}{\text{ERPG}-1}$$ **Computation for Chlorine:** $$CEI_{Cl_2} = 65.5 \times \dfrac{220}{1} = 65.5 \times 220$$ $$\boxed{CEI_{Cl_2} = 14{,}410}$$ **Interpretation:** A CEI of **14,410** is far in excess of the action threshold of 1000. This indicates a **severely toxic release scenario** requiring: - Multiple redundant isolation valves - Real-time chlorine gas detectors with emergency scrubber activation - Population-level consequence analysis - Notification of district emergency response authorities **Computation for Ammonia (same release rate):** $$CEI_{NH_3} = 65.5 \times \dfrac{220}{25} = 65.5 \times 8.8$$ $$\boxed{CEI_{NH_3} = 576.4}$$ **Comparison and Engineering Insight:** CEI for chlorine is approximately **25 times larger** than CEI for ammonia at the same mass release rate. This is because chlorine has a much lower ERPG-1 (1 ppm vs. 25 ppm) — i.e., chlorine is toxicologically more hazardous at lower concentrations. The index thus captures the **dose-response** relationship correctly: it is not the *mass* released that matters alone, but the *concentration at the receptor*. This is why a kilogram of chlorine released near a populated area is treated as a far more serious safety case than a kilogram of ammonia, even though ammonia is also toxic. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. Students often use ERPG-2 or ERPG-3 in place of ERPG-1 — the problem **must** specify which is intended. > 2. The constant **65.5** is not universal — it depends on the Dow CEI version and the chosen dispersion model. Always state the source. > 3. Students frequently forget unit consistency. $V$ is in **kg/min** but ERPG is in **ppm** — the constant carries the unit conversion. Do not insert a density term in this simplified form. > 4. In Part (a) domino questions, students describe the *consequence* but forget to mention how **inventory analysis (radius of exposure)** quantitatively identifies the at-risk neighbours — that is the core link. > 5. For Dow F&EI, students often **sum the wrong GPH items** (e.g., adding "Endothermic" and "Exothermic" simultaneously — they are mutually exclusive). Read the question stem carefully. > 6. The Damage Factor must be read from the **Dow chart**, not computed analytically. In KTU papers, if a chart is provided, students lose marks by quoting approximate analytical values without justification. --- ### **TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember** - [ ] Inventory Analysis is the **first step** in process plant hazard identification — no advanced index (F&EI, CEI) is meaningful without it. - [ ] **Material Factor (MF)** uses **NFPA 704** ratings: $MF = N_f \times N_r$, with oxidiser correction for oxygen/chlorine dioxide/hydrogen peroxide. - [ ] **Process Unit Hazards Factor** is $PUHF = 1.0 + GPH + SPH$; **GPH** applies generally; **SPH** applies only when specific dangerous conditions exist. - [ ] **F&EI = MF × PUHF**, categorised as Light (1–60), Moderate (61–96), Heavy (97–127), Severe (>127). - [ ] **Radius of Exposure** is $R = 0.256 \times F\&EI$ in metres (Dow 8th edition). - [ ] **Damage Factor** is read from the Dow Radius vs. Damage Radius chart (empirical). - [ ] **MPP_D actual = Replacement Cost × DF × 0.82 × LCCF**. - [ ] **CEI** focuses on toxic release: $CEI = 65.5 \times V / \text{ERPG}-1$; threshold for concern is **CEI > 1000**. - [ ] **Domino effect** must be evaluated when overlapping radii of exposure exist between adjacent units. - [ ] Always identify and report **CAS numbers, NFPA ratings, ERPG values, TLVs, LFL/UFL, flash point, AIT** in any inventory analysis report. - [ ] Threshold screening uses **OSHA PSM**, **EPA RMP**, **KSPCB**, and **Factories and Boilers** rules; familiarity with cut-offs (e.g., chlorine 1500 lb, propane 10,000 lb) is essential. - [ ] Five-layer workflow: Identification → Quantity/State → Property → Location → Threshold Screening. - [ ] The Bhopal disaster (1984) and Flixborough disaster (1974) are classic KTU-referenced case studies of inventory analysis failures. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > In Module 3 questions, examiners expect you to **state the published MF** for common chemicals (acetone = 16, toluene = 16, methane = 21, hydrogen = 21, acrylonitrile = 24). Memorising at least 8–10 standard MFs will save calculation time in the ESE. Always end your F&EI calculation with a **one-line severity classification** and a **one-line recommendation**.

topics.title: Dow Fire and Explosion Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dow Fire and Explosion Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)** is a quantitative, objective scoring methodology developed originally by the **Dow Chemical Company in 1964** to rank the relative fire and explosion hazard potential of individual process units within a chemical or petrochemical plant. It is widely accepted as a **"ranking"** tool rather than an absolute design specification, allowing safety engineers to identify the most hazardous units in a facility so that risk-reduction resources (loss-control measures) can be prioritized rationally.

Mathematically, the index is the product of an intrinsic property of the chemical (the **Material Factor**) and two dimensionless penalty multipliers that account for process conditions and layout (the **General Process Hazards Factor** and the **Special Process Hazards Factor**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU's PECHT743 Module 3 specifically tests the student's ability to (a) explain the Dow F&EI methodology step-by-step, (b) compute the index numerically for a given process unit, and (c) interpret the resulting hazard ranking.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hospital emergency room where patients are continuously being admitted with varying severities of illness. The doctors do not have the resources to treat every patient with maximum care, so they use a **triage scoring system** — they quickly assess each patient and assign a numerical "severity score." Patients with the highest scores receive attention first.

The **Dow F&EI works exactly like that triage system**:
- The **Material Factor (MF)** is the patient's baseline "illness" — the intrinsic toxicity and reactivity of the chemical.
- The **General Process Hazards (GPH)** are like the patient's vital signs — temperature, pressure, quantity, all general indicators that any chemical would respond to.
- The **Special Process Hazards (SPH)** are the patient's specific pre-existing conditions (diabetes, allergies, etc.) — special conditions unique to that specific chemical/process combination.
- The **final F&EI score** tells you "how sick" the process unit is, so you know where to apply immediate life-saving measures.

A unit with **F&EI = 50** is like a mildly sick patient (low priority), while **F&EI > 159** is the equivalent of a critically ill patient requiring intensive care (severe hazard).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Categories

The Dow F&EI classifies process units into the following hazard bands:

| **F&EI Range** | **Hazard Degree** |
|:---:|:---:|
| **1 – 60** | Light |
| **61 – 96** | Moderate |
| **97 – 127** | Intermediate |
| **128 – 158** | Heavy |
| **159 and above** | Severe |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant to Remember:** The radius of exposure is empirically derived from approximately **250 historical major fire incidents** catalogued by Dow Chemical. The empirical constant **0.256** (imperial, feet) and **0.84** (SI, meters) are derived from this statistical correlation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six-Stage Calculation Logic

The Dow F&EI methodology is executed through a disciplined, sequential logic. Each stage feeds numerically into the next.

**Stage 1 — Identify the Process Unit**
The first step is to partition the plant into discrete, manageable "process units." A process unit is a logical grouping of equipment (reactors, columns, exchangers, drums, pumps, piping) that performs a coherent function. Typical candidates are distillation columns, reactors, storage spheres, compressor houses.

**Stage 2 — Determine the Material Factor (MF)**
The MF is the intrinsic energy content and reactivity of the worst-case chemical handled in the unit. It is read directly from tables published in the Dow Guide based on the chemical's **Heat of Combustion** (for flammability) and **Reactivity** with water/other substances. The MF typically ranges from **1 (slate dust, sand) to 40 (nitroglycerine, acetylene)**. Common reference values:
- Methanol: MF = 16
- Acetone: MF = 16
- Hexane: MF = 16
- Styrene: MF = 24
- Hydrogen: MF = 21

**Stage 3 — Apply General Process Hazards Penalties (F₁)**
Six generic "operational penalties" that apply to any chemical:
1. Exothermic chemical reactions (+1 to +3)
2. Endothermic processes (+1 to +3)
3. Material handling and transfer (+1 to +3)
4. Enclosed or indoor process units (+1)
5. Access for emergency response (+1)
6. Drainage and spill control (+1 to +2)

The penalty factor is **F₁ = 1.00** (sum of normalized penalties, default 1.00 = no penalty).

**Stage 4 — Apply Special Process Hazards Penalties (F₂)**
Twelve specific penalties tied to the chemistry and configuration:
1. Toxic materials (+1 to +5)
2. Sub-atmospheric pressure (+1 to +3)
3. Operation in or near flammable range (+1 to +3)
4. Dust explosion (+1 to +3)
5. Relief pressure (+1 to +4)
6. Low temperature (+1 to +3)
7. Quantity of flammable/unstable material (+1 to +6)
8. Corrosion and erosion (+1 to +3)
9. Leakage from joints and packing (+1 to +3)
10. Use of fired equipment (+1)
11. Hot oil heat exchange system (+1)
12. Rotating equipment (+1)

**Stage 5 — Compute Process Unit Hazards Factor (F₃) and F&EI**
$$F_3 = F_1 + F_2$$

$$\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times F_3$$

**Stage 6 — Translate to Consequence Quantities**
Using the F&EI, derive:
- Damage Factor (DF) — from published lookup table
- Radius of Exposure (R) — empirically correlated
- Area of Exposure (A) — geometric from radius
- Maximum Probable Property Damage (MPPD)
- Maximum Probable Days Outage (MPDO)
- Business Interruption Loss (BI)

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Notes** |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| Process Unit Hazards Factor | $F_3 = F_1 + F_2$ | dimensionless | $F_1$ default = 1.00 |
| Dow Fire & Explosion Index | $\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times F_3$ | dimensionless | Final index score |
| Radius of Exposure (imperial) | $R = 0.256 \times \text{F\&EI}$ | feet | Empirical correlation |
| Radius of Exposure (SI) | $R = 0.84 \times \text{F\&EI}$ | meters | Empirical correlation |
| Area of Exposure | $A = \pi R^2$ | m² or ft² | Geometric circle |
| Replacement Value of Area | $V_{\text{area}} = A \times \text{Value per unit area}$ | currency | Plant-specific |
| Maximum Probable Property Damage | $\text{MPPD} = V_{\text{area}} \times \text{DF}$ | currency | DF from lookup table |
| Maximum Probable Days Outage | $\text{MPDO} = f(\text{MPPD})$ | days | From MPPD-MPDO curve |
| Business Interruption Loss | $\text{BI} = \dfrac{\text{MPPD}}{100} \times \text{VPM} \times \text{MPDO}/30$ | currency | VPM = value of monthly production |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Note:** Students often confuse **F₁** with the absolute value **1 + penalties**. The convention is **F₁ = 1.00 baseline + Σ(penalties)**. A unit with no penalties has F₁ = 1.00, NOT 0. F₂ follows the same convention. The factors are normalized so that the bare chemical alone gives F&EI = MF × 1.00.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In real engineering practice, the Dow F&EI is used to:
1. **Prioritize Loss Prevention Surveys** — plant managers use it to schedule safety audits.
2. **Compare Design Alternatives** — engineers compare F&EI scores for different process layouts to select the safer option.
3. **Insurance Underwriting** — insurers use it as a quantitative basis for premium calculations.
4. **Regulatory Documentation** — many KTU-affiliated internships use the F&EI to comply with Factories Act / OSHA PSM requirements.
5. **Capital Allocation** — the MPPD and BI numbers feed directly into management's risk-informed CapEx decisions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Radius of Exposure Correlation

The empirical constant **0.256** in the formula $R = 0.256 \times \text{F\&EI}$ is derived from historical incident data on the **circular area around a process unit within which significant property damage occurred** during a major fire/explosion. The correlation was obtained by plotting F&EI values against the damage radius (in feet) observed in 250 documented incidents.

The mathematical underpinning of this empirical constant comes from a linear regression fit of the form:

$$R = k \times \text{F\&EI} + b$$

For high-F&EI units (severe), the regression yields a slope $k \approx 0.256$ ft/index-unit and intercept $b \approx 0$ (since a unit with negligible F&EI has near-zero radius). Therefore the line passes through the origin, giving:

$$\boxed{R = k \times \text{F\&EI} \approx 0.256 \times \text{F\&EI} \text{ feet}}$$

Converting to SI (1 ft = 0.3048 m):

$$\boxed{R = 0.256 \times 0.3048 \times \text{F\&EI} \approx 0.84 \times \text{F\&EI} \text{ meters}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Area of Exposure

The exposure is modeled as a **2-D circular footprint** centered on the process unit. The geometric area of a circle of radius R is:

$$\boxed{A = \pi R^2}$$

This represents the surface area "at risk" — i.e., the region within which all equipment and inventory will likely be damaged in the worst-case incident.

### 3.3 Derivation of MPPD (Maximum Probable Property Damage)

The MPPD accounts for the fact that not all property within the radius of exposure will be destroyed — only a percentage equal to the **Damage Factor (DF)**. The DF is empirically tabulated as a function of F&EI (a published lookup table in the Dow Guide). Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{MPPD} = V_{\text{area}} \times \text{DF}}$$

where $V_{\text{area}}$ is the **replacement cost** of the exposed area (including equipment, buildings, piping, instrumentation).

### 3.4 Derivation of Business Interruption (BI)

The BI combines MPPD, Value of Monthly Production (VPM), and the outage duration. The relationship used in the Dow methodology is:

$$\text{BI} = \frac{\text{MPPD}}{100} \times \text{VPM} \times \frac{\text{MPDO}}{30}$$

The logic behind this: (a) MPPD/100 is a normalized intensity factor, (b) VPM scales with plant throughput, (c) MPDO/30 normalizes days to a fractional month. The result is a **currency amount** representing lost production during the recovery period.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

**Problem Statement:**
A process unit in a refinery handles **methyl ethyl ketone (MEK)**. The Material Factor is **MF = 16**. The General Process Hazard penalty sums to **F₁ = 1.50** (penalty contributions from quantity handling, drainage, and access). The Special Process Hazard penalties sum to **F₂ = 3.20** (relief pressure +2, low temperature +0.5, corrosion +0.7). Compute the F&EI, the Radius of Exposure (in meters), and the Area of Exposure. Take Damage Factor DF = 0.82 (from F&EI lookup curve).

**Step 1 — Compute F₃:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_3 &= F_1 + F_2 \\
&= 1.50 + 3.20 \\
&= 4.70
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute F&EI:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{F\&EI} &= \text{MF} \times F_3 \\
&= 16 \times 4.70 \\
&= 75.2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret Hazard Degree:**
F&EI = 75.2 falls in the range **61–96**, so the unit is classified as **"Moderate"** hazard.

**Step 4 — Radius of Exposure (meters):**

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= 0.84 \times \text{F\&EI} \\
&= 0.84 \times 75.2 \\
&= 63.17 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Area of Exposure:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \pi R^2 \\
&= \pi \times (63.17)^2 \\
&= \pi \times 3990.4 \\
&= 12537.4 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — MPPD (given V_area = ₹ 5,00,00,000):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MPPD} &= V_{\text{area}} \times \text{DF} \\
&= 5 \times 10^7 \times 0.82 \\
&= 4.10 \times 10^7 \\
&= \text{₹ } 4.10 \text{ Crores}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Penalty Categorization

| **Hazard Class** | **Typical Penalty Range** | **Example Penalties** |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| General Process Hazards (F₁) | 0 to 6 | Exothermic reaction, indoor unit, drainage |
| Special Process Hazards (F₂) | 0 to 12 | Toxicity, sub-atmospheric, dust, quantity |
| Material Factor (MF) | 1 to 40 | Hydrogen (21), Gasoline (16), Styrene (24) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does MF dominate?** A unit with MF = 40 and small penalties can still yield F&EI > 159 (severe). Conversely, even high penalties on MF = 1 (inert dust) will keep the unit in "Light" hazard. This shows that **intrinsic chemical energy is the dominant driver** of hazard.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Flow Diagram of Dow F&EI Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Select Process Unit] --> B[Determine Material Factor MF]
    B --> C[Sum General Process Hazards Penalties to F1]
    C --> D[Sum Special Process Hazards Penalties to F2]
    D --> E[Compute Process Unit Hazards Factor F3 = F1 + F2]
    E --> F[Compute F&EI = MF times F3]
    F --> G{Lookup Damage Factor DF from F&EI Table}
    G --> H[Compute Radius of Exposure R = 0.84 times F&EI meters]
    H --> I[Compute Area of Exposure A = pi times R squared]
    I --> J[Compute Replacement Value of Area]
    J --> K[Compute MPPD = Value times DF]
    K --> L[Lookup MPDO from MPPD-MPDO Curve]
    L --> M[Compute Business Interruption BI Loss]
    M --> N[Classify Hazard Degree from F&EI]
    N --> O[End: Final Loss Estimation Report]
```

### 4.2 Hazard Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Dow F&EI Score] --> Q{F&EI between 1 and 60}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Light Hazard - Routine Precautions]
    Q -->|No| S{F&EI between 61 and 96}
    S -->|Yes| T[Moderate Hazard - Enhanced Controls]
    S -->|No| U{F&EI between 97 and 127}
    U -->|Yes| V[Intermediate Hazard - Special Protection]
    U -->|No| W{F&EI between 128 and 158}
    W -->|Yes| X[Heavy Hazard - Engineered Protection]
    W -->|No| Y[F&EI 159 and above]
    Y --> Z[Severe Hazard - Total Loss Prevention Measures]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Penalty Aggregation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GPH[General Process Hazards F1]
        GPH1[Exothermic Reaction Penalty]
        GPH2[Endothermic Penalty]
        GPH3[Material Handling Penalty]
        GPH4[Enclosed Unit Penalty]
        GPH5[Drainage Penalty]
        GPH6[Access Penalty]
    end
    subgraph SPH[Special Process Hazards F2]
        SPH1[Toxicity Penalty]
        SPH2[Pressure Penalty]
        SPH3[Flammable Range Penalty]
        SPH4[Dust Explosion Penalty]
        SPH5[Corrosion Penalty]
        SPH6[Quantity Penalty]
    end
    GPH --> SUM1[Sum F1 Penalties]
    SPH --> SUM2[Sum F2 Penalties]
    SUM1 --> F3[Compute F3 = F1 + F2]
    SUM2 --> F3
    MF[Material Factor MF] --> FEI[Compute F&EI = MF x F3]
    F3 --> FEI
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

Define the **Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)**. What are the three primary input parameters used to compute the F&EI?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI) is a quantitative hazard classification methodology developed by the Dow Chemical Company to rank the relative fire, explosion, and reactivity hazard potential of individual process units in a chemical plant. It serves as a **ranking tool** for prioritizing loss-control resources.

The three primary input parameters are:
1. **Material Factor (MF)** — an intrinsic property of the chemical, ranging from 1 (inert) to 40 (highly unstable).
2. **General Process Hazards Factor (F₁)** — penalty factor accounting for generic process conditions (default 1.00 + penalties).
3. **Special Process Hazards Factor (F₂)** — penalty factor accounting for specific chemistry-driven and configuration-driven risks (default 1.00 + penalties).

The index is computed as: $\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times (F_1 + F_2)$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Question]**

What is the **Material Factor (MF)**? List any **two example chemicals** with their approximate MF values.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Material Factor (MF)** is a dimensionless number assigned to a chemical that quantifies its intrinsic potential energy released during combustion or uncontrolled reaction, along with its chemical reactivity. It is the starting point of the Dow F&EI calculation and is determined from published tables in the Dow Guide based on the chemical's heat of combustion and reactivity (with water or other substances).

Examples:
1. **Methanol** — MF = 16
2. **Styrene** — MF = 24
3. **Hydrogen** — MF = 21

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write only the chemical names without quoting the **numerical MF value**, losing 1 mark. Always cite the **numerical range** (MF = 1 to 40) and **at least one example with its MF** to secure full 3 marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure** to calculate the Dow Fire and Explosion Index for a process unit.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A process unit handles **benzene**. Given: MF = 16, F₁ = 1.60, F₂ = 2.40. Compute the F&EI, the Radius of Exposure in meters, and the Area of Exposure. State the hazard classification. (Assume DF = 0.65 from lookup and $V_{\text{area}}$ = ₹ 80,00,000.)

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The procedure to calculate the Dow F&EI involves the following systematic steps:

1. **Step 1 — Select the Process Unit:** Partition the plant into discrete process units (e.g., a distillation column, a reactor train). Identify the worst-case chemical handled.

2. **Step 2 — Determine the Material Factor (MF):** Look up MF from the published Dow Material Factor table. It is based on Heat of Combustion and chemical reactivity.

3. **Step 3 — Calculate General Process Hazards Factor (F₁):** Apply six generic penalties (exothermic, endothermic, material handling, enclosed unit, drainage, access). Sum these penalties and add to the baseline of 1.00.

4. **Step 4 — Calculate Special Process Hazards Factor (F₂):** Apply twelve specific penalties (toxicity, sub-atmospheric, flammable range, dust, relief pressure, low temp, quantity, corrosion, leakage, fired equipment, hot oil, rotating equipment). Sum these and add to 1.00.

5. **Step 5 — Compute Process Unit Hazards Factor (F₃):** $F_3 = F_1 + F_2$.

6. **Step 6 — Compute F&EI:** $\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times F_3$.

7. **Step 7 — Classify Hazard Degree:** Compare F&EI with the standard bands (1–60 Light, 61–96 Moderate, 97–127 Intermediate, 128–158 Heavy, ≥159 Severe).

8. **Step 8 — Derive Consequence Quantities:** Compute Damage Factor (lookup), Radius of Exposure, Area of Exposure, MPPD, MPDO, and BI.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1: Compute F₃**

$$F_3 = F_1 + F_2 = 1.60 + 2.40 = 4.00$$

**Step 2: Compute F&EI**

$$\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times F_3 = 16 \times 4.00 = 64$$

**Step 3: Hazard Classification**

F&EI = 64 falls in the range **61–96**, hence classified as **"Moderate"** hazard.

**Step 4: Radius of Exposure (in meters)**

$$R = 0.84 \times \text{F\&EI} = 0.84 \times 64 = 53.76 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Area of Exposure**

$$A = \pi R^2 = \pi \times (53.76)^2 = \pi \times 2890.0 = 9079.4 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 6: Maximum Probable Property Damage**

$$\text{MPPD} = V_{\text{area}} \times \text{DF} = 80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.65 = \text{₹ } 52{,}00{,}000$$

**Final Answers:** F&EI = 64 (Moderate), R = 53.76 m, A = 9079.4 m², MPPD = ₹ 52,00,000.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **General Process Hazards (GPH)** and **Special Process Hazards (SPH)** in Dow's methodology. List **at least three examples** from each category with their typical penalty range.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A naphtha storage tank farm has MF = 16, F₁ = 2.00, F₂ = 4.50. Compute (i) F&EI, (ii) Radius of Exposure in feet, (iii) Area of Exposure in ft². If the replacement value of exposed area is **$20 million** and DF = 0.75, compute (iv) the MPPD.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**General Process Hazards (F₁)** are penalty factors that reflect common operational risks applicable to any chemical process:

1. **Exothermic chemical reactions** — penalty range +1 to +3 (based on reaction energy and temperature).
2. **Enclosed or indoor process units** — penalty range +1 (confined space amplifies blast damage).
3. **Material handling and transfer** — penalty range +1 to +3 (number and complexity of transfer operations).

Other GPHs include endothermic processes, drainage/spill control, and access for emergency response.

**Special Process Hazards (F₂)** are penalty factors specific to the chemistry and configuration:

1. **Toxicity of materials** — penalty range +1 to +5 (based on TLV/PEL values).
2. **Operation in or near flammable range** — penalty range +1 to +3.
3. **Quantity of flammable/unstable material** — penalty range +1 to +6 (scales with mass).

Other SPHs include sub-atmospheric pressure, dust explosion, relief pressure, low temperature, corrosion, leakage, fired equipment, hot oil heat exchange, and rotating equipment.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1: Compute F₃**

$$F_3 = F_1 + F_2 = 2.00 + 4.50 = 6.50$$

**Step 2: Compute F&EI**

$$\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times F_3 = 16 \times 6.50 = 104$$

**(Falls in the range 97–127, hence "Intermediate" hazard.)**

**Step 3: Radius of Exposure (feet)**

$$R = 0.256 \times \text{F\&EI} = 0.256 \times 104 = 26.62 \text{ ft}$$

**Step 4: Area of Exposure (ft²)**

$$A = \pi R^2 = \pi \times (26.62)^2 = \pi \times 708.6 = 2225.9 \text{ ft}^2$$

**Step 5: MPPD**

$$\text{MPPD} = V_{\text{area}} \times \text{DF} = 20{,}000{,}000 \times 0.75 = \$15{,}000{,}000$$

**Final Answers:** (i) F&EI = 104 (Intermediate), (ii) R = 26.62 ft, (iii) A = 2225.9 ft², (iv) MPPD = \$ 15 million.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong radius constant:** Students often confuse **0.256** (feet) with **0.84** (meters). Using the wrong one with the wrong unit is a 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting to add 1.00 baseline to F₁ and F₂:** Always remember the baseline — penalties are added on top of 1.00.
> 3. **Confusing GPH with SPH:** GPH applies to any process; SPH applies to specific chemistry/configuration. This distinction is a frequent 7-mark discriminator.
> 4. **Skipping the hazard classification step:** Always state the band (Light/Moderate/Intermediate/Heavy/Severe). Skipping it costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Units inconsistency:** Mixing m and ft² in the area calculation. Always declare units explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition:** Dow F&EI is a quantitative ranking tool (not an absolute design spec) for fire and explosion hazards of process units.
- **Origin:** Dow Chemical Company, 1964; current edition is the 7th (1994) Hazard Classification Guide.
- **Core Formula:** $\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times (F_1 + F_2)$.
- **MF Range:** 1 (inert) to 40 (highly unstable like nitroglycerine).
- **F₁ Range:** 1.00 baseline + up to +6 penalties.
- **F₂ Range:** 1.00 baseline + up to +12 penalties.
- **Hazard Bands:** 1–60 Light | 61–96 Moderate | 97–127 Intermediate | 128–158 Heavy | ≥159 Severe.
- **Radius (m):** $R = 0.84 \times \text{F\&EI}$.
- **Radius (ft):** $R = 0.256 \times \text{F\&EI}$.
- **Area:** $A = \pi R^2$.
- **MPPD:** $\text{MPPD} = V_{\text{area}} \times \text{DF}$ (DF from lookup table).
- **MPDO:** Read from MPPD–MPDO empirical curve.
- **BI Loss:** $\text{BI} = (\text{MPPD}/100) \times \text{VPM} \times (\text{MPDO}/30)$.
- **Six GPH Categories:** Exothermic, Endothermic, Material handling, Enclosed unit, Access, Drainage.
- **Twelve SPH Categories:** Toxicity, Sub-atmospheric, Flammable range, Dust, Relief pressure, Low temp, Quantity, Corrosion, Leakage, Fired equipment, Hot oil, Rotating equipment.
- **Always state the hazard classification band explicitly** — KTU expects this in the final answer.
- **Use correct unit conventions** — distinguish feet vs meters in the radius constant.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mond Fire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Mond Fire # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Mond Process & Mond Fire The **Mond Process** is a vapour-phase metallurgical refining technique developed by **Ludwig Mond in 1899** for the purification of nickel. It exploits the unique ability of nickel to form a volatile carbonyl complex, **Nickel Tetracarbonyl [Ni(CO)₄]**, which can be selectively formed, transported as a gas, and then thermally decomposed to yield **99.99% pure nickel**. **Mond Fire** is the process safety term used to describe the catastrophic fire and explosion hazards associated with the Mond Process. The hazard arises because **Ni(CO)₄ is simultaneously**: - A highly flammable vapour - A pyrophoric / auto-ignitable compound (ignition temperature ≈ **60 °C in air**) - An acutely toxic chemical (TLV-TWA = **0.05 ppm / 0.12 mg/m³**) - A potent reducing agent capable of violent decomposition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The Mond case study is a classic example used in KTU to teach the **interaction between chemistry, equipment design, and human toxicity** in process plants. It is a high-yield topic for Part B (14-mark) questions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Intuition: "The Chameleon Metal Trick"** > Imagine a sponge (nickel ore) soaked in dirty water (impurities). The Mond process is like a magic solvent (carbon monoxide gas) that, when blown through the sponge, picks up **only** the sponge material itself and turns it into an invisible vapour. The vapour is then carried to a separate, clean chamber where the solvent is "switched off" by heat, and pure sponge material is deposited. The genius is also the danger — the invisible vapour is **deadly** the moment it meets air, like a snake that remains lethal even after separating from the rest of the system. ## 1.3 Process Overview (Three Functional Stages) | Stage | Reaction | Temperature | Purpose | |:------|:---------|:------------|:--------| | Reduction | NiO + H₂ → Ni + H₂O | 350 – 400 °C | Convert oxide to metallic Ni | | Carbonylation | Ni + 4CO → Ni(CO)₄ | 50 – 100 °C | Volatilise Ni as gaseous carbonyl | | Decomposition | Ni(CO)₄ → Ni + 4CO | 230 – 300 °C | Recover pure Ni & regenerate CO | > [!IMPORTANT] > The temperature window **between 50 °C and 230 °C** is the **"Mond Fire Danger Zone"** — within this range, Ni(CO)₄ is a stable gas in the absence of air, but catastrophic if air ingress occurs. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Chemistry of the Mond Process The chemistry proceeds via a **two-step volatile intermediate mechanism**: **Step 1 — Reduction (Pre-treatment):** The nickel oxide ore (typically a matte containing ~75% Ni, ~25% Cu/Fe/S) is first roasted to remove sulfur, then reduced in a hydrogen atmosphere: $$\text{NiO}_{(s)} + \text{H}_{2(g)} \rightarrow \text{Ni}_{(s)} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(g)}$$ **Step 2 — Carbonylation (Volatilisation):** At low temperature, metallic nickel alone (not Cu, Fe, or Co under these conditions) reacts reversibly with CO: $$\text{Ni}_{(s)} + 4\,\text{CO}_{(g)} \rightleftharpoons \text{Ni(CO)}_{4(g)}, \quad \Delta H = -191 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ The reaction is **exothermic and reversible**. Le Chatelier's principle is exploited: at low temperature the equilibrium shifts **right** (carbonyl forms), and at high temperature it shifts **left** (carbonyl decomposes). **Step 3 — Decomposition (Deposition):** $$\text{Ni(CO)}_{4(g)} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{Ni}_{(s)} + 4\,\text{CO}_{(g)}$$ The pure nickel pellets are deposited on nickel "seed" nuclei, while the liberated CO is recycled. ## 2.2 Why Mond Fire is a Unique Process Safety Problem The hazard is rooted in a **triple-threat molecule**: > [!NOTE] > 1. **Toxicity** — Ni(CO)₄ is approximately **30 times more toxic than CO** alone. The LEL (Lower Explosive Limit) is **2 vol%**, and the odour threshold (musty, sooty) is far **above** the toxic threshold → smell gives **no warning**. > 2. **Flammability** — Vapour forms explosive mixtures with air over a wide range (LEL = 2%, UEL = 34%). > 3. **Auto-decomposition** — Above **~150 °C**, Ni(CO)₄ can undergo **violent, exothermic decomposition** even in absence of air, generating metallic Ni dust (pyrophoric) and hot CO. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Value / Formula | Units | Notes | |:----------|:----------------|:------|:------| | Molar mass of Ni(CO)₄ | $M = 58.69 + 4(28.01) = 170.73$ | g/mol | Used for ppm ↔ mg/m³ | | TLV-TWA (ACGIH) | **0.05** | ppm (8 hr) | Acute inhalation toxicity | | IDLH (Immediately Dangerous) | **5** | ppm | NIOSH value | | Auto-ignition temperature | **~60** | °C | In presence of air | | LEL / UEL in air | **2 / 34** | vol% | Extremely wide range | | ppm → mg/m³ conversion | $C_{mg/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{M}{24.45}$ | mg/m³ | At 25 °C, 1 atm | | Stoichiometric O₂ for combustion | 6 mol O₂ per mol Ni(CO)₄ | — | Balanced equation below | | Mond Carbonylation T | **50 – 100** | °C | Exothermic, equilibrium controlled | | Mond Decomposition T | **230 – 300** | °C | Endothermic reverse | | Heat of formation ΔHf | $-632$ | kJ/mol | Highly exothermic formation | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The Mond process is the **basis for producing high-purity nickel** used in: - **Stainless steel** (austenitic grades) for chemical reactors - **Coinage metals** and battery electrodes - **Superalloys** for jet engine turbine blades - **Catalyst precursor** in hydrogenation plants From a safety-engineering perspective, the Mond case study is a **textbook lesson** in: - Hazard identification (HAZOP) of organometallic intermediates - Designing for **inert gas blanketing** in equipment - Continuous **gas monitoring** with IR / UV spectrometers - Establishing **emergency isolation valves (EIVs)** and **explosion suppression** # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Oxygen Requirement for Complete Combustion The combustion of Ni(CO)₄ in excess air proceeds as: $$\text{Ni(CO)}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NiO} + 4\,\text{CO}_2$$ But the NiO itself can be further oxidised (not required in air, so we balance with NiO on the right). For **complete combustion with no free carbon**: $$\text{Ni(CO)}_4 + 6\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NiO} + 4\,\text{CO}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \text{ (excess assumed)}$$ **Step 1.** Count Ni: 1 on left → 1 NiO on right. **Step 2.** Count C: 4 → 4 CO₂ on right. **Step 3.** Count O on right: 1 (NiO) + 8 (4 CO₂) = 9 oxygen atoms. **Step 4.** O available from Ni(CO)₄ = 4 (already in molecule). **Step 5.** Additional O needed from O₂ = (9 − 4) / 2 = 2.5 → 6 O₂ gives 12 O atoms → 4 already present in carbonyl → 8 needed for 4 CO₂ + 1 for NiO = 9 → 4 already there → 5 still needed → 2.5 O₂. Therefore: $$\text{Ni(CO)}_4 + 2.5\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NiO} + 4\,\text{CO}_2$$ Multiplying by 2 (to clear the half): $$\boxed{2\,\text{Ni(CO)}_4 + 5\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{NiO} + 8\,\text{CO}_2}$$ **Stoichiometric ratio = 2.5 mol O₂ per mol Ni(CO)₄** (or 5/2). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Conversion of TLV from ppm to mg/m³ For converting an air-borne concentration in ppm to mg/m³ at **25 °C and 1 atm**: $$C_{mg/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \frac{M}{24.45}$$ For Ni(CO)₄, M = 170.73 g/mol, TLV = 0.05 ppm. **Step 1.** Substitute values: $$C_{mg/m^3} = 0.05 \times \frac{170.73}{24.45}$$ **Step 2.** Evaluate denominator to 4 significant figures: $$\frac{170.73}{24.45} = 6.9828$$ **Step 3.** Multiply by 0.05: $$C_{mg/m^3} = 0.05 \times 6.9828 = 0.349 \, mg/m^3$$ **Result:** $$\boxed{C_{TLV} \approx 0.35 \, mg/m^3 \text{ for Ni(CO)}_4}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mass of Ni Produced per kg of Ni(CO)₄ Required for material balance in a Mond plant design. **Step 1.** Moles of Ni(CO)₄ in 1 kg: $$n = \frac{1000 \, g}{170.73 \, g/mol} = 5.857 \, mol$$ **Step 2.** Moles of Ni produced (1:1 stoichiometry from decomposition): $$n_{Ni} = 5.857 \, mol$$ **Step 3.** Mass of Ni: $$m_{Ni} = 5.857 \times 58.69 = 343.7 \, g$$ **Result:** $$\boxed{m_{Ni} \approx 0.344 \, kg \, Ni \text{ per kg of Ni(CO)}_4 \text{ decomposed}}$$ Mass yield ≈ **34.4 %**. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Lower Flammability Limit (Volume % Calculation) For a flammable vapour, the LEL expressed in mass per unit volume is: $$LEL_{mg/m^3} = \frac{LEL_{\%} \times M \times 10}{24.45}$$ **Step 1.** LEL of Ni(CO)₄ = 2 vol% → LEL fraction = 0.02. **Step 2.** Substitute M = 170.73 g/mol: $$LEL_{mg/m^3} = \frac{0.02 \times 170.73 \times 10}{24.45}$$ **Step 3.** Numerator: $0.02 \times 170.73 \times 10 = 34.146$ **Step 4.** Divide by 24.45: $$LEL_{mg/m^3} = \frac{34.146}{24.45} = 1.397 \, g/m^3 \approx 1397 \, mg/m^3$$ **Result:** $$\boxed{LEL_{Ni(CO)_4} \approx 1.4 \, g/m^3 \text{ at 25 °C}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This LEL value is **~4000 times the toxic TLV** (0.35 mg/m³). In other words, **the toxic threshold is reached long before the flammability threshold**, making Ni(CO)₄ a **toxicity-dominated hazard** rather than a flammability-dominated one. This is a key KTU insight. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Hazard Comparison Table | Property | CO | Ni(CO)₄ | Comment | |:---------|:---|:--------|:--------| | TLV (ppm) | 25 | 0.05 | Ni(CO)₄ is **500× more toxic** | | Odour threshold (ppm) | None | ~1 | **Both** give no early warning at toxic levels | | Auto-ignition T (°C) | 609 | 60 | Mond vapour ignites **spontaneously** near process T | | Density relative to air | 0.97 | 5.9 | Ni(CO)₄ is **heavy** → accumulates in pits | | LEL (vol%) | 12.5 | 2.0 | Mond vapour ignites more easily | --- ## 3.6 Engineering Control Derivation: Minimum Purge Volume for an Inerting Sequence For a vessel of volume V, the number of displacement volumes $N$ of inert gas required to reduce a flammable gas from $C_0$ to $C_f$ is: $$N = \frac{\ln(C_0 / C_f)}{\ln(1 + Q/V)}$$ For a well-mixed plug-flow approximation, simplification to a continuous stirred-tank reactor gives the practical engineering formula: $$C = C_0 \cdot e^{-Q t / V}$$ **Step 1.** Required final concentration $C_f$ for Ni(CO)₄ below 25 % of LEL: $$C_f = 0.25 \times 0.02 = 0.005 \text{ (i.e., 0.5 vol %)}$$ **Step 2.** Initial concentration $C_0$ = 1.0 (pure vapour). **Step 3.** Solve for the number of displacement volumes: $$N = \ln(1.0 / 0.005) = \ln(200) = 5.30$$ **Result:** $$\boxed{N \approx 5.3 \text{ vessel volumes of N}_2 \text{ purge required}}$$ This is why Mond plant purging sequences are **long** (often 8–12 hours for a reactor vessel). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mond Plant Process Flow with Safety Interlocks (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Ore Feed
NiO Matte] --> B[Roaster
Air at 700 C] B --> C[Reduction Reactor
H2 at 400 C] C --> D[Carbonyl Reactor
CO at 50 to 100 C] D --> E{NI CO4 Vapour
Sealed Duct} E --> F[Decomposer
Pellets at 250 C] F --> G[Pure Ni Pellets
99.99 percent] F --> H[CO Recycle
Compressor and Scrubber] H --> D D --> I[Safety Scrubber
Aqueous NaOH] I --> J[Liquid Effluent
Ni Recovery] E --> K[IR Gas Monitor
Detection at 0.05 ppm] K --> L{Safety PLC
Logic Solver} L -->|Trip| M[Emergency Isolation
Valves EIV] M --> D M --> F L --> N[N2 Inerting
System] L --> O[Vapour Recovery
Activated Carbon] L --> P[Fire Suppression
Foam and CO2] style D fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c00 style E fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c00 style F fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c00 style K fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#aa0 style L fill:#b0e0ff,stroke:#06c style P fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#080 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation:** > - The **red-shaded blocks** (Carbonyl Reactor, Vapour Duct, Decomposer) constitute the **primary Mond fire zone** — these are sealed, inerted, and continuously monitored. > - The **IR gas monitor** is the **first line of defence**, with detection limit at the toxic TLV. > - The **Safety PLC** executes a **SIL-2 interlock** that simultaneously: (i) closes EIVs, (ii) initiates N₂ inerting, (iii) starts vapour capture on carbon, and (iv) actuates fire suppression. ## 4.2 HAZOP Logic Flow for a Mond Carbonyl Reactor ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Process Variable] --> D[Deviation] D --> C[Causes] C --> S[Safety Consequences] S --> AC[Action Required] P1[Temperature High] --> D1[More than 100 C] D1 --> C1[Loss of CO Feed
or Exotherm Runaway] C1 --> S1[Ni CO4 Decomposes
Violently in Duct] S1 --> AC1[High Temperature Trip
Activate N2 Quench] P2[Pressure High] --> D2[More than 2 barg] D2 --> C2[CO Leak from Seal
or Blocked Vent] C2 --> S2[Toxic Release
Personnel Exposure] S2 --> AC2[Pressure Relief Valve
and EIV Shutdown] P3[Flow No CO] --> D3[Loss of Carbonyl
Carrier Gas] D3 --> C3[Reactor Cooling
No Reaction] C3 --> S3[Stagnant Ni CO4
Buildup] S3 --> AC3[Interlock to Safe
Nitrogen Blanket] P4[Air Ingress] --> D4[O2 Detected
More than 50 ppm] D4 --> C4[Seal Failure or
Drain Valve Open] C4 --> S4[Explosion and Fire
Mond Fire] S4 --> AC4[Triple Redundant
Oxygen Analyzers] style S4 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#900 style AC4 fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#080 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is Mond Fire? Why is it a unique safety hazard in process plants?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Mond Fire refers to the fire and explosion hazard arising from the volatilisation of nickel as **Ni(CO)₄** in the **Mond nickel refining process** developed by Ludwig Mond. It is unique because Ni(CO)₄ simultaneously possesses three hazardous properties: **(1)** extreme inhalation toxicity (TLV = 0.05 ppm, ~500× more toxic than CO), **(2)** high flammability with a very low LEL of 2 vol%, and **(3)** a low auto-ignition temperature of ~60 °C in air. The toxic threshold is reached **well below** the LEL, meaning operators may be fatally exposed before any flammability alarm is triggered, and the process operates at temperatures **above** the auto-ignition point of the vapour, making air ingress immediately catastrophic. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **State the three principal reactions of the Mond process and indicate the temperature window in which each occurs.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Reduction:** $\text{NiO} + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{Ni} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ at **350–400 °C**. 2. **Carbonylation:** $\text{Ni} + 4\,\text{CO} \rightarrow \text{Ni(CO)}_4$ at **50–100 °C** (exothermic). 3. **Decomposition:** $\text{Ni(CO)}_4 \rightarrow \text{Ni} + 4\,\text{CO}$ at **230–300 °C** (endothermic). The process is reversible: low temperature favours the carbonyl, high temperature favours free Ni. --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before You Write!** > - Do **NOT** confuse the Mond process with the **Mond–Langer** or **Hunter** processes (both are different and not in the KTU syllabus). > - Do **NOT** state that CO is "the toxic gas" — examiners expect you to identify **Ni(CO)₄** as the primary toxin. Marks lost = **up to 2**. > - Always **quote units** (ppm, °C, mg/m³) and **show conversion steps** in numerical parts. A correct answer without units loses ~1 mark. > - For 14-mark questions, **diagram** is **mandatory**. A neat block diagram of the process fetches a minimum of **2 marks** even if text is partial. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU Model Paper 2024, Module 3]* **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **Mond process** for refining of nickel. Identify the **three principal reactions** and explain why the temperature profile is critical to the process. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss in detail the **fire and explosion hazards** associated with the Mond process. What **engineering controls** are recommended to mitigate Mond Fire in a commercial plant? **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution (a):** 1. **Block Diagram:** Draw the four-stage flow as shown in Section 4.1 above (Feed → Roaster → Reduction → Carbonylation → Decomposer → Pure Ni + CO recycle). 2. **Three Reactions:** - **Roasting / Reduction:** $\text{NiO} + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{Ni} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (350–400 °C). - **Carbonylation:** $\text{Ni} + 4\,\text{CO} \rightarrow \text{Ni(CO)}_4$ (50–100 °C) — **exothermic**, so the temperature is held low to drive equilibrium towards the volatile product. - **Decomposition:** $\text{Ni(CO)}_4 \rightarrow \text{Ni} + 4\,\text{CO}$ (230–300 °C) — endothermic, CO is regenerated and recycled. 3. **Why temperature is critical:** The reaction is reversible with $\Delta H < 0$. By Le Chatelier's principle, **low T** favours the carbonyl product (right shift) and **high T** favours decomposition (left shift). The narrow operating window — never above 100 °C in the carbonylation reactor and always above 200 °C in the decomposer — is **essential**; if a hot spot occurs in the duct, premature decomposition and Mond Fire can result. **(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for reactions, 2 for temperature logic)** ### **Model Solution (b):** **Fire and Explosion Hazards (4 marks):** - **Wide flammable range:** LEL = 2 %, UEL = 34 % — gives a high probability of flammable mixtures in ducts. - **Low auto-ignition T (60 °C):** Air ingress in any part of the cold section (50–100 °C) causes immediate ignition. - **Heavy vapour (MW = 170.7, density ≈ 5.9× air):** Accumulates in low-lying areas, forming explosive pockets. - **Catalytic decomposition:** Even at temperatures as low as 150 °C, Ni(CO)₄ can decompose violently on hot surfaces, depositing pyrophoric Ni powder and liberating hot CO. **Engineering Controls (3 marks):** 1. **Inert gas blanketing:** Continuous N₂ or CO₂ pad in all vessels and ducts to maintain O₂ < 50 ppm. 2. **Tight sealing & welding:** All-carbon-steel hermetically welded construction; double mechanical seals on pumps. 3. **Continuous IR/UV monitoring** of the gas phase with trip at 0.05 ppm (toxic) and 25 % of LEL. 4. **Emergency Isolation Valves (EIVs)** at reactor inlet and outlet, with **fire-safe** certification. 5. **Vapour capture system** — activated carbon adsorbers with steam regeneration to capture any leaked Ni(CO)₄. 6. **Ventilation** — low-level mechanical exhaust (5–10 air changes/h) to disperse heavy vapour accumulations. 7. **PPE** — supplied-air respirators (SCBA) for all entry into the carbonyl area; no skin exposure (Ni(CO)₄ is also absorbed dermally). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** A Mond plant processes **1.2 kmol/h** of Ni(CO)₄ vapour through a decomposer. The vapour is initially at **80 °C** and must be heated to **250 °C** to deposit nickel. The heat of decomposition is **–191 kJ/mol** (endothermic reverse). Calculate: - (i) the **rate of heat input required** (kW) for the decomposition, and - (ii) the **mass of pure nickel** produced per hour. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **toxicological profile of Ni(CO)₄**. Why is it particularly dangerous compared to other industrial gases? What **occupational exposure limits** apply and what are the symptoms of acute exposure? **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution (a):** **Given:** - $\dot{n} = 1.2 \text{ kmol/h} = 1200 \text{ mol/h} = 1200/3600 = 0.3333 \text{ mol/s}$ - $\Delta H = 191 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (endothermic for decomposition) **(i) Rate of heat input:** $$\dot{Q} = \dot{n} \times \Delta H$$ Substituting: $$\dot{Q} = 0.3333 \, \frac{mol}{s} \times 191 \, \frac{kJ}{mol}$$ $$\dot{Q} = 63.67 \, kW$$ **Result:** $$\boxed{\dot{Q} \approx 63.7 \, kW}$$ **(ii) Mass of pure Ni per hour:** Stoichiometry: 1 mol Ni(CO)₄ → 1 mol Ni. Moles per hour = 1200 mol. $$m_{Ni} = 1200 \times 58.69 \, g = 70{,}428 \, g = 70.43 \, kg$$ **Result:** $$\boxed{m_{Ni} \approx 70.4 \, kg/h}$$ **(7 marks: 3 for (i), 4 for (ii))** ### **Model Solution (b):** **Toxicological Profile of Ni(CO)₄ — 7 marks:** 1. **Acute Toxicity (3 marks):** - **TLV-TWA (ACGIH):** 0.05 ppm = 0.35 mg/m³. - **IDLH (NIOSH):** 5 ppm — defined as the level posing **immediate or delayed threat to life**. - **LC₅₀ (rat, 30 min):** ≈ 35 ppm. - **Routes of entry:** inhalation (primary), **dermal absorption** (secondary but significant — liquid splash on skin), and ingestion (rare). - **Mechanism:** Ni(CO)₄ decomposes in vivo to release Ni²⁺ ions and CO; the nickel ions interfere with cellular enzymes and cause oxidative damage to lungs, liver, and kidneys. 2. **Symptoms of Acute Exposure (2 marks):** - **"Nickel Fitters' Fever":** Delayed (12–36 hr) flu-like symptoms — chills, fever, cough, chest pain. Often the **first** clinical sign. - **Pulmonary oedema** (delayed 2–5 days) — the primary cause of death. - **Cyanosis, giddiness, vomiting, dizziness** (from the CO component). - **Chronic exposure** → carcinogenic (lung and nasal cancers) — IARC Group 1. 3. **Why particularly dangerous (2 marks):** - **Odour threshold (~1 ppm) is 20× the toxic TLV** — smell gives no warning at hazardous levels. - **TLV is 500× lower than that of CO** — most air-line monitors configured for CO will not detect Ni(CO)₄. - **Delayed symptoms** — workers may be severely exposed before they feel ill. - **Heavy vapour** — accumulates in trenches, pits, and low spots, creating hidden hazards. - **Pyrophoric nature** — secondary fire hazard amplifies inhalation risk. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print & Carry!** - ☐ **Mond (1899)** is a **vapour-phase refining** process exploiting the volatility of **Ni(CO)₄**. - ☐ **Three reactions:** Reduction (350–400 °C) → Carbonylation (50–100 °C) → Decomposition (230–300 °C). - ☐ **Ni(CO)₄ is a triple-threat** molecule: toxic + flammable + pyrophoric. - ☐ **TLV = 0.05 ppm**, **IDLH = 5 ppm**, **LEL = 2 %**, **auto-ignition ≈ 60 °C**. - ☐ **Toxic threshold is ~4000× below the LEL** → toxicity dominates the hazard. - ☐ **Heavy vapour (ρ ≈ 5.9× air)** → low-level exhaust ventilation is mandatory. - ☐ **Olfactory warning absent** at toxic levels → continuous IR/UV monitoring is essential. - ☐ **Key engineering controls:** N₂ inerting, EIVs, leak-tight welded construction, activated-carbon vapour capture, O₂ analysers with trip at 50 ppm. - ☐ **In a KTU 14-mark answer:** always include a **block diagram** and quote **units**. - ☐ **First clinical sign of overexposure** is delayed "**nickel fitters' fever**" (12–36 hr post-exposure) — not immediate. - ☐ **IARC classifies Ni(CO)₄ as Group 1 carcinogen** (lung and nasal). - ☐ **PPE:** Supplied-air respirator + full skin coverage (no skin absorption). - ☐ **Stoichiometric O₂ for combustion** = 2.5 mol O₂ per mol Ni(CO)₄. - ☐ **Mass yield** of Ni from Ni(CO)₄ ≈ **34.4 %** by mass. - ☐ **Purge volume** to reach 25 % of LEL = ~5.3 vessel-volumes of N₂. - ☐ **Mond ≠ Mond–Langer ≠ Hunter** — examiners deduct marks for confusing these. > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-Line Takeaway for Viva:** *"Mond Fire is the unique case where a process is sustained by the same molecule that can instantly destroy the plant — a lesson in chemistry-driven safety engineering."*

topics.title: Explosion and Toxicity Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Explosion and Toxicity Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Explosion Index

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Explosion Index (K_St)** is a quantitative measure of the *violence* or *severity* of a dust or gas explosion inside a closed vessel. It is a critical parameter used by process safety engineers to size explosion vents, design containment systems, and classify hazardous areas in chemical process plants.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Safety Engineering of Process Plants* (PECHT743), the **Explosion Index** is treated as the characteristic constant of a combustible dust cloud. The German standard **VDI 3673** and the international standard **ISO 6184/EN 14491** define it as:

$$K_{St} = \left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3}$$

where $(dP/dt)_{max}$ is the maximum rate of pressure rise observed in the standard 1 m³ vessel test, and $V$ is the vessel volume.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Popcorn Pressure Cooker"

Imagine you have two pressure cookers — one filled with ordinary popcorn kernels and the other filled with high-moisture, oil-rich popcorn kernels. When you heat both, the second cooker will "pop" with dramatically more violence, throwing its lid across the kitchen. The *Explosion Index* is essentially a **scientific "popping score"** given to dust clouds based on how violently they release energy when ignited. Just as you would never use a thin-walled cooker for the violent popcorn, a process engineer would never install a thin rupture disc for a high-K_St dust.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant used:** Atmospheric pressure reference **P₀ = 101.325 kPa (1 atm)** and standard test temperature **T₀ = 293 K (20 °C)** for all explosion index determinations.

## 1.2 The Toxicity Index

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Toxicity Index** is a numerical rating assigned to a chemical substance that quantifies its potential to cause harm to living organisms (especially humans) upon short-term exposure. It integrates lethal concentration values, permissible exposure limits, and the physical state of the chemical.
