The KTU syllabus predominantly refers to the **Dow Chemical Exposure Index (CEI)**, originally published by the American Institute of Chemical Engineers (AIChE) / Dow Chemical Company in **1994**. It is a *relative* ranking system used to evaluate the acute health hazard to nearby communities from a chemical release.

The **Toxicity Index** also encompasses classical toxicological endpoints:

- **TLV** — Threshold Limit Value (ACGIH, 8-hr TWA exposure)
- **LC₅₀** — Lethal Concentration that kills 50 % of test population
- **LD₅₀** — Lethal Dose that kills 50 % of test population

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Chemical Spicy Rating"

Think of toxicity like the *Scoville Heat Units* of a chili pepper. A bell pepper scores 0, a jalapeño scores ~5,000, and a Carolina Reaper scores ~1,600,000. The **Toxicity Index** does the same job for industrial chemicals — it tells a safety engineer, at a glance, *how dangerous* a release would be if the chemical escaped containment. A high index means "evacuate the neighborhood"; a low index means "vent the room."

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Toxicity References used by KTU examiners:** TLV-TWA (8-hr Time-Weighted Average) and **LC₅₀ (rat, 4-hr inhalation)** values are the most commonly quoted benchmarks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Explosion Index — Components and Logic Steps

The Explosion Index is not a single number; it is a *family* of related explosion severity parameters. The following bullet logic is what KTU examiners expect from a Module 3 answer:

- **Step 1 — Lower Explosion Limit (LEL):** The minimum concentration of a combustible substance in air that can propagate a flame. Below this, the mixture is *too lean* to burn.
- **Step 2 — Upper Explosion Limit (UEL):** The maximum concentration above which the mixture is *too rich* to burn (insufficient oxidizer).
- **Step 3 — Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE):** The smallest spark energy capable of igniting the optimum mixture.
- **Step 4 — Maximum Explosion Pressure (P_max):** The peak pressure reached during a closed-vessel deflagration. Typically **7 to 9 bar gauge** for most organic dusts.
- **Step 5 — Maximum Rate of Pressure Rise ((dP/dt)_max):** The peak rate of pressure rise, measured in **bar/s**.
- **Step 6 — Explosion Index (K_St):** Derived by the cube-root law, allowing volume-independent comparison.

### Explosion Classes (Dust Explosions)

The K_St value classifies dusts into four internationally recognized severity classes per **ISO 6184**:

| Explosion Class | K_St Range (bar·m/s) | Severity     | Engineering Implication                              |
|-----------------|----------------------|--------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| St 0            | 0                    | Non-explosive | No venting needed                                    |
| St 1            | 0 < K_St ≤ 200       | Weak         | Standard explosion vents sufficient                  |
| St 2            | 200 < K_St ≤ 300     | Strong       | Reinforced vents + isolation required                |
| St 3            | K_St > 300           | Very Strong  | Suppression systems + flameless venting recommended |

## 2.2 The "Cube-Root Law" — The Heart of the Explosion Index

Bartknecht (1971, 1989) discovered empirically that for geometrically similar vessels:

$$\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = K_{St} = \text{constant for the dust}$$

This cube-root relationship is the foundation of *all* explosion vent sizing equations in **VDI 3673, NFPA 68, and EN 14491**. The reason is that flame front area scales with $V^{2/3}$, and $(dP/dt) \propto$ flame area / vessel volume $\propto V^{-1/3}$, leading to the cube-root law.

## 2.3 Toxicity Index — Components and Logic Steps

The **Dow Chemical Exposure Index (CEI)** is computed using:

$$\text{CEI} = (A \cdot B) \cdot \left(\frac{ER \cdot Q}{660}\right)$$

where:
- **A** = Airborne Quantity Factor (dimensionless, 0.1 → 5 based on quantity)
- **B** = Toxicity Factor (from ERPG, AEGL, or LD₅₀ data)
- **ER** = Evaporation Rate (or release rate) of the chemical at process conditions
- **Q** = Quantity released (in pounds, per Dow's original convention)
- **660** = Empirical constant (Dow 1994)

The **CEI Rating Bands** are:

| CEI Value     | Rating     | Action Required                                       |
|---------------|------------|-------------------------------------------------------|
| 0 – 20        | Light      | Standard operating procedures                         |
| 21 – 40       | Low        | Notify local authorities                              |
| 41 – 60       | Moderate   | Engineering controls + community alerting             |
| 61 – 75       | High       | Detailed emergency plan + dual containment            |
| 76 – 100      | Very High  | Major review of plant location                        |
| > 100         | Extreme    | Relocation strongly advised                           |

## 2.4 Classical Toxicological Endpoints

| Endpoint | Symbol | Definition                                                    | Standard Reference |
|----------|--------|---------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|
| Threshold Limit Value | TLV-TWA | 8-hr time-weighted average exposure limit | ACGIH |
| Lethal Concentration 50 | LC₅₀ | Concentration lethal to 50 % of test rats over 4 hr | OECD 403 |
| Lethal Dose 50         | LD₅₀ | Single oral/dermal dose lethal to 50 % of rats | OECD 401/402 |
| Immediately Dangerous to Life and Health | IDLH | 30-min exposure limit threatening life or irreversible effects | NIOSH |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The **Hodge-Sterner Toxicity Classification** uses LD₅₀ (mg/kg, oral, rat) to rank substances from "Practically Non-toxic" (>15,000 mg/kg) to "Extremely Toxic" (<1 mg/kg).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Explosion Index (K_St):** Used to size *explosion relief vents* on silos, dust collectors, and bag-house filters in pharmaceutical, food, and polymer industries.
- **Toxicity Index (CEI):** Used in *Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)* for land-use planning around chemical facilities, in accordance with **CCPS Guidelines** and Indian **MoEFCC rules** (Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989 — amended 2000).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cube-Root Law for K_St

We begin with the assumption that the rate of pressure rise inside a closed spherical vessel during a dust deflagration is proportional to the burning velocity and the flame surface area.

**Step 1 — Flame Surface Area Scaling**

For a spherical flame of radius $r$ inside a spherical vessel of radius $R$:

$$A_{flame} = 4\pi r^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Vessel Volume**

$$V = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Fractional Burnt Volume**

$$f = \frac{r^{3}}{R^{3}}$$

**Step 4 — Burning Velocity Relationship**

The rate of pressure rise is empirically proportional to the rate of burnt-volume formation:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} \propto \frac{df}{dt} = \frac{3r^{2}}{R^{3}} \cdot \frac{dr}{dt}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting the Burning Velocity**

For a turbulent flame, $dr/dt = S_u$ (the burning velocity). At the moment of maximum rate of pressure rise, the flame has consumed roughly half the volume, so $r \approx 0.5R$. The flame area is then:

$$A \propto R^{2} \propto V^{2/3}$$

**Step 6 — Assembling the Final Relationship**

$$\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \propto \frac{A}{V} \propto \frac{V^{2/3}}{V} = V^{-1/3}$$

Multiplying both sides by $V^{1/3}$:

$$\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = K_{St}$$

This is the *cube-root law*, the cornerstone of the **Explosion Index**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Explosion Index Determination

**Problem:** A maize starch dust cloud is tested in a closed 1 m³ vessel. The maximum rate of pressure rise recorded is **400 bar/s**. Determine:
(a) The Explosion Index K_St.
(b) The explosion class.
(c) The expected rate of pressure rise in a 27 m³ silo containing the same dust.

### Solution

**(a) Calculation of K_St**

Using $K_{St} = (dP/dt)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3}$:

$$K_{St} = 400 \cdot (1)^{1/3} = 400 \text{ bar·m/s}$$

**(b) Explosion Class**

Since $K_{St} = 400 > 300$, maize starch falls in **Explosion Class St 3 (Very Strong)**.

**(c) Extrapolation to 27 m³ Silo**

Using the cube-root law in reverse:

$$\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{27} = \frac{K_{St}}{V^{1/3}} = \frac{400}{(27)^{1/3}} = \frac{400}{3} = 133.3 \text{ bar/s}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — CEI (Toxicity Index)

**Problem:** A storage tank contains **10,000 lb** of anhydrous ammonia. The evaporation rate at process conditions is **50 lb/min**. The Toxicity Factor (B) from Dow tables is **6**, and the Airborne Quantity Factor (A) is **1**. Compute the CEI and rate the hazard.

### Solution

Using $\text{CEI} = A \cdot B \cdot \dfrac{ER \cdot Q}{660}$:

$$\text{CEI} = (1)(6) \cdot \frac{(50)(10{,}000)}{660}$$

$$\text{CEI} = 6 \cdot \frac{500{,}000}{660}$$

$$\text{CEI} = 6 \cdot 757.58$$

$$\text{CEI} = 4545.5$$

Since this vastly exceeds 100, the hazard rating is **EXTREME** — the facility must be relocated or undergo a major redesign.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Explosion vs. Toxicity Indices

| Parameter                  | Explosion Index (K_St)               | Toxicity Index (CEI)                  |
|----------------------------|--------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| **Phenomenon Addressed**   | Mechanical violence of combustion    | Biological harm from chemical release |
| **Standard Test Vessel**   | 1 m³ closed sphere (Siwek/ISO 6184)  | No physical test — uses published data |
| **Output Dimension**       | bar·m/s                              | Dimensionless rating                  |
| **Key Inputs**             | P_max, (dP/dt)_max, MIE              | A, B, ER, Q (quantity released)       |
| **Used For**               | Vent sizing, dust collector design   | Land-use planning, QRA                |
| **Engineered Safeguard**   | Explosion vents, suppression         | Dykes, scrubbers, emergency isolation |
| **Reference Standard**     | ISO 6184, VDI 3673, NFPA 68          | Dow CEI 1994, AIHA ERPG, NIOSH IDLH   |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Architecture of Explosion Severity Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Combustible Dust Sampled] --> B[Standard 1 m³ Vessel Test]
    B --> C[Measure P_max bar]
    B --> D[Measure dPdt max bar per s]
    C --> E[Compute K_St via Cube Root Law]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Compare K_St Value}
    F -->|K_St equals 0| G[St 0 Non Explosive]
    F -->|K_St 1 to 200| H[St 1 Weak]
    F -->|K_St 200 to 300| I[St 2 Strong]
    F -->|K_St above 300| J[St 3 Very Strong]
    H --> K[Standard Vent Sizing VDI 3673]
    I --> L[Reinforced Vent plus Isolation]
    J --> M[Suppression plus Flameless Vent]
    G --> N[No Special Protection]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Dow Chemical Exposure Index

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Quantity Released Q pounds] --> M[Multiplier ER times Q over 660]
    ER[Evaporation Rate ER lb per min] --> M
    A[Airborne Quantity Factor A] --> X[Product Computation]
    M --> X
    B[Toxicity Factor B from ERPG] --> X
    X --> CEI[Chemical Exposure Index CEI]
    CEI --> R{Rating Band Lookup}
    R -->|0 to 20| L1[Light]
    R -->|21 to 40| L2[Low]
    R -->|41 to 60| L3[Moderate]
    R -->|61 to 75| L4[High]
    R -->|76 to 100| L5[Very High]
    R -->|above 100| L6[Extreme]
```

## 4.3 Explosion vs Toxicity Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Process Hazard Identification] --> Q1{Is Substance Flammable?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| EI[Compute Explosion Index K_St]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is Substance Toxic?}
    EI --> V[Size Explosion Vents]
    Q2 -->|Yes| TI[Compute Toxicity Index CEI]
    Q2 -->|No| Safe[Process Inherently Safe]
    TI --> LP[Land Use Planning and QRA]
    V --> EP[Equipment Protection]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the term *Explosion Index* (K_St) and state its unit.

**Model Answer:**
The Explosion Index (K_St) is a material-specific constant that quantifies the violence of a dust explosion in a closed vessel. It is defined as the product of the maximum rate of pressure rise and the cube root of the vessel volume:

$$K_{St} = \left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3}$$

The unit of K_St is **bar·m/s**.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List any **three** commonly used toxicity indices employed in process plant safety evaluation.

**Model Answer:**
The three commonly used toxicity indices are:
1. **TLV (Threshold Limit Value)** – ACGIH 8-hr TWA permissible exposure.
2. **LC₅₀ (Lethal Concentration 50 %)** – Acute inhalation toxicity in rats.
3. **LD₅₀ (Lethal Dose 50 %)** – Single-dose oral/dermal toxicity.
4. *(Additional valid entry: CEI – Dow Chemical Exposure Index.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Explosion Index Focus

**(a) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Explain the *Cube-Root Law* used to characterize dust explosions. Derive the relationship and discuss how it is used to size explosion relief vents.

**Model Solution:**

The Cube-Root Law, discovered by Bartknecht (1971), states that for geometrically similar vessels containing the same dust at optimum concentration:

$$K_{St} = \left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = \text{constant}$$

**Derivation Summary:**
1. Rate of pressure rise is proportional to the flame surface area, $A \propto V^{2/3}$.
2. Therefore $(dP/dt)_{max} \propto V^{-1/3}$.
3. Multiplying by $V^{1/3}$ yields the constant $K_{St}$.

**Application to Vent Sizing (VDI 3673):**
- Measure (dP/dt)_max in a 1 m³ laboratory vessel.
- Compute $K_{St} = (dP/dt)_{max} \cdot 1^{1/3}$.
- Use the *nomographs* in VDI 3673 / NFPA 68 with the known $P_{red}$ (reduced explosion pressure) and $K_{St}$ to determine the required vent area $A_v$ for the industrial vessel.

**(b) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** A polyethylene dust cloud is tested in a 1 m³ closed vessel. The peak pressure rise rate is **350 bar/s**. Classify the dust and estimate the maximum pressure rise rate in a **64 m³** storage silo.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate K_St:**

$$K_{St} = (dP/dt)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = 350 \cdot (1)^{1/3} = 350 \text{ bar·m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Classification:**

Since $K_{St} = 350 > 300$, polyethylene dust belongs to **Explosion Class St 3 (Very Strong)**.

**Step 3 — Extrapolation to 64 m³ Silo:**

$$\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{64} = \frac{K_{St}}{V^{1/3}} = \frac{350}{(64)^{1/3}} = \frac{350}{4} = 87.5 \text{ bar/s}$$

**Conclusion:** Although the rate of pressure rise is lower in the larger vessel (87.5 bar/s), the *total* explosive energy release is far greater. Flameless venting and explosion suppression are mandatory.

### Question B — Toxicity Index Focus

**(a) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** Describe the **Dow Chemical Exposure Index (CEI)**. State its mathematical expression and explain the significance of each factor.

**Model Solution:**

The **Dow Chemical Exposure Index (CEI)**, published in 1994, is a relative ranking of the acute health hazard to the off-site community following an accidental release of a toxic chemical.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$\text{CEI} = (A \cdot B) \cdot \frac{ER \cdot Q}{660}$$

**Significance of Each Factor:**

- **A — Airborne Quantity Factor (0.1 → 5):** Accounts for the proportion of the released material that becomes airborne. A volatile liquid at ambient conditions gets A = 5; a non-volatile solid might get A = 0.1.
- **B — Toxicity Factor (1 → 10):** Derived from published ERPG, IDLH, or LD₅₀ values. Higher toxicity → higher B.
- **ER — Evaporation Rate (lb/min):** Rate at which the chemical vaporises at process conditions.
- **Q — Quantity Released (lb):** Total inventory that escapes containment.
- **660 — Empirical Constant:** Normalisation factor chosen by Dow to make CEI values fall in the 0–100 range for typical scenarios.

**Rating Bands:** Light (0–20), Low (21–40), Moderate (41–60), High (61–75), Very High (76–100), Extreme (>100).

**(b) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** A pressurized vessel contains **5,000 lb** of chlorine. The evaporation rate is **20 lb/min**. The Airborne Quantity Factor **A = 4** and the Toxicity Factor **B = 9**. Calculate the CEI and recommend appropriate mitigation measures.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Substitute into the CEI Equation:**

$$\text{CEI} = (4 \cdot 9) \cdot \frac{(20)(5{,}000)}{660}$$

$$\text{CEI} = 36 \cdot \frac{100{,}000}{660}$$

$$\text{CEI} = 36 \cdot 151.52$$

$$\text{CEI} = 5454.7$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:**

CEI = 5454.7 → **EXTREME HAZARD** (more than 50× the upper boundary).

**Step 3 — Mitigation Measures:**

- Reduce inventory to the absolute minimum required for operations.
- Install double-walled containment with chlorine-compatible leak detection.
- Provide caustic scrubber (NaOH) emergency neutralisation system.
- Implement water-curtain dispersion barriers for vapor cloud mitigation.
- Conduct community alerting drills and prepare evacuation plan up to **5 km** radius.
- Relocate storage to an isolated, uninhabited zone if possible.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cube root** when extrapolating K_St — many students write $(dP/dt)_{max} \cdot V$ instead of $(dP/dt)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3}$.
> 2. **Mixing up A and B** in the CEI formula — A is *airborne*, B is *toxicity*. Swapping them costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Omitting the constant 660** in the CEI denominator — this is the most common error and results in an inflated (and wrong) CEI.
> 4. **Confusing K_St units** — K_St has units of **bar·m/s**, not bar/s. KTU examiners are strict on this.
> 5. **Writing "Toxicity Index" without specifying which one** — always name the index (CEI, TLV, LC₅₀, etc.).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Explosion Index (K_St)** = $(dP/dt)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3}$; units = **bar·m/s**.
- **Dust Explosion Classes:** St 0 (0), St 1 (≤200), St 2 (200–300), St 3 (>300).
- **Maximum Explosion Pressure (P_max)** for organic dusts typically lies between **7–9 bar gauge**.
- **Cube-Root Law** is empirical but universally accepted under ISO 6184 / VDI 3673 / NFPA 68.
- **Dow CEI** = $(A \cdot B) \cdot \dfrac{ER \cdot Q}{660}$; CEI > 100 = **EXTREME** hazard.
- **Classical Toxicity Endpoints:** TLV-TWA (ACGIH), LC₅₀ (OECD 403), LD₅₀ (OECD 401/402), IDLH (NIOSH).
- **Hodge-Sterner Toxicity Scale:** from "Practically Non-toxic" (LD₅₀ > 15,000 mg/kg) to "Extremely Toxic" (LD₅₀ < 1 mg/kg).
- Both indices feed into **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** and **Land-Use Planning (LUP)** in compliance with the Indian **MSIHC Rules 1989 (amended 2000)**.
- Always state the **units** of every numerical answer — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.
- Always specify **test standards** (ISO 6184, VDI 3673, Dow CEI 1994) when answering full-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emergency Planning - Onsite and Offsite Emergency Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Emergency Planning - Onsite and Offsite Emergency Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Emergency Planning** is a systematic, documented, and pre-arranged set of actions designed to mitigate the consequences of major accidental hazardous events in process industries by ensuring rapid, coordinated, and effective response through clearly defined organizational structures, communication protocols, resource mobilization, and recovery procedures.

In the context of process plant safety under the KTU 2024 scheme aligned with **NEP 2020** outcomes, emergency planning is classified into two hierarchical tiers:

| Tier | Scope | Custodian | Primary Objective |
|------|-------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Onsite Emergency Plan (OEP)** | Within the battery limit of the plant | Plant management | Protect personnel, property, and operations inside the plant boundary |
| **Offsite Emergency Plan (OEP)** | Beyond the plant boundary, up to the impact zone | District/State authority (District Collector / SDO) | Protect the public, environment, and infrastructure in the surrounding community |

### 1.1.1 Statutory Anchors (Indian Context)

> [!NOTE]
> Under the **Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989 (MSIHC Rules)** — amended 2000 — Rule 13 mandates that every industrial activity handling hazardous chemicals above threshold quantities must prepare:
> - An **Onsite Emergency Plan**, and
> - An **Offsite Emergency Plan** (prepared by the District Crisis Group chaired by the District Collector).

Other enabling regulations include:
- **Factories Act, 1948** (Section 41B – emergency plans)
- **Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991**
- **National Disaster Management Act, 2005**
- **EPA Risk Management Program (USA)** — analogous reference

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

Imagine a **large hospital complex** with an in-house fire brigade and an ambulance tied up with the city’s 108 emergency network.

- The **Onsite Emergency Plan** is like the hospital’s *internal Code Blue team* — they swing into action the moment an emergency occurs *within* the hospital walls. They know every corridor, every oxygen line, and every patient.
- The **Offsite Emergency Plan** is like the *city-wide disaster response* — fire stations, police, traffic control, neighbouring hospitals — coordinated by a higher authority (the District Collector) when the emergency *spills out* of the hospital and threatens the surrounding neighborhood.

> [!TIP]
> **Single-line mnemonic**: *Onsite = Inside the fence (plant owns it). Offsite = Outside the fence (district owns it).*

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **E-1 Zone**: Evacuation distance ≤ **500 m** from incident (worker refuge)
- **E-2 Zone**: Public assembly area beyond **500 m** (typical small plant)
- **Maximum credible event (MCE)** — usually defined as the **1 × 10⁻⁶ per year** probability event for offsite planning (international benchmark per **CCPS Guidelines**).
- **Emergency response time target**: ≤ **3 minutes** for first aid/fire team arrival on scene.
- **Muster time** for full headcount: ≤ **15 minutes** post-alarm.

---

## 1.4 Outcome-Based Education Mapping (CO Alignment)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This topic directly satisfies:
> - **CO3 (Module 3):** *Develop emergency preparedness plans for process plants including onsite and offsite emergency response systems.*
> - **Bloom’s Level:** L4 (Analyze) → L5 (Evaluate) → L6 (Create)
> - **PO Mapping:** PO1 (Engineering Knowledge), PO3 (Design/Development of Solutions), PO9 (Individual & Team Work), PO12 (Life-long Learning)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Framework of Emergency Planning

Emergency planning in process plants is structured as a **four-tier pyramid** under international frameworks (COMAH, OSHA PSM, EPA RMP):

```
                    ▲
                   ╱ ╲
                  ╱ T4 ╲       Tier IV: Community / Offsite
                 ╱──────╲       (District Crisis Group)
                ╱   T3   ╲
               ╱──────────╲     Tier III: Site Mutual Aid
              ╱     T2     ╲    (Neighbouring industries)
             ╱──────────────╲
            ╱       T1       ╲  Tier I: Onsite / Plant Internal
           ╱──────────────────╲
          ▲        Base         ▲
```

### 2.1.1 Tier I — Onsite Emergency Plan (OEP)

**Scope**: Battery limit of the plant. Covers all employees, contractors, visitors, and assets.

**Key Elements**:
1. **Hazard identification & risk assessment (HIRA)** of credible scenarios
2. **Emergency organization** with Incident Commander (IC), Safety Officer, Fire Chief, Medical In-Charge, Communication Officer
3. **Emergency Control Centre (ECC)** — hardened, blast-resistant, dedicated
4. **Communication systems** — Public Address (PA), sirens, two-way radios, dedicated hotline
5. **Escape routes & assembly points** (muster stations)
6. **Fire-fighting & spill-control facilities** — hydrants, monitors, foam systems, booms
7. **Medical facilities** — first-aid, occupational health centre, ambulance
8. **Emergency shutdown (ESD) systems** — valves, interlocks, depressurizing
9. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — proximity suits, SCBA, escape respirators
10. **Training & mock drills** — quarterly tabletop + annual full-scale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Authority on site**: The **Site Incident Commander (SIC)** has absolute authority during an emergency, overriding normal hierarchy.

### 2.1.2 Tier II–IV — Offsite Emergency Plan (OEP)

**Scope**: Beyond plant boundary, covering surrounding population within the **Emergency Planning Zone (EPZ)**.

**EPZ Determination** (typical methodology):

$$
\text{EPZ radius} = f(\text{MCE}, \text{toxic dispersion}, \text{thermal radiation flux}, \text{VCE overpressure})
$$

Where the key input parameters are derived using consequence analysis:
- **ERPG-2 / AEGL-2** for toxic gases (≥ 30 min exposure)
- **Thermal radiation** ≥ **5 kW/m²** (2nd-degree burn in < 60 s)
- **Overpressure** ≥ **2 psi (≈ 14 kPa)** (partial building collapse)

**Offsite Plan Stakeholders**:

| Stakeholder | Role |
|-------------|------|
| District Crisis Group (Chair: District Collector) | Apex authority, plan approval |
| Sub-Divisional Magistrate | Field coordination |
| Local Police | Traffic control, perimeter security, evacuation |
| Fire Services (State Fire Dept.) | Specialized fire-fighting, HAZMAT response |
| Health Department | Mass casualty management, hospitals |
| Civil Defence / SDRF / NDRF | Search & rescue |
| Plant Management | Technical advisor, resource provider |
| Neighbouring industries (Mutual Aid) | Reinforcement of resources |
| Public / Media / NGOs | Information dissemination, rehabilitation |

---

## 2.2 Command Structure — Incident Command System (ICS)

The **Incident Command System (ICS)** is the universally accepted framework (per **NFPA 1561**, **OSHA 1910.120**, and Indian NDMA guidelines):

> [!NOTE]
> **Five functional pillars of ICS**:
> 1. **Command** (single Incident Commander)
> 2. **Operations** (tactical response)
> 3. **Planning** (resource & information management)
> 4. **Logistics** (supply & support)
> 5. **Finance / Administration** (cost tracking)

In Indian statutory form, this maps onto:

$$
\text{Onsite IC (Plant)} \xrightarrow{\text{handover}} \text{District Magistrate (Offsite IC)}
$$

The **transition point** is a critical decision — triggered when the incident crosses the plant boundary OR the District Crisis Group declares a state emergency.

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Emergency Response Sequence

### Phase 1 — Pre-Incident (Preparedness)
1. Hazard identification, scenario development
2. Resource inventory (fire tenders, ambulances, PPE stockpile)
3. Mutual Aid Agreements (MAA) signed with neighbours
4. Training, drills, plan documentation
5. Community awareness in EPZ

### Phase 2 — Incident Detection & Notification
1. Detection by sensor / operator / public
2. **Alarms raised**: Distinct siren codes (e.g., 3 short = fire; 1 long = gas leak; 2 long = toxic release; continuous = shelter-in-place)
3. Site Emergency Coordinator (SEC) confirms and activates plan

### Phase 3 — Onsite Response
1. ECC activated; IC assumes command
2. Site evacuation if required
3. Fire / spill control teams deployed
4. Casualty triage (START protocol: Green/Yellow/Red/Black)
5. Headcount at muster station

### Phase 4 — Offsite Escalation
1. IC informs District Control Room
2. Joint decision: Is offsite evacuation needed?
3. Warning disseminated: sirens, SMS, public broadcast
4. Traffic management: contra-flow on highways
5. Shelter-in-place or evacuation of EPZ population

### Phase 5 — Recovery & Termination
1. Incident stabilised → **Termination Command** by IC
2. Investigation (root-cause)
3. Decontamination, area re-entry
4. **After-Action Review (AAR)**
5. Plan revision, lessons learnt

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Numerical Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU exams, the following are the most-tested numerical/quantitative relationships:**

| # | Concept | Formula / Threshold | Variables / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Toxic Emergency Planning Zone | $r_{EPZ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{Q \cdot \tau}{\pi \cdot C_{ERPG-2} \cdot u}}$ | Pasquill-Gifford simplified; $Q$ = release rate, $\tau$ = exposure time, $u$ = wind speed |
| 2 | Toxic exposure dose | $D = C^n \cdot t$ | $n$ = 1 for non-reactive, $n$ = 3 for reactive (Haber's law) |
| 3 | Thermal radiation threshold (2nd-degree burn) | $q'' \geq 5 \text{ kW/m}^2$ for 30 s | Used to draw thermal EPZ |
| 4 | Overpressure damage threshold | $\Delta P \geq 2 \text{ psi (≈ 14 kPa)}$ | Partial building damage threshold |
| 5 | Population at risk (PAR) | $PAR = 2\pi r \cdot L \cdot \rho \cdot \alpha$ | $r$ = radius, $L$ = sector length, $\rho$ = pop. density, $\alpha$ = occupancy |
| 6 | Drills per year (statutory) | $\geq 2$ full-scale + 4 tabletop | Per MSIHC Rules & NDMA |
| 7 | Siren audibility radius | $R_{audio} = 10^{(SL - 90)/20} \cdot d_{ref}$ | $SL$ = siren SPL (dB), $d_{ref}$ = 1 m |
| 8 | ALOHA / PHAST consequence tool inputs | Wind speed, stability class, temperature, humidity, release rate | Standard ALOHA inputs |

> [!WARNING]
> Avoid using the literal pipe `|` symbol in equations. In LaTeX within KTU answer sheets, write absolute values as $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries & Petrochemicals** (e.g., BPCL Kochi, CPCL Chennai) — large LPG/HC inventories necessitate multi-tier plans.
- **LNG terminals** (Dahej, Hazira) — cryogenic & dispersion hazards.
- **Chlor-alkali plants** — chlorine gas EPZ planning.
- **Ammonia storage** — toxic gas EPZ often 5–10 km radius.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Toxic Emergency Planning Zone (EPZ) Radius

The **Emergency Planning Zone (EPZ)** radius for a continuous toxic gas release is derived from the **Pasquill-Gifford dispersion model** with a worst-case neutral stability assumption.

### Given
- Release rate: $Q$ (kg/s)
- Wind speed at 10 m height: $u$ (m/s) (typically 2 m/s for worst case)
- ERPG-2 concentration: $C_{ERPG-2}$ (mg/m³ or ppm)
- Exposure duration: $\tau$ (s) (typically 3600 s = 1 hour for planning)

### Step 1 — Plume Centreline Concentration

For a continuous, ground-level release with Pasquill class D (neutral) stability:

$$
C(x, 0, 0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi \cdot \sigma_y(x) \cdot \sigma_z(x) \cdot u}
$$

Where $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ are horizontal and vertical dispersion coefficients.

### Step 2 — Conservative Simplification

For long-range planning (large $x$), assume $\sigma_y \approx \sigma_z \approx a \cdot x^b$, where $a$ and $b$ are Pasquill-D constants ($a \approx 0.08$, $b \approx 0.894$).

Using the **engineering approximation** that for a downwind distance $r$:

$$
\sigma_y(r) \cdot \sigma_z(r) \approx \kappa \cdot r^{1.8}
$$

Where $\kappa$ is a constant combining Pasquill-D coefficients.

$$
C(r) \approx \dfrac{Q}{\pi \cdot \kappa \cdot r^{1.8} \cdot u}
$$

### Step 3 — Equate to ERPG-2 Threshold

Set $C(r) = C_{ERPG-2}$ and solve for $r = r_{EPZ}$:

$$
C_{ERPG-2} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi \cdot \kappa \cdot r_{EPZ}^{1.8} \cdot u}
$$

$$
r_{EPZ}^{1.8} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi \cdot \kappa \cdot C_{ERPG-2} \cdot u}
$$

$$
\boxed{r_{EPZ} = \left( \dfrac{Q}{\pi \cdot \kappa \cdot C_{ERPG-2} \cdot u} \right)^{1/1.8}}
$$

Or equivalently, expressed as a practitioner-friendly equation:

$$
\boxed{r_{EPZ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{Q \cdot \tau}{\pi \cdot C_{ERPG-2} \cdot u}}}
$$

The square-root form is obtained by integrating the steady-state plume over the exposure time $\tau$, using a sector-averaged concentration approximation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Population at Risk (PAR)

The **Population at Risk (PAR)** within a sector of radius $r$ and angle $\theta$ is:

### Step 1 — Geometric Setup

A downwind sector of half-angle $\theta$ radians corresponds to a circular sector.

$$
\text{Area} = \pi r^2 \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{2\pi} = \dfrac{\theta \cdot r^2}{2}
$$

(where $\theta$ in radians, sector angle from $0$ to $\theta$).

### Step 2 — Apply Population Density

Let $\rho$ = population density (persons/km²) and $\alpha$ = occupancy factor (fraction of people present at time of release — typically 0.7 for residential, 0.5 for industrial day, 1.0 for schools/hospitals).

$$
PAR = \dfrac{\theta \cdot r^2}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot \alpha
$$

### Step 3 — Alternative Annular Form (for distance-binned PAR)

If we want PAR between radii $r_1$ and $r_2$:

$$
PAR_{(r_1, r_2)} = \dfrac{\theta}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot \alpha \cdot (r_2^2 - r_1^2)
$$

This is highly useful for **sheltering vs. evacuation** zone delineation.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical (Illustrative)

**Given**: Chlorine release, $Q = 0.5$ kg/s, ERPG-2 = 0.5 ppm ≈ 1.5 mg/m³, wind $u$ = 2 m/s, $\tau$ = 3600 s, population density $\rho$ = 5000 persons/km², sector angle $\theta = 30° = \pi/6$, $\alpha = 0.7$.

$$
r_{EPZ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.5 \times 3600}{\pi \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2}} \text{ m}
$$

$$
r_{EPZ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1800}{9.42 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{191{,}000} \approx 437 \text{ m}
$$

Convert to km: $r_{EPZ} \approx 0.44$ km.

$$
PAR = \dfrac{(\pi/6) \times (0.44)^2}{2} \times 5000 \times 0.7 = 0.101 \times 5000 \times 0.7 \approx 354 \text{ persons}
$$

This is the kind of numerical that earns full marks in KTU Part B questions.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Onsite vs. Offsite Comparison

| Parameter | Onsite Emergency Plan | Offsite Emergency Plan |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Custodian | Plant Management | District Crisis Group (Collector) |
| Legal basis | MSIHC Rule 13(1); Factories Act 41B | MSIHC Rule 13(2); Disaster Mgmt Act 2005 |
| Geographic scope | Plant battery limit | 1–10 km radius around plant |
| Trigger event | Any incident within fence | Incident crossing fence or declared by IC |
| Lead time for activation | Seconds to minutes | Minutes to hours |
| Key resources | Fire tenders, ambulances, SCBA, ECC | District fire, SDRF/NDRF, hospitals, police |
| Communication | PA, sirens, internal phone | Public sirens, SMS, All India Radio, Doordarshan |
| Documentation | Plant-level manual | District-level plan, approved by Crisis Group |
| Drill frequency | Quarterly mock drill | Annual community drill |
| Funding | Plant budget | District/state disaster funds |
| Review cycle | Annual | Biennial or post-incident |

---

## 3.4 Decision Logic — When to Escalate Onsite → Offsite

A **decision flowchart** governs the handover. The decision is typically based on:

1. **Hazard magnitude**: Has toxic plume crossed fence? (Yes/No)
2. **Casualty count**: ≥ 5 serious injuries or ≥ 1 fatality
3. **Cascading risk**: Imminent BLEVE, runaway reaction, large unignited vapour cloud
4. **Resource exhaustion**: Plant's own capability saturated
5. **Public concern**: Media amplification, protest, political pressure

The official **hand-over** is recorded in writing with timestamps.

---

## 3.5 Mapping to KTU Assessment Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **14-mark questions**, KTU expects:
> - Definition + regulatory citation (2 marks)
> - Hierarchy / command structure diagram (3 marks)
> - Step-by-step response sequence (4 marks)
> - Comparison table onsite vs. offsite (3 marks)
> - Numerical example (e.g., EPZ radius, PAR) (2 marks)

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Differentiate between Onsite Emergency Plan and Offsite Emergency Plan in a process plant.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Onsite Emergency Plan (OEP) | Offsite Emergency Plan (OEP) |
|-------|----------------------------|------------------------------|
| Authority | Plant Management | District Crisis Group (Collector) |
| Jurisdiction | Within plant battery limit | Beyond plant boundary up to EPZ |
| Statutory basis | MSIHC Rule 13(1); Factories Act 41B | MSIHC Rule 13(2); DM Act 2005 |
| Resources | Plant fire team, internal ambulance | District fire, police, hospitals, SDRF/NDRF |
| Activation time | Seconds to minutes | Minutes to hours |
| Communication | PA, internal siren | Public siren, SMS, broadcast |

*(3 marks — table format with regulatory citation earns full marks)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **List the five functional sections of the Incident Command System (ICS) with their roles.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Command** — Single Incident Commander; overall authority, decision-making.
2. **Operations** — Tactical response: fire-fighting, rescue, spill control, medical.
3. **Planning** — Resource status, situation mapping, demobilization planning.
4. **Logistics** — Supplies, communication, transport, food & shelter.
5. **Finance / Administration** — Cost tracking, insurance, claims, documentation.

*(3 marks — all five sections with single-line role descriptions)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **hierarchical structure of emergency planning** in a major hazard process plant. Discuss the role of the District Crisis Group. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A process plant stores **200 tonnes of liquid chlorine** in a pressurized bullet. Using the Pasquill-Gillboard dispersion model (neutral stability, wind speed 2 m/s, ERPG-2 for Cl₂ = 0.5 ppm ≈ 1.5 mg/m³, exposure time 3600 s), estimate the **EPZ radius** and the **Population at Risk (PAR)** if the downwind population density is **6,000 persons/km²** with an occupancy factor of 0.7 in a 30° sector. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — (a)

**Hierarchy of Emergency Planning:**

1. **Tier I — Onsite Emergency Plan**: Internal plant response managed by the Site Incident Commander. Activated automatically for any incident within battery limit. Includes fire teams, medical, ECC, evacuation, spill control.

2. **Tier II — Mutual Aid**: Neighbouring industries share resources via signed Mutual Aid Agreements. Provides reinforcement in case of resource exhaustion.

3. **Tier III — Offsite Emergency Plan (District Level)**: Coordinated by the **District Crisis Group**, chaired by the **District Collector**, with members from Police, Fire Services, Health, Civil Defence, Forest, Pollution Control Board, and the plant management.

4. **Tier IV — State and National Level**: For catastrophic events, escalated to State Disaster Management Authority (SDMA) and National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA), including NDRF deployment.

**Role of District Crisis Group (DCG):**
- Approves and owns the Offsite Emergency Plan.
- Coordinates inter-agency response.
- Conducts annual public awareness drives.
- Reviews plan after every mock drill and real incident.
- Mobilizes district-level resources (fire, hospital, traffic).

**Schematic**: Refer to SECTION 4.1 Mermaid diagram for the hierarchy.

*(7 marks: 3 for tier description + 2 for DCG roles + 2 for schematic)*

---

### Model Solution — (b)

**Given Data:**

$$
Q = 0.5 \text{ kg/s (assuming 1% release per minute from 200 t over 1 hour, conservative)}
$$

For conservative regulatory assessment, assume release rate $Q$ from rupture of largest flange:

$$
Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P} \approx 0.5 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 1: EPZ Radius**

$$
r_{EPZ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{Q \cdot \tau}{\pi \cdot C_{ERPG-2} \cdot u}}
$$

$$
r_{EPZ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.5 \times 3600}{\pi \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 2}} \text{ m}
$$

Numerator: $0.5 \times 3600 = 1800$
Denominator: $3.1416 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 = 9.42 \times 10^{-3}$

$$
r_{EPZ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1800}{9.42 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{191{,}082} \approx 437.1 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{r_{EPZ} \approx 0.44 \text{ km}}
$$

**Step 2: Population at Risk (PAR)**

$$
PAR = \dfrac{\theta}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot \alpha \cdot r_{EPZ}^2
$$

With $\theta = 30° = \pi/6$ rad, $\rho = 6000$ persons/km², $\alpha = 0.7$, $r_{EPZ} = 0.44$ km:

$$
PAR = \dfrac{\pi/6}{2} \times 6000 \times 0.7 \times (0.44)^2
$$

$$
PAR = 0.2618 \times 6000 \times 0.7 \times 0.1936
$$

$$
PAR = 0.2618 \times 813.12 \approx 213 \text{ persons}
$$

$$
\boxed{PAR \approx 213 \text{ persons}}
$$

**Interpretation**: The offsite emergency plan must ensure warning dissemination and evacuation capability for **at least 213 persons** in the 30° downwind sector within the **440 m** EPZ.

*(7 marks: 2 for EPZ equation + 2 for numerical + 1 for PAR equation + 2 for numerical answer)*

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]**
>
> **(a)** Discuss the **elements of an Onsite Emergency Plan** for a petrochemical complex. Describe the role of the Emergency Control Centre (ECC). **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is the **Emergency Planning Zone (EPZ)**? Explain how it is determined for a **toxic gas release** and a **vapour cloud explosion (VCE)** with the relevant threshold criteria. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — (a)

**Elements of Onsite Emergency Plan:**

1. **Hazard Identification & Risk Assessment (HIRA)** of credible scenarios (fire, explosion, toxic release, runaway reaction).
2. **Emergency Organization Structure** — Incident Commander, Fire Chief, Safety Officer, Medical In-Charge, Communication Officer, Evacuation Warden.
3. **Emergency Control Centre (ECC)** — purpose-built, blast-resistant building with redundant power, communication systems (PA, telephone, radio, CCTV), maps, plans, and live displays.
4. **Communication Systems** — distinct siren codes, two-way radios, public address.
5. **Escape Routes & Assembly Points** — clearly marked, multiple redundant routes.
6. **Fire-Fighting Facilities** — hydrants, monitors, foam systems, portable extinguishers, sprinklers.
7. **Medical Facilities** — first aid stations, occupational health centre, ambulance.
8. **Emergency Shutdown (ESD) Systems** — automated valves, blowdown, isolation.
9. **PPE Inventory** — proximity suits, SCBA, escape respirators, chemical suits.
10. **Training, Mock Drills, Plan Review** — quarterly drills, annual full-scale.
11. **Mutual Aid Agreements** with neighbouring industries.
12. **Records & Documentation** — incident logs, post-event reports.

**Role of ECC**:
- Acts as the **nerve centre** of the emergency response.
- Houses the Incident Commander and all section chiefs.
- Provides real-time information via CCTV, sensors, and field reports.
- Coordinates with offsite agencies via dedicated hotline.
- Maintains updated plans, drawings, and resource inventories.
- Served by redundant utilities (power, water, ventilation) and is **hardened** to withstand overpressure.

*(7 marks: 4 for elements + 3 for ECC role)*

---

### Model Solution — (b)

**Definition of EPZ:**
The **Emergency Planning Zone (EPZ)** is the geographical area surrounding a hazardous installation within which special emergency planning arrangements (warning, evacuation, sheltering) are required to protect the public.

**Determination for Toxic Gas Release:**

Threshold criterion: Concentration ≥ **ERPG-2** (or AEGL-2) for 30 min–1 hour.

EPZ radius is computed using dispersion modelling:

$$
r_{EPZ, toxic} = \sqrt{\dfrac{Q \cdot \tau}{\pi \cdot C_{ERPG\text{-}2} \cdot u}}
$$

For chlorine releases, EPZ may extend 1–5 km. For ammonia, 3–10 km.

**Determination for Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE):**

Threshold criterion: Overpressure ≥ **2 psi (≈ 14 kPa)** corresponding to partial building damage.

EPZ radius for VCE:

$$
r_{EPZ, VCE} = R_{2psi}
$$

Where $R_{2psi}$ is determined from TNT-equivalency or TNO Multi-Energy method:

$$
r_{EPZ, VCE} \propto \sqrt[3]{M_{HC} \cdot H_c / P_{atm}}
$$

For a 50-tonne HC cloud:

$$
R_{2psi} \approx 200 - 400 \text{ m (typical)}
$$

EPZ for VCE is generally smaller than for toxic release, but damage intensity is higher.

**Combined EPZ**: The final EPZ is the **union** of toxic, thermal, and overpressure zones.

*(7 marks: 1 definition + 3 toxic + 3 VCE)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Emergency Planning Questions:**
>
> 1. **Missing statutory citation** — Students often forget to mention *MSIHC Rules 1989, Rule 13*. KTU examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing IC vs. ECC** — Incident Commander is a *person*, Emergency Control Centre is a *place*. Mixing them loses 2 marks.
> 3. **No mention of mock drills** — Drills are **statutorily mandated** (twice yearly minimum). Omitting loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Numerical errors in EPZ** — Using wrong units (ppm vs. mg/m³) or omitting $\pi$ in denominator. Always convert and check units.
> 5. **Ignoring wind direction** — EPZ is **sectoral**, not circular. Use $\theta/2$ in PAR formula, not full $2\pi$.
> 6. **No handover mechanism** — Onsite-to-offsite transition must specify **who decides** and **what triggers it**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember Points for KTU 2024 Module 3 — Emergency Planning:**

- ☐ Emergency planning is **two-tiered**: Onsite (plant-owned) and Offsite (district-owned).
- ☐ **Legal basis**: MSIHC Rules 1989, Rule 13(1) and 13(2); Factories Act 41B; Disaster Management Act 2005.
- ☐ **ICS** has 5 sections: Command, Operations, Planning, Logistics, Finance (+ Liaison + Safety Officer).
- ☐ **Site Incident Commander (SIC)** holds absolute authority during an emergency.
- ☐ **Emergency Control Centre (ECC)** is the nerve centre — hardened, blast-resistant.
- ☐ **EPZ** = Emergency Planning Zone — sectoral geographic area for offsite planning.
- ☐ **ERPG-2 / AEGL-2** = toxic threshold for EPZ.
- ☐ **5 kW/m²** = thermal threshold for 2nd-degree burn in 60 s.
- ☐ **2 psi overpressure** = partial building damage threshold (VCE EPZ).
- ☐ **PAR formula**: $PAR = (\theta/2) \cdot \rho \cdot \alpha \cdot r^2$.
- ☐ **EPZ radius (toxic)**: $r = \sqrt{Q\tau / (\pi C u)}$.
- ☐ **Distinct siren codes** for different emergencies (fire, gas, toxic, shelter-in-place).
- ☐ **Mock drills**: ≥ 2 full-scale + 4 tabletop exercises per year (statutory).
- ☐ **Mutual Aid Agreements (MAA)** with neighbouring industries.
- ☐ **After-Action Review (AAR)** is mandatory post-incident and post-drill.
- ☐ **Five-phase response**: Preparedness → Detection → Onsite Response → Offsite Escalation → Recovery.
- ☐ **Hand-over trigger**: Toxic plume crosses fence, OR ≥ 5 serious injuries, OR resource exhaustion.
- ☐ **Indian statutory apex**: District Crisis Group chaired by **District Collector**.
- ☐ **National apex**: NDMA / NDRF for catastrophic events.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Emergency Planning (Onsite and Offsite)**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and Bloom's Taxonomy Levels L4–L6.*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mock Drill

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mock Drill (Planned Emergency Simulation Exercise):**
> A *Mock Drill* is a structured, pre-planned, and controlled simulation of an emergency scenario (such as fire, toxic gas release, explosion, or process upset) conducted within a process plant to **test, validate, and improve** the effectiveness of the Emergency Response Plan (ERP), the competency of emergency response teams, the integrity of safety systems, and the preparedness of all personnel — without causing actual harm or production loss.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT743)**, mock drills are categorized under *Emergency Preparedness and Response (EPR)*, which is a mandatory component of the **Process Safety Management (PSM)** framework and aligns with standards such as **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119(n)** and the **OISD (Oil Industry Safety Directorate) Standard 116** on emergency planning.

A mock drill is not merely a "fire alarm practice." It is a **documented engineering exercise** with measurable Key Performance Indicators (KPIs), regulatory compliance linkage, and a formal **After-Action Review (AAR)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Cricket Net Practice":**
> Imagine a cricket team that has never practiced in the nets before being sent to play a World Cup final. Even if they know the rules, the bowlers won't know the batsman's weaknesses, the fielders will misjudge catches, and the captain will panic under pressure. A **mock drill** is the "net practice" for a process plant. The real emergency is the "World Cup final." The bowler (fire), the stadium crowd (panic), and the umpire (regulator) are all real, but the *consequences* of failure are simulated. The goal is to find the **gaps in coordination, communication, and equipment readiness** when the stakes are low, so that when the actual crisis hits, the team acts like a well-oiled machine.

## 1.3 Why Mock Drills Are Statistically Essential

Process industry data shows that **over 70% of major industrial disasters** (Bhopal 1984, Texas City 2005, Jaipur 2009) were aggravated by the **absence or failure of rehearsed emergency response**. A mock drill quantifies the **Mean Response Time (MRT)** and **Evacuation Compliance Ratio (ECR)** before a real event occurs.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are typically used to benchmark a mock drill:

- **Mean Response Time (MRT):** Target $\leq$ **5 minutes** for initial response.
- **Total Evacuation Time (TET):** Target $\leq$ **10–15 minutes** depending on plant complexity.
- **Headcount Accuracy (HCA):** Target $\geq$ **98%** of personnel accounted for.
- **Communication Failure Rate (CFR):** Target $\leq$ **2%** of attempted communications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 3 emphasizes that mock drills are *not optional* in chemical process plants. They are a **statutory obligation** under the **Factories Act 1948 (Section 41-B)**, the **Public Liability Insurance Act 1991**, and the **National Disaster Management Act 2005**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Mock Drill Architecture

A KTU-evaluable mock drill framework rests on three pillars:

1. **Pre-Drill Phase (Planning & Design)**
2. **Execution Phase (Live Simulation)**
3. **Post-Drill Phase (Evaluation & Closure)**

### 2.1.1 Pre-Drill Phase

- **Scenario Selection:** Choose a *credible worst-case* scenario (e.g., BLEVE of an LPG bullet, ammonia leak from a refrigeration skid, hydrogen sulfide release in a hydrocracker).
- **Objectives Definition:** SMART objectives — *Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound*.
- **Resource Allocation:** Manpower (Emergency Response Team — ERT), equipment (SCBA sets, fire tenders, gas detectors, ambulances), and budget.
- **Notification Hierarchy:** Inform statutory bodies (Factory Inspector, District Disaster Management Authority) if the drill is *unannounced* or *offsite-impact*.
- **Drill Controller & Observers:** Appoint an independent **Drill Master** (usually a senior HSE manager) and external observers (e.g., mutual aid partners).

### 2.1.2 Execution Phase

- **Scenario Injection:** A designated "Plant Faker" injects the emergency cue (e.g., triggers a leak simulation using a smoke machine or a controlled inert gas release).
- **Live Activation:** Sirens, public-address announcements, shutdown interlocks, and team mobilization.
- **Time-Stamping:** Every event (alarm raised, team arrival, valve closure, evacuation complete) is logged with **HH:MM:SS** precision.
- **Safety Pause:** At any time, the Drill Master can call a **"STOP"** to abort if real danger emerges.

### 2.1.3 Post-Drill Phase

- **Debriefing (Hot Wash):** Within 24 hours, all participants gather to share observations.
- **After-Action Review (AAR):** A formal document comparing *what was planned* vs. *what happened*.
- **Corrective Action (CA) Register:** A list of deficiencies with assigned owners and deadlines.
- **Regulatory Filing:** Submit AAR to the Factory Inspectorate and internal HSE records.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the key quantitative metrics used to evaluate mock drills. KTU examiners frequently test these formulas either directly (as 3-mark short problems) or embedded in a 14-mark design question.

| **Metric (Symbol)** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Target Benchmark** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean Response Time $MRT$ | $MRT = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{i}$ | minutes | $\leq 5$ min |
| Evacuation Compliance Ratio $ECR$ | $ECR = \dfrac{N_{evacuated}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | percent | $\geq 98\%$ |
| Headcount Accuracy $HCA$ | $HCA = \dfrac{N_{accounted}}{N_{present}} \times 100\%$ | percent | $\geq 98\%$ |
| Communication Failure Rate $CFR$ | $CFR = \dfrac{N_{failed\,comms}}{N_{attempted}} \times 100\%$ | percent | $\leq 2\%$ |
| Drill Effectiveness Score $DES$ | $DES = w_1 \cdot ECR + w_2 \cdot HCA - w_3 \cdot CFR$ | score (0–100) | $\geq 85$ |
| Total Elapsed Time $TET$ | $TET = t_{alarm} - t_{all\,clear}$ | minutes | $\leq 15$ min |
| Equipment Readiness Index $ERI$ | $ERI = \dfrac{N_{functional}}{N_{total\,equipment}} \times 100\%$ | percent | $= 100\%$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Note:** The vertical bar in $N_{evacuated}$ is the LaTeX `\vert` style — KTU board evaluators prefer explicit notation like $N_{evacuated}$ to avoid ambiguity with absolute value bars.

## 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering Practice

- **MRT** is the *gold standard* regulator metric. The **OSHA Emergency Response Standard** and **OISD-116** use it to grade site readiness.
- **ECR** quantifies *human behavior* under stress — a critical input for ergonomic redesign of escape routes.
- **DES** is a composite KPI that plant managers report to the Board of Directors. A DES below 80 typically triggers a *mock-drill frequency increase* (e.g., from bi-annual to quarterly).

## 2.4 Types of Mock Drills (KTU-Exam-Relevant Classification)

| **Type** | **Description** | **Frequency (Best Practice)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Tabletop Exercise (TTX)** | Discussion-based, no field movement. A scenario is "talked through" in a conference room. | Quarterly |
| **Walk-Through / Orientation Drill** | Participants physically walk the evacuation route without emergency activation. | Half-yearly |
| **Partial Simulation Drill** | Tests a *specific subsystem* (e.g., fire-water pump activation, gas detection network). | Quarterly |
| **Full-Scale Mock Drill** | Complete plant-wide simulation with ERT mobilization, evacuation, medical response. | **Half-yearly (Mandatory)** |
| **Off-Site Joint Drill** | Coordinated with district administration, fire services, hospitals. | **Annually (Mandatory)** |
| **Unannounced Drill** | Triggered without prior warning to test real-world response latency. | Annually |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Composite Drill Effectiveness Score (DES)

The DES is a *weighted linear combination* of the three core metrics. Below is the full derivation, from first principles, in the format KTU board evaluators reward.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Let there be a plant with:
- Total present personnel $N_{total} = 500$
- Personnel successfully evacuated $N_{evacuated} = 490$
- Personnel accounted for in the assembly register $N_{accounted} = 495$
- Communication attempts $N_{attempted} = 200$
- Communication failures (dropped calls, missed acknowledgments) $N_{failed\,comms} = 6$

We need to compute ECR, HCA, CFR, and DES, with weights $w_1 = 0.5$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.2$ (chosen such that $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1 — Evacuation Compliance Ratio (ECR)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ECR &= \frac{N_{evacuated}}{N_{total}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{490}{500} \times 100\% \\
&= 0.98 \times 100\% \\
&= 98.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Reasoning:* Out of 500 people present, 490 reached the assembly point. The remaining 10 either did not hear the alarm, refused to evacuate, or were in remote isolated locations. 98% compliance is the KTU benchmark.

**Step 2 — Headcount Accuracy (HCA)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HCA &= \frac{N_{accounted}}{N_{present}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{495}{500} \times 100\% \\
&= 0.99 \times 100\% \\
&= 99.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Reasoning:* HCA is *not* the same as ECR. A person can be evacuated (reaches the muster point) but missing from the headcount register due to paperwork failure. Here, 5 evacuees did not register.

**Step 3 — Communication Failure Rate (CFR)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CFR &= \frac{N_{failed\,comms}}{N_{attempted}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{6}{200} \times 100\% \\
&= 0.03 \times 100\% \\
&= 3.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Reasoning:* Out of 200 radio/PA system calls, 6 failed (e.g., interference, dead batteries). The KTU target is $\leq 2\%$, so this drill **fails the CFR test**.

**Step 4 — Composite Drill Effectiveness Score (DES)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DES &= w_1 \cdot ECR + w_2 \cdot HCA - w_3 \cdot CFR \\
&= (0.5 \times 98) + (0.3 \times 99) - (0.2 \times 3) \\
&= 49.0 + 29.7 - 0.6 \\
&= 78.1
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Reasoning:* A DES of 78.1 is **below the 85 threshold**. The plant is *conditionally safe* but the corrective action register must mandate a re-drill within 60 days, specifically targeting communication system overhaul.

### 3.1.3 Analytical Decision Matrix

| **DES Range** | **Grade** | **Action** |
|---|---|---|
| $DES \geq 90$ | A — Excellent | Continue current schedule |
| $85 \leq DES < 90$ | B — Acceptable | Minor corrective actions |
| $75 \leq DES < 85$ | C — Conditional | Re-drill within 60 days |
| $DES < 75$ | F — Fail | Plant-wide emergency audit, regulatory notification |

## 3.2 Derivation of Mean Response Time (MRT) — Worked Example

Suppose the following time-stamps were recorded for a fire alarm in a refinery:

| **Event** | **Time (HH:MM:SS)** |
|---|---|
| Leak detected | 14:02:10 |
| Operator confirms | 14:03:25 |
| Alarm raised | 14:03:40 |
| ERT team paged | 14:03:45 |
| ERT reaches site | 14:07:30 |
| Fire pump started | 14:08:00 |
| Foam applied | 14:09:15 |

Step 1 — Identify the four response legs.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= t_{confirm} - t_{detect} = 14{:}03{:}25 - 14{:}02{:}10 = 75 \text{ s} \\
t_2 &= t_{alarm} - t_{confirm} = 14{:}03{:}40 - 14{:}03{:}25 = 15 \text{ s} \\
t_3 &= t_{ERT\,arrives} - t_{alarm} = 14{:}07{:}30 - 14{:}03{:}40 = 230 \text{ s} \\
t_4 &= t_{foam\,applied} - t_{ERT\,arrives} = 14{:}09{:}15 - 14{:}07{:}30 = 105 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Compute the Mean Response Time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
MRT &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i \\
&= \frac{1}{4} (75 + 15 + 230 + 105) \\
&= \frac{425}{4} \\
&= 106.25 \text{ s} \approx 1.77 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Reasoning:* An MRT of 1.77 min is **well within the 5-min benchmark**, indicating a healthy response chain. However, the *longest leg* ($t_3 = 230$ s) signals that the ERT is taking too long to reach the unit. A corrective action should focus on a closer ERT bay or a pre-positioned quick-response vehicle.

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Table

| **Phase** | **Key Output** | **KTU Marker (Examiner Cue)** |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-Drill | Scenario Document, Notification Letter | "Plan a mock drill for an ammonia leak" |
| Execution | Time-stamped Event Log | "Compute MRT from the log" |
| Post-Drill | AAR + Corrective Action Register | "Evaluate the drill using DES" |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Mock Drill Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Step0: Plant Risk Assessment] --> A1[Step1: Identify Credible Scenarios]
    A1 --> A2[Step2: Define SMART Objectives]
    A2 --> A3[Step3: Appoint Drill Master and Faker]
    A3 --> A4[Step4: Notify Regulatory Bodies]
    A4 --> B1[Step5: Alarm Activation]
    B1 --> B2[Step6: ERT Mobilization]
    B2 --> B3[Step7: Evacuation and Muster]
    B3 --> B4[Step8: Emergency Mitigation]
    B4 --> C1[Step9: All Clear Announcement]
    C1 --> C2[Step10: Hot Wash Debrief]
    C2 --> C3[Step11: Compute KPIs MRT ECR HCA CFR DES]
    C3 --> C4[Step12: After Action Review Report]
    C4 --> C5[Step13: Corrective Action Register]
    C5 --> C6[Step14: Regulatory Submission]
    C6 --> D1[Step15: Schedule Re Drill if DES less than 85]
    D1 --> A1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of an Emergency Response Cell

```mermaid
graph LR
    INC[Incident: Leak Fire Explosion] --> DET[Gas Detector or Flame Sensor]
    DET --> CTRL[Control Room Operator]
    CTRL --> PA[Public Address and Siren System]
    PA --> ERT[Emergency Response Team]
    PA --> EVAC[Plant Personnel Evacuation]
    ERT --> MIT[Fire Water or Containment]
    EVAC --> MSTR[Muster Point and Headcount]
    MIT --> MSTR
    MSTR --> MED[Medical First Responder]
    MSTR --> OBS[Independent Observer Logbook]
    OBS --> AAR[After Action Review Document]
    AAR --> MGMT[Plant Manager and Board]
    AAR --> REG[Factory Inspector and DM Office]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Decision Tree for Drill Effectiveness

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Compute DES] --> S1{DES greater than or equal to 90}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2[Grade A: Maintain Schedule]
    S1 -- No --> S3{DES between 85 and 89}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Grade B: Minor Corrective Action]
    S3 -- No --> S5{DES between 75 and 84}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Grade C: Re Drill in 60 days]
    S5 -- No --> S7[Grade F: Full Audit and Regulatory Notification]
```

## 4.4 Text-Based Schematic — Mock Drill Command Structure

```
+---------------------------------------------------+
|             INCIDENT COMMANDER                    |
|   (Plant Manager / Senior HSE Manager)            |
+----------------------+----------------------------+
                       |
        +--------------+---------------+
        |                              |
   HSE OFFICER                    ERT LEADER
        |                              |
+-------+-------+              +-------+-------+
|       |       |              |       |       |
FIRE  MEDICAL  SECURITY    FIRE    HAZMAT   RESCUE
TEAM   TEAM     TEAM       SQUAD   TEAM     SQUAD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block-Level Architecture?**
> Physical drawings of plant layouts are impossible in a text-based interface. KTU evaluators accept **functional block diagrams** that demonstrate the *flow of information* (alarm → control room → ERT → muster → AAR) as a valid substitute for site plans. The diagrams above clearly show the **information flow and command hierarchy**, which is the *conceptual core* of a mock drill.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a mock drill. List any four objectives of conducting a mock drill in a process plant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A mock drill is a *planned, simulated emergency exercise* conducted to test the effectiveness of the Emergency Response Plan (ERP), validate the competency of the Emergency Response Team (ERT), and identify gaps in evacuation, communication, and equipment readiness — *without causing actual harm*.

Four objectives (½ mark each):
1. **Validation** of the Emergency Response Plan under realistic conditions.
2. **Evaluation** of response times (MRT, TET) against benchmarks.
3. **Identification of deficiencies** in communication, equipment, and personnel training.
4. **Statutory compliance** with the Factories Act 1948, OISD-116, and OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119(n).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a Tabletop Exercise (TTX) and a Full-Scale Mock Drill.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Tabletop Exercise (TTX)** | **Full-Scale Mock Drill** |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Discussion-based | Field-execution based |
| Movement | No physical movement | Actual evacuation, ERT mobilization |
| Resources | Minimal (room, flipchart) | Full (SCBA, fire tenders, ambulances) |
| Frequency | Quarterly | Half-yearly |
| Realism | Low–Medium | High |
| Cost | Low | High |

*(1 mark per major difference; well-tabulated answers get 3 marks easily.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — From [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **pre-drill planning phase** of a mock drill for a hydrocracker unit handling hydrogen sulfide (H₂S). List at least six specific planning activities. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A fire alarm was raised at 09:15:00. The ERT was paged at 09:15:30, arrived on site at 09:18:45, and the fire was extinguished at 09:24:10. Calculate the **Mean Response Time (MRT)** and comment on whether the drill meets the KTU/OISD benchmark of 5 minutes. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Pre-Drill Planning for a Hydrocracker (7 marks):**

1. **Hazard Identification (1 mark):** Recognize that the hydrocracker handles H₂S (toxic, lethal at $\geq$ **100 ppm**), hydrogen (explosive), and hydrocarbons under high pressure ($\geq$ **100 bar**).
2. **Scenario Selection (1 mark):** Choose a credible scenario such as *"H₂S release from a flange on the reactor effluent line"*.
3. **Objectives Definition (1 mark):** SMART objectives, e.g., *"ERT must reach the leak site in $\leq$ 4 min and complete isolation in $\leq$ 8 min."*
4. **Notification (1 mark):** Inform the Factory Inspector, District Disaster Management Authority, and mutual-aid hospitals *at least 7 days in advance* (or as per OISD-116).
5. **Resource Checklist (1 mark):** SCBA sets (one per ERT member), H₂S detectors (calibrated), stretchers, ambulance on standby, wind direction flags.
6. **Roles Assignment (1 mark):** Drill Master (independent observer), Faker (initiates the leak cue), Safety Officer (pause authority), Communication Coordinator.
7. **Risk Assessment of the Drill Itself (1 mark):** Ensure the drill does not trigger a real process upset — isolate the actual unit or use a *simulated* control-room readout.

**Part (b) — MRT Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant time-stamps (1 mark).**
- Alarm raised: $t_{alarm} = $ 09:15:00
- ERT paged: $t_{page} = $ 09:15:30
- ERT arrives: $t_{arrives} = $ 09:18:45
- Fire extinguished: $t_{extinguish} = $ 09:24:10

**Step 2 — Compute the three response legs (3 marks).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_1 &= t_{page} - t_{alarm} = 30 \text{ s} \\
\Delta t_2 &= t_{arrives} - t_{page} = 03{:}15 = 195 \text{ s} \\
\Delta t_3 &= t_{extinguish} - t_{arrives} = 05{:}25 = 325 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the MRT (2 marks).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MRT &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta t_i \\
&= \frac{30 + 195 + 325}{3} \\
&= \frac{550}{3} \\
&= 183.33 \text{ s} \\
&\approx 3.06 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Benchmarking and Comment (1 mark).**
Since $MRT = 3.06 \text{ min} < 5 \text{ min}$ (KTU/OISD benchmark), the drill **meets the target**. However, the longest leg ($\Delta t_3 = 325$ s for fire extinguishment) suggests that the *suppression phase* is the bottleneck, warranting a review of foam concentrate inventory and pump discharge pressure.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **(a)** What is an **After-Action Review (AAR)**? Describe its structure and the four standard sections it must contain. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** In a mock drill at a fertilizer plant, the following data was recorded: $N_{total} = 800$, $N_{evacuated} = 776$, $N_{accounted} = 790$, $N_{attempted} = 250$, $N_{failed\,comms} = 8$. Calculate the **DES** with weights $w_1 = 0.5$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.2$. Grade the drill. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — After-Action Review (7 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (2 marks):** An After-Action Review (AAR) is a *formal document* prepared within 7–14 days of a mock drill that compares the *planned* response with the *actual* response, identifies *root causes* of deviations, and prescribes *corrective actions* to close the gaps.

**Four standard sections of an AAR (5 marks — 1.25 each):**

1. **Executive Summary:** Drill type, date, scenario, key KPIs (MRT, ECR, DES).
2. **What Was Planned vs. What Happened:** A side-by-side table of expected vs. actual outcomes.
3. **Root Cause Analysis (RCA):** For each major deviation, perform a *5-Why* or *Fishbone* analysis to determine the underlying cause (e.g., failed communication → dead battery → no checklist).
4. **Corrective Action Plan (CAP):** Each finding gets an owner, a deadline, and a verification method.

**Part (b) — DES Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — ECR (2 marks).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ECR &= \frac{N_{evacuated}}{N_{total}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{776}{800} \times 100\% \\
&= 97.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — HCA (2 marks).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HCA &= \frac{N_{accounted}}{N_{present}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{790}{800} \times 100\% \\
&= 98.75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — CFR (1 mark).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CFR &= \frac{N_{failed\,comms}}{N_{attempted}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{8}{250} \times 100\% \\
&= 3.20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — DES (2 marks).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DES &= (0.5 \times 97) + (0.3 \times 98.75) - (0.2 \times 3.20) \\
&= 48.50 + 29.625 - 0.64 \\
&= 77.485 \\
&\approx 77.49
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Grading.** Since $75 \leq DES < 85$, the drill is **Grade C — Conditional**. A re-drill must be scheduled within 60 days, with special focus on the communication system (CFR = 3.20% exceeds the 2% target).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Confusing *ECR* and *HCA*. Examiners report that **30% of students** write $HCA = \frac{N_{evacuated}}{N_{total}}$ — this is wrong. ECR measures *evacuation completion*, HCA measures *headcount registration*. They are *not interchangeable*.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting the **negative sign** on the CFR term in the DES formula. A common error is $DES = w_1 \cdot ECR + w_2 \cdot HCA + w_3 \cdot CFR$ — this *rewards* communication failures, which is logically wrong. The correct form **subtracts** $w_3 \cdot CFR$.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Reporting $DES$ without units or grading. A *bare number* loses 1–2 marks. Always state the **grade (A/B/C/F)** and the **required corrective action**.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** In 14-mark design questions, students write *plans* but forget to mention the **regulatory notification** to the Factory Inspector — this alone costs 1 full mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **Mock drill** = planned, simulated emergency exercise to test ERP, ERT, and equipment.
- Statutory basis: **Factories Act 1948 (Sec 41-B)**, **OISD-116**, **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119(n)**, **NDMA 2005**.
- **Six types:** Tabletop, Walk-Through, Partial Simulation, Full-Scale, Off-Site Joint, Unannounced.
- **Frequency:** Full-scale = **half-yearly**; Off-site joint = **annually** (mandatory).
- **Three phases:** Pre-Drill (Planning) → Execution (Simulation) → Post-Drill (AAR + CAP).
- **Key KPIs (must memorize):**
  - $MRT \leq 5$ min
  - $ECR \geq 98\%$
  - $HCA \geq 98\%$
  - $CFR \leq 2\%$
  - $DES \geq 85$ (Grade B or higher)
- **DES formula:** $DES = w_1 \cdot ECR + w_2 \cdot HCA - w_3 \cdot CFR$ (note the **minus sign**).
- **Grading:** A ($\geq 90$), B (85–89), C (75–84, re-drill in 60 days), F ($< 75$, regulatory notification).
- **AAR (After-Action Review)** must be filed within 7–14 days, with four sections: Executive Summary, Planned vs. Actual, Root Cause Analysis, Corrective Action Plan.
- **Drill Master** has absolute *STOP* authority for safety — this is a *statutory* power, not a courtesy.
- **Common KTU pitfall:** Always mention the **regulatory submission** of the AAR (Factory Inspector + District Disaster Management Authority).
- **Real-world analogy:** Mock drill = "Net practice for a process plant's World Cup final."
- **Highest-weight mark in 14-mark questions:** Usually the *calculation* (4–5 marks) + *interpretation* (2–3 marks). Practice plugging numbers into the five formulas above until they are second nature.

> **End of Module 3 — Mock Drill Notes (PECHT743, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technique for Hazard Evaluation - Hazard and Operability Study
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Technique for Hazard Evaluation - Hazard and Operability Study

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)** is a **structured, systematic, and rigorous team-based hazard evaluation technique** that uses a set of *guide words* applied to process parameters to identify potential **deviations** from the design intent, thereby uncovering their **causes**, **consequences**, and recommending **safeguards/actions** to mitigate risks in process plants.

The methodology is fundamentally rooted in the premise that **process hazards and operability problems manifest as deviations** from the designer's original operational philosophy. It was originally developed by the **Process Safety Group of Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI), United Kingdom, in the early 1960s** (around 1963–1964), initially for use in the petrochemical sector, and later standardized internationally through **IEC 61882** and **BS IEC 61882:2016**.

### 1.1.1 Key Terminology Definitions

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Hazard** | A physical situation with the potential to cause human injury, asset damage, or environmental harm. |
| **Operability Problem** | A deviation that may not directly cause injury but can disrupt plant operation, reduce product quality, or cause costly shutdowns. |
| **Deviation** | A departure from the design intent, generated by combining a guide word with a process parameter. |
| **Node** | A specific, bounded section of the process (e.g., a vessel, pipeline segment) where HAZOP study is applied. |
| **Intent** | The expected operational condition (e.g., flow of 5000 kg/h of methane from A to B). |
| **Cause** | The reason(s) why a deviation might occur. |
| **Consequence** | The outcome resulting from the deviation. |
| **Safeguard** | An existing protective measure (instrument, alarm, procedure) to prevent or mitigate the consequence. |
| **Action/Recommendation** | A new safeguard proposed by the team to address the identified issue. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** HAZOP is one of the most frequently assessed topics in Module 3. Examiners expect students to define HAZOP, list guide words, parameters, and describe the procedural steps.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Checker"

Imagine you are baking a cake following your grandmother's recipe:

- **Design Intent**: "Add 200 grams of sugar."
- **Process Parameters**: Quantity, Temperature, Time, Mixing.
- **Guide Words**: NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, OTHER THAN.

The HAZOP team acts like a **careful quality-control chef** who examines each step of the recipe and asks:

- *What if **NO** sugar is added?* (Deviation: NO + SUGAR)
- *What if **MORE** than 200 g is added?* (Deviation: MORE + SUGAR)
- *What if **OTHER THAN** sugar is added (like salt)?* (Deviation: OTHER THAN + SUGAR)
- *What if the sugar is added **REVERSE** first (before flour)?* (Deviation: REVERSE + SEQUENCE)

In a process plant, the HAZOP team applies this same skeptical, interrogative logic to **flow, pressure, temperature, level**, and other parameters at each section of a **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)**. Just as a single mistake can ruin a cake, a single unexamined deviation in a process plant can lead to catastrophic accidents like **Bhopal (1984)**, **Flixborough (1974)**, or **Texas City (2005)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Guide Words:** *"Never Make Less, Rather Observe Other Actions Poorly"* → **N**o, **M**ore, **L**ess, **R**everse, **O**ther, **A**s well as, **P**art of. (Note: Standard 7 guide words are used; some teams include **AS WELL AS** and **PART OF** as supplementary.)

## 1.3 Physical Standards & Constants

| Metric | Standard Reference | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **HAZOP Standard** | **BS IEC 61882:2016** | International standard for HAZOP study application |
| **Risk Matrix Scale** | **5 × 5 Matrix** | Standard severity (S1–S5) × Likelihood (L1–L5) matrix |
| **Acceptable Risk** | **ALARP** | As Low As Reasonably Practicable |
| **Target LOPA Frequency** | **≤ 10⁻⁵ per year** | For SIL-2 (where HAZOP feeds into SIL determination) |
