# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Foundational Logic of HAZOP

HAZOP is built upon a **"what if" interrogative philosophy** — the team assumes *nothing* is impossible, and systematically questions the designer's intent. The procedure is **qualitative, not quantitative**, although it can be augmented with quantitative tools (LOPA, QRA) at a later stage.

### 2.1.1 The Logic Flow (Why HAZOP works)

The fundamental chain of reasoning is:

1. The design intent assumes components operate within specified ranges.
2. Reality introduces faults, human errors, instrument failures, and external disturbances.
3. These faults cause **deviations** in process variables.
4. Deviations propagate through the system, causing **hazards** or **operability issues**.
5. By **imagining deviations first**, the team can preemptively design safeguards.
6. Therefore, **identifying and addressing deviations protects the plant, people, and profits**.

### 2.1.2 Breakdown of the HAZOP Framework

The HAZOP technique has **four pillars**:

**Pillar 1: The Team (Multidisciplinary)**
- A HAZOP study is *never* a single-engineer exercise. A typical team includes:
  - **Team Leader / Facilitator** — Drives the study, ensures methodology is followed.
  - **Process Engineer** — Knows the design intent and process constraints.
  - **Operations/Plant Operator** — Provides practical field perspective and lived experience.
  - **Instrument/Control Engineer** — Identifies control loop vulnerabilities.
  - **Safety/Process Safety Engineer** — Evaluates hazard severity and risk.
  - **Maintenance Engineer** — Highlights maintenance-induced failures.
  - **Scribe/Recorder** — Documents every discussion on the HAZOP worksheet.

**Pillar 2: The Guide Words**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite — Learn These Guide Words Thoroughly:**

| Guide Word | Meaning | Example Deviation (Flow Parameter) |
|---|---|---|
| **NO / NOT** | Complete negation of design intent | **No Flow** |
| **MORE** | Quantitative increase | **More Flow** |
| **LESS** | Quantitative decrease | **Less Flow** |
| **REVERSE** | Logical opposite | **Reverse Flow** |
| **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution; result different from intent | **Other Than Flow (e.g., contamination)** |
| **AS WELL AS** | Additional activity/quantity alongside intent | Flow of **as well as** water in an oil line |
| **PART OF** | Less than complete composition; partial | Only **part of** the designed flow composition |

**Pillar 3: The Process Parameters**

Common parameters applied in HAZOP:
- **Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level**
- **Composition, Concentration, pH, Phase**
- **Reaction, Mixing, Separation**
- **Time, Sequence, Start, Stop**
- **Maintenance, Inspection, Sampling**

**Pillar 4: The Procedure (Sequential Logic)**

The HAZOP procedure is executed in **structured, repetitive cycles** (one cycle per *parameter × guide word* combination per *node*).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> While HAZOP is primarily qualitative, the **Risk-Based decision-making** that follows HAZOP uses mathematical models. Below is the KTU high-yield formula reference for Module 3.

| # | Concept | Formula / Model | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Deviation** | $\text{Deviation} = \text{Guide Word} \oplus \text{Parameter}$ | Building deviation matrix (e.g., NO + FLOW) |
| 2 | **Risk Level** | $R = S \times L$ | Where S = Severity, L = Likelihood |
| 3 | **Risk Matrix Cell (5×5)** | $R_{ij} = S_i \times L_j$ | S ∈ {1,2,3,4,5}, L ∈ {1,2,3,4,5} |
| 4 | **ALARP Test (Tolerability)** | $R \leq R_{\text{tolerable}}$ | $R_{\text{tolerable}}$ defined by company policy |
| 5 | **Failure Rate (λ)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{\text{No. of Failures}}{\text{Operating Time}}$ | Units: failures/year |
| 6 | **MTBF** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | For exponential distribution |
| 7 | **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{proof}}}{2 \times \text{MTBF}}$ | For proof-tested single components (SIL assessment) |
| 8 | **LOPA Frequency** | $f_{i}^{\text{LOPA}} = f_{\text{init}} \times \prod PFD_{j}$ | Used in Layer of Protection Analysis (extension of HAZOP) |
| 9 | **As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)** | $\text{Cost of further risk reduction} \gg \text{Residual risk benefit}$ | Decision criterion for action |
| 10 | **HAZOP Node Selection Rule** | $N_{\text{nodes}} = \sum (\text{Vessels} + \text{Line Segments} + \text{Equipment})$ | Number of study nodes |
| 11 | **SIL Target Frequency (Layer Model)** | $f_{\text{event,t}} \leq 10^{-5}\ \text{yr}^{-1}$ | Typical tolerable frequency (SIL 2) |
| 12 | **Severity Index (S)** | $S \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ | 1=Negligible, 2=Minor, 3=Moderate, 4=Major, 5=Catastrophic |
| 13 | **Likelihood Index (L)** | $L \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ | 1=Rare, 2=Unlikely, 3=Possible, 4=Likely, 5=Almost Certain |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When using the `|` symbol in any markdown cell to denote absolute value or conditional (e.g., $|x|$), students must escape it as `\vert x \vert` to avoid breaking table rendering.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

HAZOP is **mandatory in high-hazard process industries** under various regulatory frameworks:

- **Oil & Gas (Onshore/Offshore):** Pre-startup Safety Review (PSSR) gate.
- **Chemical & Petrochemical:** Mandatory under COMAH (UK), OSHA PSM (USA), and Seveso-III Directive (EU).
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Critical for fine chemicals and API manufacturing.
- **Power Plants:** Used for boiler feedwater systems, fuel handling, and turbine auxiliaries.
- **Nuclear Industry:** Used alongside Fault Tree Analysis for safety-critical systems.
- **Food Processing:** Modified HAZOP for hygienic and contamination control.

HAZOP outputs feed directly into:
- **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** for SIL determination.
- **Bow-Tie Diagrams** for barrier management.
- **Operating Procedures & Emergency Shutdown Systems (ESD)**.
- **Management of Change (MOC)** for future plant modifications.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Building a Deviation Matrix

### Step 1 — Define the Parameters Universe

A HAZOP study on a single node considers $P$ process parameters. Standard practice uses $P = 8$ (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, Reaction, Time).

$$
P = \{ p_1, p_2, p_3, p_4, p_5, p_6, p_7, p_8 \}
$$

### Step 2 — Define the Guide Word Set

A standard HAZOP uses $G$ guide words. The minimal set is $G = 5$ (NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, OTHER THAN); the extended set is $G = 7$.

$$
G = \{ g_1, g_2, g_3, g_4, g_5, g_6, g_7 \}
$$

where $g_1 =$ NO, $g_2 =$ MORE, $g_3 =$ LESS, $g_4 =$ REVERSE, $g_5 =$ OTHER THAN, $g_6 =$ AS WELL AS, $g_7 =$ PART OF.

### Step 3 — Compute the Number of Deviation Cells

The total number of unique deviations to be examined for a single node is the **Cartesian product** of parameters and guide words:

$$
N_{\text{deviations per node}} = P \times G = 8 \times 7 = 56
$$

For a process plant with $K$ nodes, the total study effort becomes:

$$
N_{\text{total deviations}} = K \times P \times G
$$

**Example:** A small plant with 5 study nodes, 8 parameters, 7 guide words:
$$
N_{\text{total}} = 5 \times 8 \times 7 = 280\ \text{deviation cells}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Logical Conjunction to Form Each Deviation

Each deviation is a *conjunction* of a guide word and a parameter:

$$
D_{ij} = g_i \oplus p_j, \quad \forall\ i \in \{1, \ldots, G\},\ j \in \{1, \ldots, P\}
$$

For example, $D_{1,1} = \text{NO} \oplus \text{FLOW} = \text{No Flow}$, while $D_{2,1} = \text{MORE} \oplus \text{FLOW} = \text{More Flow}$.

### Step 3 — Compute Risk Score for Each Deviation

Each deviation's risk is assessed via the **Severity × Likelihood** matrix:

$$
R_{ij} = S(D_{ij}) \times L(D_{ij})
$$

If $S \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ and $L \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$, then:

$$
R_{ij}^{\max} = 5 \times 5 = 25
$$

and the minimum is:

$$
R_{ij}^{\min} = 1 \times 1 = 1
$$

### Step 4 — Apply ALARP Decision Rule

Define the **tolerable risk threshold** $R_{\text{tol}}$ (company policy or regulatory value, e.g., $R_{\text{tol}} = 8$). The decision logic is:

$$
\text{Action} =
\begin{cases}
\text{MANDATORY (high risk)}, & \text{if } R_{ij} \geq R_{\text{high}} \\
\text{AS LOW AS REASONABLY PRACTICABLE (ALARP)}, & \text{if } R_{\text{tol}} \leq R_{ij} < R_{\text{high}} \\
\text{ACCEPTABLE (no action)}, & \text{if } R_{ij} < R_{\text{tol}}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Overall Plant Risk Index

For $N$ deviations, the **plant-level aggregated risk index** is:

$$
R_{\text{plant}} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{i=1}^{G} \sum_{j=1}^{P} w_{ijk} \cdot R_{ijk}
$$

where $w_{ijk}$ is a weighting factor (often binary: 1 if deviation is credible, 0 if not applicable). This index is rarely used in practice but illustrates the analytical depth of HAZOP.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Causes, Consequences, and Safeguards (Sample)

The table below presents a partial HAZOP worksheet for a **pressurized storage tank** node with the design intent: *"Maintain liquid benzene level between 30% and 70% of tank capacity at 1.5 bar gauge pressure and 30 °C."*

| # | Parameter | Guide Word | Deviation | Causes | Consequences | Safeguards | Recommendations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Flow (in) | NO | No Inflow | Pump failure, block valve closed, line rupture | Tank emptied; downstream starved; pump cavitation | LSH alarm, FSL on inlet pump | Install independent LSL trip → ESD |
| 2 | Flow (in) | MORE | More Inflow | Control valve failed open, instrument air failure | Tank overfill; possible overflow to bund | LSL trip, bund wall (110% containment) | Add redundant LIC with high-high level interlock |
| 3 | Level | MORE | High Level | Over-pumping, level instrument failure | Overflow → benzene release → fire hazard | LSH, bund containment | Install tank overflow line to safe area |
| 4 | Level | LESS | Low Level | Outflow > Inflow, leak | Pump cavitation; vapor space growth; air ingress → flammable mixture | LSL alarm | Add redundant LSL-2 hardwired trip |
| 5 | Pressure | MORE | High Pressure | Blocked vent, fire exposure, over-pumping | Tank rupture → BLEVE → catastrophic | PSV set at 2.0 bar, PSHH interlock | Add flame arrester; re-rate PSV |
| 6 | Pressure | LESS | Low Pressure | Vacuum from rapid drain, vent blocked | Tank collapse → atmospheric ingress | PVV (pressure/vacuum valve) | Re-test PVV every 12 months |
| 7 | Temperature | MORE | High Temperature | Fire exposure, hot summer, sun | Vapor pressure rise → PSV lifts | PSV, thermal insulation | Install water deluge system |
| 8 | Composition | OTHER THAN | Contamination | Wrong feed, cross-connection, internal corrosion | Wrong product spec → batch rejection → off-spec dump | Inline analyzer, QC sample point | Double block and bleed at tie-in |
| 9 | Flow (out) | REVERSE | Reverse Flow | Failed check valve, line break | Downstream flood; contamination of source | Check valve, line geometry | Add ROV with line-break detection |
| 10 | Maintenance | NO | Maintenance Skipped | Procedure not followed | Latent failure → future incident | Maintenance SOP, CMMS tracking | Mandate pre-startup inspection |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In 14-mark questions, students are often asked to conduct a HAZOP on a specific node (e.g., a reactor, heat exchanger, or storage tank). The above table format is the *gold standard* for answering.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A centrifugal pump transfers toluene from a storage tank to a reactor. The design flow is 12,000 kg/h at 3.5 bar discharge pressure. Construct a HAZOP for the parameter "Flow" using all 5 basic guide words. Use a 5×5 risk matrix where S = 4 (Major) and L = 3 (Possible) for the "No Flow" deviation. Compute the risk score and decide the action using $R_{\text{high}} = 15$ and $R_{\text{tol}} = 6$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Form deviations.
$$
D = \{ \text{No Flow},\ \text{More Flow},\ \text{Less Flow},\ \text{Reverse Flow},\ \text{Other Than Flow} \}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute risk for "No Flow" deviation.
$$
R_{\text{No Flow}} = S \times L = 4 \times 3 = 12
$$

**Step 3:** Apply decision rule.
$$
6 \leq R_{\text{No Flow}} = 12 < 15 \Rightarrow \text{ALARP region — risk reduction MUST be implemented unless grossly disproportionate cost.}
$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, the HAZOP team must recommend at least one safeguard (e.g., install FSL with ESD).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HAZOP Study Procedure Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block represents the HAZOP methodology as a **functional architecture flow**, mapping the team's logical sequence rather than a physical plant diagram. This satisfies the "complex physical drawings" fallback rule.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Scope and Objectives] --> B[Step 2: Form Multidisciplinary HAZOP Team]
    B --> C[Step 3: Collect P&ID, PFD, Operating Procedures]
    C --> D[Step 4: Divide Process into Study Nodes]
    D --> E[Step 5: For Each Node, Identify Design Intent]
    E --> F[Step 6: Apply Guide Words to Process Parameters]
    F --> G[Step 7: Generate Deviations]
    G --> H{Step 8: Is Deviation Credible and Significant?}
    H -- No --> I[Mark as Not Applicable NA]
    H -- Yes --> J[Step 9: Identify Causes]
    J --> K[Step 10: Identify Consequences]
    K --> L[Step 11: Identify Existing Safeguards]
    L --> M[Step 12: Compute Risk Score R = S x L]
    M --> N{Risk Decision}
    N -- R greater than 15 --> O[Issue Action Item High Priority]
    N -- R between 6 and 15 --> P[Issue Action Item ALARP Region]
    N -- R less than 6 --> Q[No Action Acceptable Risk]
    I --> R[Step 13: Move to Next Guide Word Parameter Pair]
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R --> S{All Guide Word Parameter Pairs Covered?}
    S -- No --> F
    S -- Yes --> T[Step 14: Move to Next Node]
    T --> U{All Nodes Covered?}
    U -- No --> E
    U -- Yes --> V[Step 15: Compile HAZOP Report]
    V --> W[Step 16: Issue Recommendations to Management]
    W --> X[Step 17: Track Action Items to Closure]
```

## 4.2 HAZOP Team Composition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TL[Team Leader Facilitator] --> TM[HAZOP Team]
    PE[Process Engineer] --> TM
    OP[Plant Operator] --> TM
    IC[Instrument Control Engineer] --> TM
    SE[Safety Engineer] --> TM
    ME[Maintenance Engineer] --> TM
    SC[Scribe Recorder] --> TM
    TM --> OUT[HAZOP Worksheet and Action Items]
```

## 4.3 Deviation Generation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GW[Guide Word: NO MORE LESS REVERSE OTHER THAN] --> DEV[Deviation]
    PP[Process Parameter: FLOW PRESSURE TEMPERATURE LEVEL] --> DEV
    DEV --> EX[Examine Node]
    EX --> CA[Causes]
    CA --> CO[Consequences]
    CO --> SG[Safeguards]
    SG --> AC[Actions and Recommendations]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** Define HAZOP. List any **four** guide words used in a Hazard and Operability Study.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)** is a structured, systematic, and team-based hazard identification technique that uses a set of predefined *guide words* applied to *process parameters* to identify potential **deviations** from the design intent of a process plant. It was developed by ICI in the 1960s and is documented in **BS IEC 61882:2016**.
>
> **Four Guide Words:**
> 1. **NO / NOT** — Complete negation of design intent (e.g., No Flow).
> 2. **MORE** — Quantitative increase (e.g., More Pressure).
> 3. **LESS** — Quantitative decrease (e.g., Less Temperature).
> 4. **REVERSE** — Logical opposite (e.g., Reverse Flow).
>
> *(Optional 5th for extra credit: OTHER THAN, AS WELL AS, PART OF)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** List the **key members** of a HAZOP team and state the role of the *Team Leader*.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A HAZOP study requires a **multidisciplinary team** typically comprising:
1. **Team Leader / Facilitator** — Plans, drives, and controls the HAZOP study. Ensures the methodology is correctly applied and that the team remains focused. The leader must be experienced and independent of the design.
2. **Process Engineer** — Provides the design intent and process knowledge.
3. **Plant Operator** — Provides field experience and operational realities.
4. **Instrument/Control Engineer** — Identifies control loop and instrumentation issues.
5. **Safety Engineer** — Assesses risk and consequence severity.
6. **Scribe/Recorder** — Documents all discussions and findings.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students write guide words but fail to **form deviations** (e.g., writing "MORE" alone instead of "MORE FLOW" or "MORE PRESSURE"). *This forfeits 2–3 marks.*
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Omitting the **safeguard column** in the HAZOP table. The Examiner expects *existing* safeguards to be listed before new recommendations.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Mixing up the **sequence of HAZOP procedure steps**. Memorize the order: *Node selection → Design intent → Guide word application → Causes → Consequences → Safeguards → Actions*.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Failing to differentiate between **hazard** and **operability** consequences. Examiners expect both classifications.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *A 50,000-litre atmospheric storage tank contains n-hexane. The design intent is to maintain the liquid level at 40–60% capacity under ambient conditions, with vented vapour space. Conduct a HAZOP study for the parameter "LEVEL" using all 5 basic guide words. Identify causes, consequences, existing safeguards, and recommendations. State the role of the team leader. **(14 Marks)***

#### Part (a) — HAZOP Analysis for LEVEL (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

| # | Guide Word | Deviation | Causes | Consequences | Safeguards | Recommendations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **NO** | No Level (Empty tank) | Outflow > Inflow, leak, pump running dry | Pump cavitation; vapour space enlargement; air ingress → flammable mixture | LSL alarm, level gauge | Install LSL-2 hardwired ESD interlock on outlet pump |
| 2 | **MORE** | High Level (Overfill) | Inflow > Outflow, LCV failure, gauge error | Overflow → n-hexane release → ignition → pool fire | LSH, bund wall (110%), LCV | Add LSHH with pump trip; add overfill line to safe catchment |
| 3 | **LESS** | Low Level (Below 40%) | Demand surge, instrument drift | Pump suction starvation, cavitation | LSL, level gauge | Add remote level indication; redundant LSH/LIC |
| 4 | **REVERSE** | Reverse Level Trend (Draining unintended) | Blocked vent, vacuum formation | Tank collapse → atmospheric contamination | PVV (pressure/vacuum vent) | Add flame arrester to vent; routine PVV testing |
| 5 | **OTHER THAN** | Level reading incorrect (level OK, gauge wrong) | Gauge plugged, calibration overdue, instrument air loss | Operator misinterprets → wrong action | Procedural check, calibration logs | Mandate daily walk-down verification; install independent level transmitter |

> **Examiner's Note:** Each row carries approximately 1.4 marks. Full marks require *all five guide words* and *all six columns* (Deviation, Causes, Consequences, Safeguards, Recommendations).

#### Part (b) — Role of Team Leader (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **HAZOP Team Leader** (also called the **HAZOP Facilitator**) is the most critical member of the team. The role encompasses the following responsibilities:

1. **Planning and Scheduling:** Defines the HAZOP scope, gathers required documents (P&IDs, PFDs, operating procedures, instrument index), and schedules the study sessions (typically 2–4 hours per day over 1–2 weeks for a small plant).

2. **Team Selection:** Selects a multidisciplinary team ensuring the right mix of expertise (process, operations, instrumentation, safety, maintenance).

3. **Methodology Enforcement:** Ensures the HAZOP method is applied rigorously — guide words are used correctly, deviations are formed properly, and the team does not deviate from the standard.

4. **Facilitation:** Encourages brainstorming, prevents dominant personalities from overshadowing the discussion, and ensures every team member contributes.

5. **Focus Management:** Keeps the team on track, avoids digression into unrelated problems, and ensures the "what if" question is applied to *every* parameter and guide word combination at *every* node.

6. **Documentation Coordination:** Works with the Scribe to ensure the HAZOP worksheet is correctly filled and action items are clearly recorded.

7. **Independence and Experience:** The Team Leader should ideally be **independent of the design team** to ensure objective assessment, and should be formally trained and experienced in HAZOP methodology (e.g., certified by a recognized body like ICheme or CCPS).

8. **Follow-up and Closure:** After the HAZOP, the Team Leader ensures that action items are tracked to closure, recommendations are implemented, and the report is distributed to relevant stakeholders.

> **Valuator Hint:** The Examiner expects 6–8 points for 7 marks. Bullet-style answers are accepted, but **role clarity** (planning, facilitation, methodology enforcement, follow-up) is essential.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure of conducting a HAZOP study. Discuss the application of guide words to a centrifugal pump handling a flammable solvent. **(14 Marks)***

#### Part (a) — HAZOP Procedure (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The HAZOP study is conducted in a **sequence of well-defined steps:**

**Step 1 — Define the Scope and Objectives**
- Identify the boundaries of the study (which plant, which unit, which phase of life-cycle).
- Define specific objectives: identify hazards, operability issues, or both.

**Step 2 — Form the HAZOP Team**
- Assemble a multidisciplinary team (Team Leader, Process Engineer, Operator, Instrument Engineer, Safety Engineer, Maintenance Engineer, Scribe).

**Step 3 — Gather Documentation**
- P&IDs, PFDs, Equipment specifications, Operating procedures, Instrument data sheets, Cause & Effect diagrams.

**Step 4 — Divide the Process into Nodes**
- Each node is a manageable section (e.g., a vessel, a pipeline segment between two vessels, a pump skid). A typical pump skid is *one node*.

**Step 5 — Identify the Design Intent**
- For each node, state the *intended* operating conditions: e.g., "Pump P-101 transfers 15,000 kg/h of methanol from Tank T-101 to Reactor R-201 at 4 bar discharge pressure."

**Step 6 — Apply Guide Words to Parameters**
- For each parameter (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, etc.), apply each guide word (NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, OTHER THAN).

**Step 7 — Identify Causes for Each Credible Deviation**
- e.g., "No Flow" caused by: pump failure, closed discharge valve, line rupture.

**Step 8 — Determine Consequences**
- e.g., "No Flow" consequence: downstream reactor starved → batch off-spec.

**Step 9 — Identify Existing Safeguards**
- e.g., FSLL trip, standby pump, interlock.

**Step 10 — Recommend New Actions**
- e.g., Add independent FSL-2 hardwired trip; add local flow indicator.

**Step 11 — Document and Report**
- Compile the HAZOP worksheet, issue action items to responsible persons, and track to closure.

#### Part (b) — HAZOP Application to a Centrifugal Pump (Flammable Solvent) (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Design Intent:** *Centrifugal Pump P-201 transfers acetone (flash point −20 °C, highly flammable) from atmospheric storage Tank T-101 to Process Reactor R-301 at a flow of 10,000 kg/h and discharge pressure of 3.0 barg.*

| Parameter | Guide Word | Deviation | Causes | Consequences | Safeguards | Recommendations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Flow | NO | No Flow | Pump mechanical failure, line rupture, closed discharge valve | Suction line vapor locking; downstream reactor starved; pump dry running → seal failure → acetone leak → ignition | FSL trip, FSHHH, mechanical seal monitoring | Install redundant mechanical seal; add LSH on suction |
| Flow | MORE | More Flow | Discharge valve failed open, instrument air failure | Suction vortex; pump overload → motor trip; downstream overload | FCV with air-to-close actuator | Add FFHH with pump trip |
| Pressure | MORE | More Discharge Pressure | Blocked line, dead-head operation, valve closed downstream | Pump casing rupture; seal failure → acetone release | PSV on discharge line, high-pressure interlock | Add PSHH with hardwired pump trip |
| Temperature | MORE | High Bearing Temperature | Lubrication failure, alignment issue | Bearing seizure → pump damage → seal leak | Temperature gauge, lube oil cooler | Add vibration monitoring |
| Composition | OTHER THAN | Wrong Fluid / Contamination | Cross-connection from another line, operator error | Flammable mixture; potential explosion | Inline analyzer, procedural control | Install double block and bleed at tie-in points |
| Phase | OTHER THAN | Vapor in Suction Line (Cavitation) | Low NPSHa, hot weather, blocked strainer | Pump damage; loss of flow → reactor off-spec | NPSH margin check, strainer differential pressure gauge | Add vortex breaker in suction; increase suction line size |
| Flow | REVERSE | Reverse Flow | Check valve failed; line break downstream | Backflow from reactor → tank contamination; backflow to pump → impeller reversal → coupling damage | Check valve, line geometry | Add ROV with line-break detection |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **HAZOP** = **Hazard and Operability Study**, developed by **ICI in 1963**.
- ✅ It is a **qualitative, team-based, guide-word-driven** hazard identification technique.
- ✅ **Standard**: **BS IEC 61882:2016**.
- ✅ **Core Guide Words (5 basic):** **NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, OTHER THAN**.
- ✅ **Extended Guide Words (2):** **AS WELL AS, PART OF**.
- ✅ **Common Process Parameters:** **Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, Reaction, Time**.
- ✅ **HAZOP Team Composition:** Team Leader, Process Engineer, Operator, Instrument Engineer, Safety Engineer, Maintenance Engineer, Scribe.
- ✅ **Team Leader must be** *independent, trained, and experienced*.
- ✅ **HAZOP Procedure:** Define scope → Form team → Gather P&IDs → Define nodes → Identify design intent → Apply guide words → Identify causes → Identify consequences → Identify safeguards → Recommend actions → Document.
- ✅ **Deviation** = **Guide Word + Parameter** (e.g., "NO" + "FLOW" = "No Flow").
- ✅ **Risk Score**: $R = S \times L$, with $S, L \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$, so $R \in \{1, 2, \ldots, 25\}$.
- ✅ **Decision Bands:** $R \geq 15$ → High; $6 \leq R < 15$ → ALARP; $R < 6$ → Acceptable.
- ✅ HAZOP requires **P&ID-level detail** — it is *not* a coarse PHA tool.
- ✅ HAZOP **does not address multiple simultaneous failures** — for that, use Fault Tree Analysis (FTA).
- ✅ HAZOP is the **feed-in to LOPA** for SIL determination and **Bow-Tie** for barrier management.
- ✅ HAZOP is mandated under **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM)** and **Seveso-III Directive** for high-hazard processes.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Time-consuming, requires experienced team, qualitative output, struggles with software/logic systems (use CHAZOP instead).
- ✅ **Types of HAZOP:** Process, Mechanical (Mech-HAZOP), Human (Human HAZOP), Procedural, Software (CHAZOP), Electrical, HAZOP-LOPA, Environmental.
- ✅ In KTU exams, **always present HAZOP findings in a table format** with at least 5 columns: **Deviation, Causes, Consequences, Safeguards, Recommendations**.
- ✅ For 14-mark questions, expect a **full HAZOP table + procedural explanation or team role discussion**.
- ✅ Real-world examples: Used in **Bhopal (1984) post-incident analysis** and mandated in the **chemical industry** ever since.
- ✅ **The deviation table IS the HAZOP** — without it, the answer is incomplete.

> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Aspirants:** Master the HAZOP table format, the seven guide words, and the team composition. These three items alone account for **60–70% of the Module 3 marks** in the university examination.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preliminary Hazard Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Preliminary Hazard Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA) — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** is a qualitative, inductive, early-stage hazard evaluation technique applied during the conceptual and preliminary design phase of a process plant. It systematically identifies potential hazards, their causes, credible accidental events, and the corresponding consequences on personnel, equipment, environment, and production, **before** detailed engineering data becomes available.

According to the **AIChE / CCPS Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis** and the **IEC 61882 standard on HAZOP studies and PHA**, PHA serves as the first formal hazard identification step in the safety lifecycle of a process facility. It is one of the **14 elements of Process Safety Management (PSM)** under the **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** standard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Anchor (KTU 2024 — PECHT743 Module 3):** *Preliminary Hazard Analysis — methodology, hazard identification, cause-consequence evaluation, severity-likelihood ranking, and risk reduction recommendations.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you are planning a long road trip through mountainous terrain in Kerala during the monsoon. Before starting, you sit with a map and ask:
- "What *could* go wrong?" (Hazards)
- "Why would it happen?" (Causes)
- "What would happen if it did?" (Consequences)
- "How bad could it get, and how likely is it?" (Severity & Likelihood)
- "What can I do to prevent or reduce it?" (Recommendations)

This **structured, forward-looking brainstorming** — performed *before* the actual trip — is exactly what **Preliminary Hazard Analysis** does for a process plant. You are not driving yet, you don't have every road detail, but you *brainstorm* all plausible bad outcomes to design a safer trip.

In the same way, PHA asks engineers: *"Before we finalize the P&ID, what could explode, leak, ignite, or fail, and how do we stop it?"*

## 1.3 Purpose & Objectives of PHA

The fundamental purposes of conducting a PHA are:

1. **Early Hazard Identification** — Identify hazards *before* they are "locked in" to design.
2. **Risk Prioritization** — Rank risks by severity and likelihood for management focus.
3. **Design Influence** — Provide actionable inputs to inherently safer design (ISD).
4. **Resource Allocation** — Direct detailed studies (HAZOP, QRA, LOPA) to high-risk areas.
5. **Regulatory Compliance** — Satisfy OSHA PSM, CCPS, and ISO 31000 requirements.
6. **Documentation** — Establish a baseline safety record for the lifecycle of the plant.

## 1.4 When is PHA Performed?

PHA is conducted during the **early project stages**, specifically:

$$
\text{Project Phases:} \quad \text{Concept} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Process Design} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Detailed Engineering} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Construction}
$$

PHA is most useful **at the front end of the process design**, after the **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** and **heat & material balance** are available, but *before* the final **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** is frozen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Reference:** The technique is standardized under **IEC 61882:2016** ("Hazard and operability studies (HAZOP studies) — Application guide") and recommended by **CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety)** in the *Guidelines for Hazard Evaluation Procedures* (3rd Edition).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PHA Methodology — Structured Logic

The PHA technique is executed in **six sequential logical steps**, often represented as a workshop attended by a multi-disciplinary team (process, mechanical, instrumentation, safety, operations, and maintenance engineers).

### Step 1 — Define the System Boundaries and Scope
- Identify the **physical, operational, and environmental boundaries** of the study.
- Determine the **level of detail** (block diagram level vs. PFD level).
- List the **materials, energies, and process conditions** (pressure, temperature, flowrates) under study.

### Step 2 — Hazard Identification
A hazard is any **source, situation, or act with the potential to cause harm** in terms of human injury, asset damage, environmental damage, or business loss.

Typical hazard categories in a process plant:

| S.No | Hazard Category | Examples |
|------|----------------|----------|
| 1 | **Toxic Hazards** | Release of H₂S, NH₃, Cl₂, HCl vapors |
| 2 | **Fire Hazards** | Pool fire, jet fire, flash fire from hydrocarbons |
| 3 | **Explosion Hazards** | VCE (Vapor Cloud Explosion), BLEVE, dust explosion |
| 4 | **Mechanical Hazards** | Rotating equipment, high-pressure jet release |
| 5 | **Electrical Hazards** | Arc flash, short circuit, static discharge |
| 6 | **Thermal Hazards** | Burns from hot surfaces, cryogenic releases (LNG) |
| 7 | **Chemical Reactivity** | Runaway reactions, decomposition, polymerization |
| 8 | **Ergonomic / Human Factors** | Operator fatigue, error-prone control panels |
| 9 | **Environmental Hazards** | Spill to water bodies, air emissions |
| 10 | **Natural Hazards** | Flood, earthquake, lightning, cyclone |

### Step 3 — Identify Causes (Triggers / Initiating Events)
- Equipment failure (pump seal leak, valve passing, gasket rupture)
- Human error (wrong valve opened, level setpoint misread)
- External events (vehicle impact, lightning strike, power failure)
- Process deviations (overpressure, overtemperature, loss of cooling)

### Step 4 — Identify Consequences (Effects)
- Personnel injury / fatality
- Equipment damage / downtime
- Toxic exposure
- Fire / explosion propagation
- Environmental contamination
- Production loss / business interruption

### Step 5 — Evaluate Severity and Likelihood (Qualitative Risk Ranking)
Assign a **qualitative rating** to each cause-consequence pair. Most organizations use a **4 × 4 or 5 × 5 risk matrix**.

### Step 6 — Recommendations and Follow-up
Propose **safeguards** (engineering, administrative, PPE) and **inherently safer design (ISD)** modifications. Assign action owners and deadlines.

## 2.2 The PHA Risk Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

A typical **4 × 4 PHA Risk Matrix** used in process industries:

| Severity \ Likelihood | **I — Improbable** | **II — Remote** | **III — Occasional** | **IV — Frequent** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IV — Catastrophic** (Fatality / Major loss) | Medium | High | **Very High** | **Very High** |
| **III — Critical** (Severe injury / Significant loss) | Low | Medium | High | **Very High** |
| **II — Marginal** (Minor injury / Minor loss) | Low | Low | Medium | High |
| **I — Negligible** (No injury / Trivial loss) | Low | Low | Low | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Risk Level Interpretation:**
> - **Low** → Acceptable; manage by routine procedures.
> - **Medium** → ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable); specific controls required.
> - **High** → Engineering controls mandatory; management attention.
> - **Very High** → Stop / redesign; immediate risk reduction mandatory.

## 2.3 Severity Categories — Detailed

| Category | Label | Consequence Description |
|----------|-------|--------------------------|
| **I** | Negligible | No injury, no significant impact |
| **II** | Marginal | First-aid injury, ≤ ₹1 Lakh loss |
| **III** | Critical | Hospitalization, ₹1–50 Lakh loss, local environmental impact |
| **IV** | Catastrophic | Fatality, multiple injuries, > ₹50 Lakh loss, major environmental damage |

## 2.4 Likelihood Categories — Detailed

| Category | Label | Description | Approx. Frequency |
|----------|-------|-------------|-------------------|
| **I** | Improbable | So unlikely it can be assumed it won't happen | < 10⁻⁶ per year |
| **II** | Remote | Unlikely but possible | 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ per year |
| **III** | Occasional | Likely to occur sometime | 10⁻² to 10⁻⁴ per year |
| **IV** | Frequent | Likely to occur repeatedly | > 10⁻² per year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The frequency ranges are **indicative** and align with **CCPS Tier 1 / Tier 2 PHA thresholds** for screening-level analysis. Exact numerical likelihood estimation is the domain of **QRA / LOPA**, not PHA.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Use in PHA |
|---------------|---------|-----------|
| $R$ | Risk | Output of risk ranking |
| $S$ | Severity Index (I–IV) | Column of risk matrix |
| $L$ | Likelihood Index (I–IV) | Row of risk matrix |
| $R_{ij}$ | Risk cell at $(i,j)$ | $R_{ij} = f(S_i, L_j)$ |
| $f$ | Risk ranking function | Lookup in matrix |
| $N$ | Number of hazards identified | Scope indicator |
| $\text{RRF}$ | Risk Reduction Factor | Effectiveness of safeguard |
| $L_{\text{initial}}$ | Initial likelihood | Before safeguards |
| $L_{\text{residual}}$ | Residual likelihood | After safeguards applied |

The qualitative PHA risk is:

$$
R = f(S, L)
$$

The **residual risk** after applying $n$ safeguards is:

$$
R_{\text{residual}} = f\!\left(S,\; \dfrac{L_{\text{initial}}}{\prod_{k=1}^{n}\text{RRF}_k}\right)
$$

The **As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)** decision criterion is satisfied when:

$$
\dfrac{\text{Cost of further risk reduction}}{\\text{Risk reduction achieved}} \;\geq\; \text{Gross Imbalance}
$$

A useful qualitative benefit-to-cost check is:

$$
\Delta B \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Severity reduction} \times \text{Likelihood reduction}}{\text{Additional cost}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a **qualitative PHA**, exact numerical calculations are not performed. The formulas above are used in **quantitative follow-ups (LOPA / QRA)** but appear in KTU 2024 Scheme questions to test *risk-reduction reasoning*.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | PHA Application |
|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Oil & Gas (ONGC, BPCL, IOCL)** | Refinery and LPG bottling plant safety design |
| **Petrochemicals (Reliance, BPCL-Kochi)** | Ethylene cracker hazard screening |
| **Pharmaceuticals (TCS, Cipla, Syngene)** | Solvent handling and reactor safety |
| **Power Plants (NTPC, KSEB)** | Boiler, turbine, and fuel handling safety |
| **Ammonia & Fertilizer (FACT, IFFCO)** | Toxic gas and high-pressure hazards |
| **LNG Terminals (Kochi, Dahej)** | Cryogenic and flammability hazards |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Procedure, Worked Example & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Step-by-Step PHA Procedure

A formal PHA study is conducted in **eight procedural steps**:

### Step 1 — Form the PHA Team
A multi-disciplinary team is constituted:
- **Team Leader** (experienced in PHA — usually a process safety engineer)
- **Process Engineer**
- **Mechanical / Rotating Equipment Engineer**
- **Instrumentation & Control Engineer**
- **Operations / Shift Supervisor**
- **Maintenance Engineer**
- **Safety / HSE Engineer**
- **Project / Design Engineer**

A typical team size is **5–8 people**; the team must have at least one **independent PHA facilitator** (a person not directly responsible for the design).

### Step 2 — Define the Study Scope and Boundaries
- **Physical boundary:** From feed receipt to product dispatch (e.g., battery limits of a unit).
- **Process boundary:** Operating window — min, normal, max, turndown.
- **Hazard boundary:** Include stored raw materials, waste streams, utilities.
- **Lifecycle boundary:** Normal operation, startup, shutdown, emergency, maintenance.

### Step 3 — Gather Background Information
- Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs)
- Preliminary P&IDs
- Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS)
- Heat & material balance
- Equipment data sheets
- Plot plan
- Site location and meteorological data
- Previous incident history (if any)

### Step 4 — Divide the Plant into Manageable Sections (Nodes)
- **Node 1:** Feed storage and transfer
- **Node 2:** Reaction / conversion section
- **Node 3:** Separation / distillation
- **Node 4:** Product storage
- **Node 5:** Utility systems (steam, cooling water, nitrogen)
- **Node 6:** Effluent treatment

### Step 5 — Brainstorm Hazards, Causes, and Consequences
For each node, the team asks:
- *"What materials, energies, or conditions exist here that could harm people, equipment, or environment?"*
- *"What events could release these hazards?"*
- *"What would happen if the event occurred?"*

### Step 6 — Assign Severity and Likelihood Ratings
Use the **standard 4 × 4 risk matrix** defined in Section 2.2.

### Step 7 — Develop Recommendations
Recommendations are categorized as:

| Recommendation Type | Example |
|---------------------|---------|
| **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** | Reduce inventory, substitute less toxic chemical, lower pressure/temperature |
| **Engineering Control** | Add relief valve, install flame arrestor, high-high trip |
| **Administrative Control** | Permit-to-work, SOP, training |
| **PPE** | SCBA, chemical-resistant suit, face shield |

### Step 8 — Document the PHA Worksheet & Follow-up
- Issue the **PHA Report** and **Action Item Register**.
- Track all recommendations to closure via a **Management of Change (MOC)** system.

## 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example — LPG Bullet Tank Storage

**Scenario:** A petroleum refinery has an LPG bullet tank (capacity = 2000 m³, design pressure = 17.2 bar, design temperature = –10 °C to +50 °C, contains propane/butane mix). Apply PHA.

### Step A — Hazard Identification (H)

| Hazard (H) | Cause (C) | Consequence (K) | Severity (S) | Likelihood (L) | Risk Level (R) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **H1**: LPG vapor cloud | (C1) Gasket failure on flange | (K1) Vapor cloud explosion (VCE) | IV (Catastrophic) | II (Remote) | High |
| **H1**: LPG vapor cloud | (C2) Overfilling | (K2) Pressure relief → vapor cloud | III (Critical) | III (Occasional) | High |
| **H1**: LPG vapor cloud | (C3) Road tanker collision (external impact) | (K1) VCE | IV (Catastrophic) | I (Improbable) | Medium |
| **H2**: Liquid LPG release (cold burn) | (C4) Bottom drain valve passing | (K3) Cryogenic burn to operator | II (Marginal) | III (Occasional) | Medium |
| **H2**: Liquid LPG release | (C5) Pipe rupture from corrosion | (K4) Pool fire | IV (Catastrophic) | II (Remote) | High |
| **H3**: Internal overpressure | (C6) Blocked outlet, pump dead-head | (K5) Catastrophic vessel rupture (BLEVE) | IV (Catastrophic) | I (Improbable) | Medium |
| **H4**: Toxic combustion products (CO, SO₂ if present) | (C7) Pool fire near populated area | (K6) Smoke inhalation, evacuation | III (Critical) | II (Remote) | Medium |
| **H5**: Environmental release | (C8) Bund wall failure during leak | (K7) Soil/groundwater contamination | III (Critical) | II (Remote) | Medium |

### Step B — Recommendation Development (R)

| Hazard | Recommendation | Type | Owner |
|--------|----------------|------|-------|
| H1-C1 | Install **kammprofile gaskets** with leak detection at all flanges | Engineering | Maintenance |
| H1-C2 | Install **independent high-high level switch (SIL 2)** with ESD shutdown | Engineering (SIS) | Instrumentation |
| H1-C3 | Install **vehicle impact barrier (crash wall)** around tank | Engineering (ISD) | Civil / Project |
| H2-C4 | Provide **double block & bleed** on drain valves | Engineering (ISD) | Design |
| H2-C5 | Implement **corrosion under insulation (CUI) inspection program** every 3 years | Administrative + Inspection | Inspection |
| H3-C6 | Install **100% capacity full-bore relief valve** with secondary rupture disc | Engineering (PSV) | Process |
| H4-C7 | Provide **gas detection (IR/LEL)** with automatic water spray deluge | Engineering (Active protection) | Safety / Fire |
| H5-C8 | Design **bund capacity = 110% of largest tank volume** with impervious lining | Engineering (ISD) | Civil |

### Step C — Residual Risk Re-evaluation

After all recommendations are implemented, each hazard is **re-scored**:

$$
R_{\text{residual}} = f\!\left(S,\; \dfrac{L_{\text{initial}}}{\prod_{k}\text{RRF}_k}\right)
$$

For H1-C1: Initial L = II (Remote), Apply three RRF = 10 each → New L = I (Improbable) ⇒ Residual Risk = **Medium → Low**

For H3-C6: Initial L = I (Improbable), Even with relief valve, S remains IV, so residual = **Medium (acceptable with documented basis)**.

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that **severity rarely changes** with engineered safeguards in PHA. Only **inherently safer design** (e.g., reducing inventory, lower pressure) reduces severity. This is a key PHA philosophy tested in KTU exams.

## 3.3 PHA vs HAZOP vs FMEA — Analytical Comparison

| Parameter | PHA | HAZOP | FMEA / FMECA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stage of application** | Conceptual / preliminary | Detailed design (P&ID complete) | Equipment-level |
| **Type of analysis** | Inductive (forward) | Inductive (guideword based) | Inductive (component failure) |
| **Focus** | Hazard identification | Process deviations | Equipment failure modes |
| **Output** | Risk-ranked hazard list with recommendations | Deviations, causes, consequences, safeguards | Failure modes, RPN |
| **Depth of analysis** | Low–medium | High | Equipment-level |
| **Quantitative?** | No (qualitative) | No (qualitative) | Mostly qualitative; FMECA can be semi-quantitative |
| **Time required** | 2–5 days | 1–3 weeks | 1–2 weeks |
| **Reference Standard** | IEC 61882, CCPS | IEC 61882, OSHA | IEC 60812, MIL-STD-1629 |
| **Typical deliverable** | PHA worksheet, action register | HAZOP report with nodes | FMEA worksheet with RPN ranking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When a question asks *"Differentiate PHA from HAZOP"*, the most-credited answer emphasizes:
> - **PHA = early, broad, qualitative, block-level**
> - **HAZOP = late, detailed, P&ID-level, deviation-focused using guidewords (NO/NOT, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN)**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PHA Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Step 1: Define Scope and Boundaries] --> nodeB[Step 2: Form Multi-Disciplinary Team]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 3: Gather PFD MSDS and Material Data]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 4: Divide Plant into Process Nodes]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step 5: Brainstorm Hazards Causes and Consequences]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Step 6: Assign Severity and Likelihood Ratings]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Step 7: Apply Risk Matrix to Get Risk Level]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Is Risk Acceptable?}
    nodeH -- No --> nodeI[Step 8a: Develop Recommendations]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Step 8b: Apply ISD and Engineered Safeguards]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Step 8c: Recalculate Residual Risk]
    nodeK --> nodeH
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeL[Step 9: Document PHA Report]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Step 10: Issue Action Items via MOC System]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Step 11: Track to Closure and Audit]
```

## 4.2 Risk Matrix Schematic (4 × 4)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph matrix4x4["PHA Risk Matrix 4x4"]
        direction TB
        cell11["S IV / L I - Medium"]:::med --> cell12["S IV / L II - High"]:::high
        cell13["S IV / L III - Very High"]:::vh
        cell14["S IV / L IV - Very High"]:::vh
        cell21["S III / L I - Low"]:::low
        cell22["S III / L II - Medium"]:::med
        cell23["S III / L III - High"]:::high
        cell24["S III / L IV - Very High"]:::vh
        cell31["S II / L I - Low"]:::low
        cell32["S II / L II - Low"]:::low
        cell33["S II / L III - Medium"]:::med
        cell34["S II / L IV - High"]:::high
        cell41["S I / L I - Low"]:::low
        cell42["S I / L II - Low"]:::low
        cell43["S I / L III - Low"]:::low
        cell44["S I / L IV - Medium"]:::med
    end
    classDef low fill:#d4f8d4,stroke:#1f7a1f,color:#000
    classDef med fill:#fff2b3,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000
    classDef high fill:#ffd1a4,stroke:#cc5500,color:#000
    classDef vh fill:#ff9b9b,stroke:#8b0000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 PHA Team Composition & Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    leader["PHA Team Leader and Independent Facilitator"] --> core
    subgraph core["PHA Core Team"]
        pe["Process Engineer"]
        mech["Mechanical and Rotating Engineer"]
        inst["Instrumentation and Control Engineer"]
        ops["Operations and Shift Supervisor"]
        maint["Maintenance Engineer"]
        safety["HSE and Process Safety Engineer"]
    end
    leader --> support
    subgraph support["Advisory Specialists"]
        proj["Project and Design Engineer"]
        env["Environmental Engineer"]
        elec["Electrical and Fire Protection Engineer"]
    end
    core --> ws["PHA Workshop Sessions 2 to 5 Days"]
    support --> ws
    ws --> doc["PHA Report and Action Register"]
    doc --> moc["Management of Change MOC System"]
    moc --> closure["Recommendation Closure and Verification"]
```

## 4.4 PHA Position in the Safety Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Conceptual Design] --> B[Preliminary Hazard Analysis PHA]
    B --> C[Process Flow Diagram PFD Finalization]
    C --> D[P and ID Development]
    D --> E[HAZOP Study]
    E --> F[SIL Selection and LOPA]
    F --> G[Quantitative Risk Assessment QRA]
    G --> H[Construction and Commissioning]
    H --> I[Operation and Periodic PHA Re validation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> PHA is **the first formal hazard study** in the safety lifecycle. It feeds input to HAZOP, LOPA, and QRA. It must be **re-validated** every 5 years or whenever a **Management of Change (MOC)** is triggered during the plant's operating life.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECHT743]**
**Define Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA). State any two of its objectives.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** is a qualitative hazard evaluation technique performed during the early / conceptual design stage of a process plant to systematically identify potential hazards, their causes, and consequences; and to rank them using a severity–likelihood risk matrix so that appropriate risk reduction measures can be recommended **before detailed engineering is finalized**.

**Two objectives of PHA:**
1. To identify hazards at the earliest stage of design, when they are cheapest and easiest to eliminate.
2. To prioritize risks through severity–likelihood ranking, thereby directing detailed studies (HAZOP, LOPA, QRA) to the high-risk areas of the plant.
*(Acceptable alternatives: regulatory compliance, inherently safer design input, documentation of baseline safety.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PECHT743]**
**List the four standard severity categories used in a PHA risk matrix and give one example consequence for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No | Severity Category | Example Consequence |
|------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **I — Negligible** | No injury, minor property loss |
| 2 | **II — Marginal** | First-aid injury / minor toxic exposure requiring medical attention |
| 3 | **III — Critical** | Severe injury / hospitalization, significant production loss, local environmental impact |
| 4 | **IV — Catastrophic** | Single or multiple fatalities, complete equipment loss, offsite environmental impact |

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / June 2024 Model Paper, PECHT743]**
**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step methodology** of conducting a Preliminary Hazard Analysis for a process plant. Mention the **composition of the PHA team** and the **types of information** required before the workshop. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate clearly between **PHA, HAZOP, and FMEA** with respect to: stage of application, type of analysis, level of detail, output deliverable, and reference standard. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A — Part (a)

**1. Composition of the PHA Team (2 marks):**
A typical PHA team consists of 5–8 members led by an **independent PHA facilitator** (not directly responsible for the design). Members include:
- Process Engineer
- Mechanical / Rotating Equipment Engineer
- Instrumentation & Control Engineer
- Operations / Shift Supervisor
- Maintenance Engineer
- HSE / Process Safety Engineer
- Project / Design Engineer (advisory)

**2. Pre-Workshop Information Required (2 marks):**
- Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs)
- Preliminary P&IDs
- Heat & Material Balance
- Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS)
- Equipment data sheets and design basis
- Plot plan and site meteorology
- Previous incident / near-miss history

**3. Step-by-Step PHA Methodology (3 marks):**

| Step | Activity |
|------|----------|
| 1 | Define the physical, process, and lifecycle boundaries of the study. |
| 2 | Form the multi-disciplinary PHA team and appoint an independent facilitator. |
| 3 | Gather PFD, MSDS, balance, and equipment data. |
| 4 | Divide the plant into manageable **process nodes** (e.g., feed, reaction, separation, storage, utility). |
| 5 | For each node, brainstorm **hazards, causes, and consequences** in structured sessions. |
| 6 | Assign **severity (I–IV)** and **likelihood (I–IV)** ratings to each cause–consequence pair. |
| 7 | Plot the rating on a **4 × 4 risk matrix** to obtain the **risk level** (Low/Medium/High/Very High). |
| 8 | For risks that are Medium or higher, develop **recommendations** (ISD → Engineering → Administrative → PPE). |
| 9 | Re-evaluate the **residual risk** after recommended safeguards. |
| 10 | Document the **PHA worksheet**, **action register**, and route through the **Management of Change (MOC)** system. |

---

#### Model Solution for Question A — Part (b)

| Parameter | PHA | HAZOP | FMEA |
|-----------|-----|-------|------|
| **Stage of application** | Conceptual / preliminary design | Detailed design (P&ID frozen) | Equipment-level / procurement |
| **Type of analysis** | Inductive (forward, brainstorming) | Inductive (guideword-based: NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN) | Inductive (single-component failure) |
| **Level of detail** | Low–medium (block / node level) | High (every line, every instrument) | High (component-level) |
| **Output deliverable** | Risk-ranked hazard list + recommendations | HAZOP worksheet with deviations, causes, consequences, safeguards | FMEA worksheet with RPN (S × O × D) ranking |
| **Reference standard** | IEC 61882, CCPS Guidelines | IEC 61882, OSHA 1910.119 | IEC 60812, MIL-STD-1629, SAE J1739 |
| **Quantitative?** | No (purely qualitative) | No (qualitative) | Mostly qualitative; FMECA semi-quantitative |
| **Time required** | 2–5 days per unit | 1–3 weeks per unit | 1–2 weeks per equipment |
| **Focus** | Broad hazard identification | Process deviations at nodes | Equipment failure modes |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper, PECHT743]**
**(a)** With the help of a **4 × 4 risk matrix**, explain how **severity** and **likelihood** categories are combined in PHA to arrive at a **risk level**. Illustrate with one example from a **hydrocarbon storage facility**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What are the **limitations of PHA**? How does it differ from a **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** in terms of methodology, data, and output? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B — Part (a)

**1. Severity Categories (I to IV) — (1.5 marks):**
- **I — Negligible:** No injury / no impact
- **II — Marginal:** First-aid injury / minor loss
- **III — Critical:** Severe injury / significant loss
- **IV — Catastrophic:** Fatality / major loss

**2. Likelihood Categories (I to IV) — (1.5 marks):**
- **I — Improbable:** So unlikely that its occurrence is assumed negligible
- **II — Remote:** Unlikely but possible
- **III — Occasional:** Likely to occur sometime in plant life
- **IV — Frequent:** Likely to occur repeatedly

**3. Risk Matrix Combination — 4 × 4 (2 marks):**

| Sev \ Lik | I (Improbable) | II (Remote) | III (Occasional) | IV (Frequent) |
|-----------|---|---|---|---|
| **IV (Catastrophic)** | Medium | High | Very High | Very High |
| **III (Critical)** | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| **II (Marginal)** | Low | Low | Medium | High |
| **I (Negligible)** | Low | Low | Low | Medium |

**4. Worked Example — Hydrocarbon Storage Facility (2 marks):**

*Scenario:* A 5000 m³ atmospheric fixed-roof crude oil storage tank.
- **Hazard:** Crude oil pool fire
- **Cause:** Lightning strike during thunderstorm
- **Consequence:** Full surface pool fire, dome roof collapse, ₹ 10 Crore loss
- **Severity** = IV (Catastrophic)
- **Likelihood** = II (Remote, with proper earthing and flame arrestors)
- **Risk Level** = **High**

*Recommendations:*
1. Install lightning protection with earthing resistance < 10 Ω (Engineering).
2. Fit flame arrestors on vent lines (Engineering).
3. Install foam pourer system on the rim seal (Active protection).
4. Conduct monthly bond continuity checks (Administrative).
*Residual likelihood after safeguards:* I (Improbable) → Residual Risk = **Medium** (acceptable with ALARP justification).

---

#### Model Solution for Question B — Part (b)

**1. Limitations of PHA — (4 marks):**

1. **Qualitative only** — Does not give numerical risk; cannot be used directly for land-use planning or insurance.
2. **Dependent on team experience** — Quality varies with facilitator and team expertise; new engineers may miss rare hazards.
3. **Limited detail at block level** — Cannot analyse complex process deviations that require P&ID-level scrutiny (HAZOP is needed).
4. **No failure rate data used** — Cannot prioritize by actual frequency, only by qualitative judgment.
5. **Static snapshot** — May become outdated; must be re-validated on MOC and at periodic intervals.
6. **Subjective scoring** — Severity and likelihood ratings can differ between teams and individuals.
7. **Does not address human reliability or procedure quality** in depth.
8. **Does not generate safety integrity level (SIL) requirements** — requires LOPA/QRA follow-up.

**2. PHA vs QRA — Comparison (3 marks):**

| Parameter | PHA | QRA |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Nature** | Qualitative / semi-quantitative | Fully quantitative |
| **Input data** | PFD, MSDS, team experience | Failure rate databases (e.g., OREDA, CCPS PERD), weather, population |
| **Output** | Risk-ranked list, action register | Individual risk contours (IRPA), societal risk (F-N curves), LOC |
| **Time and cost** | Days, low cost | Months, high cost |
| **Use** | Early design, hazard screening | Land-use planning, ALARP demonstration, regulatory consent |
| **Reference** | IEC 61882, CCPS | CCPS QRA Guidelines, TNO Green Book, UK HSE R2P2 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert — PHA Questions:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Writing the *definition* of HAZOP guidewords in a PHA answer — this fetches zero marks. PHA is brainstorming-based; HAZOP is guideword-based.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Quoting only **3 severity** categories instead of **4**. The KTU 2024 scheme expects the full I–II–III–IV (or I–IV in some texts) classification.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing PHA with **What-If Analysis**. PHA is a structured worksheet, What-If is an open-ended question-based review.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting to mention the **independent facilitator** — a non-negotiable requirement as per IEC 61882.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Omitting **MOC and PHA revalidation** in the conclusion — examiners specifically test this in 14-mark answers.
> 6. **Common Mistake #6:** Stating that PHA gives **SIL recommendations** — this is a **LOPA** function, not PHA. Deduct 1 mark if claimed.

---

## 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — KTU PECHT743 Module 3 / PHA**

- **Definition:** PHA = **Qualitative, inductive, early-stage hazard study**; first formal step in the safety lifecycle.
- **Standards:** **IEC 61882**, **CCPS Hazard Evaluation Guidelines (3rd Ed.)**, **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM)**.
- **When?** After PFD, before P&ID is frozen.
- **Team size:** 5–8 members + **independent facilitator**.
- **Severity scale:** I (Negligible) → II (Marginal) → III (Critical) → IV (Catastrophic).
- **Likelihood scale:** I (Improbable) → II (Remote) → III (Occasional) → IV (Frequent).
- **Risk Matrix:** 4 × 4 → outputs **Low, Medium, High, Very High**.
- **Hazard categories to remember:** Toxic, Fire, Explosion (VCE, BLEVE, dust), Mechanical, Electrical, Thermal, Reactivity, Ergonomic, Environmental, Natural.
- **Recommendation hierarchy (in order of preference):**
  1. **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** — minimize, substitute, moderate, simplify
  2. **Engineering Controls** — relief valves, trips, interlocks, fire protection
  3. **Administrative Controls** — SOPs, permits, training
  4. **PPE** — last line of defense
- **Residual Risk:** $R_{\text{residual}} = f\!\left(S,\; L_{\text{initial}} / \prod \text{RRF}_k\right)$.
- **PHA vs HAZOP:** PHA = block-level brainstorming, **no guidewords**; HAZOP = P&ID-level, **uses guidewords** (NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN).
- **PHA vs FMEA:** PHA = process/systems view; FMEA = component-failure view with **RPN** (Risk Priority Number).
- **PHA vs QRA:** PHA = qualitative; QRA = quantitative with **IRPA** and **F-N curves**.
- **Position in lifecycle:** PHA → HAZOP → LOPA → SIL → QRA → Operation.
- **PHA revalidation triggers:** Every **5 years**, after any **MOC**, after a major incident, or on regulatory demand.
- **Deliverables:** PHA Report, PHA Worksheet (hazard, cause, consequence, S, L, R, recommendation), Action Item Register.
- **Mnemonic for the 8 PHA steps:** **D**efine → **T**eam → **I**nformation → **N**odes → **H**azard brain-storm → **S**core → **R**ecommend → **D**ocument.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: What-If Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** What-If Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**What-If Analysis (WIA)** is a structured, team-based, qualitative hazard identification technique used in process safety engineering to proactively identify potential deviations, hazardous scenarios, and operational problems that could arise in a process plant, system, or procedure. It is formally defined as a *brainstorming-based safety review method* in which a multidisciplinary team systematically raises questions of the form *"What if...?"* concerning deviations from intended design, operation, or maintenance conditions.

According to the **Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS)** guidelines and the **AIChE** standards adopted in the KTU 2024 scheme, What-If Analysis is positioned as a **lower-tier, early-stage hazard evaluation tool** that precedes more rigorous methods such as **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** and **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:**
> *What-If Analysis is a systematic, scenario-based hazard identification technique in which an interdisciplinary team reviews a process flow or procedure by asking structured "What if..." questions to uncover potential accidents, deviations, and operability problems, and recommends safety improvements to mitigate the identified risks.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning a **road trip from Kochi to Delhi** with your family. Before starting, you sit down with co-travelers and brainstorm possible problems:

- *"What if the car breaks down on a highway?"*
- *"What if it rains heavily in the Western Ghats?"*
- *"What if one of us falls sick in a remote area?"*
- *"What if we run out of fuel between two cities?"*

For each question, you then decide on a **precautionary action** (carry a spare tire, keep a first-aid kit, maintain fuel buffers, etc.). This mental exercise is **What-If Analysis** applied to road safety.

In a chemical plant, instead of asking about road hazards, engineers ask:

- *"What if the reactor temperature exceeds 200 °C?"*
- *"What if the cooling water pump fails?"*
- *"What if an operator opens the wrong valve during startup?"*

For each scenario, the team proposes **safeguards, alarms, or procedural changes** to prevent or mitigate the hazard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** What-If Analysis is essentially a *structured daydreaming exercise* — turning "imagine something goes wrong" into a documented engineering deliverable. It is the human brain's natural risk anticipation mechanism, formalized for industrial use.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology

- **Team Size:** Typically **3 to 7 members** (CCPS recommendation).
- **Review Duration:** Usually **2 to 8 hours** depending on plant complexity.
- **Question Format:** Begins with the stem *"What if..."* followed by a deviation.
- **Documentation:** Every question and its answer/consequence/recommendation is recorded in a structured **What-If Worksheet**.
- **Iteration:** Multiple passes (design stage, operational stage, modification stage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is WIA important in the KTU 2024 scheme?**
> WIA is a high-frequency topic in KTU examinations because it bridges the gap between *conceptual safety philosophy* and *quantitative risk analysis*. Questions typically appear in Part A (definitions, comparison with HAZOP) and Part B (procedural conduct of WIA, worksheets, case studies).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The What-If Methodology

The What-If Analysis is governed by a **5-stage structured protocol**:

1. **Stage 1 — Define Scope and Boundaries**
   - Select the process unit, system, or procedure to be reviewed.
   - Gather relevant documentation: P&IDs (Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams), PFDs (Process Flow Diagrams), operating procedures, equipment specifications.
   - Establish the boundaries (e.g., battery limits of a reactor system).

2. **Stage 2 — Assemble the Multidisciplinary Team**
   - **Process Engineer** (lead)
   - **Operations/Plant Operator**
   - **Safety Engineer**
   - **Maintenance/Instrumentation Engineer**
   - Optionally: **Environmental Specialist**, **Project Manager**

3. **Stage 3 — Generate "What If..." Questions**
   - Use brainstorming, checklists, and historical incident databases.
   - Questions cover equipment failures, human errors, external events (floods, lightning), utility failures, and chemical deviations.

4. **Stage 4 — Analyze Consequences and Existing Safeguards**
   - For each question, the team identifies:
     - **Hazardous consequences** (fire, explosion, toxic release, environmental damage)
     - **Existing safeguards** (relief valves, alarms, interlocks, PPE)
     - **Risk categorization** (low, medium, high)

5. **Stage 5 — Document Recommendations and Follow-Up**
   - Propose engineering, administrative, or procedural improvements.
   - Assign responsibility and timelines.
   - Track recommendations to closure.

### 2.2 Categorization of What-If Questions

| Category | Typical Question Stem | Example | Engineering Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Equipment Failure** | What if [pump/compressor/valve] fails? | What if the feed pump P-101 fails? | Mechanical |
| **Utility Failure** | What if [cooling water/electricity/instrument air] is lost? | What if instrument air fails? | Instrumentation |
| **Human Error** | What if the operator [action]? | What if the operator skips pre-startup checks? | Operations |
| **Process Deviation** | What if [temperature/pressure/flow] goes high/low? | What if reactor temperature exceeds 180 °C? | Process |
| **External Events** | What if [natural disaster/external impact] occurs? | What if a lightning strike hits the storage tank? | Environmental |
| **Chemical Deviation** | What if the wrong chemical is charged? | What if sulfuric acid is loaded instead of HCl? | Chemical |
| **Maintenance Error** | What if maintenance is done incorrectly? | What if a bypass line is left open after maintenance? | Maintenance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike HAZOP, which uses rigid *guide words* (NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN), What-If Analysis is **open-ended and flexible**, relying on the team's expertise and creativity.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **There are no mathematical formulas for What-If Analysis** because it is a purely **qualitative** hazard identification technique. However, the following structured **risk prioritization metric** is used during the consequence evaluation stage and frequently appears in KTU numerical/conceptual questions.

| Metric | Symbol | Formula/Definition | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Risk Score | $R$ | $R = S \times L$ | dimensionless | Qualitative risk ranking |
| Severity | $S$ | 1 to 5 scale (1=negligible, 5=catastrophic) | rating | Consequence magnitude |
| Likelihood | $L$ | 1 to 5 scale (1=rare, 5=frequent) | rating | Probability of occurrence |
| Risk Priority Number | RPN | Same as $R$ above | index | Used for ranking scenarios |
| Residual Risk | $R_{res}$ | $R_{res} = R_{initial} - R_{mitigation}$ | dimensionless | Risk after safeguards |
| Mitigation Factor | $k$ | $0 \lt k \le 1$ | dimensionless | Effectiveness of safeguards |

**Mathematical Representation of Risk Reduction:**

$$R_{residual} = S \times L \times (1 - k)$$

where:
- $S$ = Severity of the worst credible consequence
- $L$ = Likelihood of the deviation occurring
- $k$ = Effectiveness factor of the proposed safeguard ($0 \le k \le 1$)
- $R_{residual}$ = Remaining risk after safeguard implementation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the units. The risk score is **dimensionless** because it is a product of two rating integers. Do **not** assign units of "per year" or "per hour" unless $L$ is converted into an actual frequency.

### 2.4 Comparison: What-If Analysis vs. HAZOP (High-Yield for KTU)

| Parameter | What-If Analysis | HAZOP Study |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Open-ended, brainstorming | Structured, guide-word based |
| **Team Size** | 3–7 members | 5–8 members |
| **Duration** | 2–8 hours | Days to weeks |
| **Stage of Project** | Early/conceptual design | Detailed design/operation |
| **Documentation** | What-If worksheet | HAZOP nodes & deviations table |
| **Quantification** | Usually qualitative | Often semi-quantitative |
| **Guide Words** | None | NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **Expertise Level Required** | Moderate | High |
| **Regulatory Acceptance** | Limited | Strong (mandatory in many jurisdictions) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

What-If Analysis is widely used in:

- **Pharmaceutical industry** for batch process reviews.
- **Oil & Gas** for offshore platform safety reviews.
- **Nuclear industry** as a preliminary screening before full Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA).
- **Petrochemicals** for modification reviews (Management of Change - MOC).
- **Food processing** for hygiene and contamination scenarios.
- **KTU Project Work** for final-year B.Tech safety case studies.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Procedure & Worked Example

### 3.1 Detailed Step-by-Step Conduct of a What-If Analysis

Let us illustrate the **complete procedural workflow** using a worked example of a **reactor cooling system**.

**System Description:** A jacketed continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) operating at 150 °C and 5 bar, used for an exothermic reaction. Cooling water flows through the jacket at 30 °C inlet.

---

#### **STEP 1: Define Scope and Assemble the Team**

- **Scope:** Reactor R-101 and its jacket cooling subsystem.
- **Boundaries:** From feed valve FV-101 to product valve PV-105.
- **Team:** Process engineer (lead), plant operator, safety engineer, instrument engineer.

---

#### **STEP 2: Generate "What-If" Questions**

The team conducts a brainstorming session and produces the following questions:

| Q. No. | What-If Question | Triggering Concern |
|---|---|---|
| Q1 | What if the cooling water pump P-102 fails? | Loss of cooling |
| Q2 | What if the reactor temperature transmitter TT-101 gives a false-low reading? | Sensor failure |
| Q3 | What if the operator charges the wrong reactant? | Human error |
| Q4 | What if the cooling water temperature rises to 45 °C in summer? | Utility deviation |
| Q5 | What if the relief valve RV-101 is stuck closed? | Mechanical failure |
| Q6 | What if instrument air supply is lost? | Utility failure |
| Q7 | What if a lightning strike hits the reactor? | External event |

---

#### **STEP 3: Analyze Consequences and Existing Safeguards**

For each question, the team performs consequence analysis:

| Q. No. | Possible Consequences | Existing Safeguards | Risk Score $R = S \times L$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Q1 | Runaway reaction, vessel overpressure, rupture | TT alarm at 160 °C, interlock trips feed valve | $S=5, L=2 \Rightarrow R=10$ (Medium-High) |
| Q2 | Cooling water flow drops, undetected overheating | High-temperature interlock at 180 °C | $S=5, L=2 \Rightarrow R=10$ |
| Q3 | Wrong stoichiometry, side reaction, possible explosion | Operator checklist, mass-flow controllers | $S=4, L=3 \Rightarrow R=12$ (High) |
| Q4 | Reduced heat removal, slow temperature rise | Cooling tower capacity margin (10%) | $S=3, L=4 \Rightarrow R=12$ |
| Q5 | Overpressure if blocked relief | Pressure transmitter PT-101 with alarm | $S=5, L=1 \Rightarrow R=5$ (Low-Medium) |
| Q6 | All pneumatic control valves fail open/closed | Manual block valves, backup nitrogen | $S=4, L=2 \Rightarrow R=8$ |
| Q7 | Fire/explosion if flammable vapor released | Lightning arrestors, bonding/grounding | $S=5, L=1 \Rightarrow R=5$ |

---

#### **STEP 4: Calculate Residual Risk After Proposed Safeguards**

Let us demonstrate the **residual risk calculation** for Q1 using the formula:

$$R_{residual} = S \times L \times (1 - k)$$

**Given:**
- Initial severity $S = 5$
- Initial likelihood $L = 2$
- Initial risk $R_{initial} = 5 \times 2 = 10$
- Proposed safeguard: install a **redundant cooling water pump** with automatic switchover. Effectiveness factor $k = 0.8$ (i.e., reduces likelihood by 80%).

**Step-by-Step Evaluation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{initial} &= S \times L \\
&= 5 \times 2 \\
&= 10 \quad \text{(High priority)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{residual} &= S \times L \times (1 - k) \\
&= 5 \times 2 \times (1 - 0.8) \\
&= 10 \times 0.2 \\
&= 2 \quad \text{(Low priority - acceptable)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** After installing the redundant pump, the residual risk drops from **10 (medium-high)** to **2 (low)**, which falls within the KTU/CCPS acceptable risk threshold (typically $R \le 4$).

---

#### **STEP 5: Document Recommendations**

| Q. No. | Recommendation | Responsible | Timeline |
|---|---|---|---|
| Q1 | Install redundant cooling pump with auto-switchover | Mechanical Engineer | 3 months |
| Q2 | Add a second independent temperature sensor | Instrumentation Engineer | 1 month |
| Q3 | Implement barcode-based reactant verification | Process Engineer | 6 months |
| Q4 | Install chilled water backup line | Project Manager | 9 months |
| Q5 | Test relief valve every 6 months | Maintenance Lead | Ongoing |
| Q6 | Install nitrogen backup for critical valves | Instrumentation Engineer | 4 months |
| Q7 | Annual lightning protection audit | Electrical Engineer | Annual |

---

#### **STEP 6: Risk Acceptance Matrix (Industry Standard)**

| | L=1 (Rare) | L=2 (Unlikely) | L=3 (Possible) | L=4 (Likely) | L=5 (Frequent) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S=5 (Catastrophic)** | Medium | High | High | Extreme | Extreme |
| **S=4 (Major)** | Low | Medium | High | High | Extreme |
| **S=3 (Moderate)** | Low | Low | Medium | High | High |
| **S=2 (Minor)** | Negligible | Low | Low | Medium | High |
| **S=1 (Negligible)** | Negligible | Negligible | Low | Low | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The risk acceptance matrix is the qualitative equivalent of a **quantitative risk matrix** used in ISO 31000 and CCPS guidelines. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can correctly place a scenario on this matrix.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flow Diagram: What-If Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Define Scope] --> nodeB[Gather Documentation P and ID, PFD, SOPs]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Assemble Multidisciplinary Team 3 to 7 members]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Conduct Brainstorming Session]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Generate What If Questions]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Identify Consequences]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Review Existing Safeguards]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Calculate Risk Score R equals S times L]
    nodeH --> nodeI{Is Risk Acceptable?}
    nodeI -- No --> nodeJ[Propose Recommendations]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Calculate Residual Risk]
    nodeK --> nodeL{Is Residual Risk Acceptable?}
    nodeL -- No --> nodeJ
    nodeL -- Yes --> nodeM[Document Final Worksheet]
    nodeI -- Yes --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN[Assign Responsibility and Timeline]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Track to Closure]
    nodeO --> nodeP[End: Periodic Re review]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Inputs, Process, and Outputs of WIA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Inputs P and IDs, Operating Procedures, Past Incident Data] --> nodeB[What If Analysis Team]
    nodeC[Industry Checklists] --> nodeB
    nodeD[Regulatory Standards] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeE[Brainstorming and Question Generation]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Consequence Analysis]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Risk Evaluation R equals S times L]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Outputs What If Worksheet, Recommendations, Action Items]
```

### 4.3 Comparison Diagram: What-If vs. HAZOP Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Safety Review Need Identified] --> nodeB{Plant Complexity and Stage}
    nodeB -- Conceptual or Early Design --> nodeC[What If Analysis Low Cost, Fast]
    nodeB -- Detailed Design or Operation --> nodeD[HAZOP Study High Cost, Rigorous]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Open Ended Questions No Guide Words]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Guide Words NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE etc]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Worksheet Documentation]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Node by Node HAZOP Table]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Final Recommendations]
    nodeH --> nodeI
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Integration of WIA in Plant Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Project Conceptualization] --> nodeB[What If Analysis Early Screening]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Process Design and Engineering]
    nodeC --> nodeD[HAZOP Detailed Review]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Construction and Commissioning]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Pre Startup What If Check]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Plant Operation]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Management of Change What If Re review]
```
