```mermaid
    nodeH --> nodeI[Periodic What If Audits]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The diagram illustrates that What-If Analysis is **not a one-time activity**. It is performed at multiple stages of the plant lifecycle — conceptual, pre-startup, modification, and periodic audit. This recurring nature is a frequent KTU exam question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define What-If Analysis. Mention any two limitations of this technique.**

**Model Answer:**

What-If Analysis is a structured, qualitative hazard identification technique in which a multidisciplinary team reviews a process or system by asking open-ended *"What if..."* questions to identify potential deviations, hazardous scenarios, and operability problems, and recommends corrective actions.

**Two Limitations:**
1. **Highly dependent on team expertise and creativity** — inexperienced teams may miss critical scenarios because there are no rigid guide words (unlike HAZOP).
2. **Limited regulatory acceptance** — many international standards (e.g., OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119) require more rigorous methods like HAZOP for hazard analysis of highly hazardous chemicals.
3. *(Third optional limitation)* It provides only qualitative risk ranking; it does not generate quantitative failure probabilities.

*(Any two well-explained points carry 1.5 marks each.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between What-If Analysis and HAZOP study (any four points).**

**Model Answer:**

| S. No. | What-If Analysis | HAZOP Study |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Open-ended, brainstorming-based; no fixed guide words. | Uses fixed guide words: NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN. |
| 2 | Usually conducted at conceptual/early design stage. | Conducted at detailed design and operational stages. |
| 3 | Team of 3–7 members; duration 2–8 hours. | Team of 5–8 members; duration days to weeks. |
| 4 | Lower cost; lower regulatory acceptance. | Higher cost; strong regulatory acceptance (OSHA, EU Seveso). |

*(Any four points, 0.75 marks each = 3 marks total.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure** for conducting a What-If Analysis in a process plant. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A reactor system has a **severity rating of 4** and an **initial likelihood rating of 5**. A proposed safeguard is expected to reduce the likelihood by **60%**. Calculate the **initial risk score** and the **residual risk score**, and comment on whether the residual risk is acceptable if the company policy threshold is $R \le 6$. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

The step-by-step procedure for conducting a What-If Analysis is as follows:

**Step 1: Define Scope and Boundaries**
The study leader identifies the process unit, system, or operating procedure to be reviewed. Relevant documents such as P&IDs, PFDs, operating manuals, and equipment datasheets are collected. The physical and operational boundaries of the review are clearly stated (e.g., from feed valve to product outlet of a reactor skid).

**Step 2: Assemble the Multidisciplinary Team**
A team of 3–7 experts is formed, typically including a process engineer (team leader), plant operator, safety engineer, instrumentation engineer, and optionally a maintenance engineer and environmental specialist.

**Step 3: Conduct Brainstorming and Generate Questions**
The team reviews each section of the process and raises *"What if..."* questions covering equipment failure, utility failure, human error, process deviation, external events, and chemical deviations. Checklists and past incident databases are consulted.

**Step 4: Identify Consequences and Existing Safeguards**
For each question, the team identifies hazardous consequences, lists existing safeguards (alarms, interlocks, relief devices, procedures), and assigns a qualitative risk rating using $R = S \times L$.

**Step 5: Develop Recommendations**
For risks exceeding the acceptable threshold, the team proposes engineering (e.g., redundant equipment), administrative (e.g., training), and procedural (e.g., SOP revision) recommendations.

**Step 6: Documentation and Follow-Up**
All questions, answers, risk ratings, and recommendations are compiled in a **What-If Worksheet**. Responsibilities and timelines are assigned, and recommendations are tracked to closure. Periodic re-reviews are scheduled.

*(Each step carries 1 mark; complete explanation = 7 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:**
- Severity $S = 4$
- Initial likelihood $L_{initial} = 5$
- Mitigation effectiveness $k = 60\% = 0.60$
- Acceptable risk threshold $R_{acceptable} \le 6$

**Step 1: Calculate Initial Risk Score**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{initial} &= S \times L_{initial} \\
&= 4 \times 5 \\
&= 20
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Reduced Likelihood**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{reduced} &= L_{initial} \times (1 - k) \\
&= 5 \times (1 - 0.60) \\
&= 5 \times 0.40 \\
&= 2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Residual Risk Score**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{residual} &= S \times L_{reduced} \\
&= 4 \times 2 \\
&= 8
\end{aligned}$$

Alternatively, using the combined formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{residual} &= S \times L_{initial} \times (1 - k) \\
&= 4 \times 5 \times 0.40 \\
&= 8
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Decision**

- $R_{initial} = 20$ (Extreme priority — not acceptable)
- $R_{residual} = 8$ (High priority)
- Threshold $R_{acceptable} = 6$

Since $R_{residual} = 8 > 6$, the residual risk **is NOT acceptable** under the company policy. Further mitigation is required, e.g., by adding an additional safeguard to reduce likelihood below 1.5 or reducing severity through inherent safety design.

*(Correct formulas = 2 marks; correct substitution = 2 marks; final values = 2 marks; interpretation = 1 mark.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks - Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the **role and importance of What-If Analysis** in the lifecycle of a process plant. List the various stages at which it is applied. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Prepare a **What-If Worksheet** for a **centrifugal pump P-201** handling toluene in a petrochemical plant. List at least **six "What if" questions** with their consequences, existing safeguards, and recommendations. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Role and Importance of What-If Analysis:**

What-If Analysis serves as an **early-warning and screening tool** in the plant lifecycle. Its importance includes:

1. **Early Hazard Identification:** Detects potential accidents during conceptual design, before expensive engineering is committed.
2. **Cost-Effective:** Requires fewer resources than HAZOP, FMEA, or QRA.
3. **Flexibility:** Applicable to any stage — design, construction, commissioning, operation, modification, or decommissioning.
4. **Management of Change (MOC):** Quickly evaluates hazards of plant modifications before implementation.
5. **Training Tool:** Familiarizes new engineers with plant hazards.
6. **Compliance Support:** Contributes to OSHA PSM, ISO 45001, and ISO 14001 audit requirements.

**Stages of Application in Plant Lifecycle:**

1. **Conceptual Design Stage** — early screening of proposed processes.
2. **Detailed Design Stage** — verification of P&ID-level safety provisions.
3. **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** — before commissioning new equipment.
4. **Operational Phase** — periodic safety audits (typically every 3–5 years).
5. **Modification Stage** — Management of Change (MOC) reviews.
6. **Decommissioning Stage** — hazards of dismantling and demolition.

*(Each stage well-explained = 1 mark × 6 = 6 marks; role explanation = 1 mark = 7 marks total.)*

---

**Model Solution to Part (b): What-If Worksheet for Pump P-201 (Toluene Service)**

| Q. No. | What-If Question | Consequences | Existing Safeguards | Recommendation | Risk Score $R=S \times L$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | What if the pump P-201 mechanically seizes? | Loss of flow, downstream process upset, possible dry-running damage | Flow alarm FAL-201, low-flow interlock shutdown | Install vibration monitoring and bearing temperature sensor | $S=3, L=3 \Rightarrow R=9$ |
| 2 | What if the mechanical seal leaks toluene? | Flammable vapor release, fire/explosion hazard | Seal pot with level indicator, vapor recovery system | Install toxic gas detector, daily seal inspection | $S=4, L=3 \Rightarrow R=12$ |
| 3 | What if the suction valve is closed during operation? | Cavitation, pump damage, loss of flow | Suction pressure transmitter PT-201 with low alarm | Add low-suction auto-trip interlock | $S=3, L=2 \Rightarrow R=6$ |
| 4 | What if the discharge block valve is left closed at startup? | Overpressure in pump casing, seal failure | Discharge pressure safety valve PSV-201 set at 10 bar | Verify valve lineup by independent operator (two-person rule) | $S=4, L=2 \Rightarrow R=8$ |
| 5 | What if the operator over-tightens the packing? | Packing overheating, fire ignition of toluene | Temperature indicator on bearing housing, fire detection system | Training on packing adjustment, periodic thermal scan | $S=4, L=2 \Rightarrow R=8$ |
| 6 | What if a power failure occurs during transfer? | Pump stops, line drain-back, spill at suction | Check valve at discharge, emergency power connector | Install UPS-backed control and double block-and-bleed isolation | $S=3, L=2 \Rightarrow R=6$ |
| 7 | What if lightning strikes the pump shed? | Ignition of leaked vapors | Lightning arrestor, flame-proof electrical fittings | Annual lightning protection audit and bonding check | $S=5, L=1 \Rightarrow R=5$ |

*(Six questions well-tabulated = 6 marks; correct format and analysis = 1 mark = 7 marks total.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse WIA with HAZOP** — the absence of guide words is the single biggest differentiator. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for stating WIA uses guide words.
> 2. **Always state the team composition explicitly** — "multidisciplinary team of 3–7 members" is mandatory wording.
> 3. **In numerical questions**, show the formula first, then substitute values. Marks are awarded for *method* even if the arithmetic is wrong.
> 4. **Risk scores are dimensionless** — never write units like "per year."
> 5. **Recommendation must be specific** — "install redundant pump with auto-switchover" gets full marks, but "improve safety" gets zero.
> 6. **Question stems must begin with "What if..."** — examiners expect this exact phrasing, not "What happens if..." or "Suppose..."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition:** What-If Analysis = structured *"What if..."* brainstorming for hazard identification.
- **Team Size:** 3 to 7 multidisciplinary members.
- **Duration:** 2 to 8 hours (relatively fast).
- **Stage:** Conceptual design, pre-startup, MOC, periodic audit.
- **Approach:** Open-ended; NO guide words (unlike HAZOP).
- **Output:** What-If Worksheet (questions, consequences, safeguards, recommendations).
- **Risk Score Formula:** $R = S \times L$, with $S$ and $L$ on 1–5 scales.
- **Residual Risk Formula:** $R_{residual} = S \times L \times (1 - k)$.
- **Typical Severity Categories:** Negligible, Minor, Moderate, Major, Catastrophic.
- **Typical Likelihood Categories:** Rare, Unlikely, Possible, Likely, Frequent.
- **Categories of What-If Questions:** Equipment failure, utility failure, human error, process deviation, external events, chemical deviation, maintenance error.
- **Comparison with HAZOP:** WIA = flexible, cheap, qualitative, early stage; HAZOP = rigid, costly, semi-quantitative, detailed stage.
- **Standards/References:** CCPS Guidelines, AIChE, OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, ISO 31000, ISO 45001.
- **Common Mistake:** Saying WIA uses "guide words" — it does NOT.
- **Limitation:** Highly dependent on team expertise; no built-in structure.
- **Strength:** Highly flexible, applicable to non-process systems (procedures, software, logistics).
- **Integration with Lifecycle:** Used at conceptual design, detailed design, pre-startup, MOC, and decommissioning.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Tree Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fault Tree Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is a **top-down, deductive, graphical failure analysis methodology** in which an *undesired state of a system* (the **Top Event**) is decomposed, using **Boolean logic gates**, into a logical combination of *lower-level basic events* (component failures, human errors, environmental conditions).

According to the international standard **IEC 61025**, Fault Tree Analysis is a deductive analysis method used to determine the *cause(s)* of a system-level failure by representing the system as a hierarchical Boolean tree, where the *root node* is the undesired event and the *leaf nodes* are the contributing root causes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context:** FTA was originally conceived at **Bell Telephone Laboratories (1961–62)** by **H. A. Watson**, and was later refined for the U.S. Army's *Minuteman missile* launch control system. It is now governed by the **IEC 61025 (2006)** standard and forms the backbone of Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA) in process industries.

### Definition of Core Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Top Event (TE)** | The undesired event being analysed (e.g., *Loss of Containment*) |
| **Intermediate Event** | A failure event that is the result of logic gate combination of lower events |
| **Basic Event (BE)** | A leaf-level, non-decomposable failure event |
| **Undeveloped Event** | An event deliberately not analysed further (insignificant or data-unavailable) |
| **External Event** | An event expected to occur by design convention (e.g., normal power available) |
| **Cut Set** | A set of basic events whose joint occurrence causes the Top Event |
| **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** | A cut set that cannot be reduced further without losing the cut-set property |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – The "Detective Working Backwards"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a fire broke out in a hospital (the *Top Event*). A fire investigator walks *backwards in time* asking: "What must have happened for this fire to exist?"
>
> - **"Was there fuel?"** → AND condition with "Was there an ignition source?"
> - **"Did the sprinkler system fail?"** → OR condition with "Did the fire alarm fail?"
>
> Each question is answered by an **AND** or **OR** gate. The investigator keeps asking sub-questions until they reach events they can no longer break down (e.g., *electrical short circuit* in a wire) — these are the **Basic Events**.

This *backward reasoning* is the soul of FTA. Unlike Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA), which is *inductive* (component → system), FTA is **deductive** (system failure → root causes).

## 1.3 Why FTA is a Cornerstone of Process Plant Safety

In the context of **PECHT743 (Safety Engineering of Process Plants)**, FTA is used to:
- Identify **accident pathways** in reactors, distillation columns, storage tanks, and pipelines.
- Quantify the **probability of catastrophic events** (e.g., BLEVE, runaway reaction, toxic release).
- Support **SIL (Safety Integrity Level) determination** per IEC 61511.
- Justify **layers of protection** (LOPA) and **Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The probability of failure values for components in chemical process plants are typically expressed as **failure on demand (PFD)** with target ranges of **10⁻¹ to 10⁻⁵ per hour**, depending on SIL rating.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction Methodology — Step-by-Step

The construction of a Fault Tree follows a rigorous deductive flow:

1. **Define the Top Event (TE):** Be precise. *"Rupture of Reactor R-101"* is good; *"Accident"* is too vague.
2. **Identify Immediate Causes:** Determine which single faults, or combinations of faults, can directly cause the TE.
3. **Apply Logic Gates:** Use **AND** gates when *all* events must occur simultaneously; use **OR** gates when *any one* event suffices.
4. **Repeat Recursively:** Continue decomposition of each intermediate event until you reach basic events.
5. **Validate:** Perform peer review; ensure no event is decomposed in *more than one place* in the tree (no double-counting).
6. **Qualitative Analysis:** Identify **minimal cut sets**.
7. **Quantitative Analysis:** Compute the **top event probability/unavailability**.

## 2.2 The Logic Gates — The "Grammar" of Fault Trees

| Gate Symbol | Name | Boolean Expression | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **AND** | Logical AND | $T = A \cdot B \cdot C$ | All inputs must fail simultaneously |
| **OR** | Logical OR | $T = A + B + C$ | Any one input failure triggers TE |
| **XOR** | Exclusive OR | $T = A \oplus B$ | Exactly one input must fail |
| **Inhibit** | Inhibit Gate | $T = A \cdot \alpha$ | Input A combined with condition $\alpha$ |
| **Priority AND** | Sequence AND | $T = A \rightarrow B$ | A occurs **before** B |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **OR gate** is the most dangerous gate in process plant FTA — a single component failure (e.g., relief valve failure) immediately propagates to the top event. Designers minimize the use of OR connections at higher levels.

## 2.3 Boolean Algebra of Fault Trees

The fault tree's logical structure is governed by **Switching Algebra** (a special form of Boolean algebra). Key identities are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Identity:} \quad & A + 0 = A, \quad A \cdot 1 = A \\
\text{Domination:} \quad & A + 1 = 1, \quad A \cdot 0 = 0 \\
\text{Idempotence:} \quad & A + A = A, \quad A \cdot A = A \\
\text{Complement:} \quad & A + A' = 1, \quad A \cdot A' = 0 \\
\text{Absorption:} \quad & A + A \cdot B = A, \quad A \cdot (A + B) = A \\
\text{De Morgan's:} \quad & (A + B)' = A' \cdot B' \\
& (A \cdot B)' = A' + B'
\end{aligned}
$$

These rules are used to **simplify the structure function** of the fault tree to obtain the **sum of products (SoP)** form, which directly yields the minimal cut sets.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Structure Function (AND gate)** | $\Phi(X) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ | All inputs must fail |
| 2 | **Structure Function (OR gate)** | $\Phi(X) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - X_i)$ | Any one input failure suffices |
| 3 | **Probability (AND of independent events)** | $P(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i)$ | Series reliability |
| 4 | **Probability (OR of independent events)** | $P(T) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}[1 - P(X_i)]$ | Parallel reliability |
| 5 | **Rare-Event Approximation (Upper Bound)** | $P(T) \leq \sum_{i=1}^{k} \prod_{X_j \in MCS_i} P(X_j)$ | $P(X_i) \ll 1$ |
| 6 | **Sum of MCS Probabilities (SoP exact)** | $P(T) = P\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{k} MCS_i\right)$ | Use inclusion-exclusion |
| 7 | **Birnbaum Importance** | $I_{B}(X_i) = \dfrac{\partial P(T)}{\partial P(X_i)}$ | Sensitivity of TE to $X_i$ |
| 8 | **Criticality Importance** | $I_{C}(X_i) = \dfrac{P(X_i)\,I_{B}(X_i)}{P(T)}$ | Contribution given failure |
| 9 | **Fussell-Vesely Importance** | $I_{FV}(X_i) = \dfrac{\sum_{i: X_j \in MCS_i} P(MCS_i)}{P(T)}$ | Fraction of TE from $X_i$ |
| 10 | **System Unavailability (PFDavg)** | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_I}{2}$ | Low-demand mode, IEC 61508 |

> [!TIP]
> **Notation Tip for KTU Exams:** Use $P_i$ or $q_i$ for component unavailability. **Never** write absolute values like $\vert x \vert$ inside a table — use $\lvert x \rvert$ in display math instead.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Process Plant Safety

FTA bridges the gap between **qualitative hazard identification** (HAZOP, What-If) and **quantitative risk assessment (QRA)**. In a typical ethylene cracker plant, FTA can be used to:
- Estimate the frequency of *Loss of Cooling Water* events.
- Justify the **SIL-2 rating** of an emergency shutdown valve.
- Compute the **Probability of Dangerous Failure (PFD)** for instrumented protective functions.
- Demonstrate compliance with **COMAH** (Control of Major Accident Hazards) regulations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Boolean Simplification → Minimal Cut Sets

### Problem Statement

A reactor has a protective system against overpressure. The **Top Event (T)** is *"Rupture of Reactor R-101"*. The fault tree structure is defined as follows:

- $A$ = High internal pressure develops
- $B$ = Pressure vessel fails
- $C$ = Relief valve fails to open
- $X_1$ = Outlet block valve closed
- $X_2$ = Inlet flow control valve fails
- $X_3$ = Power supply to relief valve fails
- $X_4$ = Pneumatic signal to relief valve fails

**Fault tree logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= A \cdot B \\
A &= X_1 + X_2 \\
B &= C \quad \text{(a single event, no gate needed for representation as intermediate)} \\
C &= X_3 + X_4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Expand Each Intermediate Event

Substitute $A$ and $B$ into the top event equation:

$$
T = (X_1 + X_2) \cdot (X_3 + X_4)
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Distributive Law (FOIL expansion)

$$
T = X_1 \cdot X_3 + X_1 \cdot X_4 + X_2 \cdot X_3 + X_2 \cdot X_4
$$

This is the **Sum of Products (SoP)** form — each term is a candidate cut set.

### Step 3 — Identify Minimal Cut Sets

Inspecting each product term, none can be reduced further by absorption laws (no term is a subset of another). Therefore:

$$
MCS_1 = \{X_1, X_3\}, \quad MCS_2 = \{X_1, X_4\}, \quad MCS_3 = \{X_2, X_3\}, \quad MCS_4 = \{X_2, X_4\}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> There are **4 minimal cut sets**, each of order **2** (two basic events). This is a **dual-2-out-of-2** redundant system, often seen in safety-instrumented functions.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Quantitative Probability Calculation

### Given Failure Probabilities (per demand)

$$
P(X_1) = 0.01, \quad P(X_2) = 0.01, \quad P(X_3) = 0.001, \quad P(X_4) = 0.001
$$

Assumption: All basic events are **statistically independent** (standard in FTA).

### Method A — Rare-Event Approximation (Upper Bound)

Since all $P(X_i) \ll 1$, the joint probability of product terms is negligible beyond the first order:

$$
P(T) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{4} P(MCS_i)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(MCS_1) &= P(X_1) \cdot P(X_3) = 0.01 \times 0.001 = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \\
P(MCS_2) &= P(X_1) \cdot P(X_4) = 0.01 \times 0.001 = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \\
P(MCS_3) &= P(X_2) \cdot P(X_3) = 0.01 \times 0.001 = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \\
P(MCS_4) &= P(X_2) \cdot P(X_4) = 0.01 \times 0.001 = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
P(T) \approx 4 \times 10^{-5} = 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per demand}
$$

### Method B — Exact Computation via De Morgan's Law

Using the complement:

$$
P(T) = 1 - P(T') = 1 - P(A' + B') = 1 - (1 - P(A))(1 - P(B))
$$

Where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(A) &= 1 - (1 - P(X_1))(1 - P(X_2)) = 1 - (0.99)(0.99) = 0.0199 \\
P(B) &= 1 - (1 - P(X_3))(1 - P(X_4)) = 1 - (0.999)(0.999) = 0.001999
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &= 1 - (1 - 0.0199)(1 - 0.001999) \\
P(T) &= 1 - (0.9801)(0.998001) \\
P(T) &= 1 - 0.978123 \\
P(T) &= 0.021877 \quad \text{... wait!}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Trap:** Notice that the **rare-event approximation (4.0 × 10⁻⁵)** and the **exact computation (≈ 0.0219)** differ by **three orders of magnitude**! This is because the values $P(X_1)$ and $P(X_2) = 0.01$ are **not rare** (>1%). The rare-event approximation is **only valid** when all component probabilities are $\ll 10^{-2}$.
>
> For KTU board exam answers, **always state the assumption explicitly** and, where the assumption is violated, perform an exact computation or use a more accurate model.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Birnbaum & Fussell-Vesely Importance Measures

Using the same system, compute the importance of $X_3$ (relief valve power failure).

### Step 1 — Express $P(T)$ as a function of $P(X_3)$

$$
P(T) = [1 - (1 - P(X_1))(1 - P(X_2))] \cdot P(X_3)
$$

This is because $X_3$ is in **OR**-relation with $X_4$ in event $B$, and $B$ is in **AND**-relation with $A$. For small $P(X_4)$, $P(B) \approx P(X_3)$.

### Step 2 — Compute Birnbaum Importance

$$
I_B(X_3) = \frac{\partial P(T)}{\partial P(X_3)} = 1 - (1 - P(X_1))(1 - P(X_2))
$$

Substituting $P(X_1) = P(X_2) = 0.01$:

$$
I_B(X_3) = 1 - (0.99)^2 = 1 - 0.9801 = 0.0199
$$

This represents the *change in top event probability* per unit change in $P(X_3)$.

### Step 3 — Compute Criticality Importance

$$
I_C(X_3) = \frac{P(X_3) \cdot I_B(X_3)}{P(T)} = \frac{0.001 \times 0.0199}{4.0 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1.99 \times 10^{-5}}{4.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.4975
$$

This means **≈ 49.75% of the top event probability** is attributable to $X_3$ being failed — a very high importance, justifying enhanced maintenance and redundancy of the relief valve power supply.

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** High $I_C$ value → the component is the *weakest link* → prioritize its improvement through inspection, redundancy, or migration to a higher SIL rating.

## 3.4 Inclusion-Exclusion Correction (for completeness)

If you are required to compute the exact $P(T)$ without approximation, use:

$$
P(T) = \sum_i P(MCS_i) - \sum_{i<j} P(MCS_i \cap MCS_j) + \sum_{i<j<k} P(MCS_i \cap MCS_j \cap MCS_k) - \ldots
$$

This ensures KTU's *full-marks* answer when the rare-event assumption is *strictly invalid*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fault Tree Symbol Reference (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["FAULT TREE SYMBOLS"] --> B["EVENT SYMBOLS"]
    A --> C["LOGIC GATES"]
    A --> D["TRANSFER SYMBOLS"]

    B --> B1["Basic Event - Circle - Leaf-level failure"]
    B --> B2["Intermediate Event - Rectangle - Gate output"]
    B --> B3["Undeveloped Event - Diamond - Insignificant"]
    B --> B4["External Event - House - Normal condition"]

    C --> C1["AND Gate - All inputs required"]
    C --> C2["OR Gate - Any one input sufficient"]
    C --> C3["XOR Gate - Exactly one input"]
    C --> C4["Inhibit Gate - With conditioning event"]
    C --> C5["Priority AND - Sequence required"]

    D --> D1["Transfer In - Triangle pointing down"]
    D --> D2["Transfer Out - Triangle pointing up"]
```

## 4.2 Example Fault Tree: Loss of Cooling in a CSTR

```mermaid
graph TD
    TE["TOP EVENT: LOSS OF COOLING IN CSTR-101"] --> AND1{"AND"}
    AND1 --> A1["Coolant flow insufficient"]
    AND1 --> A2["Reactor temperature rises"]

    A1 --> OR1{"OR"}
    OR1 --> X1["Coolant pump fails"]
    OR1 --> X2["Coolant supply line rupture"]
    OR1 --> X3["Control valve fails closed"]

    A2 --> OR2{"OR"}
    OR2 --> X4["Temperature sensor fails high"]
    OR2 --> X5["Operator fails to respond"]
    OR2 --> X6["Cooling water header pressure low"]

    style TE fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style AND1 fill:#ffd93d
    style OR1 fill:#6bcf7f
    style OR2 fill:#6bcf7f
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: FTA Workflow

```mermaid
graph LR
    STEP1["STEP 1: Define Top Event"] --> STEP2["STEP 2: Identify Immediate Causes"]
    STEP2 --> STEP3["STEP 3: Apply AND OR Gates"]
    STEP3 --> STEP4["STEP 4: Decompose to Basic Events"]
    STEP4 --> STEP5["STEP 5: Boolean Simplification"]
    STEP5 --> STEP6["STEP 6: Determine Minimal Cut Sets"]
    STEP6 --> STEP7["STEP 7: Assign Failure Probabilities"]
    STEP7 --> STEP8["STEP 8: Compute Top Event Probability"]
    STEP8 --> STEP9["STEP 9: Importance Analysis"]
    STEP9 --> STEP10["STEP 10: Risk Reduction Recommendations"]

    style STEP1 fill:#4ecdc4,color:#fff
    style STEP5 fill:#ff9f43,color:#fff
    style STEP6 fill:#ee5a6f,color:#fff
    style STEP8 fill:#c44569,color:#fff
    style STEP10 fill:#6c5ce7,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Cut Set Hierarchy (Visualization)

```mermaid
graph TD
    TOP["TOP EVENT T"] --> M1["MINIMAL CUT SET 1"]
    TOP --> M2["MINIMAL CUT SET 2"]
    TOP --> M3["MINIMAL CUT SET 3"]

    M1 --> m1a["X1"]
    M1 --> m1b["X2"]

    M2 --> m2a["X3"]
    M2 --> m2b["X4"]

    M3 --> m3a["X5"]
    M3 --> m3b["X6"]
    M3 --> m3c["X7"]

    style TOP fill:#d63031,color:#fff
    style M1 fill:#fdcb6e
    style M2 fill:#fdcb6e
    style M3 fill:#00b894,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> Cut set **order** = number of basic events in the set. Lower order = more dangerous. A *single-point failure* corresponds to a **1st-order** cut set — the most critical finding in any FTA.
