<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define Fault Tree Analysis. List the symbols used in constructing a fault tree.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Fault Tree Analysis is a top-down, deductive failure analysis technique in which an undesired system state (Top Event) is logically decomposed, using AND/OR gates, into a hierarchy of intermediate and basic events, in order to identify the combinations of failures that can cause the undesired event and to quantify its probability.

**Symbols (1 Mark):** The principal symbols are:
- **Basic Event** — Circle representing a leaf-level failure
- **Intermediate Event** — Rectangle representing a gate output
- **Undeveloped Event** — Diamond representing events not further analysed
- **External Event** — House-shaped symbol for assumed-normal conditions
- **AND Gate** / **OR Gate** / **Inhibit Gate** — Boolean logic connectors
- **Transfer In / Transfer Out** — Triangle symbols for tree continuation

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series II]`
**What is a Minimal Cut Set? Why is the rare-event approximation used in FTA?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Minimal Cut Set (1.5 Marks):** A minimal cut set is the smallest combination of basic events whose joint occurrence causes the Top Event, and which cannot be reduced further without losing the property of causing the Top Event. The order of the cut set is the number of basic events it contains.

**Rare-Event Approximation (1.5 Marks):** When all basic event probabilities are $\ll 1$ (typically $< 10^{-2}$), the joint occurrence probability of more than one basic event in a cut set becomes negligible. Therefore, $P(T) \approx \sum_{i} P(MCS_i)$. This approximation **avoids the computationally expensive inclusion-exclusion expansion** and is widely used in industrial FTA software.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. There is an **internal choice** — attempt either **OR Set A** or **OR Set B** in full.

---

### 🔷 **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]`

**A reactor has the following protection system: Overpressure leads to vessel rupture if (i) the pressure relief valve fails AND the rupture disc is also blown, OR (ii) the high-pressure trip fails to actuate.**

Define the Top Event, draw the fault tree, derive the Boolean expression, and compute the top event probability given:
$P(PRV) = 0.01$, $P(RD) = 0.05$, $P(HPT) = 0.02$ per demand.

#### **Part (a) — Fault Tree Construction & Boolean Expression (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Define the Top Event:**
$T$ = "Rupture of Reactor R-200" *(0.5 Marks)*

**Step 2 — Identify Intermediate Events:**
- $A$ = Pressure relief system fails
- $B$ = Pressure exceeds design limit
- $C$ = Pressure relief valve (PRV) fails
- $D$ = Rupture disc (RD) blows

**Step 3 — Fault Tree Logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= A + B \\
A &= C \cdot D \quad \text{(AND gate: both PRV must fail AND RD must blow)} \\
B &= HPT \quad \text{(single intermediate event)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Fault tree diagram carries 2 marks — show using a Mermaid/hierarchical diagram)*

**Step 4 — Boolean Expansion:**

$$
T = (C \cdot D) + HPT
$$

Substituting $C = PRV$, $D = RD$:

$$
T = (PRV \cdot RD) + HPT
$$

**Step 5 — Minimal Cut Sets:**

$$
MCS_1 = \{PRV, RD\}, \quad MCS_2 = \{HPT\}
$$

> $MCS_2$ is a **single-point failure** (1st-order cut set) — extremely dangerous. **Marks 6–7 awarded for identifying the 1st-order cut set.**

---

#### **Part (b) — Probability Computation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute $P(MCS_1)$:**

$$
P(MCS_1) = P(PRV) \cdot P(RD) = 0.01 \times 0.05 = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P(MCS_2)$:**

$$
P(MCS_2) = P(HPT) = 0.02
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the OR gate probability formula:**

$$
P(T) = 1 - [1 - P(MCS_1)][1 - P(MCS_2)]
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - [1 - 5.0 \times 10^{-4}][1 - 0.02]
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - (0.9995)(0.98)
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - 0.97951 = 0.02049
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

$$
\boxed{P(T) \approx 2.05 \times 10^{-2} \text{ per demand}}
$$

> The **HPT failure dominates** (≈ 97.6% of the top event probability). The high-pressure trip system must be upgraded to **SIL-2** to reduce $P(HPT)$ to below $10^{-3}$.

---

### 🔷 **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A storage tank containing liquefied ammonia is fitted with two pressure sensors (S1, S2) and a single emergency isolation valve (EIV). The tank ruptures if (a) both pressure sensors fail to detect high pressure AND the operator fails to actuate the isolation valve manually, OR (b) the EIV fails to close on automatic command.**

Assume independent failures with $P(S_1) = P(S_2) = 0.005$, $P(\text{operator}) = 0.01$, and $P(EIV) = 0.001$ per demand. Construct the fault tree, identify the minimal cut sets, and compute the top event probability.

#### **Part (a) — Fault Tree & Minimal Cut Sets (7 Marks)**

**Top Event:** $T$ = "Rupture of Ammonia Storage Tank"

**Fault Tree Structure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= A + B \\
A &= S_1 \cdot S_2 \cdot O \quad \text{(AND gate, 3rd-order cut set)} \\
B &= EIV
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $A$ = *"Failure of detection + operator action"* and $B$ = *"Failure of automatic isolation"*.

**Minimal Cut Sets:**

$$
MCS_1 = \{S_1, S_2, O\}, \quad MCS_2 = \{EIV\}
$$

> $MCS_2$ is a **single-point failure** → add a *second, independent* EIV in series to eliminate the 1st-order cut set. *(Recommendation carries full marks for qualitative analysis.)*

**Boolean Expression:**

$$
\Phi(T) = (S_1 \cdot S_2 \cdot O) + EIV
$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Quantitative Analysis (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Probability of $MCS_1$:**

$$
P(MCS_1) = P(S_1) \cdot P(S_2) \cdot P(O) = (0.005)(0.005)(0.01) = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 2 — Probability of $MCS_2$:**

$$
P(MCS_2) = P(EIV) = 0.001
$$

**Step 3 — Rare-Event Check:** $P(MCS_1) = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \ll 1$ ✓ — approximation valid.

**Step 4 — Apply the OR gate formula:**

$$
P(T) \approx P(MCS_1) + P(MCS_2) = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} + 1.0 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
P(T) \approx 1.00025 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\boxed{P(T) \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per demand}}
$$

**Step 5 — Recommendation:** Add a *redundant EIV* and a *high-integrity pressure transmitter*. This would reduce the 1st-order cut set to a 2nd-order cut set, dropping $P(T)$ to ≤ $10^{-5}$, suitable for **SIL-2 compliance**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
>
> 1. **Forgetting the rare-event assumption:** If $P(X_i) > 0.01$, the approximation $P(T) \approx \sum P(MCS_i)$ is **invalid**. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks if you don't state the assumption. Always verify: $P \ll 1$.
> 2. **Missing the 1st-order cut set:** A single-point failure (1st-order MCS) is a *critical* finding. Not identifying it explicitly loses **2 full marks**.
> 3. **Confusing cut sets with path sets:** A *cut set* causes the system to fail; a *path set* is a set of components whose success guarantees the system works. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 4. **Ignoring common cause failures (CCF):** In real systems, two redundant sensors may fail due to the same root cause (e.g., shared power supply). A common KTU question asks for the β-factor correction.
> 5. **Units mismatch:** PFD per *demand* ≠ failure rate per *hour*. Always state units. Failure on demand $\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_I / 2$ is for **low-demand mode** only.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 3: Fault Tree Analysis**

- ✅ **FTA is deductive** (top-down), unlike FMEA which is inductive (bottom-up).
- ✅ **Top Event** must be precisely defined; avoid vague terms like *"accident"*.
- ✅ **AND gate** = redundancy-vulnerable (all must fail); **OR gate** = single-failure-vulnerable.
- ✅ **Minimal Cut Set** order matters: 1st-order = single-point failure (worst case).
- ✅ Boolean simplification uses **distributive law**: $(A+B)(C+D) = AC + AD + BC + BD$.
- ✅ **Rare-event approximation** valid only when all $P(X_i) \ll 1$ (typically $< 10^{-2}$).
- ✅ **Birnbaum importance** measures sensitivity; **Fussell-Vesely** measures contribution.
- ✅ Always state the **independence assumption** when computing probabilities.
- ✅ **IEC 61025** is the governing standard; cite it in long answers.
- ✅ For process plants, FTA outputs feed into **LOPA → SIL determination** per IEC 61511.
- ✅ FTA complements (does not replace) HAZOP, FMEA, and Event Tree Analysis.
- ✅ Top event probability for a 1st-order cut set = single component failure probability.
- ✅ Use **transfer symbols** when a fault tree is too large for one page.
- ✅ Common symbols: **Circle** = basic; **Rectangle** = intermediate; **Diamond** = undeveloped; **House** = external; **Triangle** = transfer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event Tree Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Event Tree Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** is an *inductive*, forward-deductive probabilistic risk assessment technique that graphically models the chronological sequence of outcomes following a specified **initiating event (IE)**, by tracing the success or failure of successive **safety functions (SFs)** or barriers deployed in the plant. Each branching fork represents a binary (or multi-state) logic gate — typically **YES (success)** or **NO (failure)** — so that every terminal node of the tree corresponds to a unique, well-defined **consequence outcome** with an associated cumulative probability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Alignment):**
> *"Event Tree Analysis is a forward-logic diagrammatic method used to determine the various possible outcomes of an initiating event by evaluating the performance of successive safety barriers, enabling the quantification of accident sequence probabilities in process industries."*

Key terminological anchors (must be memorized for ESE):

| Term | KTU Standard Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Initiating Event (IE)** | The first event in the sequence — start point of the tree |
| **Safety Function (SF)** | An engineered, human, or procedural barrier (alarm, relief valve, ESD, operator action) |
| **Branching Node** | The point where success/failure of an SF is evaluated |
| **Outcome Path / Sequence** | A unique top-to-bottom trajectory through the tree |
| **End-state / Consequence** | The terminal result (e.g., Safe Shutdown, Minor Leak, Major Fire) |
| **Cumulative Frequency** | Sum of all path frequencies leading to a given consequence |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Domino Cascade of Choices"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine you are driving a car on a highway and a deer suddenly jumps onto the road (**Initiating Event**). Your brain now runs a "decision tree":
> - **SF1 — Brake:** *Works?* → YES → slow down | NO → continue at speed
> - **SF2 — Steer sideways:** *Successful?* → YES → avoid deer | NO → collision imminent
> - **SF3 — Airbag deploys:** *Works?* → YES → minor injury | NO → severe injury
> 
> **Event Tree Analysis does exactly this — but for a process plant.** Given an initiating deviation (overpressure, leak, runaway reaction), it asks: *"For each safety barrier in sequence, did it perform?"* The path traced top-to-bottom becomes a unique accident scenario with its own probability.

## 1.3 Why ETA Matters in Process Plant Safety

Process plants (refineries, petrochemicals, LNG terminals) house highly energetic inventories (hydrocarbons, high-pressure gases, exothermic reactants). A single initiating event may escalate catastrophically if barriers fail. ETA is the **only technique** that:

- (i) **Preserves chronology** — order of barrier deployment matters
- (ii) **Quantifies endpoint frequencies** — converts event probabilities into consequence frequencies
- (iii) **Identifies dominant accident sequences** — the ones with highest contribution to risk

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** When asked "Why ETA over Fault Tree?", the gold-standard answer is: *ETA proceeds forward from an initiating event and directly produces consequences with frequencies, whereas FTA works backward from a top event and only yields minimal cut-sets without explicit chronology.*

## 1.4 Position in the Risk Assessment Toolbox

ETA is the *downstream* half of the **Bow-Tie Diagram**:

$$
\underbrace{\text{Fault Tree}}_{\text{left side: cause-to-event}} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Initiating Event}}_{\text{central knot}} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Event Tree}}_{\text{right side: event-to-consequence}}
$$

The combined Bow-Tie gives **cause-frequency → consequence-frequency** for full QRA (Quantitative Risk Assessment).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction Logic — The Six Engineering Steps

1. **Identify the Initiating Event (IE)**
   - Defined either from prior HAZOP/FMEA studies, incident databases (e.g., OGP, CCPS), or fault-tree top events.
   - Examples: Pump failure, Block valve closure, Instrument air failure, Cooling water pump trip.

2. **Identify the Safety Functions in Chronological Order**
   - List every engineered, procedural, and human barrier that is *expected* to respond to the IE.
   - **Order matters** — earlier functions take precedence over later ones.

3. **Assign Branching Logic at Each Node**
   - **Binary trees**: YES (success) vs NO (failure) — most common in KTU problems.
   - **Multi-state trees**: 3 or more outcomes (e.g., partial success, delayed response) — advanced problems only.

4. **Quantify Branch Probabilities**
   - Use component reliability data (failure rates from OREDA, IEEE Std 500, CCPS PERD).
   - Human error probabilities from THERP (Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction) or HEART.

5. **Compute Path Probabilities**
   - Multiply the probability of the IE by the probability of each branch selected along that path.

6. **Aggregate Endpoint Frequencies**
   - Sum the probabilities of all paths leading to a given consequence to obtain the cumulative frequency of that consequence.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas required for solving ESE Module-3 Event Tree problems are summarized below. Memorize before exam.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{\text{path},i} = P_{\text{IE}} \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{n} p_{j,k}$ | Probability of path *i* = P(IE) × product of branch probabilities along path | dimensionless (freq·yr⁻¹ if P_IE is freq) |
| 2 | $F_{\text{consequence}} = \sum_{i \in C} P_{\text{path},i}$ | Cumulative frequency of consequence *C* = sum over all paths producing *C* | yr⁻¹ |
| 3 | $p_{\text{SF,success}} + p_{\text{SF,failure}} = 1$ | Branch probability complement rule | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{Probit } Y = a + b \ln(C^n \cdot t)$ | Consequence severity calculation (toxic/fire) — connected via endpoint | — |
| 5 | $\text{Individual Risk (IR)} = \sum_k F_k \cdot p_{\text{direction},k}$ | Risk metric downstream of ETA | yr⁻¹ |
| 6 | $P(\text{NOT occurrence}) = 1 - \sum_i P_{\text{path},i}$ | Probability that the IE causes no listed consequence | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Frequency Reduction Factor} = \dfrac{P_{\text{IE}}}{F_{\text{worst outcome}}}$ | Measure of barrier effectiveness | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Exam Trap:** When P_IE is given as a *frequency* (events per year, e.g., **1×10⁻² yr⁻¹**), the resulting endpoint is also a *frequency* (yr⁻¹), not a probability. Do not divide by 1 yr.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where ETA Is Deployed

- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** of LNG terminals, offshore platforms, refinery hydrocrackers
- **COMAH / Seveso-III** safety report submissions in EU plants
- **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** as the consequence-side engine
- **Emergency Response Planning** — identifies which accident sequences need which response
- **ALARP demonstration** — quantifies residual risk after barriers

## 2.4 Event Tree vs Fault Tree — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Parameter | Event Tree (ETA) | Fault Tree (FTA) |
|---|---|---|
| Logic direction | Forward (deductive-from-cause) | Backward (inductive-from-top) |
| Starting point | Initiating event | Unwanted top event |
| Branching element | Success/Failure of barriers | AND / OR / NOT gates |
| Output | Frequencies of consequences | Minimal cut-sets, top-event frequency |
| Chronology | Preserved | Not preserved |
| Use stage | After identifying initiating events | Before ETA in Bow-Tie |

## 2.5 Limiting Assumptions in ETA

- Events along a path are assumed **statistically independent** (a strong assumption often violated by common-cause failures).
- Each safety function is modeled as **binary** — partial degradation is collapsed into pass/fail.
- Operator action time windows are treated as instantaneous success/failure thresholds.
- The **branching probabilities are time-invariant** — ignores aging, maintenance state.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Numerical Construction of an Event Tree

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Model Problem Statement (Exam-grade):**
> A refinery *LPG bullet storage tank* is fitted with the following safety chain against the initiating event *Tank Overfill* (IE):
> - **SF₁:** Independent High-Level Alarm (IHLA) — works with probability **0.95**
> - **SF₂:** Operator Intervention within 5 minutes — works with probability **0.85**
> - **SF₃:** Emergency Shutdown Valve (ESDV) auto-closes — works with probability **0.90**
> - **SF₄:** Bund-wall containment holds — holds with probability **0.99**
> 
> The frequency of tank overfill is **P_IE = 5×10⁻³ yr⁻¹**.
> 
> **(a)** Construct the Event Tree.
> **(b)** Determine the cumulative frequency of each end-state.
> **(c)** Identify the dominant accident sequence.

### 3.2 Tree Construction (Binary Branching)

Each safety function produces a YES/NO branch (Top branch = success, Bottom branch = failure).

There are 4 safety functions → $2^4 = 16$ paths, but only 4 distinct end-states (since the consequence depends on how many barriers failed).

### 3.3 Symbolic Derivation of Path Frequencies

Let us define the **branch probabilities** explicitly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1^s &= 0.95, & p_1^f &= 1 - 0.95 = 0.05 \\
p_2^s &= 0.85, & p_2^f &= 1 - 0.85 = 0.15 \\
p_3^s &= 0.90, & p_3^f &= 1 - 0.90 = 0.10 \\
p_4^s &= 0.99, & p_4^f &= 1 - 0.99 = 0.01
\end{aligned}
$$

(Superscript *s* = success, *f* = failure.)

For any path *i*, the conditional probability given the IE is:

$$
P(\text{path}_i \mid \text{IE}) = \prod_{j=1}^{4} p_{j,\text{branch}(i,j)}
$$

The absolute **frequency** of that path per year is:

$$
F_{\text{path},i} = P_{\text{IE}} \cdot P(\text{path}_i \mid \text{IE})
$$

### 3.4 Path-by-Path Numerical Computation

We enumerate the **16 paths** and group them by **end-state**.

**End-State A — Safe Shutdown** (all 4 barriers succeed; SSSS):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{SSSS} \mid \text{IE}) &= (0.95)(0.85)(0.90)(0.99) \\
&= 0.95 \times 0.85 = 0.8075 \\
&\quad \times 0.90 = 0.72675 \\
&\quad \times 0.99 = 0.71948
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
F_A = (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.71948 = 3.597 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**End-State B — Minor Spill, contained in bund** (only bund fails; SSSF, SSSF... actually SF₄ fails after SF₁-3 success = SSSF, and combinations where earlier ones succeed):

The bund *fails* but all upstream SFs succeed → **only 1 path**: SF₁=S, SF₂=S, SF₃=S, SF₄=F.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{SSSF}) &= (0.95)(0.85)(0.90)(0.01) \\
&= 0.72675 \times 0.01 = 7.2675 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
F_B = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.2675 \times 10^{-3} = 3.634 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**End-State C — Bund Holds but earlier barrier fails** (paths where some of SF₁-SF₃ fail but SF₄ holds — these are inside-bund pools / partial spills). There are $2^3 - 1 = 7$ such paths.

We compute the **probability that SF₄ holds given at least one earlier failure**, which equals $0.99 \times [1 - P(\text{all three earlier succeed})]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{at least one of SF}_1\text{-SF}_3 \text{ fails}) &= 1 - (0.95)(0.85)(0.90) \\
&= 1 - 0.72675 = 0.27325
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
P(\text{End-State C} \mid \text{IE}) = 0.27325 \times 0.99 = 0.27052
$$

$$
F_C = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.27052 = 1.353 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**End-State D — Major Pool Fire / VCE** (bund fails AND at least one of SF₁-SF₃ fails) — 7 paths:

$$
P(\text{End-State D} \mid \text{IE}) = 0.27325 \times 0.01 = 2.7325 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
F_D = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.7325 \times 10^{-3} = 1.366 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

### 3.5 Frequency Conservation Check (Mandatory in ESE)

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_A + F_B + F_C + F_D &\stackrel{?}{=} P_{\text{IE}} \\
3.597 \times 10^{-3} + 3.634 \times 10^{-5} + 1.353 \times 10^{-3} + 1.366 \times 10^{-5} &= ? \\
4.999 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{yr}^{-1} &\approx 5.000 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{yr}^{-1} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sum must equal P_IE exactly (within rounding).** Always include this check in KTU 14-mark answers to earn the validation mark.

### 3.6 Dominant Accident Sequence Identification

Sort all 16 paths by descending frequency:

| Rank | Path (SF₁-SF₂-SF₃-SF₄) | Probability | F_path (yr⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | S S S S | 0.71948 | **3.597 × 10⁻³** |
| 2 | F S S S | 0.03786 | 1.893 × 10⁻⁴ |
| 3 | S F S S | 0.10050 | 5.025 × 10⁻⁴ |
| 4 | S S F S | 0.08075 | 4.038 × 10⁻⁴ |
| 5 | F F S S | 0.00504 | 2.520 × 10⁻⁵ |
| … | (others much smaller) | … | … |

The dominant sequence is **S-F-S-S** (SF₂ — operator action — fails, others succeed) with $F = 5.025 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{yr}^{-1}$. **Recommendation:** improve operator training or install a second automatic isolation valve independent of operator action.

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — End-State Summary

| End-State | Symbol | Cumulative Frequency (yr⁻¹) | % of IE | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Safe Shutdown | A | **3.597 × 10⁻³** | 71.95% | System performs as designed |
| Minor Spill (Bund OK) | C | **1.353 × 10⁻³** | 27.05% | Bund absorbs incident |
| Bund-only Fail | B | **3.634 × 10⁻⁵** | 0.73% | Bund integrity issue |
| Major Pool Fire / VCE | D | **1.366 × 10⁻⁵** | 0.27% | Worst case — target of ALARP study |
| **Total** | — | **5.000 × 10⁻³** | 100% | — |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of ETA Construction

The figure below maps the **engineering workflow** of Event Tree Analysis — from initiating-event identification through endpoint frequency aggregation. Each block corresponds to a discrete work-package that a Process Safety Engineer performs in industry.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HAZOP / FMEA / Incident DB] --> B[Identify Initiating Event IE]
    B --> C[List Safety Functions in Chronological Order]
    C --> D[Assign Branch Probabilities from OREDA THERP HEART]
    D --> E[Construct Binary Event Tree Nodes]
    E --> F[Compute Conditional Path Probabilities]
    F --> G[Multiply by P IE to get Path Frequencies]
    G --> H[Group Paths by End State Consequence]
    H --> I[Sum Frequencies for Cumulative F per Outcome]
    I --> J[Rank Dominant Accident Sequences]
    J --> K[ALARP Recommendations and Barrier Improvements]
    K --> L[Feed into LOPA Bow Tie QRA]
```

## 4.2 Event Tree Topology — The LPG Overfill Example

This Mermaid diagram renders the **chronological branching structure** of the worked example from Section 3. Top branch at each node = SUCCESS, bottom branch = FAILURE. Terminal nodes show the cumulative frequency in $\text{yr}^{-1}$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IE[IE Tank Overfill F equals 5E-3 per yr] --> N1
    N1{SF1 IHLA Works} --> N1Y[YES]
    N1Y --> N2{SF2 Operator Action Works}
    N1 --> N1N[NO fail branch]
    N1N --> N2F{N2 fail path}
    N2 --> N3{SF3 ESDV Auto Close Works}
    N3 --> N4{SF4 Bund Wall Holds}
    N4 --> ES1[END STATE A Safe Shutdown F equals 3.597E-3]
    N4 --> ES2[END STATE B Bund Fails F equals 3.634E-5]
    N2 --> ES3[END STATE C Partial Spill Bund Holds F equals 1.353E-3]
    N2F --> ES4[END STATE D Major Pool Fire F equals 1.366E-5]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Convention:** Each leftward descending branch represents a **failure** state. The terminal row lists the four consolidated end-states with their cumulative yearly frequencies. This is the exact structure examiners expect in a 14-mark answer.

## 4.3 Schematic Mapping — ETA in the Bow-Tie Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HZ[HAZARDS] --> FT[Fault Tree CAUSE side]
    FT --> IE[(Initiating Event Central Knot)]
    IE --> ET[Event Tree CONSEQUENCE side]
    ET --> CS1[Safe State]
    ET --> CS2[Minor Spill]
    ET --> CS3[Major Fire]
    ET --> CS4[Catastrophic VCE]
```

The Bow-Tie unifies Fault Tree (left) and Event Tree (right) at the **Initiating Event** node. This is the gold-standard architecture used in **ISO 31000** and **CCPS Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis**.

## 4.4 Conceptual Mapping — Branch-State Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[START Initiating Event] --> Q1{SF1 Status}
    Q1 --> P1A[Success Downstream Path]
    Q1 --> P1B[Failure Downstream Path]
    P1A --> Q2{SF2 Status}
    P1B --> Q2
    Q2 --> P2A[Success Path]
    Q2 --> P2B[Failure Path]
    P2A --> Q3{SF3 Status}
    P2B --> Q3
    Q3 --> END[End States Assigned]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always draw the event tree from **top to bottom** (initiating event at top, end states at bottom) with **left = success** and **right = failure**. This is the IEC 62502 / CCPS standard convention.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
*Define Event Tree Analysis. List its key components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Event Tree Analysis (ETA) is a forward-deductive probabilistic risk assessment technique that evaluates the sequence of outcomes following an initiating event in a process plant. **(1 mark)**
Its key components are:
1. **Initiating Event (IE)** — the starting point of the analysis. **(1 mark)**
2. **Safety Functions / Barriers** — engineered or procedural responses evaluated in chronological order. **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Branching Nodes** — binary YES/NO logic points representing success or failure of each barrier. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
*Differentiate between Event Tree Analysis and Fault Tree Analysis.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Event Tree | Fault Tree |
|---|---|---|
| Logic direction | Forward (cause → consequence) | Backward (consequence → causes) |
| Output | Frequencies of all outcomes | Minimal cut-sets of top event |

**(2 marks)** plus a working example or any one additional distinguishing feature **(1 mark)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]

**(a)** Explain in detail the step-by-step methodology for conducting Event Tree Analysis in a process plant. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An *ethylene oxide reactor* has the initiating event "Loss of Cooling" with frequency **1×10⁻² yr⁻¹**. The plant has three independent barriers:
- SF₁: Temperature trip → operates correctly with probability **0.98**
- SF₂: Emergency dump valve → operates correctly with probability **0.92**
- SF₃: Quench system → operates correctly with probability **0.96**

Calculate the cumulative frequency of:
- (i) Safe controlled shutdown *(3 marks)*
- (ii) Runaway reaction *(4 marks)*

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Methodology (7 marks):**
The seven-step methodology must include:
1. IE identification (1 mark)
2. Chronological SF listing (1 mark)
3. Branch logic assignment (1 mark)
4. Probability quantification using reliability data (1 mark)
5. Path probability computation (1 mark)
6. Endpoint aggregation (1 mark)
7. Dominant sequence ranking + ALARP recommendations (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Branch probabilities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1^f &= 0.02, & p_2^f &= 0.08, & p_3^f &= 0.04
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Safe Shutdown — all three barriers succeed (path SSS):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{SSS}) &= (0.98)(0.92)(0.96) \\
&= 0.98 \times 0.92 = 0.9016 \\
&\quad \times 0.96 = 0.8655
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
F_{\text{safe}} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.8655 = 8.655 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{yr}^{-1} \;\;\textbf{(3 marks)}
$$

**(ii) Runaway — at least one barrier fails (complement of all-success):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Runaway}) &= 1 - 0.8655 = 0.1345 \\
F_{\text{runaway}} &= 1 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.1345 = 1.345 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{yr}^{-1} \;\;\textbf{(4 marks)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often forget to multiply the conditional path probability by P_IE to obtain a frequency. The unit check is mandatory — if your final number has no units of yr⁻¹ for frequency, you have lost at least 2 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction of an Event Tree for an LNG leak scenario in a regasification terminal. Clearly identify the initiating event, safety functions, branching nodes, and end-states. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A *chlorine storage vessel* has initiating event "Vessel Rupture" with frequency **2×10⁻⁴ yr⁻¹**. There are four safety barriers in sequence:
- SF₁: Gas detection system — works with prob **0.95**
- SF₂: Isolation valve auto-close — works with prob **0.93**
- SF₃: Water curtain neutralization — works with prob **0.88**
- SF₄: Population evacuation (within 30 min) — works with prob **0.80**

Determine:
- (i) The frequency of full safe control. *(2 marks)*
- (ii) The frequency of toxic cloud reaching populated area (all four barriers fail). *(2 marks)*
- (iii) The frequency of partial neutralization (only SF₄ fails). *(3 marks)*

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Sketch + Construction (7 marks):**
Diagram should show: IE "LNG Leak" at top → SF1 Gas detection → SF2 ESDV → SF3 Water spray → SF4 Emergency shutdown → 5 end-states (no ignition / flash fire / pool fire / jet fire / VCE). Each barrier with branch probability marked. **(Full 7 marks for neat labeled tree with all five SFs and their probabilities.)**

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Branch probabilities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1^f = 0.05, \quad p_2^f = 0.07, \quad p_3^f = 0.12, \quad p_4^f = 0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Full safe control (all four succeed):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{SSSS}) &= (0.95)(0.93)(0.88)(0.80) \\
&= 0.95 \times 0.93 = 0.8835 \\
&\quad \times 0.88 = 0.7775 \\
&\quad \times 0.80 = 0.6220
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
F_{\text{safe}} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.6220 = 1.244 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{yr}^{-1} \;\;\textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**(ii) All four barriers fail (FFFF path):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{FFFF}) &= (0.05)(0.07)(0.12)(0.20) \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-2} \times 7 \times 10^{-2} = 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \\
&\quad \times 1.2 \times 10^{-1} = 4.2 \times 10^{-4} \\
&\quad \times 2.0 \times 10^{-1} = 8.4 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
F_{\text{toxic cloud}} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.4 \times 10^{-5} = 1.68 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{yr}^{-1} \;\;\textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**(iii) Only SF₄ fails (path SSSF — three upstream succeed):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{SSSF}) &= (0.95)(0.93)(0.88)(0.20) \\
&= 0.7775 \times 0.20 = 0.15550
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
F_{\text{partial}} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.15550 = 3.110 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{yr}^{-1} \;\;\textbf{(3 marks)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Confusing **probability** with **frequency** — if P_IE is a frequency, multiply; if P_IE is dimensionless, do not.
> 2. Summing endpoint frequencies incorrectly — only paths leading to the **same** end-state are summed.
> 3. Forgetting the **frequency conservation check** (sum of all end-states must equal P_IE).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] ETA is **forward-deductive**, FTA is **backward-inductive** — must be stated in every comparison.
- [ ] Always identify the **Initiating Event** explicitly before drawing the tree.
- [ ] **Chronological order** of safety functions is non-negotiable in ETA.
- [ ] Each branching node is **binary** (success/failure) in basic problems; multi-state in advanced.
- [ ] Path probability formula: $P_{\text{path}} = P_{\text{IE}} \cdot \prod p_{\text{branch}}$
- [ ] Cumulative frequency of a consequence: $F_C = \sum_{i \in C} P_{\text{path},i}$
- [ ] **Sum of all end-state frequencies = P_IE** (use this as a verification step).
- [ ] Dominant accident sequence = the path with the **highest individual frequency**.
- [ ] Human error probabilities come from **THERP / HEART** databases.
- [ ] Equipment failure probabilities come from **OREDA / IEEE Std 500 / CCPS PERD**.
- [ ] ETA pairs with FTA to form the **Bow-Tie** — used in QRA per ISO 31000.
- [ ] Assumptions: statistical independence, binary performance, time-invariant failure rates.
- [ ] Consequence severity (fire radiation, toxic dose) is calculated **separately** using Probit equations and multiplied by ETA-derived frequencies for individual & societal risk.
- [ ] In KTU 14-mark answers, **always include a labeled sketch** + numerical table of end-state frequencies + dominant sequence recommendation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Modes and Effects Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Failure Modes and Effects Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** is a systematic, bottom-up, inductive (forward-logic) analytical technique used in process safety engineering to identify and evaluate the potential **failure modes** of a system, component, process, or design, the corresponding **effects** of those failures on the system, and the **causes** that lead to such failures, with the ultimate goal of prioritizing risk reduction actions.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – Process Safety Engineering**, FMEA is formally classified as a **qualitative (or semi-quantitative) hazard identification and risk assessment tool** that forms an integral part of the Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) family, alongside techniques such as HAZOP, What-If Analysis, and Fault Tree Analysis (FTA).

The official procedure follows the framework codified in:
- **IEC 60812** – *"Analysis techniques for system reliability – Procedure for failure mode and effects analysis"*
- **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (2019)** – used in automotive and process industries
- **MIL-STD-1629A** – the original U.S. Military standard that birthed the technique in **1949**

### Core Nomenclature (KTU Board Expected Terminology)

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Failure Mode** | The manner in which a component, subsystem, or process can fail (e.g., *leak, rupture, stuck-open, stuck-closed, no flow, over-pressurization*). |
| **Effect** | The immediate consequence of the failure mode on the **next higher level** of the system (local effect), or on the **entire plant** (system effect), or on the **end user/customer** (final effect). |
| **Cause** | The mechanism or root-event that triggers the failure mode (e.g., corrosion, erosion, human error, power failure). |
| **Severity (S)** | A numerical rating (typically **1–10**) of the worst credible consequence of the failure. |
| **Occurrence (O)** | A numerical rating (typically **1–10**) of the likelihood that the failure mode will actually occur. |
| **Detection (D)** | A numerical rating (typically **1–10**) of the likelihood that the **current process control** will *detect* the failure *before* it reaches the customer/environment. |
| **RPN** | **Risk Priority Number** = product of Severity × Occurrence × Detection. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of FMEA as a "Pre-Mortem for a Machine before it is even built."**

Imagine you are designing a **domestic pressure cooker** (a real chemical process plant in miniature). Before the first prototype is welded, you sit down and ask three questions about every single part:

1. **"What can go wrong?"** (Failure Mode) → *The safety valve could stick closed.*
2. **"If it goes wrong, what happens?"** (Effect) → *The cooker would explode, endangering the user.* (Severity = 10, Catastrophic)
3. **"Why might it go wrong?"** (Cause) → *Mineral deposits from boiling water, lack of periodic maintenance.* (Occurrence = 6, Moderate)
4. **"Would we know it has gone wrong before disaster?"** (Detection) → *Currently, no alarm — user would only feel the lid lock.* (Detection = 9, Very Low Detectability)

By multiplying **S × O × D**, you get a single Risk Priority Number. Parts with high RPN are redesigned first. This is precisely the philosophy of FMEA — **anticipate failure on paper so it doesn't happen in steel**.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Bottom-Up" Logic of FMEA:**
> Start at the **component level** (a pump seal, a solenoid valve) and trace failure consequences **upward** to the system. This is the *opposite* of Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), which is "top-down."

---

## 1.3 Why FMEA is Indispensable in Process Safety

- It forces **interdisciplinary review** (design, operations, maintenance, safety, instrumentation).
- It produces a **living document** that evolves with the design and operating history.
- It is a **mandatory regulatory deliverable** in automotive (IATF 16949), aerospace (AS9100), medical devices (ISO 14971), and is heavily referenced in process safety regulations (OSHA 1910.119, EPA RMP).
- It is the **foundation for developing a Plant Safety Booklet** and forms the building block of **Hazard and Operability (HAZOP)** studies.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of FMEA Studies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 examiners expect students to distinguish between the **three principal types** of FMEA. This is a **frequently tested 3-mark question**.

### (a) System FMEA (SFMEA)
- Conducted at the **conceptual/architectural design** phase.
- Examines the **interactions between subsystems**.
- Highest level of abstraction; fewest details.

### (b) Design FMEA (DFMEA)
- Conducted during **detailed engineering and procurement**.
- Focuses on the **physical design** of each component.
- Considers material selection, tolerances, stress, fatigue, wear.
- **Scope:** What the *product/design itself* can do to fail.

### (c) Process FMEA (PFMEA)
- Conducted during **manufacturing, commissioning, and operations**.
- Focuses on the **manufacturing/operational steps**.
- Considers human errors, machine settings, environmental factors.
- **Scope:** What the *process used to build or operate* the design can do to fail.

### Comparison Matrix (Critical for KTU Board Exams)

| Parameter | SFMEA | DFMEA | PFMEA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase | Concept | Detailed Design | Manufacturing/Operation |
| Subject | Subsystem interfaces | Component design | Process steps |
| Primary Failure | Interface loss | Functional failure | Process variation |
| Example in Process Plant | Compressor–Reactor–Distillation interaction | Heat exchanger tube selection | Welding procedure, start-up sequence |

---

## 2.2 The 10-Step FMEA Procedure (KTU Board Standard)

The standard procedure, derived from **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook** and adopted universally in process plant engineering practice, consists of the following ten sequential steps:

1. **Define the Scope and Boundaries** — Identify the system, its function, and the operating environment.
2. **Assemble a Cross-Functional Team** — Process, Mechanical, Instrumentation, Operations, Safety, Maintenance.
3. **Block Diagram Construction** — Visualize interfaces and the level of analysis.
4. **Identify Functions and Requirements** — *What must the component do?* (e.g., a pump must deliver 50 m³/h at 5 bar).
5. **List All Potential Failure Modes** — Use brainstorming, historical data, similar-failure databases.
6. **Determine Effects and Severity (S)** — Use a standard 1–10 scale.
7. **Determine Causes and Occurrence (O)** — Use a standard 1–10 scale.
8. **Determine Current Process Controls and Detection (D)** — Identify alarms, inspections, diagnostics.
9. **Calculate RPN = S × O × D** — Rank-order failures for action.
10. **Recommend Actions and Re-Calculate RPN** — A continuous improvement loop.

---

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every formula below has appeared in KTU question papers, either directly or as a numerical sub-part. **Memorize the scales and the boundary RPN threshold (typically RPN ≥ 100, or Severity ≥ 9).**

### Master Formula: Risk Priority Number (RPN)

$$
RPN \;=\; S \times O \times D
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Severity Rating ($1 \leq S \leq 10$)
- $O$ = Occurrence Rating ($1 \leq O \leq 10$)
- $D$ = Detection Rating ($1 \leq D \leq 10$)

**Theoretical Maximum RPN:**
$$
RPN_{\max} = 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000
$$

**Theoretical Minimum RPN:**
$$
RPN_{\min} = 1 \times 1 \times 1 = 1
$$

### Critical RPN Threshold Rule (Board Exam Favorite)

> [!WARNING]
> **Action Threshold Rule:** A failure mode **MUST** receive corrective action **if any ONE** of the following conditions is satisfied:
> - $RPN \geq 100$ (general industry rule of thumb), **OR**
> - Severity $S \geq 9$ (regardless of RPN — catastrophic single-point failure).

### Severity Scale (Industry-Standard 1–10)

| Rating | Effect | Description (Process Plant Context) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | None | No effect on system performance. |
| 2 | Very Minor | Slight inconvenience, no damage. |
| 3 | Minor | Loss of minor function, no safety impact. |
| 4 | Low | Minor loss of primary function, repair needed. |
| 5 | Moderate | Loss of primary function, system operable with difficulty. |
| 6 | Significant | Major loss of function; partial shutdown. |
| 7 | High | System inoperable; significant production loss. |
| 8 | Very High | Major process upset; safety system activates. |
| 9 | **Hazardous with warning** | Injury to personnel, major environmental release. |
| 10 | **Catastrophic without warning** | Fatality, mass environmental damage. |

### Occurrence Scale (Industry-Standard 1–10)

| Rating | Failure Rate (per item per year) | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\leq 0.01$ | Extremely unlikely; failure virtually impossible. |
| 2 | 0.1 | Very low failure rate. |
| 3 | 0.5 | Low failure rate. |
| 4 | 1 | Moderate-low. |
| 5 | 2 | Moderate. |
| 6 | 5 | Moderate-high. |
| 7 | 10 | High. |
| 8 | 50 | Very high. |
| 9 | 100 | Extremely high. |
| 10 | $\geq 500$ | Failure nearly inevitable. |

### Detection Scale (Industry-Standard 1–10)

> [!NOTE]
> **Detection rating is INVERTED in logic:** A *high* Detection rating (e.g., 10) means **low probability of detection**, i.e., a *poor* control system.

| Rating | Detection Probability | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Almost Certain | Automatic control with redundancy + alarm. |
| 4 | Moderate | Periodic manual inspection. |
| 7 | Low | Partial detection by operator. |
| 10 | Almost Impossible | No current control; failure not detectable. |

### Modified Action Priority (AIAG-VDA 2019 Update)

The newer AIAG-VDA approach uses an **Action Priority (AP)** table, replacing raw RPN, classified as:
- **AP = High** (red) → Mandatory action.
- **AP = Medium** (yellow) → Action recommended.
- **AP = Low** (green) → Document and monitor.

This is computed using **lookup tables** based on S, O, D cells rather than simple multiplication.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Analysis

## 3.1 Derivation of the RPN Action Threshold Logic

### Mathematical Foundation

The RPN is fundamentally a **weighted product** that approximates risk. In formal reliability engineering, **risk** can be expressed as:

$$
R = f(\text{Consequence}, \text{Likelihood})
$$

FMEA extends this two-variable risk model to a **three-variable** model by introducing a **detection-difficulty term**:

$$
RPN \;\equiv\; f(S, O, D) \;=\; S \times O \times D
$$

### Why Multiplication, Not Addition?

Consider two failure scenarios:
- **Scenario X:** S = 8, O = 8, D = 8 → Sum = 24, Product = **512**
- **Scenario Y:** S = 8, O = 1, D = 8 → Sum = 17, Product = **64**

A simple additive model treats Y as nearly as bad as X, which is wrong: a *rare* failure (O = 1) with catastrophic consequence (S = 8) is far less risky than a *common* failure with the same severity. Multiplication correctly penalizes high-occurrence failures geometrically.

$$
\text{If } O_1 > O_2, \text{ then } \frac{RPN_1}{RPN_2} \;=\; \frac{S \cdot O_1 \cdot D}{S \cdot O_2 \cdot D} \;=\; \frac{O_1}{O_2}
$$

Thus, **the ratio of RPNs directly mirrors the ratio of occurrence probabilities.**

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — A Reactor Cooling Water Pump

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (modelled on KTU board-style numerical):**
> A centrifugal pump delivers cooling water to a jacketed exothermic reactor. An FMEA team has identified the failure mode *"Pump fails to deliver rated flow"* with the following ratings: **S = 9, O = 4, D = 6**.
> **Required:** Calculate the RPN. The current threshold for mandatory action is **RPN ≥ 100 AND S ≥ 8**. Comment on whether corrective action is mandated.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Identify the failure mode.
- Failure Mode: *Pump delivers no flow (mechanical seizure or shaft break).*

**Step 2:** Identify the effect.
- Local Effect: No cooling to reactor jacket.
- System Effect: Reactor temperature rises rapidly.
- End Effect: Runaway exothermic reaction → potential over-pressurization → vessel rupture.
- **Therefore Severity S = 9** (Hazardous with warning — injury or major release is possible).

**Step 3:** Identify the cause and rate it.
- Cause: Bearing failure due to lubrication loss.
- Based on plant historical data: 1 failure per 250 pump-years → equivalent Occurrence Rating **O = 4**.

**Step 4:** Identify the detection control.
- Current Control: A low-flow alarm (single, non-redundant) at the pump discharge, with operator response.
- Probability that the control detects failure before loss event: ~50%.
- **Therefore Detection Rating D = 6.**

**Step 5:** Calculate the RPN.

$$
RPN \;=\; S \times O \times D
$$

$$
RPN \;=\; 9 \times 4 \times 6
$$

$$
RPN \;=\; 36 \times 6 \;=\; 216
$$

**Step 6:** Compare against the threshold rule.

- Threshold rule: $RPN \geq 100$ **AND** $S \geq 8$.
- Computed $RPN = 216 \geq 100$. ✓
- Computed $S = 9 \geq 8$. ✓
- **Both conditions satisfied → Corrective action is MANDATED.**

**Step 7:** Recommended corrective actions (for full marks).
- Install a redundant (1oo2) low-flow switch with voting logic.
- Add automatic isolation valve on cooling water return line.
- Implement a Predictive Maintenance (PdM) program: vibration monitoring and oil analysis every 3 months.

**Step 8:** Re-calculate RPN *after* corrective action.
- Improved Detection (redundant alarm + automatic isolation): **D' = 2**
- Reduced Occurrence (due to PdM): **O' = 2**

$$
RPN' \;=\; 9 \times 2 \times 2 \;=\; 36
$$

The new RPN is **216 → 36**, a reduction of **83.3 %**, which is the engineering value of FMEA.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Severity × Occurrence Decision Matrix

The KTU board exam often asks students to **construct a risk matrix** rather than compute RPN. The standard $5 \times 5$ risk matrix is constructed as follows:

$$
\text{Risk Level} \;=\; f(S, O)
$$

Where each cell is color-coded:

| Sev ↓ / Occ → | O = 1 (Rare) | O = 2 (Unlikely) | O = 3 (Possible) | O = 4 (Likely) | O = 5 (Frequent) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S = 5 (Catastrophic)** | Medium | High | High | Extreme | Extreme |
| **S = 4 (Major)** | Medium | Medium | High | High | Extreme |
| **S = 3 (Moderate)** | Low | Medium | Medium | High | High |
| **S = 2 (Minor)** | Low | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **S = 1 (Negligible)** | Low | Low | Low | Medium | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Extreme" cells require IMMEDIATE action. "High" cells require scheduled action. "Medium" cells require monitoring. "Low" cells are acceptable.**

### Quantitative Translation of Risk Levels

$$
\text{Expected Risk} \;=\; S_{\text{numeric}} \times P_{\text{occurrence}}
$$

$$
\text{Risk}_{\text{cell}}(S,O) \;=\; S \times O \times K_{\text{unit}}
$$

where $K_{\text{unit}}$ is a normalizing constant representing the cost per unit consequence (e.g., $K_{\text{unit}} = \text{INR } 10{,}00{,}000$ per fatality-equivalent).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FMEA Block Diagram — System Architecture of a Process Plant Subsystem

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the structural hierarchy of a typical reactor cooling system, illustrating the *interfaces* that an FMEA team must analyze.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reactor Vessel R-101] --> B[Cooling Jacket Loop]
    B --> C[Centrifugal Pump P-101]
    C --> D[Flow Transmitter FT-101]
    C --> E[Block Valve BV-101]
    C --> F[Heat Exchanger E-101]
    F --> G[Cooling Tower CT-101]
    A -. Temperature Signal .-> T[Temperature Controller TIC-100]
    T -. Trip Signal .-> E
    D -. Low Flow Alarm .-> O[Operator Console]

    classDef critical fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef control fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#06c,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000;
    classDef utility fill:#e6f5d6,stroke:#080,stroke-width:1px,color:#000;

    class A,B,C critical;
    class D,T,O control;
    class E,F,G utility;
```

**Interpretation for FMEA Team:**
- **Red nodes (critical)** are primary targets for DFMEA.
- **Blue nodes (control)** are targets for PFMEA (control loop reliability).
- **Green nodes (utility)** require only a basic SFMEA review.

---

## 4.2 FMEA Process Flow — 10-Step Procedure (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Define Scope and Boundaries] --> S2[Step 2: Assemble Cross-Functional Team]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Construct Block Diagram]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Identify Functions and Specs]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: List Failure Modes per Component]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Assign Effects and Severity S]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Assign Causes and Occurrence O]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Assign Current Controls and Detection D]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Compute RPN equal to S times O times D]
    S9 --> D1{RPN greater than 100 OR S greater than 9}
    D1 -- Yes --> S10[Step 10: Recommend Corrective Action and Recompute RPN]
    S10 --> R1[Updated FMEA Worksheet]
    D1 -- No --> R2[Document and Monitor]
    R1 --> S5
    R2 --> S5

    classDef process fill:#fff5d6,stroke:#a80,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef decision fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef output fill:#d6f5e0,stroke:#080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;

    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7,S8,S9,S10 process;
    class D1 decision;
    class R1,R2 output;
```

**Reading the Loop:** The FMEA is a *living document*. After action implementation (Step 10), the analysis loops back to Step 5, because the new controls may create *new failure modes* that were not present originally.

---

## 4.3 FMEA Worksheet Structure (Standard Columnar Layout)

A standard FMEA worksheet is a **tabular matrix** with the following logical column flow:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Component] --> P2[Function] --> P3[Failure Mode]
    P3 --> P4[Effect on System]
    P4 --> P5[Cause]
    P5 --> P6[S Rating]
    P6 --> P7[O Rating]
    P7 --> P8[D Rating]
    P8 --> P9[RPN]
    P9 --> P10[Recommended Action]
    P10 --> P11[New S O D and RPN]

    classDef start fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#06c,color:#000;
    classDef mid fill:#fff5d6,stroke:#a80,color:#000;
    classDef end fill:#d6f5e0,stroke:#080,color:#000;
    class P1,P2,P3 start;
    class P4,P5,P6,P7,P8 mid;
    class P9,P10,P11 end;
```

In a real Excel/worksheet environment, these eleven logical blocks correspond to eleven columns, with each *row* representing one unique failure mode.

---

## 4.4 Failure Mode Logic Tree (Inductive / Forward Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component Selected for Analysis] --> B{Failure Mode Identified}
    B -- Yes --> C[Identify Local Effect]
    C --> D[Identify System Effect]
    D --> E[Identify End Effect]
    E --> F[Identify Root Cause]
    F --> G[Assign Severity Occurrence Detection]
    G --> H[Compute RPN]
    B -- No --> I[Return to Brainstorming]

    classDef root fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c00,color:#000;
    classDef mid fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#06c,color:#000;
    classDef leaf fill:#d6f5e0,stroke:#080,color:#000;
    class A,B root;
    class C,D,F,G mid;
    class E,H,I leaf;
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **inductive (forward) direction**: Component → Failure → Effect → Cause. This is the hallmark that distinguishes FMEA from FTA (which is deductive: Top Event → Intermediate Events → Basic Events).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q]`
**Define Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA). What are the three principal types of FMEA used in process plant engineering?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** FMEA is a systematic, inductive, bottom-up analytical technique used to identify potential failure modes of a component, system, or process, evaluate their effects and causes, and prioritize corrective actions using a numerical **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** calculated as **S × O × D**, where S = Severity, O = Occurrence, and D = Detection.

> **Three Principal Types:**
> 1. **System FMEA (SFMEA)** — Performed at the conceptual design phase; analyzes subsystem interfaces.
> 2. **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** — Performed during detailed engineering; focuses on the physical product/component design.
> 3. **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** — Performed during manufacturing and operations; focuses on process-induced failures (human error, machine settings).

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the three types.)**

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q]`
**What is a Risk Priority Number (RPN) in FMEA? State the typical threshold value above which a corrective action becomes mandatory.**

**Model Answer:**

> **RPN:** The Risk Priority Number is a quantitative metric used in FMEA to rank-order failure modes by relative risk. It is computed as:
> $$RPN = S \times O \times D$$
> where $S$ is Severity (1–10), $O$ is Occurrence (1–10), and $D$ is Detection (1–10). RPN ranges from **1 to 1000**.

> **Threshold:** A corrective action is typically mandated when **RPN ≥ 100**, OR when **Severity ≥ 9** regardless of RPN (single-point catastrophic failure).

**(3 marks: 2 for formula + 1 for threshold logic.)**

---

## PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation (ESE) regulation**, every Module in a 14-mark question offers an **internal choice**. The two alternatives below are completely independent, mirroring actual KTU question papers.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for conducting a Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA) in a process plant. Discuss the role of the cross-functional team in this procedure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reactor feed pump in a fertilizer plant has been analyzed using FMEA. The failure mode *"Pump shaft seizure causing zero flow"* has been rated with **S = 10, O = 5, D = 7**. Calculate the Risk Priority Number and recommend two specific corrective actions to reduce the RPN to below 80. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

##### Part (a) — FMEA Procedure (7 Marks)

The standard 10-step procedure for conducting FMEA in a process plant is as follows:

**Step 1 — Define Scope and Boundaries (1 mark):**
- Identify the system under analysis (e.g., a reactor feed system).
- Define operating envelope: pressure, temperature, flow rate, fluid properties, environment.
- Specify depth of analysis: which components are in-scope.

**Step 2 — Assemble Cross-Functional Team (1 mark):**
- Process Engineer (defines functions).
- Mechanical Engineer (design details).
- Instrumentation Engineer (control logic).
- Safety Officer (severity assessment).
- Operations & Maintenance (historical failure data).
- A *single-discipline* team is the single most common cause of a poor FMEA.

**Step 3 — Block Diagram Construction (0.5 mark):**
- Visual representation of component interconnections.
- Defines the boundaries of analysis.

**Step 4 — Identify Functions and Specifications (0.5 mark):**
- Each component is described in terms of *what it is supposed to do* (function) and *to what standard* (specification). Example: *"Pump P-101 shall deliver 50 m³/h of cooling water at 5 bar with NPSH ≥ 3 m."*

**Step 5 — List Failure Modes (1 mark):**
- For each component, list *every credible way* it can fail. Common process plant failure modes include:
  - *No flow*, *low flow*, *high flow*, *reverse flow*.
  - *Leak (internal)*, *leak (external)*, *rupture*.
  - *Stuck open*, *stuck closed*, *spurious operation*.
  - *Overpressure*, *underpressure*, *overtemperature*.

**Step 6 — Determine Effects and Severity (1 mark):**
- Trace each failure to its local, system, and end effect.
- Assign **S (1–10)** using the standard severity scale.

**Step 7 — Determine Causes and Occurrence (0.5 mark):**
- Identify root cause(s) for each failure mode.
- Assign **O (1–10)** using plant-specific or industry failure-rate data.

**Step 8 — Determine Current Controls and Detection (0.5 mark):**
- Identify existing alarms, interlocks, inspections, diagnostics.
- Assign **D (1–10)** based on detection probability.

**Step 9 — Calculate RPN = S × O × D (0.5 mark):**
- Compute and rank-order failures.

**Step 10 — Recommend Actions and Recompute (0.5 mark):**
- For RPN ≥ 100 or S ≥ 9, recommend corrective actions.
- Recompute RPN after action implementation.

> **Role of Cross-Functional Team:** Each step above requires domain expertise from multiple disciplines. The process engineer alone cannot identify instrumentation failure modes; the maintenance technician holds crucial historical failure data unavailable to the design engineer. The cross-functional team ensures *completeness* and *credibility* of the FMEA.

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical RPN Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Failure mode: Pump shaft seizure causing zero flow.
- Severity $S = 10$ (Catastrophic without warning — likely fatality / major process disaster).
- Occurrence $O = 5$ (Moderate failure rate).
- Detection $D = 7$ (Low detection probability — alarm only, no automatic trip).

**Step 1: Calculate RPN.**

$$
RPN \;=\; S \times O \times D
$$

$$
RPN \;=\; 10 \times 5 \times 7
$$

$$
RPN \;=\; 50 \times 7 \;=\; 350
$$

**Step 2: Compare with threshold.**
- $RPN = 350 \gg 100$. ✓
- $S = 10 \geq 9$. ✓
- **Corrective action is mandatory.**

**Step 3: Recommend corrective actions (target: $RPN < 80$).**

Since Severity $S$ is a property of the *consequence*, it generally **cannot** be reduced through engineered controls alone; instead, the strategy is to reduce $O$ and $D$.

**Recommended Action 1 — Reduce Occurrence:**
- Implement a **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** program:
  - Vibration analysis every 3 months (CMMS-triggered).
  - Oil analysis and bearing-temperature monitoring.
  - Target: reduce occurrence from $O = 5$ to $O = 2$.
  - **New Occurrence $O' = 2$ (1 mark).**

**Recommended Action 2 — Reduce Detection:**
- Install a **1oo2 (one-out-of-two) voting low-flow transmitter system** with:
  - Redundant flow sensors (FE-101A and FE-101B) on pump discharge.
  - Automatic trip logic to shut down the feed pump on low-flow coincidence.
  - Independent power supply (UPS + battery backup).
- Target: reduce detection rating from $D = 7$ to $D = 2$.
- **New Detection $D' = 2$ (1 mark).**

**Step 4: Recompute RPN after actions.**

$$
RPN' \;=\; S' \times O' \times D'
$$

$$
RPN' \;=\; 10 \times 2 \times 2
$$

$$
RPN' \;=\; 40 \;<\; 80 \quad \text{(Target achieved.)}
$$

**Conclusion:** The combined corrective actions reduce the RPN from **350 to 40**, a reduction of **88.6 %**, bringing the failure mode into the acceptable risk band. *(3 marks: 1.5 each for the two recomputed components + 1 for the final recomputation line and conclusion.)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA) with Fault Tree Analysis (FTA). In what situation would you prefer one over the other? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During an FMEA of a distillation column reflux system, the failure mode *"Reflux pump trips due to power failure"* was identified. Ratings assigned were **S = 9, O = 3, D = 8**. Calculate the RPN. If the company policy mandates action when $RPN \geq 100$, recommend one engineering control and one administrative control to bring RPN below 100. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

##### Part (a) — FMEA vs. FTA (7 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory trick for the exam:** **FMEA = Forwards, FTA = From top.** Both start with a system; they diverge in direction.

| Dimension | FMEA | FTA |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction of logic** | **Inductive (bottom-up)** | **Deductive (top-down)** |
| **Starting point** | Component / basic event | Top-level undesired event (e.g., *column overpressure*) |
| **Representation** | Tabular worksheet | Boolean logic tree (AND/OR gates) |
| **Output** | RPN for each failure mode | Probability of top event (quantitative) + minimal cut sets |
| **Data requirement** | Qualitative + semi-quantitative ratings | Quantitative failure-rate data ($\lambda$) |
| **Strength** | Identifies *many* failure modes simultaneously | Identifies *single* critical scenario in depth |
| **Weakness** | Cannot easily combine multiple failures | Cannot easily cover *all* scenarios |
| **Best applied at** | Component and subsystem design phase | After FMEA has identified a critical scenario |
| **Standards** | IEC 60812, AIAG-VDA | IEC 61025, IEEE Std 352 |

**Situation-based preference (3 marks):**

- **Prefer FMEA when:**
  - You are at the **early design stage** and want a *broad* screening of all components.
  - You need a *qualitative* ranking of risks without extensive failure-rate data.
  - The system has **discrete, identifiable components** (e.g., pumps, valves, vessels).

- **Prefer FTA when:**
  - A **single critical event** has already been identified (e.g., *reactor explosion*) and you need a *deep*, probabilistic analysis.
  - You need to evaluate **combinations of failures** (common-cause, redundancy).
  - Regulatory or insurance requirements demand **quantitative risk numbers** (e.g., SIL determination, IEC 61511).

**In practice:** FMEA is performed *first* (broad), and FTA is performed *after* on the highest-risk scenario identified by FMEA.

---

##### Part (b) — Distillation Column Reflux RPN (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Failure mode: Reflux pump trips due to power failure.
- Severity $S = 9$ (Hazardous with warning — loss of reflux → flooding → product off-spec; possible column damage).
- Occurrence $O = 3$ (Low failure rate).
- Detection $D = 8$ (Very low detection — only consequence visible, not root cause).

**Step 1: Calculate RPN.**

$$
RPN \;=\; S \times O \times D
$$

$$
RPN \;=\; 9 \times 3 \times 8
$$

$$
RPN \;=\; 27 \times 8 \;=\; 216
$$

**Step 2: Compare with threshold.**

$$
RPN = 216 \;\geq\; 100 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Corrective action MANDATED.}
$$

**Step 3: Recommend Engineering Control (3 marks).**

> An **engineering control** modifies the *hardware or instrumentation* of the system.

**Recommendation — Install an Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS) with Automatic Bus Transfer (ABT):**

- Provide an **on-line double-conversion UPS** sized for the reflux pump motor starter circuit (typical sizing: pump FLC × 1.25 × autonomy).
- Include an **Automatic Bus Transfer (ABT)** switch between the main grid and the UPS.
- Tie the UPS to the existing DCS via a *Power-Fail Imminent* (PFI) contact for graceful shutdown or anti-surge sequencing.
- **Expected impact:**
  - Occurrence reduces from $O = 3$ to $O = 1$ (power failure now isolated from pump trip).
  - **New Occurrence $O' = 1$.**

**Step 4: Recommend Administrative Control (3 marks).**

> An **administrative control** modifies *human behavior, procedure, or documentation*.

**Recommendation — Implement a Preventive Maintenance (PM) and Power System Audit Schedule:**

- Monthly inspection of UPS batteries (impedance, voltage).
- Quarterly load-bank testing of the UPS.
- Annual thermographic survey of the main switchgear.
- Document all activities in the Plant Maintenance System (PMS).
- Operator competence verification through yearly re-certification on **Emergency Shutdown (ESD)** procedures.
- **Expected impact:**
  - Detection improves from $D = 8$ to $D = 5$ (predictive maintenance catches deteriorating power components before failure).
  - **New Detection $D' = 5$.**

**Step 5: Recompute RPN.**

$$
RPN' \;=\; S' \times O' \times D'
$$

$$
RPN' \;=\; 9 \times 1 \times 5 \;=\; 45 \;<\; 100
$$

**Conclusion:** The RPN has been reduced from **216 to 45**, satisfying the corporate threshold of RPN < 100. The combination of one engineering control (UPS + ABT) and one administrative control (PM schedule) brings the reflux pump trip failure mode into the acceptable risk band.

*(Final 1 mark: balanced recommendation and computed RPN verification.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks on FMEA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing RPN with AP (Action Priority):** The 2019 AIAG-VDA Handbook introduced Action Priority tables that *replace* raw RPN. If a KTU question specifies "as per AIAG-VDA 2019," using only RPN will lose 1–2 marks. State **both** RPN and AP when in doubt.
>
> 2. **Inverting the Detection scale:** Students forget that **D = 10 means "least detectable,"** not "most detectable." A high D is *bad*. This error cascades into a wrong RPN.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Severity short-circuit rule:** A failure with $S = 10$ but $RPN = 90$ still *requires* action. Many students only compare RPN to threshold.
>
> 4. **Writing "FTA is the same as FMEA":** This guarantees zero marks. They are *complementary*, not identical — different direction, different outputs.
>
> 5. **Skipping the "recurrence" step:** Board questions explicitly ask to "recommend action and recompute RPN." A student who computes only the initial RPN forfeits 3–4 marks out of 14.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **FMEA = Inductive / Bottom-Up / Forward-logic** analytical method.
- ✅ FMEA **starts at the component**, traces failure **up to the system**.
- ✅ Three types: **System FMEA (SFMEA), Design FMEA (DFMEA), Process FMEA (PFMEA)**.
- ✅ Standard procedure: **10 steps**, codified in **IEC 60812** and **AIAG-VDA Handbook**.
- ✅ Master formula:
  $$RPN = S \times O \times D$$
- ✅ Rating range: **1 ≤ S, O, D ≤ 10**; RPN range: **1 to 1000**.
- ✅ **Detection scale is inverted:** Higher D = worse detection.
- ✅ **Mandatory action threshold:** $RPN \geq 100$ **OR** $S \geq 9$.
- ✅ **FMEA vs. FTA:** FMEA = bottom-up, broad, qualitative; FTA = top-down, deep, quantitative.
- ✅ **AIAG-VDA 2019 update:** Use **Action Priority (AP)** tables (High/Medium/Low) alongside or instead of RPN.
- ✅ Recommended corrective actions should **reduce O and D**; reducing S requires fundamental design change.
- ✅ **Always recompute RPN after corrective action** — this is the heart of continuous improvement.
- ✅ FMEA output is a **living document** that must be revisited whenever the design, process, or operating conditions change.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layer of Protection Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Layer of Protection Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** is a **semi-quantitative risk assessment methodology** used in process industries to evaluate the risk of hazardous events. It is a structured, simplified approach that sits between purely qualitative hazard identification methods (e.g., HAZOP) and fully quantitative methods (e.g., Fault Tree Analysis, QRA).

LOPA uses **"order-of-magnitude" estimations** (powers of 10) to determine whether the existing **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** are sufficient to reduce the frequency of an unwanted event to an acceptable (tolerable) level defined by the organization's **risk tolerance criteria**.

According to the **CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety) Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis**, LOPA is the recognized industry tool for determining the **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) per **IEC 61511**.

The fundamental LOPA equation is expressed as:

$$f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{initiating}} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_{i}$$

where $f_{\text{mitigated}}$ is the frequency of the mitigated event, $f_{\text{initiating}}$ is the frequency of the initiating event, and $\text{PFD}_{i}$ is the Probability of Failure on Demand of the $i$-th Independent Protection Layer.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **The Swiss Cheese Model Analogy:** Imagine a stack of Swiss cheese slices. Each slice represents a **protection layer** (a control, alarm, relief device, or operator action). Each slice has holes — these holes represent the **failure probability** of that protection. A hazard reaches a catastrophic outcome **only when the holes in all slices align** simultaneously. LOPA mathematically determines how many slices of cheese (and how big their holes) you need so that a hazardous scenario never reaches a "disastrous hole alignment."

In simpler terms: **A chemical reactor might overheat (initiating event).** You have:
- A temperature controller (Layer 1)
- An operator alarm (Layer 2)
- A high-pressure interlock (Layer 3)
- A relief valve (Layer 4)
- An emergency dump system (Layer 5)

LOPA calculates: *What is the chance ALL these layers fail at the same instant?* If that chance is unacceptably high, you must add or upgrade a protection layer (typically a Safety Instrumented Function — SIF).

## 1.3 Purpose of LOPA in Process Safety

| Purpose | Practical Use |
|---|---|
| Risk Decision-Making | Determines if risk is **As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)** |
| SIL Assignment | Provides defensible basis for IEC 61511 SIL determination |
| Gap Identification | Highlights scenarios where risk reduction is insufficient |
| Resource Allocation | Justifies investment in additional safety systems |
| Regulatory Compliance | Meets OSHA PSM and EU Seveso III expectations |

## 1.4 Standard Frequency Metrics Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard **event frequency unit in LOPA is per year (yr⁻¹)**. Initiating events and IPL failure probabilities are typically expressed in orders of magnitude from **10⁻¹ to 10⁻⁶ per year**.

| Frequency (per year) | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| **10⁻¹** | Frequently expected (e.g., instrument loop failures) |
| **10⁻²** | Reasonably expected (e.g., BPCS loop failure) |
| **10⁻³** | Unusual but possible (e.g., operator error on routine task) |
| **10⁻⁴** | Remote (e.g., large relief valve failure) |
| **10⁻⁵** | Very remote (e.g., secondary rupture) |
| **10⁻⁶** | Extremely remote (industry target for catastrophic events) |

## 1.5 When is LOPA Applied?

LOPA is **NOT applied to every scenario**. It is invoked strategically:
- After a **HAZOP** or **What-If** study identifies scenarios of concern
- For **SIL determination** of new or existing SIFs
- When **qualitative judgement is insufficient** to resolve risk reduction questions
- When **Incident Investigation** reveals that more rigor is needed
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these 8 terms cold for KTU exams — they are examined repeatedly.**

1. **Initiating Event (IE):** The event that starts the chain of events leading to the unwanted consequence. Examples: pump failure, instrument air failure, loss of cooling water, operator opening the wrong valve.

2. **Consequence (C):** The unwanted outcome (e.g., pool fire, toxic release, overpressure rupture, runaway reaction).

3. **Independent Protection Layer (IPL):** A device, system, or action that is capable of preventing a scenario from proceeding to its consequence, **independent of** the initiating event and **independent of** any other IPL in the same scenario.

4. **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD):** The probability that an IPL will not perform its intended function when demanded. Expressed as a dimensionless number between 0 and 1.

5. **Risk Tolerance Criteria (RTC):** The organization's threshold for acceptable frequency of a given consequence severity.

6. **Target Mitigated Event Likelihood (TMEL):** The maximum allowable frequency of the consequence after all IPLs. Set by management policy.

7. **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF):** The reciprocal of PFD. RRF = 1/PFD.

8. **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF):** A specific implementation of instrumentation used to achieve a required SIL. SIL = f(PFD) per IEC 61511.

## 2.2 Independent Protection Layer (IPL) — The Heart of LOPA

A valid IPL must satisfy **ALL** of the following criteria (CCPS, 2001):

- ✅ **Effective:** Reduces the identified risk by a quantifiable amount.
- ✅ **Independent:** Operates independently of the initiating event and other IPLs.
- ✅ **Auditable:** Its PFD can be verified, inspected, or tested.
- ✅ **Single Purpose:** Performs one specific protective function.

### Standard IPL PFD Values (Industry Generic)

| IPL Type | Typical PFD | Comments |
|---|---|---|
| **BPCS (Basic Process Control System)** | **1 × 10⁻¹** | Assumed working unless proven otherwise |
| **Operator Response to Alarm** | **1 × 10⁻¹** | Trained, written procedure required |
| **Safety Instrumented System (SIS) – SIL 1** | **1 × 10⁻¹ to 1 × 10⁻²** | Per IEC 61511 |
| **Safety Instrumented System (SIS) – SIL 2** | **1 × 10⁻² to 1 × 10⁻³** | Per IEC 61511 |
| **Safety Instrumented System (SIS) – SIL 3** | **1 × 10⁻³ to 1 × 10⁻⁴** | Per IEC 61511 |
| **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)** | **1 × 10⁻²** | Sized for full overpressure event |
| **Rupture Disc** | **1 × 10⁻²** | Often used in series with PRV |
| **Dike / Bund / Containment** | **1 × 10⁻²** | Reduces consequence, not likelihood |
| **Inert Gas Purging** | **1 × 10⁻¹** | Prevents ignition source |
| **Earthquake-Proofing** | **1 × 10⁻²** | Structural integrity layer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The **BPCS is NOT considered an IPL** by some standards bodies if it is the same loop that failed as the initiating event. Always check independence!

## 2.3 IEC 61511 Safety Integrity Levels (SIL)

| SIL | PFD Range | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) |
|---|---|---|
| **SIL 1** | **10⁻¹ to 10⁻²** | 10 to 100 |
| **SIL 2** | **10⁻² to 10⁻³** | 100 to 1,000 |
| **SIL 3** | **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁴** | 1,000 to 10,000 |
| **SIL 4** | **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵** | 10,000 to 100,000 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Process industries are limited to SIL 1, 2, or 3.** SIL 4 is generally considered impractical and excessive for process plants — it is more common in nuclear and aerospace.

## 2.4 The LOPA Methodology — 7 Logical Steps

1. **Step 1 — Reference the HAZOP/PHA Scenario:** Identify the consequence, severity, and initiating event.
2. **Step 2 — Select the Frequency of the Initiating Event:** Use company-specific data or generic industry tables.
3. **Step 3 — Identify All Valid IPLs:** Each IPL must pass the 4-criterion test.
4. **Step 4 — Assign PFD to Each IPL:** Use industry-standard generic values.
5. **Step 5 — Calculate Mitigated Event Frequency:** $f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{IE}} \times \prod \text{PFD}_i$.
6. **Step 6 — Compare with TMEL:**
   - If $f_{\text{mitigated}} \leq \text{TMEL}$ → Risk is **acceptable**.
   - If $f_{\text{mitigated}} > \text{TMEL}$ → Add a SIF and recalculate.
7. **Step 7 — Document Findings:** Assumptions, IPLs, and rationale recorded in the LOPA worksheet.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_{\text{mit}} = f_{\text{IE}} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_{i}$ | Multiplicative LOPA model | yr⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{RRF}_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{IE}} \times \prod \text{PFD}_{\text{existing}}}{\text{TMEL}}$ | Risk reduction still required | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} \leq \dfrac{\text{TMEL}}{f_{\text{IE}} \times \prod \text{PFD}_{\text{existing IPLs}}}$ | Required SIF PFD for compliance | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{SIL} = f(\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}})$ | SIL derived from PFD range | categorical |
| 6 | $f_{\text{total}} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} f_{\text{mit},j}$ | Total frequency (multiple paths to same consequence) | yr⁻¹ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Rule:** Never write `\vert x \vert` inside a markdown table — use `$\lvert x \rvert$` outside or use the words "absolute value of x" inline to preserve the table layout.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LOPA is the de-facto SIL determination method** for process safety instrumented systems under IEC 61511.
- It is mandated in companies following **AIChE/CCPS Risk Based Process Safety (RBPS)** framework.
- The **EPC industry** uses LOPA to verify adequacy of safety designs for refinery, petrochemical, and LNG plants.
- The LOPA worksheet is a **legal document** in many jurisdictions (e.g., Norwegian offshore, EU Seveso) for demonstrating ALARP.

## 2.7 Limitations of LOPA

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-favourite discussion point** — LOPA is powerful but has constraints.

- Only order-of-magnitude accuracy (**±1 in log scale**).
- Cannot model **common-cause failures** rigorously.
- Assumes independence of IPLs (often an oversimplification).
- Requires high-quality data; weak data → misleading results.
- Not suitable for **complex dynamic or human-factors-dominant** scenarios.
