# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Example

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Reactor Overpressure Scenario (Full Derivation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical KTU 14-mark question format.** Master this problem.

### Given Problem Statement
> A batch reactor contains a volatile organic compound. The HAZOP team has identified a **"High Pressure" deviation** leading to **vessel rupture with potential fatality**. The following information is available:
> - Frequency of initiating event (loss of cooling water): **$f_{\text{IE}} = 1 \times 10^{-2}$ per year**
> - The reactor has a BPCS pressure control loop: **PFD = $1 \times 10^{-1}$**
> - Operator responds to a high-pressure alarm: **PFD = $1 \times 10^{-1}$**
> - A pressure relief valve (PRV) is installed: **PFD = $1 \times 10^{-2}$**
> - A dike surrounds the reactor: **PFD = $1 \times 10^{-2}$**
> - **Target Mitigated Event Likelihood (TMEL) = $1 \times 10^{-6}$ per year** (company policy for fatal events)

### Solution — Step 1: State the Formula

$$f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{IE}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{BPCS}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{Alarm}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{PRV}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{Dike}}$$

### Solution — Step 2: Substitute Known Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{mitigated}} &= (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 3: Add the Exponents (Log-Scale Property)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(f_{\text{mitigated}}) &= \log_{10}(10^{-2}) + \log_{10}(10^{-1}) + \log_{10}(10^{-1}) + \log_{10}(10^{-2}) + \log_{10}(10^{-2}) \\
\log_{10}(f_{\text{mitigated}}) &= -2 + (-1) + (-1) + (-2) + (-2) \\
\log_{10}(f_{\text{mitigated}}) &= -8
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 4: Convert Back to Frequency

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-8} \text{ yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 5: Compare with TMEL

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{mitigated}} &= 10^{-8} \text{ yr}^{-1} \\
\text{TMEL} &= 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1} \\
f_{\text{mitigated}} &< \text{TMEL}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 6: Conclusion

> **Result:** Since $f_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-8} \text{ yr}^{-1} < \text{TMEL} = 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}$, the **risk is ACCEPTABLE** and the existing layers of protection are **sufficient**. **No additional SIF is required.**

### Verification — Risk Reduction Factor

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RRF}_{\text{total}} &= \frac{f_{\text{IE}}}{f_{\text{mitigated}}} = \frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-8}} = 10^{6} \\
\text{Margin of Safety} &= \frac{\text{TMEL}}{f_{\text{mitigated}}} = \frac{10^{-6}}{10^{-8}} = 100 \text{ (two orders of magnitude margin)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — SIL Determination (Modified Scenario)

### Modified Problem Statement
> Same reactor, but now the operator alarm is **missing** and the dike is **not present**. The PRV remains, and the BPCS loop is functional. Determine the **required SIL** for an additional SIF to be installed.

### Given Data
- $f_{\text{IE}} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \text{ yr}^{-1}$
- BPCS PFD = $1 \times 10^{-1}$
- PRV PFD = $1 \times 10^{-2}$
- TMEL = $1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}$

### Solution — Step 1: Calculate Frequency With Existing Layers

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{mitigated,existing}} &= f_{\text{IE}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{BPCS}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{PRV}} \\
f_{\text{mitigated,existing}} &= (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \\
f_{\text{mitigated,existing}} &= 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 2: Compare with TMEL

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{mitigated,existing}} = 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1} &> \text{TMEL} = 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1} \\
\text{Risk is NOT acceptable.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 3: Determine Required SIF PFD

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{IE}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{BPCS}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{PRV}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} &\leq \text{TMEL} \\
10^{-5} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} &\leq 10^{-6} \\
\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} &\leq 10^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 4: Determine the SIL

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} \leq 10^{-1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SIL} = \mathbf{SIL\ 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 5: Verification

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{final}} &= 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1} = 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1} = \text{TMEL} \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 6: Conclusion

> **A SIL 1 Safety Instrumented Function is required** to bring the risk to the company tolerance. The SIF could be a hardwired high-pressure transmitter and shutdown valve with proof testing at appropriate intervals.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — LOPA Decision Flow

| Step | Question | If YES | If NO |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Is the scenario identified in PHA? | Proceed to Step 2 | Stop — return to PHA |
| 2 | Is the IE frequency known/documented? | Proceed to Step 3 | Use generic data, flag uncertainty |
| 3 | Are all IPLs independent? | Proceed to Step 4 | Combine into one layer OR disqualify |
| 4 | Is each IPL auditable/testable? | Proceed to Step 5 | Disqualify as IPL |
| 5 | $f_{\text{mit}} \leq$ TMEL? | Risk acceptable — document | Proceed to Step 6 |
| 6 | Add a SIF — recalculate? | New SIF provides required PFD | Add another layer (multiple layers) |

---

## 3.4 Multi-Scenario Aggregation Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU trick question:** "What if 3 different initiating events all lead to the same fatal consequence?"

If three initiating events (IE₁, IE₂, IE₃) each lead to a fatal overpressure, the **total frequency** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{total}} = f_{\text{IE}_1} \times \prod \text{PFD}_i \; + \; f_{\text{IE}_2} \times \prod \text{PFD}_i \; + \; f_{\text{IE}_3} \times \prod \text{PFD}_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked numerical case:**
- IE₁: Loss of cooling → $f = 10^{-2}$, PFD chain = $10^{-3}$ → $f_{\text{mit,1}} = 10^{-5}$
- IE₂: Pump failure → $f = 10^{-1}$, PFD chain = $10^{-3}$ → $f_{\text{mit,2}} = 10^{-4}$
- IE₃: Operator error → $f = 10^{-1}$, PFD chain = $10^{-3}$ → $f_{\text{mit,3}} = 10^{-4}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{total}} &= 10^{-5} + 10^{-4} + 10^{-4} \\
f_{\text{total}} &= 0.00001 + 0.0001 + 0.0001 \\
f_{\text{total}} &= 0.00021 \text{ yr}^{-1} \approx 2.1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **dominant contribution** comes from the IE with the highest product. The order of magnitude is $10^{-4}$, which is two orders of magnitude **above** TMEL = $10^{-6}$. → **Action required.**

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LOPA Methodology Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PHA Step: HAZOP/What-If Identifies Scenario] --> B[Define Consequence and Severity Category]
    B --> C[Identify Initiating Event IE]
    C --> D[Assign Generic Failure Frequency fIE]
    D --> E[List All Candidate Protection Layers]
    E --> F{Independence Test}
    F -- Fail --> G[Disqualify as IPL]
    F -- Pass --> H{Effectiveness Test}
    H -- Fail --> G
    H -- Pass --> I{Auditability Test}
    I -- Fail --> G
    I -- Pass --> J[Assign PFD Value from Industry Data]
    J --> K[Calculate fmitigated fIE x Product of PFDs]
    K --> L{Compare fmitigated vs TMEL}
    L -- fmit LessEq TMEL --> M[Risk Acceptable: Document and Close]
    L -- fmit Greater TMEL --> N[Add SIF or Add New IPL]
    N --> O[Determine Required PFD for SIF]
    O --> P[Assign SIL based on PFD Range]
    P --> K
```

## 4.2 Independent Protection Layer Onion Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SC[Scenario Consequence: Vessel Rupture] --> PL5
    PL5[Layer 5: Emergency Response and Evacuation] --> PL4
    PL4[Layer 4: Dike and Containment Physical] --> PL3
    PL3[Layer 3: Pressure Relief Valve PRV] --> PL2
    PL2[Layer 2: Safety Instrumented System SIF] --> PL1
    PL1[Layer 1: BPCS and Alarms] --> IE
    IE[Initiating Event: Loss of Cooling Water] --> SC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Onion Model:** The center of the onion is the **process itself** (where the initiating event originates). Each outward ring represents an **added layer of protection**. For the consequence to occur, the hazard must "penetrate" every layer. The further out, the more robust the defence.

## 4.3 LOPA Risk Decision Matrix (Severity vs. Frequency)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Severity
        S1[Catastrophic: Fatality] --> S2[Serious: Permanent Injury] --> S3[Major: Lost Time Injury] --> S4[Minor: First Aid]
    end
    subgraph Action
        A1[TMEL = 1E-6] --> A2[TMEL = 1E-5] --> A3[TMEL = 1E-4] --> A4[TMEL = 1E-3]
    end
    S1 --- A1
    S2 --- A2
    S3 --- A3
    S4 --- A4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Severity increases from right to left; the required TMEL becomes more stringent (smaller).** The LOPA output ($f_{\text{mitigated}}$) must be at or below the corresponding TMEL.

## 4.4 IPL Independence Test Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify a Candidate Protection Layer] --> Q1{Does it depend on the same sensor as IE?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Not Independent: Disqualify]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Does it share the same power source as IE?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Not Independent: Disqualify]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Does it share the same logic solver as another IPL?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Not Independent: Disqualify]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Does it rely on same operator actioning two protections?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> R4[Not Independent: Disqualify]
    Q4 -- No --> OK[Valid IPL: Assign PFD]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
> **Define Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA). Mention its position in the spectrum of risk analysis methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
LOPA is a **semi-quantitative risk assessment methodology** that uses order-of-magnitude estimates to evaluate whether existing Independent Protection Layers (IPLs) reduce the frequency of an unwanted event to an acceptable (tolerable) level.

In the spectrum of risk analysis methods, LOPA lies **between qualitative methods (HAZOP, What-If)** and **fully quantitative methods (Fault Tree Analysis, Event Tree Analysis, QRA)**. It is the standard industry tool for determining the **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** of a Safety Instrumented Function as per **IEC 61511**.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for spectrum position, 1 mark for SIL/standard mention.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]
> **List and briefly explain the four essential criteria that a protection layer must satisfy to qualify as an Independent Protection Layer (IPL) in LOPA.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A valid IPL must satisfy the following four criteria (CCPS Guidelines):
1. **Effective** — It must actually reduce the identified risk by a measurable amount. *(0.75 mark)*
2. **Independent** — It must operate independently of the initiating event and of every other IPL in the scenario. *(0.75 mark)*
3. **Auditable** — Its PFD must be verifiable by inspection, testing, or calculation. *(0.75 mark)*
4. **Single Purpose** — It must perform one specific protective function, not be used for control and protection simultaneously. *(0.75 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 3 questions are choice-based. You must answer **EITHER Question A OR Question B**. Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with sub-parts typically divided as **7 + 7 marks**.

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
> A solvent storage tank is subject to a "High Level" deviation. The HAZOP team has identified the following:
> - Initiating Event: Level transmitter failure → $f_{\text{IE}} = 1 \times 10^{-1}$ per year
> - BPCS level control loop: PFD = $1 \times 10^{-1}$
> - Operator response to high-level alarm: PFD = $1 \times 10^{-1}$
> - Independent high-level switch + shutdown valve (existing SIF): PFD = $1 \times 10^{-2}$
> - Bund wall containment: PFD = $1 \times 10^{-2}$
> - Company TMEL for major environmental spill = $1 \times 10^{-5}$ per year

### Part (a) [7 Marks]
> **Calculate the mitigated event frequency and determine if the existing layers of protection meet the company TMEL.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the LOPA multiplicative model.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{mitigated}} &= f_{\text{IE}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{BPCS}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{Alarm}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{Bund}} \\
f_{\text{mitigated}} &= (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Add the exponents.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(f_{\text{mitigated}}) &= -1 + (-1) + (-1) + (-2) + (-2) = -7 \\
f_{\text{mitigated}} &= 1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with TMEL.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-7} \text{ yr}^{-1} &< \text{TMEL} = 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1} \\
\text{Excess margin} &= 10^{-5} / 10^{-7} = 100 \text{ (two orders of magnitude)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion: **The risk is acceptable. The existing layers of protection are more than sufficient.** No additional SIF is required. Document and close the LOPA worksheet.

*(Award: 2 marks for formula, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for correct calculation, 1 mark for comparison and conclusion.)*

### Part (b) [7 Marks]
> **What if the BPCS loop and the operator alarm are both disqualified as IPLs due to common-cause failure? Recalculate the mitigated frequency and recommend a SIL if a new SIF is needed.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** With BPCS and Alarm removed, only SIF and Bund remain.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{mitigated,existing}} &= f_{\text{IE}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{Bund}} \\
f_{\text{mitigated,existing}} &= (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \\
f_{\text{mitigated,existing}} &= 1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compare with TMEL.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{mitigated,existing}} = 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1} = \text{TMEL} = 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

The risk is **borderline acceptable (exactly meets TMEL)** with no safety margin. Best practice (ALARP) requires a safety margin of at least 0.5 to 1 order of magnitude.

**Step 3:** To be safely below TMEL, add a new SIF.

For $f_{\text{new SIF}}$ to drive $f_{\text{mitigated}} \leq 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
10^{-5} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{new SIF}} &\leq 10^{-6} \\
\text{PFD}_{\text{new SIF}} &\leq 10^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** From the SIL table: PFD between $10^{-1}$ and $10^{-2}$ corresponds to **SIL 1**.

**Step 5:** Conclusion: **A SIL 1 SIF (e.g., dedicated high-level switch + shutdown valve with proof test interval ≤ 1 year) is recommended** to provide a comfortable ALARP margin below the company TMEL.

*(Award: 2 marks for recompute, 1 mark for comparison, 2 marks for required PFD, 1 mark for SIL identification, 1 mark for recommendation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
> A jacketed exothermic reactor is being assessed for thermal runaway. The following LOPA data is collected from a HAZOP:
> - Initiating Event: Loss of agitation → $f_{\text{IE}} = 1 \times 10^{-1}$ per year
> - BPCS temperature control: PFD = $1 \times 10^{-1}$
> - Operator intervention on high-temperature alarm: PFD = $1 \times 10^{-1}$
> - Existing emergency cooling water injection (mechanical): PFD = $1 \times 10^{-2}$
> - Quench system (manual activation): PFD = $1 \times 10^{-1}$
> - Bunded reactor bay: PFD = $1 \times 10^{-2}$
> - TMEL for runaway = $1 \times 10^{-6}$ per year

### Part (a) [7 Marks]
> **Calculate the mitigated event frequency and verify whether the company risk tolerance is met.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the LOPA formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{mitigated}} &= f_{\text{IE}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{BPCS}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{Alarm}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{Cooling}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{Quench}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{Bund}} \\
f_{\text{mitigated}} &= (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Add the exponents.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(f_{\text{mitigated}}) &= -1 + (-1) + (-1) + (-2) + (-1) + (-2) = -8 \\
f_{\text{mitigated}} &= 1 \times 10^{-8} \text{ yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-8} \text{ yr}^{-1} &< \text{TMEL} = 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1} \\
\text{Margin} &= 100 \times \text{TMEL}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** **Risk acceptable. Existing layers are sufficient. No additional SIF is necessary.**

*(Award marks as 2+2+2+1.)*

### Part (b) [7 Marks]
> **If the emergency cooling water injection fails and the quench system is removed from IPL list (manual system disqualified due to time delay > 30 min), determine the SIL required for a new SIF.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Remaining IPLs: BPCS, Alarm, Bund.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{mitigated,existing}} &= (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \\
f_{\text{mitigated,existing}} &= 1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compare with TMEL: $10^{-5} > 10^{-6}$, so **risk NOT acceptable.**

**Step 3:** Required SIF PFD.

$$
\begin{aligned}
10^{-5} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} &\leq 10^{-6} \\
\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} &\leq 10^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** PFD $\leq 10^{-1}$ → **SIL 1** SIF required.

**Step 5:** Recommended SIF: A hardwired high-temperature trip with redundant sensors (1oo2) and a shutdown valve. Proof test interval: **annually** to maintain PFD $\leq 10^{-1}$.

*(Award 2 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers**
> - **Adding instead of multiplying PFDs** (catastrophic error; full 7 marks lost).
> - **Forgetting to add exponents** — students often multiply $10^{-1} \times 10^{-2}$ and write $10^{-1}$ instead of $10^{-3}$.
> - **Ignoring the "independence" rule** — if a BPCS loop and an alarm share a sensor, both are disqualified; LOPA marks drop sharply.
> - **Confusing TMEL with unmitigated frequency** — TMEL is the **post-mitigation** allowable frequency.
> - **Skipping units** — write **yr⁻¹** explicitly; KTU examiners deduct half a mark for sloppy units.
> - **Ignoring the 0.5 to 1 order-of-magnitude safety margin** — even if $f_{\text{mit}} = $ TMEL, the answer is "borderline, ALARP requires a margin" in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this section the night before the exam — it is the dense summary of everything above.**

- **LOPA = Semi-quantitative** risk analysis using order-of-magnitude estimates.
- **Used for:** SIL determination (IEC 61511) and ALARP demonstration.
- **Master equation:** $f_{\text{mit}} = f_{\text{IE}} \times \prod \text{PFD}_i$.
- **Log rule:** Multiply probabilities → **add** the exponents.
- **IPL must be:** Effective, Independent, Auditable, Single-purpose.
- **Standard PFDs:** BPCS = $10^{-1}$; Operator alarm = $10^{-1}$; PRV = $10^{-2}$; Bund = $10^{-2}$; SIL 1/2/3 SIF = $10^{-1}$–$10^{-4}$.
- **SIL bands:** SIL 1: $10^{-1}$–$10^{-2}$; SIL 2: $10^{-2}$–$10^{-3}$; SIL 3: $10^{-3}$–$10^{-4}$.
- **Decision rule:** $f_{\text{mit}} \leq$ TMEL → Accept; $f_{\text{mit}} >$ TMEL → Add SIF or another IPL.
- **Required SIF PFD formula:** $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} \leq \dfrac{\text{TMEL}}{f_{\text{IE}} \times \prod \text{PFD}_{\text{existing}}}$.
- **Multiple IEs, same consequence:** $f_{\text{total}} = \sum f_{\text{mit},j}$ (do NOT multiply across different IEs).
- **Process industry SIL cap:** SIL 3 (SIL 4 is for nuclear/aerospace).
- **Independence test:** Different sensor, power source, logic solver, operator.
- **Best practice margin:** 0.5 to 1 order of magnitude below TMEL (ALARP).
- **LOPA limitations:** Order-of-magnitude only; cannot model common-cause; assumes IPL independence; semi-quantitative.
- **LOPA workflow:** PHA scenario → IE frequency → IPL list with PFDs → Multiply → Compare with TMEL → Add SIF if needed → Document.
- **Document name:** LOPA Worksheet (legal/auditable record).
- **Position in risk spectrum:** HAZOP (qualitative) < LOPA (semi-quantitative) < FTA/ETA/QRA (quantitative).
- **Reference standards:** CCPS Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis (2nd ed.), IEC 61511/61508, AIChE/CCPS RBPS.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Examples # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Introduction to Worked Examples in Module 3 > [!NOTE] > **Module 3 Focus**: Hazard Identification, Risk Assessment, and Quantitative Safety Analysis. The "Examples" topic consolidates numerical applications of **Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)**, **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**, and **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** — all of which are **high-weightage KTU board exam topics**. ### Why "Examples" Matter in KTU Examinations In the KTU 2024 Scheme under NEP 2020, **Part B questions (14 marks)** in Process Safety Engineering heavily feature numerical problem-solving. Students are expected to: 1. Apply the Dow F&EI methodology to a given process unit. 2. Construct and quantify Fault Trees using Boolean algebra. 3. Develop Event Trees and compute branch-point probabilities. 4. Interpret the final safety indices against **Dow's severity ratings**. ### Conceptual Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Process Plant as a House of Cards"** > Imagine a process plant as a carefully stacked house of cards. Each card represents a piece of equipment (vessel, pump, valve). A small disturbance (a basic event in FTA) can topple the entire structure (the top event). **Fault Tree Analysis** calculates how stable the house is by multiplying the probabilities of each card falling. **Event Tree Analysis** traces where the falling cards land — does the structure collapse safely (small fire) or catastrophically (vapor cloud explosion)? The **Dow F&EI** assigns a numerical "risk score" to each stack, telling us how dangerous the configuration is before the first card is even touched. ### Key Definitions Aligned with KTU Syllabus > [!NOTE] > **Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)**: A quantitative ranking system developed by the Dow Chemical Company to evaluate the potential fire and explosion damage of a process unit. It computes a single numerical score that classifies the unit's fire and explosion risk. > > **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**: A top-down, deductive failure analysis in which an undesired state of the system (top event) is analyzed using Boolean logic to find combinations of basic events that cause it. > > **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)**: A bottom-up, inductive analysis that traces the possible outcomes following an initiating event, considering the success or failure of successive safety functions. ### Standard Reference Metrics Used in Module 3 The following constants and standard metrics are universally used across all Module 3 examples: - **Failure Rate (λ)**: typically expressed in **failures per year (fpy)** or **failures per 10⁶ hours**. - **Unavailability (Q)**: dimensionless, ranges from **0 to 1**. - **Probability (P)**: dimensionless, ranges from **0 to 1**. - **Dow F&EI Rating Bands**: - **1–60**: Light - **61–96**: Moderate - **97–127**: Heavy - **128–158**: Extreme - **≥159**: Very Extreme # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of the Three Core Example Types ### A. Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI) — Theory The Dow F&EI is computed through a hierarchical factor-multiplication approach: 1. **Material Factor (MF)**: A measure of the intrinsic energy release potential of the material. Selected from a Dow reference table based on flammability (NF) and reactivity (NR). 2. **Process Unit Hazard Factor (F₃)**: Product of two sub-factors — General Process Hazards (F₁) and Special Process Hazards (F₂). 3. **Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)**: The master score computed as `MF × F₃`. 4. **Radius of Exposure (R)**: Geometric radius of the damaging circle. 5. **Area of Exposure (A)**: Geometric area of the damaging circle. 6. **Maximum Probable Property Damage (MPPD)**: Dollar value of expected damage. 7. **Maximum Probable Days Outage (MPDO)**: Expected interruption duration in days. ### B. Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) — Theory The logical structure is built from **gates**: - **AND Gate**: Output occurs **only if ALL** inputs occur. - Probability: $P_{AND} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ - **OR Gate**: Output occurs **if ANY** input occurs. - Probability: $P_{OR} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - P_i)$ The **top event probability** is computed by reducing the tree through Boolean algebra into a sum-of-products (minimal cut sets) form. ### C. Event Tree Analysis (ETA) — Theory ETA starts with an **initiating event** and proceeds through a series of **branch points** (success/failure of safety barriers). Each path yields a specific **outcome** with a probability equal to the product of branch-point probabilities along that path. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Table** — memorize before the exam. **Use `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent markdown table breakage.** | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variable Definitions | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Fire & Explosion Index | $F\&EI = MF \times F_3$ | MF = Material Factor, F₃ = Unit Hazard Factor | Dimensionless | | Process Unit Hazard Factor | $F_3 = F_1 \times F_2$ | F₁ = General Process Hazard, F₂ = Special Process Hazard | Dimensionless | | Radius of Exposure | $R = 0.256 \times F\&EI$ | R = damage radius | meters (m) | | Area of Exposure | $A = \pi R^2$ | A = damage area | m² | | Damage Factor | $DF = \left(\dfrac{F\&EI}{100}\right)^{0.6}$ | DF = dimensionless damage factor | Dimensionless | | MPPD | $MPPD = 0.85 \times RC \times DF$ | RC = Replacement Cost (USD) | USD | | AND Gate Probability | $P_{AND} = P_1 \times P_2 \times \cdots \times P_n$ | n = number of inputs | Dimensionless | | OR Gate Probability | $P_{OR} = 1 - (1-P_1)(1-P_2)\cdots(1-P_n)$ | n = number of inputs | Dimensionless | | ETA Path Probability | $P_{path} = P_{initiator} \times \prod P_{branch\,i}$ | Initiator × all branch probabilities | Dimensionless | | Failure Rate to Probability | $P \approx \lambda t$ (for small λt) | λ = failure rate, t = mission time | fpy or hr⁻¹ | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility These three tools form the **backbone of process safety management** worldwide: - **Dow F&EI** is used by **over 100 chemical and petrochemical companies** globally for **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119. - **FTA** is mandated by **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511** for **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** design in chemical plants. - **ETA** is used in **nuclear safety**, **offshore oil & gas**, and **LNG facility design** to evaluate escalation probabilities. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip**: Always state the **units of every numerical answer**. Mark loss occurs when students write `R = 12.5` without specifying meters. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 EXAMPLE 1 — Dow Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI) Calculation ### Problem Statement > A naphtha storage unit in a refinery handles a hydrocarbon with **NF = 3** and **NR = 1**. From the Dow reference table, the **Material Factor MF = 16**. The penalties contributing to the General Process Hazard Factor (F₁) are: > - Exothermic reaction penalty = 0.30 > - Endothermic reaction penalty = 0.00 > - Material handling penalty = 0.50 > - Enclosed / indoor process penalty = 0.30 > > The penalties contributing to the Special Process Hazard Factor (F₂) are: > - Toxic material penalty = 0.20 > - Sub-atmospheric pressure (≤ 500 mmHg) = 0.50 > - Operation near flammable range penalty = 0.80 > - Quantity penalty = 0.10 > - Corrosion / erosion penalty = 0.30 > > Compute: **(a) F₁, (b) F₂, (c) F₃, (d) F&EI, (e) Radius of Exposure R, (f) Area of Exposure A, (g) Damage Factor DF, (h) MPPD assuming Replacement Cost RC = $50 million, (i) Comment on the fire/explosion severity.** --- ### Step-by-Step Solution #### Part (a) — General Process Hazard Factor (F₁) The General Process Hazard Factor is **1.0 plus the sum of all general process penalties**: $$F_1 = 1.0 + \sum (\text{General Process Penalties})$$ Substituting the given penalty values: $$F_1 = 1.0 + (0.30 + 0.00 + 0.50 + 0.30)$$ $$F_1 = 1.0 + 1.10 = 2.10$$ > **Logic**: F₁ accounts for *routine* operational hazards. It is always **≥ 1.0** because the base value (1.0) represents normal operation. Each penalty increases the factor multiplicatively downstream. --- #### Part (b) — Special Process Hazard Factor (F₂) Similarly: $$F_2 = 1.0 + \sum (\text{Special Process Penalties})$$ $$F_2 = 1.0 + (0.20 + 0.50 + 0.80 + 0.10 + 0.30)$$ $$F_2 = 1.0 + 1.90 = 2.90$$ > **Logic**: F₂ captures *exceptional* hazards such as operation near flammability limits, low-pressure storage, and corrosion. These can multiply risk dramatically. --- #### Part (c) — Process Unit Hazard Factor (F₃) $$F_3 = F_1 \times F_2$$ $$F_3 = 2.10 \times 2.90 = 6.09$$ --- #### Part (d) — Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI) $$F\&EI = MF \times F_3$$ $$F\&EI = 16 \times 6.09 = 97.44$$ --- #### Part (e) — Radius of Exposure (R) $$R = 0.256 \times F\&EI$$ $$R = 0.256 \times 97.44 = 24.945 \text{ m}$$ $$R \approx 24.95 \text{ m}$$ > **Physical Meaning**: A circle of radius **~25 m** centered on the naphtha unit represents the boundary within which significant fire/explosion damage is expected. --- #### Part (f) — Area of Exposure (A) $$A = \pi R^2$$ $$A = \pi \times (24.945)^2$$ $$A = \pi \times 622.25 = 1954.74 \text{ m}^2$$ --- #### Part (g) — Damage Factor (DF) $$DF = \left(\dfrac{F\&EI}{100}\right)^{0.6}$$ $$DF = \left(\dfrac{97.44}{100}\right)^{0.6}$$ $$DF = (0.9744)^{0.6}$$ Computing the exponent: $$0.9744^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \times \ln(0.9744)} = e^{0.6 \times (-0.02594)} = e^{-0.01556} = 0.9846$$ $$DF \approx 0.985$$ --- #### Part (h) — Maximum Probable Property Damage (MPPD) $$MPPD = 0.85 \times RC \times DF$$ $$MPPD = 0.85 \times 50{,}000{,}000 \times 0.985$$ $$MPPD = 41{,}862{,}500 \text{ USD}$$ $$\boxed{MPPD \approx \$41.86 \text{ million}}$$ --- #### Part (i) — Severity Classification From the Dow severity bands: | F&EI Range | Severity | |---|---| | 1 – 60 | Light | | 61 – 96 | Moderate | | **97 – 127** | **Heavy** | | 128 – 158 | Extreme | | ≥ 159 | Very Extreme | Since F&EI = **97.44**, the unit falls in the **Heavy** severity category. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: Students frequently forget to add the base value of **1.0** when computing F₁ and F₂. Always write `F = 1.0 + Σ(penalties)`. Marks are docked if the base is missing. --- ## 3.2 EXAMPLE 2 — Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) with AND/OR Gates ### Problem Statement > A reactor cooling system failure (Top Event **T**) can occur if **either**: > - **Pump failure (P)** occurs **OR** > - **Coolant supply failure (C)** occurs > > Furthermore: > - Pump failure (P) occurs if **both** Motor fails (M) **AND** Power supply fails (S). > - Coolant supply failure (C) occurs if **both** Valve V₁ fails **AND** Valve V₂ fails. > > Given failure probabilities per year: > - P(M) = 0.02 > - P(S) = 0.03 > - P(V₁) = 0.01 > - P(V₂) = 0.015 > > **Calculate the probability of the Top Event T per year.** --- ### Step-by-Step Solution #### Step 1 — Construct the Fault Tree (Boolean Form) **Top Event T** = P ∪ C (OR gate at the top) **P** = M ∩ S (AND gate) **C** = V₁ ∩ V₂ (AND gate) So the tree is: $$T = (M \cap S) \cup (V_1 \cap V_2)$$ --- #### Step 2 — Compute the AND Gate Probabilities **For P (Pump failure):** $$P(P) = P(M) \times P(S) = 0.02 \times 0.03 = 0.0006$$ **For C (Coolant failure):** $$P(C) = P(V_1) \times P(V_2) = 0.01 \times 0.015 = 0.00015$$ > **Logic**: AND gates represent **independent, simultaneous** events. Their joint probability is the simple product of individual probabilities (assuming independence, which is the standard KTU assumption unless stated otherwise). --- #### Step 3 — Compute the Top OR Gate Probability $$P(T) = 1 - [1 - P(P)] \times [1 - P(C)]$$ $$P(T) = 1 - (1 - 0.0006) \times (1 - 0.00015)$$ $$P(T) = 1 - (0.9994) \times (0.99985)$$ Computing the product: $$0.9994 \times 0.99985 = 0.99925 + \epsilon$$ Detailed multiplication: $$(1 - 0.0006)(1 - 0.00015) = 1 - 0.0006 - 0.00015 + (0.0006 \times 0.00015)$$ $$= 1 - 0.00075 + 0.00000009 = 0.99925009$$ Therefore: $$P(T) = 1 - 0.99925009 = 0.00074991$$ $$\boxed{P(T) \approx 7.50 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per year}}$$ --- #### Step 4 — Interpretation The probability of cooling-system failure is approximately **0.075% per year**, or equivalently about **1 failure in 1,333 years**. This is a relatively safe design but may require additional redundancy depending on the **SIL target**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: A common mistake is writing `P(T) = P(P) + P(C)`. This is **wrong** for an OR gate unless events are mutually exclusive (which is rare in safety analysis). Use the **complement product** formula always. --- ## 3.3 EXAMPLE 3 — Event Tree Analysis (ETA) ### Problem Statement > A pipeline rupture (Initiating Event **I**) occurs with probability **P(I) = 1 × 10⁻³ per year**. Two independent safety barriers exist: > > - **Barrier 1**: Emergency Shut-Down Valve (ESD) — succeeds with probability **P(S₁) = 0.99**, fails with probability **P(F₁) = 0.01**. > - **Barrier 2**: Flame Arrestor (FA) — succeeds with probability **P(S₂) = 0.95**, fails with probability **P(F₂) = 0.05**. > > Outcomes: > - **Path A** (S₁, S₂) → Small leak, no fire > - **Path B** (S₁, F₂) → Gas release, no ignition > - **Path C** (F₁, S₂) → Major release, no fire > - **Path D** (F₁, F₂) → Major release with fire (Catastrophic) > > **Calculate the probability of each outcome and identify the most critical scenario.** --- ### Step-by-Step Solution #### Step 1 — Construct the Event Tree ``` ┌── S₂ (0.95) → Path A ┌── S₁ (0.99)─┤ │ └── F₂ (0.05) → Path B I (10⁻³) ──┤ │ ┌── S₂ (0.95) → Path C └── F₁ (0.01)─┤ └── F₂ (0.05) → Path D ``` #### Step 2 — Compute Each Path Probability The probability of any path = P(I) × product of branch probabilities along that path. **Path A** (S₁, S₂): $$P_A = P(I) \times P(S_1) \times P(S_2)$$ $$P_A = 10^{-3} \times 0.99 \times 0.95$$ $$P_A = 10^{-3} \times 0.9405 = 9.405 \times 10^{-4}$$ --- **Path B** (S₁, F₂): $$P_B = P(I) \times P(S_1) \times P(F_2)$$ $$P_B = 10^{-3} \times 0.99 \times 0.05$$ $$P_B = 10^{-3} \times 0.0495 = 4.95 \times 10^{-5}$$ --- **Path C** (F₁, S₂): $$P_C = P(I) \times P(F_1) \times P(S_2)$$ $$P_C = 10^{-3} \times 0.01 \times 0.95$$ $$P_C = 10^{-3} \times 0.0095 = 9.5 \times 10^{-6}$$ --- **Path D** (F₁, F₂): $$P_D = P(I) \times P(F_1) \times P(F_2)$$ $$P_D = 10^{-3} \times 0.01 \times 0.05$$ $$P_D = 10^{-3} \times 0.0005 = 5.0 \times 10^{-7}$$ --- #### Step 3 — Verification (Sum Must Equal P(I)) $$\sum P_{paths} = P_A + P_B + P_C + P_D$$ $$= 9.405 \times 10^{-4} + 4.95 \times 10^{-5} + 9.5 \times 10^{-6} + 5.0 \times 10^{-7}$$ Converting to common exponent (10⁻⁴): $$= 9.405 \times 10^{-4} + 0.495 \times 10^{-4} + 0.095 \times 10^{-4} + 0.005 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$= (9.405 + 0.495 + 0.095 + 0.005) \times 10^{-4}$$ $$= 10.000 \times 10^{-4} = 1.000 \times 10^{-3} \checkmark$$ The sum equals P(I) = 10⁻³, confirming branch-point probability conservation. --- #### Step 4 — Critical Scenario Identification Although **Path D** has the lowest probability (5 × 10⁻⁷), it represents the **catastrophic fire outcome**. The risk significance is the **product of probability × consequence severity**. | Path | Probability | Consequence Severity | Risk Rank | |---|---|---|---| | A | 9.405 × 10⁻⁴ | Low (small leak) | Low risk | | B | 4.95 × 10⁻⁵ | Medium (gas release) | Moderate | | C | 9.5 × 10⁻⁶ | High (major release) | High | | **D** | **5.0 × 10⁻⁷** | **Very High (fire)** | **Highest consequence risk** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion**: The design should focus on **improving ESD valve reliability (Barrier 1)** because failure of this single barrier is the common precursor to both high-severity outcomes (C and D). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: Students often only report probabilities without discussing **risk = probability × consequence**. KTU 2024 scheme values **higher-order thinking (CO₅, Bloom's Level 5 — Evaluate)**, so always conclude with risk-based commentary. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Fault Tree Diagram (Mermaid Block Diagram) The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **logical structure of Example 2** (Cooling System Fault Tree): ```mermaid graph TD nodeT["TOP EVENT T
Reactor Cooling Failure"] nodeP["P
Pump Failure"] nodeC["C
Coolant Supply Failure"] nodeM["M
Motor Fails
P = 0.02"] nodeS["S
Power Supply Fails
P = 0.03"] nodeV1["V1
Valve V1 Fails
P = 0.01"] nodeV2["V2
Valve V2 Fails
P = 0.015"] nodeT --> nodeP nodeT --> nodeC nodeP --> nodeM nodeP --> nodeS nodeC --> nodeV1 nodeC --> nodeV2 style nodeT fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style nodeP fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style nodeC fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style nodeM fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px style nodeS fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px style nodeV1 fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px style nodeV2 fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px ``` **Color Legend:** - 🔴 **Red**: Top Event (undesired outcome) - 🟡 **Yellow**: Intermediate Events - 🟢 **Green**: Basic Events (root causes) --- ## 4.2 Event Tree Diagram (Mermaid Block Diagram) The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **branching logic of Example 3** (Pipeline Rupture ETA): ```mermaid graph TD initEvent["INITIATING EVENT I
Pipeline Rupture
P = 1.0E-3 per year"] barrier1["BARRIER 1
ESD Valve"] barrier2["BARRIER 2
Flame Arrestor"] s1yes["S1 = SUCCESS
P = 0.99"] s1no["F1 = FAILURE
P = 0.01"] s2yes["S2 = SUCCESS
P = 0.95"] s2no["F2 = FAILURE
P = 0.05"] outA["OUTCOME A
Small Leak
P = 9.405E-4"] outB["OUTCOME B
Gas Release
P = 4.95E-5"] outC["OUTCOME C
Major Release
P = 9.5E-6"] outD["OUTCOME D
FIRE CATASTROPHE
P = 5.0E-7"] initEvent --> barrier1 barrier1 --> s1yes barrier1 --> s1no s1yes --> barrier2 s1no --> barrier2 barrier2 --> s2yes barrier2 --> s2no s2yes --> outA s2yes --> outB s2no --> outC s2no --> outD style initEvent fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style barrier1 fill:#4d96ff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff style barrier2 fill:#4d96ff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff style s1yes fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px style s1no fill:#ffa500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px style s2yes fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px style s2no fill:#ffa500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px style outA fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px style outB fill:#fff59d,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px style outC fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px style outD fill:#ef5350,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` **Color Legend:** - 🔴 **Red**: Worst-case outcome (catastrophe) - 🟠 **Orange**: High severity outcomes - 🟡 **Yellow**: Moderate outcomes - 🟢 **Green**: Safe outcomes --- ## 4.3 Dow F&EI Calculation Workflow (Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD step1["STEP 1
Identify Process Unit"] step2["STEP 2
Determine Material Factor MF
from NF and NR"] step3["STEP 3
Calculate F1
General Process Hazards"] step4["STEP 4
Calculate F2
Special Process Hazards"] step5["STEP 5
Compute F3 = F1 x F2"] step6["STEP 6
Compute FEI = MF x F3"] step7["STEP 7
Compute R = 0.256 x FEI"] step8["STEP 8
Compute A = pi x R squared"] step9["STEP 9
Compute DF from FEI"] step10["STEP 10
Compute MPPD = 0.85 x RC x DF"] step11["STEP 11
Classify Severity"] step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> step6 --> step7 --> step8 --> step9 --> step10 --> step11 style step1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#000 style step11 fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > Define the **Material Factor (MF)** used in the Dow Fire and Explosion Index. What does it represent? **Model Answer:** The **Material Factor (MF)** is a measure of the intrinsic energy release potential of the most hazardous material present in a process unit. It is selected from the Dow Chemical reference table based on two NFPA ratings: - **NF**: Flammability rating (0–4) - **NR**: Reactivity rating (0–4) MF represents the **baseline chemical energy** available for release during a fire or explosion. Common values: gasoline MF = 16, methane MF = 21, hydrogen MF = 28. Higher MF values indicate greater potential for hazardous energy release per unit mass. --- ### Question A2 > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** > Differentiate between **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** and **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** in three points. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) | Event Tree Analysis (ETA) | |---|---|---| | Approach | Deductive (top-down) | Inductive (bottom-up) | | Starting Point | Top event (undesired outcome) | Initiating event (disturbance) | | Purpose | Find causes of failure | Find consequences of an event | | Logic Gates | AND, OR, NOT, Voting | Sequential branch points | | Output | Minimal cut sets, top event probability | Outcome scenarios with probabilities | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** > A reactor processes acetone under the following conditions: > - **NF = 3, NR = 0**, hence **MF = 16** > > **General Process Penalties (F₁):** > - Exothermic reaction = 0.40 > - Material handling = 0.60 > - Enclosed process = 0.30 > > **Special Process Penalties (F₂):** > - Toxic material = 0.20 > - Operation near flammable range = 0.70 > - Quantity penalty = 0.50 > - Corrosion = 0.30 > > **Replacement Cost RC = $80 million.** > > Calculate **(a)** F₁, F₂, F₃, **F&EI** (b) Radius of Exposure R, **Area of Exposure A**, **Damage Factor DF**, **MPPD**, and **(c)** classify the severity. #### Part (a) Solution — F₁, F₂, F₃, F&EI [7 Marks] $$F_1 = 1.0 + (0.40 + 0.60 + 0.30) = 1.0 + 1.30 = 2.30$$ $$F_2 = 1.0 + (0.20 + 0.70 + 0.50 + 0.30) = 1.0 + 1.70 = 2.70$$ $$F_3 = F_1 \times F_2 = 2.30 \times 2.70 = 6.21$$ $$F\&EI = MF \times F_3 = 16 \times 6.21 = 99.36$$ #### Part (b) Solution — R, A, DF, MPPD [5 Marks] $$R = 0.256 \times 99.36 = 25.44 \text{ m}$$ $$A = \pi R^2 = \pi \times (25.44)^2 = \pi \times 647.19 = 2033.04 \text{ m}^2$$ $$DF = \left(\dfrac{99.36}{100}\right)^{0.6} = (0.9936)^{0.6}$$ $$0.9936^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \times \ln(0.9936)} = e^{0.6 \times (-0.006418)} = e^{-0.003851} \approx 0.996$$ $$DF \approx 0.996$$ $$MPPD = 0.85 \times 80{,}000{,}000 \times 0.996 = 67{,}728{,}000 \text{ USD}$$ $$\boxed{MPPD \approx \$67.73 \text{ million}}$$ #### Part (c) Solution — Severity Classification [2 Marks] Since **F&EI = 99.36** lies in the range **97–127**, the unit is classified as **HEAVY** severity. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Candidates often miscompute `(99.36/100)^0.6`. Use the identity `a^x = e^{x ln a}` to avoid calculator errors. Marks are lost when students report `DF ≈ 1` (correct is **0.996**). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** > A storage tank rupture event is analyzed. The top event **T** (catastrophic rupture) occurs if: > - **Event A** (corrosion failure) occurs, **OR** > - **Event B** (overpressure failure) occurs, **OR** > - **Event C** (mechanical impact) occurs. > > Furthermore: > - Event A occurs if **both** A₁ (internal corrosion) **AND** A₂ (external corrosion) occur. > - Event B occurs if **both** B₁ (PSV fails open) **AND** B₂ (block valve closed) occur. > > Given per-year failure probabilities: > - P(A₁) = 0.05, P(A₂) = 0.04 > - P(B₁) = 0.02, P(B₂) = 0.03 > - P(C) = 0.001 > > Compute **(a)** the probability of each intermediate event, **(b)** the top event probability P(T), and **(c)** the expected return period in years. #### Part (a) Solution — Intermediate Events [6 Marks] **Event A** (AND gate): $$P(A) = P(A_1) \times P(A_2) = 0.05 \times 0.04 = 0.0020$$ **Event B** (AND gate): $$P(B) = P(B_1) \times P(B_2) = 0.02 \times 0.03 = 0.0006$$ **Event C** is a basic event with given probability: $$P(C) = 0.001$$ #### Part (b) Solution — Top Event Probability [5 Marks] The top event uses an **OR gate** combining A, B, and C: $$P(T) = 1 - [1 - P(A)] \times [1 - P(B)] \times [1 - P(C)]$$ $$P(T) = 1 - (1 - 0.0020)(1 - 0.0006)(1 - 0.001)$$ $$P(T) = 1 - (0.9980)(0.9994)(0.999)$$ Step-by-step multiplication: $$(0.9980)(0.9994) = 0.9974012$$ $$(0.9974012)(0.999) = 0.9964038$$ $$P(T) = 1 - 0.9964038 = 0.0035962$$ $$\boxed{P(T) \approx 3.60 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per year}}$$ #### Part (c) Solution — Return Period [3 Marks] The **return period (TR)** is the reciprocal of the annual probability: $$T_R = \dfrac{1}{P(T)} = \dfrac{1}{0.0035962} = 278.07 \text{ years}$$ $$\boxed{T_R \approx 278 \text{ years}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: For OR gates with three or more inputs, students sometimes wrongly use the **small-angle approximation**: `P(T) ≈ P(A) + P(B) + P(C)`. This is only valid if **all events are mutually exclusive** OR if `Pᵢ << 1`. Always use the complement product formula for full credit. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 Examples** ### Dow F&EI Quick Facts - ✅ **F&EI = MF × F₃** where F₃ = F₁ × F₂ - ✅ **F₁ and F₂ always start from 1.0** plus penalties - ✅ **R = 0.256 × F&EI** (in meters) - ✅ **MPPD = 0.85 × RC × DF** - ✅ Severity Bands: **Light (1–60) → Moderate (61–96) → Heavy (97–127) → Extreme (128–158) → Very Extreme (≥159)** ### Fault Tree Analysis Quick Facts - ✅ **AND gate**: $P = \prod P_i$ (multiply) - ✅ **OR gate**: $P = 1 - \prod (1 - P_i)$ (complement product) - ✅ Assume **independence** unless stated otherwise - ✅ Always **verify** by summing minimal cut set probabilities (must ≤ 1) - ✅ Return period (years) = **1/P** ### Event Tree Analysis Quick Facts - ✅ Path probability = **P(initiator) × ∏(branch probabilities)** - ✅ Sum of all path probabilities **must equal P(initiator)** - ✅ Identify **risk-dominant path** using **probability × consequence** - ✅ Most critical path is **NOT always the most probable** — consequences matter equally ### Common KTU Mark Loss Points - 🚫 Forgetting **base value 1.0** in F₁ or F₂ - 🚫 Using `P(A) + P(B)` instead of complement product for OR gate - 🚫 Omitting **units** in final answers (m, m², USD) - 🚫 Not discussing **consequence severity** in ETA problems - 🚫 Skipping the **verification step** (sum check for ETA) - 🚫 Failing to classify **Dow severity band** explicitly ### Recommended Study Sequence 1. Memorize the **Dow F&EI severity bands** table 2. Practice **3–4 FTA problems** covering AND, OR, and combined gates 3. Practice **2–3 ETA problems** with 2–3 safety barriers 4. Solve **at least one cross-method problem** combining Dow F&EI with FTA 5. Review the **MCE (Maximum Credible Event) scenarios** commonly tested > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Tip**: When a KTU question asks "comment on the severity" or "identify the critical scenario", always connect **the numerical result back to engineering reality** — never leave the answer purely numerical. This demonstrates **CO₅ attainment** (Evaluate level in Bloom's Taxonomy) which carries **2–3 bonus marks** in the KTU 2024 valuation scheme.

topics.title: Consequence Analysis and Quantitative Risk Assessment - Consequence of Chemical Accidents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Consequence Analysis and Quantitative Risk Assessment - Consequence of Chemical Accidents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Consequence Analysis — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequence Analysis** is the systematic, quantitative engineering evaluation of the physical, thermal, and toxic outcomes resulting from the **uncontrolled release of hazardous materials** (flammable, explosive, or toxic) from process equipment in a chemical plant. It uses mathematical models to translate a "release event" into spatially defined **damage/impact zones** (overpressure, thermal radiation, toxic concentration contours).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Consequence Analysis is a **mandatory pillar** of Process Safety Management (PSM) and forms the computational backbone of any Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA). It answers the question: *"If the worst-case release happens at Unit 7, exactly how far does the death-zone extend?"*

## 1.2 Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** is a structured numerical methodology that integrates **(a) the likelihood** of undesired events with **(b) the magnitude of their consequences** to produce a risk metric. Risk is mathematically expressed as:
>
> $$R = f \times C$$
>
> where $R$ is the **Individual Risk** (IR) or **Societal Risk**, $f$ is the **frequency of the incident (events/year)**, and $C$ is the **consequence (fatalities, injury, property loss)**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **🍳 The "Kitchen Pan Explosion" Analogy for Consequence Analysis:**
> Imagine you forget a pressure cooker on the stove (the *release event*). Now you want to know the damage:
> - **How fast does the steam shoot out?** → *Discharge / Release modeling*
> - **Where does the hot cloud drift?** → *Dispersion modeling*
> - **If it catches fire, how big is the fireball?** → *Fire / Explosion modeling*
> - **If gas burns in a confined kitchen, how much does the wall shake?** → *Blast / Overpressure modeling*
> - **At what distance does a person get burned?** → *Effect / Damage criteria*
>
> Consequence analysis does **exactly this**, but for a 50-ton ammonia vessel in a real refinery, using mathematical models instead of intuition.

## 1.4 Key Damage Metrics Used in Consequence Analysis

| Hazard Type | Physical Driver | Standard Effect Metric |
|---|---|---|
| Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE) | Blast wave | Overpressure $\Delta P$ in **kPa** |
| Pool Fire / Jet Fire / Fireball | Thermal radiation | Heat flux $q$ in **kW/m²** |
| Toxic Release | Inhalation dose | Concentration $C$ in **ppm** |
| BLEVE | Missile + thermal | Thermal dose $t \cdot q^{4/3}$ in **kW⁴/³·s¹/³·m⁻⁸/³** |
| Missiles (Fragments) | Kinetic energy | Impact energy in **Joules** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Threshold (KTU Board Emphasis):**
> - **1% Lethality (Fatality) Overpressure:** $\Delta P = 2.0 \text{ psi} \approx 14 \text{ kPa}$
> - **50% Lethality Overpressure:** $\Delta P = 5.0 \text{ psi} \approx 35 \text{ kPa}$
> - **Skin Burn (10 sec exposure):** $q = 4.0 \text{ kW/m}^2$ (pain), $q = 12.5 \text{ kW/m}^2$ (1% lethality)
> - **IDLH Threshold (toxic):** Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health, e.g., NH₃ = **300 ppm**

## 1.5 Classification of Release Scenarios

The release of hazardous material is classified into three regimes, each governed by distinct thermodynamic equations:

1. **Liquid Release** — sub-cooled or saturated liquid from a hole.
2. **Gas/Vapour Release** — pressurised gas with no liquid present.
3. **Two-Phase Release** — flashing liquid (e.g., LPG) that partially vaporises upon depressurisation (most dangerous case).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Step Consequence Analysis Methodology

Every KTU numerical problem on this topic follows this logical pipeline:

**Step 1: Source Identification**
Identify the inventory, temperature $T$, pressure $P$, phase, molecular weight $M$, and heat of combustion $H_c$.

**Step 2: Release / Discharge Calculation**
Compute the mass (or volumetric) flow rate $\dot{m}$ through the breach using the **Bernoulli / orifice equation**. The flow is either *subsonic* or *choked (sonic)* depending on the pressure ratio.

**Step 3: Dispersion Modeling**
Track the atmospheric transport of the cloud using the **Pasquill-Gifford Gaussian Plume Model**, which considers wind speed $u$, stability class (A–F), and downwind distance $x$.

**Step 4: Fire / Explosion Modeling**
Determine the physical outcome:
- **Pool Fire / Jet Fire** → thermal radiation
- **VCE** → blast overpressure
- **BLEVE** → fireball + missiles

**Step 5: Damage / Effect Estimation**
Apply **Probit equations** ($Y = a + b \ln V$) to convert physical load into probability of fatality. Where $V$ is the load (overpressure, thermal dose, or toxic dose).

**Step 6: Risk Integration (for QRA)**
Combine frequency $f$ with consequence $C$ to compute risk:
$$\text{Individual Risk (IR)} = \sum_i f_i \cdot P_{f,i}$$

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Phenomenon | Governing Equation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Liquid discharge (non-flashing) | $\dot{m}_L = C_d A \rho_l \sqrt{ 2(P_1 - P_{atm})/\rho_l + 2gh }$ | kg/s |
| 2 | Gas discharge (choked, $P_1/P_{atm} \geq 1.89$) | $\dot{m}_g = C_d A P_1 \sqrt{ \dfrac{\gamma M}{R T_1} \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\!(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)} }$ | kg/s |
| 3 | Gas discharge (subsonic) | $\dot{m}_g = C_d A P_1 \sqrt{ \dfrac{2\gamma M}{(\gamma-1)R T_1} \left[ (P_2/P_1)^{2/\gamma} - (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma+1)/\gamma} \right] }$ | kg/s |
| 4 | Flash fraction (two-phase) | $f_v = \dfrac{c_p (T - T_b)}{H_v}$ | — |
| 5 | TNT Equivalent | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{TNT}}$ (with $H_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg) | kg TNT |
| 6 | Scaled distance (Sachs) | $Z_s = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$ | m/kg¹ᐟ³ |
| 7 | Side-on overpressure from $Z_s$ | $\dfrac{P_s}{P_{atm}} = \dfrac{1616 \left[1 + \left(\dfrac{Z_s}{4.5}\right)^2 \right]}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{Z_s}{0.048}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{Z_s}{0.32}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{Z_s}{1.35}\right)^2}}$ | — |
| 8 | Gaussian plume (centerline) | $C(x,0,0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}$ | ppm or kg/m³ |
| 9 | Probit for overpressure | $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln P_s$ | — |
| 10 | Probit for thermal radiation | $Y = -36.38 + 2.56 \ln(t \cdot q^{4/3})$ | — |
| 11 | Probit for toxic dose | $Y = a + b \ln(C^n \cdot t)$ | — |
| 12 | Pool fire diameter (HSE) | $D = 2\sqrt{ \dfrac{\dot{m}/\rho_l}{\pi \cdot m_b} }$ | m |
| 13 | Individual Risk | $IR = \sum f_i \cdot P_{f,i}$ | yr⁻¹ |
| 14 | Societal Risk (F-N Curve) | Plot of cumulative frequency $F$ vs. number of fatalities $N$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Numerical Constant:** The TNT heat of combustion used in all KTU problems is **$H_{TNT} = 4680 \text{ kJ/kg}$** and the explosion efficiency $\eta$ is typically **0.05 (5%)** for VCE unless stated otherwise.

## 2.3 Why Each Equation Matters (Engineering Utility)

- **Discharge equations (1–3):** Used to size **emergency isolation valves (ESDVs)** and **relief valves** (API 520/521).
- **Flash fraction (4):** Critical for **LNG/LPG cargo** design — dictates the explosion energy.
- **TNT Equivalent (5–7):** Used by regulatory bodies (OSHA, HSE, AIChE/CCPS) for **land-use planning** around chemical plants.
- **Gaussian Plume (8):** Standard input for **Hazardous Area Classification** and emergency response planning.
- **Probits (9–11):** Convert physics into **human vulnerability models** — used in insurance underwriting.
- **Pool fire diameter (12):** Sizes **firewater demand** and **bund wall** spacing.
- **IR & F-N Curves (13–14):** Used to demonstrate **ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)** compliance under UK HSE / Indian Factories Act.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Choked Gas Discharge Through an Orifice (Equation 2 of Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **#1 most-asked derivation** in KTU University Exams for this module. Memorise the pressure ratio threshold: **$P_1/P_{atm} \geq 1.89$** for air-like gases ($\gamma = 1.4$).

**Starting Point — Bernoulli's Equation (compressible form):**
$$\dot{m} = C_d A \sqrt{ \dfrac{2\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot P_1 \rho_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{2/\gamma} - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/\gamma} \right] }$$

**Step 1 — Maximise mass flow rate.** Take the derivative of $\dot{m}$ with respect to the pressure ratio $(P_2/P_1)$ and set it to zero.

**Step 2 — Critical pressure ratio emerges.** Solving gives the **sonic / choked condition**:
$$\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)_{\text{crit}} = \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back.** For air ($\gamma = 1.4$), this ratio is exactly **0.528**, meaning $P_1/P_{atm} = 1/0.528 = 1.89$.

**Step 4 — Replace density using ideal gas law** ($\rho_1 = P_1 M / R T_1$):

$$\dot{m}_g = C_d A P_1 \sqrt{ \dfrac{\gamma M}{R T_1} \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\!(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)} }$$

This is the canonical **choked gas discharge** formula. For **subsonic discharge**, the bracketed term is replaced with the full bracketed expression from Eq. 3 of the cheat sheet.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 5 cm diameter hole develops in a pipeline carrying methane at $P_1 = 10 \text{ bar (abs)}$, $T_1 = 298 \text{ K}$. Take $C_d = 0.62$, $\gamma = 1.32$, $M = 16 \text{ g/mol}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/mol·K}$. Calculate the mass flow rate and the total mass released in **30 minutes** if the line is isolated by an automatic block valve that closes after 60 s.

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 10 \times 10^5 = 1.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- $T_1 = 298 \text{ K}$, $A = \pi(0.025)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$
- $P_1/P_{atm} = 10/1.013 = 9.87 \gg 1.89$ → **flow is CHOKED**

**Step 1 — Calculate the choked mass flux term:**
$$\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)} = \left(\frac{2}{2.32}\right)^{2.32/0.32} = (0.8621)^{7.25}$$

**Numerical evaluation of $(0.8621)^{7.25}$:**

$$\ln(0.8621) = -0.1484$$
$$7.25 \times (-0.1484) = -1.0759$$
$$e^{-1.0759} = 0.3410$$

**Step 2 — Prefactor under square root:**
$$\dfrac{\gamma M}{R T_1} = \dfrac{1.32 \times 0.016}{8.314 \times 298} = \dfrac{0.02112}{2477.6} = 8.526 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply:**
$$8.526 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.3410 = 2.908 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 4 — Square root:**
$$\sqrt{2.908 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.706 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5 — Mass flow rate:**
$$\dot{m} = 0.62 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.0 \times 10^6 \times 1.706 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$\dot{m} = 2.076 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 6 — Total mass released (60 s before ESDV closure):**
$$m_{\text{total}} = \dot{m} \times t = 2.076 \times 60 = 124.6 \text{ kg}$$

This mass is then used as input to a **dispersion or explosion model** in subsequent sub-questions.

---

## 3.3 Worked Probit Example

> **Problem:** Using $m = 124.6$ kg of methane ($\eta = 0.05$, $H_c = 55.5 \text{ MJ/kg}$), determine the **distance at which 50% fatality** occurs due to VCE.

**Step 1 — TNT equivalent:**
$$W_{TNT} = \frac{0.05 \times 124.6 \times 55500}{4680} = \frac{345765}{4680} = 73.9 \text{ kg TNT}$$

**Step 2 — For 50% fatality**, the overpressure needed from probit $Y = 5$ is back-calculated:

$$5 = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln P_s$$
$$\ln P_s = \frac{82.1}{6.91} = 11.88$$
$$P_s = e^{11.88} = 143870 \text{ Pa} \approx 144 \text{ kPa}$$

(This is the standard reference value used by CCPS for 50% lethality from lung rupture.)

**Step 3 — Invert the Sachs scaled-distance equation.** Use the approximation for **$Z_s$ from $P_s/P_{atm}$**:

For $\Delta P \approx 144 \text{ kPa}$ and atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 101.3 \text{ kPa}$:
- $P_s/P_{atm} = 144/101.3 = 1.42$

The corresponding **scaled distance** (from Sachs charts) is approximately:
$$Z_s \approx 2.5 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to physical distance:**
$$R = Z_s \cdot W_{TNT}^{1/3} = 2.5 \times (73.9)^{1/3} = 2.5 \times 4.19 = 10.5 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** At ~10.5 m from the release point, 50% of exposed people would suffer fatal lung rupture from the VCE blast. This becomes the **fatality contour** plotted on the plant layout.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow

| If release is… | Use Equation | Then feed into… |
|---|---|---|
| Sub-cooled liquid | Eq. 1 | Pool evaporation model |
| Pressurised gas, $P_1/P_{atm} > 1.89$ | Eq. 2 (choked) | Dispersion or VCE |
| Pressurised gas, $P_1/P_{atm} \le 1.89$ | Eq. 3 (subsonic) | Dispersion |
| Flashing liquid (LPG) | Eq. 4 (flash frac.) | VCE (very high yield) |
| Pool spill (low volatility) | Pool evaporation rate | Pool Fire (Eq. 12) |
| Instantaneous release | Integration of Eq. 2/3 | Gaussian Puff Model |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 QRA Top-Down Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PLANT INVENTORY AND PROCESS DATA"] --> B["HAZARD IDENTIFICATION HAZOP What If Checklist"]
    B --> C["SCENARIO SELECTION RELEASE CATEGORIES"]
    C --> D["FREQUENCY ANALYSIS FAILURE RATES"]
    C --> E["CONSEQUENCE ANALYSIS PHYSICAL EFFECTS"]
    E --> E1["DISCHARGE MODEL ORIFICE FLOW"]
    E1 --> E2["DISPERSION GAUSSIAN PLUME"]
    E2 --> E3["FIRE OR EXPLOSION VCE POOL JET BLEVE"]
    E3 --> E4["EFFECT ZONES THERMAL BLAST TOXIC"]
    E4 --> E5["DAMAGE CRITERIA PROBIT EQUATIONS"]
    D --> F["RISK ESTIMATION INDIVIDUAL RISK IR"]
    E5 --> F
    F --> G["RISK ACCEPTANCE ALARP F N CURVE"]
    G --> H["MITIGATION RECOMMENDATIONS"]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff

    style E fill:#bdd7ee,color:#000000

    style E2 fill:#dbe5f1,color:#000000
    style E3 fill:#dbe5f1,color:#000000
    style E4 fill:#dbe5f1,color:#000000
    style E5 fill:#dbe5f1,color:#000000
    style F fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
```
