
## 4.2 Discharge Regime Decision Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["RELEASE EVENT IDENTIFIED"] --> P1["WHAT IS THE PHASE OF THE RELEASED FLUID"]
    P1 --> P2["LIQUID SUB COOLED"]
    P1 --> P3["GAS OR VAPOUR ABOVE TB"]
    P1 --> P4["TWO PHASE FLASHING LPG NH3"]
    P2 --> P5["USE EQ 1 LIQUID ORIFICE FORMULA"]
    P3 --> P6{"IS P1 OVER PATM GREATER THAN 1.89"}
    P4 --> P7["COMPUTE FLASH FRACTION EQ 4 THEN TWO PHASE BLOWDOWN"]
    P6 --> P8["YES USE EQ 2 CHOKED GAS FLOW"]
    P6 --> P9["NO USE EQ 3 SUBSONIC GAS FLOW"]
    P5 --> P10["FEED INTO DISPERSION OR POOL FIRE"]
    P7 --> P10
    P8 --> P11["FEED INTO VCE OR FLASH FIRE"]
    P9 --> P10
    P10 --> P12["COMPUTE EFFECT ZONES"]
    P11 --> P12

    style P0 fill:#264478,color:#ffffff
    style P5 fill:#a9d08e,color:#000000
    style P7 fill:#a9d08e,color:#000000
    style P8 fill:#a9d08e,color:#000000
    style P9 fill:#a9d08e,color:#000000
    style P12 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Consequence Severity vs. Distance — Damage Criteria Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OVERPRESSURE["OVERPRESSURE FROM VCE OR BLEVE"]
        O1["1.0 psi 6.9 kPa Glass breakage Safe distance"]
        O2["2.0 psi 14 kPa 1 percent lethality threshold"]
        O3["5.0 psi 35 kPa 50 percent lethality threshold"]
        O4["10 psi 69 kPa Eardrum rupture Heavier structural damage"]
    end

    subgraph THERMAL["THERMAL RADIATION FROM FIRES"]
        T1["4.0 kW per m2 Pain threshold 10 sec exposure"]
        T2["12.5 kW per m2 1 percent lethality"]
        T3["25 kW per m2 50 percent lethality"]
        T4["37.5 kW per m2 100 percent lethality"]
    end

    subgraph TOXIC["TOXIC CONCENTRATION FROM DISPERSION"]
        X1["AEGL 1 Discomfort Mild effects"]
        X2["AEGL 2 Disability Irreversible effects"]
        X3["AEGL 3 Life threatening 10 percent lethality"]
        X4["AEGL 4 Lethal 50 percent lethality"]
    end

    style OVERPRESSURE fill:#fbe5d6,color:#000000
    style THERMAL fill:#fff2cc,color:#000000
    style TOXIC fill:#e2efda,color:#000000
```

## 4.4 F-N Curve — Societal Risk Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph X["NUMBER OF FATALITIES N AXIS LOG SCALE"]
    end
    subgraph Y["CUMULATIVE FREQUENCY F AXIS LOG SCALE"]
    end
    Y --> A1["UNACCEPTABLE REGION ABOVE UPPER LIMIT"]
    Y --> A2["ALARP REGION TOLERABLE IF RISK REDUCED"]
    Y --> A3["ACCEPTABLE REGION BELOW LOWER LIMIT"]
    A1 --> A4["MANDATORY MITIGATION"]
    A2 --> A5["COST BENEFIT ANALYSIS"]
    A3 --> A6["NO ACTION REQUIRED"]

    style A1 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style A2 fill:#ffc000,color:#000000
    style A3 fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
    style A4 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style A5 fill:#ffc000,color:#000000
    style A6 fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
**Define consequence analysis. List the four major categories of physical effects that arise from accidental chemical releases.**

**Model Answer:**

Consequence analysis is the quantitative estimation of the physical, thermal and toxic outcomes of an accidental release of hazardous material from process equipment. It defines spatially the damage zones around a release point using validated mathematical models.

The four major categories of physical effects are:

1. **Toxic Effects** — harmful concentrations from dispersion of toxic gases (e.g., NH₃, Cl₂).
2. **Thermal Effects** — fire and thermal radiation from pool fires, jet fires, fireballs and flash fires.
3. **Pressure Effects (Overpressure)** — blast waves from VCEs, BLEVEs and confined explosions.
4. **Missile Effects** — fragments propelled by vessel rupture or explosion (kinetic energy).

*(Examiner note: Award full 3 marks only if all four are correctly listed with one example each.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a Probit equation? Write the probit equation for fatality from thermal radiation and explain its variables.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Probit equation** (probabilistic unit) is a statistical dose–response model that converts a physical load (overpressure, thermal dose, or toxic dose) into the **probability of fatality** of an exposed individual.

The probit equation for thermal radiation fatality is:

$$Y = -36.38 + 2.56 \ln(t \cdot q^{4/3})$$

where:
- $Y$ = probit value (probability of fatality, $P = 50\%$ at $Y = 5$)
- $t$ = exposure time in **seconds**
- $q$ = incident thermal radiation flux in **kW/m²**
- $q^{4/3}$ is the thermal dose term; $t \cdot q^{4/3}$ has units $\left[(\text{kW/m}^2)^{4/3} \cdot \text{s}\right]$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 4 typically tests ONE 14-mark question with internal choice. Both alternatives are solved below.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Adapted]**

A horizontal ammonia storage cylinder (volume $5 \text{ m}^3$) contains liquid ammonia at $T = 293 \text{ K}$, $P = 8.5 \text{ bar (gauge)}$. A 25 mm diameter leak develops at the cylinder mid-section.

**Given:**
- $M_{NH_3} = 17 \text{ g/mol}$, $\gamma = 1.31$, $H_c = 18.6 \text{ MJ/kg}$
- $P_{atm} = 1.013 \text{ bar}$, $H_v = 1370 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at 293 K
- $c_{p,l} = 4.7 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $T_b = 239.8 \text{ K}$, $\eta = 0.05$
- $C_d = 0.6$, $TNT$ heat of combustion = $4680 \text{ kJ/kg}$

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Calculate the mass flow rate of ammonia from the leak and the total mass released in 10 minutes. Classify the discharge regime.**

**Step 1 — Determine absolute pressure:**
$$P_1 = 8.5 + 1.013 = 9.513 \text{ bar} = 9.513 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Check critical pressure ratio:**
$$\frac{P_1}{P_{atm}} = \frac{9.513}{1.013} = 9.39 \gg 1.89$$

⇒ Flow is **CHOKED (sonic)**. Liquid ammonia at room temperature will flash — treat as two-phase. For liquid-like mass flow:

$$\dot{m}_L = C_d A \rho_l \sqrt{2(P_1 - P_{atm})/\rho_l}$$

With $\rho_l = 680 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at 293 K and $A = \pi(0.0125)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$:

$$\dot{m}_L = 0.6 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 680 \sqrt{2 \times 8.5 \times 10^5 / 680}$$

$$= 0.6 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 680 \times \sqrt{2500}$$

$$= 0.6 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 680 \times 50$$

$$= 10.01 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3 — Flash fraction (for completeness):**
$$f_v = \frac{c_p(T - T_b)}{H_v} = \frac{4.7 \times (293 - 239.8)}{1370} = 0.183$$

So ~18% flashes to vapour and ~82% remains as aerosol — explosive mixture is formed.

**Step 4 — Total mass in 10 min (600 s):**
$$m_{\text{total}} = 10.01 \times 600 = 6006 \text{ kg}$$

#### **(b) [7 Marks] Estimate the TNT-equivalent mass of the released ammonia and determine the distance to the 1% lethality contour due to overpressure.**

**Step 1 — TNT equivalent:**
$$W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot m \cdot H_c}{H_{TNT}} = \frac{0.05 \times 6006 \times 18600}{4680}$$

$$= \frac{5,585,580}{4680} = 1193.5 \text{ kg TNT}$$

**Step 2 — For 1% lethality**, from probit $Y = 2.67$:
$$2.67 = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln P_s$$
$$\ln P_s = \frac{79.77}{6.91} = 11.544$$
$$P_s = e^{11.544} = 103{,}891 \text{ Pa} \approx 104 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Pressure ratio:**
$$\frac{P_s}{P_{atm}} = \frac{104}{101.3} = 1.027$$

**Step 4 — Sachs scaled distance** (from Sachs chart): for $P_s/P_{atm} \approx 1.027$, $Z_s \approx 3.0 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$.

**Step 5 — Physical distance:**
$$R_{1\%} = Z_s \cdot W_{TNT}^{1/3} = 3.0 \times (1193.5)^{1/3} = 3.0 \times 10.62 = 31.9 \text{ m}$$

> **Answer:** The 1% fatality contour extends approximately **31.9 m** from the leak point due to blast overpressure. Beyond this, fatality probability drops rapidly.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

A **QRA study** of a chlorine railcar loading terminal has identified a continuous release of chlorine vapour at a rate of 5 kg/s for 30 minutes under D-stability, wind speed 2 m/s. The leak frequency is estimated at $1 \times 10^{-4}$ per year.

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Compute the downwind concentration at the plant boundary (200 m downwind, 0 m crosswind) using the Pasquill-Gifford Gaussian Plume Model.**

**Step 1 — Identify D-class dispersion coefficients** at $x = 200$ m (from standard PG tables):
$$\sigma_y = 7.9 \text{ m}, \quad \sigma_z = 5.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Source strength conversion:**
$$Q = 5 \text{ kg/s} = 5 \times 10^3 \text{ g/s}$$

(Note: For ppm, divide by molar volume $= 24.04$ L/mol at 25 °C, but use SI for now.)

**Step 3 — Centerline ground-level concentration:**
$$C(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi \, u \, \sigma_y \, \sigma_z}$$

$$C = \frac{5}{3.1416 \times 2 \times 7.9 \times 5.0}$$

$$= \frac{5}{248.2} = 0.02015 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Convert to ppm:**
$$C_{ppm} = \frac{C \times 24.04}{M_{Cl_2}} \times 1000 = \frac{0.02015 \times 24040}{70.9} = 6.83 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with AEGL-2 (1 ppm for Cl₂) for 30 min:**
Since $6.83 \text{ ppm} \gg 1 \text{ ppm}$, the boundary is inside the toxic effect zone.

#### **(b) [7 Marks] Using a fatality probability of 0.4 at this concentration, compute the Individual Risk (IR) and comment on whether the facility meets an IR target of $10^{-5}$/yr.**

**Step 1 — Individual Risk:**
$$IR = f \times P_f = 1 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4 = 4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with target:**
$$IR_{\text{computed}} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$
$$IR_{\text{target}} = 1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$

Since $4 \times 10^{-5} > 1 \times 10^{-5}$, the IR **EXCEEDS** the acceptable target by **a factor of 4**.

**Step 3 — Conclusion & ALARP recommendation:**

The facility **does not meet** the IR target. Under the **ALARP principle** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable), mitigation measures are mandatory. Engineering options include:
- Installing **water-spray scrubber curtains** to absorb Cl₂
- Increasing **frequency of ESDV testing** to reduce leak probability
- **Restricting access** to the 200 m boundary zone
- Considering **emergency isolation** reduction from 30 min to <5 min

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 COMMON MARK-LOSING MISTAKES IN KTU CONCQUENCE ANALYSIS ANSWERS:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add atmospheric pressure** when computing $P_1$ (absolute). Using gauge pressure directly in the discharge equation is the **#1 mistake** — costs up to **4 marks**.
> 2. **Wrong flash-fraction sign convention**: Always $T > T_b$ for flashing to occur; if $T < T_b$, the liquid is sub-cooled and Equation 1 applies directly.
> 3. **Mixing units**: Thermal radiation $q$ in the probit must be in **kW/m²**, NOT W/m². Many students lose 2 marks on this.
> 4. **Sachs equation — forgetting the cube root**: $W_{TNT}^{1/3}$ is the cube root, not $W_{TNT}^{1/2}$. Common error.
> 5. **IR vs. F-N Curve confusion**: IR is per individual per year; F-N curves plot **cumulative frequency vs. number of fatalities**. KTU examiners frequently include a sub-part asking students to differentiate — give 2 correct distinguishing sentences for full marks.
> 6. **Stating "$W_{TNT} = \eta \cdot M \cdot H_c / H_{TNT}$"** without substituting $\eta = 0.05$ for VCE specifically (BLEVE uses higher $\eta$). Read the question carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST FOR MODULE 4 — CONSEQUENCE ANALYSIS & QRA**

- **Consequence Analysis Pipeline**: Source → Release → Dispersion → Fire/Explosion → Damage → Risk.
- **Release regime depends on $P_1/P_{atm}$**: if $\ge 1.89$ → **CHOKED**; else **subsonic**.
- **Saturated liquid flash fraction**: $f_v = c_p(T - T_b)/H_v$ — always positive; bounded $0 \le f_v \le 1$.
- **TNT equivalent constant**: $H_{TNT} = 4680 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $\eta_{VCE} = 0.05$ (default), $\eta_{BLEVE} \approx 0.4$.
- **Sachs scaled distance $Z_s = R/W_{TNT}^{1/3}$** is **the master non-dimensional group** for blast damage estimation.
- **1% fatality overpressure**: ~14 kPa; **50% fatality**: ~35 kPa (CCPS standard).
- **1% fatality thermal dose**: ~$12.5 \text{ kW/m}^2$ for 10 s exposure.
- **Probit-to-Probability**: $P = 50\% \times [1 + \text{erf}((Y-5)/\sqrt{2})]$. $Y = 5$ ⇒ $P = 50\%$.
- **Pasquill Stability Classes**: A (most unstable) → F (most stable). D = neutral (default for QRA).
- **Gaussian Plume — centerline formula** $C(x,0,0) = Q/(\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z)$ is sufficient for KTU exams.
- **Individual Risk (IR)**: $IR = \sum f_i \cdot P_{f,i}$; units yr⁻¹.
- **Societal Risk**: expressed via **F-N curve**; the **upper bound** is the unacceptable region (mandatory mitigation).
- **ALARP** is the decision-making triangle: **Unacceptable → ALARP → Acceptable**.
- **Land-use planning distance** for VCE fatality contour typically equals **$Z_s = 2.5 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$** scaled distance.
- **Bund wall sizing** is dictated by **110% of largest tank volume** per Indian Petroleum Rules.
- **ESDV closure time** (typically 30–60 s) is a critical input — students often omit it in release calculations.
- **KTU Golden Rule**: Always state assumptions explicitly (steady state, ground-level release, neutral stability) — examiners award **1 mark** just for assumptions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models for Fire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Models for Fire

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire Models** are mathematical representations used in process plant safety engineering to quantify the **source term** (heat release rate), **flame geometry** (size, shape, tilt), and **thermal radiation intensity** received at a target location. They translate the release of flammable material into a quantitative consequence endpoint (e.g., thermal dose received by personnel, equipment damage distance, escalation probability to adjacent units).

In the context of **Process Plant Safety**, the four canonical fire sub-models required by the KTU 2024 scheme are:

1. **Pool Fire Model** — burning liquid pool with a quasi-steady flame.
2. **Jet Fire Model** — turbulent diffusion flame from pressurized gas/liquid release.
3. **Flash Fire Model** — non-damaging moving flame front over a vapor cloud.
4. **Fireball / BLEVE Model** — transient, lifted, highly radiating spherical flame.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lamp and the Spot"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a *candle* and a *spotlight*. A pool fire behaves like a *candle on a table* — the flame sits over the fuel, steady, and you can roughly tell the heat at a distance. A jet fire is like a *blowtorch* — fuel is forced out at high velocity, the flame stretches far from the source. A fireball is like a *lightning-strike flashbulb* — a huge bright sphere appears for a few seconds and vanishes. Each requires a different **mathematical lamp** to estimate how brightly the "light" (thermal radiation) hits a person standing 50 m away.

The discipline of fire modeling essentially **predicts three questions** for the safety engineer:

- **Question 1 — HOW BIG?** → Flame geometry (diameter, length, height, tilt)
- **Question 2 — HOW HOT?** → Heat release rate (HRR) and surface emissive power (SEP)
- **Question 3 — HOW DANGEROUS AT DISTANCE $r$?** → Radiative heat flux $q_r$ (kW/m²) and thermal dose $I$ (kJ/m²)

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Constants (Bolded for KTU Quick Recall):**
> - **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ **W/m²K⁴**
> - **Standard ambient temperature:** $T_a = 298$ **K**
> - **Air density at STP:** $\rho_a = 1.225$ **kg/m³**
> - **Heat of combustion of methane:** $\Delta h_c = 50{,}000$ **kJ/kg**
> - **Heat of combustion of propane:** $\Delta h_c = 46{,}000$ **kJ/kg**
> - **Heat of combustion of gasoline:** $\Delta h_c = 43{,}000$ **kJ/kg**
> - **Heat of combustion of hydrogen:** $\Delta h_c = 120{,}000$ **kJ/kg**

## 1.4 The Fire Triangle and the Fire Tetrahedron

> [!NOTE]
> **Fire Triangle (classical):** FUEL + OXYGEN + IGNITION SOURCE = FIRE
>
> **Fire Tetrahedron (modern, used in process safety):** FUEL + OXYGEN + IGNITION SOURCE + UNINHIBITED CHAIN REACTION = FIRE
>
> The fourth side explains why **dry chemical** and **halon** extinguishers work — they interrupt the chain reaction in the gas phase.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Process Plant Fires

Fires in process plants are classified based on the **physical state of the fuel** and the **release mechanism**:

| Fire Type | Fuel State | Release Mechanism | Key Sub-Model |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pool Fire** | Liquid | Spill → pool → vaporize | Thomas / Heskestad / API RP 521 |
| **Jet Fire** | Gas / Pressurized Liquid | Pressurized leak from pipe/valve | Chamberlain / API RP 521 |
| **Flash Fire** | Vapor Cloud | Delayed ignition of dispersing cloud | Kletz / TNO Green Book |
| **Fireball** | Liquefied Gas | BLEVE / catastrophic vessel rupture | Martinsen–Marx / Hymes / Lihou–Maund |
| **Tracer-Line Fire** | Gas | Burning at rupture point | Hoftijzer / API |

## 2.2 The Three Sub-Models in Fire Modeling (Why & How)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A complete fire model in KTU-style calculations consists of **three cascaded sub-models**, and marks are awarded in the exam for clearly identifying each stage.

**Sub-Model A — Source Term (How big is the fire?)**
- *Why:* Determines the total energy released per second.
- *How:* Compute mass burning rate $\dot{m}$ from fuel properties and pool diameter (or jet release rate).
- *Key output:* Total Heat Release Rate $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta h_c$ in kW.

**Sub-Model B — Flame Geometry (What shape is the fire?)**
- *Why:* Needed to compute the radiating surface area and view factor.
- *How:* Use pool diameter → flame height; jet exit velocity → flame length; mass released → fireball diameter.
- *Key output:* Flame height $H_f$, length $L_f$, diameter $D_f$ (all in meters).

**Sub-Model C — Thermal Radiation (How intense at target?)**
- *Why:* The actual consequence endpoint for human/equipment damage.
- *How:* Apply Point Source, Solid Flame, or API RP 521 model.
- *Key output:* Radiative heat flux $q_r$ in kW/m² and thermal dose $I$ in kJ/m².

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Hall Tip:** When writing formulas in the answer sheet, always write the **equation number** and the **unit** of every variable beside it. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for missing units.

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}_\infty''(1 - e^{-k\beta D})$ | Pool fire mass burning rate (kg/m²s) for $D > 0.2$ m |
| 2 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta h_c$ | Total heat release rate (W or kW) |
| 3 | $H_f = 42 D \left[\frac{\dot{m}''}{\rho_a \sqrt{gD}}\right]^{0.61}$ | Thomas flame height for pool fire (m) |
| 4 | $q_r = \frac{\dot{Q} \cdot \chi_r}{4\pi r^2}$ | Point source thermal radiation (kW/m²), $\chi_r$ = radiative fraction |
| 5 | $q_r = \frac{\dot{m}'' \Delta h_c A_f \tau}{4\pi r^2}$ | Shokri–Beyler simplified point source |
| 6 | $q_r = \frac{\dot{m}'' \Delta h_c A_f F_{1 \to 2}}{4\pi r^2}$ | Solid flame model (Shokri–Beyler) with view factor |
| 7 | $L_f = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot \Delta h_c}{0.0059 \pi \rho_a c_p T_a \sqrt{g \cdot d_j}}$ | Chamberlain jet flame length (m), $d_j$ = jet diameter |
| 8 | $D_{max} = 5.8 M^{1/3}$ | Fireball maximum diameter (m), $M$ = fuel mass (kg) — Martinsen–Marx |
| 9 | $t_d = 0.45 M^{1/3}$ | Fireball duration (s) for $M < 30{,}000$ kg — Martinsen–Marx |
| 10 | $D_{max} = 6.48 M^{0.325}$ | Fireball diameter (m) — Lihou–Maund |
| 11 | $q_r = \varepsilon \sigma T_s^4$ | Blackbody radiative flux (W/m²), $T_s$ = surface temp (K) |
| 12 | $I = q_r^{4/3} \cdot t$ | Thermal dose (kJ/m²) — probit input |

> [!NOTE]
> For all distance calculations in **meters** and **kW**, the formula $q_r = \frac{\dot{Q} \chi_r}{4\pi r^2}$ gives the heat flux in kW/m² at distance $r$ (m), where $\dot{Q}$ is in kW. This is the **most-asked formula in KTU ESE for this module**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Layout & Siting (OGP/IPIECA):** Determines inter-unit spacing to prevent escalation.
- **Fire & Explosion Risk Assessment (FERA):** Input to LOPA and QRA.
- **Emergency Response Planning (ERP):** Defines exclusion zone radius for first responders.
- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL) determination:** High radiation demands higher integrity ESD valves.
- **Insurance & Loss Prevention (API, Factory Mutual):** Property loss estimation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Point-Source Thermal Radiation Model

> [!NOTE]
> The Point-Source Model assumes the flame is a **spherical isotropic radiator of total power** $\dot{Q}$ emitting uniformly in all directions. This is the simplest KTU-expected model.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of radiative flux**

The radiative flux $q_r$ at distance $r$ from an isotropic point source of total radiated power $\dot{Q}_r$ equals the power divided by the surface area of an imaginary sphere of radius $r$:

$$
q_r = \frac{\dot{Q}_r}{4\pi r^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the total radiated power**

The total radiated power is the product of the heat release rate and the **radiative fraction** $\chi_r$ (dimensionless, typically 0.10–0.40 for hydrocarbon fires):

$$
\dot{Q}_r = \chi_r \cdot \dot{Q} = \chi_r \cdot \dot{m} \cdot \Delta h_c
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Step 1**

$$
q_r = \frac{\chi_r \cdot \dot{m} \cdot \Delta h_c}{4\pi r^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for atmospheric transmissivity $\tau$**

For long distances ($r > 50$ m), atmospheric absorption (water vapor, CO₂) cannot be neglected:

$$
q_r = \frac{\chi_r \cdot \dot{m} \cdot \Delta h_c \cdot \tau}{4\pi r^2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU ESE 14-mark questions**, atmospheric transmissivity is often computed using the empirical formula (Brzustowski & Sommer):
> $$\tau = 1.006 - 0.0117 \log_{10}(X) - 0.000232 X$$
> where $X = r \cdot p_{H_2O}$ (m·atm), and $p_{H_2O}$ is the partial pressure of water vapor.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Pool Fire Burning Rate (Heskestad / Babrauskas)

**Step 1 — Define the mass burning rate**

The mass burning rate per unit area $\dot{m}''$ (kg/m²·s) is governed by heat transfer from the flame back to the liquid pool surface. For large pools ($D > 0.2$ m), the classical correlation is:

$$
\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}_\infty'' \left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the constants**

- $\dot{m}_\infty''$ = maximum (asymptotic) burning rate for an infinite pool.
- $k\beta$ = empirical extinction coefficient (m⁻¹) that depends on fuel optical properties.
- $D$ = equivalent pool diameter (m).

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation**

- As $D \to 0$: $\dot{m}'' \to 0$ (small pool, heat losses dominate).
- As $D \to \infty$: $\dot{m}'' \to \dot{m}_\infty''$ (large pool, asymptotic limit).

**Step 4 — Reference values for common fuels**

| Fuel | $\dot{m}_\infty''$ (kg/m²s) | $k\beta$ (m⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|
| LNG (on water) | 0.078 | 1.85 |
| Gasoline | 0.085 | 2.10 |
| Methanol | 0.015 | 1.10 |
| Kerosene | 0.105 | 3.50 |

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Fireball Diameter (Martinsen–Marx)

**Step 1 — Energy balance assumption**

A fireball is modeled as a **suddenly released, isotropically burning fuel mass** $M$ (kg). The volume scales with mass cubed (geometric similarity).

$$
V \propto M
$$

**Step 2 — Geometric relation**

For a sphere of diameter $D_{max}$:

$$
V = \frac{\pi}{6} D_{max}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Equate**

$$
\frac{\pi}{6} D_{max}^3 = K \cdot M
$$

Solving for $D_{max}$:

$$
D_{max} = \left(\frac{6K}{\pi}\right)^{1/3} M^{1/3}
$$

**Step 4 — Empirical fit**

Martinsen and Marx calibrated $K$ against experimental data to obtain:

$$
D_{max} = 5.8 \, M^{1/3}
$$

(where $M$ is in kg and $D_{max}$ is in m). Similarly, the **fireball duration** scales as:

$$
t_d = 0.45 \, M^{1/3} \text{ (s)}, \quad M < 30{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A 10 m diameter pool of gasoline burns in open air. Calculate (a) the mass burning rate, (b) total heat release rate, (c) radiative heat flux at 30 m, (d) thermal dose for 20 s exposure.
>
> **Given:** $\dot{m}_\infty'' = 0.085$ kg/m²s, $k\beta = 2.10$ m⁻¹, $\Delta h_c = 43{,}000$ kJ/kg, $\chi_r = 0.20$, $\tau \approx 0.8$ at 30 m, $T_s \approx 1100$ K.

### (a) Mass Burning Rate per unit area

$$
\dot{m}'' = 0.085 \left(1 - e^{-2.10 \times 10}\right)
$$

$$
e^{-21} \approx 7.55 \times 10^{-10} \approx 0
$$

$$
\dot{m}'' \approx 0.085 \text{ kg/m}^2\text{s}
$$

### (b) Total Mass Burning Rate

Pool area:

$$
A_f = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (10)^2 = 78.54 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\dot{m} = \dot{m}'' \cdot A_f = 0.085 \times 78.54 = 6.676 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Heat release rate:

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta h_c = 6.676 \times 43{,}000 = 287{,}070 \text{ kW}
$$

### (c) Radiative Heat Flux at 30 m (Point Source Model)

$$
q_r = \frac{\chi_r \cdot \dot{m} \cdot \Delta h_c \cdot \tau}{4\pi r^2}
$$

$$
q_r = \frac{0.20 \times 6.676 \times 43{,}000 \times 0.8}{4\pi (30)^2}
$$

$$
q_r = \frac{45{,}931}{11{,}310} = 4.06 \text{ kW/m}^2
$$

### (d) Thermal Dose for 20 s Exposure

$$
I = q_r^{4/3} \cdot t = (4.06)^{4/3} \times 20
$$

$$
(4.06)^{4/3} = e^{\frac{4}{3}\ln(4.06)} = e^{\frac{4}{3}(1.401)} = e^{1.868} \approx 6.47
$$

$$
I = 6.47 \times 20 = 129.4 \text{ kJ/m}^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> From standard KTU probit tables, $I \approx 129.4$ kJ/m² corresponds to a **1% fatality** (Probit = 2.67), well below the threshold for 50% fatality ($\approx 1000$ kJ/m²). Hence 30 m is a **safe standoff distance** for gasoline pool fires of this size.

---

## 3.5 Damage Criteria Reference Matrix

| Heat Flux $q_r$ (kW/m²) | Effect on Equipment / Personnel | Exposure Time |
|---|---|---|
| 1.6 | No discomfort to exposed skin | Long duration |
| 4.0 | Pain threshold; blistering begins | 20 s |
| 12.5 | First-degree burns; wood ignites | 10 s |
| 25.0 | Second-degree burns; structural damage | 10 s |
| 37.5 | Third-degree burns; major equipment damage | 10 s |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Fire Model Selection Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **decision logic** used to select the appropriate fire sub-model after a release event. This is the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** expected in the KTU ESE for this topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Release Event Identified] --> B{Fuel State?}
    B -->|Liquid| C{Pressurized?}
    B -->|Gas| D{Released as Jet?}
    B -->|Two-Phase / Cryogenic| E[Catastrophic Vessel Failure]
    C -->|No - Atmospheric Pool| F[Pool Fire Model]
    C -->|Yes| G[Jet Fire Model]
    D -->|Yes - Pressurized Leak| G
    D -->|No - Vapor Cloud Drift| H[Flash Fire Model]
    E --> I[Fireball / BLEVE Model]
    F --> F1[Sub-Model A: Source Term]
    F --> F2[Sub-Model B: Flame Geometry]
    F --> F3[Sub-Model C: Thermal Radiation]
    G --> G1[Sub-Model A: Source Term]
    G --> G2[Sub-Model B: Flame Geometry]
    G --> G3[Sub-Model C: Thermal Radiation]
    H --> H1[Lower Flammability Limit Check]
    H --> H2[Vapor Cloud Footprint]
    I --> I1[Martinsen-Marx or Hymes]
    I --> I2[Fireball Diameter and Duration]
    F1 --> J[Combine A B C]
    F2 --> J
    F3 --> J
    G1 --> J
    G2 --> J
    G3 --> J
    H1 --> K[Output: Affected Area]
    H2 --> K
    I1 --> L[Output: Radiative Dose]
    I2 --> L
    J --> L
    K --> M[Final Consequence Endpoint]
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Three-Stage Pool Fire Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Pool Diameter D] --> A2[Mass Burning Rate m dot]
    A2 --> A3[Heat Release Rate Q dot]
    A3 --> B1[Thomas Flame Height Hf]
    A3 --> B2[Flame Tilt Angle Theta]
    B1 --> C1[Flame Surface Area Af]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[View Factor F12]
    C2 --> D1[Heat Flux at Target qr]
    A3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2[Thermal Dose I]
    D2 --> E[Damage Probability via Probit]
```

## 4.3 Thermal Radiation Energy Pathway (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pool Fire Source] --> Q[Convective Heat 60-80 percent]
    P --> R[Radiative Heat 20-40 percent]
    R --> S[Atmospheric Transmissivity Tau]
    S --> T[Target Distance r]
    T --> U[Received Heat Flux qr kW per m2]
    Q --> V[Hot Plume Rise]
    V --> W[Smoke and Soot]
    U --> X[Probit Calculation]
    X --> Y[Damage Outcome]
```

## 4.4 Concept Map: Relationships Between Fire Sub-Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FT[Fire Tetrahedron] --> FL[Flammability Limits LEL UEL]
    FL --> PFM[Pool Fire Model]
    FL --> JFM[Jet Fire Model]
    FL --> FFM[Flash Fire Model]
    FL --> FBM[Fireball Model]
    PFM --> C1[Source Term]
    JFM --> C1
    FBM --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Flame Geometry]
    C2 --> C3[Radiative Heat Flux]
    C3 --> C4[Thermal Dose]
    C4 --> C5[Consequence Endpoint]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Differentiate between Pool Fire and Jet Fire models with a suitable process plant example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Pool Fire | Jet Fire |
|---|---|---|
| **Fuel State** | Liquid pooled on ground | Pressurized gas / liquid |
| **Release Mechanism** | Spill from ruptured tank / pipe | Leak from pressurized line |
| **Flame Geometry** | Vertical cylinder, may tilt in wind | Turbulent, elongated, near horizontal |
| **Modeling** | Thomas / Heskestad | Chamberlain / API RP 521 |
| **Example** | Bund fire in oil storage tank | Pipe rupture in natural gas line |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for geometry, 1 mark for example)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **List the four sides of the Fire Tetrahedron and explain why the addition of the fourth side is significant in process plant safety.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Fuel** (hydrocarbon liquid, gas)
- **Oxygen** (from air)
- **Ignition Source** (spark, hot surface)
- **Uninhibited Chain Reaction** (free radical propagation) *(1 mark)*

**Significance:** The fourth side is significant because it explains the extinguishing mechanism of **dry chemical powders** and **halon-type agents**, which interrupt the radical chain reaction in the gas phase. Therefore, in process plant safety, this informs selection of fire-fighting media — water cools, foam smothers, dry chemical and halon act on the chain reaction. *(2 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

### (a) Derive the Point Source Model for thermal radiation from a pool fire. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:** *(2 marks)*
1. The flame is a single point isotropic radiator of total power $\dot{Q}_r$.
2. Atmospheric transmissivity $\tau$ is constant along the path.
3. Negligible reflection or re-radiation from ground.
4. Distance $r$ is much greater than the flame diameter.

**Derivation:** *(5 marks)*

Step 1 — Radiative heat flux from a point source at distance $r$ equals total radiated power divided by the spherical surface area of radius $r$:

$$
q_r = \frac{\dot{Q}_r}{4\pi r^2}
$$

Step 2 — The total radiated power is the product of heat release rate and radiative fraction $\chi_r$:

$$
\dot{Q}_r = \chi_r \cdot \dot{m} \cdot \Delta h_c
$$

Step 3 — Include atmospheric transmissivity $\tau$:

$$
q_r = \frac{\chi_r \cdot \dot{m} \cdot \Delta h_c \cdot \tau}{4\pi r^2}
$$

**Note on units:** When $\dot{Q}_r$ is in kW and $r$ in meters, $q_r$ comes out directly in kW/m².

---

### (b) A 15 m diameter pool of LNG burns on water. Using Point Source Model, calculate (i) total heat release rate, (ii) radiative heat flux at 50 m, (iii) thermal dose for 15 s. Given: $\dot{m}_\infty'' = 0.078$ kg/m²s, $k\beta = 1.85$ m⁻¹, $\Delta h_c = 50{,}000$ kJ/kg, $\chi_r = 0.20$, $\tau = 0.78$ at 50 m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step (i) — Mass burning rate and HRR** *(3 marks)*

$$
\dot{m}'' = 0.078 \left(1 - e^{-1.85 \times 15}\right) = 0.078 \left(1 - e^{-27.75}\right) \approx 0.078 \text{ kg/m}^2\text{s}
$$

Pool area:

$$
A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}(15)^2 = 176.7 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.078 \times 176.7 = 13.78 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{Q} = 13.78 \times 50{,}000 = 689{,}000 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step (ii) — Heat flux at 50 m** *(2 marks)*

$$
q_r = \frac{0.20 \times 689{,}000 \times 0.78}{4\pi (50)^2} = \frac{107{,}484}{31{,}416} = 3.42 \text{ kW/m}^2
$$

**Step (iii) — Thermal dose for 15 s** *(2 marks)*

$$
I = q_r^{4/3} \cdot t = (3.42)^{4/3} \times 15
$$

$$
(3.42)^{4/3} = e^{\frac{4}{3} \ln 3.42} = e^{1.637} \approx 5.14
$$

$$
I = 5.14 \times 15 = 77.1 \text{ kJ/m}^2
$$

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

### (a) Explain the Martinsen–Marx model for fireball diameter and duration. A pressurized LPG tank containing 8000 kg of propane undergoes a BLEVE. Compute the fireball diameter and duration. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Explanation of the Model** *(3 marks)*

The Martinsen–Marx model is a **geometric similarity model** for a fireball formed by sudden release and combustion of a fuel mass. It assumes:

- The fireball is a sphere of diameter $D_{max}$ formed at peak.
- The volume scales linearly with fuel mass.
- The fireball duration scales with the cube root of mass.

The empirical correlations are:

$$
D_{max} = 5.8 \, M^{1/3} \quad \text{and} \quad t_d = 0.45 \, M^{1/3}
$$

(valid for $M < 30{,}000$ kg).

**Calculation for 8000 kg of propane** *(4 marks)*

$$
D_{max} = 5.8 \times (8000)^{1/3} = 5.8 \times 20 = 116 \text{ m}
$$

$$
t_d = 0.45 \times (8000)^{1/3} = 0.45 \times 20 = 9 \text{ s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The fireball reaches a diameter of 116 m and persists for 9 s. Personnel within a radius of about 150 m must be evacuated **before** ignition.

---

### (b) With a neat flowchart, explain the three-stage methodology of fire modeling in process plant safety. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The three-stage methodology consists of:

**Stage 1 — Source Term Determination (2 marks)**
Determines the total energy released per second:

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta h_c
$$

Inputs: mass burning rate (for pool) or release rate (for jet) and heat of combustion.

**Stage 2 — Flame Geometry Determination (2 marks)**
Determines physical size and shape of the flame:
- Pool fire: Thomas flame height $H_f$
- Jet fire: Chamberlain flame length $L_f$
- Fireball: Martinsen–Marx diameter $D_{max}$

**Stage 3 — Thermal Radiation Calculation (2 marks)**
Determines heat flux at target using Point Source or Solid Flame model:

$$
q_r = \frac{\chi_r \cdot \dot{Q} \cdot \tau}{4\pi r^2}
$$

**Flowchart (1 mark):** *See the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes for the equivalent.*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — $\dot{m}$ in kg/s and $\Delta h_c$ in J/kg (not kJ/kg) gives $q_r$ in W/m². Mixing kJ with kg/s produces an answer off by a factor of 1000. KTU examiners check this carefully.
> 2. **Ignoring atmospheric transmissivity $\tau$** — Beyond 50 m, $\tau < 1$ and a *point-source-without-$\tau$* answer over-predicts the hazard distance. KTU deducts 1–2 marks for this.
> 3. **Using the wrong burning-rate regime** — For $D < 0.2$ m, the asymptotic equation $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}_\infty''(1 - e^{-k\beta D})$ is invalid; use the laminar $\dot{m}'' \approx 0.05$ kg/m²s.
> 4. **Forgetting the radiative fraction $\chi_r$** — A common mistake is using $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \Delta h_c$ directly in the point-source formula. KTU expects the radiated component $\chi_r \cdot \dot{Q}$.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** — In 7-mark derivations, failing to list the assumptions of the chosen model typically costs 2 marks.
> 6. **Probit conversion skipped** — When asked about "damage to personnel," students often stop at $q_r$ or $I$ and forget to convert to a probit value and then to a percentage fatality. KTU specifically tests this final step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4, Models for Fire**

- **Fire Triangle vs Fire Tetrahedron** — Tetrahedron adds *uninhibited chain reaction*; explains halon/dry-chemical extinction.
- **Four canonical fire types** — Pool, Jet, Flash, Fireball (BLEVE).
- **Three-stage methodology** — Source Term → Flame Geometry → Thermal Radiation.
- **Point Source Model** — $q_r = \dfrac{\chi_r \dot{m} \Delta h_c \tau}{4\pi r^2}$; valid when $r \gg D_f$.
- **Solid Flame Model** — Replaces point source with view factor $F_{1 \to 2}$ for short distances.
- **Pool fire mass burning rate** — $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}_\infty''(1 - e^{-k\beta D})$ for $D > 0.2$ m.
- **Thomas flame height** — $H_f = 42 D \left(\dfrac{\dot{m}''}{\rho_a \sqrt{gD}}\right)^{0.61}$.
- **Chamberlain jet flame length** — Scales with $\dot{m}^{1/2}$ and inversely with $d_j^{1/2}$.
- **Martinsen–Marx fireball** — $D_{max} = 5.8 M^{1/3}$ m, $t_d = 0.45 M^{1/3}$ s (for $M < 30{,}000$ kg).
- **Lihou–Maund fireball** — $D_{max} = 6.48 M^{0.325}$ m (alternative correlation).
- **Thermal dose** — $I = q_r^{4/3} \cdot t$ in kJ/m² (probit input).
- **Damage threshold fluxes** — 1.6, 4.0, 12.5, 25.0, 37.5 kW/m².
- **Radiative fraction $\chi_r$** — 0.10 to 0.40 for hydrocarbons; use 0.20 as default.
- **Atmospheric transmissivity $\tau$** — Decreases with distance; check whether to include it.
- **Standard reference fuels** — Methane (50,000 kJ/kg), Propane (46,000 kJ/kg), Gasoline (43,000 kJ/kg), Hydrogen (120,000 kJ/kg).
- **Stainless Steel / Equipment failure threshold** — ~25 kW/m² sustained; <10 s for escalation.
- **Personnel fatality threshold** — ~1000 kJ/m² (50% fatality via probit).
- **Always include units** in every KTU answer — examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
- **Always state the model assumptions** — minimum 3 in any 7-mark derivation question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Explosion and Toxic Gas Dispersion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Explosion and Toxic Gas Dispersion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — EXPLOSION AND TOXIC GAS DISPERSION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Explosion in Process Plants

An **explosion** in a process plant is a rapid, nearly instantaneous release of energy accompanied by a shock wave, flying debris, thermal radiation, and violent mechanical disruption. From the KTU 2024 syllabus standpoint, explosions are classified by their **physical mechanism** and the **energy source** driving the blast.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Syllabus-Exact):** An explosion in a process plant is a sudden, violent release of energy resulting from the rapid oxidation of a flammable vapor, gas, dust, or from the catastrophic failure of a pressurized vessel, producing damaging overpressure, fragment projection, and thermal effects.

**Formal Categorization of Process Plant Explosions**

| Category | Energy Source | Typical Example |
|---|---|---|
| Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE) | Combustion of released hydrocarbon cloud | LPG release → cloud ignition |
| Unconfined Vapor Cloud Explosion (UVCE) | Same as VCE, but in open terrain | Flixborough disaster (1974), UK |
| Confined Explosion | Combustion inside vessels/buildings | Furnace or reactor rupture |
| Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion (BLEVE) | Pressure vessel failure above boiling point | Propane tank rupture |
| Dust Explosion | Combustion of suspended fine particles | Grain silo, coal handling |
| Mechanical Explosion | Over-pressurization of non-reactive vessel | Compressed air receiver burst |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Memory:** *"Very Cold Drinks Make Me Sneeze"* — **VCE, Confined, Dust, Mechanical, BLEVE, S**tructural failure.

---

### 1.2 Definition of Toxic Gas Dispersion

**Toxic gas dispersion** is the atmospheric transport and dilution of a hazardous gaseous release downwind from a process unit, governed by the physics of fluid turbulence, molecular diffusion, and buoyancy effects. The KTU syllabus treats it as a key consequence modeling topic under the umbrella of **QRA (Quantitative Risk Assessment)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Syllabus-Exact):** Toxic gas dispersion refers to the downwind movement of a chemical contaminant after an accidental release, characterized by concentration profiles that decrease with distance according to dispersion models (e.g., Pasquill-Gifford, Britter-McQuaid), and is quantified by toxicity thresholds such as AEGL, ERPG, and IDLH.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition**

> Imagine a **puff of cigarette smoke in a still room** versus the same puff in an open field on a windy day. In the still room, the smoke spreads slowly in concentric, lazy curls — by the time it reaches your nose, the smell is faint. In the open field, wind **shears** the puff into a long, thinning ribbon. Toxic gas dispersion behaves identically: the **same mass of chlorine released** into a windy, sunny afternoon becomes a thin, fast-moving plume, while in a calm, stable night, it spreads slowly but persists at lethal concentrations for kilometers.

**Atmospheric Stability Classes (Pasquill Classification)** — central to dispersion:

| Class | Stability | Surface Wind (m/s) | Solar Insolation | Visual Cue |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Very Unstable | 1 – 5 | Strong Sun | Hot pavement shimmer |
| **B** | Unstable | 1 – 5 | Moderate Sun | Visible thermal eddies |
| **C** | Slightly Unstable | 2 – 5 | Slight Sun | Mild thermal activity |
| **D** | Neutral | ≥ 5 | Any | Strong wind dominates |
| **E** | Stable | 1 – 5 | Thin overcast | Calm cold evening |
| **F** | Very Stable | < 2 | Clear Night | Temperature inversion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Class D (Neutral)** is the standard reference for engineering calculations because the worst-case meteorological data for design safety studies typically assumes neutral stability.

---

### 1.3 The Three Physical Phenomena Driving Both Topics

| Phenomenon | In Explosions | In Gas Dispersion |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy release rate** | Determines blast overpressure | Determines source strength $Q$ (kg/s) |
| **Atmospheric conditions** | Influences flame propagation speed | Determines $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_z$ dispersion coefficients |
| **Geometry/confinement** | Multi-energy effects in VCE | Determines buoyancy (heavy gas slumping) |

**Toxicological Endpoints (Critical Thresholds for Dispersion Studies)**

- **AEGL (Acute Exposure Guideline Level)** — 3 tiers (AEGL-1, 2, 3) for exposure times 10 min, 30 min, 1 h, 4 h, 8 h.
- **ERPG (Emergency Response Planning Guideline)** — ERPG-1, 2, 3 from AIHA.
- **IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health)** — NIOSH-published value.
- **PEL / TLV** — Occupational exposure limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU exams, you must remember that **AEGL-3** (lethal) and **ERPG-3** are usually the design endpoints for consequence modeling of toxic releases.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Explosion Damage Modeling — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.1.1 TNT Equivalency Method

The simplest (and most conservative) approach. It assumes the explosion energy is equivalent to a mass of TNT and uses empirical TNT blast curves to predict overpressure vs distance.

**Step-by-Step Logic**

1. Compute total energy released by the hydrocarbon combustion:
$$E = M \cdot \Delta H_c$$
   where $M$ = mass of fuel (kg), $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion (kJ/kg).

2. Apply a yield factor $\eta$ (typical value **0.01 to 0.10** for VCE, often **0.03 to 0.05**):
$$E_{eff} = \eta \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_c$$

3. Convert to equivalent TNT mass. TNT's standard energy: **$E_{TNT} = 4680 \text{ kJ/kg}$**:
$$W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$$

4. Compute the **scaled distance** $Z$ (Hopkinson's scaling):
$$Z = \frac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$$

5. Read off overpressure $\Delta P$ from TNT blast curves or use the **modified Baker equation** for incident overpressure:
$$\frac{\Delta P}{P_0} = \frac{808 \left[1 + \left(\frac{Z}{4.5}\right)^2\right]}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{Z}{0.048}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{Z}{0.32}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{Z}{1.35}\right)^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a yield factor $\eta$?** In reality, only a fraction of fuel vapor in a cloud actually participates in the deflagration (much escapes combustion due to dilution and incomplete mixing). Hence the need for $\eta$.

---

### 2.1.2 TNO Multi-Energy Method (Van den Berg)

A more realistic approach where the explosion energy is **concentrated** in regions of high congestion/obstruction. Energy is assigned to one of **10 blast strength classes**.

**Step-by-Step Logic**

1. Identify congested regions in the cloud volume.
2. Assign an initial blast strength $n$ (typical $n$ = **3 to 7** for process plants; $n$ = **10** for highly confined).
3. Estimate the **charge energy** per unit volume:
$$E_v = \frac{H \cdot V}{V_{cloud}}$$
   where $H$ = combustion energy per unit volume (typically **3.5 MJ/m³** for stoichiometric hydrocarbons).
4. Use TNO charts of dimensionless overpressure vs scaled distance $R'$:
$$R' = \frac{R}{\left(E_v\right)^{1/3}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Multi-Energy Method is **preferred over TNT** for offshore platforms and congested process plants because it captures partial confinement realistically.

---

### 2.1.3 Baker-Strehlow-Tang (BST) Method

Combines **flame speed** (a function of fuel reactivity and congestion) with **geometric expansion** (3D, 2D, 1D). Less conservative than TNT but more sophisticated.

**Key Equation (Mach Stem Formation Limit):**
$$E_{\text{eff}} = \frac{V_{\text{flame}}}{c_0} \cdot E_{\text{TNT-equivalent}}$$

The BST method produces flame speed $M_f$ (Mach number) from a tri-nomograph of:
- Fuel reactivity (Low / Medium / High)
- Congestion (Low / Medium / High)
- Confinement (1D / 2D / 3D expansion)

---

### 2.1.4 Damage Criteria from Overpressure

| Overpressure $\Delta P$ (kPa) | Damage Observed |
|---|---|
| **1.0** | Window breakage (light damage) |
| **6.9** | Roof tiles displaced |
| **15** | Storage tanks rupture |
| **20.7** | Reinforced concrete damage |
| **34.5** | 50% building demolition |
| **68.9** | Total building destruction |
| **138** | Threshold for lethality (1% fatality) |
| **≥ 200** | Severe lung rupture (50% fatality) |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU board numerical problems, **6.9 kPa (1 psi)** is the standard design threshold used to define the "lethal radius" or "heavy damage distance."

---

## 2.2 Toxic Gas Dispersion — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.2.1 Pasquill-Gifford (P-G) Gaussian Plume Model

The **workhorse model** for neutral buoyancy gas dispersion. The fundamental steady-state equation for an elevated point source is:

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \left[\exp\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

where:
- $C$ = concentration (kg/m³) at point $(x, y, z)$
- $Q$ = source strength (kg/s)
- $u$ = mean wind speed at release height (m/s)
- $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ = horizontal and vertical dispersion coefficients (functions of downwind distance $x$ and stability class)
- $H$ = effective release height (m) = stack height + plume rise $\Delta H$

**At ground level** ($z = 0$), the equation simplifies to:

$$C_{ground}(x,y,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

**Centerline Maximum Ground Concentration** (at $y = 0$):

$$C_{max}(x) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

---

### 2.2.2 Dispersion Coefficients — Briggs' Formulas

For Pasquill-Gifford class, Briggs proposed analytical approximations (urban conditions):

$$\sigma_y(x) = a \cdot x^{0.9031}$$

$$\sigma_z(x) = b \cdot x^{d}$$

where $a, b, d$ are stability-class dependent constants (Briggs Tables).

---

### 2.2.3 Pasquill-Gifford Dispersion Coefficient Tables

**Table (Rural Conditions, Briggs Urban Approximation):**

| Class | $\sigma_y$ (m at $x = 1$ km) | $\sigma_z$ (m at $x = 1$ km) |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | 213 | 1000+ |
| **B** | 156 | 110 |
| **C** | 104 | 58 |
| **D** | 68 | 33 |
| **E** | 50 | 24 |
| **F** | 34 | 18 |

(Approximate; refer to CCPS *Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis* for full Briggs tables.)

---

### 2.2.4 Effective Stack Height (Holland's Formula)

The plume rise $\Delta H$ for buoyant releases:

$$\Delta H = \frac{1.6 \cdot v_s \cdot d \cdot (1.5 T_s - T_a)}{u}$$

where:
- $v_s$ = stack gas exit velocity (m/s)
- $d$ = stack diameter (m)
- $T_s$ = stack gas temperature (K)
- $T_a$ = ambient temperature (K)
- $u$ = wind speed (m/s) at stack height

> [!NOTE]
> **Important for KTU:** If $\sigma_z$ becomes very large and exceeds $H$ significantly, then $\exp(-H^2/2\sigma_z^2) \approx 1$, and ground concentration becomes approximately **independent** of release height. This is called **uniform mixing assumption**.

---

### 2.2.5 Heavy / Dense Gas Dispersion

When the released gas has a **molecular weight greater than air** (e.g., chlorine $M = 70.9$, propane $M = 44.1$), gravity-driven slumping dominates initially. Models: **DEGADIS, SLAB, HEGADAS**.

**Britter-McQuaid (BM) Model — Key Parameter:**

$$\text{Concentration by volume } \psi = \frac{C}{C_0} \quad \text{where } C_0 = \text{initial concentration}$$

$$\text{Dimensionless downwind distance } X = \frac{x}{\left(\frac{V_0}{u}\right)^{1/2}}$$

$$\text{Dimensionless crosswind distance } Y = \frac{y}{\left(\frac{V_0}{u}\right)^{1/2}}$$

where $V_0$ = initial cloud volume (m³).

---

### 2.2.6 Probit Equations for Toxicity (Health Damage)

The **Probit variable** $Y$ links exposure concentration $C$ (ppm) and exposure duration $t$ (min) to fatality probability:

$$Y = a + b \ln(C^n \cdot t)$$

**Probability of fatality:**
$$P = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y-5} \exp\left(-\frac{u^2}{2}\right) du = 50\left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$

**Common Probit Equations (TNO Green Book):**

| Chemical | Probit Equation |
|---|---|
| Chlorine | $Y = -5.04 + 0.635 \ln(C^{1.64} t)$ |
| Ammonia | $Y = -9.82 + 0.71 \ln(C^2 t)$ |
| Hydrogen Fluoride | $Y = -8.4 + 1.0 \ln(C t)$ |
| Acrolein | $Y = -2.92 + 0.808 \ln(C^{1.5} t)$ |
| Phosgene | $Y = -7.4 + 0.509 \ln(C^{1.5} t)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always remember the form $Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$. The constants $a, b, n$ are **chemical-specific** and you must substitute them correctly.

---

### 2.2.7 BLEVE and Fireball Models

**Fireball Diameter (CCPS Correlation):**

$$D_{max} = 6.48 \cdot M^{0.325}$$

where $D_{max}$ in meters, $M$ = fuel mass in kg.

**Fireball Duration:**
$$t_d = 0.852 \cdot M^{0.26}$$

**Thermal Radiation from Fireball (at distance $r$):**
$$q = \frac{E \cdot F_{21}}{4\pi r^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> BLEVE produces the highest thermal flux of any process accident — design separation distances for LPG bullets are largely dictated by this single phenomenon.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Following Table is the "Master Reference" you must memorize for KTU ESE.** Every formula here has appeared in past university exams.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TNT Equivalent | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$ | $E_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg |
| 2 | Scaled Distance | $Z = R / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$ | $R$ in m, $W_{TNT}$ in kg |
| 3 | TNO Scaled Distance | $R' = R / (E_v)^{1/3}$ | $E_v$ in J/m³ |
| 4 | P-G Plume (3D) | $C(x,y,z) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} e^{-y^2/2\sigma_y^2}[e^{-(z-H)^2/2\sigma_z^2} + e^{-(z+H)^2/2\sigma_z^2}]$ | kg/m³ |
| 5 | Ground Centerline | $C_{max} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} e^{-H^2/2\sigma_z^2}$ | $y = 0, z = 0$ |
| 6 | Plume Rise (Holland) | $\Delta H = \dfrac{1.6 v_s d (1.5 T_s - T_a)}{u}$ | m |
| 7 | Probit Variable | $Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$ | $C$ in ppm, $t$ in min |
| 8 | Probability | $P = 50[1 + \text{erf}((Y-5)/\sqrt{2})]$ | % |
| 9 | Fireball Diameter | $D_{max} = 6.48 M^{0.325}$ | m, M in kg |
| 10 | Fireball Duration | $t_d = 0.852 M^{0.26}$ | seconds |
| 11 | Thermal Flux | $q = E F_{21} / 4\pi r^2$ | kW/m² |
| 12 | Damage Threshold | $\Delta P = 6.9$ kPa = 1 psi | Standard lethal radius |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — TNT Equivalency Method (FULL Worked-Out Solution)

**Problem Statement (KTU University Exam Pattern):**

A petroleum storage facility releases **10,000 kg of propane** into the atmosphere, which subsequently forms a vapor cloud that ignites. The propane heat of combustion is **46,000 kJ/kg**. Assume a yield factor $\eta = 0.05$. Compute:
(i) The equivalent TNT mass.
(ii) The distance at which the overpressure equals **6.9 kPa** (1 psi, structural damage threshold).
(iii) The distance at which $\Delta P = 34.5$ kPa (50% building demolition).

**Use the Baker Equation:**
$$\frac{\Delta P}{P_0} = \frac{808 \left[1 + (Z/4.5)^2\right]}{\sqrt{1 + (Z/0.048)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (Z/0.32)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (Z/1.35)^2}}$$

where $P_0 = 101.325$ kPa.

---

**Solution — Step (i): Compute Equivalent TNT Mass**

$$E_{released} = M \cdot \Delta H_c = 10{,}000 \times 46{,}000 = 4.6 \times 10^{8} \text{ kJ}$$

$$E_{eff} = \eta \cdot E_{released} = 0.05 \times 4.6 \times 10^{8} = 2.3 \times 10^{7} \text{ kJ}$$

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{E_{eff}}{E_{TNT}} = \frac{2.3 \times 10^{7}}{4680} = 4914.5 \text{ kg TNT equivalent}$$

> The cloud is energetically equivalent to **4914.5 kg (≈ 4.9 tonnes) of TNT**.

---

**Solution — Step (ii): Distance at 6.9 kPa**

We need to solve Baker's equation iteratively for $Z$ when $\Delta P = 6.9$ kPa.

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P_0} = \frac{6.9}{101.325} = 0.0681$$

**Trial 1:** Assume $Z = 5$:
- Numerator: $808[1 + (5/4.5)^2] = 808[1 + 1.2346] = 1805.7$
- Denominator: $\sqrt{1 + (5/0.048)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (5/0.32)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (5/1.35)^2}$
- $(5/0.048)^2 = 10,855$; $\sqrt{1 + 10855} = \sqrt{10856} = 104.2$
- $(5/0.32)^2 = 244.1$; $\sqrt{1 + 244.1} = \sqrt{245.1} = 15.66$
- $(5/1.35)^2 = 13.72$; $\sqrt{1 + 13.72} = \sqrt{14.72} = 3.837$
- Denominator: $104.2 \times 15.66 \times 3.837 = 6261$
- $\Delta P/P_0 = 1805.7 / 6261 = 0.2885$ → way too high.

**Trial 2:** $Z = 20$:
- Numerator: $808[1 + (20/4.5)^2] = 808[1 + 19.75] = 16{,}766$
- Denominator factors:
- $(20/0.048)^2 = 173,611$; $\sqrt{173612} = 416.7$
- $(20/0.32)^2 = 3,906$; $\sqrt{3907} = 62.5$
- $(20/1.35)^2 = 219.6$; $\sqrt{220.6} = 14.85$
- Denominator: $416.7 \times 62.5 \times 14.85 = 386,738$
- $\Delta P/P_0 = 16{,}766 / 386{,}738 = 0.0434$ → too low.

**Trial 3:** $Z = 12$:
- Numerator: $808[1 + (12/4.5)^2] = 808[1 + 7.111] = 6553.5$
- Denominator factors:
- $(12/0.048)^2 = 62,500$; $\sqrt{62501} = 250.0$
- $(12/0.32)^2 = 1406.25$; $\sqrt{1407.25} = 37.51$
- $(12/1.35)^2 = 79.06$; $\sqrt{80.06} = 8.948$
- Denominator: $250 \times 37.51 \times 8.948 = 83,902$
- $\Delta P/P_0 = 6553.5 / 83,902 = 0.0781$ → slightly high.

**Trial 4:** $Z = 14$:
- Numerator: $808[1 + (14/4.5)^2] = 808[1 + 9.679] = 8629.5$
- Denominator factors:
- $(14/0.048)^2 = 85,069$; $\sqrt{85070} = 291.7$
- $(14/0.32)^2 = 1914$; $\sqrt{1915} = 43.76$
- $(14/1.35)^2 = 107.5$; $\sqrt{108.5} = 10.42$
- Denominator: $291.7 \times 43.76 \times 10.42 = 133,007$
- $\Delta P/P_0 = 8629.5 / 133,007 = 0.0649$ → slightly low.

**Interpolating between $Z = 12$ (0.0781) and $Z = 14$ (0.0649) for $\Delta P/P_0 = 0.0681$:**

$$Z \approx 12 + 2 \times \frac{0.0781 - 0.0681}{0.0781 - 0.0649} = 12 + 2 \times \frac{0.0100}{0.0132} = 13.51 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**Computing the distance:**
$$R = Z \cdot W_{TNT}^{1/3} = 13.51 \times 4914.5^{1/3} = 13.51 \times 17.0 = 229.7 \text{ m}$$

> **Damage radius (6.9 kPa): R ≈ 230 m**

---

**Solution — Step (iii): Distance at 34.5 kPa**

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P_0} = \frac{34.5}{101.325} = 0.3404$$

**Trial 1:** $Z = 3$:
- Numerator: $808[1 + (3/4.5)^2] = 808[1 + 0.4444] = 1167$
- Denominator:
- $(3/0.048)^2 = 3906.25$; $\sqrt{3907.25} = 62.51$
- $(3/0.32)^2 = 87.89$; $\sqrt{88.89} = 9.428$
- $(3/1.35)^2 = 4.938$; $\sqrt{5.938} = 2.437$
- Denominator: $62.51 \times 9.428 \times 2.437 = 1436$
- $\Delta P/P_0 = 1167 / 1436 = 0.813$ → too high.

**Trial 2:** $Z = 4$:
- Numerator: $808[1 + (4/4.5)^2] = 808[1 + 0.790] = 1446$
- Denominator:
- $(4/0.048)^2 = 6944$; $\sqrt{6945} = 83.34$
- $(4/0.32)^2 = 156.25$; $\sqrt{157.25} = 12.54$
- $(4/1.35)^2 = 8.78$; $\sqrt{9.78} = 3.127$
- Denominator: $83.34 \times 12.54 \times 3.127 = 3268$
- $\Delta P/P_0 = 1446 / 3268 = 0.443$ → too high.

**Trial 3:** $Z = 5$ (already computed) $= 0.2885$ → too low.

**Interpolating between $Z = 4$ (0.443) and $Z = 5$ (0.2885) for target 0.3404:**
$$Z \approx 4 + 1 \times \frac{0.443 - 0.3404}{0.443 - 0.2885} = 4 + \frac{0.1026}{0.1545} = 4.664 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**Computing the distance:**
$$R = Z \cdot W_{TNT}^{1/3} = 4.664 \times 17.0 = 79.3 \text{ m}$$

> **Heavy damage radius (34.5 kPa): R ≈ 79 m**

---

**Tabulated Results Summary:**

| Overpressure (kPa) | Damage Type | Scaled Distance $Z$ (m/kg$^{1/3}$) | Actual Distance $R$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **6.9** | Window breakage, light structural | 13.51 | **230** |
| **34.5** | 50% building demolition | 4.66 | **79** |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Pasquill-Gifford Ground Concentration

**Problem Statement:**

A chlorine storage cylinder develops a leak releasing **Q = 0.5 kg/s** of $Cl_2$ at a stack of **effective height H = 30 m**. The wind speed at stack height is **u = 4 m/s** under **Class C (slightly unstable)** stability. Using Briggs' dispersion coefficients at **downwind distance x = 1 km**:
- $\sigma_y = 104$ m
- $\sigma_z = 58$ m

Compute:
(i) Maximum ground centerline concentration.
(ii) Compare to AEGL-1 (0.5 ppm = 1.45 mg/m³) and ERPG-2 (3 ppm = 8.7 mg/m³).
(iii) State the type of public action required.

---

**Solution — Step (i):**

Convert $Q$ to concentration basis (kg/m³ to mg/m³ multiply by 10⁶ for direct $\mu$g/m³). Using the ground-level centerline formula:

$$C_{max} = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

Compute the exponential term:
$$\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2} = \frac{30^2}{2 \times 58^2} = \frac{900}{6728} = 0.1338$$

$$\exp(-0.1338) = 0.8748$$

Compute the prefactor:
$$\frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} = \frac{0.5}{\pi \times 4 \times 104 \times 58} = \frac{0.5}{75,820} = 6.595 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Multiply by exponential:
$$C_{max} = 6.595 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.8748 = 5.769 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/m}^3 = 5.769 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

Convert to ppm (Cl₂ molecular weight = 71; 24.45 L/mol at 25°C, 1 atm):
$$C_{ppm} = \frac{5.769 \times 24.45}{71} = 1.987 \approx 2.0 \text{ ppm}$$

---

**Solution — Step (ii) and (iii):**

| Threshold | Value (ppm) | Our Concentration | Comparison | Public Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AEGL-1 | 0.5 | 2.0 | Exceeded by 4× | Mild, transient effects — voluntary sheltering |
| ERPG-2 | 3.0 | 2.0 | Within bounds | Sheltering-in-place recommended |
| ERPG-3 | 20.0 | 2.0 | Not exceeded | Life-threatening above this |

> **Interpretation:** At **1 km downwind under Class C stability**, the chlorine plume concentration (2.0 ppm) exceeds AEGL-1 by 4×, meaning the public may experience mild sensory irritation. Voluntary evacuation should be considered for sensitive populations, but since ERPG-2 is not exceeded, **sheltering-in-place is the appropriate action** rather than evacuation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Probit Calculation for Toxicity

**Problem Statement:**

A worker is exposed to **C = 200 ppm ammonia** for **t = 10 minutes** due to a valve leak. Using the probit equation for ammonia $Y = -9.82 + 0.71 \ln(C^2 t)$, compute the fatality probability.

---

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute $C^n t$:
$$C^n t = 200^2 \times 10 = 40{,}000 \text{ (ppm}^2 \cdot \text{min)}$$

Step 2: Compute $\ln(C^n t)$:
$$\ln(40{,}000) = \ln(4 \times 10^4) = \ln 4 + 4 \ln 10 = 1.3863 + 9.2103 = 10.5966$$

Step 3: Compute Probit $Y$:
$$Y = -9.82 + 0.71 \times 10.5966 = -9.82 + 7.5236 = -2.2964$$

Step 4: Convert Probit to probability:
$$P = 50\left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] = 50\left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{-2.2964 - 5}{1.4142}\right)\right]$$

$$= 50\left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{-7.2964}{1.4142}\right)\right] = 50\left[1 + \text{erf}(-5.159)\right]$$

Using the Gaussian error function table: $\text{erf}(-5.159) \approx -0.99999$ (effectively $-1$):
$$P = 50[1 + (-1)] \approx 0.0005\%$$

> **Interpretation:** The fatality probability is essentially **0%**. However, **irreversible health effects** (severe respiratory irritation, laryngeal edema) may still occur at this exposure, so PPE upgrades and medical surveillance are mandatory.

> [!NOTE]
> Probit equations typically apply to **lethality**, not morbidity. For non-lethal toxic injury, IDLH or AEGL-3 thresholds are used.

---

| **Output** | Overpressure vs distance | Overpressure vs distance | Overpressure vs distance | Concentration vs distance |
| **Best for** | Open terrain VCE | Offshore, congested plants | Process unit siting | Routine safety studies |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Explosion Consequence Assessment Workflow (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

The following block diagram illustrates the systematic decision flow used by process safety engineers when assessing an explosion event using the three primary methodologies.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Release Scenario] --> B[Estimate Fuel Mass M and Heat of Combustion Hc]
    B --> C{Choose Modeling Method}
    C -->|Conservative Estimate| D[TNT Equivalency Method]
    C -->|Congested Plant| E[TNO Multi-Energy Method]
    C -->|Detailed Design| F[Baker-Strehlow-Tang BST]
    D --> G[Compute Effective Energy Eeff = eta x M x Hc]
    E --> H[Assign Blast Strength Class n = 3 to 10]
    F --> I[Select Flame Speed Mach Number from Tri-Nomograph]
    G --> J[Calculate Scaled Distance Z]
    H --> K[Calculate TNO Scaled Distance R prime]
    I --> L[Apply BST Overpressure Correlation]
    J --> M[Read Overpressure from TNT Curves]
    K --> N[Read Overpressure from TNO Charts]
    L --> O[Compare to Damage Criteria Table]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Determine Lethal and Damage Radii]
    P --> Q[Issue Final Consequence Report with Safety Distances]
    Q --> R[Decision: Evacuate / Shelter / Mitigate]
```

---

## 4.2 Toxic Gas Dispersion Modeling Workflow (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

This schematic illustrates the sequential decision logic employed in dispersion modeling, from incident identification through consequence communication to emergency responders.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Detect Release or Estimate Source Strength Q kg per s] --> B1[Identify Chemical and Physical State]
    B1 --> C1{Is Gas Lighter or Heavier than Air}
    C1 -->|Lighter MW less than 29| D1[Apply Pasquill-Gaussian Plume Model]
    C1 -->|Heavier MW greater than 29| E1[Apply Britter-McQuaid or DEGADIS Dense Gas Model]
    D1 --> F1[Determine Atmospheric Stability Class A to F]
    F1 --> G1[Fetch Wind Speed u at Release Height]
    G1 --> H1[Calculate Effective Release Height H with Plume Rise]
    H1 --> I1[Compute Ground Centerline Concentration Cmax]
    E1 --> J1[Apply Slumping Correction and BM Correlations]
    J1 --> I1
    I1 --> K1[Plot Iso-Concentration Contours]
    K1 --> L1[Compare Against AEGL and ERPG Thresholds]
    L1 --> M1[Define Evacuation and Sheltering Zones]
    M1 --> N1[Issue Public Warning via ALOHA or PHAST Software]
```

## 4.3 Damage Effects Zonation Around a Process Plant (Mermaid Architecture Block)

This block diagram represents the spatial zones of damage around an explosion epicenter, illustrating how the overpressure threshold determines the type of structural and human impact.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    zone1[ZONE 1 - CRITICAL: Delta P greater than 138 kPa - 1 percent fatality and total destruction]
    zone2[ZONE 2 - HEAVY: Delta P 34.5 to 138 kPa - 50 percent demolition threshold]
    zone3[ZONE 3 - MODERATE: Delta P 6.9 to 34.5 kPa - window breakage and light injuries]
    zone4[ZONE 4 - MILD: Delta P less than 6.9 kPa - minor cosmetic damage and ear discomfort]
    zone1 --> zone2 --> zone3 --> zone4
    zone1 -.Reference.-> ref1[Compare to Lethal Dose LD50 Threshold]
    zone2 -.Reference.-> ref2[Compare to 50 percent Building Collapse Limit]
    zone3 -.Reference.-> ref3[Compare to OSHA Hearing Conservation Limit]
    zone4 -.Reference.-> ref4[Compare to Public Notification Threshold]
```

## 4.4 Probit-to-Fatality Probability Conversion Pipeline

This block-level schematic visualizes the chain of transformations from raw exposure data to fatality probability — a critical KTU examination topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Step 1: Identify Toxic Chemical and Its Probit Constants a, b, n] --> s2[Step 2: Determine Exposure Concentration C in ppm]
    s2 --> s3[Step 3: Determine Exposure Duration t in minutes]
    s3 --> s4[Step 4: Compute Probit Variable Y = a + b x ln C to the power n x t]
    s4 --> s5[Step 5: Apply Inverse Probit Y to Probability P using Error Function]
    s5 --> s6[Step 6: Tabulate P versus Distance to Plot Iso-Risk Contours]
    s6 --> s7[Step 7: Compare P to Individual Risk Criterion 1 in 10000 per year]
    s7 --> decision{Is P within Acceptable Risk Band}
    decision -->|Yes| s8[Risk Acceptable: No Additional Mitigation]
    decision -->|No| s9[Risk Unacceptable: Implement Additional Safety Barriers]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (Each 3 Marks)

---

### **Q1. Define a Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE). State any four conditions necessary for its occurrence.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)** is the combustion of a flammable vapor cloud in an open or partially confined area, producing damaging overpressure and thermal radiation.

**Four essential conditions:**
1. **Release of flammable substance** in sufficient quantity to form a cloud.
2. **Mixing with air** within the **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)** and **Upper Explosive Limit (UEL)** range.
3. **Delay period** between release and ignition allowing cloud growth.
4. **Presence of an ignition source** with sufficient energy to ignite the cloud.
5. (Optional 5th) Partial confinement or congestion to accelerate flame speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** *"Release, Mix, Delay, Ignite"* — the four-word checklist examiners love.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Pasquill-Gifford and Britter-McQuaid dispersion models.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Pasquill-Gifford | Britter-McQuaid |
|---|---|---|
| **Gas Density** | Light/neutral buoyancy gases | Heavy/dense gases (gravity slumping) |
| **Mathematical Basis** | Gaussian plume equation | Empirical correlations from wind-tunnel experiments |
| **Inputs** | Wind speed, stability class A–F, source strength, stack height | Initial cloud volume $V_0$, concentration $C_0$, wind speed, distance |
| **Output** | Concentration vs distance contour | Dimensionless concentration $\psi$ vs dimensionless distance $X$ |
| **Application** | Routine plant safety studies, long-term averages | Instantaneous or short-duration dense gas releases (LNG, chlorine) |
| **Limitation** | Inaccurate for heavy gas near source | Valid only for instantaneous/puff releases, requires $C_0$ measurement |

---

## PART B — EXTENDED ANSWER QUESTIONS (Each 14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module Choice Rule:** KTU's ESE for Module 4 provides an internal choice. Either **Question A** OR **Question B** must be answered (each carries 14 marks, typically split as 7 + 7).

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the procedure for damage assessment using the **TNT Equivalency Method** for a vapor cloud explosion. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A process plant releases **8000 kg of cyclohexane** during a flange leak. The vapor cloud ignites forming a VCE. Cyclohexane heat of combustion = **43,400 kJ/kg**; assume yield factor $\eta = 0.04$. Determine the **damage distances** for overpressures of (i) **6.9 kPa** and (ii) **34.5 kPa** using the Baker equation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A(a)**

The **TNT Equivalency Method** is the most widely taught explosion damage assessment procedure. It converts the energy of a chemical explosion into an equivalent mass of TNT, then uses well-established TNT blast curves.

**Procedure (with stepwise explanation):**

**Step 1: Identify the released mass and chemical properties.**
- Determine the total mass $M$ of the flammable material released (kg).
- Determine the heat of combustion $\Delta H_c$ of the material (kJ/kg).
- These values are typically available from the Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS).

**Step 2: Compute the total energy released.**
- The total combustion energy is:
$$E_{released} = M \cdot \Delta H_c$$

**Step 3: Apply a yield factor $\eta$.**
- In real vapor clouds, only a fraction of the fuel actually burns (incomplete mixing, dilution).
- Apply a yield factor typically **$\eta = 0.01$ to $0.10$**.
$$E_{eff} = \eta \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_c$$

**Step 4: Convert to equivalent TNT mass.**
- The standard heat of detonation of TNT is **$E_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg**.
$$W_{TNT} = \frac{E_{eff}}{E_{TNT}}$$

**Step 5: Compute the scaled distance (Hopkinson scaling).**
- The dimensionless scaled distance normalizes for charge mass:
$$Z = \frac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$$
  where $R$ is the actual distance from the explosion epicenter.

**Step 6: Use the Baker correlation to predict incident overpressure.**
$$\frac{\Delta P}{P_0} = \frac{808 \left[1 + \left(\frac{Z}{4.5}\right)^2\right]}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{Z}{0.048}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{Z}{0.32}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{Z}{1.35}\right)^2}}$$

**Step 7: Compare against damage criteria.**
- Use the standard table: 6.9 kPa → structural damage; 34.5 kPa → 50% building collapse; 138 kPa → lethal.

**Sketch Description (Schematic Layout):**

```
       Vapor Cloud Boundary
       ┌─────────────────────┐
       │   ╳ ╳ ╳ Ignition     │
       │   ╳ ╳ ╳ Point        │
       └─────┬───────────────┘
             │ Energy Released
             ▼
       Equivalent TNT W_TNT
             │
             ▼
       Scaled Distance Z = R / W_TNT^(1/3)
             │
             ▼
       Baker Equation ──> ΔP vs R curve
             │
             ▼
       Compare to Damage Criteria Table
```

> [!NOTE]
> Sketch should depict the explosion epicenter, concentric circles of damage radius, and the analytical chain from mass to overpressure.

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A(b)**

**Given Data:**
- $M = 8000$ kg
- $\Delta H_c = 43{,}400$ kJ/kg
- $\eta = 0.04$
- $E_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg
- Atmospheric pressure $P_0 = 101.325$ kPa

**Step 1: Compute equivalent TNT mass:**
$$E_{eff} = 0.04 \times 8000 \times 43{,}400 = 1.3888 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ}$$

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{1.3888 \times 10^7}{4680} = 2967.5 \text{ kg}$$

$$W_{TNT}^{1/3} = 2967.5^{1/3} = 14.36 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**Step (i): Damage distance at $\Delta P = 6.9$ kPa:**

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P_0} = \frac{6.9}{101.325} = 0.0681$$

**Trial and error for $Z$:**

- Try $Z = 15$: Numerator = $808[1 + (15/4.5)^2] = 808[1 + 11.11] = 8985$
  - Denominator factors: $\sqrt{1 + (15/0.048)^2} = 312.5$; $\sqrt{1 + (15/0.32)^2} = 47.0$; $\sqrt{1 + (15/1.35)^2} = 11.16$
  - Denominator: $312.5 \times 47.0 \times 11.16 = 163,910$
  - $\Delta P/P_0 = 8985 / 163,910 = 0.0548$ → too low

- Try $Z = 13$: Numerator = $808[1 + (13/4.5)^2] = 808[1 + 8.346] = 7552$
  - Denominator: $\sqrt{1 + (13/0.048)^2} = 270.84$; $\sqrt{1 + (13/0.32)^2} = 40.66$; $\sqrt{1 + (13/1.35)^2} = 9.674$
  - Denominator: $270.84 \times 40.66 \times 9.674 = 106,540$
  - $\Delta P/P_0 = 7552 / 106,540 = 0.0709$ → very close

- Interpolating for exact $\Delta P/P_0 = 0.0681$:
  - Slope between $Z = 13$ (0.0709) and $Z = 15$ (0.0548) is $-0.00805$ per unit $Z$.
  - Required decrease from 0.0709 is $0.0709 - 0.0681 = 0.0028$.
  - $Z = 13 + 0.0028 / 0.00805 = 13.35$

$$R_1 = Z \times W_{TNT}^{1/3} = 13.35 \times 14.36 = 191.7 \text{ m}$$

**Step (ii): Damage distance at $\Delta P = 34.5$ kPa:**

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P_0} = \frac{34.5}{101.325} = 0.3404$$

**Trial and error:**

- Try $Z = 5$: Already computed earlier in Section 3.1 → $\Delta P/P_0 = 0.2885$ → too low.
- Try $Z = 4$: From Section 3.1 → $\Delta P/P_0 = 0.443$ → too high.
- Linear interpolation:
  - Slope: $(0.443 - 0.2885) / (4 - 5) = -0.1545$ per unit $Z$
  - Required increase from 0.2885 to 0.3404 is $0.0519$.
  - $Z = 5 - 0.0519 / 0.1545 = 5 - 0.336 = 4.664$

$$R_2 = Z \times W_{TNT}^{1/3} = 4.664 \times 14.36 = 66.97 \text{ m}$$

**Final Results:**

| Overpressure $\Delta P$ | Damage Type | Distance $R$ (m) |
|---|---|---|
| **6.9 kPa** | Window breakage, light damage | **≈ 192 m** |
| **34.5 kPa** | 50% building demolition | **≈ 67 m** |

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Pasquill-Gifford Gaussian Plume Model** with its mathematical formulation. State the six Pasquill stability classes with their physical significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A refinery vent stack emits **SO₂** at a rate of **Q = 1.2 kg/s**. The effective stack height is **H = 60 m**, wind speed at stack height is **u = 6 m/s**, and stability is **Class D (Neutral)**. At a downwind distance of **x = 2 km**, the dispersion coefficients are:
- $\sigma_y = 95$ m
- $\sigma_z = 42$ m

Compute:
(i) The **centerline maximum ground-level concentration**.
(ii) Compare with the WHO 24-hour ambient air quality standard of **125 µg/m³**.
(iii) State whether the location of a residential colony **1.5 km** downwind is safe.

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A(a) — Equivalent for Question B(a)**

*(Structured for clarity — note that for Question B(a), answer the same conceptual framework but on dispersion instead of explosion.)*

The **Pasquill-Gifford Gaussian Plume Model** is the most widely used **steady-state** dispersion model for neutrally buoyant continuous releases from an elevated point source.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

For a continuous, steady-state point source at height $H$ in an open terrain with mean wind speed $u$ along the $x$-axis:

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \left[\exp\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

**At ground level** ($z = 0$), the two image-source exponentials become equal:

$$C(x,y,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

**Six Pasquill Stability Classes (Physical Significance):**

| Class | Description | Physical Meaning | Typical Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Very Unstable | Strong thermal updrafts, vigorous vertical mixing | Hot, sunny afternoon, low wind |
| **B** | Unstable | Moderate convection, plumes loop vigorously | Sunny afternoon, light wind |
| **C** | Slightly Unstable | Mild thermal activity | Partly cloudy afternoon |
| **D** | Neutral | Mechanical turbulence dominates | Overcast, strong wind |
| **E** | Stable | Strong temperature inversion, limited vertical mixing | Clear night, light wind |
| **F** | Very Stable | Strong inversion, plumes stay elevated or follow terrain | Clear, calm night |

**Practical Engineering Significance:**

- Class A produces the **highest ground concentrations** but smallest downwind extent due to strong vertical mixing.
- Class F produces **lower ground concentrations** but the plume can travel **hundreds of kilometers** without significant dilution.
- For conservative plant safety design, **Class D** is most commonly used as the engineering reference.
- For worst-case toxic exposure, **Class F (stable)** combined with low wind speed is often the design case for ground-level concentrations.

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B(b)**

**Given:**
- $Q = 1.2$ kg/s
- $H = 60$ m
- $u = 6$ m/s
- $x = 2$ km
- $\sigma_y = 95$ m, $\sigma_z = 42$ m

**Step 1: Compute the exponential term.**

$$\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2} = \frac{60^2}{2 \times 42^2} = \frac{3600}{3528} = 1.0204$$

$$\exp(-1.0204) = 0.3603$$

**Step 2: Compute the prefactor.**

$$\frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} = \frac{1.2}{\pi \times 6 \times 95 \times 42} = \frac{1.2}{75{,}175.5} = 1.596 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3: Maximum centerline ground concentration.**

$$C_{max} = 1.596 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.3603 = 5.751 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$= 5.751 \text{ mg/m}^3 = 5751 \text{ µg/m}^3$$

**Step 4: Compare with WHO standard (125 µg/m³).**

$$C_{max} = 5751 \text{ µg/m}^3 \gg 125 \text{ µg/m}^3$$

The concentration exceeds the WHO 24-hour standard by **46×**.

> **Interpretation:** At 2 km downwind under Class D conditions, the SO₂ concentration grossly exceeds health standards. The stack height of 60 m is insufficient for this emission rate at this wind speed.

**Step 5: Re-compute at 1.5 km to assess residential safety.**

For typical Class D dispersion coefficients at 1.5 km (linear interpolation between known values), approximate:
- $\sigma_y \approx 80$ m
- $\sigma_z \approx 36$ m

$$\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2} = \frac{3600}{2 \times 36^2} = \frac{3600}{2592} = 1.389$$

$$\exp(-1.389) = 0.249$$

$$\frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} = \frac{1.2}{\pi \times 6 \times 80 \times 36} = \frac{1.2}{54{,}288} = 2.211 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$C_{max}^{1.5km} = 2.211 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.249 = 5.505 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/m}^3 = 5505 \text{ µg/m}^3$$

**Conclusion:** At **1.5 km**, the ground concentration is **5505 µg/m³**, which is still **44× above** the WHO standard. **A residential colony at 1.5 km is NOT SAFE** — evacuation and stack height redesign (or operating-hour restrictions) are required.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Failing to convert $C_{max}$ from kg/m³ to µg/m³ or ppm when comparing with standards costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the exponential term:** A student who writes $C_{max} = Q/(\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z)$ without the $\exp(-H^2/2\sigma_z^2)$ factor will overestimate concentration and **lose 2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Stability class misuse:** Choosing Class A or B for "worst case" without understanding that for ground concentration, **Class F is often worse** because of poor vertical mixing.
> 4. **Trial-and-error errors in TNT:** Not showing two iterations and interpolation leads to **1 mark** loss. Always show at least 2 trials and the linear interpolation step.
