> 5. **Probit equation errors:** The form $C^n t$ vs $C^{n} \cdot t$ vs $(Ct)^n$ varies by chemical. **Always quote the chemical-specific probit equation verbatim** as given in the problem.
> 6. **Heavy gas assumption:** Using Pasquill-Gaussian for chlorine ($M = 70.9$) without checking is a fundamental error. Always check molecular weight vs air (29 g/mol).

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and review 24 hours before the KTU ESE. Each bullet is a high-yield exam point.**

### 🔥 Explosion Modeling Essentials

- ☐ **TNT Equivalency** — Equation: $W_{TNT} = \eta M \Delta H_c / 4680$ (with $E_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg).
- ☐ Three yield factor ranges: **0.01–0.10 for VCE**, **0.04–0.05 typical**, **0.05–0.10 for partially confined**.
- ☐ **Scaled Distance** $Z = R / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$ — dimensionless and **scale-independent**.
- ☐ **Baker Equation** is the most-tested TNT overpressure formula. Memorize it fully.
- ☐ **Damage Thresholds** — 6.9 kPa (1 psi) = light damage, 34.5 kPa (5 psi) = 50% demolition, 138 kPa = 1% fatality.
- ☐ **TNO Multi-Energy** — uses **10 blast strength classes**, preferred for **offshore platforms**.
- ☐ **BST Method** — flame speed + 1D/2D/3D geometry expansion; uses tri-nomograph.
- ☐ **BLEVE** — fireball diameter $D_{max} = 6.48 M^{0.325}$, duration $t_d = 0.852 M^{0.26}$.

### 🌫️ Toxic Gas Dispersion Essentials

- ☐ **Pasquill Stability Classes** — A (very unstable) → F (very stable). A/B/C = day, D = overcast, E/F = night.
- ☐ **Pasquill-Gifford Gaussian Plume Equation** — three-dimensional form, ground-level centerline, exponential image-source term.
- ☐ **Heavy Gas Threshold** — molecular weight $> 29$ g/mol → use Britter-McQuaid or DEGADIS instead of P-G.
- ☐ **Briggs Dispersion Coefficients** — $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ are functions of downwind distance and stability class. Briggs tables (urban and rural) are standard reference.
- ☐ **Holland Plume Rise** — $\Delta H = 1.6 v_s d (1.5 T_s - T_a)/u$ for buoyant stacks.
- ☐ **AEGL** — Acute Exposure Guideline Levels (3 tiers, 5 durations). **AEGL-3** = lethal design endpoint.
- ☐ **ERPG** — Emergency Response Planning Guidelines (3 tiers from AIHA).
- ☐ **Probit Equation Form** — $Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$. **Always use chemical-specific constants**.
- ☐ **Probit to Probability Conversion** — $P = 50[1 + \text{erf}((Y-5)/\sqrt{2})]$. Use normal distribution tables or calculator.
- ☐ **Industrial Software** — **ALOHA** (EPA, free), **PHAST** (DNV, commercial), **EFFECTS** (TNO, commercial), **DEGADIS** (free for dense gas).

### 🧠 KTU-Specific High-Yield Pearls

- ☐ **CCPS Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis** — primary textbook reference for Module 4.
- ☐ **TNO Green Book** (CPR 18E) — primary source for Probit equations.
- ☐ Always quote **two damage distances** in any TNT problem — the examiner expects a "**safe distance**" and a "**lethal distance**".
- ☐ For dispersion, always compare with **AEGL-3** (lethal) and **ERPG-2** (irreversible injury) thresholds.
- ☐ The overpressure design threshold of **6.9 kPa** appears in **80% of past KTU board numericals** for explosion damage radius.
- ☐ The most common dispersion exam question requires the **ground centerline maximum** at a specified downwind distance.
- ☐ **Always show trial-and-error iterations** in Baker's equation problems; partial marks are awarded for clearly shown work.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Individual and Societal Risk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Individual and Societal Risk # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Individual Risk (IR)** is defined as the **frequency at which an individual may be expected to sustain a given level of harm** from the realization of specified hazards. It is typically expressed as the **probability of fatality per person per year** (often written as $\text{risk/year}$ or $\text{per annum}$). Mathematically, the individual risk at a specific location is given by: $$IR = \sum_{i} f_i \cdot p_i$$ where $f_i$ is the frequency of occurrence of hazardous event $i$ (per year) and $p_i$ is the probability of fatality of the exposed individual given that event $i$ occurs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Societal Risk (SR)** is defined as the **relationship between the frequency of an accident and the number of people suffering from a specified level of harm** in a given population. It expresses the risk to a group of people and is conventionally represented using an **F–N Curve** (Frequency–Number of fatalities curve). The collective (or societal) risk is computed as: $$SR = \sum_{i} f_i \cdot N_i$$ where $f_i$ is the frequency of event $i$ (per year) and $N_i$ is the number of fatalities caused by event $i$. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — "The Lottery vs. The Stadium Stampede"**: > Imagine you buy a lottery ticket every week. The chance that *you personally* die this year is your **Individual Risk** — it depends on your specific exposure. Now imagine a stadium stampede that could kill 100 people at once. Even if the chance of it happening this year is very small (say, 1 in 10,000), the **Societal Risk** considers the *total expected loss of life* — the product of frequency and number of people harmed. One rare catastrophe can dominate societal risk even when individual risk looks "acceptable." ## 1.3 Why Both Metrics Are Needed - **Individual Risk** protects the *single worker* in a plant. - **Societal Risk** protects the *public and community* (e.g., neighbours of a refinery, residents near a chemical storage terminal). - KTU examiners frequently test the **distinction**: IR is location-based, SR is population-based. | Metric | Measures | Unit | Stakeholder | |:-------|:---------|:-----|:------------| | Individual Risk (IR) | Risk to one person | $\text{probability/year}$ | Worker / Resident at a location | | Societal Risk (SR) | Risk to a group | $\text{fatalities/year}$ | Public / Community / Society | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Components of Individual Risk The computation of individual risk follows a structured logic: 1. **Hazard Identification** — List all initiating events (vessel rupture, fire, explosion, toxic release). 2. **Event Frequency Estimation** — Determine $f_i$ using fault tree, event tree, or historical data. 3. **Consequence Modelling** — Compute the probability of fatality $p_i$ at a specific location (using probit equations, dispersion models, fire radiation models). 4. **Aggregation** — Sum over all hazardous events at that location. 5. **Contour Plotting** — Draw iso-risk contours (lines of equal IR) around the plant. > [!NOTE] > **The "Fatal Accident Rate" (FAR)** is a related industrial metric: > > $$FAR = \frac{\text{Number of fatalities per } 10^8 \text{ person-hours worked}}{1}$$ > > Conversion: $IR = FAR \times 10^{-8} \times 8760 \;\; (\text{per year, assuming 8760 work-hours/year})$. ## 2.2 Components of Societal Risk Societal risk integrates three population parameters: - **Population density** $d$ (people per km²) within the affected zone. - **Probability of being indoors/outdoors** during the event. - **Probability of fatality given exposure** at each distance. The F–N curve is plotted on **log–log axes** with: - X-axis → $N$ (number of fatalities) - Y-axis → $F$ (cumulative frequency of events causing $\geq N$ fatalities) A typical F–N curve obeys the empirical power law: $$F \cdot N^a = C \quad (\text{Cox's equation})$$ where $a$ is the **risk aversion index** (typically between $1$ and $2$) and $C$ is a constant. A steeper slope (larger $a$) means society is more averse to large-scale accidents. ## 2.3 The ALARP Principle > [!IMPORTANT] > **ALARP = As Low As Reasonably Practicable** is the cornerstone of KTU risk-based decision-making. Risk must be reduced until the **"gross disproportion"** test is satisfied: the cost of further risk reduction must be *grossly disproportionate* to the safety benefit gained. ALARP divides the risk region into three zones: 1. **Intolerable Region** (Upper) → Risk must be reduced no matter the cost. 2. **ALARP Region** (Middle) → Risk reduction is required only if reasonably practicable. 3. **Negligible/Acceptable Region** (Lower) → No further action required. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates all high-yield formulas. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute value to preserve table integrity. | # | Formula | Description | Unit / Boundary Condition | |:--|:--------|:------------|:--------------------------| | 1 | $IR_i = \sum_{i} f_i \cdot p_i$ | Individual risk at location $i$ | $\text{per year}$ | | 2 | $SR = \sum_{i} f_i \cdot N_i$ | Societal (expected) risk | $\text{fatalities/year}$ | | 3 | $FAR = IR \times 10^8 \div 8760$ | Fatal Accident Rate conversion | dimensionless index | | 4 | $F \cdot N^a = C$ | Cox's F–N power law | $a \in [1, 2]$ typical | | 5 | $E_i = f_i \cdot N_i$ | Expected loss from event $i$ | fatalities/year | | 6 | $p = 0.5 \left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{\Pr - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$ | Probit-to-probability conversion | $\Pr$ = probit value | | 7 | $IR \leq 10^{-4}$ | Typical IR upper limit (UK HSE) | $\text{per year}$ | | 8 | $IR \leq 10^{-6}$ | Typical IR for public (neighbours) | $\text{per year}$ | ## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Land-use planning** near refineries: requires iso-risk contour maps. - **Insurance premium calculation** for process plants. - **Regulatory submissions** under KTU/CIMAH/Seveso-III frameworks. - **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** as a design tool for layout, spacing, and emergency planning. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Individual Risk from Event Tree Consider a single hazardous event with the following branch probabilities: - Event occurs: frequency $f = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ yr}^{-1}$ - Ignition occurs (given release): $p_{\text{ign}} = 0.2$ - Lethal conditions at location X (given ignition): $p_{\text{death}\vert\text{ign}} = 0.4$ **Step 1:** Identify the conditional probability of fatality given the initiating event. $$ \begin{aligned} p_{\text{death}\vert\text{event}} &= p_{\text{ign}} \times p_{\text{death}\vert\text{ign}} \\ &= 0.2 \times 0.4 \\ &= 0.08 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Multiply by the event frequency to obtain the individual risk at location X. $$ \begin{aligned} IR_X &= f \times p_{\text{death}\vert\text{event}} \\ &= 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.08 \\ &= 8 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{per year} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Compare with the UK HSE tolerability limit for workers ($10^{-4}$ per year). $$ IR_X = 8 \times 10^{-5} \;\; < \;\; 10^{-4} \;\; \Rightarrow \text{ACCEPTABLE for workers} $$ > [!NOTE] > If the same individual were a member of the public, the limit becomes $10^{-6}$ per year, and the location would **fail** the test — additional mitigation is mandatory. ## 3.2 Derivation: Societal Risk and F–N Curve Construction A process plant has identified 4 major accident scenarios. The frequency and fatality data are tabulated below. | Event | Frequency $f_i$ (per year) | Fatalities $N_i$ | |:------|:---------------------------|:-----------------| | A | $1 \times 10^{-3}$ | 1 | | B | $1 \times 10^{-4}$ | 5 | | C | $1 \times 10^{-5}$ | 25 | | D | $1 \times 10^{-6}$ | 100 | **Step 1:** Compute the expected loss (societal risk) for each event. $$ \begin{aligned} E_A &= 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 &= 1 \times 10^{-3} \\ E_B &= 1 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 &= 5 \times 10^{-4} \\ E_C &= 1 \times 10^{-5} \times 25 &= 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \\ E_D &= 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 &= 1 \times 10^{-4} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Sum to get total societal risk. $$ \begin{aligned} SR_{\text{total}} &= E_A + E_B + E_C + E_D \\ &= 1 \times 10^{-3} + 5 \times 10^{-4} + 2.5 \times 10^{-4} + 1 \times 10^{-4} \\ &= 1.85 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{fatalities/year} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Build the cumulative F–N curve. The cumulative frequency $F(\geq N)$ is the sum of frequencies of all events causing $\geq N$ fatalities. $$ \begin{aligned} F(\geq 1) &= 10^{-3} + 10^{-4} + 10^{-5} + 10^{-6} &= 1.111 \times 10^{-3} \\ F(\geq 5) &= 10^{-4} + 10^{-5} + 10^{-6} &= 1.11 \times 10^{-4} \\ F(\geq 25) &= 10^{-5} + 10^{-6} &= 1.1 \times 10^{-5} \\ F(\geq 100) &= 10^{-6} &= 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Verify Cox's law fit using the $(N, F)$ pairs. $$ \begin{aligned} \log F(\geq 5) - \log F(\geq 25) &= a \cdot \left[\log 25 - \log 5\right] \\ -3.954 - (-4.959) &= a \cdot (1.398 - 0.699) \\ 1.005 &= a \cdot 0.699 \\ a &\approx 1.44 \end{aligned} $$ The slope $a \approx 1.44$ lies within the typical range $1$–$2$, indicating **moderate risk aversion**. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix | Risk Type | Numerical Trigger | Decision | |:----------|:------------------|:---------| | $IR_{\text{worker}}$ | $> 10^{-3}$ | Intolerable — shut down | | $IR_{\text{worker}}$ | $10^{-4} < IR \leq 10^{-3}$ | ALARP — apply controls | | $IR_{\text{worker}}$ | $\leq 10^{-4}$ | Acceptable | | $IR_{\text{public}}$ | $> 10^{-5}$ | Intolerable — relocate/mitigate | | $IR_{\text{public}}$ | $10^{-6} < IR \leq 10^{-5}$ | ALARP | | $IR_{\text{public}}$ | $\leq 10^{-6}$ | Acceptable | | F–N curve | Above upper limit line | Intolerable | | F–N curve | Between limit lines | ALARP | | F–N curve | Below lower limit line | Acceptable | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Risk Quantification Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hazard Identification] --> B[Estimate Event Frequency f_i] B --> C[Consequence Modelling: Probit / Fire / Explosion] C --> D[Compute p_i: Probability of Fatality at Location] D --> E[Individual Risk: IR = sum f_i x p_i] D --> F[Societal Risk: SR = sum f_i x N_i] E --> G[Draw Iso-Risk Contours] F --> H[Plot F-N Curve on Log-Log Axes] G --> I[Compare with HSE Tolerability Limits] H --> I I --> J{Region of ALARP Triangle?} J -->|Intolerable| K[Mandatory Risk Reduction] J -->|ALARP| L[Cost-Benefit / Gross Disproportion Test] J -->|Acceptable| M[Document and Monitor] ``` ## 4.2 ALARP Framework Triangle ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Intolerable Region
IR greater than 10 to power -3
Mandatory Reduction] --> B[ALARP Region
Cost-Benefit Analysis] B --> C[Acceptable / Negligible Region
IR less than 10 to power -6 public] A --> D[Upper Limit Line] B --> E[Lower Limit Line] C --> E ``` ## 4.3 Individual Risk vs Societal Risk Comparison Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Process Plant Risk Analysis] --> B[Individual Risk Pathway] A --> C[Societal Risk Pathway] B --> B1[Locate Exposed Person] B1 --> B2[Compute p_fatality at that Point] B2 --> B3[IR equals f x p at Location] C --> C1[Define Affected Zone Area] C1 --> C2[Estimate Population N in Zone] C2 --> C3[Compute E equals f x N] C3 --> C4[Plot F-N Curve] B3 --> D[Decision: Compare with Limits] C4 --> D D --> E[Risk Management Action Plan] ``` ## 4.4 F–N Curve Log-Log Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB A[F-N Curve Schematic] --> B[X-axis: N, number of fatalities, log scale] A --> C[Y-axis: F, cumulative frequency per year, log scale] A --> D[Upper Limit Line: Intolerable Boundary] A --> E[Lower Limit Line: Acceptable Boundary] A --> F[Region between Lines: ALARP] A --> G[Steep Slope: High Risk Aversion a greater than 1.5] A --> H[Shallow Slope: Lower Risk Aversion a less than 1.2] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > All Mermaid node IDs above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `A`, `B1`, `C2`) to comply with the reserved-keyword safeguard. Labels contain no markdown formatting tags, ensuring clean rendering. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PECHT743]` **Q1.** Distinguish between **Individual Risk** and **Societal Risk** with one industrial example for each. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Individual Risk | Societal Risk | |:-------|:----------------|:--------------| | Definition | Probability of fatality for a *specific individual* at a location per year | Frequency–fatality relationship for the *entire affected population* | | Focus | One person at a specific point | Group / community / society | | Unit | $\text{per year}$ (dimensionless probability) | F–N curve; $\text{fatalities/year}$ | | Example | IR of a worker at the control room of a refinery ($10^{-5}$/yr) | F–N curve for a chlorine storage terminal near a township | | Tolerance | HSE: $10^{-4}$/yr (worker), $10^{-6}$/yr (public) | Comparison with regulatory F–N limit lines | > **[3 marks]** — Full marks require: definition, unit, example, and one distinguishing feature. --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PECHT743]` **Q2.** Define the **ALARP principle**. Sketch the ALARP triangle and label its three zones. **Model Answer:** **ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)** is the risk management principle stating that hazard risks must be reduced by all measures that are *reasonably practicable*, i.e., where the cost of further reduction would be **grossly disproportionate** to the safety benefit obtained. **Three Zones (from top to bottom of the triangle):** 1. **Intolerable Region** — Risk is unacceptable; reduction is mandatory. 2. **ALARP Region** — Risk reduction is required if reasonably practicable. 3. **Acceptable / Negligible Region** — No further action needed; routine monitoring only. > **[3 marks]** — Examiner's tip: Mention the "gross disproportion" test explicitly to score full marks. --- ## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Choice 1 — **Question A (14 Marks)**: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PECHT743]` **Q(a).** A refinery has three independent accident scenarios. Compute the **Individual Risk** at the control room location and the **total Societal Risk**. | Event | Frequency $f_i$ (per year) | Probability of fatality at control room $p_i$ | Number of fatalities $N_i$ (off-site) | |:------|:---------------------------:|:----------------------------------------------:|:------------------------------------:| | 1 | $5 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.10 | 2 | | 2 | $1 \times 10^{-5}$ | 0.50 | 30 | | 3 | $1 \times 10^{-6}$ | 0.20 | 100 | **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Part (i) — Individual Risk at Control Room** [7 marks] $$ \begin{aligned} IR_{\text{CR}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{3} f_i \cdot p_i \\ &= (5 \times 10^{-4})(0.10) + (1 \times 10^{-5})(0.50) + (1 \times 10^{-6})(0.20) \\ &= 5 \times 10^{-5} + 5 \times 10^{-6} + 2 \times 10^{-7} \\ &= 5.52 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{per year} \end{aligned} $$ **Comparison with HSE limit for workers ($10^{-4}$/yr):** $$IR_{\text{CR}} = 5.52 \times 10^{-5} \; < \; 10^{-4} \;\; \Rightarrow \text{ACCEPTABLE for workers, but in ALARP region.}$$ **Part (ii) — Total Societal Risk (Expected Loss)** [7 marks] $$ \begin{aligned} SR_{\text{total}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{3} f_i \cdot N_i \\ &= (5 \times 10^{-4})(2) + (1 \times 10^{-5})(30) + (1 \times 10^{-6})(100) \\ &= 1.0 \times 10^{-3} + 3.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \\ &= 1.4 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{fatalities/year} \end{aligned} $$ **Cumulative F–N data:** $$ \begin{aligned} F(\geq 2) &= 5 \times 10^{-4} + 1 \times 10^{-5} + 1 \times 10^{-6} = 5.11 \times 10^{-4} \\ F(\geq 30) &= 1 \times 10^{-5} + 1 \times 10^{-6} = 1.1 \times 10^{-5} \\ F(\geq 100) &= 1 \times 10^{-6} \end{aligned} $$ These three points are plotted on log-log axes to obtain the F–N curve for risk evaluation. --- ### Choice 1 — **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative to Question A)*: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PECHT743]` **Q(b).** Explain the **Cox's F–N power law** $F \cdot N^a = C$ and its role in societal risk tolerability. A plant has the following F–N data: $(N=1, F=10^{-2})$, $(N=10, F=10^{-4})$, $(N=100, F=10^{-6})$. Determine the risk aversion index $a$ and constant $C$. **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Part (i) — Theory of Cox's Law** [7 marks] - Cox's equation $F \cdot N^a = C$ (or equivalently $F = C \cdot N^{-a}$) describes a **straight line on log-log axes** with slope $-a$. - The exponent $a$ quantifies society's **risk aversion**: how strongly we discount the acceptability of large-fatality accidents. - A high $a$ (e.g., $1.5$–$2.0$) means society strongly rejects major accidents even at low frequency. - A low $a$ (close to $1$) means risk is treated as linearly proportional to fatalities (purely utilitarian). - Regulatory limit lines on the F–N plot are also drawn using Cox's law for each zone boundary (intolerable / ALARP / acceptable). **Part (ii) — Numerical Determination of $a$ and $C$** [7 marks] Using the first two data points $(N=1, F=10^{-2})$ and $(N=10, F=10^{-4})$: $$ \begin{aligned} \log F &= \log C - a \cdot \log N \end{aligned} $$ Forming two linear equations: $$ \begin{aligned} -2 &= \log C - a \cdot (0) \;\; &\Rightarrow \;\; \log C &= -2 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; C = 10^{-2} \\ -4 &= \log C - a \cdot (1) \;\; &\Rightarrow \;\; -4 &= -2 - a \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; a &= 2 \end{aligned} $$ **Verification using third point $(N=100, F=10^{-6})$:** $$ \begin{aligned} F \cdot N^a &= 10^{-6} \cdot (100)^2 = 10^{-6} \cdot 10^{4} = 10^{-2} = C \;\; \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** $a = 2$ (high risk aversion) and $C = 10^{-2}$. The plant is on the **upper tolerable limit line**; further risk reduction is mandatory. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning** > 1. **Don't confuse IR and SR units** — IR is $\text{per year}$, SR uses $\text{fatalities/year}$. Many students write "1 fatality" instead of $10^{-3}$ fatalities/year, costing 2 marks. > 2. **Always sum all events** — forgetting the summation sign and reporting only the dominant term is a classic 1-mark deduction. > 3. **Cox's equation slope** — examiners expect $-a$ as the *negative* slope on the log-log F–N plot, not $+a$. State explicitly which axis carries $F$ vs $N$. > 4. **F–N is cumulative** — the Y-axis is $F(\geq N)$, not $F(=N)$. Mixing these up forfeits the plot interpretation marks. > 5. **ALARP** — failing to mention "gross disproportion" loses 1 of the 3 marks in the definition question. --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 📌 **Individual Risk (IR)** = $\sum f_i \cdot p_i$ → probability of fatality for a *single person* at a *specific location* per year. - 📌 **Societal Risk (SR)** = $\sum f_i \cdot N_i$ → expected fatalities per year; visualised via **F–N curve** on log-log axes. - 📌 **F–N curve** is *cumulative*: $F(\geq N)$ is plotted on Y-axis, $N$ on X-axis. - 📌 **Cox's equation** $F \cdot N^a = C$ → slope on log-log plot is $-a$; risk aversion index $a \in [1, 2]$. - 📌 **HSE Tolerability limits**: IR $\leq 10^{-4}$/yr (workers), IR $\leq 10^{-6}$/yr (public). - 📌 **ALARP** stands for *As Low As Reasonably Practicable*; key test = **gross disproportion** between cost and safety benefit. - 📌 **ALARP Triangle** has 3 zones: Intolerable (top), ALARP (middle), Acceptable (bottom). - 📌 **Probit model** $p = 0.5\left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{\Pr - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$ converts probit to fatality probability. - 📌 **FAR ↔ IR conversion**: $FAR = IR \times 10^8 / 8760$. - 📌 **Iso-risk contours** are iso-lines of constant IR plotted around a plant for land-use planning. - 📌 Always sum over **all** hazardous events; never report only the dominant one. - 📌 KTU exam favourite: numerical problem combining IR + SR + F–N curve from a 3–4 event table.

topics.title: FN Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** FN Curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **F-N Curve (Frequency-Number Curve):** A graphical representation used in **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** that plots the **cumulative frequency (F)** of accidents causing **N or more fatalities** against the **number of fatalities (N)**. It is the standard tool for expressing and evaluating **societal risk** from hazardous installations.

The F-N curve was originally proposed by **F.R. Farmer (1967)** of the UK Atomic Energy Authority, and was later adopted by the **Health and Safety Executive (HSE), UK** as the statutory basis for land-use planning around major hazard installations (e.g., COMAH sites).

Mathematically, the F-N curve is expressed in its canonical power-law form:

$$F(N) = C \cdot N^{-k}$$

where the parameters $C$ and $k$ characterize the severity–frequency profile of the installation under assessment.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Earthquake Magnitude" Analogy**
> Think of an F-N curve like the **Richter scale** for industrial accidents, but plotted in reverse. Just as a Richter scale shows how **rare** very large earthquakes are (most energy goes into small, frequent tremors), an F-N curve shows how **rare** catastrophic multi-fatality accidents are. A small leak causing 0.01 fatalities/year might occur **1000 times a year** (e.g., F=1000), but a massive BLEVE killing 50 people might occur only **once in 100,000 years** (F=10⁻⁵). The curve simply **trades frequency for severity** — society tolerates frequent minor events but demands ever-rarer events as consequences grow.

## 1.3 Physical Significance and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics (HSE, UK):**
> - **Intolerable Region:** $F > 10^{-3}$ events/year
> - **Broadly Acceptable Region:** $F < 10^{-5}$ events/year
> - **ALARP Region (As Low As Reasonably Practicable):** $10^{-5} \le F \le 10^{-3}$
> - **Typical k-value (risk aversion slope):** $k = 1$ (HSE neutral standard)

The **expected value of F** is dimensionless (events per year) and **N** is a dimensionless integer count of fatalities. The slope **k** is a **dimensionless risk-aversion index**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The F-N curve is a **cumulative frequency distribution** in log-log space, constructed by summing the probabilities of all accident scenarios that result in $N$ or more fatalities. The construction proceeds in the following logical steps:

1. **Hazard Identification:** Identify all credible accident scenarios (e.g., LPG pool fire, ammonia release, BLEVE).
2. **Frequency Estimation:** Calculate the annual frequency $f_i$ of each initiating event (e.g., using fault tree analysis, OREDA database, or historical data).
3. **Consequence Modeling:** For each scenario, calculate the number of fatalities $N_i$ (e.g., using probit equations for toxicity, thermal radiation dose, or overpressure).
4. **Bucketing:** Group scenarios by their $N$ value and sum frequencies.
5. **Cumulative Summation:** Plot $F(N) = \sum_{i: N_i \ge N} f_i$ against $N$ on log-log axes.

## 2.2 The Risk-Aversion Parameter (k)

The **slope $k$** encodes **societal risk aversion**. A higher $k$ means society demands a steeper drop in frequency for an increase in fatalities. The HSE has codified the value $k = 1$ as the **neutral slope**, with specific anchor points:
- At $N = 1$ fatality: $F = 10^{-3}$ per year
- At $N = 50$ fatalities: $F = 10^{-5}$ per year

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation a student must memorize for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations on F-N curves.

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description / Use** | **Units / Domain** |
|------|-------------|------------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $F(N) = C \cdot N^{-k}$ | Canonical F-N power law | $F$ in events/year, $N$ dimensionless |
| 2 | $\log(F) = \log(C) - k \cdot \log(N)$ | Log-log linear form for plotting | — |
| 3 | $k = -\dfrac{\log(F_1 / F_2)}{\log(N_1 / N_2)}$ | Slope between any two anchor points | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $C = F \cdot N^{k}$ | Intercept constant (capacity factor) | events·year⁻¹ |
| 5 | $F(N) = \sum_{i \,:\, N_i \ge N} f_i$ | Cumulative frequency summation | events/year |
| 6 | $\text{IR} = \sum f_i \cdot p_i$ | Individual Risk (separate from societal) | Probability/year |
| 7 | $\text{PLL} = \sum f_i \cdot N_i$ | Potential Loss of Life per year | fatalities/year |
| 8 | $\text{ERL} = \sum f_i \cdot N_i$ | Expected Rate of Fatalities | fatalities/year |
| 9 | $\int_{N_1}^{N_2} F(N) \, dN$ | Area under F-N curve (risk integral) | fatalities/year |
| 10 | $\text{RI} = \dfrac{C}{k-1} \left[ N_{\min}^{1-k} - N_{\max}^{1-k} \right]$ | Risk integral of power law for $k \neq 1$ | fatalities/year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention:** Frequencies are **cumulative** (decreasing function of N), so $F$ is **monotonically non-increasing** as $N$ increases.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Land-Use Planning (LUP):** Determines consultation zones around COMAH sites (UK).
- **ALARP Demonstration:** Shows whether risk reduction measures are reasonably practicable.
- **Insurance & Reinsurance:** Quantifies maximum credible loss (MCL) for property and casualty underwriting.
- **Emergency Planning:** Defines evacuation zone radii and mutual-aid response capabilities.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Supports SEVESO-III Directive (EU), EPA Risk Management Programs (US), and ONGC/HSE India (Indian regulatory adoption).
- **Process Safety Management (PSM):** Prioritizes resource allocation for inherently safer design (ISD) interventions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The F-N Power Law from Two Anchor Points

Given two regulatory or empirical anchor points $(N_1, F_1)$ and $(N_2, F_2)$, we derive the canonical F-N equation.

**Step 1: Write the canonical form for both anchor points:**

$$F_1 = C \cdot N_1^{-k}$$

$$F_2 = C \cdot N_2^{-k}$$

**Step 2: Divide the second equation by the first to eliminate $C$:**

$$\frac{F_2}{F_1} = \left( \frac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^{-k} = \left( \frac{N_1}{N_2} \right)^{k}$$

**Step 3: Take the natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{F_2}{F_1}\right) = k \cdot \ln\left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)$$

**Step 4: Solve explicitly for the slope parameter $k$:**

$$k = \frac{\ln(F_2 / F_1)}{\ln(N_1 / N_2)} = -\frac{\log(F_1 / F_2)}{\log(N_1 / N_2)}$$

**Step 5: Solve for the intercept $C$ by back-substitution:**

$$C = F_1 \cdot N_1^{k}$$

This derivation confirms the slope–intercept form of the F-N curve and is the **most frequently examined derivation** in KTU board papers.

## 3.2 Worked Example: HSE Neutral Risk Criterion

**Problem:** Verify that the HSE neutral risk line passes through $(N_1=1, F_1=10^{-3})$ and $(N_2=50, F_2=10^{-5})$, and compute the expected annual fatalities via the risk integral between $N = 1$ and $N = 50$.

**Step 1: Compute the slope $k$:**

$$k = -\frac{\log(10^{-3} / 10^{-5})}{\log(1 / 50)} = -\frac{\log(100)}{\log(0.02)} = -\frac{2}{-1.699} = 1.177$$

**Step 2: Compute the intercept $C$:**

$$C = F_1 \cdot N_1^{k} = 10^{-3} \cdot 1^{1.177} = 10^{-3} \text{ events/year}$$

**Step 3: Write the explicit F-N equation:**

$$F(N) = 10^{-3} \cdot N^{-1.177}$$

**Step 4: Compute the risk integral (Expected Rate of Fatalities) for $k \neq 1$:**

$$\text{ERL} = \int_{1}^{50} F(N) \, dN = \int_{1}^{50} 10^{-3} \cdot N^{-1.177} \, dN$$

$$\text{ERL} = 10^{-3} \cdot \left[ \frac{N^{-0.177}}{-0.177} \right]_{1}^{50}$$

$$\text{ERL} = 10^{-3} \cdot \left( \frac{50^{-0.177} - 1^{-0.177}}{-0.177} \right)$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the numerical values:**

$$50^{-0.177} = e^{-0.177 \cdot \ln 50} = e^{-0.177 \cdot 3.912} = e^{-0.6924} = 0.5003$$

$$\text{ERL} = 10^{-3} \cdot \left( \frac{0.5003 - 1}{-0.177} \right) = 10^{-3} \cdot \left( \frac{-0.4997}{-0.177} \right) = 10^{-3} \cdot 2.823 = 2.823 \times 10^{-3} \text{ fatalities/year}$$

**Interpretation:** The expected number of fatalities per year across the integration range is approximately **2.8 × 10⁻³ fatalities/year** — consistent with the HSE design intent for tolerability boundaries.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Societal vs. Individual Risk

| **Attribute** | **Individual Risk (IR)** | **Societal Risk (F-N Curve)** |
|---------------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Definition | Probability that an average individual at a location is fatally harmed per year | Cumulative frequency of events causing N or more fatalities |
| Unit | Probability/year (dimensionless) | Events/year vs. fatalities |
| Aggregation | Per-receptor (point-based) | Population-weighted (area-based) |
| Aversion | Linear (each life equal) | Disproportionate (high-N penalized) |
| Regulatory metric | $10^{-6}$/yr (broadly acceptable), $10^{-4}$/yr (unacceptable) | $10^{-3}$/yr at N=1, $10^{-5}$/yr at N=50 (HSE) |
| Decision tool | Iso-risk contours | F-N curve + ALARP boundary |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: F-N Curve Construction Workflow

The diagram below shows the full QRA data pipeline that produces an F-N curve. This is the standard workflow taught in PECHT743 Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazard Identification HAZOP What-If] --> B[Initiating Event Frequency Estimation]
    B --> C[Fault Tree / Event Tree Analysis]
    C --> D[Scenario Consequence Modeling]
    D --> E[Fatality Estimation N via Probit Dose Response]
    E --> F[Scenario F N Pair Library]
    F --> G[Frequency Bucketing by N]
    G --> H[Cumulative Summation F greater or equal N]
    H --> I[Log Log Plot F vs N]
    I --> J{Compare to HSE Criteria}
    J --> K[Above 10 to the power of negative 3: Intolerable]
    J --> L[Between 10 to the power of negative 5 and 10 to the power of negative 3: ALARP]
    J --> M[Below 10 to the power of negative 5: Broadly Acceptable]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: F-N Curve with HSE Risk Tolerance Envelope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Intolerable Region F greater than 10 to the power of negative 3] --> B[ALARP Region]
    B --> C[Broadly Acceptable Region F less than 10 to the power of negative 5]
    subgraph X[HSE Neutral Criterion Line]
    D[Slope k equals 1.177] --> E[Anchor N1 equals 1 F1 equals 10 to the power of negative 3] --> F[Anchor N2 equals 50 F2 equals 10 to the power of negative 5]
    end
    A -.governs.-> X
    X -.governs.-> C
```

## 4.3 Cross-Reference: Where F-N Curves Sit in the Risk Assessment Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Process Plant] --> Q1[Hazard Identification]
    P --> Q2[Consequence Analysis]
    P --> Q3[Frequency Analysis]
    Q1 --> R[QRA Quantitative Risk Assessment]
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
    R --> S1[Individual Risk Iso Risk Contours]
    R --> S2[F N Curve Societal Risk]
    R --> S3[ALARP Demonstration]
    R --> S4[Safety Case Report]
    S2 --> T[Land Use Planning and Emergency Planning]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Q: Define F-N curve. What does each axis represent?**

**Model Answer:**
An F-N curve is a log-log plot of the **cumulative frequency (F)** of accidents causing **N or more fatalities** against the **number of fatalities (N)**. The **vertical (F) axis** represents the **annual frequency of events** (events/year), and the **horizontal (N) axis** represents the **number of fatalities** per event. It is the standard tool for expressing **societal risk** in QRA.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q: State the standard HSE risk criteria for the F-N curve and identify the three risk regions.**

**Model Answer:**
The HSE neutral risk line has anchor points: $F = 10^{-3}$ at $N = 1$ and $F = 10^{-5}$ at $N = 50$. The three regions are:
1. **Intolerable Region:** $F > 10^{-3}$/year (risk must be reduced).
2. **ALARP Region:** $10^{-5} \le F \le 10^{-3}$/year (reduce risk "As Low As Reasonably Practicable").
3. **Broadly Acceptable Region:** $F < 10^{-5}$/year (risk is negligible).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option A

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive the slope $k$ and intercept $C$ of an F-N curve, given two anchor points $(N_1, F_1)$ and $(N_2, F_2)$.**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: The canonical F-N power-law equation is:

$$F(N) = C \cdot N^{-k}$$

Step 2: Substitute both anchor points:

$$F_1 = C \cdot N_1^{-k} \quad \text{and} \quad F_2 = C \cdot N_2^{-k}$$

Step 3: Divide the equations to eliminate $C$:

$$\frac{F_2}{F_1} = \left( \frac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^{-k}$$

Step 4: Take the logarithm:

$$\log\left(\frac{F_2}{F_1}\right) = -k \cdot \log\left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right) = k \cdot \log\left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)$$

Step 5: Solve for $k$:

$$k = \frac{\log(F_2 / F_1)}{\log(N_1 / N_2)} = -\frac{\log(F_1 / F_2)}{\log(N_1 / N_2)}$$

Step 6: Solve for $C$:

$$C = F_1 \cdot N_1^{k}$$

**(b) [7 Marks] An LPG storage facility is characterized by F-N anchor points $N_1 = 1, F_1 = 3 \times 10^{-3}$ and $N_2 = 100, F_2 = 10^{-6}$. Determine the slope $k$, intercept $C$, and comment on whether the facility lies in the ALARP region.**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: Calculate the slope:

$$k = -\frac{\log(3 \times 10^{-3} / 10^{-6})}{\log(1 / 100)} = -\frac{\log(3000)}{\log(0.01)} = -\frac{3.4771}{-2} = 1.7386$$

Step 2: Calculate the intercept:

$$C = F_1 \cdot N_1^{k} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1^{1.7386} = 3 \times 10^{-3}$$

Step 3: The F-N equation is:

$$F(N) = 3 \times 10^{-3} \cdot N^{-1.7386}$$

Step 4: Compare with HSE neutral line $F(N) = 10^{-3} \cdot N^{-1.177}$:
At $N = 1$: $F_{\text{facility}} = 3 \times 10^{-3}$ which is **above** the HSE threshold of $10^{-3}$ → facility is in the **Intolerable Region** at this point.
At $N = 100$: $F_{\text{facility}} = 10^{-6}$ which is **below** the HSE threshold → broadly acceptable at this point.

Step 5: **Conclusion:** The curve **crosses** the HSE neutral line somewhere in between. The facility is in the **Intolerable Region** for low-N events but transitions to **ALARP/Broadly Acceptable** for high-N events. **The facility is in the ALARP region overall**, and risk reduction measures (e.g., bund walls, gas detection, water spray) must be implemented to push the entire curve into the broadly acceptable region.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option B

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks] What is the significance of the slope $k$ in an F-N curve? How is it interpreted as a "risk aversion" parameter?**

**Model Solution:**

The slope $k$ is a **dimensionless risk-aversion index** that encodes **societal preferences** for risk reduction. Its significance is interpreted as follows:

- **$k = 1$ (Linear, Risk-Neutral):** Each additional fatality reduces the tolerable frequency by a fixed proportion. Frequency is inversely proportional to consequence. This is the **mathematically neutral** position.

- **$k > 1$ (Risk-Averse):** Society demands a **steeper drop** in frequency as fatalities increase. A higher $k$ reflects a **proportionally stronger dislike** for large accidents. The HSE uses approximately $k \approx 1.18$ as a mildly risk-averse compromise.

- **$k < 1$ (Risk-Tolerant):** Society is willing to accept almost the same frequency for high-N events as for low-N events. This is generally **not used** in regulation.

- **$k = 2$ (Strongly Risk-Averse):** The frequency must fall as the **square** of the number of fatalities — every extra fatality costs a factor of 2 in frequency reduction. This is the "HSE limit line" used in the **Netherlands**.

The **physical intuition** is that 1000 events of 1 fatality each = 1 event of 1000 fatalities in expected value, but **society does not view them equally**. A single 1000-fatality disaster is a **catastrophe**; 1000 individual accidents are routine. The slope $k$ mathematically encodes this asymmetry.

**(b) [7 Marks] A process plant has the following F-N data: $F(1) = 5 \times 10^{-4}$/yr, $F(5) = 6.86 \times 10^{-5}$/yr, $F(20) = 8.50 \times 10^{-6}$/yr. Verify whether the F-N data follows a power law, and if so, determine the best-fit $k$ and $C$ using the two extreme points.**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: Check if the data is consistent with $F(N) = C \cdot N^{-k}$ by testing the log-log linearity.

Compute $\log F$ and $\log N$:

| $N$ | $F$ | $\log N$ | $\log F$ |
|-----|-----|---------|---------|
| 1   | $5 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0 | $-3.301$ |
| 5   | $6.86 \times 10^{-5}$ | 0.699 | $-4.164$ |
| 20  | $8.50 \times 10^{-6}$ | 1.301 | $-5.071$ |

Step 2: Test slope between first and third point:

$$k_{1,3} = -\frac{-3.301 - (-5.071)}{0 - 1.301} = -\frac{1.770}{-1.301} = 1.360$$

Step 3: Test slope between first and second point:

$$k_{1,2} = -\frac{-3.301 - (-4.164)}{0 - 0.699} = -\frac{0.863}{-0.699} = 1.235$$

Step 4: Test slope between second and third point:

$$k_{2,3} = -\frac{-4.164 - (-5.071)}{0.699 - 1.301} = -\frac{0.907}{-0.602} = 1.507$$

Step 5: The slopes are **not equal** (1.235, 1.507, 1.360), but they are reasonably close (within ±10%). The data **approximately** follows a power law. Best fit using extreme points:

$$k \approx 1.360, \quad C = 5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 1^{1.360} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

Step 6: The best-fit F-N equation is:

$$F(N) = 5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot N^{-1.360}$$

Step 7: **Conclusion:** The data approximately follows a power law with $k \approx 1.36$, indicating **moderate risk aversion** (slightly higher than the HSE neutral value of 1.18). For exact power-law behavior, more data points and least-squares regression would be required.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — F-N Curves:**
> 1. **Forgetting log-log axes:** Many students plot $F$ vs $N$ on linear axes and fail to recognize the power-law relationship.
> 2. **Confusing $f_i$ (scenario frequency) with $F(N)$ (cumulative frequency):** Cumulative is the **sum** of all events with $N_i \ge N$, not the frequency of exactly $N$ fatalities.
> 3. **Sign error in slope:** The formula is $k = -\log(F_1/F_2)/\log(N_1/N_2)$; dropping the negative sign flips the inequality.
> 4. **Wrong anchors for HSE:** Always use $N=1$ at $F=10^{-3}$ and $N=50$ at $F=10^{-5}$ — not $N=10$ or other values.
> 5. **Confusing Individual Risk with Societal Risk:** IR is per-receptor (prob/yr); F-N is for the whole installation (events/yr vs N).
> 6. **Ignoring units:** $C$ has units of events/year; the integral under the F-N curve yields fatalities/year.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — F-N Curves**

- **F-N curve definition:** Log-log plot of cumulative frequency $F$ (events/year) vs number of fatalities $N$.
- **Origin:** Proposed by **F.R. Farmer (1967)**; codified by **HSE, UK**.
- **Canonical equation:** $F(N) = C \cdot N^{-k}$ (power-law form).
- **Log-log form:** $\log F = \log C - k \cdot \log N$ (linear in log space).
- **HSE anchor points:** $(1, 10^{-3})$ and $(50, 10^{-5})$ → slope $k \approx 1.18$.
- **Three risk regions:**
  - $F > 10^{-3}$ → **Intolerable** (must reduce)
  - $10^{-5} \le F \le 10^{-3}$ → **ALARP** (reduce as low as reasonably practicable)
  - $F < 10^{-5}$ → **Broadly Acceptable** (negligible)
- **Risk-aversion parameter $k$:**
  - $k=1$: risk-neutral
  - $k>1$: risk-averse
  - $k<1$: risk-tolerant
  - Netherlands uses $k=2$ for very high aversion.
- **Construction steps:** HAZOP → frequency estimation (FTA/ETA) → consequence modeling (probit) → N values → bucket → cumulative sum.
- **Individual risk (IR)** is **separate** from F-N; F-N is **societal risk**.
- **Risk integral:** $\int F(N) \, dN$ gives **expected fatalities per year** (ERL).
- **Application domains:** Land-use planning, ALARP demonstration, emergency planning, insurance, SEVESO-III compliance.
- **Limitation:** F-N does not capture geographic distribution of risk — pair with iso-risk contours for full QRA.
- **Numerical check:** Always verify $F$ is monotonically non-increasing in $N$ and that the slope $k$ falls in $[1, 2]$ for HSE-style assessments.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probit Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Probit Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Probit Function** (short for *Probability Unit Function*) is a statistical dose-response model used in **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** to relate the magnitude of an exposure (dose) to the probability of a defined harmful effect in a population — most commonly, the probability of fatality of an exposed individual.

The general mathematical form is:

$$Pr = a + b \ln(V)$$

where,
- **Pr** = Probit variable (dimensionless)
- **V** = Toxic load / dose / intensity (function of concentration, intensity, or overpressure, and exposure duration)
- **a, b** = Material / hazard-specific empirical constants (derived from experimental data on animals and humans)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Probit value is **NOT** the percentage of fatalities. It is a transformed standard normal deviate. To obtain the percentage of people affected, the probit value must be converted using the standard normal cumulative distribution function.

## 1.2 Conversion Between Probit and Percentage

$$P\,(\%) = 50\left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{Pr - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$

where **erf** denotes the *Gaussian Error Function*. This converts a probit value into the corresponding cumulative probability of fatality (in percentage).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Class Ranking" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Probit as a "Z-score of death."**
> Imagine a class of 10,000 students arranged by their exam score from lowest to highest. The *rank percentile* tells you what fraction of students scored below a given number. Now replace *exam score* with *dose of toxic gas* and *students* with *humans* — the probit variable (Pr) is the transformed rank position. Higher exposure → higher probit → higher fatality percentage.

A simpler intuitive picture:
- **Low dose** → most people survive → Pr is small → P (%) is small.
- **High dose** → most people die → Pr is large → P (%) is close to 100%.
- The *probit equation* tells us **how dose maps to rank position**; the *error function* tells us **how rank maps to fatality percentage**.

## 1.4 Significance in Process Plant Safety

In a chemical/process plant, accidents can release:
1. **Thermal radiation** from pool fires, jet fires, fireballs
2. **Overpressure** from vapour cloud explosions (VCE), BLEVE
3. **Toxic gases** (Cl₂, NH₃, H₂S, CO, HF, SO₂)

For each hazard, the probit function lets the safety engineer estimate the **fraction of exposed personnel likely to suffer the harmful effect** — the foundation of consequence analysis, individual risk contours, and societal risk (F-N) curves.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of the Probit Model

The Probit model was introduced by **Chester Ittner Bliss (1934)** and adapted for industrial risk by the **TNO Green Book** (CPR-12E, 1989) and the **CCPS Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis**. For toxic substances, the dose V generally takes the form:

$$V = C^n \cdot t$$

where *C* is the airborne concentration and *t* is the exposure duration. The exponent *n* captures whether toxicity is dominated by peak concentration or cumulative exposure.

For thermal radiation and overpressure, *V* is a function of intensity and time in a different algebraic form specific to the injury mechanism.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Standard Probit Correlations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most-asked set of correlations** in any Process Safety / QRA paper. Memorize the constants.

### Table 2.1: Standard Probit Correlations (TNO Green Book / CCPS)

| Hazard Type | Effect | Probit Equation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thermal Radiation (Eisenberg) | Death (2nd/3rd degree burns) | $Pr = -14.9 + 2.56\,\ln(t \cdot I^{4/3})$ | $t$ in s, $I$ in W/m² |
| Thermal Radiation (TNO) | Death | $Pr = -10.7 + 2.56\,\ln(t \cdot I^{4/3})$ | $t$ in s, $I$ in W/m² |
| Overpressure (Lung) | Death from lung hemorrhage | $Pr = -77.1 + 6.91\,\ln(P_s)$ | $P_s$ in kPa |
| Overpressure (Head impact, projectiles) | Death | $Pr = -23.8 + 2.92\,\ln(P_s)$ | $P_s$ in kPa |
| Toxic — Hydrogen Sulphide (H₂S) | Death | $Pr = -31.42 + 3.008\,\ln(C^{1.43} \cdot t)$ | $C$ in ppm, $t$ in min |
| Toxic — Chlorine (Cl₂) | Death | $Pr = -5.3 + 0.5\,\ln(C^{2.75} \cdot t)$ | $C$ in ppm, $t$ in min |
| Toxic — Ammonia (NH₃) | Death | $Pr = -35.9 + 1.85\,\ln(C \cdot t)$ | $C$ in ppm, $t$ in min |
| Toxic — Carbon Monoxide (CO) | Death | $Pr = -37.98 + 3.7\,\ln(C^{1.036} \cdot t)$ | $C$ in ppm, $t$ in min |
| Toxic — Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂) | Death | $Pr = -15.67 + 2.10\,\ln(C \cdot t)$ | $C$ in ppm, $t$ in min |

### Table 2.2: Quick Probit → Percentage Conversion (for Examinations)

| Probit (Pr) | Percentage Fatality (%) |
|---|---|
| 2.67 | 1 |
| 3.72 | 10 |
| 4.16 | 20 |
| 4.48 | 30 |
| 5.00 | 50 |
| 5.52 | 70 |
| 5.84 | 80 |
| 6.28 | 90 |
| 7.33 | 99 |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU numericals, after computing Pr, find the **closest value** in Table 2.2 OR use the error function formula. Showing the *interpolation* step in the answer book is essential for full marks.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **Consequence Analysis** — links physical effects (heat flux, overpressure, dose) to harm.
- **Individual Risk Calculation** — required for IRP (Individual Risk Per Annum) contours around a facility.
- **Societal Risk (F-N Curves)** — feeds the cumulative frequency of N-or-more fatalities.
- **Land-Use Planning** — determines safety distances under Seveso / Indian Factories Act rules.
- **Emergency Planning** — defines evacuation zones and shelter-in-place distances.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Percentage Conversion Formula

The probit variable is defined as the **standard normal variate shifted by 5**:

$$Pr = 5 + Y$$

where *Y* is the standard normal variate. The cumulative probability of fatality is the area under the standard normal curve to the left of *Y*:

$$P = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y} e^{-u^2 / 2}\, du$$

Substitute $Y = Pr - 5$ and use the symmetry of the error function:

$$P = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{Y}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$

Therefore, in percentage:

$$\boxed{\,P\,(\%) = 50\left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{Pr - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]\,}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Thermal Radiation (Full Derivation)

**Problem:** A pool fire produces a thermal radiation intensity of **25 kW/m²** (= 25 000 W/m²) at a point where unprotected personnel are exposed for **20 seconds**. Calculate the percentage fatality using the Eisenberg probit equation.

### Step 1 — Identify the correct probit equation

From Table 2.1:
$$Pr = -14.9 + 2.56\,\ln(t \cdot I^{4/3})$$

### Step 2 — Compute the effective dose term $t \cdot I^{4/3}$

Compute $I^{4/3}$:

$$I^{4/3} = (25\,000)^{4/3} = 25\,000 \cdot (25\,000)^{1/3}$$

Calculate the cube root:

$$(25\,000)^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{25\,000} \approx 29.24$$

Therefore:

$$I^{4/3} \approx 25\,000 \times 29.24 \approx 731\,000\,\text{(W/m}^{2})^{4/3}$$

Now multiply by exposure time:

$$t \cdot I^{4/3} = 20 \times 731\,000 = 1.462 \times 10^{7}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the natural logarithm

$$\ln(1.462 \times 10^{7}) = \ln(1.462) + \ln(10^7) = 0.380 + 7 \cdot \ln(10) \approx 0.380 + 16.118 \approx 16.498$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into the probit equation

$$Pr = -14.9 + 2.56 \times 16.498$$

$$Pr = -14.9 + 42.235 \approx 27.33$$

### Step 5 — Convert probit to percentage

$$\dfrac{Pr - 5}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{27.33 - 5}{1.414} = \dfrac{22.33}{1.414} \approx 15.79$$

For arguments beyond 3, $\text{erf}(x) \approx 1$, so:

$$P\,(\%) = 50\,[1 + 1] = 100\,\%$$

**Conclusion:** Under 25 kW/m² for 20 s, virtually **100% of exposed unprotected personnel are expected to die**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Toxic Release (Full Derivation)

**Problem:** A leak of **Hydrogen Sulphide (H₂S)** produces a ground-level concentration of **1500 ppm** lasting **15 minutes**. Estimate the percentage fatality for an unprotected population using the probit model.

### Step 1 — Choose the H₂S probit equation

$$Pr = -31.42 + 3.008\,\ln(C^{1.43} \cdot t)$$

### Step 2 — Compute the toxic load $C^{1.43} \cdot t$

Compute $C^{1.43}$:

$$\ln(C^{1.43}) = 1.43 \cdot \ln(1500) = 1.43 \times 7.313 = 10.458$$

$$C^{1.43} = e^{10.458} \approx 34\,995$$

Multiply by exposure time:

$$C^{1.43} \cdot t = 34\,995 \times 15 \approx 5.249 \times 10^{5}$$

### Step 3 — Take the natural log

$$\ln(5.249 \times 10^5) = \ln(5.249) + 5 \cdot \ln(10) \approx 1.658 + 11.513 \approx 13.171$$

### Step 4 — Compute the probit value

$$Pr = -31.42 + 3.008 \times 13.171 = -31.42 + 39.619 \approx 8.20$$

### Step 5 — Convert probit to percentage fatality

$$\dfrac{Pr - 5}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{8.20 - 5}{1.414} = \dfrac{3.20}{1.414} \approx 2.263$$

From standard normal tables, $\text{erf}(2.263) \approx 0.9988$. Therefore:

$$P\,(\%) = 50\,[1 + 0.9988] \approx 99.94\,\%$$

**Conclusion:** Approximately **99.94 % fatality** at 1500 ppm H₂S for 15 minutes — confirming H₂S as a *highly* lethal toxicant.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Overpressure from VCE (Full Derivation)

**Problem:** A vapour cloud explosion produces a peak side-on overpressure of **70 kPa** at the location of an occupied control room. Estimate the percentage fatality due to lung hemorrhage.

### Step 1 — Choose the probit equation

$$Pr = -77.1 + 6.91\,\ln(P_s),\quad P_s = 70\,\text{kPa}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the natural log

$$\ln(70) = \ln(7) + \ln(10) \approx 1.946 + 2.303 = 4.248$$

### Step 3 — Compute the probit value

$$Pr = -77.1 + 6.91 \times 4.248 = -77.1 + 29.353 \approx -47.75$$

### Step 4 — Convert probit to percentage

$$\dfrac{Pr - 5}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{-47.75 - 5}{1.414} = \dfrac{-52.75}{1.414} \approx -37.30$$

For a large negative argument, $\text{erf}(-37.30) \approx -1$, so:

$$P\,(\%) = 50\,[1 - 1] = 0\,\%$$

**Conclusion:** At 70 kPa the probit is so low that **no fatality from lung hemorrhage is expected**. The threshold of fatality (1 %) is reached when:

$$Pr = 2.67 \Rightarrow \ln(P_s) = \dfrac{2.67 + 77.1}{6.91} \approx 11.54 \Rightarrow P_s \approx 105\,\text{kPa}$$

So the **lethal overpressure threshold** for lung hemorrhage is roughly **100 kPa**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Solving Strategy

| Step | Action | Tool |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify hazard type (thermal, overpressure, toxic) | Scenario data |
| 2 | Select correct probit equation and confirm units | Table 2.1 |
| 3 | Compute the dose variable $V$ | Calculator / logs |
| 4 | Compute probit value $Pr = a + b \ln(V)$ | Sub & multiply |
| 5 | Convert $Pr$ to fatality % via error function or interpolation table | Table 2.2 |
| 6 | Comment on engineering meaning | Interpretation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Probit-Based Consequence Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Hazard Identification"] --> B["Release Scenario Definition"]
    B --> C["Dispersion or Radiation or Explosion Model"]
    C --> D["Field Intensity Calculation"]
    D --> E["Identify Hazard Type"]
    E --> F1["Thermal Dose tI^(4/3)"]
    E --> F2["Peak Overpressure Ps"]
    E --> F3["Toxic Load C^n times t"]
    F1 --> G1["Apply Eisenberg or TNO Probit Equation"]
    F2 --> G2["Apply Lung or Head Probit Equation"]
    F3 --> G3["Apply Substance Specific Probit Equation"]
    G1 --> H["Compute Probit Value Pr"]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I["Convert Pr to Percentage via Error Function"]
    I --> J["Vulnerability or Fatality Percentage"]
    J --> K["Individual Risk Contour Mapping"]
    J --> L["Societal Risk F N Curve"]
    K --> M["Land Use Planning and Emergency Planning"]
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Map — Mathematical Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Dose Variable V"] --> B["Natural Log ln V"]
    B --> C["Linear Combination Pr equals a plus b ln V"]
    C --> D["Standard Normal Variate Y equals Pr minus 5"]
    D --> E["Cumulative Normal Distribution Phi"]
    E --> F["Fatality Percentage P percent"]
```

## 4.3 Hazard-Specific Probit Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Hazard Release Detected"] --> Q1{"Type of Harmful Agent"}
    Q1 -- Fire or Flame --> Q2{"Thermal Intensity known?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R1["Use Thermal Probit Pr equals minus 14.9 plus 2.56 ln tI^(4/3)"]
    Q1 -- Explosion --> R2["Use Overpressure Probit Pr equals minus 77.1 plus 6.91 ln Ps"]
    Q1 -- Toxic Gas --> Q3{"Which Chemical?"}
    Q3 -- H2S --> R3["Pr equals minus 31.42 plus 3.008 ln C^1.43 t"]
    Q3 -- Cl2 --> R4["Pr equals minus 5.3 plus 0.5 ln C^2.75 t"]
    Q3 -- NH3 --> R5["Pr equals minus 35.9 plus 1.85 ln C t"]
    Q3 -- CO --> R6["Pr equals minus 37.98 plus 3.7 ln C^1.036 t"]
    R1 --> S["Compute Pr"]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    R4 --> S
    R5 --> S
    R6 --> S
    S --> T["Convert to Percentage Fatality"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the Probit function. State its general mathematical form and explain its significance in process plant safety.**

**Model Answer:**
The Probit function is a dose–response relationship used in Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) to relate the magnitude of a harmful exposure (dose) to the probability of fatality in the exposed population. Its general form is:

$$Pr = a + b\,\ln(V)$$

where *Pr* is the probit variable, *V* is the dose (e.g., $tI^{4/3}$, $P_s$, $C^n t$), and *a, b* are empirical constants. It is significant because it converts physical hazard intensities — thermal radiation, overpressure, toxic concentration — into vulnerability percentages, which feed individual and societal risk calculations and emergency planning in process plants.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]`
**Write the probit equations for fatality due to (i) thermal radiation, (ii) overpressure-induced lung hemorrhage, and (iii) chlorine exposure. Clearly state the units of each variable.**

**Model Answer:**

(i) Thermal radiation (Eisenberg): $Pr = -14.9 + 2.56\,\ln(t \cdot I^{4/3})$, with $t$ in seconds and $I$ in W/m².

(ii) Overpressure lung hemorrhage: $Pr = -77.1 + 6.91\,\ln(P_s)$, with $P_s$ in kPa.

(iii) Chlorine exposure: $Pr = -5.3 + 0.5\,\ln(C^{2.75} \cdot t)$, with $C$ in ppm and $t$ in minutes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, the **ESE Module Internal Choice** pattern is followed. Two alternative questions, each with two sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying **7 marks each**, are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the mathematical basis of the Probit function. Derive the conversion formula that translates a probit value into a fatality percentage. List the probit equations for any four toxic substances with their constants and units. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The probit model assumes that the logarithm of the dose $V$ follows a normal distribution in the affected population. The probit variable is defined as:

$$Pr = 5 + Y$$

where $Y$ is the standard normal variate. The cumulative fatality probability is:

$$P = \Phi(Y) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^{Y} e^{-u^2/2}\,du$$

Using the identity $\Phi(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}[1 + \text{erf}(x/\sqrt{2})]$:

$$P\,(\%) = 50\left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{Pr - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$

Four toxic-substance probit equations:

1. H₂S: $Pr = -31.42 + 3.008\,\ln(C^{1.43}\,t)$, $C$ in ppm, $t$ in min.
2. Cl₂: $Pr = -5.3 + 0.5\,\ln(C^{2.75}\,t)$, $C$ in ppm, $t$ in min.
3. NH₃: $Pr = -35.9 + 1.85\,\ln(C\,t)$, $C$ in ppm, $t$ in min.
4. CO: $Pr = -37.98 + 3.7\,\ln(C^{1.036}\,t)$, $C$ in ppm, $t$ in min.

**(b)** A pool fire produces a thermal intensity of **12 kW/m²** at a work-station. Workers are exposed for **30 seconds**. Using the Eisenberg probit equation, calculate the percentage fatality and comment on whether evacuation within 20 s would have changed the outcome. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 —** Probit equation (Eisenberg):
$$Pr = -14.9 + 2.56\,\ln(t \cdot I^{4/3}),\quad I = 12\,000\,\text{W/m}^2,\; t = 30\,\text{s}$$

**Step 2 —** Compute $I^{4/3}$:
$$I^{4/3} = (12\,000)^{4/3} = 12\,000 \cdot \sqrt[3]{12\,000}$$
$$\sqrt[3]{12\,000} \approx 22.89$$
$$I^{4/3} \approx 12\,000 \times 22.89 \approx 274\,700$$

**Step 3 —** Multiply by $t$:
$$t \cdot I^{4/3} = 30 \times 274\,700 = 8.241 \times 10^6$$

**Step 4 —** Take ln:
$$\ln(8.241 \times 10^6) \approx \ln(8.241) + 6\ln(10) \approx 2.109 + 13.816 \approx 15.925$$

**Step 5 —** Compute Pr:
$$Pr = -14.9 + 2.56 \times 15.925 = -14.9 + 40.768 \approx 25.87$$

**Step 6 —** Convert to percentage:
$$\dfrac{25.87 - 5}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 14.75,\quad \text{erf}(14.75) \approx 1$$
$$P\,(\%) = 50\,[1 + 1] = 100\,\%$$

**Step 7 —** Repeat for $t = 20$ s:
$$t \cdot I^{4/3} = 20 \times 274\,700 = 5.494 \times 10^6$$
$$\ln(5.494 \times 10^6) \approx 15.52$$
$$Pr = -14.9 + 2.56 \times 15.52 \approx 24.83 \Rightarrow P \approx 100\,\%$$

**Conclusion:** Even with 20-s evacuation, fatality remains ~100 %. This indicates that *the heat flux of 12 kW/m² itself is above the survivability threshold*; engineering control (e.g., radiation shielding, increased distance) is required — evacuation alone is insufficient.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Discuss the role of the probit function in Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) of process plants. Differentiate between the probit models used for thermal, overpressure, and toxic hazards with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The probit function is the **bridge between physical-effect modelling and human-fatality estimation** in QRA. It is used at the *consequence analysis* stage to convert modelled field intensities into vulnerability percentages that subsequently feed individual risk contours and societal risk (F–N) curves.

**Differences between the three probit model families:**

1. **Thermal Probit Models** — based on heat dose received by skin; use $V = t \cdot I^{4/3}$ to capture the $t^{1/2}$ and $I^{4/3}$ burn-injury scaling law. Example: Eisenberg $Pr = -14.9 + 2.56\ln(tI^{4/3})$.

2. **Overpressure Probit Models** — based on blast-induced lung hemorrhage or whole-body translation. Variable $V = P_s$ alone (no explicit time term) because blast damage is impulsive. Example: $Pr = -77.1 + 6.91\ln(P_s)$.

3. **Toxic Probit Models** — based on inhalation dose; variable $V = C^n \cdot t$ where the exponent $n$ differs by substance. Example: H₂S $Pr = -31.42 + 3.008\ln(C^{1.43}t)$.

The constants *a, b* reflect the *intrinsic toxicity* or *lethality mechanism* of the agent.

**(b)** A **chlorine cylinder** ruptures and produces a ground-level concentration of **50 ppm** at a populated area for **60 minutes**. Using the probit equation, calculate the percentage fatality. If safety regulations demand that the probability of fatality at the boundary be ≤ 1 %, what is the maximum allowable concentration for the same 60-minute exposure? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 —** Use the chlorine probit equation:
$$Pr = -5.3 + 0.5\,\ln(C^{2.75}\,t),\quad C = 50\,\text{ppm},\; t = 60\,\text{min}$$

**Step 2 —** Compute $C^{2.75}$:
$$\ln(50^{2.75}) = 2.75 \cdot \ln(50) = 2.75 \times 3.912 = 10.758$$
$$C^{2.75} \approx e^{10.758} \approx 47\,055$$

**Step 3 —** Multiply by *t*:
$$C^{2.75}\,t = 47\,055 \times 60 \approx 2.823 \times 10^6$$

**Step 4 —** ln of dose:
$$\ln(2.823 \times 10^6) \approx \ln(2.823) + 6\ln(10) \approx 1.038 + 13.816 \approx 14.854$$

**Step 5 —** Probit:
$$Pr = -5.3 + 0.5 \times 14.854 = -5.3 + 7.427 \approx 2.127$$

**Step 6 —** Convert to %:
$$\dfrac{2.127 - 5}{\sqrt{2}} \approx \dfrac{-2.873}{1.414} \approx -2.032$$
$$\text{erf}(-2.032) \approx -0.9954$$
$$P\,(\%) = 50\,[1 - 0.9954] \approx 0.23\,\%$$

**Step 7 —** Find max concentration for $P = 1\,\%$:
For 1 % fatality, $Pr = 2.67$.
$$2.67 = -5.3 + 0.5\,\ln(C^{2.75} \cdot 60)$$
$$\ln(C^{2.75} \cdot 60) = 15.94$$
$$C^{2.75} \cdot 60 = e^{15.94} \approx 8.41 \times 10^6$$
$$C^{2.75} \approx 1.402 \times 10^5$$
$$\ln(C) = \dfrac{\ln(1.402 \times 10^5)}{2.75} = \dfrac{11.85}{2.75} \approx 4.31$$
$$C_{\max} \approx e^{4.31} \approx 74.4\,\text{ppm}$$

**Conclusion:** At 50 ppm for 60 min, only **~0.23 % fatality** is expected (well below the 1 % regulatory threshold). The maximum allowable chlorine concentration for the same 60-min exposure is **~74 ppm**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Errors**
> 1. **Wrong units** — using $t$ in seconds for toxic probits (which require minutes) or $I$ in kW/m² for thermal probit (which requires W/m²). Always cross-check with Table 2.1.
> 2. **Reporting probit value as percentage** — Pr is *not* the percentage; you must always perform the error-function conversion or interpolate from Table 2.2.
> 3. **Forgetting to take ln of the full product $C^n \cdot t$** — common error is $\ln(C) \cdot t$ instead of $\ln(C^n t)$.
> 4. **Choosing wrong probit equation** — Eisenberg (thermal) vs TNO, lung vs head impact (overpressure) — these have different constants and answer magnitudes differ.
> 5. **No interpretation** — KTU marks interpretation comments; do not end the answer after the calculation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Probit (Pr)** = transformed standard normal variate, **NOT** percentage.
- **General form:** $Pr = a + b\,\ln(V)$.
- **Conversion:** $P\,(\%) = 50\,[1 + \text{erf}((Pr-5)/\sqrt{2})]$.
- **Thermal dose** uses $V = t \cdot I^{4/3}$ with $I$ in W/m² and $t$ in seconds (Eisenberg constants $a=-14.9$, $b=2.56$).
- **Overpressure dose** uses $V = P_s$ alone (in kPa) for lung hemorrhage ($a=-77.1$, $b=6.91$).
- **Toxic dose** uses $V = C^n \cdot t$ with $C$ in ppm and $t$ in minutes; constants vary per substance.
- **Reference points:** $Pr = 5 \Rightarrow 50\,\%$; $Pr = 2.67 \Rightarrow 1\,\%$; $Pr = 7.33 \Rightarrow 99\,\%$.
- **Application:** feeds individual risk (IRP) contours and societal risk (F-N) curves in QRA.
- **Source standards:** TNO Green Book (CPR-12E), CCPS QRA Guidelines.
- **Always** interpret the result in engineering terms (safety distance, evacuation time, PPE adequacy).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements of Emergency Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Elements of Emergency Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Emergency Planning** is a documented, pre-established set of coordinated procedures, resources, organizational structures, and communication protocols designed to enable a process plant to respond effectively to uncontrolled releases of hazardous materials, fires, explosions, or natural disasters, thereby protecting personnel, the public, the environment, and physical assets.

As per the **OSHA Process Safety Management (PSM)** standard **29 CFR 1910.119(n)** and the **EPA Risk Management Program (RMP)**, every facility handling hazardous chemicals above threshold quantities must develop and implement a written **Emergency Response Plan (ERP)**. Under KTU's outcome-based framework, emergency planning is mapped to Course Outcomes dealing with **hazard identification**, **risk mitigation**, and **regulatory compliance**.

### 1.2 Key Terminology Distilled

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **On-Site Emergency Plan** | Plan that governs actions of plant personnel within the facility boundary |
| **Off-Site Emergency Plan** | Plan covering areas external to the plant, coordinated with public authorities |
| **Emergency Shutdown (ESD)** | Automated or manual system that rapidly isolates process equipment during emergencies |
| **Emergency Control Centre (ECC)** | Designated command post where Emergency Commander directs all response activities |
| **Muster Station** | Predefined safe assembly point for personnel during evacuation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine you are hosting 200 guests at a wedding in a massive banquet hall. Before the event, you brief your team: "If the kitchen catches fire → call 101, evacuate via Gate B, assemble guests at the lawn, the manager holds the master key, the medic carries the first-aid box, the band plays a specific alarm tone." That pre-rehearsed choreography is **Emergency Planning**.
>
> In a process plant, instead of a fire in a kitchen, you have a reactor runaway, a hydrocarbon pool fire, or a toxic gas cloud. Instead of 200 guests, you may have 500 workers and 50,000 neighboring villagers. The "wedding rehearsal" is a **mock drill**, and the printed brief is the **written Emergency Response Plan**.

### 1.3 Why Emergency Planning Is Non-Negotiable

- **Legal Mandate**: Factories Act 1948 (India), OSHA, EPA RMP, CIMAH (EU Seveso Directive).
- **Ethical Duty**: *Duty of Care* under common law towards employees and community.
- **Time-Critical Nature**: In the **first 30 minutes** of a major release (the "**Golden Hour**"), decisions made determine whether the incident remains a near-miss or becomes a catastrophe (Bhopal 1984, Texas City 2005).
- **Cost Benefit**: Industry data indicates that **$1 invested in emergency planning saves $4–$10 in incident losses**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Core Elements of an Emergency Plan (The "10-Pillar" Framework)

According to **API RP 753**, **OSHA 1910.38**, and **CCPS Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis (CPQRA)**, a comprehensive emergency plan consists of the following elements:

1. **Emergency Policy & Scope Statement**
2. **Emergency Organization & Chain of Command**
3. **Hazard Identification & Scenario Analysis**
4. **Communication & Alarm Systems**
5. **Emergency Response Procedures (ERP)**
6. **Emergency Shutdown Systems (ESD / ESD-1, ESD-2, ESD-3)**
7. **Emergency Control Centre (ECC) and Equipment**
8. **Evacuation, Mustering & Accounting Procedures**
9. **Mutual Aid, Medical & Spill Response**
10. **Training, Drills, Review & Updating**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Question papers frequently ask "List the essential elements of an emergency plan" (3 marks) or "Discuss the components of an on-site emergency plan with a neat diagram" (14 marks). Always answer with **all 10 elements** categorized under **Pre-Emergency**, **During-Emergency**, and **Post-Emergency** phases.

### 2.2 Emergency Shutdown (ESD) Hierarchy (IEC 61511 / ISA S84)

The ESD is graded by severity level to avoid unnecessary plant shutdown:

| ESD Level | Trigger | Action | Typical Initiation Time |
|-----------|---------|--------|-------------------------|
| **ESD-0 (Final Emergency)** | Catastrophic / un-controllable event | Total plant shutdown, depressurize, isolate from utility | < 10 s |
| **ESD-1 (Plant-wide)** | Major fire, explosion, large toxic release | Shutdown all process units, ignite flare, isolate feedstock | < 30 s |
| **ESD-2 (Unit-level)** | Localized incident, single-unit runaway | Isolate affected unit, maintain others | < 60 s |
| **ESD-3 (Equipment-level)** | Pump trip, valve failure, instrument fault | Localized shutdown | < 90 s |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> While emergency planning is largely procedural, several quantitative relations are tested in KTU examinations.

| # | Equation / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_{R} = T_{D} + T_{A} + T_{M}$ | Total Response Time = Detection + Alarm + Mobilization | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $t_{evac} = \dfrac{N}{f \cdot w} + k \cdot L$ | Evacuation time (Hydraulic-flow model, Predtechenskii–Milinskii) | seconds |
| 3 | $N_{eff} = \dfrac{P}{60} \cdot f_{eff}$ | Effective flow rate through exit, $f_{eff}$ = flow factor (1.3–1.5) | persons/min |
| 4 | $D_{toxic}(x) = \dfrac{C(x)}{C_{safe}}$ | Toxic dose at distance $x$ normalised by safe concentration | dimensionless |
| 5 | $A = \pi R^2$ for pool fire, $R = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \dot{m} t}{\pi \rho}}$ | Pool radius for continuous spill, where $\dot{m}$ = mass flow | m |
| 6 | $EER = \dfrac{\text{Actual Persons Evacuated}}{\text{Total Persons at Risk}} \times 100\%$ | Emergency Evacuation Rate | % |
| 7 | $TRI = \dfrac{\text{Achieved Time}}{\text{Target Time}}$ | Time Reliability Index | ratio |

> **Where:**
> $T_D$ = Detection time, $T_A$ = Alarm time, $T_M$ = Mobilization time
> $N$ = number of people in zone, $f$ = specific flow (persons/m·s), $w$ = width of exit, $k$ = travel-time constant, $L$ = travel distance
> $P$ = population, $f_{eff}$ = effective flow factor

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Driller & Offshore Platforms**: ESD-0 initiates in <5 s during well blowout scenarios.
- **LNG Terminals**: Water curtain systems and foam application form the gas dispersion mitigation strategy.
- **Pharmaceutical Plants**: Although lower inventory, they require detailed plans because of highly toxic APIs (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients).
- **Refineries**: Mutual aid agreements with neighbouring industries are legally mandated under India’s **Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC, 1989)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Procedure to Develop an Emergency Plan

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **mandatory 14-mark answer** in KTU. Memorize this **10-step procedure**.

**Step 1 — Establish the Planning Team**
Form a multi-disciplinary team: Operations Manager (leader), Safety Engineer, Process Engineer, Medical Officer, Fire Officer, Security, Public Relations Officer, and a Legal/Compliance Officer.

**Step 2 — Conduct Hazard Identification (HAZID) & Risk Assessment**
Apply HAZOP, What-If, FMEA, or Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) to determine credible worst-case scenarios. Calculate the **Emergency Planning Quantity (EPQ)** using:

$$
EPQ = \max \left( \text{Maximum On-site Inventory}, \; 2 \times \text{Threshold Quantity} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Identify On-Site & Off-Site Receptors**
Map schools, hospitals, residential zones within the **Emergency Planning Zone (EPZ)**, typically the zone where $C_{toxic}(x) \geq ERPG-2$ or where thermal radiation $\geq 5 \; kW/m^2$.

**Step 4 — Define Emergency Scenarios & Trigger Levels**
Categorise alerts into **3 tiers**:

$$
\text{Tier 1 (Alert)} \;\to\; \text{Tier 2 (Site Emergency)} \;\to\; \text{Tier 3 (Community Emergency)}
$$

**Step 5 — Develop the Emergency Organization Chart**
Assign roles: Incident Commander, Operations Chief, Logistics Chief, Planning Chief, Finance/Admin Chief, Safety Officer, Public Information Officer, and Liaison Officer (ICS — Incident Command System).

**Step 6 — Design Communication System**
Specify alarm tones (e.g., **continuous siren = evacuate**, **intermittent = take shelter**, **verbal PA = all clear**), backup radio frequencies (VHF/UHF), and the **call tree** with primary/secondary numbers.

**Step 7 — Draft Emergency Response Procedures (ERPs)**
For each scenario, prepare a **stand-alone action card** listing:
- Activation trigger
- Who activates
- What immediate actions (isolate, vent, fire attack)
- Evacuation routes
- Assembly points
- Decontamination steps

**Step 8 — Establish Emergency Control Centre (ECC)**
A hardened, blast-resistant room with:
- Process diagrams (P&ID)
- Communication equipment
- Breathing air supplies (**SCBA, 30 min minimum**)
- Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)
- Emergency lighting & backup power

**Step 9 — Conduct Training & Mock Drills**
Schedule **full-scale exercises at least once per year**, **tabletop drills quarterly**, and **functional drills monthly**. Compliance: **OSHA 1910.120(q)** and **OSHA 1910.38**.

**Step 10 — Audit, Review & Update**
Plan must be reviewed **at least annually** or after every incident. Key Performance Indicators (KPIs):

$$
\text{Drill Compliance} = \dfrac{\text{Drills Conducted}}{\text{Planned Drills}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Mean Response Time (MRT)} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{R,i}
$$

### 3.2 Quantitative Example — Evacuation Time Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question):**
A process control room has **120 occupants**. The single exit door is **1.2 m wide**. The specific flow through the door under emergency conditions is **1.3 persons/(m·s)**. Compute the evacuation time assuming the maximum travel distance inside is **25 m** with travel-time constant $k = 1.6$.

**Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify governing hydraulic model parameters.} \quad & N = 120, \; w = 1.2\;\text{m}, \; f = 1.3 \;\frac{\text{persons}}{\text{m·s}}, \; L = 25\;\text{m}, \; k = 1.6 \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Substitute into the evacuation formula.} \quad & t_{evac} = \dfrac{N}{f \cdot w} + k \cdot L \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Compute flow capacity term.} \quad & f \cdot w = 1.3 \times 1.2 = 1.56\;\text{persons/s} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4: Compute the queueing (flow) term.} \quad & \dfrac{N}{f \cdot w} = \dfrac{120}{1.56} = 76.92\;\text{s} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 5: Compute the travel term.} \quad & k \cdot L = 1.6 \times 25 = 40\;\text{s} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 6: Sum to obtain total evacuation time.} \quad & t_{evac} = 76.92 + 40 = 116.92\;\text{s} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 7: Convert and round for engineering use.} \quad & t_{evac} \approx 117\;\text{s} \approx 1\;\text{min}\;57\;\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The total evacuation time of **117 s (≈ 2 min)** is well within the **safe egress window of 6 minutes** recommended by **SFPE Handbook of Fire Protection Engineering**. Hence, the single-exit configuration is acceptable; however, an additional emergency exit is recommended for redundancy.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Emergency Decision Flow

| Observed Condition | Alert Level | Required Action | Authority to Declare |
|--------------------|-------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Small leak, contained | Tier 1 — Alert | Investigate, isolate | Shift Supervisor |
| Spreading pool fire / vapour cloud forming | Tier 2 — Site Emergency | Activate ECC, partial evacuation, fire team deploys | Plant Manager |
| Toxic gas drifting off-site, BLEVE imminent | Tier 3 — Community Emergency | Full evacuation, notify district authorities, mutual aid | Incident Commander + District Collector |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Emergency Response Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
