# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Module 4)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Occupational Health". List any four categories of occupational health hazards with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Occupational Health** is the promotion and maintenance of the highest degree of physical, mental, and social well-being of workers in all occupations (WHO/ILO definition).

The four categories of occupational health hazards with examples:

- **Chemical Hazards** — exposure to benzene vapors in a petrochemical plant (causes leukemia).
- **Physical Hazards** — noise from a compressor room exceeding 90 dBA (causes hearing loss).
- **Biological Hazards** — exposure to Legionella bacteria in cooling tower water (causes Legionnaires' disease).
- **Ergonomic Hazards** — repetitive lifting in a warehouse (causes musculoskeletal disorders).

*Note:* Psychosocial hazards (stress, harassment) may also be cited as the fifth category for full marks.

---

### Question 2 (Module 4)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is a Safety Committee? State the statutory requirements for its constitution in an Indian factory.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Safety Committee** is a formal body comprising equal representatives of employers and workers that promotes cooperation in developing and reviewing safety measures.

**Statutory requirements under Section 41G of the Factories Act, 1948:**

- Mandatory in factories employing **100 or more workers**.
- **Equal** representation of employer and worker nominees.
- Chaired by a **senior management representative**.
- Meets at least **once every quarter (every 3 months)**.
- Maintains **minutes** of every meeting, which are placed before the Chief Inspector of Factories.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **elements of an effective Occupational Health and Safety Administration (OHSA) system** in a process plant, with reference to the Factories Act 1948 and ISO 45001:2018.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

An effective OHSA system comprises the following **interlinked elements:**

**(i) Safety Policy and Top Management Commitment**
Top management must establish, document, and communicate a clear safety policy. Under ISO 45001 Clause 5, leadership commitment is non-delegable. The policy must include commitments to hazard prevention, legal compliance, and continual improvement.

**(ii) Organizational Structure and Accountability**
A written organizational chart defines safety responsibilities at every level — from the Managing Director (ultimate accountability) to the shop-floor worker (compliance with SOPs). Section 40 of the Factories Act mandates appointment of a **Safety Officer** in factories employing ≥1,000 workers (or hazardous processes under Sec. 41B).

**(iii) Safety Committee**
As per **Section 41G of the Factories Act 1948**, the committee has equal worker-management representation, meets quarterly, and maintains minutes.

**(iv) Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA)**
Systematic methods — HAZOP, JSA, FMEA — must be applied at the design stage and periodically during operation.

**(v) Permit to Work (PTW)**
A formal written system authorizes hazardous tasks: hot work, confined space entry, electrical isolation, lifting.

**(vi) Training and Competency**
Induction training, on-the-job training, refresher courses, and mock drills. Section 21 of the Factories Act requires safety training for all workers.

**(vii) Incident Reporting and Investigation**
All incidents (including near-misses) must be reported, investigated, and corrective actions verified.

**(viii) Emergency Preparedness**
On-site and off-site emergency plans, fire-fighting equipment, medical facilities, escape routes, and periodic drills (mandatory under MSIHC Rules 1989).

**(ix) Monitoring, Audits, and Management Review**
Internal audits, third-party audits, and management review of OHS performance.

**(x) Documentation and Records**
Maintain incident logs, training records, audit reports, PTW registers, medical records.

---

**(b)** A process plant has **250 employees** working **8 hours/day, 300 days/year**. In one year, there were **2 fatalities, 8 lost-time injuries (avg 45 days lost each), 12 non-lost-time recordable injuries, and 5 near-misses**. Calculate:
**(i)** TRIR, **(ii)** LTIFR, **(iii)** Severity Rate (using ILO 6,000-day fatality charge convention).

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Total man-hours worked:**

$$
H = 250 \times 8 \times 300 = 600{,}000 \text{ man-hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify recordable injuries:**

Recordable per OSHA 1904 = all fatalities + lost-time + non-lost-time injuries. Near-misses are NOT recordable.

$$
N_{\text{recordable}} = 2 + 8 + 12 = 22
$$

**Step 3 — Lost-time injuries count:**

$$
N_{LT} = 2 + 8 = 10
$$

**Step 4 — Total days lost (with 6,000-day fatality charge):**

$$
D = (2 \times 6000) + (8 \times 45) = 12{,}000 + 360 = 12{,}360 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 5 — TRIR (US 200,000 base):**

$$
\text{TRIR} = \dfrac{22 \times 200{,}000}{600{,}000} = 7.33 \text{ per 100 FTE}
$$

**Step 6 — LTIFR (international 10⁶ base):**

$$
\text{LTIFR} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^{6}}{600{,}000} = 16.67 \text{ per million man-hours}
$$

**Step 7 — Severity Rate (10⁶ base):**

$$
\text{SR} = \dfrac{12{,}360 \times 10^{6}}{600{,}000} = 20{,}600 \text{ days per million man-hours}
$$

**Interpretation:** The plant is significantly above world-class benchmarks and requires **urgent intervention** in hazard control and training.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of safety culture**. Discuss the **components of an effective safety management system (SMS)** as per ILO-OSH 2001 Guidelines, with a neat block diagram.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Safety Culture:**
Safety culture is the product of individual and group values, attitudes, perceptions, competencies, and patterns of behavior that determine the commitment to and the style and proficiency of an organization's health and safety management. (ACSNI, 1993).

**Components of an SMS per ILO-OSH 2001:**

The ILO-OSH 2001 Guidelines prescribe a **continuous improvement cycle** based on the **PDCA model (Plan-Do-Check-Act):**

1. **Policy:** OHS policy, worker participation, organizational arrangements.
2. **Organizing:** Defined responsibilities, accountability, competence, training, documentation, communication.
3. **Planning and Implementation:** Initial review, system planning, objectives, hazard prevention, prevention and control measures, management of change, emergency preparedness.
4. **Evaluation:** Performance monitoring, investigation of incidents, auditing, management review.
5. **Action for Improvement:** Preventive and corrective actions, continual improvement.

**Block diagram:**

```
POLICY (Plan) → ORGANIZING + PLANNING (Do)
                            ↓
                    EVALUATION (Check)
                            ↓
                   IMPROVEMENT (Act) → loops back to POLICY
```

A culture of **"zero harm"**, **visible leadership**, and **empowered workers** sustains the system.

---

**(b)** A chemical plant's annual records show: **Total man-hours = 2,000,000**; **Lost-time injuries = 6 (each causing 20 days lost)**; **Other recordable injuries = 10**; **1 fatality**. Calculate **TRIR, LTIFR, SR, and Safety Index**. State whether the plant meets world-class benchmarks (TRIR < 0.5, LTIFR < 1.0).

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Total recordable injuries:**

$$
N = 6 + 10 + 1 = 17
$$

**Step 2 — Lost-time injuries:**

$$
N_{LT} = 6 + 1 = 7
$$

**Step 3 — Total days lost (6000-day fatality charge):**

$$
D = (1 \times 6000) + (6 \times 20) = 6{,}120 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 4 — TRIR:**

$$
\text{TRIR} = \dfrac{17 \times 200{,}000}{2{,}000{,}000} = 1.7
$$

**Step 5 — LTIFR:**

$$
\text{LTIFR} = \dfrac{7 \times 10^{6}}{2{,}000{,}000} = 3.5
$$

**Step 6 — Severity Rate:**

$$
\text{SR} = \dfrac{6{,}120 \times 10^{6}}{2{,}000{,}000} = 3{,}060
$$

**Step 7 — Safety Index:**

$$
\text{SI} = \dfrac{3.5 \times 3060}{1000} = 10.71
$$

**Conclusion:** The plant **does NOT meet world-class benchmarks** (TRIR is 3.4× target, LTIFR is 3.5× target). Immediate root-cause analysis and process safety improvements are required.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 6,000-day fatality charge** in Severity Rate calculation — direct loss of 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing TRIR base (200,000) with LTIFR base (10⁶)** — answer becomes 5,000× wrong.
> 3. **Omitting near-misses from "recordable"** — near-misses are not recordable under OSHA 1904.
> 4. **Writing the safety committee composition incorrectly** — must be **equal** employer-worker representation; stating "majority management" loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Not citing the relevant Act section** (e.g., 41G, 40, 41B) — examiners explicitly look for Section numbers.
> 6. **Confusing OHSA (US) with OSHA** (the agency) — OHSA is the system; OSHA is the US regulatory body. Careless loss of 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **OHSA = OH (health, long-term) + OS (safety, acute) + Administration (system)**
- **ILO/WHO definition of OH** must be quoted verbatim for full marks
- **5 hazard categories:** Chemical, Physical, Biological, Ergonomic, Psychosocial
- **5-step Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE
- **Safety Committee (Sec 41G):** ≥100 workers, equal reps, quarterly meetings
- **Safety Officer (Sec 40/41B):** mandatory in hazardous factories
- **Permit to Work:** Hot work, cold work, confined space, electrical isolation, excavation, lift
- **TRIR base = 200,000 hrs; LTIFR base = 10⁶ hrs** — never swap
- **6,000-day fatality day-charge** is the ILO convention for Severity Rate
- **ISO 45001:2018** replaced OHSAS 18001 in 2018 — current global OHS standard
- **ILO-OSH 2001 Guidelines** are the international reference for SMS architecture
- **Indian statutes to remember:** Factories Act 1948, MSIHC Rules 1989, Public Liability Insurance Act 1991
- **World-class benchmarks:** TRIR < 0.5, LTIFR < 1.0
- **Near-misses are NOT recordable** injuries under OSHA 1904
- **PDCA cycle** is the backbone of every modern SMS (Plan-Do-Check-Act)
- **Inherently Safer Design (ISD):** Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Provisions in Factories Act
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT743 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safety Provisions in Factories Act

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to the Factories Act, 1948

The **Factories Act, 1948** is one of the earliest and most comprehensive pieces of labour welfare legislation enacted by the Parliament of India. It was passed on **23rd September 1948** and came into force on **1st April 1949**. The Act was formulated to consolidate and amend the law regulating labour in factories, with the primary objective of ensuring the **safety, health, and welfare** of workers employed in factory premises across India.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus**: For PECHT743, the emphasis is on the **Safety and Health Provisions** of the Act (Chapter III and Chapter IV), as they directly govern the design, operation, and management of process plants. Welfare provisions are secondary but frequently appear in Part A questions.

The Act extends to the **whole of India** and applies to the territories of **Goa, Daman and Diu, and Pondicherry** as well. It is administered by the **Ministry of Labour and Employment**, Government of India, with enforcement machinery vested in the State Governments through the office of the **Inspector of Factories**.

## 1.2 Formal Definition (Section 2 – Key Definitions)

Under **Section 2 of the Factories Act, 1948**, the following terminologies are defined with statutory precision:

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 2(e) – Approval, Licensing and Registration of Factories**
> **Section 2(k) – Definition of Factory**
> **Section 2(l) – Definition of Occupier**
> **Section 2(m) – Definition of Manufacturing Process**
> **Section 2(n) – Definition of Worker**

### Key Statutory Definitions

**(i) "Factory" [Section 2(k)]**
A factory is defined as any premises (including precincts) wherein:
- **10 or more workers** are employed on any day of the preceding 12 months, and in which a **manufacturing process** is being carried on with the aid of power, OR
- **20 or more workers** are employed on any day of the preceding 12 months, and in which a **manufacturing process** is being carried on **without the aid of power**.

$$\text{Factory Threshold} = \begin{cases} \geq 10 \text{ workers with power} \\ \geq 20 \text{ workers without power} \end{cases}$$

**(ii) "Manufacturing Process" [Section 2(m)]**
Any process for:
- Making, altering, repairing, ornamenting, finishing, packing, oiling, washing, cleaning, breaking up, demolishing, or otherwise treating or adapting any article or substance with a view to its use, sale, transport, delivery, or disposal;
- Pumping oil, water, sewage, or any other substance;
- Generating, transforming, or transmitting power;
- Composing types for printing, printing by letterpress, lithography, photogravure, or other similar process;
- **Such other process as the State Government may declare** under notification.

**(iii) "Worker" [Section 2(n)]**
A person is deemed to be a "worker" employed in a factory if:
- He/she is employed by the **manager** or **occupier** to do any skilled, semi-skilled, unskilled, manual, clerical, or supervisory work **for hire or reward**;
- The terms of employment are express or implied;
- It includes persons employed through a contractor (subject to certain conditions) but **excludes** members of the Armed Forces of the Union.

**(iv) "Occupier" [Section 2(l)]**
The person who has **ultimate control over the affairs of the factory**. In the case of a company, any director, managing director, or other officer who has ultimate control is treated as the occupier. In a partnership, the managing partner(s) are considered occupiers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legal Liability Anchor**: The term "Occupier" is the **principal duty-holder** under the Act. All statutory obligations regarding safety, health, and welfare are legally fastened upon the **occupier**, not merely the manager or supervisor.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Factory as a Living Organism" Model

Imagine a factory as a **human body**:

| Body Part | Factory Equivalent | Safety Provision (in Act) |
|---|---|---|
| **Skeletal System** | Machinery & Structural Framework | Safety Provisions (Fencing, Guarding) |
| **Respiratory System** | Ventilation & Dust/Fume Control | Health Provisions |
| **Circulatory System** | Piping, Electrical Network | Pressure Vessel & Electrical Safety |
| **Skin (Protective Layer)** | Fire Escapes, PPE, Fire-fighting | Fire Protection & Welfare |
| **Nervous System** | Inspectorate & Reporting | Inspections, Accident Notification |
| **Immune System** | Canteen, Rest Rooms, Crèches | Welfare Provisions |

Just as a doctor must ensure each organ functions safely (heart beats at **60–100 bpm**, lungs maintain **O₂ saturation > 95%**), the Factories Act prescribes **safety thresholds** for guarding machinery, ventilation standards, and welfare benchmarks.

## 1.4 Historical Evolution (Brief)

- **1881** – First Factories Act in India (following the British Factory Acts)
- **1911** – Revised Act introduced after the Royal Commission on Labour
- **1934** – Second major revision; expanded definition of "worker"
- **1948** – Current comprehensive Act enacted; came into force **1st April 1949**
- **Amendments** – Significant amendments in **1976, 1987, and 2005 (Section 7-A)** for hazardous processes

> [!NOTE]
> The **Factories (Amendment) Act, 1987** introduced the landmark **Section 7-A**, mandating safety reports and risk assessments for industries dealing with **hazardous processes** — a critical reference for KTU students studying Safety Engineering of Process Plants.

## 1.5 Scope and Applicability

The Act applies to:
- All factories employing **10+ workers (with power)** or **20+ workers (without power)**
- All seasonal factories (subject to special provisions)
- Factories engaged in **hazardous processes** (additional obligations under Section 7-A)
- Contract labour engaged through contractors is also covered under the principal employer's obligations.

It **does not apply to**:
- Mines and oilfields (covered under Mines Act, 1952)
- Mobile units of armed forces
- Hotels, restaurants, and eating houses (covered separately)
- Establishments of the Atomic Energy Department (exempted under notification)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Architecture of the Factories Act

The Factories Act, 1948 is organized into **12 Chapters** containing **120 Sections** and **3 Schedules**. For KTU 2024 Scheme, the modules of greatest academic weight are:

| Chapter | Sections | Subject Matter | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chapter II** | 3–9 | Inspecting Staff (Certifying Surgeons, Inspectors) | Medium |
| **Chapter III** | 11–20 | **Health Provisions** | High |
| **Chapter IV** | 21–41 | **Safety Provisions** | Very High |
| **Chapter V** | 42–50 | **Welfare Provisions** | Medium |
| **Chapter VI** | 51–66 | Working Hours | Medium |
| **Chapter VII** | 67–77 | Employment of Women & Young Persons | Low |
| **Chapter VIII** | 78–84 | Annual Leave with Wages | Low |
| **Chapter IX-A** | 87–91-A | **Hazardous Processes (Section 7-A)** | High |

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Health Provisions (Chapter III, Sections 11–20)

### Section 11 – Cleanliness
- Effective arrangements must be made for **regular sweeping and cleaning** of floors, passages, and staircases.
- The floor of every workroom must be cleaned at least **once every week** (or as prescribed by State Government).
- Disposal of dirt, refuse, and effluent must be in a manner that does not create a nuisance.

### Section 12 – Disposal of Wastes and Effluents
- Effective arrangements for treatment and disposal of **wastes and effluents** generated due to manufacturing processes.
- Must conform to standards prescribed under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**.

### Section 13 – Ventilation and Temperature
- **Effective and suitable provisions** must be made for ventilation by circulation of fresh air.
- Temperature must be maintained such that workers are not subjected to discomfort.
- The **wet-bulb temperature** in workrooms should not exceed:

$$T_{wb} \leq 30^\circ C \text{ (or as prescribed)}$$

- Where temperature exceeds limits, cooling arrangements (fans, AC, ventilation ducts) are mandated.

### Section 14 – Dust and Fume
- Effective measures must be taken to:
  - Prevent the **inhalation, ingestion, or absorption** of dust, fumes, or other impurities
  - Prevent their accumulation in any workroom to such an extent that they constitute a danger to health
- Methods include **local exhaust ventilation (LEV), mist projectors, masks, and respirators**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Connection**: Section 14 is **directly applicable to chemical process plants** where dust (e.g., cement, fertilizer) and fumes (e.g., SO₂, NOₓ, VOCs) pose chronic health hazards such as silicosis, asbestosis, and pneumoconiosis.

### Section 15 – Artificial Humidification
- Where humidity is artificially increased, water used must be pure and free from contamination.
- The prescribed **relative humidity (RH)** standard is between **40% and 70%**.

### Section 16 – Overcrowding
- Minimum floor space per worker:

$$A_{\text{min}} = 14.2 \text{ m}^3 (500 \text{ cubic feet)}$$

- During shift changeover, **no worker shall be made to occupy a space less than the prescribed minimum**.

### Section 17 – Lighting
- Effective provision for **sufficient and suitable lighting**, natural or artificial, or both.
- Glare, shadows, and sudden variations of light must be avoided.
- Minimum illumination standards (as per Indian Standards):

| Location Type | Minimum Lux (lm/m²) |
|---|---|
| General Workroom | **100 lux** |
| Fine Bench Work | **300 lux** |
| Very Fine Work (e.g., watchmaking) | **500–1000 lux** |
| Stores, Corridors | **50 lux** |

### Section 18 – Drinking Water
- A **sufficient supply of wholesome drinking water** must be provided at convenient points.
- The water must be **clearly marked "Drinking Water"** and located outside the workrooms.
- Tests must be conducted periodically for **potability**.

### Section 19 – Latrines and Urinals
- **Separate latrines and urinals** for male and female workers.
- Sanitary standards must be maintained.

### Section 20 – Spittoons
- Sufficient spittoons of a standard design must be provided.

## 2.3 Detailed Analysis of Safety Provisions (Chapter IV, Sections 21–41)

This is the **most important chapter** from a KTU examination standpoint for Safety Engineering.

### Section 21 – Fencing of Machinery
- Every moving part of a prime mover and every flywheel connected to it must be **securely fenced**, regardless of its position.
- Parts of **electric motors, generators, and rotating machinery** must be fenced.
- The standard fencing height and mesh size specifications are governed by **Indian Standard IS: 802 (Part I)**.

### Section 22 – Work on or Near Machinery in Motion
- No woman or young person (under 18) shall be allowed to clean, lubricate, or adjust any part of machinery while it is in motion.
- Adult male workers may do so only when:
  - The machinery is of a type prescribed by rules
  - Adequate precautions are taken (use of long-handled tools)

### Section 23 – Employment of Young Persons on Dangerous Machines
- No young person (under 18) shall work at any **dangerous machine** unless:
  - He has been fully instructed in the dangers and precautions
  - He has received sufficient training
  - He works under adequate supervision

### Section 24 – Striking Gear and Devices for Cutting Off Power
- Every machine driven by power must be fitted with:
  - An efficient **striking gear** to move the driving belt to the loose pulley
  - An efficient **device for cutting off power** from the传动 device

### Section 25 – Self-Acting Machines
- A **traversing part of a self-acting machine** must not be allowed to run out within a distance of **18 inches (45 cm)** from any fixed structure not belonging to the machine.

### Section 26 – Casing of New Machinery
- All machinery installed after **1st April 1949** must be equipped with **efficient protecting devices** of specified design.

### Section 27 – Prohibition of Employment of Women and Children Near Cotton Openers
- No woman or child (under 14) shall be employed in any part of a cotton room or near a cotton opener.

### Section 28 – Hoists and Lifts
- Every hoist and lift must be:
  - Of good mechanical construction
  - Sound material
  - Adequate strength
  - Fitted with **safety gates**
  - Tested periodically at intervals not exceeding **6 months** by a competent person

### Section 29 – Lifting Machines, Chains, Ropes, and Lifting Tackles
- Must be of sound material, adequate strength, free from defects.
- All chains, ropes, and lifting tackles must be **thoroughly examined by a competent person once every 12 months**, and a register maintained.

### Section 30 – Revolving Machinery
- Provisions for safe working near circular saws, planing machines, etc.

### Section 31 – Pressure Plant / Pressure Vessels
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for Process Plants (PECHT743)**: Every vessel used as a pressure vessel must be:
> - Constructed of sound material
> - Properly designed, and
> - **Hydraulically tested** once every **2 years** (or as prescribed)

### Section 32 – Floors, Stairs, and Means of Access
- All floors, stairs, passages, and gangways must be of sound construction and properly maintained.
- Handrails of adequate strength must be provided on staircases.

### Section 33 – Pits, Sumps, Openings in Floors
- Must be securely covered or fenced.

### Section 34 – Excessive Weights
- No person shall be employed to lift, carry, or move any load **so heavy as to be likely to cause injury**.

### Section 35 – Protection of Eyes
- Effective screens or goggles must be provided for protection against particles emitted from operations like grinding, chipping, etc.

### Section 36 – Precautions Against Dangerous Fumes
- Where workers are exposed to **dangerous fumes** (sulphuric acid fumes, mercury vapours, etc.), all practicable measures must be taken to prevent their inhalation.

### Section 37 – Precautions Regarding the Use of Portable Electric Light
- No portable electric light shall be used in any factory unless it is:
  - Of a voltage not exceeding **24 V** in confined spaces
  - Protected by a casing of substantial construction

### Section 38 – Explosive or Inflammable Dust, Gas, Vapour
- Effective measures must be taken to:
  - Prevent the formation of explosive mixtures
  - Remove any accumulation through ventilation
  - Avoid sources of ignition

### Section 39 – Precautions Against Fire
- Every factory must have:
  - Adequate means of escape in case of fire
  - Proper fire-fighting equipment
  - All workers trained in fire-fighting procedures

### Section 40 – Power to Require Specifications of Defective Parts
- The Inspector may require the occupier to disclose the specifications of any machinery or part believed to be defective.

### Section 41 – Maintenance of Buildings
- All buildings must be maintained in a structurally sound condition.

## 2.4 Welfare Provisions (Chapter V)

| Section | Provision |
|---|---|
| 42 | Washing facilities |
| 43 | Facilities for storing and drying clothing |
| 44 | Facilities for sitting |
| 45 | First-aid appliances (one **first-aid box** for every **150 workers**) |
| 46 | Canteens (where **250+ workers** are employed) |
| 47 | Shelters, rest rooms, and lunch rooms |
| 48 | Crèches (where **30+ female workers** are employed) |
| 49 | Welfare Officers (where **500+ workers** are employed) |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Statutory Thresholds)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No physical "formulae" exist for this Act, but the following numerical thresholds are critical for KTU numerical/scoring questions.**

| Parameter | Statutory Threshold | Section |
|---|---|---|
| Factory with power | **≥ 10 workers** | 2(k) |
| Factory without power | **≥ 20 workers** | 2(k) |
| Minimum floor space per worker | **14.2 m³ (500 ft³)** | 16 |
| Wet-bulb temperature ceiling | **30°C** | 13 |
| Relative humidity range | **40%–70%** | 15 |
| Hoist/Lift testing interval | **Every 6 months** | 28 |
| Chain/Rope testing interval | **Every 12 months** | 29 |
| Pressure vessel test interval | **Every 24 months** | 31 |
| First-aid box per worker count | **1 box per 150 workers** | 45 |
| Canteen threshold | **≥ 250 workers** | 46 |
| Crèche threshold | **≥ 30 female workers** | 48 |
| Welfare Officer threshold | **≥ 500 workers** | 49 |
| Maximum working hours/week | **48 hours** | 51 |
| Daily hours limit | **9 hours** | 54 |
| Minimum interval for rest | **30 minutes** (after 5 hrs) | 55 |
| Maximum overtime per quarter | **75 hours** | 64 |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Process Plant Engineering

In process industries (petrochemical, pharmaceutical, fertilizer, paint, refining), the Factories Act intersects with:

1. **PSM (Process Safety Management)** – OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 shares similar principles with Indian **Section 7-A**.
2. **Hazardous process regulations** – Requirement of Safety Reports, on-site emergency plans.
3. **Pressure Vessel Standards** – IS 2825, ASME Section VIII – synced with Section 31.
4. **Fire protection** – NFPA codes and Indian Standards on fire-fighting equipment.
5. **Industrial hygiene** – Threshold Limit Values (TLV) and Permissible Exposure Limits (PEL) referenced through Sections 13 and 14.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Takeaway**: Compliance with the Factories Act is **not just legal compliance**; it reduces plant downtime, insurance premiums, and operational risks, while enhancing the **License to Operate** in the community.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Since the Factories Act is a **legal/statutory** subject, traditional mathematical derivations do not apply. Instead, this section provides:

1. **Analytical Decision Matrix** – To determine applicability of the Act
2. **Statutory Threshold Derivation** – For numerical problem-solving
3. **Comparative Analytical Framework** – For KTU Part B analytical questions

## 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix: Determining Act Applicability

This is a frequently asked **KTU Part A (3 marks)** type analytical problem.

### **Problem Framework (KTU Style)**

A factory has **N workers** working in shifts. Manufacturing process uses **power** (or not). Determine:
(a) Whether the Factories Act applies
(b) Whether a canteen is mandatory
(c) Whether a Welfare Officer must be appointed
(d) How many first-aid boxes are required
(e) Working hours compliance check

### **Solution Methodology (Step-by-Step)**

**Given Data:**
- Number of workers = N
- Power used = Yes / No
- Female workers = F
- Children/young persons = Y
- Hazardous process = Yes / No

**Step 1: Factory Applicability Check**
$$\text{If (Power = Yes and } N \geq 10) \text{ OR (Power = No and } N \geq 20): \text{ Factory under Act}$$

**Step 2: Welfare Thresholds**
$$\text{Canteen} \Rightarrow N \geq 250$$

$$\text{Welfare Officer} \Rightarrow N \geq 500$$

$$\text{Crèche} \Rightarrow F \geq 30$$

**Step 3: First-aid Boxes**
$$\text{Number of First-Aid Boxes} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{150} \right\rceil$$

where $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ denotes the **ceiling function** (round up to next integer).

**Step 4: Maximum Workers per Shift Calculation**
$$\text{Maximum workers in any workroom} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{Volume of workroom (m}^3)}{14.2} \right\rfloor$$

### **Worked Example**

A factory uses power and has **245 workers** (including **42 female workers**). The total volume of the main workroom is **3500 m³**. Determine compliance requirements.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Power = Yes, N = 245 ≥ 10 → **Act applies**.

**Step 2:** Canteen threshold check: 245 < 250 → **Canteen NOT mandatory**.

**Step 3:** Welfare Officer threshold: 245 < 500 → **Welfare Officer NOT mandatory**.

**Step 4:** Crèche threshold: F = 42 ≥ 30 → **Crèche MANDATORY**.

**Step 5:** First-aid boxes:
$$\text{Boxes} = \left\lceil \frac{245}{150} \right\rceil = \lceil 1.633 \rceil = 2 \text{ boxes}$$

**Step 6:** Maximum workers in workroom (overcrowding check):
$$\text{Max workers} = \left\lfloor \frac{3500}{14.2} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 246.47 \rfloor = 246 \text{ workers}$$

→ Since workroom accommodates up to 246 workers, current workforce (245) is **just within limits**.

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Safety Provisions vs. Process Plant Reality

This matrix demonstrates **how each Section translates to engineering practice**:

| Section | Legal Text (Simplified) | Engineering Implementation in Process Plants |
|---|---|---|
| 21 | Fencing of machinery | Installation of **machine guards**, interlocked enclosures |
| 24 | Striking gear and power cutoff | **Emergency stop buttons**, lock-out tag-out (LOTO) systems |
| 28 | Hoists/lifts tested every 6 months | **Crane inspection logs**, third-party certification |
| 29 | Chain/rope examination every 12 months | **NDE of slings**, proof load testing |
| 31 | Pressure vessel test every 2 years | **Hydrostatic testing**, RBI (Risk-Based Inspection) |
| 36 | Precautions against dangerous fumes | **LEVs, scrubbers, gas detectors** (H₂S, SO₂, CO) |
| 38 | Explosive dust/gas prevention | **ATEX zoning**, intrinsically safe equipment (IS) |
| 39 | Fire precautions | **Foam systems, sprinkler networks, fire hydrants** |

## 3.3 Analytical Hierarchy of Duties (Section 7-A – Hazardous Processes)

For hazardous process plants, **Section 7-A** prescribes a layered responsibility framework:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  LAYER 1: WORKER OBLIGATIONS                 │
│  • Comply with safety procedures             │
│  • Use PPE provided                          │
│  • Report hazards immediately                │
└────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┘
                 │
┌────────────────▼─────────────────────────────┐
│  LAYER 2: MANAGER OBLIGATIONS                │
│  • Day-to-day compliance                     │
│  • Maintenance of records                    │
└────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┘
                 │
┌────────────────▼─────────────────────────────┐
│  LAYER 3: OCCUPIER OBLIGATIONS               │
│  • Ultimate liability                        │
│  • Safety Report preparation                 │
│  • On-site Emergency Plan                    │
└────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┘
                 │
┌────────────────▼─────────────────────────────┐
│  LAYER 4: INSPECTOR / STATE GOVT.            │
│  • Enforcement                               │
│  • Power to prohibit dangerous operations    │
│  • Power to issue improvement notices        │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## 3.4 Penalty Calculation Framework

Under **Section 92 of the Factories Act, 1948**, penalties are graduated:

| Offender | Maximum Imprisonment | Maximum Fine |
|---|---|---|
| Occupier / Manager (first offence) | **2 years** | **₹1,00,000** |
| Occupier / Manager (subsequent) | **Up to 3 years** | Up to ₹2,00,000 |
| Worker (wilful contravention) | — | **₹500** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Students often confuse "Occupier" with "Manager". The **Manager is responsible for daily operations**, but the **Occupier bears ultimate legal liability**. This distinction appears regularly in viva and Part A questions.

## 3.5 Notification Framework – Accidents and Diseases

**Section 88** mandates reporting of:
- Accidents causing death OR bodily injury preventing work for **48 hours or more**
- Occupational diseases listed in **Schedule III** (e.g., lead poisoning, silicosis, byssinosis, asbestosis, coal miners' pneumoconiosis)

**Time Limit**: Notice to Inspector within **24 hours** in case of death or serious bodily injury.

The diseases listed in Schedule III include:

$$\text{Schedule III Diseases} = \{ \text{Lead Poisoning, Silicosis, Byssinosis, Asbestosis,}$$ $$\text{Anthrax, Bagassosis, Brucellosis, Primary Epitheliomatous Cancer,} \ldots \}$$

This is **directly relevant** to process industries handling chemicals, dusts, and biological agents.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define the term "Factory" under Section 2(k) of the Factories Act, 1948. How does it differ for power-driven and non-power-driven establishments?**

**Model Answer:**

As per **Section 2(k)** of the Factories Act, 1948, a "factory" means any premises including the precincts thereof, whereon **10 or more workers** are employed (or were employed on any day of the preceding 12 months) and a manufacturing process is being carried on **with the aid of power**, OR whereon **20 or more workers** are employed and a manufacturing process is being carried on **without the aid of power**.

**Distinction:**

| Criterion | With Power | Without Power |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum workers | 10 | 20 |

The threshold for non-power factories is **double** that of power factories because manually operated processes inherently involve a larger workforce for comparable output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Tip**: "**P**ower = **10** = **P**air" (easy to recall)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**List the statutory intervals for testing (a) Hoists and Lifts, (b) Lifting Machines and Tackles, (c) Pressure Vessels, as per the Factories Act, 1948.**

**Model Answer:**

| Equipment | Section | Testing Interval | By Whom |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hoists and Lifts | Section 28 | **Every 6 months** | Competent Person |
| Lifting Machines, Chains, Ropes, Tackles | Section 29 | **Every 12 months** | Competent Person |
| Pressure Vessels | Section 31 | **Every 24 months (2 years)** | Competent Person |

A register of test results must be maintained in the prescribed form.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

**(a) Discuss in detail the Safety Provisions of the Factories Act, 1948 (Chapter IV) as applicable to a process plant dealing with hazardous chemicals. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Factories Act, 1948, Chapter IV (Sections 21–41) contains the most critical safety provisions applicable to process plants.

**Section 21 – Fencing of Machinery:** All moving parts of prime movers, flywheels, and rotating machinery must be securely fenced. In a chemical plant, mixers, agitators, conveyors, and centrifuge rotors must be guarded using **interlocked enclosures** that automatically stop the machine when opened.

**Section 22 – Work on Machinery in Motion:** Adult male workers may only lubricate or adjust parts of machinery in motion if prescribed by rules and using **long-handled tools** to keep hands away from danger points. **No woman or young person (under 18)** shall perform such work.

**Section 24 – Striking Gear and Power Cut-off:** Every power-driven machine must have an efficient striking gear and a **power cut-off switch** within easy reach of the operator. In process plants, **emergency stop (E-stop) buttons** must be provided at strategic locations.

**Section 28 – Hoists and Lifts:** Cranes, hoists, and lifts used in process plants must be tested by a competent person **every 6 months**, with safety gates and limit switches installed.

**Section 29 – Lifting Machines and Tackles:** Slings, chains, and ropes must be examined **every 12 months**, and a register of examinations maintained.

**Section 31 – Pressure Vessels:** Every pressure vessel used in a process plant (reactors, distillation columns, storage tanks) must be of sound material, properly designed, and **hydraulically tested every 2 years**. This is critical for plants handling compressed gases, steam, and high-temperature reactions.

**Section 36 – Dangerous Fumes:** Where workers are exposed to toxic fumes (e.g., H₂S, SO₂, Cl₂, NH₃), effective measures must be taken — installation of **local exhaust ventilation (LEV)**, gas detectors, alarms, and providing **respiratory protective equipment (RPE)**.

**Section 38 – Explosive or Inflammable Dust, Gas, Vapour:** In a chemical plant handling flammable solvents (acetone, toluene, hexane), measures include:
- **Area classification** (Zone 0, 1, 2 for gases; Zone 20, 21, 22 for dusts)
- Use of **flame-proof electrical equipment** (IS/IEC 60079)
- Provision of **intrinsically safe** instrumentation
- Bonding and earthing to prevent static charge accumulation
- Adequate **ventilation** to keep vapour concentrations below LEL (Lower Explosive Limit)

**Section 39 – Precautions Against Fire:** Adequate means of escape, fire-fighting equipment, fire alarms, and worker training. For a chemical plant, this includes **foam pourers, water spray systems (deluge valves), dry chemical powder (DCP) extinguishers**, and a dedicated fire-water reservoir.

---

**(b) What are the duties of an Occupier as stipulated in the Factories Act? Discuss the legal consequences of non-compliance. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Occupier (Section 2(l)):** The person who has **ultimate control over the affairs of the factory**. In a company, the **Managing Director or any Director** with ultimate control is deemed the occupier. In a partnership, the **managing partners** are occupiers.

**Duties of the Occupier (Selected Key Sections):**

1. **Section 7-A – Disclosure of Information:** Where a hazardous process is carried on, the occupier must:
   - Disclose all information regarding hazards to the Inspector, workers, and the local authority
   - Prepare a **Safety Report** containing details of process hazards, risk analysis, safety systems, and emergency procedures
   - Prepare an **On-site Emergency Plan**
   - Appoint a **Safety Officer** and qualified safety personnel
   - Conduct **safety audits** periodically

2. **Section 11–20 (Health):** Ensure cleanliness, ventilation, dust control, drinking water, latrines, and spittoons are in proper condition.

3. **Section 21–41 (Safety):** Provide fencing of machinery, hoists, pressure vessel testing, fire precautions, eye protection, and structural maintenance.

4. **Section 42–49 (Welfare):** Provide washing facilities, first-aid boxes (1 per 150 workers), canteen (≥ 250 workers), crèche (≥ 30 female workers), and Welfare Officer (≥ 500 workers).

5. **Section 79 – Maintenance of Registers:** Maintain a **Register of Adult Workers**, **Register of Young Persons**, and a **Register of Accidents**.

6. **Section 88 – Notice of Accidents:** Within **24 hours**, report to the Inspector any accident causing death or serious bodily injury (preventing work for ≥ 48 hours).

7. **Section 88-A – Notice of Dangerous Occurrences.**

**Legal Consequences of Non-Compliance (Section 92):**

| Nature of Offence | Imprisonment | Fine |
|---|---|---|
| First offence (by occupier/manager) | Up to **2 years** | Up to **₹1,00,000** |
| Subsequent offence | Up to **3 years** | Up to **₹2,00,000** |
| Wilful default by worker | — | Up to **₹500** |

If the contravention continues after conviction, an additional fine of up to **₹1,000 per day** may be imposed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning**: Students frequently **omit mentioning the specific sections** while listing duties. Always quote the section number (e.g., "Section 21", "Section 31") to fetch full marks. Examiners in KTU strictly award marks based on **statutory specificity**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[Alternative Choice for Internal Selection]`

**(a) Explain in detail the Welfare Provisions (Chapter V) of the Factories Act, 1948. Provide the employee-thresholds for each provision. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Chapter V (Sections 42–49) of the Factories Act, 1948 deals with welfare provisions aimed at improving the working conditions and well-being of factory workers.

**Section 42 – Washing Facilities:** Suitable and adequate washing facilities (including shower baths for workers in dangerous or dirty operations) must be provided and maintained. Separate facilities for male and female workers.

**Section 43 – Facilities for Storing and Drying Clothing:** Suitable arrangements must be provided for workers to store and dry their clothing that they are not wearing during work hours (e.g., work overalls).

**Section 44 – Facilities for Sitting:** In factories where workers are required to work standing, **suitable arrangements for sitting** must be provided so that workers may take advantage of any opportunity for rest.

**Section 45 – First-Aid Appliances:**
- A **first-aid box** must be provided and maintained, easily accessible, with contents as prescribed.
- Threshold: **1 first-aid box for every 150 workers**.
- Calculation:
$$\text{Number of Boxes} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{\text{workers}}}{150} \right\rceil$$

**Section 46 – Canteens:**
- Mandatory where **250 or more workers** are ordinarily employed.
- Must be located away from the workroom, adequately lighted, ventilated, and maintained.
- Food at subsidized rates; managed by a Canteen Managing Committee with worker representation.

**Section 47 – Shelters, Rest Rooms, and Lunch Rooms:**
- Where **100 or more workers** are employed, a **suitable shelter or rest room** must be provided.
- Must be sufficiently lighted, ventilated, and furnished.

**Section 48 – Crèches:**
- Mandatory where **30 or more female workers** are employed.
- Must provide accommodation for children under 6 years of age of female workers.
- Must be adequately furnished and staffed by trained women.

**Section 49 – Welfare Officers:**
- Mandatory in factories employing **500 or more workers**.
- A qualified Welfare Officer (degree/diploma holder in Labour Welfare/Social Science) must be appointed.
- Duties: advise on worker welfare, statutory compliance, and liaison with workers and management.

**Summary Table of Welfare Thresholds:**

| Provision | Section | Worker Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| First-aid boxes | 45 | 1 per 150 workers |
| Shelters/Rest rooms | 47 | ≥ 100 workers |
| Canteen | 46 | ≥ 250 workers |
| Crèche | 48 | ≥ 30 female workers |
| Welfare Officer | 49 | ≥ 500 workers |

---

**(b) Describe the powers and duties of an Inspector of Factories under the Factories Act, 1948. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Inspector of Factories** is appointed by the **State Government** under **Section 8** of the Act. The Inspector is the principal enforcement officer.

**Powers of the Inspector (Section 9 and 10):**

1. **Power of Entry and Inspection:** The Inspector may, at any reasonable time, enter any factory or premises to:
   - Inspect and examine the factory
   - Examine the processes, machinery, registers, certificates, and other documents
   - Take samples of substances used or handled

2. **Power to Examine Witnesses:** May examine the occupier, manager, or any worker regarding factory affairs.

3. **Power to Seize:** May seize any register, certificate, or document that may serve as evidence of an offence.

4. **Power to Take Measurements and Samples:** May take measurements of temperatures, humidity, ventilation, and **samples of dust, fumes, or effluents** for analysis.

5. **Power to Issue Orders:**
   - Direct the occupier to take measures within a specified time to remedy defects
   - Order the **stoppage of work** if the process is dangerous (Section 40)
   - Order the **stoppage of use** of any machinery, vessel, or apparatus deemed unsafe (Section 40-A)

6. **Power to Prosecute:** May file complaints in court for offences under the Act (Section 99).

**Duties of the Inspector:**

1. **Ensure Compliance:** Verify that the factory complies with all provisions of the Act and Rules.
2. **Investigate Accidents:** Conduct inquiries into accidents and dangerous occurrences.
3. **Advise the Occupier:** Provide guidance on statutory compliance and improvements.
4. **Submit Reports:** Submit periodic reports to the State Government.
5. **Maintain Records:** Keep records of inspections, notices issued, and actions taken.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction**: The Inspector is **not the employer**; he/she is an independent statutory authority. While they have wide powers of inspection, they **cannot modify plans** or give construction approvals — those powers lie with the **Chief Inspector** under Section 3.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning**: Students often **overstate the Inspector's powers**, claiming they can "close down the factory". In fact, the Inspector can only recommend stoppage of work or specific machinery; permanent closure requires a **Court order** under Section 102-A.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Safety Provisions in Factories Act**

✅ **Act Year & Enforcement**: 1948 (enforced from 1 April 1949)

✅ **Definition of Factory**: 10+ workers (with power) OR 20+ workers (without power) [Section 2(k)]

✅ **Occupier = Ultimate Control Holder** (Managing Director in case of company) [Section 2(l)]

✅ **Fencing of Machinery** is mandatory under Section 21 — applies to all moving parts.

✅ **Pressure Vessels** must be hydrostatically tested **every 2 years** [Section 31].

✅ **Hoists/Lifts** — 6 months test; **Chains/Ropes** — 12 months test.

✅ **Floor Space Per Worker** — minimum **14.2 m³ (500 ft³)** [Section 16].

✅ **First-Aid Box** — 1 box per **150 workers** [Section 45].

✅ **Canteen** — mandatory at **≥ 250 workers** [Section 46].

✅ **Crèche** — mandatory at **≥ 30 female workers** [Section 48].

✅ **Welfare Officer** — mandatory at **≥ 500 workers** [Section 49].

✅ **Section 7-A** is the **Hazardous Process Section** introduced in 1987 — must know: Safety Report, On-site Emergency Plan, disclosure of information.

✅ **Accident Notification** to Inspector — within **24 hours** for fatal/serious accidents [Section 88].

✅ **Maximum Working Hours** — **48 hours/week** and **9 hours/day** [Section 51 and 54].

✅ **Overtime Limit** — **75 hours per quarter** [Section 64].

✅ **Penalties** — First offence: 2 years imprisonment + ₹1,00,000 fine [Section 92].

✅ **Schedule III** lists 29 notifiable occupational diseases.

✅ **Inspector** is appointed by State Government under Section 8 — has powers of entry, inspection, sampling, and prosecution under Section 9.

✅ **Certifying Surgeon** is appointed under Section 10 for medical examination of young persons.

✅ **Special Provisions** — No woman or child near cotton openers [Section 27].

✅ **Wet-bulb temperature** ceiling — **30°C** [Section 13].

✅ **Relative humidity range** — **40–70%** [Section 15].

✅ **Lighting standards** — minimum **100 lux** for general workrooms [Section 17].

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Tip**: In KTU exams, when you mention a provision, **always quote the section number** (e.g., "As per Section 21 of the Factories Act, 1948..."). Examiners specifically look for this statutory citation, and it often differentiates a 6-mark answer from a 7-mark answer.
